# PREFACE TO SIXTH EDITION

Following the custom of issuing a revision of Carroll's Official Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky every six years the present edition is published with a view of enabling the bench and bar to procure the latest text of the practice laws.

All amendments to the Civil and Criminal Codes enacted since the last revision have been inserted, and a very great number of new notes of decisions of the Court of Appeals construing or affecting the text of the various sections have been added. The annotations have included cases from the Kentucky Opinions, and the decisions cited have been brought down to volume 182 of the Kentucky Reports (January, 1919), the cross references to the Southwestern Reporter being also given.

A new and, it is believed, valuable feature of the present edition is to be found in the insertion, in smaller type, of various sections of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, which relate to procedure or which affect, in any way, sections of the Codes. In this manner vexatious and unnecessary delays, which might otherwise occur through necessity of consulting another source for statutes governing civil or criminal procedure, will be avoided.

An endeavor has thus been made to render this revision of the Code a complete volume of the practice law.

Another new feature will be found in the cross references, in black-faced type, to sections of Newman's Pleading, Practice and Forms (3rd Edition) where the particular section of the Code is cited or referred to, thus enabling the practitioner to secure from this reference and the subjoined notes the fullest treatment of the subject. In like manner references have been made to Judge Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate of Persons Under Disability," under Sections 489, et seq. Also, under those sections governing appeals to the Court of Appeals in civil cases cross references have been given to Judge Miller's treatise on "Appellate Practice and Forms." The several sections of the Criminal Code have been treated by Judge Gregory in his "Kentucky Criminal Law and Procedure," and copious cross references to this Kentucky text book will aid in administration of the law. As each of these books (except that of Judge Grider) contains numerous Forms these latter will, with the Forms contained in the back part of this edition of the Codes, form a valuable source of aid.

# PREFACE TO SINTH EDITION

A complete table of amendatory acts will be found in the index. It is believed that a decided improvement over former editions will be found to be the insertion of the reference to the chapter and page of the Session Acts where the amendatory act and its title may be found. This has not been given heretofore.

The practice laws and laws governing the various courts, the laws governing removal of actions to the Federal Court, etc., as contained in the Appendix, have been inserted according to a revised plan.

The Index has been revised and enlarged, thereby increasing its value. In all ways the endeavor has been to prepare for the courts and attorney, and for all other persons desiring to consult the practice statutes, an accurate, complete and thoroughly useful copy of the law in annotated form.

THE BALDWIN LAW BOOK COMPANY.

Louisville, Ky., January, 1919

# PREFACE TO FIFTH EDITION

The first edition of these Codes was prepared and issued by me in 1888, and since that time, at intervals of about six years, new editions have been issued, the present being the fifth one.

This edition has been entirely printed from new plates, and contains all amendments to date, and the annotations include all cases reported in 150 Kentucky, bringing the notes of decisions down to December, 1912. There has also been added to the notes citations of the South Western Reporter, so that the citations include the Kentucky Law Reporter, the South Western Reporter and the Kentucky Reports.

The Kentucky Law Reporter, in which all opinions of the court were published either in full or in abstract, was first issued in July, 1880, and continued until March, 1908, when its publication was suspended. During this time there were issued thirty-two volumes—volume thirty-three was commenced and a few cases published in it, but as no index has ever been prepared to this volume the publication of the Reporter may be said to have ended with volume thirty-two.

The South Western Reporter, in which all the opinions of the court are published, was first issued in August, 1880—beginning with volume eight of the Law Reporter and volume eighty-four of the Kentucky Reports—and its publication has continued to the present time.

Prior to September, 1910—when the weekly publication by the State of the Advance Sheets of the Kentucky Reports was begun—the Kentucky Reports do not contain all opinions of the court, only such cases being published in the Kentucky Reports as the court directed, but beginning with 140 Kentucky Reports, in September, 1910, all opinions of the court are published in full in the Kentucky Reports and in the Advance Sheets of the Kentucky Reports.

In the Appendix will be found sections of the Kentucky Statutes relating to subjects that are often used in connection with the Code.

JOHN D. CARROLL.
New Castle, Ky., January, 1913.

# PREFACE TO FOURTH EDITION

The Codes of Practice edited by me have met with a generous reception from the profession and I have endeavored to make some substantial improvement in each edition.

This edition has been revised and printed from new plates, and contains all amendments and acts relating to the Code, including those passed by the General Assembly of 1906.

The annotations include all cases in the first twelve numbers of 28 Kentucky Law Reporter,—cited as "R,"—bringing notes down to March 15, 1906.

# PREFACES TO THIRD AND SECOND EDITIONS

publications are cited. The Law Reporter begins with 78 Ky. and 116 Ky. is the last Report issued, since its issual the Law Reporter alone is cited.

In the Appendix will be found the rules of the Court of Appeals and the sections of the Kentucky Statutes relating:

1. To the jurisdiction of the courts of the State.
2. To courts having six judges.
3. To courts having continuous sessions.
4. To selection of special judges.
5. To change of venue in civil and criminal cases.

JOHN D. CARROLL.

NEW Castle, Ky., June, 1906.

# PREFACE TO THIRD EDITION

This edition contains all amendments and notes of decisions to July 1, 1900, including cases reported in 101 Ky. and 21 Ky. Law Reporter. The book has been entirely reprinted and revised, and all notes will be found in appropriate places. The number of the sections on each page are placed at top of page, and other improvements have been made in the arrangement.

In the Appendix will be found the rules of the Court of Appeals and the sections of the Kentucky Statutes relating:

1. To the jurisdiction of the courts of the State.
2. To courts having four judges.
3. To courts having continuous sessions.
4. To selection of special judges.
5. To change of venue in civil and criminal cases.

Also the laws of the United States concerning removal of actions and prosecutions to the Federal courts.

Vew Castle, Ky., Sept., 1900. JOHN D. CARROLL

# PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION

This edition of the Codes contains all amendments and notes of decisions to January, 1895, including cases reported in 95 Ky. and 16 Ky. Law Reporter. Unreported decisions of the Court of Appeals found in the Ky. Law Reporters bearing upon sections of the Codes have also been inserted.

An index in the middle of a book seems out of place, and I have put indexes to both Codes, as well as forms, at the end of the volume.

The catch words to sections, the indexes and the notes have been revised, rewritten and made more complete, and many forms not in former editions have been added.

In an appendix will be found the sections of the Kentucky Statutes relating:

1. To the jurisdiction of all courts in the State.
2. To courts having four judges.
3. To courts having continuous sessions.

# PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION.

4. To selection of special judges.

5. To change of venue in civil and criminal cases.

Also the laws of the United States relating to the Removal of Actions and Prosecutions to the United States Courts, and the Rules adopted by the Court of Appeals of Kentucky.

In the citation of cases I have not assumed to criticize or comment, nor undertaken to say what the Court of Appeals will decide when it comes to construe sections not heretofore passed upon, but have contented myself with stating the law as I have found it in the cases. My observation has been that the court does not always adopt the views of the commentator, and that expressions of opinion are more misleading than useful.

The Kentucky Statutes contains the general laws of the State not found in the Codes, and the relation between the Statutes and Codes is so intimate that I have in every instance where either a section of the Codes or a note thereto contained any reference to the Statutes, or any section of the Statutes had any bearing on the Codes, cited and noted the section of the Statutes applicable.

JOHN D. CARROLL.
New Castle. Ky., March. 1895

# PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION.

The original Code of Civil Practice was adopted by the Legislature in March. 1851; this Code was revised and amended, a Criminal Code prepared, and, in 1854. the Codes of Practice in civil and criminal cases were adopted.

The present Codes were prepared, under an act of the Legislature, by Richard A. Buckner and Joshua F. Bullitt, Commissioners, and Alvin Duvall, umpire; were adopted by the Legislature in 1876, and took effect January 1, 1877.

This edition contains all amendments to date, and a number of acts relating directly to the subject-matter of the Codes. I have added at the end of the Civil Code an appendix giving original and appellate jurisdiction of courts in civil cases; and article 2, chapter 66, General Statutes relating to rent-mode and manner of recovery.

The notes of decisions of the Court of Appeals have, as a rule, been confined to cases decided since 1851—but a number of notes of decisions prior to that date have been inserted—and include cases reported in 84 Ky., bringing notes down to January term, 1887.

The sections in brackets refer to corresponding sections in the Codes of 1854; following the index of the Criminal Code is a table showing corresponding sections of that Code and Code of 1854.

I trust that the notes, references and forms will be found complete, the arrangement convenient, and that, as a whole, the book will meet with favor and prove useful to the profession.

I desire to acknowledge my deep obligations to Judge Joseph Barbour for valuable suggestions and advice frequently asked for, and cheerfully given. To E. W. Hines, Esq., I am indebted for his courtesy in furnishing me advance pages of 84 Ky.

JOHN D. CARROLL.
New Castle, KY., September, 1888.

THE CIVIL CODE OF PRACTICE.

# AN ACT Regulating Practice in Civil Actions.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, as follows:

# TITLE I.

# PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS.

1. Civil cases defined.
2. Civil actions defined.
3. Special proceedings.

# § 1. [Civil cases defined.]

Civil cases are actions or special proceedings. Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 5.

# § 2. [Civil actions defined.]

A civil action is a demand, by pleadings, in a court of justice, for the enforcement of an alleged right of a plaintiff against a defendant. ("Action" further defined, sec. 732-34.) Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 5.

# § 3. [Special proceedings.]

Every other civil case is a special proceeding. Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 5.

# § 2. (1) Civil actions.

See note to sec. 3. Provisional remedy, such as an attachment or injunction, is not a cause of action, it is incidental to the action in which it is sought, and can only be obtained in the court in which the action was brought or is pending at the time it is sought. Moore v. Sheppard, 1 Met. 97.

# 3. (1) Special proceedings.

The word "action" embraces special proceedings. Secs. 732-34. In the absence of any special authority for a summary remedy the party must resort to a civil action for the enforcement and protection of his private rights. Gay v. Morgan, 4 Bush 606, and it is only in cases where a specific remedy is provided by statute that rights can be enforced otherwise than by civil action. Stephens v.

# § 4 ["] [Form of action.]

There shall be but one form of action.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.) secs. 5a, 158, 368.

# § 5 [3] [Kinds of actions.]

Actions are ordinary or equitable.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 6.

# § 6 [4, 5] [Action—when to be equitable; when ordinary.]

Unless otherwise provided by this Code or other statute—

1. Equitable actions. Actions of which courts of chancery had jurisdiction.
2. Recovery must be alleged, and every essential allegation required to make a declaration good at common law upon demurrer must be made. The facts must be alleged so as to enable the opposite party to know what is meant to be proved, and also that an issue may be formed in regard to the subject-matter in dispute and to enable the court to pronounce the law upon the facts stated.
3. For some of the cases in which summary proceedings may be resorted to, see sec. 444.
4. Bastardy case is a civil or special proceeding.

Cross reference: Miller, 80 Ky. 47, 3 R. 523; Kinney v. O'Bannon, 6 Bush 692; Combs v. Com., 24 R. 1310, 71 S. W. 501.

Proceeding to enforce a forfeited recognizance or bond is a civil proceeding. Com. v. Hughes, 13 Bush 349.

Forms, not substance, abolished by Code; and the pleader must state in ordinary and concise language the facts constituting the cause of action. Every fact necessary to enable the plaintiff to.

Distinction between courts of equity and courts of law has not been abolished by the Code, but if an action be brought in equity when it should have been in ordinary, or vice versa, it shall not on that account be dismissed, but transferred to the proper docket. Fraley v. Peters, 12 Bush 469; and see sec. 8.

Title II] Form of Civil Actions.

# § 7 [6] [Lost or mutilated writing-action on.]

An ordinary or equitable action may be brought upon a bill of exchange, or a note or other obligation, or upon an indorsement or assignment thereof, which, or upon a judgment the record of which, is lost, destroyed, mutilated, or defaced, without fraud on the part of the plaintiff, or of those under whom he claims. But no action shall be brought upon an instrument transferable by delivery, merely, which is alleged to be lost, destroyed, mutilated, or defaced, without a previous tender by the plaintiff to the defendant, if his name and place of residence be known to the plaintiff, of an indemnifying bond, with good surety; nor shall judgment be given against the defendant, in such action, until such bond is given, with good surety approved by the court. (Form of bond, Form 95.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 76; Forms Nos. 4a, 332, 393.

fined. The pleadings in equitable and ordinary actions are the same, except that a petition in equity must be described by the words "petition in equity." Secs. 110-12. Without attempting to state fully what actions should be equitable and what ordinary, it may be stated that equity has jurisdiction of actions on return of "no property" and for discovery, sec. 439; by surety against principal before debt matures, sec. 237, and after maturity of debt, sec. 661; for sale of real property of infants and persons of unsound mind, sec. 459; and of joint owners, sec. 490; to settle trust estates and estates of deceased persons, sec. 428; to grant divorces and for alimony, sec. 420; to grant injunction, see. 271; to partition land and allot dower, see. 499; to enforce liens on real and personal property. Actions for assault and battery, assumpsit, covenant, debt, to recover possession of real estate and specific personal property, or for fraud or deceit in the sale of property, or for malicious prosecution, or seduction, or slander, or libel, or trespass, or to recover rent, should be brought in ordinary. Newman's Pleading and Practice.

# Rélief in equity.

If a party may maintain a common law action for damages for an injury to property, he may, in a proper state of case, resort to equity to prevent the injury. Greenup Co. v. 19

# Form of Civil Actions

# § 8 [7] [Error as to form of action may be corrected.]

An error of the plaintiff as to the form of action shall be cause, not for the abatement or dismissal of it, but merely for a change into the proper proceedings by an amendment of the pleadings and a transfer of the action to the proper docket.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 62, 95, 197a; Form No. 176.

# § 9 [8] [Error—how corrected.]

The error mentioned in the last section may be corrected by the plaintiff, without motion, at any time before the defendant answers, or afterward by leave of court.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 62, 75, 96.

# § 10 [0]

1. [Equitable action, transfer of-transfer generally.] The defendant, by motion made when he answers, may have an equitable action transferred to the ordinary docket, if, according to the provisions of section 6, it should have been an ordinary action, and if the answer presents a defense of which he is entitled to a jury trial.
2. [Motion of defendant to transfer before or when he answers.] The defendant, by motion made when or before he answers, may have an ordinary action transferred to the equity docket, if, according to the provisions of section 6, it should have been an equitable action.

Action shall be filed or the reason for failing to file must be stated in the pleading, and see Stout v. Ashton, 5' Mon. 251; Hills v. Lackey, 9 Dana 81; Graham v. Hackwith, 1 Mar. 315.

(4 Instruments transferable by delivery—definition of in meaning of this section. Hill v. Gizzard, 133 Ky. 816, 119 S. W. 169; Hoyland v. Nat. Bank, 137 Ky. 692, 126 S. W. 356; Foster v. Metcafe, 144 Ky. 385, 138 S. W. 314.

5 Petition should aver that instrument was lost without fraud. Hoyland v. Nat. Bank, 137 Ky. 682, 126 S. W. 356, and see 150 Ky. 336.

9. Motion to transfer to ordinary is equivalent to an objection to a trial by the chancellor, and if motion is improperly overruled it is reversible error. Walker v. Leslie, 90 Ky. 642, 12 R. 581, 14 S. W. 682.

10. (1) Court may transfer on its own motion. Finley v. Meadows, 134 Ky. 70, 119 S. W. 216.

§ 8. (1) Error as to form of action—how corrected. When the court has jurisdiction, an error as to the form of action adopted does not authorize a dismissal; it should be transferred to the proper docket. Turner v. Newman, 19 R. 231, 39 S. W. 504; Landsdale v. Mitchell, 14 B. M. 348; Foster v. Watson, 16 B. M. 377; 15 B. M. 168; 1 Met. 593; 12 Bush 20.

# § 11

# Form of Civil Actions.

# § 11 [10] [Ordinary action-how transferred to equity-practice.]

In an ordinary action properly commenced as such.--

1. Transfer for trial of several equitable issues. If there be several issues, all of which were, before the first day of August, 1851, cognizable in chancery, though none was exclusively so, either party may, by motion, have the case transferred to the equity docket. (See subs. 4, sec. 10.)
2. Transfer for trial of one equitable issue. Either party may, by motion, have the case transferred to the equity docket for the trial of any issue which, before the said day, was exclusively cognizable in chancery.

# (6) Practice-Motion for new trial.

When an ordinary action is brought and heard in equity the court will treat the evidence copied and certified by clerk as part of record without a bill of exceptions. Fraley v. Peters, 12 Bush 469, and a motion for a new trial is not necessary. City of Cov. v. Limerick, 19 R. 330, 40 S. W. 254; but where an issue out of chancery is submitted to a jury a motion for a new trial and bill of exceptions is necessary. Sinms v. Harris, 19 R. 1439, 38 S. W. 490. See further as to practice and bills of exception. Dupoyster v. Fort Jeff. Improvement Co., 28 R. 504, 89 S. W. 509.

# Practice. See further notes to secs. 11, 12.

# (8) Waiver of objection to form of action.

If an action at law is brought in equity, and neither party moves to transfer, and it is not transferred by the court, it is the duty of the court to render judgment according to the rights of the parties. Ky. Mut. Co. v. Turner, 89 Ky. 665, 11 R. 793, 13 S. W. 104; KiNeOn v. Rich, 100 S. W. 249, 30 R. 1107. Turner v. Newman, 19 R. 231, 39 S. W. 504, and its judgment on the facts will be treated as the verdict of a jury by the Court of Appeals. Fraley v. Peters, 12 Bush 469; Louisville, St. L. & T. Ry. Co. v. Taylor, 96 Ky. 241, 16 R. 579, 28 S. W. 666.

# (9) Waiver of right to transfer-motion too late.

Fritts v. Kirehdorfer, 136 Ky. 643, 124 S. W. 882; Harmon v. Thompson, 119 Ky. 528, 84 S. W. 569, 27 R. 181; Kineon v. Rich, 100 S. W. 249, 30 R. 1107; Lewis v. Helton, 144 Ky. 595, 139 S. W. 772; Gray Tie Co. v. Clark, 30 R. 409, 98 S. W. 1000.

# (10) Harmless error.

An error in overruling a motion to transfer a cause to the ordinary docket and a motion for an issue out of chancery was held harmless, where the jury could not have come to any other conclusion than that reached by the chancellor. Riffe & Jones v. McKinney Deposit Bank, 171 Ky. 757, 188 S. W. 775.

# (11) Quiet title-actions to.

A petition in equity seeking to quiet title alleged that defendants were in possession. The action must be treated as a common law ejectment, and the decision of the chancellor as the verdict of a properly instructed jury, although no transfer was made to the ordinary docket. Reynolds v. Binion, 177 Ky. 189, 197 S. W. 641. If a suit to quiet title is tried as an equity suit, and no objection or motion is made to transfer it to the ordinary docket, objection cannot be made after judgment. Perry v. Eagle Coal Co., 170 Ky. 824, 186 S. W. 875.

# (12) Amendment of character of action.

A petition in equity sought an injunction. An amendment converted the action into an ejectment. It was properly transferred to the ordinary docket. Harrod v. Armstrong, 177 Ky. 317, 197 S. W. 816.

# § 11.

1. Defendant not entitled to transfer of an ordinary action, properly commenced as such, unless the answer presents a valid equitable defense. Jones v. Letcher, 13 B.'M. 363, but see subsection 4, sec. 10, and note 4 thereto. A defendant in an action to enforce a mechanic's lien was not entitled to a transfer of the case to the ordinary docket for trial of issues of fact. Rieger v. Schulte, 151 Ky. 129, 151 S. W. 395.
2. Equitable defense. When an answer filed in an ordinary action contains a valid equitable defense, no judgment can be rendered until some disposition is made of the answer, and in such case the action should be transferred to equity. Bosley v. Mattingly, 14 B. M. 89; Harreld v. Howard, 80 Ky. 51, 3 R. 554.
3. Equitable and legal defense. If the answer to an ordinary action presents an equitable and a legal defense,

Title II] Form of Civil Actions. [§ 12

# 3. [Trial of common law issue first.]

If there be an issue which was not cognizable in chancery, and an issue which was exclusively cognizable in chancery, before the said day, the plaintiff may have the former issue tried before the latter is disposed of.

# 4. [Consent of parties when necessary.]

If there be an issue which was not cognizable in chancery, and an issue which was cognizable in chancery, but not exclusively so, before the said day, the case shall not be transferred to the equity docket without consent of the parties.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 62, 100, 102, 814, 817.

# § 12. [Equitable action-trial of issue by jury-practice.]

In an equitable action, properly commenced as such, either party may, by motion, request that the equitable issue should be tried by the court and the legal issue referred to a jury, unless a jury is waived. Both issues should be disposed of before judgment. Smith v. Moberly, 15 B. M. 70; Petty v. Malier, 15 B. M. 591; Geoghegan v. Ditto, 2 Met. 433; Bennett v. Titherington, 6 Bush 192.

# (4) Where legal and equitable issues are formed in an ordinary action,

the court should transfer only the equitable issue to equity, because if the equitable defense fail, the defendant is entitled to a jury to try the legal issue; but if the equitable issue is a complete defense to the action, the refusal to submit legal issue to a jury is not error. Winmer v. Ficklin, 14 Bush 193.

# (5) Error in transferring ordinary action.

If the plaintiff in an ordinary action is entitled to a jury trial, it is error to transfer action to equity, and he is not concluded by the judgment dismissing his petition, although he failed to take proof to support his cause of action. Creager v. Walker, 7 Bush 1; nor does a party waive his right to a trial by jury by consenting to a transfer so that certain issues may be tried with a pending equitable action. Betz v. Newport P. M. A., 6 R. 222. Issue as to title to land in action to enjoin destructive trespass triable by jury and not to be transferred. Higdon v. Wayne Co. Security Co., 154 Ky. 337, 157 S. W. 708.

The constitution guarantees a jury trial, and a party cannot be deprived of that right by converting a legal right into an equitable one or by giving the chancellor an exclusive right to try legal issues, because there is some equitable right that arises out of them. Carder v. Weisenburgh, 95 Ky. 135, 15 R. 497, 23 S. W. 964; O'Connor v. Henderson Bridge Co., 95 Ky. 633, 16 R. 244, 27 S. W. 251, 78 Ky. 435. See further note 4, sec. 10.

# (7) Failure to transfer an action properly brought at law to equity

for the purpose of having alleged mistakes corrected is not a reversible error when the case is disposed of by the judge in the same manner as if it had been tried by the chancellor. Mullins v. Pendleton Co., 6 R. 598.

# (8) Issues triable by jury-Practice, right of parties to jury.

Moraneck v. Martineck, 107 S. W. 759, 32 R. 971, 128 Ky. 155.

# (8) Judgment of chancellor in equitable action

is not entitled to the weight of the verdict of a properly instructed jury. Farmer's Bank v. Stapp, 97 Ky. 432, 17 R. 290, 30 S. W. 1000; Scott v. Mitchell, 19 R. 218, 39 S. W. 507; McCampbell v. McCampbell, 103 Ky. 745, 20 R. 552, 46 S. W. 18; Boli v. Irwin, 21 R. 366, 51 S. W. 444; Campbell v. Trosper, 108 Ky. 602, 22 R. 277, 57 S. W. 245. See further cases cited in note 3, sec. 10.

# (9) Parties may elect to try ordinary action in equity

and if they do, it will be so treated on appeal and disposed of on its merits as an equity case. Cornet v. Burchfield, 142 Ky. 357, 134 S. W. 466; Northrup v. Summers, 132 Ky. 156, 116 S. W. 699; Campbell v. Trosper, 108 Ky. 602, 57 S. W. 245, 22 R. 277.

# (10) Fraud-issue of.

An issue as to whether or not fraud existed in a transaction was one of which courts of chancery had jurisdiction before the first day of August, 1851, and a defendant is not entitled to a jury trial as a matter of right. Gray v. Grimm, 157 Ky. 603, 163 S. W. 762.

# §12. (1) Depositions-or oral evidence-practice.

Moraneck v. Martineck, 128 Ky. 155, 107 S. W. 759, 32 R. 971;

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 62, 98; Form No. 178.

Lewis v. Helton, 144 Ky. 595, 139 S. W. 772; Talbot v. Bedford, 21 R. 897, 53 S. W. 294.

# Issue out of chancery

If the issue involved in an equitable action is purely a legal one, and the equitable right depends upon the decision of such issue, the case, on motion, must be transferred for a jury trial, and the court has no discretion in the matter; but if the case is purely equitable, the court may or may not, in its discretion, obtain the advisory aid of a jury as to an issue of fact.

('ar-der v. Weisenburgh, 95 Kv. 135, 15 R. 497, 23 S. W. 964; O'Connor v. Heuderson Bridge Co., 95 Ky. 633, 16 R. 244, 27 S. W. 251, 983; Hill v. Phillips. 87 Ky. 169, 10 R. 31, 7 S. W. 917; BAxtEr v. Knox, 31 S. W. 284, 17 R. 489; Small v. Reeves, 104 Ky. 289, 46 S. W. 726, 20 R. 504; Meck v. MeCall, 80 Ky. 371, 4 R. 255; Reese v. Youtsey, 113 Ky. 839, 24 R. 603, 69 S. W. 708; Jones v. Wood, 24 R. 840, 70 S. W. 45; Blakey v. Johnson. 13 Bush 197; Kennedy v. Ten Broeck, 11 Bush 241; Landrum v. Wells, 122 S. W. 214; Wisdon v. Nichols-Shepherd Co., 139 Ky. 506, 97 S. W. 18, 29 R. 1128; Barnes v. Johnson, 111 S. W. 373; Lewis v. Helton, 144 Ky. 595, 139 S. W. 772; King Co. v. L. &N. R. Co., 13I Ky. 46, 114 S. W. 308; Moraneck v. Martineck. 128 Ky. 155, 107 S. W. 759. 32 R. 793; Bush v. Eastern Ky. T. & L. Co., 90 S. W. 547, 28 R. 773; Walton Brick Co. v. Anderson F. & M. Co.. 142 Ky. 274. 134 S. W. 136; Barton v. Barton, 142 Ky. 487, 134 S. W. 902; Comingor v. Louisville Trust Co., 128 Ky. 697, 108 S. W. 950, 111 S. W. 681.)

# Jury trial-effect of verdict

When a distinct legal issue is submitted to a jury in an action begun in equity and transferred for the settlement of the legal issue, the verdict of the jury is to be treated as in ordinary jury trials, and will not be disturbed unless palpably against the evidence.

Hill v. Phillips, 87 Ky. 169, 10 R. 31, 7 S. W. 917; Moraneck v. Martineck, 128 Ky. 155. 107 S. W. 759, 32 R. 793, and cases cited in preceding note; but when an issue of fact in a purely equitable action is submitted to a jury, their verdict is not necessarily conclusive, and the chancellor may disregard it.

Hill v. Phillips, S7 Ky. 169, 10 R. 31, 7 S. W. 917; MeElwain v. Russell, 11 R. 649, 11 S. W. 1067; Ford v. Ellis, 21 R. 1837, 56 S. W. 512; Moraneck v. Martineck, 128 Kv. 155, 107 S. W. 759, 32 R. 793, and cases cited in preceding note.

# Motion for a new trial and bill of exceptions

is necessary when an issue out of chancery is submitted to a jury. Sims v. Lanehart, 19 R. 1439.

# Motion to transfer

comes too late after the court has rendered a judgment determining all the issues involved. Hartford Ins. Co. v. Hans, S7 Ky. 531, 10 R. 573. 9 S. W. 720; the motion must be made in a reasonable time or it will be denied. Chcnault v. Eastern Ky. T. & L. C0., 26 R. 107S, 83 S. W. 552; Lewis v. Helton, 144 Ky. 595, 139 S. W. 772; Blackburn v. Simpson, 144 Ky. 503, 139 S. W. 758, and see further notes to sec. 10.

# Reversible error

to refuse trial of issue, when party entitled to jury. Tucker v. Russell, 26 R. 1086, 83 S. W. 555.

# See further as to trial of issues

— Sees. 312, 313 and notes thereto.

# Several issues-separate answers

Where several issues are submitted they should be answered separately by the jury. Bannon v. Bannon Sewer Pipe Co., 136 Ky. 556, 119 S. W. 1170.

# Certain issues-when transferable and when transfer improper

Trespass, in action to enjoin threatened trespass. Consolidation Coal Co. v. Vanover, 166 Ky. 172, 179 S. W. 43. Mechanic's lien suit. Rieger v. Schulte, 131 Ky. 129, 151 S. W. 395. Division of land. Williams v. Capital Mining, Lumber and Oil Co., 153 Ky. 772, 156 S. W. 409. Adverse possession. Winchester v. Watson, 169 Ky. 213, 183 S. W. 483.

# Amendment changing character of action

Harrod v. Armstrong, 177 Ky. 317, 197 S. W. 816.

# Issue must exist

No right to transfer under this section exists unless there be an issue. Keystone Com. Co. v. City of Maysville, 154 Ky. 239, 157 S. W. 25.

Title II] Form of Civil Actions.

# § 13. [Transfer of actions from one court to another.]

In counties wherein the jurisdiction of circuit courts is vested in different courts—

1. [Application of this chapter.] The provisions of this chapter, concerning transfers to the proper docket, apply to transfers to the proper court.
2. [Transfer from one court to another—practice.] Upon an order for the transfer of an action from one court to another, the papers in the action and a copy of the order shall be delivered by the clerk of the court which makes the order to the clerk of the court to which the action is transferred; and it shall proceed as if it had been brought in the latter court.* (Transfer of attachments, see. 210.)

*See note.-Ed.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 98.

# § 14 [11] [Bond may be required before ordinary action transferred.]

In an ordinary action, properly commenced as such, if the defendant be entitled to the transfer of one or more issues to the equity docket, and move for such transfer, the plaintiff may file his affidavit that he verily believes he will succeed in the action, and that the collection of his claim, after judgment, will be endangered by delay arising from such transfer; and if such affidavit be filed, the court shall not grant the motion of the defendant to transfer, except upon condition that the defendant execute a bond, with good surety, approved by the court, that he will pay any judgment rendered in favor of the plaintiff: Provided, That no such bond shall be required if the trial of the case or issue transferred take place during the term at which the transfer is made, nor until all issues not transferred are tried or disposed of in favor of the plaintiff. (Form of affidavit, Form 105; form of bond, Form 94.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 100, 111b; Form No. 392.

# § 15 [12] [Waiver of error as to form of action-"exception."]

An error as to the kind of proceedings adopted in the action is waived by failure to move for its correction at the time and in the manner prescribed in this chapter; and errors in the decisions of the court, on any of the motions named in this chapter, are waived, unless excepted to at the time—which may be done by the clerk noting, at the end of such decision, words.

§ 13. This section is obsolete, as there is now only one Circuit Court in each county. Although in some of the larger counties the court has several branches or divisions. The practice is further regulated by statute. See Appendix.-Ed.

§ 14. (1) Failure to give bond-practice. To a suit on a note, defendant pleaded an exclusively equitable defense and moved to transfer to equity. The plaintiff filed the affidavit provided for in this section, and, failing to execute bond, his motion was overruled—as to proper practice. See Harreld v. Howard, 80 Ky. 51, 3 R. 554.

(2) Transfer without bond-court may direct. Davis v. Ferguson, 92 S. W. 968, 29 R. 214.

# § 16 [13] [Provisions of Code apply to all civil actions.]

The provisions of this Code apply to all civil actions, unless the contrary appears.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# § 17 [14] [Ordinary judgment—when corrected in equity-set-off or counter claim.]

A judgment obtained in an ordinary action shall not be annulled or modified by any order in an equitable action, except for a defense which arises or is discovered after rendition of the judgment. But such judgment does not prevent the recovery of any claim which was not, though it might have been, used as a defense by way of set-off or counter claim in the action. (As to how judgments vacated and modified, secs. 513 to 524.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 77, 436, 437, 437a, 475b, 501, 508c, 614e, 671, 791, 816, 817, 849; Forms Nos. 30, 31, 271.

# § 16. (1) Conflict of laws.

In Com. v. Watts, 84 Ky. 537, 8 R. 571, 2 S. W. 123, the court says: "It was therefore not intended that either the act adopting the Civil or the one adopting the Criminal Code should repeal the General (now Kentucky) Statutes as a whole or any part thereof, except such as in the repealing sections of both comes within the purview of the acts respectively," and see sec. 838.

"Finding such an intermingling of rights and remedies in the Code and Statutes, this court holds that a remedial act found in the General (now Kentucky) Statutes is not repealed unless a remedy for the enforcement of the same right is found in the Code." Newman v. Ecton, 100 Ky. 653, 14 R. 793, 21 S. W. 526.

# § 18 Parties to Actions Generally.

Every action must be prosecuted in the name of the real party in interest, except as is provided in section 21.

1. Administrator de bonis non may maintain an action on a note taken by former administrator in his own name for a debt due the intestate, he not being charged with it in the settlement of his accounts. Burrus v. Roulhae, 2 Bush 39; Maraman v. Trunnell, 3 Met. 146.
2. Apportionment warrants. Voris v. Gallagher, 27 R. 1101. 57 S. W. 775.
3. Assignments for creditors. Creditors may sue where assignee refuses. Ellis v. Western National Bank, 136 Ky. 310, 124 S. W. 334.
4. Assignor. Payee of note who has transferred it by parol is necessary party to an action in name of assignee. (ill v. Johnson, 1 Met. 619; Perry v. Seitz, 2 Duy. 122; but if assignment is in writing assignor is not necessary party. True v. Triplett, 4 Met. 57.
5. Holder of a check who can trace legal title to it may maintain action on it, whether he possesses beneficial interests in it or not. Harpending v. Daniel, 80 Ky. 449. 4 R. 330.
6. The holder of an unaccepted check on bankers may maintain action on it against them, if drawer had sufficient deposit to pay it at the time it was drawn, and they were notified it was drawn on them. Lester v. Given, 8 Bush 357.
7. Bailee. Plaintiff in action for damages for destruction of property not allowable. Sandy Valley & Elkhorn Ry. Co. v. Bentley, 175 Ky. 736, 194 S. W. 906.
8. Beneficiary of contract. Party for whose benefit a contract is made may sue thereon in his own name, although the undertaking is not directly to or with him. Allen v. Thomas, 3 Met. 195; Smith v. Smith, 5 Bush 625.
9. Covenants real run with the land, and if broken the right of action is in the transferee of the covenantee. Nunnally v. White, 3 Met. 584.

Title II Parties to Actions Generally.

# § 19 [81] [Assignee of chose in action, defenses allowed against--check, note or bill.]

In the case of an assignment of a thing in action, the action by the assignee is without prejudice to any discount, set-off or person who agreed therein to pay his debts. *Garvin v. Mobley, 1 Bush 48.*

# (17) Equitable owner.

Action for debt not assignable by statute must be brought in name of equitable owner, and the assignor is a necessary party, either plaintiff or defendant. *Lytle v. Lytle, 2 Met. 127; 4 Bush 420; 1 Met. 649; 2 Duv. 122; 12 Bush 140, and see sec. 19.*

# (18) Officer's bond.

Action on may be brought by a party in interest in his own name, if there is any covenant in the bond to the benefit of which he is entitled. *Hughes v. Cotton, 13 Bush 596; Lane v. Kasey. 1 Met. 410; Bottom v. Ayres, 110 S. W. 385; or action may be brought in name of Commonwealth for benefit of party injured. Com. v. Teal, 14 B. M. 29, Kv. Stat., sec. 3752.*

# (19) Fraudulent conveyance.

Action to set aside fraudulent conveyance made by a bankrupt should be in the name of assignee. *Anderson v. Anderson, 80 Ky. 638, 4 R. 579; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 84.*

# (20) Guaranty, action on.

In an action by a depositor of a bank on a guaranty to pay the depositors the amount of their deposits, the other depositors are not necessary parties. *Stedman v. Guthrie, 4 Met. 147.*

# (21) Heirs at law.

Allegation that parties to action are heirs at law of the deceased under whom they claim is not sufficient to show them to be the real parties in interest. *Dailey v. O'Brien, 96 S. W. 521, 29 R. 811.*

# (22) Insured-insurance company.

Action may be brought in name of owner of insured property against railroad company for burning it. *Illinois R. Co. v. Hicklin, 131 Ky. 624, 115 S. W. 752.*

# (23) Members of church.

Members of church who have been excommunicated can not maintain an action concerning the church property. *Shannon v. Frost, 3 B. M. 253. And see Humphrey v. Burnside, 4 Bush 215.*

# (24) Mistake by bank.

Mistake by bank in paying out too much money on check of general depositor, action to recover money should be in name of bank. *Keene v. Collier, 1 Met. 415.*

# (25) Obligation to two persons.

Neither has a separate right of action unless other has assigned to him. *Quisenberry v. Artis, 1 Duv. 30; but see Perry v. Perry, Exr., 98 Ky. 242, 17 R. 868, 32 S. W. 755.*

# (26) Obligor and payee same person.

§ 19. (1) Assignments authorized by statute, see Kentucky Statutes, sec. 474; as to what promissory notes are placed on footing of bills of exchange, see Kentucky Statutes.

# § 20 [32] Assignment pending action-practice.

If the right of the plaintiff be transferred or assigned during the pendency of the action, it may be continued in his name; or the court may allow the person to whom the transfer or assignment is made be substituted in the action, proper orders being made as to security for the costs.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 122c, 176d.

tucky Statutes, sec. 483, and "negotiable instruments" act, sec. 3720b, Ky. Stat. 11 R. 686, 12 S. W. 775; overruling Davis v. Morton, 5 Bush 160.

1. Assignment-how made. Any order, writing or act which makes an appropriation of the fund amounts to an equitable assignment. No set form of words is necessary. Frankfort Bank v. Hunter, 3 Mar. 292; Newby v. Hill, 2 Met. 530; and a chose in action may be assigned by parol. Gray v. Briscoe, 6 Bush 687; 2 Met. 530.
2. Bills and notes-defenses against. When a note payable and negotiable at an incorporated bank is discounted before maturity by such a bank, partial or total failure of consideration or fraud will not be available as a defense to it, nor will any discount or set-off be allowed against it. Kelly v. Smith, I Met. 313; Spencer v. Biggs, 2 Met. 123; but if discounted after maturity it is subject to such defenses as might have been made against original owner. Greenwell v. Haydon, 78 Ky. 332; Lester v. Givens, 8 Bush 357.
3. A note in the hands of one who has procured it to be discounted in a bank and has afterward taken it up is subject to any defense that could have been made against it before discount. Cline v. Templeton, 78 Ky. 550, 1 R. 276; and the holder of commercial paper who has received it as collateral security for an antecedent debt is not a bona fide holder. 78 Ky. 332, 1 Met. 628.
4. A promissory note payable in another state, and placed by the laws of that state on the footing of a bill of exchange, is to be treated here as a bill of exchange. Stevens v. Gregg, 89 Ky. 461.

§ 20. (1) Assignment pending suit-costs. Where a right of action is as

Title III Parties to Actions Generally.

# § 21 [88] [Fiduciary or person authorized by statute-action by.]

A personal representative, guardian, curator, committee of a person of unsound mind, trustee of an express trust, a person with whom or in whose name a contract is made for the benefit of another, a receiver appointed by a court, the assignee of a bankrupt, or a person expressly authorized by statute to do so, may bring an action without joining with him the person for whose benefit it is prosecuted. (Who is "personal representative," sec. 732-17.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 86, 106, 111b, 113, 115, 116, 117, 118, 129, 131, 141, 151d, 176d, 185, 185d; Form No. 39. See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 79.

# (1) Administrator refusing to sue heir or distributee may sue to recover property due the intestate, but the personal representative is a necessary party.

McChord v. Fisher. 13 B. M. 194. Heir may also defend if administrator refuses. Baxter v. Knox, 17 R. 489, 31 S. W. 284: nnd see MeLemore v. Sebree Coal Co.. 28 R. 25, 121 Ky. 53, 88 S. W. 1062; Miller v. Smythe, 122 Ky. 699, 92 S. W. 964, 29 R. 242.

# (2) Assignment before suit.

This section has no application where the assignment is made before the institution of the action. Stewart v. Stewart, 154 Ky. 367, 157 S. W. 706.

# (3) Assignee may be substituted as plaintiff or action may proceed in name of plaintiff for benefit of assignee.

Western Bank v. Coldeway, 94 S. W. 1, 29 R. 65; and is entitled to have all the relief that the plaintiff could get. Staples v. Shiver. 122 S. W. 826; Atkins v. Globe B. & T. Co.. 124 S. W. S79.

# (4) Compromise after notice of transfer.

After a claim in suit has been transferred to a third person not a party to the action, and the defendant notified of the transfer, he cannot compromise with the plaintiff to the prejudice of the assignee. Cantrell v. Hewlett, 2 Bush 311.

# (5) Notice of substitution.

Where the plaintiff in an action makes an assignment for the benefit of his creditors, it is not necessary to give defendant notice of the substitution of the assignee as plaintiff. Jewell v. Porter, 11 R. 162, 11 S. W. 717.

# (6) Rights of assignee.

This section confers on assignee the right to be substituted as plaintiff or to prosecute action in name of plaintiff without such substitution. Cantrell v. Hewlett, 2 Bush 311.

# (7) Revivor of judgment assigned.

Under this section and sec. 18, an answer to a motion to revive a judgment, which alleges merely an assignment by the plaintiff after the action was commenced, without a showing of prejudice to defendant, is not a defense to the motion. Brown's Admr. v. Little, 160 Ky. 765, 170 S. W. 168.

# (8) Security should be required for past and future costs.

Dougherty v. Smith, 4 Met. 279.

# (9) Assignee should bring.

Assignee should bring action in his own name.

(4a) Citizen—against public utility. A water company contracted with a city to furnish a supply of water sufficient to furnish protection to the inhabitants and the property of the city against fire. A citizen who sustained a loss resulting from failure of the water company to perform its contract has a cause of action against the water company. Tobin v. Frankfort Water Co., 158 Ky. 348. 164 S. W. 956.

(5) Commonwealth—action in name of on statutory bond. Heath v. Com., 129 Ky. 835. 113 S. W. 69.

(6) County court may sue on bond executed to county court by tax collector without joining county, as a party. Hardy v. Logan Co., 15 R. 405, 23 S. W. 661.

Executor, who by will is directed to lease a ferry, can maintain in his own name an action in equity to be quieted in the enjoyment of the franchise. City of Newport v. Taylor, 16 B. M. 700.

(8) Guardian to sue in name of ward. Guardian can not sue in his own name for personal property of his ward unlawfully detained. The suit must be in name of infant by the guardian. Anderson v. Watson, 3 Met. 510; nor can he maintain in his own name action to eject tenant of his ward after expiration of lease; suit must be in name of ward. Powell v. Gossom, 18 B. M. 179, 13 B. (8a) Landlord. One who rents land in own name may prosecute a forcible detainer. Hickman v. Fordyee, 179 Ky. 737, 201 S. W. 307.

(9) Person with whom or in whose name a contract is made for the benefit of another may bring action. Kenton Water Co. v. Glenn, 141 Ky. 529, 133 S. W. 573; Mutual Ins. Co. v. Hammond.

# § 23 [35] [Interested person may be made defendant.]

Any person may be made a defendant who claims an interest in the controversy adverse to the plaintiff, or who is a necessary party to a complete determination of the question involved in the action.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 177.

(2) Under a contract for transportation of persons or property made by several persons at same time each has separate action for breach of it. Baughman v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 94 Ky. 150, 14 R. 775, 21 S. W. 757; Southern R. W. v. Marshall, 111 Ky. 561, 23 R. 813, 64 S. W. 418.

(3) Vendor and person to whom he has assigned one of the land notes may unite as plaintiffs in an action for judgment on the notes, and to prevent waste. Foster v. Lyons, 19 R. 1906, 44 S. W. 625.

(4) Several parties injured by same cause-suit for injunction by. ( Same-damages on bond. Certain bonds to secure work done under certain street contracts and materials furnished were deposited with the comptroller of the city. City of Louisville, 165 Ky. 38, 176 S. W. 364.

(5) In action to partition land persons claiming it may be made parties defendant. Miller v. Pryse, 20 R. 1544, 49 S. W. 776; but see McIntyre v. McIntyre, 82 Ky. 502, 6 R. 559; Bacon v. Boyd, 17 R. 1276, 34 S. W. 525; and notes to sec. 499.

(6) Sureties on bond of guardian given in county court are not necessary parties to an action on the bond of same guardian given in circuit court in an action to sell land of ward. Johnson v. Chandler, 15 B. M. 534.

(7) Specific performance of contract. In an action seeking specific performance of a contract for sale of land all persons claiming adversely to plaintiff's title may be made parties and their claims adjudicated. Huber v. Johnson, 174 Ky. 697, 192 S. W. 821.

# §24 Parties to Actions Generally.

§ 24 [36] [Persons united in interest must be made parties.] Parties who are united in interest must be joined as plaintiffs or defendants; but if the consent of one who should be joined as plaintiff cannot be obtained, he may be made a defendant, the reason being stated in the petition.

1. Possession-right of as against sheriff under habere facias possessionem. Puckett v. Jameson, Sheriff, 157 Ky. 172, 162 S. W. 801.
2. Administrator of debtor. In an action to set aside a voluntary conveyance the administrator of the debtor is a necessary party. Alexander v. Quigley, 2 Duv. 399.
3. Assignee. In an action by the stockholders of a corporation that has assigned, against the officers, the assignee is a necessary party. Johnson, 10 Bush 649. Jones v.
4. Auditor of State-city council. Suit may proceed against the successor in office of the auditor by suggesting the fact on the record. Lindsey v. Auditor, 3 Bush 231. As to actions against city council, see Maddox v. Graham, 2 Met. 56; 18 B. M. 9.
5. Beneficiaries in trust deed. In an action to recover the trust estate, where the title is held as a naked trust with only limited power to convey, the beneficiaries are necessary parties. C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Bowler, 9 Bush 468; see further, Krieger v. Bissell, 80 Ky. 330, 4 R. 11; Union Trust Co. v. Brashears, 19 R. 37, 39 S. W. 44.
6. County. In action by taxpayers to prevent money raised by taxation from being illegally paid out, county is proper party. Howard v. Dep. Bank, 80 Ky. 496, 4 R. 408.
7. Court may make parties. Any person having adverse interest may be made party by the court. 15 B. M. 205; and see sec. 28 and notes thereto.
8. Distributees and heirs. Distributees cannot join in a suit against an administrator to recover balance due them on settlement. Pelly v. Bowyer, 7 Bush 513; nor can one action be maintained against guardian of four wards to recover specific amounts due by each of them, St. Joseph Society v. Wolpert, 80 Ky. 86, 3 R. 573.
9. Heirs and distributees may be permitted to be made parties, and to prosecute or defend suit by or against the administrator of their intestate if it appears from affidavit that the administrator is not acting in good faith, or that it will be advantageous to their interest. Gibson v. Higdon, 15 B. M. 205; Lusk v. Anderson, 1 Met. 426; McLemore v. The Sebree Coal Co., 28 R. 25, 121 Ky. 53, 88 S. W. 1062.
10. Grantors in deed conveying property with restrictions as to its use should all be made parties to action to restrain grantee from improper use. Klasener v. Robinson, 100 S. W. 255, 30 R. 1032.
11. Heirs may sue-if personal representative or guardian having the right refuses, but the fiduciary should be made a party. McLemore v. The Sebree Coal Co., 121 Ky. 53, 28 R. 25, 88 S. W. 1062; Miller v. Smythe, 122 Ky. 699, 92 S. W. 964, 29 R. 242.
12. Heirs of vendor, who has not conveyed land sold, are necessary parties to an action by his administrator to enforce the vendor's lien; or to an action to subject land by attachment to purchase-money. Anderson v. Sutton, 2 Duv. 480, 79 Ky. 465, 3 R. 271.
13. In action to subject land, holder of legal title is necessary party. Ogle v. Clough, 2 Duv. 145.
14. In an action against an executor for specific performance of a contract to convey land, heirs of vendor are necessary parties. Craig v. Johnson, 3 J. J. M. 572.
15. Holders of different land notes are necessary parties to an action by the holder of one of them to enforce his lien. Graham v. Chatoque Bank, 5 B. M. 45; 2 B. M. 29; and see secs. 692-694, and notes thereto.
16. Infants named in petition, but not in caption, are made parties in an action filed by their guardian to sell their realty. Revill v. Claxon, 12 Bush 558; and see Ellis v. Smith, 147 Ky. 99, 143 S. W. 776.
17. Joint owners of land may join in one action against several persons who are in possession of the land holding different parcels. Woolfolk v. Ashley, 2 Met. 288. In Sale v. Crutchfield, 8 Bush 636, a similar case, it is said plaintiffs could have been required to elect which defendant they would prosecute. See further Bryant v. Stephens, 28 R. 718, 82 S. W. 423.
18. In a suit by one joint owner against the other to have the land owned by them divided, an adverse claimant of the land cannot be made a party on his

# Title III Parties to Actions Generally

# § 25 [s7] Numerous parties—one or more may sue or defend for all.

If the question involve a common or general interest of many persons, or if the parties be numerous and it is impracticable to bring all of them before the court within a reasonable time, one or more may sue or defend for the benefit of all.

petition. McIntire v. McIntire, 82 Ky. 502, 6 R. 559; but in action for partition claimants may be made parties defendant. Miller v. Pryse, 20 R. 1544, 49 S. W. 776; Bacon v. Boyd, 17 R. 1276, 34 S. W. 525.

(18) Married woman is a necessary party to an action to enforce a mortgage lien on her husband's land. Morgan v. Wickliffe, 24 R. 1039, 70 S. W. 680.

(19) Mortgagee-duty of. It is the duty of the mortgagee in an action to enforce his lien to bring subsequent known mortgages before the court. Macey v. Fenwick, 4 B. M. 306. See further, secs. 692-694, and notes thereto.

(19a) Partners-sued as corporation. A firm not incorporated was sued as if a corporation; the partners employed counsel and defended in the partnership name, but did not deny the allegation that it was a corporation. They were held individually liable on the judgment in the action. Heavrin v. Lack. Malleable Iron Co., 153 Ky. 329, 155 S. W. 729.

(20) Practice. A person who is made defendant when he should have been plaintiff can not make this objection for first time in Court of Appeals. Pritchard v. Peace, 17 R. 662, 32 S. W. 296.

(21) School trustees have a separate cause of action against a defaulting school commissioner and his sureties. The trustees of all the districts can not unite in one action, nor can the subsequently appointed commissioner sue. Hammond v. Crawford, 9 Bush 75; and see further as to action by trustees. Wright v. Baker, 94 Ky. 343, 15 R. 109, 22 S. W. 335; Lewis v. Trustees, 5 R. 767.

(22) Surety. In proceeding by surety to have judgment he has paid assigned to him, the principal should have notice of the application. Veach v. Wickersham, 11 Bush 261.

# § 25. (1) Construction of section.

It is only where a suit involves a common or general interest of many persons that one or more may sue for all. It is not sufficient that the matters presented by the pleadings raise a question of law of common or general interest, nor is the action of the lower court in permitting one or more to sue for all conclusive. Oswald v. Morris, 92 Ky. 48, 13 R. 355, 17 S. W. 167; and see Alexander v. Gish, 88 Ky. 13, 10 R. 989, 9 S. W. 801; B. & L. R. R. v. Metcalfe, 4 Met. 199; and the persons who bring the action must have an interest in common with those for whom it is brought. Overton v. Overton, 123 Ky. 311, 96 S. W. 469, 29 R. 736. Persons who sued a gas company to recover deposits severally required of them as a condition to furnishing service did not have a common or general interest. Union Light, Heat and Power Co. v. Mulligan; Same v. Tracy, 177 Ky. 662, 197 S. W. 1081. A person who has paid usury can not sue for the benefit of himself and other persons who have paid usury on loans to which he was not a party. Thomas v. Ky. Trust & Security Co., 156 Ky. 260, 160 S. W. 1037.

# (2)

Where the parties interested are so numerous as to vexatiously prolong the suit and probably prevent a full hearing, or it is impracticable to bring all of them before the court in a reasonable time, court may permit portion to sue for all. Hendrick v. Money, 1 Bush 306; L. & O. T. P. Co. v. Ballard, 2 Met. 105; Robinson v. Robinson. 11 Bush 174; Claflin v. Gibson, 21 R. 337, 51 S. W. 439; 4 Met. 199; and where parties are numerous and an action is brought by some for benefit of all, unless others object, it will be presumed they assent to its prosecution. Flint v. Spurr, 17 B. M. 500. Trustee for bondholders, etc. See note 12 to sec. 21.

# (3)

Where a number of persons have claims of the same character against a city, one may sue for benefit of all. Gorley v. City, 23 R. 1782.

# (3a)

A number of persons brought separate suits in an inferior court against a defendant on causes of action growing out of the same transaction; the defendant was allowed to maintain an equitable action in the circuit court to enjoin the prosecution of these suits and compel the plaintiffs to assert what claims they had in the injunction suit. Ill. Cent. Ry. Co. v. Baker, 155 Ky. 512, 159 S. W. 1169.

# Parties to Actions Generally

# § 26 [38] [Contracts, bills, checks—action on against sureties, and persons severally liable.]

Persons severally liable upon the same contract, and parties to bills of exchange, to promissory notes placed upon the footing of bills of exchange, or to common orders and checks, and sureties on the same or separate instruments, may all or any of them, or the representatives of such as may have died, be included in the same action at the plaintiff's option. (Judgment against, secs. 370, 373; rchen cannot be rendered, sec. 80.)

# § 27 [30] [Actions against decedents and persons jointly bound on contract-effect of judgment against one.]

If two or more persons be jointly bound by contract, the action thereon may be brought against all or any of them, at the plaintiff's option. If any of the persons so

and concerning church property. Shannon v. Frost, 3 B. M. 253; Humphrey v. Burnside, 4 Bush 215: 8 B. M. 70; 7 B. M. 481; or action may be prosecuted or defended by trustees. Ky. Stat., sec. 321.

2. Part of heirs may contest will for benefit of all concerned when it is impracticable to bring all of them before court. Randolph v. Lampkin, 90·Ky. 551, 12 R. 517, 14 S. W. 538.
3. Practice. Trustee for bondholders may maintain action in his own name for their benefit, but in such case where the beneficiaries are not parties the court should retain control over the money collected for the benefit of those entitled thereto. B. & L. R. R. v. Metcalfe, 4 Met. 199.
4. Masonic lodge-some of members may sue for all. Payne v. McClure Lodge. 115 S. W. 764.

# § 26

1. Construction of section. Plaintiff holding two or more separate obligations against same parties may sue upon all of them in one action, or if the principal in two or more obligations is same person and the sureties in part the same, he may in one action sue on both obligations, omitting as defendants those sureties not on each paper. Com. v. Tate, 89 Ky. 608, 12 R. 9, 13 S. W. 117.
2. Guarantors. Several signers of contract of guaranty for the payment each of $100 may be sued jointly. Wilde v. Haycraft, 2 Duv. 309; Ky. Live Stock Asso. v. Miller, 27 R. 39, 84 S. W. 301.
3. Owners of property and city. The city and owners of property fronting streets improved may be sued in the same action by the contractor. City of Lou. v. Henderson, 5 Bush 515.
4. Sub-lessee and assignee. A lessee may sue jointly a sub-lessee and his assignee upon a contract of the sub-lessee to pay him rent. Trabue v. McAdams, 8 Bush 74.
5. Subscribers, several. In an action to recover on subscriptions from a number of subscribers, all may be joined as defendants. Hastings Industrial Co. v. Jones, 167 Ky. 714, 181 S. W. 364.
6. Warranty. Where several join in same deed, each warranting his undivided interest, a suit against each for his proportion of the amount due for breach of warranty is proper. Evans v. Sanders, 10 B. M. 291.

# § 27

1. Joint obligors-separate action against. The common law rule has been changed by the Code, and persons jointly liable may be sued separately.

# § 476. Death of joint obligor-liability of heir or representative.

If any person jointly bound with another in any contract or by judgment, shall die in the lifetime of such other obligor, his heir, devisee or representative may be charged in the same manner as if the contract or judgment had been separate as against the decedent. (Ky. Stat., sec. 476.)

# § 477. Obligation to dead person—proceeding by representative.

A written obligation to a person or persons who, or some of whom, happen to be dead at the time of its execution, may be proceeded on by the representative of such person or by the survivor, as if it had been executed in the lifetime of such dead person or persons. (Id., sec. 477.)

# § 28. [40, 400] [New parties-court may require-practice.]

The court may determine any controversy between parties before it, if it can do so without prejudice to others; if it cannot do so, it must require such other persons to be made parties, or must dismiss the action without prejudice. (When court may dismiss action, sec. 371-2.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 177, 802, 905, Waits v. McClure, 10 Bush 763; Gossom v. Badgett, 6 Bush 97, 1 Duv. 30. Warranty, Evans v. Sanders, 10 B. M. 291.

# (1) Appearance.

Persons required by the court to appear and assert their claims, if any, to a fund in controversy entered their appearance by filing special and general demurrers. Underwood v. Wood, 93 Ky. 177, 14 R. 129, 19 S. W. 405.

One or all of the partners may be sued by a creditor of the firm, and judgment against one or more will not be a bar to proceeding against the others. Williams v. Rogers, 14 Bush 776; Moore v. # § 29 Parties to Actions Generally.

§ 29 [41, 257] [Claimant of property may be made party-practice.] In an action or proceeding for the recovery of real or personal property, or for the subjection thereof to a demand of the plaintiff under an attachment or other lien, any person claiming a right to, or interest in, the property or its proceeds, may, before payment of the proceeds to the plaintiff, file, in the action, his verified petition, stating his claim and controverting that of the plaintiff; whereupon the court may order him to be made a defendant; and upon that being done, his petition shall be treated.

As to whether a party is necessary, the court says: "It was not proper for the court to require the plaintiff to bring the corporation before it. This was plaintiff's duty, as in the absence of the corporation no relief could be granted." Shawhan v. Zinn, 79 Ky. 300, 3 R. 12.

(5) The court should order all persons interested in the controversy to be brought before it, although they have agreed not to sue until the happening of a certain event. Lou. v. Henderson, 5 Bush 515. See Com. v. Frankfort, 13 Bush 185. Gibson v. Higdon, 15 B. M. 209. And if, upon hearing the evidence, the court should believe that a party mentioned in the pleadings has such an interest as to render him a necessary party, he should be brought in. Vanbuskirk v. Levy, 3 Met. 134; Board of Education v. Allin, 134 Ky. 763, 121 S. W. 676.

(11) Payee not necessary party to an action by his assignee to enforce lien. Leacock v. Hall, 13 B. M. 211.

§ 29. (1) Claimant who presents his petition to be made a party, and is considered by the court and parties as a party, and the action proceeds as if he was, will be so considered, although actually never made a party. Schwein v. Sims, 2 Met. 209. In Taylor v. Taylor, 3 Bush 118, it is held that a claimant need not be made a party to the original suit; with its litigation he has nothing to do. The issue to be tried as to him is the validity of the claim he presents.

(7) When petition omits necessary parties, the chancellor ought, before final hearing, notify the plaintiff and give him reasonable time to supply defect. Ogle v. Clough, 2 Duv. 145.

(8) Defect of parties-how objected to. An objection to a pleading because of defect of parties can be made by demurrer; secs. 92-4, or by pleading, sec. 118; and, if not made in one of these ways, is waived, secs. 92, 118; Waits v. McClure, 10 Bush 763, and cannot be made for first time in Court of Appeals. Hardee v. Hall, 12 Bush 327; McAlister v. Savings Bank, 80 Ky. 684, 4 R. 682; 10 Bush 23; 3 Bush 200.

(8a) Dismissal without prejudice. Absolute dismissal for failure to bring in necessary parties is erroneous. Puckett v. Jameson, Sheriff, 157 Ky. 172, 162 S. W. 801.

# § 4. Widow or child of person killed-action by.

The widow and minor child, or either or both of them, of a person killed by the careless, wanton or malicious use of fire-arms, or by any weapon popularly known as Colt's brass knuckles, or slung-shots, or other deadly weapon, or sand-bag or any imitation or substitute therefor, not in self-defense, may have an action against the person who committed the killing, and all others aiding or promoting, or any one or more of them; and in such actions the jury may give vindictive damages. (Ky. Stat., sec. 4.)

# § 5. Widow or child of person killed in duel-action by.

The widow and minor child of a person killed in a duel, or either of them, may have an action against the surviving principal, the seconds and all others aiding or promoting the duel, or against any one or more of them for reparation of the injury; and in such action the jury may give punitive damages. The failure to include any of the persons mentioned as defendants in the action shall discharge them from liability. (Id., sec. 5.)

Scott v. Wilson, 6. R. 308; and see McIntire v. McIntire, 82 Ky. 502, 6 R. 559.

(5) After final judgment it is too late for a stranger to the action to file a petition to be made a party. Meadows v. Goff, 90 Ky. 510, 12 R. 495. 14 S. W. 535.

(6) To authorize an intervening petition there must be a claim of such a nature that the rights of the intervenor will be directly affected by the judgment that might be rendered between the original parties. Vanmeter v. Fidelity Trust Co., 107 Ky. 108, 21 R. 744, 53 S. W. 10.

§ 6. Death resulting from negligence - who must sue. Title IIIj] Parties to Certain Actions. [§ 30-31]

# CHAPTER II.

# PARTIES TO CERTAIN ACTIONS.

# 30. Claimant of property may be made party-practice.

# 31. Preceding section applicable to officer sued.

# 32. Substitution of plaintiff in writ for officer sued.

# 33. Property distrained-action to recover.

# 30 [42] [Claimant of property may be made party-practice.]

Upon affidavit of a defendant before answer, in an action upon contract, or for the recovery of personal property, that a person who is not a party to the action, without collusion with him, makes a claim to the subject of the action, and that the affiant is ready to pay or dispose thereof as the court may direct, the court may make an order for the safekeeping of the subject of the action, or for its payment or deposit in court, or for its delivery to such person as the court may direct, and an order requiring such alleged claimant to appear in a reasonable time and maintain or relinquish his claim against the defendant, and, in the meantime, stay the proceeding. If such alleged claimant, being served with a copy of the order, fail to appear, the court may declare him barred of all claim in respect to the subject of the action against the defendant therein. If he appear he shall be allowed to make himself defendant in the action, in lieu of the original defendant, who shall be discharged from all liability to either of the other parties in respect to the subject of the action upon his compliance with the order of the court for the payment, deposit, or delivery thereof. (Provisions concerning deposit in court, sees. 303, 304.)

# 31 [48] [Preceding section applicable to officer sued to recover personal property taken by him.]

The provisions of section 30 shall be applicable to an action against a sheriff or other officer for the recovery of personal property taken by him under execution or distress warrant, or for the proceeds of such property so taken and sold by him. And the defendant in such action shall be entitled to the benefit of those provisions against the party in whose favor the execution or distress warrant issued, upon exhibiting to the court the process under which he acted, with his party the court should, where the pleadings justify it, submit the question of ownership to a jury, and order the money paid over according to the verdict. Davis v. Watkins, 2 Bush 224. See 3 J. J. M. Starling v. Brown, 7 Bush 164. 64; 5 J. J. M. 235; 3 J. J. M. 672; 3 Bibb 302; 7 Dana 411, for decisions upon questions of interpleader before the Code.

Parties to Certain Actions. [Title III]

# § 32 [44] [Substitution of plaintiff in writ for officer sued-security for costs.]

In an action against a sheriff or other officer for the recovery of property taken under an execution or distress warrant the court may, upon the application of the defendant and of the party in whose favor the execution or distress warrant is issued, permit the latter to be substituted as the defendant, security for the costs being given.

# § 33 [45] [Property distrained—action to recover.]

An action to recover the possession of specific personal property taken under a distress warrant, if it be brought by the tenant, or his assignee or undertenant, may be against the party who sued out the warrant; and the property claimed in such action may, under the order for its delivery, be taken from the officer who seized it if he have no other claim to hold it than that derived from the warrant. The indorsement of a levy on the property, made upon the warrant by the officer holding it, shall be a sufficient taking of the property to sustain the action against the party who sued out the warrant.

(Horv order of delivery obtained, sec. 181.)

# 32. (1) Costs-security for.

If one defendant be substituted for another, and security for costs not given at the time, it will be considered waived, unless party was taken by surprise. *Gunn v. Gudchus, 15 B. M. 447.*

# (2) Substitution of creditor for officer

does not exonerate officer from liability to a claimant when the officer failed to take proper bond of indemnity. *Curry v. Ball, 22 R. 592. 58 S. W. 534.*

# § 33. (I)

A tenant may sue to recover specific personal property wrongfully taken under a distress warrant. *Board v. Luigart, 150 Ky. 791, 151 S. W. 9.*

34. Married women-actions by and against.

1. When may sue and be sued.
2. Husband to be joined—when.
3. Defense of actions by.
4. Desertion by husband.
5. Marriage—effect of.
6. Practice if of unsound mind or imprisoned.

# § 34 [40 to 52] [Married women—actions by and against.]

1. [Life may sue alone.] [A married woman may sue, and be sued, as a single woman.*]

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 148.

# §34.

(1) Construction of section. The common law rule is in force except in the cases mentioned in this section. and wife can not sue alone in any case not embraced by it. A next friend can not prosecute a suit in the name of the infant wife against the wishes of her infant husband. Anderson v. Anderson, 11 Bush 327, but see Ky. Stat., sec. 2128.

(2) The only object of this section (49, same as sec. 34-1 of this Code) is to dispense with the necessity for a next friend where the action concerns the separate property of the wife, or is between herself and husband. It confers no new right. Matson v. Matson. 4 Met. 262, and see Ky. Stat., sec. 2128, conferring on wife power to sue and be sued alone in all cases.

(3) Judgment against husband for debt or tort of wife made before marriage, see Clark v. Miller, 88 Ky. 108, 10 S. W. 277, 10 R. 691; Medley v. Tandy, 85 Ky. 566, 9 R. 168, 4 S. W. 308; Fultz v. Fox, 9 B. M. 499; Beaumont v. Miller, 1 Met. 68; Agnew v. Williams, 1 Bush 4; 6 Bush 34; 7 Bush 214; and see Ky. Stat. sec. 2130, fixing liability of husband.

For tort of wife, see Curd v. Dodd, 6 Bush 43.

# § 34] Persons Under Disability.

[Title III]

*Subsection 1 is a substitute for subsections 1 and 2 of Section 34 of the Code. It is a part of the "Weissinger Act" (March 15, 1894, c. 76, p. 176, scc. 33) and, being inconsistent with the old provisions, the latter are omitted. Said subsections (as amended June 10, 1892, c. 55, p. 85, as shown in brackets) were as follows:

"1. In actions between husband and wife; in actions concerning her separate property; and, in actions concerning her general property [and in actions for the personal suffering of, or injury to her person or character] in which he refuses to unite, she may sue or be sued alone.

"2. In all other actions by or against a wife she and her husband must join or be joined as plaintiffs or defendants."-E.d.

2. [Wife and husband must join.] [Obsolete. See * note above.—Ed.]

3. [When wife may defend.] She may defend an action against her and her husband for herself, and for him also if he fail to defend.

4. [Deserted wife.] If a husband desert his wife, she may bring or defend for him any action which he might bring or defend, and shall have the powers and rights with reference thereto which he would have had but for such desertion.

5. [Marriage of female party.] If a female party to an action marry, her husband may be made a party by a motion, causing the fact to be stated upon the record; and the action shall not be delayed by reason of the marriage.

6. [Wife of unsound mind or imprisoned.] But if a wife be of unsound mind, or imprisoned, the actions mentioned in subsections 1, 3 and 4, of this section must be prosecuted or defended by her committee or curator, if she have one, and if she have none, must be prosecuted by her next friend, or defended by her guardian ad litem. (As to service of notice, sec. 629; see Ky. Stat., sec. 2128, giving married women right to sue and be sued.)
