Title III Persons Under Disability.
# ARTICLE 2. OTHER PERSONS UNDER DISABILITY.

# § 35. Action-by whom brought.

§ 35 [58, 58] [Action-by whom brought.] Excepting married women-

1. [Action by person under disability.] The action of a person who is under disability must be brought by his guardian, curator, or committee, if he have one residing in this State, unless it be against his guardian, curator, or committee; or unless said guardian, curator, or committee refuse to sue, in which case he must be made a defendant.
2. [Adult prisoner.] The action of an adult, who is imprisoned in or out of this State, and who has no curator residing herein, or his action against his curator, may be brought by him, or, with his consent, by his next friend.

# 35. (1) Action in name of infant.

Actions should be prosecuted in the name of the infant by the guardian or next friend, Illinois C. R. R. v. Head, 119 Ky. 809, 27 R. 270, 84 S. W. 751; 12 Bush 215; Anderson v. Watson, 3 Met. 509; Woodram v. C. & K. R. R., 18 R. 945; but a guardian may sue in his own name in some cases, as upon a note taken payable to himself. McChord v. Fisher, 13 B. M. 194; 3 Met. 510, or in action against former guardian. Mahan v. Steele, 109 Ky. 31, 22 R. 546, and see Ellis v. Smith, 147 Ky. 99; M. H. & E. R. R. v. Wiar, I44 Ky. 206. 138 S. W. 255. The fact that in an action of an infant, by next friend, the petition calls the next friend the plaintiff where it is evident that the intention is only to call him next friend, will not vitiate the proceedings; advantage may be taken of it by special demurrer. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Case, 158 Kv. 594, 165 S. W. 968.

# (2) Appeal should be prosecuted in name of infant by guardian.

Ramsey v. Keith, 25 R. 1302. 77 S. W. 693.

# (3) Guardian of infant must prosecute and defend actions for him, unless he declines, and in such case the infant may appear by his next friend, making guardian defendant.

Miller v. Cabell, 81 Kv. 178. 4 R. N62; Tabor v. Tabor, 98 Ky. 173, 17 R. 739, 32 S. W. 414; McMakin v. Stratton, 82 Ky. 226, 6 R. 154; or the court may appoint a guardian ad litem without affidavit therefor if guardian fails to make defense. Gardner v. Letcher. 16 R. 778, 29 S. W. 868; and the court has no power except for good cause to displace the guardian and permit next friend or guardian ad litem to represent infant. Walker v. Smyser, 80 Ky. 620, 4 R. 662; 81 Ky. 178, 4 R. 962.

# (4) Guardian and next friend.

The fact that an infant sues both by his guardian and next friend may be grounds for special demurrer, but is not a reversible error unless it appear that the rights of the infant have been prejudiced. Henning v. Barringer, 10 R. 674, 10 S. W. 136; and see Taylor v. Cline, 18 R. 9, 35 S. W. 109.

# (5) Infants-actions by.

Agreement of infant plaintiff to dismiss action must be pleaded, and can not be made available by motion. Hopkins v. Virgin, 11 Bush 677.

# (6) Poor infant and insolvent next friend will be permitted to prosecute an action without securing costs, as provided in sec. 619.

Westerfield v. Wilson, 12 Bush 125; and see sec. 37-2.

# (7) Infant seeking to avoid contract on ground of fraud can not avail himself of his infancy unless he pleads it.

Bryant v. Pottinger. 6 Bush 473.

# (8) Next friend of infant female can not prosecute an action in her name against wishes of her infant husband.

Anderson v. Anderson, 11 Bush 327;

Persons Under Disability

# 3. Resident infant or person of unsound mind.

The action of an infant, or of a person of unsound mind, who resides in this State, and who has no guardian, curator, or committee residing herein, or whose guardian, curator, or committee refuses to sue, or his action against his guardian, curator, or committee, may be brought by his next friend.

# 4. Non-resident infant or person of unsound mind.

The action of an infant, or of a person of unsound mind, who resides in a foreign country, and who has a guardian, curator, or committee residing therein, may be brought by such guardian, curator, or committee, or by his next friend.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 109, 109a, 111b, 112; Forms No. 144, 148.

Where an infant plaintiff intermarries with an infant husband it is proper to enter him as co-plaintiff and allow action to proceed in name of her next friend as next friend of both. *Hopkins v. Virgin, 11 Bush 677.*

(9) Infant married woman can prosecute in her own name an action against her husband for alimony or divorce. *Snedager v. Kincaid, 22 R. 1347, 60 S. W. 522.*

(10) Next friend cannot commute debts or judgments due infant. *Forbes v. Brents, 1 J. J. M. 441; and next friend may be changed by leave of court. Burks v. Shain, 2 Bibb 341.*

(11) Non-resident guardian of non-resident infants may bring suit for sale of land for indivisibility under Code, sec. 490, sub-sec. 2, if thereto authorized by the county court of the county in which the land lies. The failure to file before judgment a copy of the order of appointment or of the order authorizing the foreign guardian to act may be cured by filing such copies after a sale and exceptions thereto. *Baker v. Weaks, 178 Ky. 515, 199 S. W. 53. A foreign guardian may sue under our Code for his infant. Miller v. Cabell, 81 Ky. 178, 4 R. 962. But a non-resident guardian has no power to sue for the infant or otherwise act as guardian in this State, unless authorized by a county court of this State, as provided by sec. 2041, Ky. Stats. Watts v. Wilson, 93 Ky. 495, 14 R. 463, 20 S. W. 505; and see Williams v. Duncan, 92 Ky. 125, 13 R. 389, 17 S. W. 330; Shelby v. Harrison, 84 Ky. 144, 8 R. 83. A dismissal as to the guardian is a dismissal of the proceeding. Miller v. Cabell, 81 Ky. 178, 4 R. 962.*

(12) "Person of unsound mind," as used in this section, embraces not only lunatics but persons whose minds have become so impaired by disease or other cause as to be unable to take care of their own interests. *Howard v. Howard, 87 Ky. 616, 10 R. 478, 9 S. W. 411; and see further as to persons of unsound mind, Shaw v. Dixon, 6 Bush 644, Ky. Stat., sec. 2149, and Barron v. City of Lex., 105 S. W. 395, 32 R. 92.*

(13) If a person alleged to be of unsound mind appears and protests that he is not incapable of managing his estate and that suit is prosecuted without his authority, the chancellor should impanel a jury to pass upon the question of his capacity. *Howard v. Howard, 87 Ky. 616, 10 R. 478, 9 S. W. 411.*

(14) The trustee of a lunatic having sold his land and appropriated the proceeds, a creditor of the lunatic sued his sureties to recover the money. A successor of the trustee, having subsequently brought suit to have sale set aside as fraudulent, it was held that he and not creditor had right to choose remedy and maintain action. *Trunk v. Eastern Asylum, 6 R. 521.*

(15) Where in an action in behalf of an infant lunatic brought by his mother as next friend, the defendant pleaded that the lunatic had a committee residing in this State, the refusal of the trial court to abate the action is not prejudicial error where during the pendency of the action the lunatic dies and the mother is thereafter permitted to file an amended petition and prosecute the action in her name as heir of the infant lunatic. *Skidmore v. Harris, 157 Ky. 756, 164 S. W. 98.*

Title III] Persons Under Disability. [§ 36
# § 36 [55, 60, 63] [Defense by-judgment—guardian ad litem.]

Excepting married women, if a defendant, who is under disability and who is summoned in this State, have a defense—

1. [Infant or person of unsound mind.] That of an infant or person of unsound mind must be made by his guardian or committee, or by a guardian ad litem.
2. [Adult prisoner.] That of an adult prisoner in this or any other State must be made by his curator or by an attorney employed by him, or by a guardian ad litem.
3. [Defense for before judgment against-practice.] No judgment shall be rendered against an infant-other than a feme covert-nor against a person of unsound mind, who is summoned in this State, until the regular guardian, or committee, or guardian ad litem of such defendant shall have made defense, or have filed a report, stating that, after a careful examination of the case, he is unable to make defense. His report, that he cannot make a defense, must be filed on or before the day for making defense, unless, for cause shown, the court extend the time; and failure to file such report, as required hereby, or by the court, shall be punished as a contempt. (When defense must be filed, sec. 102; judgment, before filed, vacated, secs. 517, 519; time allowed infant to vacate, sec. 391; appointment of guardian ad litem, sec. 38.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 441, 443, 444, 446, 450, 453a; Form No. 475.

# §36. (1 Clerical misprision.

Where no defense is made for infants or report of guardian ad litem filed, the judgment against them, as provided in sec. 517, is a clerical misprision, and the error cannot be considered on appeal until motion is made in lower court to correct it, as directed in sec. 516. Morrison v. Beckham, 96 Ky. 72, 16 R. 294, 27 S. W. 868; Palmer v. Husbands, 119 S. W. 762, 134 Ky. 152; Reynolds v. Steel, 170 Ky. 153, 185 S. W. 820, and notes 7-9.

# (2 Guardian ad litem-duty of court.

Action should not be dismissed because plaintiff neglects to have guardian ad litem appointed for the infant defendants. It is the duty of the court to appoint one whether the plaintiff does or does not apply, and where an action is submitted before appointment of guardian ad litem court should set aside submission and appoint. C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Bowler, 9 Bush 468.

# (3 Guardian ad litem should be appointed in bastardy proceeding.

Chandler v. Com., 4 Met. 66.

# (4 Answer of guardian ad litem sufficiently complies with Code,

although statement "that after careful examination of case he is unable to make defense" is not found in the body of answer, but in jurat thereto. Vissman v. Bryant, 14 R. 874, 21 S. W. 759; Gardner v. Letcher, 16 R. 778, 29 S. W. 868; and see Womble v. Trice, 112 Ky. 533, 23 R. 1939, 66 S. W. 370; as to duty and answer of guardian ad litem.

# (5) Guardian should conduct defense for his wards,

although a guardian ad litem may have been appointed, unless there is some valid reason for denying him the right. Walker v. Smyser, 80 Ky. 620, 4 R. 662; Miller v. Cabell, 81 Ky. 178, 4 R. 962; but the court may appoint a guardian ad litem to defend, although there be a guardian. McMakin v. Stratton, 82 Ky. 226, 6 R. 154; Adams v. De Dominques, 112 S. W. 663, 129 Ky. 599; and see Tabor v. Tabor, 98 Ky. 173, 17 R. 739, 32 S. W. 414; Gardner v. Letcher, 16 R. 778, 29 S. W. 868.

(Infant-appeal by-bar. Infant after becoming twenty-one prosecuted an appeal to reverse judgment rendered

# § 36 Persons Under Disability

during infancy; the judgment was affirmed. As to any matter which was or could have been heard on the appeal, the judgment of affirmance is a bar to petition filed to vacate judgment appealed from. *Speak v. Mattingly, 4 Bush 310, and see Maddox v. Williams*, 87 Ky. 147, 9 R. 975, 7 S. W. 907, and notes Sec. 391.

(12) Infant who has arrived at age may answer for himself, although his guardian ad litem had answered for him while an infant. *Mitchell v. Berry, 1 Met. 602; and an infant whose action is prosecuted by a next friend may, upon arriving at age, be substituted as plaintiff by a suggestion on the record. Clements v. Ramsey*, 9 R. 172.

(3) Non-resident infant. Judgment against infant defendant constructively summoned without appointment of guardian ad litem, and before defense, is not void. *Simmons v. McKay, 5 Bush 25; Myers v. Pedigro, 24 R. 1923, 72 S. W. 734; Oliver v. Park, 101 Ky. '1, 19 R. 179, 39 S. W. 423; Ramsey v. Keith, 25 R. 582, 77 S. W. 693; and see important case of Powell v. Baer*, 143 Ky. 282, 136 S. W. 629.

(14) Persons of unsound mind-who are, see *Howard v. Howard, 87 Ky. 616, 10 R. 478, 9 S. W. 411; and Ky. Stat., secs. 2149-2156, and notes. In an action to sell land of a decedent for payment of his debts (Code, sec. 428), a lunatic who had no committee was a defendant. Process was served by delivering a copy of the summons to him and to the person having charge of him. Before judgment a committee was appointed, but no service was made on the committee, nor did he appear. A judgment directing the sale of land was void as to the lunatic. Barry v. Fains, Admr.*, 172 Ky. 30S, 189 S. W. 220.

(15) Practice. Consent order that infants be made parties does not dispense with service of process upon them, or authorize judgment against them without defense by a guardian. *Pond v. Doneghy*, 18 B. M. 558.

(16) Presumption. Where the record is lost, but it appeared that an answer was filed for infants who were very young, the presumption is that it was filed by their guardian. *Johnson v. McDyer*, 11 R. 28, 9 S. W. 778.

(17) Counter-claim against infant-process. No process was necessary on a counter-claim against infant plaintiffs; but a defense or report should have been made for them. *Whalen v. Hopper's Gdn.*, 152 Ky. 727, 154 S. W. 40.

(18) Infant becoming of age pendente lite. When an infant defendant becomes of age during the pendency of the suit the facts should be suggested of record. *Gray v. Grimm*, 157 Ky. 603, 163 S. W. 762.

Title III] Persons Under Disability. [§ 37-38

# ARTICLE 3.

# GENERAL PROVISIONS CONCERNING PERSONS UNDER DISABILITY.

# § 37. Next friend, qualifications of-liability for costs.

# 1 [53, 58] [Qualifications of next friend.]

No person shall sue as next friend unless he reside in this State and be free from disability, nor unless he file his own affidavit showing his right to sue as next friend according to the provisions of this chapter.

# 2 [54, 50] [Liability for costs.]

A guardian, curator, committee; or next friend who brings or prosecutes an action for a person who is under disability is liable for the costs which accrue during his conduct of the action, unless he be allowed to sue in forma pauperis, by an order of the court, or by an order of the judge thereof during vacation. (Meaning of "disability," sec. 732-31; zehen required to secure costs, sec. 619; liability for costs, see Ky. Stat.. secs. 892 and 893.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 109a, 109c, 111b.

# § 38. [Guardian ad litem-appointment-duties -compensation.]

# 1. [When may be appointed.]

No appointment of a guardian ad litem

# 37. (1 Affidavit-next friend.

Person suing as next friend must, when required, file affidavit and give bond for costs. Spicer v. Holbrook, 23 R. 1812, 66 S. W. 180, but it is too late after answer filed to make motion to dismiss for want of affidavit, and an affidavit tendered when motion is made should be received. Staton v. Bryant, 5 R. 426, and see Henning v. Barringer, 10 R. 674, 10 S. W. 136; Campbell v. Dreher, 110 S. W. 353. Failure to file an affidavit required by this section can be reached only by special demurrer, and is waived by general demurrer. Maiden v. Stewart, 163 Ky. 551, 174 S. W. 5.

# (2) Next friend.

An action instituted by the next friend was prosecuted to judgment by him after the arrival of infant at age, and the affidavit required by this section was not made. It was held too late after judgment to make objection. Bramel v. Cunningham, 3 R. 512. Judgment in favor of infant by next friend, although action was prosecuted by guardian, is valid. Tabor v. Cline, 18 R. 9. Objection that next friend is a non-resident can not be made by general demurrer. Stevenson v. Stevenson, 7 R. 680; the proper practice is to raise question by rule or motion. Hall v. Snipes, 10 R. 435, 9 S. W. 388, and see Henning v. Barringer, 10 R. 674, 10 S. W. 136.

# (3) Poor infant and insolvent next friend will be permitted to prosecute an action without securing costs.

Westerfield v. Wilson, 12 Bush 125; an infant who is unable to procure a next friend may institute an action in his own name and the court may appoint a next friend for him and allow him to sue forma pauperis. Richardson v. Hunt, 5 R. 931, and the person who brings the action for the infant is only liable for costs to the extent of assets in his hands. Doty v. Doty, 26 R. 63, 80 S. W. 803.

# 4 Next friend may employ attorneys and make contract for a reasonable fee.

Sanders v. Woodbury, 146 Ky. 153, 142 S. W. 207.

# § 38. (1) Affidavit-appointment.

It was held in McMakin v. Stratton, 82 Ky. 226, 6 R. 154; and Gardner v. Letcher, 16 R. 778, 29 S. W. 868, not to be a reversible error to appoint a guardian ad

# Title Ⅲ

shall be made until the defendant is summoned, or until a person is summoned for him, as is authorized by section 52; nor until an affidavit of the plaintiff, or of his attorney, be filed in court; or with the clerk, or presented to the judge during vacation, showing that the defendant has no guardian, curator, nor committee, residing in this State, known to the affiant.

# 1. Who may be and how appointed—removal of.

A guardian ad litem must be a regular, practicing attorney of the court; and may be appointed by the court, or the judge thereof, whether a guardian, curator, or committee appear for the defendant or not: or by the clerk of the court, during vacation, if the affidavit mentioned in subsection 1 of this section be filed. The guardian ad litem may be appointed upon the motion of the plaintiff or of any friend of the defendant; but neither the plaintiff nor his attorney shall be appointed, nor be permitted to suggest the name of the proposed guardian ad litem; and the court or judge may change the guardian so appointed whenever the interest of the infant may appear to require such change.

# 2. To prepare case and procure evidence.

It shall be the duty of the guardian ad litem to attend properly to the preparation of the case; and in an ordinary action he may cause as many witnesses to be subpoenaed as he may think proper, subject to the control of the court; and in an equitable action he may take depositions, not, however, exceeding three, without leave of the court.

# § 39 [65] Action—how commenced.

An action is commenced by filing, in the office of the clerk of the proper court, a petition stating the plaintiff's cause of action; or, in cases wherein written pleadings are not required, by filing in such court the account, or the written contract, or a short written statement of the facts on which the action is founded; and, in either case, by causing a summons to be issued, or a warning order to be.

# § 39. (1) Summons must issue.

The filing of a petition or amended petition and directing of the clerk to issue summons is not the commencement of an action if no summons is actually issued.

Casey v. Newport Rolling Mill' Co., 156 Ky. 623, 161 S. W. 528, explaining L. & N. R. R. v. Smith. 87. Ky. 50i. 10 R. 514. 9.S. W. 493; and see L. & N. R. R. v. Bowen, 18 R. 1099, 39 S. W. 31, holding that limitation ceased to run when the petition was filed and summons ordered, although clerk made a mistake in name of plaintiff, but if clerk fails to file suit because tax is not paid the delivery of petition to clerk does not stop the statute. L. & N. R. R. v. Hall, 115 Ky. 567, 24 R. 2487, 74 S. W. 280.

# (2) Judgment without service is void.

# § 2524. Action commenced when summons issues.

An action shall be deemed to have been commenced at the date of the first summons or process issued in good faith from the court or tribunal having jurisdiction of the cause of action. (Ky. Stat., sec. 2524.)

The summons shall command the officer to whom it is directed to summon the defendant named therein to answer the petition filed by the plaintiff, giving his name, at a time stated therein, under the penalty of the petition being taken for confessed, or of the defendant being proceeded against for contempt of court on his failure to do so. Title IV] Manner of Commencing An Action. [§ 41-46

# Circuit Courts Having Continuous Sessions.

# § 1003. Defendant allowed twenty days to answer.

The time fixed in the summons for the defendant to answer shall be twenty days after the service thereof, if in the county where such courts sit, and thirty days if elsewhere in the State. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1003.)

# § 41 [67] [Summons to any county—costs.]

A summons shall be issued at any time, to any county, against any defendant, at the plaintiff's request. But a summons not served shall not be taxed in the costs unless otherwise ordered by the court.

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 334-336.

# § 42 [68] [Copy for each defendant.]

With every summons the clerk shall issue a copy thereof for each defendant named therein, unless otherwise ordered by the plaintiff.

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 334-336.

# § 43 [6a] [Time fixed in for return.]

The time fixed in the summons for the defendant to answer shall be the day the summons is returnable. (When answer must be filed, sec. 102.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 334-336.

# § 44 [70] [Return day of summons.]

The summons shall be returnable to the first day of the next term of the court, which does not begin within ten days from the date of the summons. (See practice act, sec. 367a.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 334-336; 335-338.

# § 45 [71] [Delivery to officer.]

The summons and copies shall be delivered to the sheriff or other officer authorized to execute it. (Sheriff to indorse time of reception, sec. 674; officers authorized to execute, secs. 47, 667.)

# § 46 [72] [Sheriff to attend clerk's office daily.]

It shall be the duty of the sheriff, or of one of his deputies, to attend at the clerk's office daily - Sundays excepted - to receive any process that may be issued, and the clerk shall deliver to him any process remaining in his office.

# § 45. Officers who may execute.

Plaintiff has a right to have summons directed to and executed by any officer authorized to execute, at his option.

Boaz v. Nail, 2 Met. 245; and summons directed to sheriff or other officer may be executed by constable or any officer to whom it might have been directed.

1. [What officer may serve.] By the officer to whom it is directed, or any officer to whom it might have been directed, and his return thereon shall be proof of the time and manner of service.

§ 47. (1) Recital as to service in summons out of the county in which he resides and is appointed. Lillard v. Brannin, 91 Ky. 511, 13 R. 74. 16 S. W. R. 862, 29 S. W. 327; Long v. Montgomery, 6 Bush 394; but is entitled to considerable weight. Lenming v. Mullins, 6 R. 523; Francis v. Lilly, 98 S. W. 996, 30 R. 393, 124 Ky. 230.

(2) Special bailiff. Parol authority is not sufficient to authorize a special bailiff to execute a summons, and written authority after service will not render the service valid. Lillard v. Brannin, 91 Ky. 80, 12 R. 664, 16 S. W. 349; and see sec. 667 and notes thereto.

(3) There must be a substantially strict compliance with the statute in returns made by a special bailiff; the presumptions in favor of the return of an officer do not exist; the affidavit of the special bailiff must be signed by him or it will not be sufficient. Simms v. Simins, 88 Ky. 642, 11 R. 131, 11 S. W. 665; but return showing how and when summons was executed is sufficient. Barbour v. Newkirk, 83 Ky. 529, 7 R. 555.

(4) Special bailiff cannot execute a summons "not found," new authority in writing is necessary to enable him to execute an alias summons. Lillard v. Brannin, 91 Ky. 80, 12 R. 664, 16 S. W. 349.

(5) When a special bailiff returns a summons "not found," new authority in writing is necessary to enable him to execute an alias summons. Lillard v. Brannin, 91 Ky. 80, 12 R. 664, 16 S. W. 349.

(6) Deputy sheriff can appoint special bailiff to execute summons, but appointment should be in name of principal by deputy. Norman v. Norman, 6 Bush 495.

(7) Presumption in favor of an officer's return does not arise when return is made by a special bailiff. Lloyd v. MeCaulcy, 14 B. M. 535; and see Barbour v. Newkirk, 83 Ky. 529, 7 R. 555; Webber v. Webber, 1 Met. 18.

(8) Decretal recital. Herman v. Martin, 107 Ky. 642, 21 R. 1396, 55 S. W. 429; Cox v. Fowler, 33 R. 928, 111 S. W. 56.

# § 4562

Special bailiff may be appointed to execute process. A sheriff may, by writing, empower any person to execute an original or mesne process. The person so appointed shall endorse his action on the instrument empowering him, and shall make affidavit to the truth of the endorsement, and attach the same to the process and deliver the endorsement and affidavit to the sheriff or his deputy, to be returned to the proper office. The endorsement, made as above directed, shall have the same validity and effect in law as if made by the sheriff. (Ky. Stat., sec. 4562.)

(1) False return. Where a summons has been returned as executed, when in fact it was not, and judgment rendered thereon, the return is conclusive, if the plaintiff acted in good faith, and cannot be impeached in proceeding to set aside judgment. 1. Amendment of return. Sheriff may amend his return by leave of court, even after his term of office expires. Newton v. Prather, 1 Duv. 100; L., H. & St. Louis R. R. v. Com., 104 Kv. 35, 20 R. 371, 40 S. W. 207; and after judgment has been rendered. Thompson v. Moore, 91 Ky. 80, 12 R. 664, 15 S. W. 6; Tyler v. Jewell, 10 R. 887, 11 S. W. 25; and see Russell v. Durham, 16 R. 516, 29 S. W. 16; Ramey v. Francis, Day & Co., 169 Kv. 469. 184 S. W. 380.
2. Answer waives service. Filing answer waives right to object to want of legal service. Elliott Co. v. Kitchen, 14 Bush 259; and see further as to waiver, note 9, sec. 39.
3. Clerk's entry of return on docket when summons is lost is entitled to more weight than recollection of defendant and sheriff. Leming v. Mullins, 6 R. 523. 78 Ky. 6; Berry v. Foster, 22 R. 745.
4. False return. See note 1, sec. 48.

Title IV Service of Summons-Actual Service

# § 50 [7e] [Acknowledgment of service.]

Service may be acknowledged by the person to be summoned by an indorsement upon the summons, signed and dated by him, and attested by a witness. The affidavit of the witness shall be proof of the service. (Form of affidavit, Form 115.)

# § 51 [77-s0] [County - city -- corporation - carrier.]

1. [Action against county.] In action against a county the summons must be served on the presiding judge of the county court, or, if he be absent from the county, upon its attorney.

(5) Quashing return. See cases cited in note 15, sec. 48. Special bailiff-service by. See notes to sec. 47. Sufficiency 6-14, sec. 48.

# § 50.

1. Acceptance of service. Under this section and sec. 52, it will be presumed that a recital, in the record of an action to sell land of an estate, "accepted by the rest of the defendants," is the record of a valid acceptance of service by the mother of infants in interest for them. Harrod v. Harrod, 167 Ky. 308. 180 S. W. 997.
2. Service of summons against a lunatic accepted by the superintendent of the asylum must be accompanied by the required affidavit of a witness to the attestation to be valid, but where the superintendent thereafter, in behalf of the asylum, filed his verified claim in the suit against the estate of the lunatic, this appearance of the superintendent furnished sufficient evidence of the genuineness of the acknowledgment and dispensed with the proof of it required by this section. Both the return on the summons and the fact showing the appearance of the superintendent are parts of the record and should be considered together in determining the sufficiency of the service and the genuineness of the acknowledgment. Allen v. Jenkins, 157 Ky. 406, 163 S. W. 234.
3. In an action brought pursuant to sec. 73 against a common carrier, summons may be served in the county where action is brought upon the defendant's chief officer or agent who resides therein. Adams Ex. Co. v. Crenshaw, 78 Ky. 136; as to service on general ticket or passenger agent, see C. & O. R. R. v. Cowherd, 96 Ky. 113, 16 R. 373, 27 S. W. 990; as to service upon station agent, see L., H. & St. L. R. R. v. Com., 104 Ky. 35, 20 R. 371, 46 S. W. 207.
4. Designation of agent to serve process on. The Ky. Stat., sec. 571, providing that corporations shall designate agents upon whom summons may be executed does not annul the manner of serving under the Code. New South Brewing Co. v. Price, 21 R. 11, 50 S. W. 963; Cumberland Co. v. Lewis, 32 R. 1300, 108 S. W. 347.

# § 51.

1. Adams Express Company- process upon it may be served as if it was a foreign corporation. Adams Ex. Co. v. Schofield, 111 Ky. 832, 23 R. 1120, 64 S. W. 903.
2. Amendment inserted as sub-section 5 is constitutional. M. & B. S. R. R. v. Ball, 108 Ky. 241, 21 R. 1693, 56 S. W. 188.
3. Common carrier. As to service of summons on connecting carrier in action against on contract, see N., C. & St. L. R. v. Carrico, 95 Ky. 489, 16 R. 66, 26 S. 59.

# § 51] Service of Summons

# Actual Service. [Title IV]

1. [Municipal corporation.] In an action against a municipal corporation the summons must be served upon its mayor or the chairman of its board of trustees; or, if he be absent from the county, upon the defendant's official attorney, if it have one; or, if it have no such attorney, upon its marshal.
2. [Private corporation or railroad.] In an action against a private corporation the summons may be served, in any county, upon the defendant's chief officer, or agent, who may be found in this State; or it may be served in the county wherein the action is brought, upon the defendant's chief officer or agent who may be found therein; [or if the defendant operate a railroad, it may be served upon the defendant's passenger or freight agent stationed at or nearest to the county seat of the county in which the action is brought.] (IV words in brackets added by Act of March 13, 1893, c. 161, p. 588. Service of process on insurance company. See Ky. Stat., sec. 631. Service on agent of corporation, see Ky. Stat., sec. 571.)
3. [Common carrier or railroad.] [In every action] against a common carrier, the summons may be served, in any county, upon the defendant's chief officer or agent; or it may be served, in the county wherein the action is brought, upon the defendant's chief officer or agent who resides therein; or, if the defendant operate a railroad, it may be served upon defendant's passenger or freight agent stationed at or nearest to the county seat of the county in which the action is brought. (Section as amended by an Act of June 28, 1893, c. 237, p. 1207.)
4. [Lessee or person operating railroad appearance, how entered.] Where the defendant corporation is the owner or the lessee of a railway in this State, or the builder or constructor of a railway in this State, and cannot be served with summons under existing laws, then the person or corporation controlling or operating the railway so owned or built or constructed shall be treated as the representative of the defendant, and service of summons upon such of the officers or agents of.

# §52 [81] [Infant under fourteen.]

1. Parties to be served. If the defendant be under the age of fourteen years the summons must be served on his father, or, if he have no father, on his guardian; or, if he have no guardian, on his mother; or, if he have no mother, on the person having charge of him.

Unincorporated association. A voluntary unincorporated association cannot be sued in the name of the corporation; the point is waived unless raised by special demurrer or by answer where the facts do not appear upon the face of the petition. United Mine Work. of Amer. v. Cromer, 159 Ky. 605, 167 S. W. 891.

# § 52.

1. Clerk to appoint guardian ad litem when required affidavit is filed, or when petition states necessary facts and is verified. Tyler v. Jewell, 10 R. 887, 11 S. W. 25; and although affidavit is defective if guardian ad litem makes defense the judgment will not be void. Walch v. Davis, 17 R. 634, 32 S. W. 281; and see Robinson v. Clark, 17 R. 1401, 34 S. W. 1083; Mullins v. Mullins, 27 R. J048, 87 S. W. 764; and notes to sec. 38.
2. Clerk has no authority to appoint a guardian ad litem for an infant over fourteen, until he has been summoned. Womble v. Trice, 112 Ky. 533, 23 R. 1939, 66 S. W. 370.
3. Delivery of summons for all the infants named therein to the person authorized to be served with process for them is sufficient. Cheatham v. Whitman, 86 Ky. 614, 9 R. 761, 6 S. W. 597; Donaldson v. Stone, 11 R. 27, 11 S. W. 462; Rodgers v. Rodgers, 17 R. 358, 31 S. W. 139.
4. Guardian ad litem-service on. Cornell v. Cornell, 115 S. W. 795, 131 Ky. 650. An action was brought under Code, sec. 489, sub-sec. 3. against infant under fourteen years of age, whose mother was dead, by a statutory guardian, who had charge of the infant. The father of the infant was a non-resident of Kentucky. Service on a guardian ad litem appointed by the clerk of the court was a sufficient service on the infant. Brown v. Allen's Gdn., 175 Ky. 570, 194 S. W. 801.
5. Infant over fourteen must be served with process. Taylor v. Taylor's Gdn., 149 Ky. 707, 149 S. W. 1000. May be served upon father, if a defendant, although his interest is antagonistic. Cox v. Interstate Coal Co., 157 Ky. 373, 163 S. W. 231. Service on petition for revivor after decease of mortgagor following sale of land for the lien on infant under fourteen and opportunity of guardian ad litem being void passes no title to purchaser. Brown v. Brown, 157 Ky. 804, 164 S. W. 70. If an infant under fourteen years of age be served by delivery to her of the summons and not as provided in this section, and if the record of the action shows untruly that she was then over fourteen years of age, the judgment cannot be collaterally attacked. Relief may be had under Code, sec. 518. Fraize v. Walls, 130 Ky. 168, 202 S. W. 310.
6. Mother-service on when sufficient. Palmer v. Husbands, 119 S. W. 762, 134 Ky. 152; Betty v. Petrie, 138 Ky. 426, 128 S. W. 320. Under this section and sec. 50, it will be presumed that a recital, in the record of an action to sell land of an estate, "accepted by the rest of the defendants," is the record of a valid acceptance of service by the mother of infants in interest for them. Harrod v. Harrod, 167 Ky. 308, 180 S. W. 997.
7. Non-resident infant-constructive service sufficient. Powell v. Baer, 143 Ky. 282, 136 S. W. 629; Terry v. Cornett, 136 Ky. 629, 124 S. W. 870.
8. Person in charge of infant, although under twenty-one, may be served with summons. Lawrence v. Conner, 12 R. 86, 14 S. W. 77.
9. Return of officer. In action against an infant whose father was dead, the officer's return, showing that summons was executed upon infant and his mother, is sufficient, nothing appearing in the record to show he had a guardian. It will be presumed officer did his duty. Webber v. Webber, 1 Met. 18; Rodgers v. Rodgers, 17 R. 358, 31 S. W. 139; and see Cheatham v. Whitman, 86 Ky. 614, 9 R. 761, 6 S. W. 595; Bailey v. Fanning, 12 R. 644, 14 S. W. 908; Donaldson v. Stone, 11 R. 27, 11 S. W. 462.
10. Return of special bailiff, showing that he executed process upon infant and his mother, without showing that father or guardian could not be found, is not

# § 53 [82]

[Person of unsound mind.] If the defendant be of unsound mind the summons must be served on him and on one of the following named persons, if residing in the county, viz.: on his committee; or, if he have no committee, on his father; or, if he have no father, on his guardian; or, if he have no guardian, on his wife; or, if he have no wife, on the person having charge of him; or, if the defendant be a married woman of unsound mind, and her husband be plaintiff in the action, the summons must be served on her and her committee; or, if her husband be not plaintiff in the action, upon her and her committee, if she have one; or, if she have no committee, upon her and her husband: Provided, That the summons shall not be served upon a person of unsound mind, if he be under charge or treatment of a physician who certifies that, in his opinion, the service would be injurious to such person. # § 54 [83] [Prisoner—service on.]

If the defendant be a prisoner a copy of the petition must accompany the summons, and the service must be upon the keeper of the penitentiary, who shall deliver the copies of the petition and summons to the prisoner. And a copy of the summons must also be delivered to the curator of the defendant, if he have one; or, if he have no curator, to his wife, if he have one; or, if he have no wife, it shall be left at the place, if known, where he resided, or claimed to reside, prior to his confinement, with some person of the age of sixteen years.

(Venue of action against, sec. 69.)

# § 55 [84] [Shakers—service on.]

If the defendant be a community of Shakers, holding property in common, the service must be made by posting a copy of the summons at the door of its meeting house, and by delivering a copy thereof to some member of the community.

# § 57 [88 to 90] [Constructive service--warning order.]

If the defendant be—

1. [Non-resident corporation.] A corporation having no agent in this State, known to the plaintiff, upon whom a summons can be lawfully served; or,
2. [Non-resident person.] A non-resident of this State and believed to be absent therefrom; or,
3. [Person who has left State to defraud.] Have departed therefrom with intent to delay or defraud his creditors; or,
4. [Absence from State four months.] Have been absent therefrom four months; or,
5. [Leaving county to avoid service.] Have left the county of his residence to avoid the service of a summons; or,

§ 57. (1) Courts in continuous session. Warning order, see Appendix. Divorce proceedings. Resident wife may sue non-resident husband for divorce. In such case no bond is necessary. Rhyms v. Rhyms, 7 Bush 316.

(3 It was held under Code of 1854 that court granting divorce on constructive service might award a new trial within time allowed, although party obtaining divorce should marry. Meyar v. Meyar, 3 Met. 297. But see sec. 414 of this Code.

(4) Husband can not proceed against his wife as a non-resident when she is absent from the State in obedience to his will, or is confined by him in an asylum or other place, with no power to return or respond to the warning order. Newcomb v. Newcomb, 13 Bush 544.

(5) Infant under fourteen may be brought before the court by constructive process. Powell v. Baer, 143 Ky. 282, 136 S. W. 629.

(6) Judgment sustaining attachment against non-resident defendant who does not appear, without warning order against him, is erroneous. Allen v. Brown, 4 Met. 342.

(7) Seven years' absence from State. Practice when it is not known whether person is living or dead. Hogue v. Yeager, 107 Ky. 582, 21 R. 1299, 54 S. W. 961.

(8) Strict compliance with Code necessary. In all proceedings upon constructive service, the provisions of the Code regulating the same must be literally followed. Nothing short of a substantial compliance with every requisite will give court jurisdiction. Brownfield v. Dyer, 7 Bush 505. See Jackson v. Speed, 2 Duv. 426; Grigsby v. Barr, 14 Bush 330; Clark v. Raison, 104 S. W. 342, 31 R. 907, 126 Ky. 486.

(9) Summons and warning order not necessary. In proceeding against defendant by constructive service, the warning order is commencement of action. Hoffman v. Brungs, 83 Ky. 400.

(10) Warning order-practice. Judgment rendered upon warning order citing.

Payne v. Hardesty, 12 R. 336, 14 S. W. 348; Herd v. Cist, 14 R. 644, 20 S. W. 1035; 7 Bush 505; see further, notes sec. 59.

(11) Warning order is in the nature of a rule and is not a process in the meaning of the Code or that section of Constitution providing that the style of all process shall be "the Commonwealth of Kentucky." Northern Bank v. Hunt, 93 Ky. 67, 14 R. 1, 19 S. W. 3.

(1) The court may direct the clerk to enter a warning order in the order book, and when it is made it is as effectual as if made by the clerk in vacation. Northern Bank v. Hunt, 93 Ky. 67, 14 R. 1, 19 S. W. 3; and see Hackworth v. Harlan, 14 R. 76, 19 S. W. 172.

of section 57, except upon an affidavit of the plaintiff; or of his agent or attorney, if he be absent from the county; or of his guardian, curator, committee, or next friend, if he be under disability; or of the attorney of either of them, if absent from the county, stating the ground of the application for such order.

1. What affidavit shall state. Nor shall the clerk make such order on any of the grounds mentioned in subsections 1, 2, and 4, of section 57, unless the affidavit also state in what country the defendant, or, if the defendant be a corporation, in what country its chief officer or agent resides or may be found, and the name of the place wherein a post-office is kept nearest to the place where the defendant or its chief officer or agent resides or may be found; or unless the affidavit state the affiant's ignorance of such of those facts as he does not know.
2. Affidavit of agent or attorney. Nor shall the clerk make such order upon an affidavit of an agent or attorney of the plaintiff, stating any of the grounds mentioned in subsections 1, 2, 4, and 7, of section 57, unless the affidavit state the affiant's belief that the plaintiff is ignorant of such of the facts as are unknown to the affiant.
3. Affidavit or return on summons necessary. The clerk may make such order upon an affidavit of the plaintiff, or of his agent or attorney if he be absent from the county; or of his guardian, curator, committee, or next friend, if he be under disability; or upon the return of a summons by the proper officer, stating either of the grounds mentioned in subsections 5 and 6, of section 57.

# § 59

1. What affidavit of agent or attorney must state. But the clerk shall not make such order upon the affidavit of an agent or attorney unless it also state the affiant's connection with the plaintiff, and the fact that he is absent from the county.
2. Affidavit—when evidence. An affidavit made pursuant to the foregoing provisions of this section, unless it be controverted by the defendant's affidavit, shall be sufficient evidence of the facts therein stated for the support of the action as well as of the warning order. # § 59.

(1) Appearance-appeal enters. Prosecution of appeal by non-resident enters appearance. Gill v. Johnson, 1 Met. 649; Salter v. Dunn, 1 Bush 311; 4 Met. 341.

# § 59 Service of Summons Constructive Service

# 1. Duty of attorney and report by.

Such attorney must make diligent efforts to inform the defendant, by mail, concerning the pendency and nature of the action against him, and must report to the court, during the first term, which does not commence within sixty days after his appointment, the result of his efforts.

# 2. Powers of attorney limited.

The duties and powers of such attorney are governed by the last foregoing paragraph unless he be employed by the defendant; or unless he be unable to inform the defendant concerning the action; or unless he learn that the defendant is under disability, other than coverture, or other than infancy and coverture combined.

# 3. Employment of attorney—effect of.

Such attorney, if employed by the defendant, shall so report to the court; and such report shall be treated as an appearance of the defendant in the action.

# 4. Affirmative defense—when attorney to make.

If such attorney cannot inform the defendant concerning the action, or if he learn that the defendant is under disability, other than coverture, or other than infancy and coverture combined, he shall so report to the court, and shall make an affirmative defense if he can; or, if he cannot make such defense, he shall so report to the court, and shall be subject, with reference to such report, to the provisions of subsection 3 of section 36; and no act of his shall be treated as an appearance of such defendant.

# 5. Person constructively summoned

who appears for first time by appealing from judgment against him will only be held to have appeared in lower court on day mandate is entered, and no judgment can be rendered against him at that term. Bealzey v. Maret, 1 Bush 466.

# 6. An attorney for the plaintiff

should not in any case be allowed to enter the appearance of the defendant. Ball v. Poor, 81 Ky. 26, 4 R. 746; and see notes to sec. 410.

# 7. Answer enters appearance.

Non-resident who by his attorney files an answer sworn to him in another State must be regarded as personally before the court, although he disclaims in an answer any intention of entering his appearance. Tipton v. Wright, 7 Bush 448.

# 8. Attorney employed by non-resident

should so state in his report, and when he fails to do this it will not be presumed that he has been employed. Christie v. Garrity, 14 R. 910, 22 S. W. 158.

# 9. No allowance should be made to attorney

for non-resident appointed by the court when he is acting under contract with the non-resident and looking to him for compensation. Cabell v. Cabell, 1 Met. 319.

# 10. No allowance should be made to an attorney

appointed to defend for non-resident when no report is filed by him or duty performed. Southwood v. Myers, 3 Bush 681; Jackson v. McElroy, 2 Bush 132.

# 11. Attorney must state in his report

that he has carefully examined the case and is unable to make defense unless as authorized by sub-sec. 7. he can make defense. Dineen v. Hall, 112 Ky. 273, 23 R. 1615, 65 S. W. 445; Ramsey v. Keith, 25 R. 582, 76 S. W. 142.

# 12. Failure to appoint-effect of.

The court says: "And, although the failure

# § 61 [02] [Actual service on defendant-effect.]

The plaintiff may, at any time before judgment, have a summons served on the defendant if found in this State, although a warning order may have been previously entered against him; and after such service the case shall proceed as in other cases of actual service. (See secs. 415, 416.)

# (3) Trial-when action stands for.

If warning order made more than sixty days before term commences, equity action does not stand for trial at that term, unless pleadings are made up ninety days [now sixty] before commencement of term, as provided by secs. 91, 137, 393 of Code (same as secs. 60, 102, 364 of this Code). These sections must be construed together. Harris v. Adains, 2 Duv. 141.

# (4) Land sold for indivisibility.

In an action to sell land for indivisibility no evidence was introduced, although one defendant was constructively summoned. A sale under the judgment in the action was valid. Huber v. Johnson, 174 Ky. 697, 192 S. W. 821.

# § 62 [03] [Real property—concerning.]

Actions must be brought in the county in which the subject of the action, or some part thereof, is situated—

(1) Attachment-Land may be levied on and sold under an attachment in other county than one in which action is pending in which attachment is obtained. Nixon v. Jack, 16 B. M. 174. See also Webb v. Wright, 2 Bush 126; Dawkins v. Hough, 112 Ky. 855, 23 R. 1997, 66 S. W. 1047, and sec. 201.

(2a) Decedent's estate. An action brought by devisees of a decedent against the other devisees for sale of land devised under the will and division of the proceeds must be brought in the county where the personal representative qualified. Boering v. Melcon, 159 Ky. 14, 166 S. W. 612.

(2) Enforcement of contract concerning land where it does not involve a sale of the land to satisfy a lien is transitory. Henderson v. Perkins, 94 Ky. 207, 14 R. 782, 21 S. W. 1035; and see Bullitt v. Eastern Land Co., 99 Ky. 324, 18 R. 230, 36 S. W. 16; Todd v. Laneaster, 104 Ky. 427, 20 R. 623, 47 S. W. 336; Collins v. Park, 93 Ky. 6, 13 R. 905, 18 S. W. 1013.

(3) Infant's land-jurisdiction to sell. Circuit court of one county has no jurisdiction to sell land of infant in another county on petition of guardian of infant, although land descended to infant from his father, who died intestate in county where suit brought, and his administrator qualified in that county. Montgomery v. Montgomery, 2 Bush 49; Goldsmith v. Hieatt, 28 R. 741, 90 S. W. 259; unless the action comes under secs. 65 or 66. See those sections and notes thereto.

# § 62 County in Which An Action Must or May Be Brought.

# 1. Recovery of real property.

For the recovery of real property, or of an estate or interest therein.

# 2. Partition of real property.

For the partition of real property except as is provided in section 66.

# Injury to land.

Action for injury to land-as cutting trees-must be brought in county in which land is located. *Meehan v. Edwards*, 92 Ky. 574, 13 R. 803, 18 S. W. 454.

(5) Injury to property in another state caused by acts of wrongdoer in this state. Action may be brought in either State. *Smith v. Southern Ry. Co.*, 123 S. W. 678, 136 Ky. 162.

(6) Injunction bond-action on is transitory. *Smith v. Miler*, 140 Ky. 308, 131 S. W. 5.

(7) Insolvent estate. In action to settle, court may order sale of land situated in another county. *Fishback v. Green, 87 Ky. 107, 9 R. 959, 7 S. W. 881; Mechanics' Trust Co. v. Cobb, 14 R. 444, 20 S. W. 391; but see now sec. 65 as amended and Reddick v. U. S. B. & L.*, 106 Ky. 94, 20 R. 1720, 49 S. W. 1075.

(8) Jurisdiction in two counties-action may be brought in either. *Galloway v. Craig, 29 R. 1, 92 S. W. 320, 122 Ky. 447; and see Cent. Ky. Nat. Gas. Co. v. Stevens*, 134 Ky. 306, 120 S. W. 282.

(9) Land in two counties adjoining. Action to recover may be brought in either county. *Harlan v. Howard, 79 Ky. 373, 2 R. 368; and so where a mortgage lien is sought to be enforced. Hendrick v. Nesbitt*, 96 Ky. 652, 16 R. 746, 29 S. W. 627.

(10) Land-to enforce lien on. Circuit court of one county has no jurisdiction to enforce lien on land in another county. *Webb v. Wright, 1 Bush 107; Stofer v. U. S. Building & Loan Ass'n's Assignee, 106 Ky. 94, 20 R. 1720, 49 S. W. 1075; Shields v. Yellman, 100 Ky. 655, 18 R. 1092, 39 S. W. 30; unless the proceeding in rem attaches as an incidental remedy, 2 Bush 126. See Doty v. Deposit B. & L., 103 Ky. 710, 20 R. 625, 46 S. W. 219; Shields v. Yellman*, 100 Ky. 655, 18 R. 1092, 39 S. W. 30.

(10a) An action to enforce a lien on minerals severed from the land is transitory; an action to enforce a lien on minerals for purchase-money due and payable before severance must be brought in the county in which the land lies. *Kennedy v. Hicks*, 180 Ky. 562, 203 S. W. 318.

(11) Person or creditor having an interest in thing sought to be subjected may object to jurisdiction. *Emmons v. L. & C. Mining Co.*, 112 Ky. 91, 23 R. 1445, 65 S. W. 593.

(12) Land-injury to by act of common carrier should be brought in county where land lies. *Gillen v. Illinois R. Co.*, 125 S. W. 1047, 137 Ky. 375.

(13) Land-action to recover or subject must be brought in county where land lies, although an attachment may have been levied upon it that issued in suit pending in another county. *Bramlett v. Couch*, 105 S. W. 460, 32 R. 311.

(14) Land-jurisdiction-when court has jurisdiction for one purpose it may take it for all incidental purposes. *Morton Iron Works v. Moreland*, 113 S. W. 481.

(15) "Lien or other incumbrance"—these words in sub-sec. 3 refer to liens or incumbrances created by contract or judgment. *Hatton v. Rogers*, 121 S. W. 698, 134 Ky. 840.

(16) Mortgage—action to enforce against heirs of mortgagor may be brought in county where land is located. *Galloway v. Craig*, 29 R. 1, 92 S. W. 320, 122 Ky. 417.

(17) Oil and gas-action to recover royalty due for is not local to county where land is situated. *Central Ky. Nat. Gas Co. v. Stevens*, 120 S. W. 282, 134 Ky. 306.

(18) Rescission of contract concerning land. Action for is transitory and may be brought in county other than that in which land is situated. *Thompson v. Elmore, 13 R. 692, 18 S. W. 235; but if it is also sought to enforce a lien on the land the action must be brought in county where land is located; Bullitt v. Eastern Ky. Land Co., 99 Ky. 324, 18 R. 230, 36 S. W. 16; and if defendant in action for rescission is a non-resident action, must be filed in county where land is located. Todd v. Lancaster*, 104 Ky. 427, 20 R. 623, 47 S. W. 336.

(19) Sale of indivisible land of decedent for distribution between heirs-action should be brought in county where land is located. *Perkins v. MeCarley, 79 Ky. 43, 16 R. 801, 29 S. W. 867; Goldsmith v. Hieatt*, 28 R. 741, 90 S. W. 259.

(20) Sale of land held jointly. Separate action may be brought for in county where land lies, although an action...

1. Sale of real property. For the sale of real property under title 10, chapter 14, or under a mortgage, lien, or other encumbrance of charge, except for debts of a decedent. (See further secs. 65, 66.)
2. Injury to real property. For an injury to real property. (Definition of "real property," sec. 732.)

# § 63 [94] [Fine or forfeiture to recover—officer against.]

Actions must be brought in the county where the cause of action, or some part thereof, arose.

1. Fine, penalty, or forfeiture to recover. For the recovery of a fine, penalty, or forfeiture, imposed by a statute; but if the offense for which the claim is made be committed on a water-course or road which is the boundary of two counties, the action may be brought in either of them.

Specific execution of contract concerning land and to enforce lien thereon, action for must be brought in county where land is located, and in such action a personal judgment for the purchase-money may be rendered, although the only defendant is summoned in another county. Collins v. Park, 93 Ky. 6, 13 R. 905, 18 S. W. 1013; Henderson v. Perkins, 94 Ky. 207, 14 R. 782, 21 S. W. 1035; and see Todd v. Lancaster, 104 Ky. 427, 20 R. 623, 47 S. W. 336. Petition failing to show situs is specially demurrable. Bingham v. Asher, 165 Ky. 53, 176 S. W. 343.
Title to quiet. Party having legal title and possession of land may institute suit against party claiming it, to quiet his title, in county where land lies. Landrum v. Farmer, 7 Bush 46.
Title to perfect. Action to perfect title to land is transitory, and comes under section 78. Page v. McKee, 3 Bush 135.
Transitory. Action for use and occupation is. Swart v. Reveal, 16 R. 503, 29 S. W. 24, and so is action to enforce a lien on standing trees that are to be cut. Tilford v. Dotson, 106 Ky. 755, 21 R. 333, 51 S. W. 583.
Trespass to real property may be waived and action brought to recover value of property taken. Dennis Bros. v. Strunk, 108 S. W. 957, 32 R. 1231; office. Com. v. Remington Typewriter.

# 2. [Public officer-ugainst.]

Against a public officer for an act done by him in virtue or under color of his office, or for a neglect of official duty. (In respect to executions, see Ky. Stat., sec. 1715.)

# 3. [Action on official bond.]

Upon the official bond of a public officer.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 29, 546b; Form No. 42.

# § 64 [05] [Wills—concerning.]

A proceeding to establish, or an action to set aside, a will must be brought in the county in which the will, if valid, ought, according to law, to be recorded. (County will must be recorded in, Ky. Stat., sec. 4849.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 26, 546b.

# § 65 [06] [Deceased person or assigned estate.]

An action to settle the estate of a deceased person, of a person, corporation or company assigned for the benefit of creditors of any estate in the hands of a receiver of court must be brought in the county in which such personal representative, assignee or receiver qualified, and for the purpose of a settlement of such estates, such personal representative, assignee or receiver, shall have the same power to sue as had the deceased person, the assignor, or owner.

Co., 105 S. W. 399, 127 Ky. 177, 32 R. 189.

# (5) Sheriff--failing to return or collect execution.

Action on sheriff's bond for failing to return execution from another county, or failing to pay over money collected on execution, must be brought in county from whence execution issued, Ingram v. Turner, 21 R. 283. 51 S. W. 148; Adams v. Simmons, 23 R. 1389, 65 S. W. 152.

# (6) United States marshal is public officer in meaning of this section.

Layne v. Sharp, 105 S. W. 373, 32 R. 38.

# (7) Waiver of right to object to jurisdiction.

See Gillen v. Illinois Cent. R. Co., 125 S. W. 1047, 137 Ky. 375.

# §64. Will—to set aside.

Petition seeking division of land will not confer jurisdiction to set aside will unless court had jurisdiction independent of suit for division. Hughey v. Sidwell, 18 B. M. 259. See 1 Bush 515; 17 B. M. 632; as to when will may be set aside, see Ky. Stat., sec. 4861, and Abbott v. Traylor, 11 Bush 335.

# §65.

# (1) Action for sale of land and distribution of proceeds as directed by will must be brought in county where will is probated.

Flint v. Spurr, 17 B. M. 499; and see notes to sec. 66; and see Boering v. Meleon, 159 Ky. 14, 166 S. W. 612.

# (2) Corporation.

Action to settle insolvent must be brought in county in which assignee qualifies. Louisville B. & L. Asso. v. Smith, 90 S. W. 1080, 28 R. 950. Receiver may sue members of insolvent mutual insurance company in county of court appointing receiver. White v. Harbeson, 169 Ky. 224, 183 S. W. 475. Same, as against subscribers on unpaid stock subscriptions to insurance company. Lock v. Stout, 173 Ky. 304, 191 S. W. 90.

# ( Personal representative.

Action against to recover money wrongfully paid out must be brought in county where he qualified. Dinning v. Conn, 124 Ky. 623, 09 S. W. 914, 30 R. 855.

# ( Settlement of estate-parties.

In action to settle decedent's estate only the persons named in sec. 428 are necessary parties, and debtors of the estate or persons liable to it are not proper or necessary parties, and the court by service of process in another county can not acquire jurisdiction over them. Citizens' Bank v. Boswell, 93 Ky. 92, 14 R. 17, 19 S. W. 174.

# (4a) A suit by an administrator of a deceased guardian to settle his estate was held properly brought in the county where the administrator qualified and in which the deceased guardian qualified, although one of the defendants had its

Title V]  County in Which An Action Must or May Be Brought. [§ 66

of the estate in such receiver's hands, but any action brought by such personal representative, assignee or receiver, for the recovery of or sale of under a mortgage, deed of trust or other lien, or charges upon, or injury to real estate or an estate or interest therein, must be brought in the county in which such real estate is situated, and not elsewhere, and that in any suit heretofore brought and which is now pending in any county in this State, for the settlement of the estate of a deceased person, or of a person, corporation or company assigned for the benefit of creditors, and in which the enforcement of mortgaged liens, or other liens, upon real estate, or for the recovery of real estate or an interest therein, the judge of said court in which such action is pending, shall by proper orders transfer, for hearing and trial, that part of the record necessary for the enforcement of such liens or the recovery of such real estate to the circuit court of the county in which such land or a part thereof is situated, and such shall be docketed and stand for trial in the court to which same is transferred under this act, as other causes of like nature, as if originally brought in such court. Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 27, 27a, 27d; Form No. 142.

# § 66 [07] [Distribution, partition, sale of decedent's estate.]

An action for the distribution of the estate of a deceased person, or for its partition among his heirs, or for the sale, for payment of his debts, of property domicile in another county. Taylor v. Harris' Admr., 164 Ky. 654, 176 S. W. 168.

# (5) Settlement of estate-sale of land.

In action to settle estate of a decedent land in county other than one in which action is pending may be sold, Fishback v. Green, 87 Ky. 107, 9 R. 959, 7 S. W. 81; De Haven v. De Haven, 104 Ky. 41, 20 R. 663, 46 S. W. 215; but note that section has been amended since these decisions, and see Reddick v. U. S. B. & L. Ass'n, 106 Ky. 94, 20 R. 1720, 49 S. W. 1075; and Galloway v. Craig, 122 Ky. 447, 92 S. W. 320, 29 R. 1; Boering v. Melcon, 159 Ky. 14, 166 S. W. 612.

# (6) Set-off.

In an action on a note the defendant cannot plead as a set-off a claim that would involve the settlement of an estate upon which administration was granted in another county. Hurst v. Hamilton, 19 R. 1753, 44 S. W. 432; Bennett v. McCrocklin, 3 Met. 322.

# § 66.

# (1) Jurisdiction.

In an action for the sale of a decedent's estate for the purpose of reinvestment as to the infants and a division of the proceeds as to the adults, the court of the county in which the decedent died had jurisdiction to order the sale of real property in other counties. Phalan v. L. S. V. & T. Co., 88 Ky. 24, 10 R. 663, 10 S. W. 10; Goldsmith v. Hieatt, 90 S. W. 259, 28 R. 741.

# (2) Action against administrator and heirs.

to subject land descended to the payment of decedent's debts must be brought in county where administrator qualified, and so must an action by ward against administrator of his guardian and his heirs to subject land descended. Willis v. Roberts, 90 Ky. 122, 11 R. 929, 13 S. W. 358.

# (3) Action by administrator and heirs.

for sale of land and division of proceeds must be brought in county where representative qualified and land located in another county may be sold. Walker v. Yowell, 94 Ky. 205, 14 R. 829, 21 S. W. 873; DeHaven v. DeHaven, 104 Ky. 41, 20 R. 603, 46 S. W. 215; Boering v. Melcon, 159 Ky. 14, 166 S. W. 612.

# (4) Jurisdiction to settle an estate.

confers jurisdiction to adjudge all incidental questions and issues that may.

# § 67 [08] [Ward against guardian.]

An action by a ward against his guardian for a settlement of his accounts, for additional security, or for his removal, must be brought in the county in which the guardian was qualified.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 28, 546b; Forms Nos. 41, 144.

# § 68 [00] [Sinking Fund Commissioners or Board of Education.]

An action against the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund, or the Board of Education, of this State, must be brought in the county that includes the seat of government.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 2Oc, 29b, 546b.

(5) Parties to action. In action under this section the only necessary parties are those mentioned in sec. 428, debtors of the estate or persons liable to it are not necessary or proper parties. Citizens' Bank v. Boswell, 93 Ky. 92, 14 R. 17. 19 S. W. 174.

(6) Partition of land. Suit should be brought in county where administration was granted for partition of lands of an intestate, but it not appearing that administration was granted, suit was properly brought in county where he died. Driskell v. Hanks, 18 B. M. S55.

(7) Personal representative. Action against to recover money wrongfully paid. Dinning v. Conn, 124 Ky. 623, 99 S. W. 914, 30 R. 855.

(8) Real property owned jointly. Action to sell under sec. 490 must be brought in county where land is located. Perkins v. McCarley, 97 Kv. 43, 16 R. 801, 29 S. W. 867; and see Flint v. Spur, 17 B. M. 499.

(9) Set-off by distributee against administrator. Distributee of estate sued by administrator for debt due latter in his own right can not plead as set-off amount due him by administrator on the settlement of the estate, in a county other than that in which he qualified. This section applies to set-off Bennett v. McCrocklin, 3 Met. 322; and see Hurst v. Hamilton, 19 R. 1753, 44 S W. 432.

(10) Venue in more than one county action may be brought in either. Galloway v. Craig, 92 S. W. 320, 29 R. 1. 122 Ky. 447.

(11) Waiver of right to object to jurisdiction. See Gillen v. Illinois Cent. R. Co., 125 S. W. 1047, 137 Ky. 375.

67. (1) Jurisdiction. Trustee of an express trust who desires to settle his accounts must bring action to do so in county where instrument creating trust is recorded. Cunningham v. Frazie, 85 Ky. 35, 8 R. 638.

(2) Action by administrator of ward against guardian must be brought in county where guardian qualified. Stone v. Powell, 13 B. M. 342.

(3) Action by ward against guardian for balance due on settlement must be brought in county where guardian qualified. Greenly v. Daniels, 6 Bush 41; and so as to action to surcharge settlement of guardian, and in such action deed to land in another county may be cancelled. Dawkins v. Hough, 112 Ky. 855, 23 R. 1997., 66 S. W. 1047.

(4) Action by ward against administrator and heirs of his guardian to subject land descended to them must be brought in county where administrator qualified. Willis v. Roberts, 90 Ky. 122, 11 R. 929, 13 S. W. 358.

(5) The guardian of a certain infant died. His administrator qualified in the county in which he had qualified as guardian. A suit by the administrator and by a new statutory guardian of the infant who was appointed in that county was brought in that county for the purpose of settling his estate and recovering.

# Title V County in Which An Action Must or May Be Brought. [§ 69-71]

# § 69 [100] [Prisoner or inmate of asylum.]

An action, not mentioned in sections 62 to 67, both inclusive, nor in section 70, against a prisoner in this State, or a person confined in an asylum for persons of unsound mind in this State, must be brought in the county, if known, in which he resided, or claimed his residence, when confined. (Service of summons on, secs. 53-54.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 32, 546b.

# § 70 [474] [Upon return of "no property found."]

An action upon a return of no property found, pursuant to section 439, must be brought in the county in which the judgment is rendered, or in which the defendant resides or is summoned.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 25, 42, 546b; Form No. 148.

# § 71 [101] [Bank or insurance company.]

Excepting the actions mentioned in sections 62 to 66, both inclusive, and in sections 70 and 75, an action against an incorporated bank or insurance company may be brought in the county in which its principal office or place of business is situated; or, if it arise out of a transaction with an agent of such corporation, it may be brought in the county in which such transaction took place. (Service of summons on, sec. 51.)

# § 69. Prisoner-act of 1856.

Action under act of 1856 to have conveyance made by prisoner operate as assignment for benefit of his creditors may be brought in the county where the person to whom property is transferred is summoned, and where part of the property is situated. McAlister v. Savings Bank, 80 Ky. 684, 4 R. 682.

# § 70.

(1) Change made by this Code. The words "county in which the judgment is rendered" are used in this Code in place of the words "in the court from which the execution issued," found in Code of 1854.

(Jurisdiction. Action against railroad company, if not brought in court whence execution issued, must be in county where its president or chief officer resides, or its principal office or place of business is. McDormant v. L. C. & L. R. R. Co., 1 Bush 386.

(3) Action upon return of no property may be brought in county in which defendant resides. Parsons v. Spencer, 83 Ky. 305, 7 R. 329; Martin v. Byrd, 19 R. 1030, 42 S. W. 1112. See further, notes to sec. 439.

(4) Action may be brought in circuit court to subject personalty upon a return of no property on an execution from a justice's court. Austin v. Payne, 7 Bush 480. But not land. Weatherford v. Myers. 2 Duv. 91: 7 Bush 480. See notes to sec. 439.

(5) In action brought under this section the debtor of a judgment debtor who resides in another county may be brought before the court. Parks v. Jellico Coal Co., 124 S. W. 868, 136 Ky. 622.

# § 71.

(1) Insurance company. Under this section it was held that an action against an insurance company might be brought in Bourbon County, although its charter granted in 1839 required all suits against it to be filed in Jefferson County Circuit Court. Howard v. K. & L. Ins. Co., 13 B. M. 281.

(2) Action against may be brought in county in which the contract of insurance or settlement with agent was made, and process may be served upon chief officer in another county or insurance commissioner. Ky. Mut. Ins. Co. v. Logan, 90 Ky. 364, 12 R. 327, 14 S. W. 337; Mutual Ins. Co. v. Hammond, 106

# §72 County in Which An Action Must or May Be Brought. [Title V]

§ 631. Foreign insurance company-process against. Before authority is granted to any foreign insurance company to do business in this State, it must file with the commissioner a resolution adopted by its board of directors, consenting that service of process upon any agent of such company in this State, or upon the Commissioner of Insurance of this State, in any action brought or pending in this State, shall be a valid service upon said company; and if process is served upon the commissioner it shall be his duty to at once send it by mail, addressed to the company at its principal office. (Ky. Stat., scc. 631.)

Cross reference: - See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 34, 546b; Form No. 35.

# § 72 [101, 103] [Corporations generally upon a contract or for a tort.]

Excepting the actions mentioned in sections 62 to '66, both inclusive, and in sections 68, 70, 71, 73, 75, and 77, an action against a corporation which has an office or place of business in this State, or a chief officer or agent residing in this State, must be brought in the county in which such office or place of business is situated or in which such officer or agent resides; or, if it be upon a contract, in the above-named county, or in the county.

Ky. 386, 20 R. 1944, 50 S. W. 545; Ward v. Citizens Life Ins. Co., 114 S. W. 751, 131 Ky. 129.

1. Action on policy ordered by letter may be brought in county in which policy was issued by the agent. Sun Ins. Co. v. Crist, 19 R. 305, 39 S. W. 837.
2. This section applies to foreign as well as domestic corporations. Barnes v. Union Cent. Life Ins. Co., 168 Ky. 253, 182 S. W. 169.
3. If a contract is to be performed in more than one county, the fact that part performance is to be had in one county does not give jurisdiction to the court of that county. Job Iron & Steel Co. v. Clark, 150 Ky. 246, 150 S. W. 367.
4. An obligation for the payment of money at a certain place is a contract to be performed there; but a county is not a corporation within the meaning of this section, Bd. Church Extension v. Taylor Co., 152 Ky. 518, 153 S. W. 747.
5. In an action brought against a corporation for breach of contract in the county where the contract was made or to be performed, process in which contract was performed has jurisdiction, and summons may be executed in another county. Owensboro Shovel & Tool Co. v. Moore, 154 Ky. 431, 157 S. W. 1121.
6. A contract was signed by defendant in Powell County and mailed to plaintiffs in Harlan County, where it was signed by plaintiffs and returned by mail to defendant. Held, that under this section a suit was properly brought upon the contract in Harlan County. Swann-Day Lumb. Co. v. Cornett, 161 Ky. 98, 170 S. W. 516.
7. A written order was taken by a salesman in a county in Kentucky, subject to the acceptance of the salesman’s employer outside the State.

# §73 [102] [Common carrier—upon a contract to carry property or for a tort.]

Excepting the actions mentioned in section 75, an action against a common carrier, whether a corporation or not, upon a contract to carry property, must be brought in the county in which the defendant, or either of several defendants, resides; or in which the contract is made; or in which the carrier agrees to deliver the property. # (5) Corporation-tort-waiver.

Action to recover damages for injury to land should be brought in county where land is situated, but venue may be waived by failure to object in time. Gillen v. Illinois Cent. R. Co., 125 S. W. 1047, 137 Ky. 375.

# (6) Where tort committed-venue of action.

The tort is committed in that county in which the injury to person or property actually occurs and in that county the action may be brought. Peaslee-Gaulbert Co. v. MeMath, 148 Ky. 265, 146 S. W. 770.

# (7) Person or creditor having an interest in thing sought to be subjected may raise question as to jurisdiction, although corporation does not.

(5) Where a contract was made in M. # (9) Injury to stock-venue.

Action against carrier for injuring stock in transportation must be brought in county specified in this section. Jewell v. L. & N. R. Co., 145 Ky. 493, 140 S. W. 689. An action was brought against a common carrier, a foreign corporation, for damages growing out of the negligent shipment of cattle from a point in Indiana to a point in Kentucky. The bill of lading stipulated that the carrier was not to make delivery at the point of destination, but was to transport the cattle over its line and deliver them to connecting carriers. The action was rightly brought in the county in which the point of ultimate destination was located. C., C., C. & St. L. Ry. Co. v. Young, 175 Ky. 841, 195 S. W. 93.

Jurisdiction of action to recover damages for loss of. Brunk v. Ohio R. Co., 105 S. W. 443, 32 R. 175, 127 Ky. 304; Wilson v. L. & N. R. Co., 112 S.'W. 585.

# (l6a) Telephone company.

This section does not apply to an action against a telephone company for failure to carry and deliver a message, nor authorize the institution of such an action in a county in which the telephone company does not reside. Gainesboro Tel. Co. v. Buckner, 82160 Ky. 604, 169 S. W. 1000.

Title V]  County in Which An Action Must or May Be Brought.            [5 74-75

# § 74. [Person or character-injury to.]

Every other action for an injury to the person of the plaintiff, and every action for an injury to the character of the plaintiff, against a defendant residing in this State, must be brought in the county in which the defendant resides, or in which the injury is done: [Provided, That in actions for libel the action shall be brought in the county in which the plaintiff resides or in the county in which the newspaper or publication is printed or published, or in the county in which the transaction, or act or declaration to which the publication relates is stated, or purported to have been done or taken place.] (The words in brackets were added by an Act of March 18, 1910, c. 28, p. 85.*) (Sec also secs. 123-124.)

*This section had theretofore been amended by an Act of March 21, 1906. c. 100, p. 389, which added this same "proviso," and added at the end thereof certain other words, but the latter were not referred to nor included in the Act of 1910.—Ed.

# § 75 {105}

[Persons constructively summoned.] Excepting the actions mentioned in sections 62 to 67, both inclusive, and in section 70 and section 76, an action against a defendant who may be proceeded against by a warning order, as is authorized by section 57, must be brought in a county in which the defendant resides at the commencement of the action; or in which he has property; or in which a person resides against whom he has a valid claim for money or property.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 30, 30b, 49, 546b; Forms Nos. 124, 129.

# (17) Waiver.

Right to object to jurisdiction will be waived if not made in proper time. Gillen v. Illinois Cent. R. Co., 125 S. W. 1047, 137 Ky. 375.

# (6) Service of summons in another county.

Where the Code fixes the county in which an action must be brought, summons issued in the action may be served in any county in which the defendant may be found, although he was in that county as a witness at the time of service. Rains v. Smith, 155 Ky. 766, 160 S. W. 493.

Evans v. M. & B. S. R., 25 R. 1258, 77 S. W. 708.

County in Which An Action Must or May Be Brought.  [Title V]

§ 76. [Alimony or divorce.] An action for alimony or divorce must be brought in the county where the wife usually resides, if she have an actual residence in this State; if not, in the county of the husband's residence. (Sec also secs. 420 to 427, and as to venue of action see Ky. Stat., sec. 2120, same as this.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 43, 546b, 546f; Forms Nos. 149, 151.

§ 77. [Contractor for public work.] Excepting the actions mentioned in section 75, an action against a contractor who undertakes to construct, in whole or in part, any turnpike, bridge, railway, lock, dam, or other public work, for labor done, or materials or supplies furnished, for the construction or promotion of said work, may be brought in the county wherein the labor, or most of it, is done, or wherein the materials, or supplies, or most thereof, are furnished; and if such bridge connect two counties, such action may be brought in either of them.

Cross reference:—See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20c, 37, 546b.

§ 78 [1o6] [Transitory actions.] An action which is not required by the foregoing sections of this article to be brought in some other county may be brought in any county in which the defendant, or in which one of the defendants resides.
