# §79-80 County in Which An Action Must or May Be Brought.

# § 79 [107] [Against single defendant-judgment.]

In action brought pursuant to section 78, against a single defendant, there shall be no judgment against him, unless he be summoned in the county wherein the action is brought; or, unless he reside in such county when the action is brought and be summoned elsewhere in this State; or, unless he make defense to the action before objecting to the jurisdiction of the court.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 51b, 52, 52a, 546b; Form No. 161.

# § 80 [108, 108] [Several defendants--judgment—bankrupt.]

In an action brought pursuant to section 78, against several defendants, no judgment shall be rendered against any of them, upon the service of a summons out of the county in which the action is brought, if no one of them be summoned in that county, nor resided therein when the action was commenced; nor if the action be discontinued or dismissed as to the defendant who resided, or was summoned, in that county; nor if judgment be rendered in

| (21) | Bankrupt. Person who has been adjudged a bankrupt may be joined as a defendant to confer jurisdiction in county where he is summoned. Austin v. First Nat. Bank, 148 Ky. 587, 147 S. W. 35.                                            |
| ---- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| (22) | County, suits against a county in courts of another. Board of Church Ex. v. Taylor Co., 152 Ky. 518, 153 S. W. 747; Stone v. Winn, 165 Ky. 9, 176 S. W. 933.                                                                           |
| (23) | Foreign insurance company. Summons served on insurance commissioner. Barnes v. Union Cent. Life and A. Co., 168 Ky. 253, 182 S. W. 169. Policy issued in another State. South v. Continental Casualty Co., 170 Ky. 249, 185 S. W. 858. |

# § 79.

1. Evidence as to residence. Defendant sued in one county and served with process in another; evidence that he lived in former county when suit brought and judgment rendered did not authorize judgment; but if he removed from former county after suit filed, judgment would have been proper on evidence of that fact. Dyas v. Lindsey, 5 Bush 506. Residence is a question of fact to be submitted to the jury under proper instructions. L. & N. R. R. v. Munford. 24 R. 416, 68 S. W. 635.
2. Personal judgment in action to enforce lien on land. Collins v. Park, 93 Ky. 6, 13 R. 905, 18 S. W. 1013; Mitchell v. Fidelity Trust Co., 20 R. 713, 47 S. W. 446; and see sec. 376 and notes.
3. Service in another county. Suit was brought in county of defendant's residence and process issued to that and another county, and was served in latter, but not in former, in time for judgment. It was error to render judgment on service in another county. Raymon v. Reed, 16 B. M. 345.
4. Defendant need not respond to summons in such case. Dyas v. Lindsey, 4 Bush 349; Ruby v. Grace, 2 Duv. 540.
5. Appearance not entered, when. If an objection to the jurisdiction of the court is properly made and overruled, the defendant may make defense to the merits without entering his appearance. Muir v. Edelen, 156 Ky. 212, 160 S. W. 1048.

Non-resident of State. An action was brought in Pike County against a non-resident of Kentucky who had property in Pike County; defendant was afterwards served with process in Boyd County. The Pike Circuit Court had jurisdiction to render judgment against the non-resident. Campbell v. The Males Co., 152 Ky. 802. 154 S. W. 918.

# § 80.

1. Dismissal as to defendant served in county. If several defendants are sued and one is served with process in another county, judgment cannot be rendered against him if action is dismissed as to, or judgment is not rendered against, one served in county where suit was brought. Duekworth v. Lee, 10 Bush 51; Reid v. Cain, 3 R. 329; Ward v. George, 1 Bush 357; see notes to secs. 78 and 79.

Judgment by default on defective petition-effect. Judgment by default against one of several defendants served in county where suit brought, when petition does not state cause of action against him, and it is not made out by

his favor, unless a defendant summoned out of that county make defense without objecting to the jurisdiction of the court: Provided, That a judgment for a defendant, who resided or was summoned in that county upon a plea of his discharge as a bankrupt, shall not prevent a judgment against any other defendant, in an action brought before the commencement of the proceedings in which the discharge was obtained; but after such judgment, upon a plea of discharge in bankruptcy, a defendant not summoned in the county, nor residing therein at the commencement of the action, may, by answer, deny the liability of such bankrupt. The issue as to the original liability of such bankrupt shall be tried as if he were still a party, and the plaintiff shall not have judgment against the defendant not summoned nor residing in the county, unless it be decided that the bankrupt was originally liable. (See further, as to judgment, secs. 370-373.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 51c, 52, 52a, 453c, 546b.

proof, will not authorize judgment against a defendant served in another county and who pleads to jurisdiction. Meguiar v. Rudy, 7 Bush 432; Fernold v. Speer, 3 Met. 459.

1. Objection to jurisdiction does not enter appearance. Dyas v. Lindsey, 4 Bush 349; Barbour v. Newkirk, 83 Ky. 529, 7 R. 555; C. & O. R. Co. v. Heath, 87 Ky. 651. 10 R. 646, 9 S. W. 832; Muir v. Edelen, 156 Ky. 212, 160 S. W. 1048; and see cases cited in notes to sec. 39.
2. Waiver of objection. Where the action is dismissed as to only defendant summoned in the county by consent with leave to other defendants to file an answer they are regarded as having waived their right to object to jurisdiction. Meehan v. Edwards, 92 Ky. 574, 13 R. 803, 18 S. W. 519.
3. Appeal by defendant improperly joined who objects to jurisdiction will not enter his appearance if his objection is sustained. White v. Kirby, 147 Ky. 496, 144 S. W. 369.
4. See notes to sec. 78.
5. Fraudulent vendees in distinct transfers can not be joined as defendants in one action. Friedman v. Sinnott's Ex., 150 Ky. 430, 150 S. W. 520.
6. A defendant was properly served in the county where the suit was brought. The court obtained jurisdiction against other defendants served with process in another county. Austin v. First Nat. Bk. of Scottsville, 150 Ky. 113, 150 S. W. 8.
7. In an action against two persons who were jointly bound on a debt process v. Prichard, 172 Ky. 190, 189 S. W. 14.

# §81-82]  County in Which An Action Must or May Be Brought.  [Title V

# § 81. [Defendant can not be summoned—when.]

In an action brought pursuant to section 78, a defendant who is summoned out of the county in which it is brought, and who did not reside therein when the action was begun, can not be summoned in that or any other action of the plaintiff whilst visiting such county for the sole purpose of defending the first-named action. (And see further, sec. 542.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 35, 51a, 52, 546b.

# § 82 [11o] [Removal of defendant after commencement of action.]

If, after the commencement of an action in the county of the defendant's residence, he move therefrom, the service of a summons upon him in any other county shall have the same effect as if it had been made in the county from which he moved.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 51, 546b.

# § 81. Defendant can not be summoned while in county for purpose of defending action.

Jackson v. Bank of Lockport, 137 S. W. 767, 144 Ky. 43.

Title VI] Joinder of Actions. [§ 83

# TITLE VI.

# JOINDER OF ACTIONS.

# § 83. Causes of action that may be joined.

# § 84. Party may strike from pleading cause of action.

# § 85. Court may require party to elect—may strike out.

# § 86. Waiver of objection to misjoinder.

# § 83 [111] [Causes of action that may be joined.]

Several causes of action may be united, if each affect all the parties to the action, may be brought in the same county, and may be prosecuted by the same kind of action; and if all of them be brought

1. [Upon contracts.] Upon contracts, express or implied; or.
2. [Real property, concerning.] For the recovery of real property and the rents, profits and damages for withholding it; or,
3. [Personal property, concerning.] For the recovery of specific personal property, and damages for the taking or withholding it; or.

# § 83.

1. Assignee-Action by to recover possession of personal property can not be united with an action by him to settle and distribute the estate. Atchinson v. Jones, 8 R. 259.
2. Causes must exist at commencement of action. The joinder of actions referred to in this section applies to causes of action existing at time of suit and not such as arise subsequently; in action for slander plaintiff can not recover for slanderous words spoken during the action and set up in an amended petition. Taylor v. Moran. 4 Met. 127; see as to lien debts, secs. 135, 694.
3. Common interest. When it is of common interest to several parties to set aside a judgment, which being done, all questions will relate to the same parties and subject-matter, there is no misjoinder. MeCormick v. MeCormick, 9 R. 519, 5 S. W. 573.
4. Contract-fraud. Action upon contract and for fraud can not be joined. Wilson v. Thompson, 1 Met. 123, unless the cause of action for fraud or negligence is directly connected with the cause of action upon the contract. Jones v. Johnson, 10 Bush 649; Gregg v. Woods, 3 R. 526; Jones v. Johnson, 86 Ky. 530, 9 R. 789, 6 S.W. 582.
5. Creditor against guardian and wards. Guardian of several wards can not be sued in same action for claims due by each of his wards distinct from the others. St. Joseph O. S. v. Wolpert, 80 Ky. 86, 3 R. 573.
6. Debtor and persons who won from him gaming. See Caldwell v. Caldwell, 2 Bush 446; and Whemhoff v. Rutherford, 98 Ky. 91, 17 R. 659, 32 S. W. 288.
7. Distributees against administrator. Distributees can not sue jointly an administrator for balance due by him on settlement. Pelly v. Bowyer. 7 Bush 513; Jackson v. Robinett, 2 Bibb 292.
8. Ejectment-Action for may be brought against several defendants. Bryant v. Stephens, 26 R. 718. 82 S. W. 423.
9. Improper joinder for the purpose of giving a court jurisdiction not allowed. Johnson v. Bradford, 114 Ky. 96, 24 R. 864, 70 S. W. 193. A common law cause of action against some of the defendants can not be united in the same petition with an equitable cause of action against all of them. Metcalfe v. Johnson, 151 Ky. 823, 152 S. W. 951; and notes to sec. 78.
10. Joint owners against trespassers. Joint owners of land may maintain joint action for its recovery against several persons claiming and holding separate parcels of it. Woolfolk v. Ashby, 2 Met. 288. See Sale v. Crutchfield, 8 Bush 636.

# Joinder of Actions

# 4. Partition of real or personal property.

For partition of real or personal property, or both; or,

# 5. Injuries to character.

For injuries to character : or,

# 6. Injuries to person and property.

For injuries to person and property. (And see as to actions for trespass, Ky. Stat., sec. 12.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 35, 327b, 328, 330, 360, 367, 368, 370, 372, 374, 375, 376, 382, 580, 768, 767, 768; Forms N0S. 76, 145.

and Bryant v. Stephens, 26 R. 718, 82 S. W. 423.

# 11) Jurisdiction.

Misjoinder can not give as to defendant summoned out of county. See note to sec. 86.

# 11a) Demurrer to jurisdiction.

Where several causes of action were joined of one of which the court had jurisdiction, a demurrer to the jurisdiction was properly overruled. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Mitchell, 162 Ky. 253, 172 S. W. 527.

# 12) Land--damages-separate actions.

In action to recover land and damages for its detention, the judgment for the land and damages for its detention are as distinct as if separate judgments were rendered in different suits, and in such action the judgment for recovery of land may be affirmed and for damages reversed. "Shean v. Cunningham, 6 Bush 123.

# 13) Land-rents-trespass.

Plaintiff may in one action sue for land and rents, and in another to recover damages for trespasses and injuries committed by destruction of timber and other property on the land; Burr v. Woodrow, 1 Bush 602, 18 B. M. 541. But may, if he choose, sue for land in one action and in another for rents: if he unites both in same action he can not in separate action sue for rents, but may in separate action recover for extraordinary expense such as was not and could not have been set up in action for land and rent. Walker v. Mitchel, 18 B. M. 541; and see 6 Bush 123.

# 14) Libel and slander.

Causes of action for may be united. Hargan v. Purdy, 93 Ky. 424, 14 R. 383, 20 S. W. 432; Fred v. Traylor, 115 Ky. 94, 24 R. 1906. 72 S. W. 768. An action for slander and an action for alienation of affections of a spouse can not be joined, but this section was not violated by pleading in an action for slander showing special damages to plaintiff on the alienation of her husband's affections by reason of the slander. Felty v. Felty, 164 Ky. 355, 175 S. W. 613; and see note 22.

# 15) Lienholders.

Two or more may unite in same action for the purpose of enforcing their respective liens. Boyd v. Jones, 8 R. 602, 2 S. W. 552; Foster v. Lyons, 19 R. 1906, 44 S. W. 625; and see secs. 692-4 and notes.

# 16) Officials in public and private capacity.

An action can not be maintained against officers of a municipal corporation in both their official and private capacity for the same injury. Hancock v. Johnson, 1 Met. 242.

# 17) Order of delivery-enforcement of mortgage.

Petition seeking to recover a debt, and to enforce mortgage lien to secure its payment, and to obtain order of delivery for possession of mortgaged property, which had been subsequently mortgaged to another, is a misjoinder of actions. McKee v. Pope, 18 B. M. 548.

# 18) Penalties.

It was held in L. & N. R. R. v. Com., 102 Ky. 300, 19 R. 1462, 43 S. W. 458, that the Com. could not unite in one action several offenses for violation of sec. 784. Ky. Stat. In Farmers' Tobacco W. H. Co. v. Minor, 107 Ky. 287, 21 R. 990, 53 S. W. 641, the recovery in one action of several penalties for violations of sec. 4803, Ky. Stat., was upheld.

# 19) Personal injury.

Action for may be maintained against several who contribute to it, whether by joint or separate acts. Pugh v. C. & O. R. R., 101 Ky. 77, 19 R. 149, 39 S. W. 695; L. & E. Mail Co. v. Clara Barnes, 25 R. 2036; Cumberland T. & T. Co. v. Ware, 115 Ky. 581, 24 R. 2519, 74 S. W. 289. And two separate distinct acts of negligence may be sued for in same action. L. & N. R. Co. v. Adams, 148 Ky. 513, 147 S. W. 384.

# 20) Remote obligor in bond.

The owner of a slave hired her for a year to A and B, and they hired her to C, taking from C a covenant to A, with D as surety, for the return of the slave. C caused her death, and ran off. The owner and A and B could maintain an action.

# Title VIj

# Joinder of Actions.

# [§ 84]

A party may strike from his petition, answer or reply any cause of action, before the final submission of the case to the jury, or the court, if the trial be by the court. (May dismiss without prejudice, sec. 371.)

on the covenant against D. Carney v. Walden, 16 B. M. 388.

# (21) Settlement of estate.

In action for, persons indebted to the estate can not be joined as defendants. Citizens' Bank v. Boswell, 93 Ky. 92, 14 R. 17, 19 S. W. 174; Hilton v. Hilton, 110 Ky. 522, 22 R. 1934, 62 S. W. 6.

# (22) Slander - false imprisonment - malicious prosecution.

Action for false imprisonment or malicious arrest and slander can not be joined. Dragoo v. Levi, 2 Duv. 520; Tandy v. Riley, 26 R. 98, 80 S. W. 776.

# (23) Splitting causes of action.

Splitting causes of action, so as to bring two suits in place of one, will not be allowed, and if it is done the court should require the actions consolidated. Powell v. Weiler, 11 B. M. 187; and see Pilcher v. Gegan, 91 Ky. 228, 12 R. 860, 15 S. W. 513; Weinstock v. Bellwood, 12 Bush 139; Cole v. I. C. R. R., 27 R. 1087, 87 S. W. 1082; Lou. Bridge Co. v. L. & N. R. Co., 116 Ky. 258, 25 R. 405, 75 S. W. 285; C. & C. B. Co. v. Kliemier, 105 Ky. 609, 20 R. 1415, 49 S. W. 484; Coles v. I. C. R. Co., 27 R. 1087, 120 Ky. 686, and 150 Ky. 564.

# (24) Trespassers.

Joint and several judgments against; see Ky. Stat., sec. 12; Alexander v. Humber, 86 Ky. 565, 9 R. 734, 6 S. W. 453; Cent. Pass. Ry. Co. v. Kuhn, 86 Ky. 578, 9 R. 725, 6 S. W. 441; and see cases cited in note 19.

# (25) Vendees—to set aside fraudulent deed to.

In an action by an assignee in bankruptcy to set aside as fraudulent several conveyances made by the bankrupt, the several fraudulent vendees may be joined as defendants. Anderson v. Anderson, 80 Ky. 638, 4 R. 579.

# (26) Warranty - fraudulent concealment.

Causes of action for growing out of same transaction may be united. Gregg v. Woods, 3 R. 526.

# (27) Contract-tort.

Action to obtain relief on both grounds can not be joined. C. & O. R. Co. v. Patton, 146 Ky. 656; Wilson v. L. & N. R. Co., 112 S. W. 585; Willis v. Tomes, 141 Ky. 431, 132 S. W. 1043; Little v. Consolidation Coal Co., 169 Ky. 514, 184 S. W. 873; Foy-Proctor Co. v. Marshall & Thorn, 169 Ky. 377, 183 S. W. 940; but plaintiff may waive tort growing out of a trespass and sue in contract. Roberts v. Moss, 127 Ky. 657, 106 S. W. 297, 32 R. 525.

# (28) Settle estate and set aside deed made by decedent.

Action can not be joined. Dailey v. O'Brien, 96 S. W. 521, 29 R. 811.

# (29) Attachment.

Action for maliciously suing out, can not be joined with action on indemnifying bond. Bell v. Thompson, 102 S. W. 830, 31 R. 474.

# (30) Joint tort feasors.

May be joined as defendants. Pickerill v. City of Lou., 125 Ky. 213, 100 S. W. 873, 30 R. 1239; and see cases cited in notes 19 and 24.

# (31) Personal injury-death.

Action for damages caused by death, and for suffering can not be joined. Hendricks v. American Express Co., 138 Ky. 704, 128 S. W. 1089; Lewis v. Taylor Coal Co., 112 Ky. 845, 66 S. W. 1044, 23 R. 2218.

# (32) Alienation of affections.

Actions for alienation of affections and for criminal conversation may be joined under this section. Merritt v. Cravens, 168 Ky. 155, 181 S. W. 970. Alienation and libel and slander. Felty v. Felty, 164 Ky. 355, 175 S. W. 643.

# (33) Surety-bond of city comptroller.

A petition filed by a city stating a cause of action and praying that certain specific securities be returned to the city, and in the alternative for their value if not to be had, states but one cause of action. Nat. Surety Co. v. City of Louisville, 165 Ky. 38, 176 S. W. 364.

# (34) An action.

By a widow to cancel an ante-nuptial contract and a subsequent settlement and deed in pursuance thereof and also to recover dower in her husband's land and half the personalty was not a case of misjoinder within this section, Robinson v. Robinson, 153 Ky. 828, 156 S. W. 903.

# § 84. Amended petition making misjoinder.

In an action on contract plaintiff filed an amended petition setting up and relying on fraud in obtaining contract. Held, that plaintiff might in an amended petition set up action that could not be joined with action in petition, and thus abandon his original action and rely on that in amendment. Hord v. Chandler, 13 B. M. 403; but see secs. 113, 114 and note 1 to sec. 85.

# Joinder of Actions

# § 85 [11s] [Court may require party to elect-may strike out.]

The court, at any time before defense shall, on motion of a party, require the adverse party to elect which of two or more causes of action, improperly joined, he will prosecute; and, upon his refusal so to elect, shall strike out of the petition, answer or reply any cause of action improperly joined with another. (See further, sec. 113.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 173d, 365, 370, 377, 396h, 550e, 550f, 798; Form No. 172.

# § 86 [114] [Waiver of objection to misjoinder.]

Objections to the misjoinder of causes of action are waived, unless they are made pursuant to section 85.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 365a, 377, 396h, 550e, 550f, 798.

# 85. (1 Amendment -objection to for misjoinder.

If an amended petition would make a misjoinder of actions by setting up a new cause, objection can be made to its filing. Hancock v. Johnson, 1 Met. 242.

# (2 Misjoinder-how objected to.

Motion to dismiss petition because there is misjoinder is not proper. Objection to misjoinder should be made by motion to elect. Hunt v. Semonin, 79 Ky. 270, 2 R. 334; Humphrey v. Hughes, 79 Ky. 487, 3 R. 273. An objection to the misjoinder of causes of action can not be raised by demurrer to the jurisdiction. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Mitchell, 162 Ky. 253, 172 S. W. 527.

# (3) Motion to elect-duty of court.

If causes of action be misjoined, plaintiff may on motion be required to elect, and, on failure, court should strike out one of the causes misjoined; if plaintiff then refuse to proceed, the petition should be dismissed without prejudice. Dragoo v. Levi, 2 Duv. 520; Hilton v. Hilton, 110 Ky. 522, 22 R. 1934, 62 S. W. 6; Jenkins v. Taylor, 22 R. 1137, 59 S. W. 853; Bannon v. Bannon, 119 S. W. 1170, 136 Ky. 556; Metcalfe v. Johnson, 151 Ky. 823, 152 S. W. 951.

# (4) Where the plaintiff refuses to elect.

The court should not dismiss the action, but strike out the cause improperly joined. Sheppard v. Stephens, 8 R. 603.

# (5) One cause but irrelevant matter.

No election can be required if petition state but one substantial cause of action, although it may contain irrelevant and redundant matter. Bonney v. Reardin, 6 Bush 34.

# § 86. (1 Misjoinder-waiver.

Objection for misjoinder must be made in circuit court. McKee v. Pope, 18 B. M. 548; and failure to make motion at proper time operates as a waiver of objection. Wilson v. Thompson, 1 Met. 123; Caldwell v. Caldwell, 2 Bush 446; 8 Bush 636; 79 Ky. 270, 2 R. 334; Murray v. Booker, 22 R. 781, 58 S. W. 788; and waiver by filing answer remains although answer be withdrawn. Chiles v. Drake, 2 Met. 146; Stovall v. Hibbs, 17 R. 906, 32 S. W. 1087; Bryant v. Stephens, 26 R. 718, 82 S. W. 423; Bannon v. Bannon, 119 S. W. 1170, 136 Ky. 556.

# (2 Misjoinder can not give jurisdiction.

Although misjoinder is waived by not objecting in time, yet such misjoinder can not give court jurisdiction over defendant summoned in another county, when otherwise court would not have jurisdiction. Ransdall v. Shropshire, 4 Met. 326; see Maddox v. Williams, 5 R. 695; Basye v. Brown, 78 Ky. 553; 1 R. 256; and see notes to sec. 78.

# (3) Misjoinder shown by evidence.

If the pleadings do not show a misjoinder of parties, and such misjoinder is disclosed by the evidence, a motion requiring plaintiff to elect against which party he will proceed, may thereupon be made. Louisville Gas and Electric Co. v. Wall 178 Ky. 33, 198 S. W. 745.

§ 87. Pleadings defined.

§ 88. Form and sufficiency of.

§ 89. Pleadings allowed.

§ 87 [115] [Pleadings defined.] Pleadings are statements by parties to an action of their causes of controversy. (Caption of, sec. 110; what may contain, sec. 113-2; verification of, and by whom, secs. 116, 117; issue must be formed, sec. 114; when allegations confessed, sec. 126; when must be proved, sec. 126; what writings must, or may, be filed, secs. 120, 128. Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 1, 425.

87. (1) Allegation and proof. It is as necessary to state a cause of action as it is to sustain it by proof. The absence of either prevents a recovery. Murrell v. McAlister, 79 Ky. 311, 2 R. 323; and the plaintiff can only recover upon proof of the cause of action alleged in his petition. Gossom v. Badgett, 6 Bush 97; 1 Met. 339. As to variance between pleading and proof, see secs. 129, 130, 131, and notes thereto.

(2) An allegation neither proved nor admitted or a ground of defense not alleged in the pleadings, although proven, is to be treated as if such allegation or defense did not exist. Wilkins v. Barnes, 79 Ky. 323. 2 R. 278.

(3) Proof that a party introduces must not be sufficient to state the evidence relied.

# 88-89] Pleadings in General.

# [Title VII]

# § 88 [116] [Form and sufficiency of.]

The forms of pleadings and the rules by which their sufficiency is to be determined are those prescribed by this Code. (See as to rules of pleading, sec. 110.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 126a.

# § 89 [117] [Pleadings allowed.]

The pleadings allowed are—

1. [Petitions, answers and other pleadings.] Petitions, answers and replies, and such additional pleadings, by way of rejoinder and rebutter, as may be necessary to form a material issue of fact.
2. [Demurrers.] Demurrers.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 6, 509, 811, 861.

on, nor necessary to set forth evidence of the truth of the facts relied on. L. & P. Canal Co. v. Murphy, 9 Bush 522; Bentley v. Bustard, 16 B. M. 643; 2 Met. 44900.

(8) Verdict-answer. Defects in pleadings that are cured by. See note 12, sec. 95.

(9) Withdrawal of pleadings unless an attested copy is left not proper. Wyles v. Berry, 116 Ky. 377, 25 R. 606, 7 S. W. 126.

(10) Writings filed with pleadings. See note 18, sec. 90.

88. Forms, but not substance, abolished by Code; and every essential averment required to make a declaration good at common law must be made in the petition. L. & P. Canal Co. v. Murphy, 9 Bush 522; and see note to sec. 4.

89. (1) Pleadings allowed by Code of 1854 were the petition, demurrer, answer, reply, set-off and counter-claim, and by act of 1857, a cross-petition was allowed. Myers' Code, sec. 117.

Title VII Petition

# CHAPTER II.

# PETITION.

# § 90. Requisites of—prayer for relief.

§ 90 [ais] [Requisites of-prayer for relief.] The petition must state facts which constitute a cause of action in favor of the plaintiff against the defendant, and must demand the specific relief to which the plaintiff considers himself entitled; and may contain a general prayer for any other relief to which the plaintiff may appear to be entitled. If no defense be made, the plaintiff can not have judgment for any relief not specifically demanded; but, if defense be made, he may have judgment for other relief, under a prayer therefor. (Forms of petitions in various actions, Forms 1-50, may be amended, secs. 109, 132, 134; verification of, secs. 116, 137. See as to notice required when lien on real property is asserted. Ky. Stat., sec. 2358a.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 192, 205, 319, 349, 350, 351, 353, 359, 413, 425, 426f, 501, 502, 503, 510, 526h, 550h, 559, 694, 709, 789, 791, 792.

# 90. (1 Account.

A promise is implied to pay reasonable value from the sale and delivery of the goods at defendant's request. Wood v. Wells, 2 Bush 197; Thruston v. Oldham, 6 Bush 16. Averment of sale and delivery to defendant at his request of goods worth a certain sum, or the rendition of services to defendant at his request worth a certain sum, is sufficient to authorize judgment by default; a promise to pay is implied. Skillman v. Muir, 4 Met. 282.

# (2

A petition alleging in substance that the defendant is indebted to plaintiff in the sum of $1,963 for goods bought, the whole of which is due and unpaid, is not good. There should be an averment that the goods were sold and delivered to defendant at his instance and request, or that they were sold upon the promise of defendant to pay. Drake v. Semonin, 82 Ky. 291, 6 R. 341.

# (3) Administrator-against.

It is not necessary to allege that the debt sued for was demanded of administrator, accompanied by proper affidavit, before action commenced. If demand has not been made, it can be reached by rule. Rogers v. Mitchell, 1 Met. 22; Thomas v. Thomas, 15 B. M. 178.

# (4) Assault and battery.

How averments asking special damages should be set out. Jesse v. Shuck, 1 R. 463, 12 S. W. 304. Petition for must set out facts constituting the assault. Stivers v. Baker, 87 Ky. 508, 10 R. 523, 9 S. W. 491.

# (5) Assignee against assignor.

In action by assignee against assignor of a note it is necessary to aver the consideration paid for the assignment. Humphrey v. Hughes, 79 Ky. 487, 3 R. 273; Ky. Stat., sec. 475. And before he can recover he must institute his action against the payer at the first term of the court and prosecute him to insolvency without unreasonable delay. Francis v. Gant, 80 Ky. 190, 2 R. 285.

# (6) Attorney - against.

In action against attorney for money collected, demand must be made before suit filed. Roberts v. Armstrong, 1 Bush 263.

# (7) Award-to set aside.

To set aside an award on the ground of fraud, the specific acts of fraud relied on must be averred. Phillips v. Phillips, 81 Ky. 147, 4 R. 941.

# (8) Blanks in petition.

Plaintiff, in order to recover specified amounts, should state the amounts, and not leave blank places for them in his pleading. Jesse

# Petition

by servant against master to recover damages for wrongful discharge.

# (13a) Conversion

In an action for the conversion of trees, the value of the trees should be stated in the petition, but where their size and quality are given it will be assumed, on demurrer and appeal that they were of some value. *Ford Lumber & Mfg. Co. v. Burt & Brabb Lumber Co., 157 Ky. 706, 163 S. W. 1105.*

# (9) Bond

In action on bond, the petition should set out the covenant of the bond, and the breach of them relied on. *Mann v. Martin, 82 Ky. 242. 6 R. 346.*

# (10)

In action on bond, the terms and stipulations of the bond must be set out, as well as the breach of it: filing bond with petition, or referring to it, is not sufficient. *Riggs v. Maltby, 2 Met. 88; Campbell v. Galbreath, 12 Bush 459; 14 B. M. 252.*

# (11)

Petition on injunction bond conditioned to pay judgment enjoined, if injunction dissolved, is insufficient unless it states that the judgment is unpaid. *Crawford v. Woodworth, 9 Bush 745.*

# (12) Constable - against

In action against constable for money collected, it is necessary to allege a demand and refusal to pay. *Harris v. Perry, 2 Bush 101; Huston v. Hagar, I Duv. 24.*

# (13) Contract-for breach of

The breach of a contract is an essential part of the cause of action, and should be assigned in the words of the contract, or in words which are co-extensive with the import and effect of it. The rules of pleading which require that the contract shall be substantially set out, as well as the breach of which plaintiff complains are fundamental, and have not been changed by the Code. *Moxley v. Moxley, 2 Met. 309: 8 B. M. 377.*

# (18) Departure in pleading

A new cause of action set up in a reply will not support a judgment by default thereon. *Spiess v. Bartley, 113 S. W. 127, 130 Ky. 277.*

# (19) Defects cured by answer—verdict

See note 12 to sec. 95.

# (20) Defense need not be anticipated

It is not necessary to notice and remove objections that may exist and can be relied on for defense. *L. & P. Canal Co. v. Murphy, 9 Bush 522; Shelbyville Trustees v. S. & E. T. P. Co., I Met. 54; Bentley v. Bustard, 16 B. M. 643.*

# (21) Dower

In action by widow against purchaser from her husband to have dower allotted, the petition did not aver that her husband was the owner of the land during coverture, nor that she was his wife when purchase was made by defendant, and was not good. *Yancy v. Smith, 2 Met. 408; sec Fritz v. Tudor, 2 Duv. 173.*

# (22) Exhibits filed with or copied into

a pleading will not cure defects in it or supply omitted averments; the pleading must be complete in itself. An exhibit will not make a pleading good, but may make it bad, as on demurrer the exhibit will be considered for the purpose of showing that petition is not good, and if...

# Title VII

# Petition

[§ 90

it contradicts the pleading, the exhibit will control. Altemus v. Asher, 24 R. 2416, 74 S. W. 245; Newport Lumber Co. v. Lichtenfeldt, 24 R. 1969, 72 S. W. 778; Gardner v. Continental Ins. Co., 25 R. 426, 75 S. W. 283; Miller v. McConnell, 26 R. 181; Goff v. Janeway, 26 R. 525, 80 S. W. 1103; B. & T. Co. v. Lou. R. W. Co., 25 R. 122; and see sec. 128.

# (23) False imprisonment.

See Robinson v. Morgan, 100 Ky. 529; 18 R. 903, 38 S. W. 868; Reynolds v. Price, 22 R. 5, 56 S. W. 502; Southern R. Co. v. Shirley, 90 S. W. 597, 121 Ky. 863, 28 R. 860; Roberts v. Thomas, 12i S. W. 961, 135 Ky. 63. The allegation in a petition that an arrest was made by a deputy sheriff in his official capacity, is not the statement of a fact, but a mere legal conclusion. Jones v. Van Bever, 164 Ky. 80, 174 S. W. 795.

# (24) Fraud.

If the petition sets out the facts showing the fraud they will be given their legal effect although the word "fraud" is not used. Ky. Electric Co. v. Barrett, 116 S. W. 1186, 132 Ky. 717.

# (25) Guaranty.

When notice of the acceptance of an offer of guaranty is requisite in action on guaranty, the allegation of notice in the petition should be special to enable the court to determine whether or not it is sufficient. General averment "of all of which defendant had notice" is not sufficient. Steadman v. Guthrie, 4 Met. 147; Mast v. Lehman, 100 Ky. 464, 18 R. 949, 38 S. W. 1056.

# (26) Guardian's settlement-to surcharge.

In action to surcharge settlement of guardian, the petition must specify the particular items objected to. Tanner v. Skinner, 11 Bush 120; Fielder v. Harbison, 93 Ky. 482, 14 R. 481, 20 S. W. 508.

# (27) Heir for debt of ancestor.

To make the heir responsible for debt of ancestor it must be alleged that he received assets from ancestor. Massie v. Hiatt. 82 Ky. 314; 6 R. 176; 10 Bush 234; 80 Ky. 152, 3 R. 647; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 2084. As to proper averment that one is heir; see Montgomery v. White, 10 R. 905, 11 S. W. 10; 7 Bush 51; and 18 B. M. 72; Craig v. Welch Coal Co., 24 R. 2225, 73 S. W. 1035.

# (28) Husband-for debt of wife.

In action by husband to recover debt made by wife before marriage, it is not necessary to allege that the husband received property by the wife. Beaumont v. Miller, 1 Met. 68; Fultz v. Fox, 9 B. M. 499; Medley v. Tandy, 85 Ky. 566, 9 R. 168, 4 S. W. 308; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 2130.

# (29) Indorsers of note.

In action upon a note executed to plaintiff by D, payable in bank and indorsed in blank by F, N, and C before its delivery, whereby, as alleged, they "intended to be equally bound as obligors," it was not alleged that they indorsed the note as accommodation indorsers, or with the view of having it discounted in bank, or for the purpose of guaranteeing its payment. Petition held not good against indorsers. Kellogg v. Dunn, 2 Met. 215.

# (30) The presumption is that a note in the hands of the maker of it, indorsed by the payee, has been paid, and one to whom the maker has discounted it can not recover against the indorser without averring that it was indorsed for maker's accommodation.

Callahan v. First Nat. Bank, 78 Ky. 604, 1 R. 342; and see Graham v. Louisville City Bank, 103 Ky. 641, 20 R. 205, 45 S. W. 870.

# (30a) Insurance companies.

Suits on policies, allegations in. Vicars v. Aetna Life Ins. Co., 158 Ky. 1, 164 S. W. 106; Henry Fire Ins. Co. v. Barkley, 160 Ky. 153, 169 S. W. 747; Phil. Life Ins. Co. v. Farnsley's Admr., 162 Ky. 27, 171 S. W. 1004. Suit on contract made by agent; petition negatived agent's powers. Central Life Ins. Co. v. Vickers, 164 Ky. 848, 176 S. W. 375.

# (31) Interest.

To recover interest accruing anterior to the institution of the action on an unliquidated account, it is necessary that plaintiff should ask for that character of relief. Adams Express Co. v. Milton, 11 Bush 49.

# (32) Interest is allowable as a matter of law upon an account from the time when, by the contract, it is payable, or from the time it becomes a liquidated one.

Henderson Mfg. Co. v. Lowell M. S., 86 Ky. 668, 9 R. 831, 7 S. W. 142; Travelers Ins. Co. v. Henderson, 120 Ky. 218, 27 R. 653, 85 S. W. 1090.

# (33) Judgments.

In action on foreign judgment, it is necessary to allege facts showing that the court rendering the judgment had jurisdiction of the subject-matter and the person of the defendant. Gebhard v. Garnier, 12 Bush 321; Wood v. Wood, 78 Ky. 624, 1 R. 358; 10 Bush 160; 12 Bush 274; 115 Ky. 157, 24 R. 2004. As to pleading judgment of court of this State, see sec. 122.

# (34) Law of another State, when depended upon, must be pleaded as any other fact, so that the court may judge of the effect of the law.

Roots v. Merriwether, 8 Bush 397; Bidwell v. Robinson, 79 Ky. 29, 1 R. 249.

§ 90] Petition. [Title VII

# (35) Legal conclusion.

Averment that defendant extorted and obtained three hundred dollars from plaintiff without any consideration, and was therefore indebted to him in that amount, is merely a legal conclusion, and petition is not good. Murphy v. Estes, 6 Bush 532. See 1 Duv. 30; 4 Met. 327; Stivers v. Baker, 87 Ky. 508, 10 R. 523, 9 S. W. 491. That a certain judgment and execution are void. Angel v. Byars, 153 Ky. 20S, 154 S. W. 1109. That a certain ordinance was passed and that it required defendants to construct certain improvements. City of Richmond v. Madison Female Institute, 153 Ky. 301, 155 S. W. 371. That a deputy sheriff made an arrest in his official capacity. Jones v. Van Bever, 164 Ky. 80, 174 S. W. 795. An allegation that a judgment was recovered is not an allegation of a fact of which the judgment is prima facie evidence. Turner v. Hamlin, 152 Ky. 469, 153 S. W. 778.

# (36) Legislature no power to prescribe form of pleading

that dispenses with allegations of essential facts, or cuts off substantial defenses. City of Louisville v. Cochran, 82 Ky. 15, 5 R. 833; Meguiar v. Henry, 84 Ky. 1, 7 R. 695.

# (37) Malicious prosecution.

See Lancaster v. Langston, 18 R. 299, 36 S. W. 521; Lucas v. Hunt, 91 Ky. 279. 12 R. 871, 15 S. W. 781; Hegan Mantel Co. v. Alford, 114 S. W. 290.

# (38) Marriage contract.

Where no time and place are agreed upon for the consumption of the marriage, no action can be maintained by either party for failure to perform, unless plaintiff aver an offer to perform and a failure of the defendant to comply. Fible v. Caplinger. 13 B. M. 464; and see Shakleford v. Hamilton, 93 Ky. 80, 14 R. 11, 19 S. W. 5.

# (38a) Mental injury-fright.

There can be no recovery for fright unless accompanied by physical injury. Southern Ry. Co. in Ky. v. Owen, 156 Ky. 827, 162 S. W. 110.

# (39) Misnomer of defendant.

A party who makes defense to an action can not afterward set up that he was sued by wrong name, and thereby defeat recovery by plea of limitation. Heckman v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 85 Ky. 631, 9 R. 297, 4 S. W. 342; and see L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Hall, 12 Bush 131; and see note 4, sec. 39.

# (40) Money had and received.

Averment that defendant received $400 for the use of plaintiff, and thereby became indebted to him, and had paid no part of it, is insufficient. The facts showing how it was received, such as created an indebtedness, must be alleged. Faught v. Henry, 13 Bush 471.

# (41) Negligence.

It is sufficient in action for personal injury to allege in general terms that the injury complained of was caused by the carelessness and negligence of the defendant. Chiles v. Drake, 2 Met. 146; L., C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Case, 9 Bush 728. A general charge of negligence is sufficient in the courts of this State in personal injury actions whether arising under the employer's liability act or not. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Stewart's Admrx., 156 Ky. 550, 161 S. W. 557; but if the petition specifies the negligence complained of, a recovery can not be had for any other negligence, and this rule applies where the agent or officer charged with the negligence is specified. Thomas v. L. & N. R. R., 18 R. 104, 35 S. W. 910; Chun v. K. & L. Bridge Co., 23 R. 1092, 64 S. W. 649. It is not necessary to allege that there was no contributory negligence on the part of plaintiff. L. & P. Canal Co. v. Murphy, 9 Bush 522; P. & M. R. R. Co. v. Hoehl, 12 Bush 41; 80 Ky. 82, 3 R. 576; 10 Bush 263; 84 Ky. 330, 8 R. 376; and if petition charges several acts of negligence and either of them is sustained by the evidence, a recovery may be had. L. & N. R. R. v. Shearer, 22 R. 929, 59 S. W. 330; L. & N. R. R. v. Gamble's Admrx., 156 Ky. 91, 160 S. W. 795.

# (42) Petition to recover for injury arising from negligence or want of skill

should state such facts as would authorize an action in case at common law; and, if for an injury done with force, such facts as would authorize action in trespass. Kountz v. Brown, 16 B. M. 577. An allegation of negligence in general terms will not authorize a recovery by a servant for failure of the master to use care to furnish a safe place to work or safe appliances. The failure to object to evidence as to unsafe appliances was not a waiver of this objection, where the evidence so admitted was competent on an issue in the case. Ohio Val. Coal & Min. Co. v. Heine, 159 Ky. 586, 167 S. W. 873. As to necessary allegations in action by employe against employer, where the injury is caused by defective appliances or machinery, see Mellott v. L. & N. R. R., 101 Ky. 212, 19 R. 379, 40 S. W. 696; Pfisterer v. Peter & Co., 117 Ky. 501, 25 R. 1605, 78 S. W. 450.

# (43) In an action under sec. 4. Ky. Stat.

it is necessary to aver that killing was not done in self-defense. Becker v. Crow, 7 Bush 198; 13 Bush 636; Hol-

Title VIIJ Petition. [§90
# Case Law References

lingsworth v. Warnock, 112 Ky. 96, 23 R. 1395, 65 S. W. 163.

# Negligence

(44) Negligence resulting in personal injury-how pleaded. Monroe v. Standard Mfg. Co., 141 Ky. 549, 133 S. W. 214; Gains v. Johnson, 105 S. W. 381, 28 R. 58, 133 Ky. 507.

(45) Negligence-averment of gross includes lower degrees of. Fendley v. Illinois Cent. R. Co., 92 S. W. 1, 28 R. 1325.

(16) Negligence-the petition may set out as many separate acts of negligence as the facts warrant. Ford v. Providence Coal Co., 30 R. 698, 99 S. W. 609, 124 Ky. 517. A petition to recover damages for injuries inflicted by negligence may set up separate and distinct acts of negligence; and a recovery may be had upon any of the grounds, although the others are not proved. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Gamble's Admrx., 156 Ky. 91, 160 S. W. 795.

# New Promise

(47) New promise. Action on new promise to pay an account is sufficient when the petition sets out a good cause of action on the account, and the promise to pay the amount due within two years before suit. Adams v. O'Rear, 80 Ky. 129, 3 R. 605; Meyer v. Zotel, 96 Ky. 362, 16 R. 506, 29 S. W. 28.

# Notes

(48) Note not assigned. Plaintiff cannot maintain an action on a note averring that it was executed to himself when the note was executed to another person, and not assigned to plaintiff, and no allegation as to how he obtained it. Dodd v. King, 1 Met. 430; Haney v. Tempest, 3 Met. 95; 1 Bush 70.

(49) Note payable to two persons. Neither has a separate right of action upon it without an averment of assignment from his co-payee. Quisenberry v. Artis, 1 Duv. 30; but see Perry v. Minor, 08 Ky. 242, 17 R. 868, 32 S. W. 755.

# Nuisance

(50) Nuisance. Although nuisance be public in its nature, an individual may recover damages caused by reason of it, if he allege and prove special injury to himself. Corley v. Lancaster, 81 Ky. 171, 5 R. 39.

# Obligors

(51) Obligor in name of obligee. One of several joint and several obligors may pay off the note, and, by agreement with the obligee, reserve the right to sue the other obligors in the name of the obligee. Smith v. Latimer, 15 B. M. 75.

# Parties

(52) Parties-names of to be stated. The full names of all the parties, plaintiff and defendant, and the fiducial or other character in which they sue, or are sued, should be stated in the body of the petition; and, if partners, the full name of all the members of the firm should be stated. Newman's Pleadings and Practice, pages 218, 287; Quinn v. N. N. & M. V. Co., 15 R. 74, 22 S. W. 223.

# Partnership Settlement

(53) Partnership settlement-to correct. After the settlement of a partnership, in an action by one of the partners attacking it for fraud or mistake, he must allege the specific act of fraud or the particular mistakes relied on. A general averment of fraud and mistake is not sufficient. Loesser v. Loesser, 81 Ky. 139, 4 R. 942.

# Petition Provisions

(54) Petition -provisions concerning. A pleading may contain as many causes of action, legal and equitable, as there may be grounds for on behalf of the pleader (section 113-2); but, if more than one, each one must be stated in a separate, numbered paragraph (section 113-3). The statements of the pleading must be consistent, and not inconsistent, with those of any other pleading previously filed by the same party, unless he allege in the alternative the existence of one or another fact, and state that one, but which one he does not know, is true (section 113-4). The pleading must be signed by the party or attorney (section 115), and need not state facts of which judicial notice is taken, or the evidence (section 119-1). Private statute, how pleaded (section 119-2), and judgment of court (section 122).

# Content Requirements

(55) Petition must contain in its own body and not merely by reference to another paper a statement of the facts constituting the cause of action. Corbin v. Oldham, 1 R. 327; Goff v. Janeway, 26 R. 525, 82 S. W. 267.

(56) The petition must state in ordinary and concise language the facts constituting the cause of action, and every fact necessary to enable the plaintiff to recover must be alleged, and every essential averment required to make a declaration good at common law must be stated. The facts must be so alleged as to enable the opposite party to know what is meant to be proved, and also that an issue may be formed in regard to the subject-matter in dispute and to enable the court to pronounce the law upon the facts as stated. L. & P. Canal Co. v. Murphy, 9 Bush 522; Stivers v. Baker, 87 Ky. 508; 10 R. 523, 9 S. W. 491; 14 B. M. 83; 7 Bush 532; 6 Bush 532.

# Prayer of Petition

(57) Prayer of petition. The petition stated the amount of claims sued on, and prayer was for judgment for the amount of said claims, with interest due on each. Held sufficient to entitle plaintiff to

# Petition

# Title VII

judgment for claims and interest. Harris v. Perry, 2 Bush 101; and see further as to prayer for relief, 80 Ky. 529, 4 R. 470; 80 Ky. 371, 4 R. 255: Cum. T. & T. Co v. City of Hickman, 118 S. W. 311.

(58) The body of petition and prayer must be consistent, and the prayer for general relief must be consistent with that specifically prayed for. Crow v. O. & N. R. R., 82 Ky. 134, 6 R. 6; Allsmiller v. Freutchnect, 86 Ky. 198, 9 R. 509, 5 S. W. 746; Rochester v. Anderson, Little, S. C. 144.

(59) A party is not entitled to relief that he does not ask for in his pleading. Radiord v. Southern Mutual Insurance Co., 12 Bush 434; Howland v. Brown, 13 Bush 681; Duff v. Combs, 117 S. W. 259, 132 Ky. 710; 11 Bush 49; and if petition contains no prayer for relief the court can not grant any. Bowman v. Ray, 25 R. 2131, 80 S. W. 516; but where defense is made and there is a general prayer for relief the plaintiff may recover what the evidence shows he is entitled to. Bank v. Coke, 20 R. 291, 45 S. W. 867; Travelers Ins. Co. v. Henderson Mills, 27 R. 653, 85 S. W. 1090, 120 Ky. 218; Harbison-Walker Ref. Co. v. McFarland's Admr., 156 Ky. 44, 160 S. W. 798.

(60) In an action to enforce a mortgage lien the prayer was, "Wherefore plaintiff prays that the mortgage be foreclosed, and for all other proper relief," no defense being made it was error to render a personal judgment. Hansford v. Holdam, 14 Bush 210.

(61) Prayer of petition in action to enjoin collection of judgment "for all proper relief" did not authorize judgment against defendant for amounts plaintiff claimed to be entitled to. Walker v. Thomas, 88 Ky. 486, 11 R. 20, 11 S. W. 434. If no defense is made plaintiff can not have judgment for any relief not specifically demanded. Miller v. Allen, 104 Ky. 114, 20 R. 463, 46 S. W. 523; and see further as to relief that may be granted under prayer for "general relief," Lillard v. Brannin, 91 Ky. 511, 13 R. 74, 16 S. W. 349; Bank v. Coke, 20 R. 291, 45 S. W. 867; 80 Ky. 529, 4 R. 470; 80 Ky. 371, 4 R. 255. Alternative relief, see 9 R. 166.

(62) When the defendant answers and there is a trial on the merits a liberal rule should be applied in determining the sufficiency of the prayer to authorize the relief granted. Abernathy v. Myers, 99 S. W. 942, 30 R. 844; and where issue is joined in a suit in equity the court may under a prayer for general relief grant any relief to which the parties show themselves entitled. Heckling v. Gehring, 100 S. W. 824, 30 R. 1198; Illinois Cent. R. Co. v. Davidson, 115 S. W. 770; Alexander v. Owen Co., 124 S. W. 386, 136 Ky. 420; Harbison-Walker Ref. Co. v. McFarland's Admr., 156 Ky. 44, 160 S. W. 798; Ky. River Con. Coal Co. v. Frazier, 161 Ky. 374, 170 S. W. 986.

(63) The body of the petition and the prayer should both be looked to, and a deficiency in one may be supplied by the averments of the other. Brashears v. Brashears, 110 S. W. 303; Bradley v. Sears, 138 Ky. 230, 127 S. W. 782. Where there are two or more paragraphs and in each the same character of relief is sought a prayer at the end of the last paragraph will answer for all the paragraphs. L. & N. R. Co. v. Adams, 148 Ky. 513, 147 S. W. 384.

(64) Paragraphing. See section 113 and notes thereto. Dailey v. O'Brien, 96 S. W. 521, 29 R. 811.

(65) Personal injuries-description of in petition. Louisville R. Co. v. Gaugh, 118 S. W. 276, 133 Ky. 467; C. N. O. & T. P. R. Co. v. Bennettee, 119 S. W. 181, 134 Ky. 19; Maysville R. Co. v. Willis, 104 S. W. 1016, 31 R. 1251.

(66) Precedent condition. At common law the pleader was required not only to aver the performance of the condition precedent, "but to state the time and manner of its performance. This rule was changed by sec. 149 of Code of 1854. Sec. 149 is omitted from this Code, and its omission restores the common law rule. Averbeck v. Hall, 14 Bush 505; Ormsby v. City of Lou., 79 Ky. 197, 2 R. 297. The petition must aver the performance of all conditions precedent in the contract when the liability of defendant depends on their performance. Without such averments, no cause of action exists. Frye v. L. & B. S. R. R. Co., 2 Met. 314; Escott v. White, 10 Bush 169.

(66a) Subsequent condition. In an action on a contract where the things to be done by the plaintiff are subsequent in point of time to the things done by the defendant, the plaintiff need not aver performance; non-performance is matter of defense. Hall v. Inter. Lib. Union of the World, 161 Ky. 299, 170 S. W. 631.

(67) Promissory note - immaterial omissions. A petition founded on a note must so set out the promise, its terms and its breach as to enable the court to render a judgment by default without being compelled to resort to the note on file; a promise or agreement to pay must be averred or petition will not be good.

Title IIJ
# Petition

[§ 90]

Huffaker v. Nat. Bank of Monticello, 12 Bush 287; Davis v. Moxey, 19 R. 178, 39 S. W. 1116 Moore v. Moxey, 19 R. 160, 39 S. W. 420; see Hibler v. Shipp, 78 Ky. 64; Bank of Anderson v. Foster, 146 Ky. 179, 142 S. W. 225. In Totten v. Cooke, 2 Met. 275, it is held that the failure to state date of note sued on and filed with petition does not make petition defective, if it state amount of note, time it is payable and aver that defendant is indebted in amount of note, naming it, and see Burton v. White, i Bush 9. The accidental omission of the words "the defendants," the subject of the verb "agreed," is not a substantial defect. The omission of those things which are silently expressed is of no consequence. Warfield v. Gardner, 79 Ky. 583, 3 R. 423; and see Hibler v. Shipp, 78 Ky. 64; Guigley Arteburn, 17 R. '565, 32 S. W. 165; Graham v. Louisville City Bank, 103 Ky. 641, 20 R. 295, 45 S. W. 870.

# (68)

The petition must state when note falls due, or so specify dates that the fact may be ascertained without going outside the pleading. Corbin v. Oldham. 1 R. 327.

# (69)

In an action on a note payable at a certain time and place it is not necessary to aver presentation and demand at such time and place-this is matter of defense. New South Co. v. Price, 21 R. 11, 50 S. W. 963.

# (70)

Slander. In action for slander, it is necessary to set forth the specific words complained of; it is not sufficient to state the effect of the words. Taylor v. Moran, 4 Met. 126. And it is necessary to aver that the words were spoken maliciously. Williams v. Gordon, 11 Bush 693. In an action for libel and slander, alienation of the affections of the spouse of plaintiff resulting from libel or slander may be pleaded as special damages, although the two causes could not be joined. A recovery in the action for libel or slander where such special damages as pleaded would be a bar to a new suit for alienation. Felty v. Felty, 164 Ky. 356, 175 S. W. 643; and see secs. 123, 124.

# (71)

Splitting cause of action not allowable, and where one has sued for part of an entire demand he will not be allowed to sue for remainder in another action. Pilcher v. Ligon, 91 Ky. 228, 12 R. 861, 15 S. W. 513; Weinstock v. Bellwood, 12 Bush 139; Powell v. Weiler, 11 B. M. 187; Cole v. I. C. R. R., 27 R. 1087, 87 S. W. 1082; and this rule applies to actions to recover land. Dills v. Justice, 137 Ky. 822, and see 150 Ky. 564.

# (72)

State. Action against agencies of the government when allowable. Herr v. Central Asylum, 97 Ky. 458, 17 R. 320, 30 S. W. 971.

# (73)

Surety against cosurety. Action cannot be maintained by one surety against a cosurety unless the principal is insolvent, and this fact must be averred. Bolling v. Doneghy, 1 Duv. 220; Lee v. Forman, 3 Met. 113.

# (74)

Surety against principal. In action by surety against principal to recover money paid for him the petition alleged that the note was paid in full by plaintiff as surety, and that no part of it has been repaid by defendant. The petition set out a good cause of action on the implied promise. Bridges v. Reed, 9 Bush 329.

# (75)

Special damages-necessary averments in action to recover. I. C. R. Co. v. Beeler, 142 Ky. 777, 135 S. W. 305; L. & N. R. Co. v. Dickey, 31 R. 895, 104 S. W. 329; Lex. Ry. Co. v. Johnson, 139 Ky. 328, 122 S. W. 830. Damages of an unusual and extraordinary nature, not the necessary result of the wrong complained of, must be pleaded as special damages in the petition; an omission to plead them cannot be supplied by a bill of particulars. Town of Erlanger v. Cody, 158 Ky. 625, 166 S. W. 245.

# (76)

Tax title. Sheriff's deed is prima facie evidence of regularity of sale. Husbands v. Polivick, 29 R. 890, 96 S. W. 825; Alexander v. Aud, 121 Ky. 105, 28 R. 68, 88 S. W. 1103; Hughes v. Owens, 29 R. 140, 92 S. W. 595.

# (77)

Title-to quiet-See Campbell v. Disney, 93 Ky. 41, 13 R. 919, 18 S. W. 1027; Smith v. White, 19 R. 802, 41 S. W. 436; Packard v. Beaver Valley Co., 96 Ky. 249, 16 R. 451, 28 S. W. 779; Henderson v. Clark, 163 Ky. 192, 173 S. W. 367.

# (78)

Trespass. No one except the person in actual possession can maintain an action for trespass. Walden v. Conn, 84 Ky. 312, 8 R. 281; J. M. & I. R. R. Co. v. Esterle, 13 Bush 667. Actual possession not necessary now to maintain action. See Ky. Stat., sec. 2361. In an action for trespass on land, the petition need not state a legal title in the plaintiff. Stephens v. Stephens, 165 Ky. 353, 176 S. W. 1137.

# (79)

Vendor's lien-to enforce. The petition must set forth the terms of the contract, the character of title to be made, and that the vendor is able and willing to convey according to the contract. Calvin v. Duncan, 12 Bush 101; Moore v. Moxey, 19 R. 160, 39 S. W. 420;

# Petition

Williams v. Abrahams, 3 Bush 186; 79 Ky. 465, 3 R. 271; 78 Ky. 607, and should describe land sought to be subjected. Lawless v. Barger, 9 Bush 665; Runyon v. Darnall, 10 Bush 67; and see secs. 125, 692, 694, and notes.

# (80) Warranty in sale of land.

Action for breach of, see Mercantile Trust Co. v. South Park Co., 94 Ky. 271, 15 R. 70, 22 S. W. 314; Jones v. Jones, 87 Ky. 82, 9 R. 942, 7 S. W. 886; Elliott v. Saufley, 89 Ky. 52, 10 R. 958, 11 S. W. 200; Graham v. Dyer, 16 R. 541, 29 S. W. 34C.

# (81) Writing should be set out.

If action is founded on a writing it should be filed, and so much of it set forth as will show that the plaintiff by reason of alleged acts or omissions on his part, and on part of defendants, is entitled to relief; the petition must contain in its own body and not merely by reference to another paper a statement of facts constituting cause of action. Hill v. Barrett, 14 B. M. 83; Murphy v. Estes, 6 Bush 532; 14 B. M. 252; 4 Met. 97. The plaintiff cannot rely on a written contract to support his action unless he has it set up in his pleading. Thompson v. Eversole, 162 Ky. 836, 173 S. W. 165; and see note 18, and sec. 120.

# (82) Writing filed with petition will not cure defects in petition.

Huffaker v. Nat. Bank of Monticello, 12 Bush 287; Gebhard v. Garnier, 12 Bush 321; 6 Bush 532; 4 Met. 97; 2 Met. 88; 80 Ky. 368, 4 R. 192, and see note 18.

# (83) If petition does not aver that the contract is in writing, or refer to any writing.

It will be presumed it is parol. Byassee v. Reese, 4 Met, 371; Hocker v. Gentry, 3 Met. 463; 15 B. M. 443; 14 Bush 776.

# (84) Upon undertaking to pay debt of another, petition must show undertaking was in writing.

Smith v. Fah, 15 B. M. 443; Linn Boyd Co. v. Terrill, 13 Bush 463; Smith v. Theobald, 86 Ky. 141, 9 R. 449, 5 S. W. 394; and see Ky. Stat. sec. 470.

# (85) An exhibit filed as the foundation of the action controls the averments of the petition.

Kalfus v. Davie's Ex., 164 Ky. 390, 175 S. W. 652.

# (86) Failure to file a writing under Civil Code 120 is no ground for demurrer.

Com. v. Hume, 155 Ky. 475, 159 S. W. 966.

# (87) Trespass.

A petition which failed to describe the real estate on which a trespass was charged to have been committed was fatally defective. Hackney v. Dillan, 9 Ky. Opinions 38.

# (88) Land, recovery of.

In an action for the recovery of land it is not necessary for the petition to show how the title was derived. War Fork Land Co. v. Spivey, 162 Ky. 600, 172 S. W. 1042.

# (89) Will, construction of.

A petition seeking to obtain the proper construction of a will in a case in which there was a real controversy between the parties in interest stated a cause of action. Hanna v. Prewitt, 153 Ky. 310, 155 S. W. 726.

# (90) Corporation, suit on note of.

In an action against a corporation on a promissory note, executed in the name of the corporation, no allegation was made as to the officer who had executed it, nor as to his authority. The petition was good on demurrer. Williamsburg Canning Co. v. Delaney, 158 Ky. 649, 166 S. W. 192.

# (91) Death from negligence.

In an action under Kentucky Statutes 6, it is unnecessary to allege that the decedent did not know of the danger or defects. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Greenwell's Admr., 155 Ky. 799, 160 S. W. 479.

# (92) Reformation of pleadings on appeal.

Where the record is so imperfect that the court cannot determine with fairness to the litigants what judgment should be entered, the case will be remanded to reform the pleadings and take such evidence as may be necessary to present clearly the issues between the parties, so that justice may be done. Nickels v. Collins, 158 Ky. 117, 164 S. W. 984.

Title VII] Demurrer. [5 91-92

# CHAPTER III.

# DEMURRER.

# § 91 [120, 121] [Special and general.]

Demurrers are special or general. (Form of, Forms 51-54.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 193, 540, 560.

# § 92 [120, 121] [Special demurrer defined—waiver—costs.]

A special demurrer is an objection to a pleading which shows-

1. [Court no jurisdiction.] That the court has no jurisdiction of the defendant or of the subject of the action; or,
2. Error as to proceeding. That without prejudice. Walton v. Washburn, 23 R. 1008, 64 S. W. 634; Sec. 371.
3. Special demurrer not necessary when plaintiff has no right at all to maintain action; question can be raised by general demurrer. L. & N. R. R. v. Brantley, 96 Ky. 297, 16 R. 691, 28 S. W. 477.
4. A special demurrer reaches only defects apparent on the face of the petition. Louisville & N. R. Co. v. Stewart, 163 Ky. 164, 173 S. W. 757.
5. Where the court has jurisdiction of the subject-matter, objection to the want of jurisdiction over the parties may be waived. Hughes v. Hardesty, 13 Bush 364; 4 Bush 619; 80 Ky. 212, 3 R. 746.
6. In action in the name of A. J. Morrison & Co. on a note executed to A. J. Morrison & Co., it does not appear from the petition whether A. J. M. & Co. is one person or a firm, and demurrer will not lie. Morrison v. Tate, I Met. 569.
7. If defect does not appear in petition, it must be taken advantage of by answer, or it will be waived. "Sec. 118; 17 B. M. 598; 3 Met. 134; 3 Bush 200; 80 Ky. 684. 4 R. 682; 79 Ky. 583, 3 R. 423; and filing a general demurrer is a waiver of objection for defect of parties. New Bell Jellico Co. v. Braznell's Admr., 149 Ky. 418. 149 S. W. 888.

# § 93 [General defined—costs.]

# § 94 [Amendment after demurrer—costs.]

# Demurrer.

1. [Plaintiff no legal capacity to sue.] That the plaintiff has not legal capacity to sue; or,
2. [Another action pending.] That another action is pending in this State, between the same parties, for the same cause; or,
3. [Defect of parties--waiver-costs.] That there is a defect of parties, plaintiff or defendant. Either of said grounds of objection, shown to exist by a pleading, is waived, unless distinctly specified by a demurrer thereto, except the objection to the jurisdiction of the court of the subject of the action, which objection is not waived by failing so to make it; but a party failing so to make it when or before he files a pleading, other than a demurrer, is liable for all costs resulting from such failure. (May be filed in vacation, sec. 109; may be made to part of pleading, sec. 113; ground of shown by pleading, sec. 118.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 20a, 20d, 21, 52, 52b, 71, 190a, 193, 386, 387, 392d, 392h, 393, 393a, 540, 541, 544, 547, 548, 571, 574, 741, 801, 879, 905; Forms Nos. 165-170, 179, 407-409.

was transferred to Clinton County. While pending in the court of latter county, A brought suit in Wayne and obtained an attachment for the same property. The petition showed that the action in Clinton was pending, and that the same property was sought to be recovered in each. A demurrer to petition in Wayne was properly sustained. Moore v. Sheppard, '1 Met. 97. But action to enforce lien and action on note secured by the lien may be prosecuted at the same time. Julian v. Pilcher, 2 Duv. 254, and see sec. 376.

An action will not be abated on a plea that there is an action pending between the same parties on the same debt in a court of another State. The Code only applies to actions pending in this State. Davis v. Morton, 4 Bush 442. But the pendency of an action in the federal courts of this State will bar an action for the same cause in State courts. Wilson v. Milliken, 103 Ky. 165, 19 R. 1843, 44 S. W. 660. An action will be abated by reason of the pendency of another; but they must both be prosecuted for the same cause of action. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Ohio Val. Tie Co., 161 Ky. 212, 170 S. W. 633.
Pendency of an action in a court that has no jurisdiction does not prevent institution of action in proper court. Lou. Water Co. v. Clark, 94 Ky. 47, 14 R. 886, 21 S. W. 246.
An objection that a former action is pending is removed by its dismissal or discontinuance even after plea of abatement in the second suit. Citizens Bank v. Forman, 111 Ky. 206, 23 R. 613, 63 S. W. 454; Wilson v. Milliken, 103 Ky. 165, 19 R. 1843, 44 S. W. 660.
Verification-misjoinder. Objection that petition not verified cannot be made by demurrer. Harris v. Ray, 15 B. M. 628; it should be by rule to verify. Nor can misjoinder of parties be reached by demurrer. Dean v. English, 18 B. M. 132. An objection to the misjoinder of causes of action cannot be raised by demurrer to the jurisdiction L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Mitchell, 162 Ky. 253, 172 S. W. 527; see sec. 85.
Waiver. See sub-sec. 4 of this section, and sec. 118 and notes thereto.
Waiver. Although action is brought in a county other than the one in which it should be under sec. 62 the objection to the jurisdiction will be waived if not made in proper manner and time. Gillen v. Illinois Cent. R. R. Co., 125 S. W. 1047, 137 Ky. 375.
Objection to jurisdiction may be made in separate paragraph of answer and will not enter appearance. Louisville Home Tel. Co. v. Beeler. 101 S. W. 397, 31 R. 23, 125 Ky. 366. Under this section and sec. 1ls the general appearance of the defendant in a bastardy proceedings, before making objection to the jurisdiction of the court, does not waive such objection. Com. v. Adkins, 169 Ky. 487, 184 S. W. 372. A petition did not show whether the county in which the action was brought was or was not the proper county in which to bring it; the question of jurisdiction could not be.

Title VII Demurrer

# § 93 [121] [General demurrer defined-costs.]

1. A general demurrer is an objection to a pleading because it does not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action or a defense, or because it does not state facts sufficient to support a cause of action or a defense.

2. [Effect of failure to demur-costs.] Failure so to make such objection is not a waiver thereof, but a party failing to make it when or before he files a pleading, other than a demurrer, shall be liable for all costs resulting from such failure. (May be filed in vacation, sec. 109; may be made to part of pleading, sec. 113.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 193, 386, 387, 540, 541, 543, 550, 561, 574, 741; Forms Nos. 180, 181, 410.

raised by demurrer. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Stewart, 163 Ky. 164, 173 S. W. 757.

(l5) Parties-defect of may be taken advantage of by special demurrer and ruling of lower court is subject to review on appeal. Klasener v. Robinson, 100 S. W. 255, 30 R. 1032; but objection will be waived if not made in trial court. Haley v. Cochran, 102 S. W. 852, 31 R. 505; Wileoxson v. Martin, 129 S. W. 96.

Where a petition or other pleading, on its face, shows a defect of parties, complaint of such defect may be presented by a special demurrer; but if such defect of parties does not so appear it should be disclosed by the answer or other pleading of the opposite party. Puckett v. Jameson, Sheriff, 157 Ky. 172, 162 S. W. 80l.

A voluntary unincorporated association cannot be sued in the name of the corporation; the point is waived unless raised by special demurrer or by answer where the facts do not appear upon the face of the petition. United Mine Work. of Amer. v. Cromer, 159 Ky. 605, 167 S. W. 89l.

The fact that in an action of an infant by a next friend the petition calls the next friend the plaintiff where it is evident that the intention is only to call him next friend, will not vitiate the proceedings; advantage may be taken of it by special demurrer. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Case, 158 Ky. 594, 165 S. W. 968.

(16) Objection that plaintiff did not have capacity to sue cannot be made by general demurrer. L. & N. R. Co. v. Herndon, 104 S. W. 732, 31 R. 1059, 126 Ky. 589.

(17) Objection, how made-If the petition does not disclose the want of jurisdiction the question should be raised by pleading as provided in sec. 118. Richardson v. L. & N. R. Co., 112 S. W. 582, 129 Ky. 449.

A special demurrer cannot raise objection to the jurisdiction by reciting facts not shown by the petition. Louisville & N. R. Co. v. Mitchel, 162 Ky. 253, 172 S. W. 527, and sec note 15, supra.

# § 93. (1) Admissions by. Demurrer

admits the truth of all facts well pleaded, and every allowable and proper deduction therefrom. Norman v. Ky. Board of Managers, 93 Ky. 537, 14 R. 529, 20 S. W. 901; Morgan v. Ballard, 1 Mar. 558; Bank v. Newport, 1 B. M. 13; but exhibits referred to must be taken into view as controlling any statements inconsistent with them. "Bush v. Maderia, 14 B. M. 212; Collins v. Blackburn, 14 B. M. 252; and see note 18, sec. 90.

(2) The admission of facts by a demurrer is equivalent to proof by a witness in open court. Francis v. Wood, 81 Ky. 16, 4 R. 616; but only facts well pleaded are admitted and not legal conclusions. Norman v. Ky. Board of Managers, 93 Ky. 537, 14 R. 529, 20 S. W. 901.

# (3) Answer -effect of.

Defendant waives all objection to petition by answering except objection to jurisdiction, and that petition does not state cause of action; and upon demurrer to answer, no objection to petition except these can be considered. Mitchell v. Mattingly, 1 Met. 237 and see Schooler v. Yancy, 118 S. W. 940, 133 Ky. 695.

# (4) After demurrer to petition is overruled and answer filed, no objection to petition is available, unless it amounts to cause for arrest of judgment. Duncan v. Brown, 15 B. M. 186.

# (5) Where an answer is filed at same time with demurrer, the fact that parties proceed to trial without noticing demurrer authorizes inference that demurrer was overruled or waived. D. L. & N. Co. v. Stewart, 2 Met. 119.

# (6) Filing answer and demurrer at same time does not preclude defendant

# Demurrer

from waiving right to trial upon merits and standing by his demurrer. Bridges v. Reed, 9 Bush 329.

(7) Appearance entered by. Defendant by filing a general demurrer waives his right to thereafter object to the jurisdiction of the court over his person. McDowell v. C., O. & S. W. R. R. Co., 90 Ky. 346, 12 R. 331, 14 S. W. 338; Chapin v. Fulkerson, 95 Ky. 277, 15 R. 635, 24 S. W. 1066; but does not by making motion to quash return on summons or by filing an answer objecting to jurisdiction. C., O. & S. W. R. R. v. Heath, 87 Ky. 651, 10 R. 646, 9 S. W. 832; Barbour v. Newkirk, 83 Ky, 529, 7 R. 555; and see note 9, sec. 39.

(8) Contributory neglect is a defense which confesses and avoids the plaintiff's case, and as a rule must be affirmatively pleaded, but where the petition states facts showing that injury sued for was caused by neglect of plaintiff, it may be reached by demurrer. Favre v. L. & N. R. R., 91 Ky. 541, 13 R. 116, 16 S. W. 370.

(9) Costs on. The failure of a party to demur at proper time does not render him liable for costs unless he subsequently enters a demurrer. American Wire Co. v. Bavless, 91 Ky. 94, 12 R. 694, 15 S. W. 10. But if the defendant fails to demur to a defective petition and appeals from a judgment by default, he must pay the costs in the Court of Appeals. Moore v. Moxey, 19 R. 160, 39 S. W. 420; Bush v. Lou. Trust Co., 24 R. 2182, 73 S. W. 775; and see Combs v. Pridmore, 19 R. 1934, 43 S. W. 681: holding that a failure to demur renders defendant liable for all costs incurred by the failure.

(10) Defects in form. Defects in form of pleading can only be reached by motion to make averments more specific. Posey v. Green, 78 Ky. 162; Ib. 210; and see sec. 134.

(11) Although petition fails to show that party is entitled to the specific relief asked, if it shows a right to any relief a demurrer should not be sustained. Foster v. Watson, 16 B. M. 377.

(12) Setting out several defenses in one paragraph is not cause of demurrer; the objection can be reached by motion to paragraph. Williams v. Langford, 15 B. M. 566; see sec. 113-3. And if one of several paragraphs in an answer presents a defense, a demurrer should not be sustained to it as a whole. Archer v. Nat. Ins. Co., 2 Bush 226.

(13) Demurrer brings all previous pleadings before the court, and judgment on the demurrer should be against party who committed first error. Wile v. Sweeney, 2 Duv. 161; Young v. Duhme, 4 Met. 239; Martin v. McDonald, 14 B. M. 544; 1 Met. 237; 79 Ky. 487, 3 R. 273.

(14) Judgment on demurrer-bar. An order sustaining a demurrer cannot be appealed from unless a judgment follow it. Alexander v. de Kermel, 81 Ky. 345, 5 R. 382; Com. v. L. & N. R. R., 16 R. 484, 29 S. W. 331; but see Zable v. Orphans' Home, 92 Ky. 89, 13 R. 385, 17 S. W. 212.

(15) Judgment on demurrer, unless petition states cause of action, is no bar to future action alleging facts entitling plaintiff to recover. Birch v. Funk, 2 Met. 544; Pepper v. Donelly, 87 Ky. 259, 10 R. 140, 8 S. W. 441; Potter v. Benge, 24 R. 24, 67 S. W. 1005. But he will not be allowed, after setting forth a state of facts in his petition, whether sufficient or insufficient, to authorize a recovery after a general demurrer has been sustained to his pleading and his action dismissed, to bring another action setting forth substantially the same facts. Woolley v. Lou. Banking Co., 81 Ky. 527, 5 R. 562; Thomas v. Bland, 91 Ky. 1, 12 R. 640, 14 S. W. 955.

(16) Limitation. Demurrer to petition upon ground that action is barred by limitation cannot be sustained unless petition shows not only a sufficient lapse of time, but the non-existence of any ground of avoidance. Rankin v. Turney, 2 Bush 555; Board v. Jolly, 5 Bush 86; Chiles v. Drake, 2 Met. 146; Com. v. Gardner, 17 R. 75, 30 S. W. 413; Schneider v. Schlentker, 23 R. 951, 64 S. W. 505. But when the petition shows that the action is barred and that plaintiff is not within any of the exceptions contained in the statute which save his right to sue, a demurrer will be sustained. Stillwell v. Leavy, 84 Ky. 379, 8 R. 321, 1 S. W. 590.

(17) Misjoinder. That amended petition departs from cause of action is not ground for demurrer. Motion to strike out is proper way to reach misjoinder. Hord v. Chandler, 13 B. M. 402. See sec. 85, and sec. 113-4.

(18) Misnomer of writing. The misnomer of writing sued on is not cause of demurrer. Bradley v. Mason, 6 Bush 602.

(19) Objection in Court of Appeals to sufficiency of petition is confined to the point that it does not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action, and if the issue in lower court was made by answer or submitted to and tried by a jury, defects in petition must be disre-

Title VIIJ Demurrer.

# § 94 [122] [Amendment after demurrer—costs.]

If the court sustain the demurrer, the party may amend the pleading, with leave of court; but in all cases he shall pay the costs resulting from the demurrer.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 190, 560, 581, 601, 902; Forms Nos. 407, 410.

# Case References

regarded in Court of Appeals. Davis v. Wiley, 3 R. 315.

# (20) Petition, how tested.

On general demurrer to petition, its sufficiency should not be tested by blending with its averments any matter of defense defendant may have. Yewell v. Bradshaw, 2 Duv. 573; Macklin v. Trustees, 88 Ky. 592, 11 R. 75, 11 S. W. 657.

# (21) Practice.

A general demurrer to an answer containing several paragraphs is not good if either of the paragraphs presents a defense, but upon a demurrer to "each of the paragraphs" it is proper to sustain it as to those that are defective. Sanford v. Lowenthall, 5 R. 206.

# (22) Plaintiff filed two amended petitions.

and then defendant demurred to "whole case"; this, though inartificial, put in issue sufficiency of all facts set out in petition and amendments. Miles v. Collina, 1 Met. 308.

# (23) Where the court erroneously sustains a general demurrer

it is proper afterward to disregard the ruling. Stevenson v. Flournoy, 89 Ky. 561, 11 R. 745, 13 S. W. 210; and although a defendant does not demur to the petition the court has a right upon the final hearing to decide that no cause of action is presented. American Wire Nail Co. v. Bayless, 91 Ky. 94, 12 R. 694, 15 S. W. 10.

# (24) When demurrer to answer is carried back to petition

the petition is to be considered without reference to answer or exhibits filed with it, although a demurrer to petition has been overruled before filing of answer. Macklin v. Trustees, 88 Ky. 592, 11 R. 75, 11 S. W. 657.

# (24a) If the uncertainty in a pleading

is such that it states no cause of action or defense, a demurrer is proper. Reid v. Lyttle, 150 Ky. 304, 150 S. W. 357.

# (24b) In an action on an accident insurance policy

the petition failed to state that the death was accidental. Issues of fact were made and tried; but none was made upon this point. A judgment notwithstanding the verdict was proper; but the defendant was liable for costs accruing from the time its answer was filed. Farnsley's Admr. v. Phil. Life Ins. Co., 156 Ky. 699, 161 S. W. 111.

# (25) Special demurrer is not necessary

where plaintiff has no right at all to maintain action; objection can be made by general demurrer. L. & N. R. R. v. Brantley, 96 Ky. 297, 16 R. 691, 28 S. W. 477.

# (26) Statute of frauds.

When petition shows want of necessary writing, it is not necessary to plead statute of fraud; question can be raised by demurrer. Linn Boyd Co. v. Terrill, 13 Bush 463; Smith v. Theobald, 86 Ky. 141, 9 R. 449, 5 S. W. 394.

# (27) Practice-amended petition.

Where the whole cause of action is set out in an amended petition its sufficiency is to be determined by its averments alone. Robards v. Bannon Sewer Pipe Co., 130 Ky. 380, 113 S. W. 429.

# § 94.

1. Practice-court not required to offer party leave to amend, but party may ask for that privilege. Wilson v. Com., 99 Ky. 167, 18 R. 51, 35 S. W. 274; and see as to amendments, sec. 134 and notes.
2. Amended pleading-delay in tendering when not ground for dismissal for want of prosecution. Quinn v. Cincinnati R. Co., 97 S. W. 379, 30 R. 15.
