# CHAPTER IV.

# ANSWER, COUNTER-CLAIM, SET-OFF, CROSS-PETITION.

# § 95. Answer-what it may contain.

An answer may contain-

1. [Traverse.] A traverse.
2. [Facts constituting estoppel or avoidance.] A statement of facts which constitute an estoppel against, or avoidance of, a cause of action stated in the petition.

# ANSWER,

95. (1) Accord and satisfaction. To make the plea good, it should be alleged that the matter was accepted in satisfaction. Averment that the party agreed to accept it in satisfaction is bad pleading. Johnson v. Hunt, 81 Ky. 321, 5 R. 194; Hale v. Grogan, 99 Ky. 170, 18 R. 46, 35 S. W. 282. See Williams v. Langford, 15 B. M. 566.

Administrator against distributee. To a suit brought on a note by an administrator against husband of distributee, the answer stated that no distribution had been made, and asked to be allowed to retain amount as advancement; held not good because it did not aver that administrator had in his hands for distribution an amount sufficient to entitle each distributee to a sum equal to amount of note. Haggard v. Hay, 13 B. M. 174.

(3) Amended petition. No answer is required to amended petition when the denials of the answer to petition are sufficient, and apply as well to the amended as to the original petition. Robinson v. Williamson, 7 Bush 604.

(4) Appearance to action is entered by filing answer. Elliott Co. v. Kitchen, 14 Bush 289; and see further notes to sec. 39. as to what will enter appearance.

(5) Attachment without cause. In action for suing out an attachment without good cause the answer was "that the grounds alleged by the defendant as constituting the cause for its issual did in fact, as he believes, exist and were true;" held insufficient. Mitchell v. Mattingly, 1 Met. 237; see further, Ky. Stat., sec. 7.

(6) Conclusions of law. In action to recover homestead, statement in answer that defendant is an "innocent purchaser" is merely a conclusion of law. Wing v. Hayden, 10 Bush 276.

(7) In action against carrier for loss of goods, averment in answer that accident was unforeseen, inevitable or unavoidable, or that it was caused by dangers of the river, are conclusions of law and not good; the facts should be stated. Bentley v. Bustard, 16 B. M. 643.

(8) In action against sheriff for failing to collect execution, answer stating that all the property owned by execution defendant was exempt from execution, without averring that he was a housekeeper with a family, is bad. Com. v. Lay, 12 Bush 283.

Conjunctive denial-grouping facts. Denial in the conjunctive is not good. Taylor v. Farmer, 81 Ky. 458, 5 R. 487; nor can two or more facts be grouped together and denied; each material fact must be separately denied. Preston v. Roberts, 12 Bush 570; Morgan v. Booth, 13 Bush 480; Stephens v. Wilson, 115 Ky. 27, 24 R. 1832, 72 S. W. 336; Johnson v. Asher, 105 S. W. 943, 32 R. 31.

(10) Contributory neglect—how it may be pleaded. C. & O. R. R. v. Smith, 101 Ky. 104, 18 R. 1079, 39 S. W. 832; reply is necessary to plea of, L. & N. R. R. v. Mayfield, 18 R. 224, 35 S. W. 924; L. & N. R. R. v. Copas, 95 Ky. 460, 16 R. 14, 26 S. W. 179; Gore v. II. Central R. R., 17 R. 799, 32 S. W. 754.

(11) Decedent's estate. In suit for settlement of an estate, creditor may file petition setting up his claim, and if claim

# Title IIj

# Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition.

[§ 95]

1. [Set-off or counter-claim.] A statement of facts which constitute a set-off or counter-claim.
2. [Cross-petition.] A cross-petition. (Forms of answers, Forms 55-79; time of filing, secs. 102, 104; may be filed in vacation, secs. 107, 108; traverse defined, sec. 113; what allegations must be traversed, sec. 126. See further, Practice Act, sec. 367a.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 397, 402, 413, 418, 425, 426f, 682; Forms Nos. 182, 184-258, 259-268.

is controverted, has a right to demand that it shall be done by answer, as in all other cases. Horner v. Harris, 10 Bush 357; Callender v. Callender, 24 R. 1145, 70 S. W. 844.

(12) Defective allegations in petition cured by answer or verdict. It is a settled rule of practice that defective allegations of the plaintiff's pleading may be supplied and cured by the answer; and the doctrine of the Code is that verdicts and judgments which have been rendered upon substantial issues of fact fairly presented by the pleadings shall not be disturbed on account of mere technical defects not affecting the merits of the controversy. Code, sec. 134. Riggs v. Maltby, 2 Met. 88 C. & O. R. R. v. Thieman, 96 Ky. 507, 16 R. 611, 29 S. W. 357; the failure to allege a fact that would have rendered the petition bad on demurrer will be cured by an answer denying the existence of the fact. L. & N. R. R. v. Lawson, 88 Ky. 496, 11 R. 38, 11 S. W. 511; Pugh v. White, 73 Ky. 210; 78 Ky. 435; 78 Ky. 463; 13 Bush 601; 82 Ky. 291, 6 R. 341; but if the petition fail to allege matter which is essential to make out a cause of action, the defect will not be cured by answer. Bogenschutz v. Smith, 84 Ky. 330, 8 R. 376; Drake v. Semonin, 82 Ky. 291, 6 R. 341; C. & O. R. R. v. Thierman, 96 Ky. 507, 16 R. 611, 29 S. W. 357.

(13) Defense by one that goes to merits of action presents defense for all. Rouse v. Howard, 1 Duv. 31; Williams v. Rogers, 14 Bush 776.

(4) Defenses to be pleaded. The Code requires all defenses, equitable as well as legal, to be pleaded to an action at law; and an equitable right available may be lost unless thus litigated. Hackett v. Schad, 3 Bush 353; sec. 113-2.

(15) Denial of knowledge or information. Want of knowledge or information sufficient to form a belief is not sufficient denial of the alleged presentation, demand, refusal of payment and protest of a bill, when the bill showing these facts is filed with petition. Griddler v. F. and D. Bank, 12 Bush 333; Ib. 287. Nor is denial that note was duly protested, or that party received legal notice of protest, sufficient. 7 Bush 474; 12 Bush 287. Denial of knowledge or information by a personal representative is equivalent to a direct denial. Drake v. Semonin, 82 Ky. 291, 6 R. 341; and see sec. 113-7.

(16) When the record as made up when plea is filed furnishes the necessary information, or when the fact is necessarily within the personal knowledge of the party, the plea of want of knowledge or information is not good. Barrett v. Godshaw, 12 Bush 592; Ib. 333; Augustus v. Holt, 13 R. 8, 15 S. W. 1064; McClure v. Bigstaff, 18 R. 601, 37 S. W. 294; Lucas

# Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition

(23) In an action on a note executed for price of a slave, statement in answer "that the vendor had no title and had not and could not make any title" does not present a defense, as it fails to aver any warranty of title. Patrick v. Swinney, 5 Bush 421.

(17) Denial of knowledge sufficient to form a belief is not good; it should be a denial of any knowledge or information. Trustees v. Fleming, 10 Bush 234, and see C. & S. R. R. Co, v. Gray, 10 R. 292, 8 S. W. 876.

(18) That defendant "knows nothing on the subject of his own knowledge" is not sufficient. Terrill v. Jennings, I Met. 450. That defendant has "no knowledge of any of the facts set up in the petition, and no means of forming a belief, and therefore denies them all and requires proof," is not good denial. Corbin v. Com., 2 Met. 380. Statement that defendant does not admit certain facts and calls for proof, or does not owe debt sued for, is not sufficient denial. Clark v. Finnell, 16 B. M. 329. See Hutchins v. Moore, 4 Met. 109; and answer which merely denies "each averment of the petition" is no answer at all. Evans v. Evans, 93 Ky. 510, 14 R. 628, 20 S. W. 605.

(19) An answer to suit for goods sold and delivered, denying "that defendant is, to the best of his knowledge and belief, indebted to the plaintiff as stated in petition," is insufficient. Francis v. Francis, 18 B. M. 57; 8 Bush 583. Nor is a denial that defendant is indebted as alleged, or that account is just, sufficient. Thruston v. Oldham, 6 Bush 16.

(20) It is only where the allegations of the petition are not necessarily within the knowledge of the party charged, that this character of denial is good; as where the party is sought to be made liable for the action or misconduct of others, or where some fact is asserted that defendant could not say whether it is true or false. Wing v. Dugan, 8 Bush 583; 12 Bush 605. Questions of law cannot be denied by a statement of want of knowledge or information sufficient to form a belief. Ky. Nav. Co. v. Com., 13 Bush 435; 83 Ky. 410, 7 R. 419.

(21) In action against carrier for neglect in carrying goods a specific denial of its negligence is necessary. N., C. & St. L. R. R. v. Carrico, 95 Ky. 489, 16 R. 66, 26 S. W. 177.

(22) Denial of title. When a vendor sues to enforce an executory contract, averring that he is able, ready and willing to convey title, and tenders conveyance, vendee must aver he has no title; or, if it is defective, must point out defects. Logan v. Bull, 78 Ky. 607.

(30) Legislature no power to prescribe form of answer that cuts off substantial defenses by limiting defendant to certain defenses. City of Lou. v. Cochran, 82 Ky. 15, 5 R. 833; Meguiar v. Henry, 84 Ky. 1, 7 R. 695.

(31) Limitation. Statute of limitation must be pleaded, although action be apparently barred, unless the petition shows the non-existence of every ground of.

Title VII! Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition.

# § 96 [126, 128] [Counter-claim, set-off, cross-petition.]

1. [Counter-claim, defined.] A counter-claim is a cause of action in favor of a defendant against a plaintiff, or against him and another, which arises out of the contract, or transactions, stated in the petition as the foundation of the plaintiff's claim, or which is connected with the subject of the action. (See further, secs. 99, 372. See as to notice when lien on real property is asserted, Ky. Stat., sec. 2358a.)

avoidance. Chiles v. Drake, 2 Met. 146; son v. City of Mayfield, 93 Ky. 230, 14 R. 370, 19 S. W. 598.

(40) Traverse. Every material allegation of a pleading must, for the purposes of the action, be taken as true, unless specifically traversed (sec. 126). A material allegation is one which is necessary for the statement or support of a cause of action or defense (sec. 127). A traverse is a denial of facts alleged in an adverse pleading. See sec. 113-7; and each material fact must be denied separately; it will not do to group two or more facts together and deny them. Preston v. Roberts, 12 Bush 570; Morgan v. Booth, 13 Bush 480; nor is a denial in the conjunctive good. 81 Ky. 458, 5 R. 487.

(34) Non est factum. Plea of non est factum must be made in affirmative language and sworn to. Trustees v. Fleming, 10 Bush 234; Dugan v. Harris, 6 R. 596. As to sufficiency of plea when made by a surety, see Garrett v. Ashcraft, 19 R. 38, 39 W. 51; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 473.

(35) Under general plea of non est factum, a question as to the conditional signing and delivery of a writing cannot be considered; it must be specially pleaded. Hall v. Smith, 14 Bush 604.

(36) Nul tiel record. Plea of nul tiel record is not good in an action in this State on the judgment of a justice of the peace of another State. McElfatrick v. Taft, 10 Bush 160.

(37) Payment. Plea of payment does not require a reply. Logan County Bank v. Barclay, 104 Ky. 97, 20 R. 773, 46 S. W. 675; Ermert v. Deitz, 19 R. 1639, 14 S. W. 138.

(38) Statute of frauds. It is not necessary to answer and rely on the statute of frauds when petition shows want of necessary writing; question can be made by demurrer. Linn Boyd Co. v. Terrill, 13 Bush 463; Smith v. Theobald, 86 Ky. 141, 9 R. 449, 5 S. W. 394.

# COUNTER-CLAIM.

(39) Taxes. Set-off is not allowable against demand for taxes unless expressly authorized by statute. Anderson.

§ 96. (1 Counter-claim." First, those causes of action which arise out of the

# § 96] Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition.

# 2. [Set-off defined.]

A set-off is a cause of action arising upon a contract, judgment or award in favor of a defendant against a plaintiff, or against him and another; and it cannot be pleaded except in an action upon a contract, judgment or award. (See further, secs. 97, 372, 377. See as to notice when lien on real property is asserted, Ky. Stat., sec. 2358a.)

# 3. [Cross-petition defined.]

A cross-petition is the commencement of an action by a defendant against a co-defendant, or a person who is not a party to the action, or against both; or by a plaintiff against a co-plaintiff, or a person who is not a party to the action, or against both; and is not allowed to a defendant, except upon a cause of action which affects, or is affected by, the original cause of action; nor to a plaintiff, except upon a cause of action which affects, or is affected by, a set-off or counter-claim. (In what pleadings counter-claim and set-off allowed. sec. 111; trial of, sec. 372; judgment on, sec. 387; in what pleading cross-petition allowed, secs. 111, 692. See as to notice when lien on real property is asserted, Ky. Stat., sec. 2358a.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 456, 457, 458, 463, 467, 468, 475c, 475d, 477, 480, 485, 486, 487, 490, 494, 512c, 755, 816, 832, 833, 850, 854; Forms Nos. 259-268, 269, 271.

contract set forth in the plaintiff's petition as the foundation of his claim; secondly, those causes of action arising out of the transaction set forth in the petition as the foundation of the plaintiff's claim; and, thirdly, those causes of action connected with the subject of the suit." Newman's Pleading and Practice, page 602. Counter-claim is an action (sec. 732-34) and may be presented in a reply against a set-off (sec. 98-3). The caption of the answer should contain the words "answer and counter-claim" (sec. 97-4). Nutter v. Johnson, 80 Ky. 426, 4 R. 305; Cason v. Cason, 79 Ky. 558, 3 R. 367. The only restriction the Code makes as to a counter-claim is, that it shall be a cause of action arising out of the contract or transaction set forth in the petition as the foundation of plaintiff's demand; or that it be connected with the subject of the action. It is not required that the counter-claim itself shall be founded in contract or arise out of the contract set forth in the petition, but it is sufficient that it arises out of the transactions set forth in the petition or be connected with the subject of the action. Allen v. Hodge, 106 S. W. 255, 32 S. W. 509; Tinsley v. Tinsley, 15 B. M. 454; Forbes v. Cooper, 88 Ky. 285, 10 R. 865, 11 S. W. 24; and see Rennebaum v. Atkinson, 103 Ky. 555, 20 R. 254, 45 S. W. 112.

Title VIIJ
# Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition.

[§ 96]

1. Of 1354 counter-claim was only allowed against plaintiffs.
2. In an action for the contract price of a bridge, the defendant can plead as a counter-claim damages for the failure of plaintiff to build the bridge according to contract. *Moore v. Caruthers, 17 B. M. 669; and see Hurst v. Combs, 17 R. 84, 30 S. W. 416; 10 Bush 169.*
3. M agreed that if H, as surety for L, would sign a note executed to him by L, he would procure the signature of G as co-surety. In a suit by M on the note, H pleaded as a counter-claim the damages he sustained by the failure of M to procure the signature of G. *Murphy v. Hubble, 2 Duv. 247.*
4. Purchaser of property at a sale by administrators cannot, in an action on the note executed for the property, plead as a counter-claim the conversion of part of the property by one of the administrators, after the sale, in his individual capacity. *Phillips v. Keifer, 2 Met. 478; Hanrock v. Hancock, 24 R. 664, 69 S. W. 757.*
5. In action by the mortgagor against the mortgagee for taking possession of the mortgaged property before debt due, the mortgagee can plead as a counter-claim the mortgage debt. *Brown v. Phillips, 3 Bush 656.*
6. In action by a railroad company on a subscription toward building the road, the defendant may plead as a counter-claim an agreement made at the time of the subscription that any damage done to his property by the road might be set-off against subscription, and the damage so caused is a valid counter-claim. *L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Thompson, 18 B. M. 735.*
7. In proceeding by mandamus to compel a county court to levy a tax to pay for a bridge, counter-claim cannot be set up to recover back money paid on bridge; but may be maintained for defective execution of the work as a defense against balance due. *Anderson Co. v. Stone, 18 B. M. 848.*
8. L sued J on a promise to indemnify him as surety of H to recover money paid as such surety. J pleaded as a counter-claim that "H had given L a mortgage to secure him in part of the money paid as surety, and asked that the mortgage be foreclosed. Held not a good counter-claim, not being connected with the subject of the action. *Jones v. Letcher, 13 B. M. 363.*
9. In a suit by a widow for dower against vendee of her husband, the vendee cannot, by counter-claim against the heirs of the vendor, recover on the warranty. *Hill v. Golden, 16 B. M. 551.*
10. G sold to C a mill and accounts due; one of the accounts being against M, he required C to deduct from it $190. In an action by G against C to recover the contract price, C pleaded as a counter-claim the reduction made to M. *Cook v. Gray, 2 Bush 121.*
11. A sued B and obtained an attachment. B controverted the grounds of attachment, and made his answer a counter-claim, claiming damages for maliciously suing out attachment. Held not a good counter-claim. *Nolle v. Thompson, 3 Met. 121.*
12. In action on foreign judgment, defendant can plead as a counter-claim a cause of action dismissed for want of prosecution, in the court where judgment rendered. *Rankin v. Barnes, 5 Bush 20.*
13. In action upon a note executed for an interest in a partnership, defendant can plead as a counter-claim damages resulting to the business of the firm by the fraud and dishonest conduct of the other partners. *Boughner v. Black, 83 Ky. 521, 7 R. 562.*
14. In an action for trespass to land, the plaintiff averred title in himself; the defendant alleged that he was owner of land, and sought by counter-claim to recover it. Held that he could maintain counter-claim. *Whitlock v. Ledford, 82 Ky. 390, 6 R. 387.*
15. In an action for an assault, defendant may plead as a counter-claim damages for an assault committed on him by plaintiff at same time as assault complained of in petition. *Slone v. Slone, 2 Met. 339; but a claim arising out of an unlawful act cannot be pleaded as a counter-claim. Sutton v. Morris, 102 Ky. 611, 19 R. 1654, 44 S. W. 127.*
16. In an action on an injunction bond to recover damages for being kept out of the possession of land, the defendant who had possession of the land during pendency of injunction can plead, as a counter-claim, any interference by plaintiff which rendered the use of the land less valuable to the defendant. *Tinsley v. Tinsley, 15 B. M. 454.*
17. In an action for trespass by A against B, the purchaser of A's land under a void execution, B having paid the execution debt, cannot plead the amount so paid as a counter-claim. *Geoghegan v. Ditto, 2 Met. 434.*
18. In action by wife for divorce she set up claim to a tract of land, her husband answered and averred that through mistake his name was omitted from deed.

# Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition

conveying land to wife and children, and his answer asking to have deed reformed was properly made a counter-claim against wife and children. *Grimes v. Grimes*, 88 Ky. 20, 10 R. 658, 9 S. W. 840.

(21) 'Surety in supersedeas bond may plead as a counter-claim to an action on the bond a claim of the principal in the bond against the person suing on it. *Parrish v. Ross*, 103 Ky. 33, 19 R. 1676, 44 S. W. 134.

(21a) In pleading a counter-claim the facts must be set up with the particularity which would have been required in a petition brought upon the same cause of action. (Subsection 1.) *Cross v. Snyder's Admx.*, 164 Ky. 370, 175 S. W. 64.

(21b) Where a guardian suing his ward to recover of the estate money advanced for the ward's education and support and for preservation of the estate, had been guilty of breach of trust, the guardian ad litem defending the ward can counter-claim for such neglect or breach of trust by the guardian, and ask for his removal. *Bell v. Bell's Gdn.*, 167 Ky. 430, 180 S. W. 803.

(21c) Where plaintiff sued to enjoin defendants from maintaining a ditch on the ground that it diverted the natural flow of water and injured plaintiff's land, it was held that since defendants' cause of action does not arise out of contract judgment or award, nor out of the transaction stated in the petition, it was not available as set-off or counter-claim. *Payne v. Vowels*, 171 Ky. 377, 188 S. W. 413.

(22) Partner when sued for a firm debt has the right to rely upon any counter-claim that the firm could have asserted if sued. *MeMasters v. Burnett*, 92 Ky. 358, 13 R. 617, 17 S. W. 1021.

(23) Requisites of. A pleading to be treated as a counter-claim should contain, not facts merely constituting a defense, and pleaded as such, but should present a cause of action in favor of defendant, supported by every allegation necessary to uphold an original petition founded thereon. *Bowen v. Sebree, 2 Bush 112; Pritchard v. Peace, 98 Ky. 99, 17 R. 662, 32 S. W. 296; Gordon v. City Nat. Bank, 140 Ky. 47, 130 S. W. 818; Davis v. Dycus*, 7 Bush 4; 4 Met. 57; Ib. 110.

(31) Unliquidated damages may be recovered on counter-claim. *Cranor Smith Lumber Co. v. Frith*, 118 S. W. 307.

(32) Attachment--damages caused by can not be pleaded as defense in action in which attachment was obtained. *Abernathy v. Meyer-Bridges Co.*, 99 S. W. 942, 30 R. 844.

# SET-OFF

(1) Administrator - distributee. Administrator or heirs may plead as set-off.

# Title VII] Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition

# [§ 96]

against distributee's interest his indebtedness to the estate. Brown v. Mattingly, 91 Ky. 275, 12 R. 869, 15 S. W. 353.

(1) Assignee against. A set-off to be available against an assignee must have existed before notice of the assignment. A debt or demand cannot be used as a set-off before it becomes due, and unless it is due before notice of the assignment, it is not available as a set-off against the assignee. A surety, until he pays the money for his principal, has no demand against him which can be pleaded as a set-off, even if the principal is insolvent. Walker v. McKay, 2 Met. 294; 1 Met. 112; and see Chenault v. Bush, 84 Ky. 528, 8 R. 490, 2 S. W. 160; Henderson N. Bk. v. Lagow, 3 R. 173; nor will a party who has notice that his note has been assigned, although he may not know to whom, be allowed to buy up claims against assignor and set them off against note. Small v. Browder, 11 B. M. 213; but the defendant may after suit is filed buy valid claims against the plaintiff and plead them as a set-off. Overby v. Wells, 21 R. 1316, 54 S. W. 955.

(2) Assignment of note to be used as. Defendant can plead as a set-off a note of the plaintiff assigned to him under a contract to use the note as a set-off, and account to the assignor only for the amount he can make in that way, and reassign the balance remaining unpaid. Graham v. Tilford, 1 Met. 112; Dorsey v. Reese, 14 B. M. 157; McBrayer v. Dean, 100 Ky. 398, 18 R. 847, 38 S. W. 508; and see Overby v. Wells, 21 R. 1316, 54 S. W. 955. But where a note is so assigned, to be used as a set-off, a court of equity, where the rights of others than the party who owes the note intervene, would be reluctant to sanction such a speculation by aiding the party making the venture to defeat an acknowledged equity. Such contracts are not to be favored, but where the debtor alone is affected, he will not be heard to complain. Tinley v. Martin, 80 Ky. 463, 4 R. 327.

(3) Commonwealth-action by. In a suit by the Commonwealth against a citizen, the latter may, by way of set-off, make such defense as will defeat recovery, but cannot get judgment over. Com. v. O. & N. R. R. Co., 81 Ky. 572, 5 R. 650; 9 Bush 708; and see Sinking Fund Comr. v. Northern Bank, 1 Met. 174.

(4) The Commonwealth by its judgment against the sheriff obtained a lien on the sheriff's claim against his deputy; the deputy, surety of the sheriff, has been compelled to pay money for him before the lien of the Commonwealth attached, the sum so paid is a valid set-off against the Commonwealth. Harlan v. Lumsden, 1 Duv. 86.

(5) Co-surety who pays entire debt may plead the amount he is entitled to recover from his co-surety as a set-off to an action against him by the general assignee of said surety on a note executed by him to such surety, although the debt did not mature nor was it paid by him until after the assignment. Chenault v. Bush, 84 Ky. 528, 8 R. 490, 2 S. W. 160.

(6) Damages unliquidated. A demand for unliquidated damages for breach of warranty of the quality of a commodity for which the note sued on was executed may be relied on as a set-off against the note when the vendor is insolvent or a non-resident. Forbes v. Cooper, 88 Ky. 285, 10 R. 865, 11 S. W. 24; Taylor v. Stowell, 4 Met. 175; Abernathy v. Myer-Bridges Co., 100 S. W. 862, 30 R. 1230; and see Boyd v. Day, 3 Bush 617; Adams v. Crayeroft, 1 R. 330. But where there is an adequate remedy at law, and no facts, such as insolvency or non-residence are shown, unliquidated damages, although arising upon a contract, cannot be pleaded as a set-off. Garner v. Jones, 94 Ky. 135, 14 R. 792, 21 S. W. 647; Shropshire v. Conrad, 2 Met. 143; Doy sher v. Adams, 16 R. 582, 29 S. W. 348. A cause of action in favor of a defendant against plaintiff for unliquidated damages cannot be sued as a set-off in the absence of non-residence or insolvency of the plaintiff or some other sufficient equity which authorizes its use as a set-off. Cross v. Snyder's Admx., 164 Ky. 370, 175 S. W. 641.

(7a) Same See equitable set-off, post.

(8) Decedent's estate against. A claim against a decedent pleaded as a set-off must be verified and proved as claims are required to be when sued on by original action; affidavit in body of petition is not sufficient; no demand is necessary. Warfield v. Gardner, 79 Ky. 583, 3 R. 423. Distributee cannot plead amount due him as a set-off in a suit by the administrator, in an action pending in county in which administrator did not qualify. 3 Met. 322.

(9) In a suit by an administrator, the defendant had a right to plead, as a set-off, a demand due him by a decedent whose estate plaintiff's intestate wrongfully appropriated. McKenzie v. Pendleton, 1 Bush 164.

# §96] Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition.

(10) If an administrator is charged in the settlement of the estate with a note taken as administrator, and afterward sues on it, the obligor can set off against it an individual demand due by the administrator. *Jones v. Everman, 15 B. M. 631; and see Lee v. Russell, 18 R. 951, 38 S. W. 874.*

(11) Dedication of fund by order of county court to payment of certain debts precluded sheriff from pleading as set-off to suits by persons entitled to fund amount due him on general account. *Gearhart v. Pritchard, 2 R. 225.*

(12) Demands must be mutual. To authorize a set-off, there must be mutual subsisting demands at the commencement of the action. *Hayes v. Goodwin, 4 Met. 80; 14 B. M. 133; and a joint debt can not be set off against an individual demand, nor an individual demand against a joint one. Lee v. Russell, 18 R. 951, 38 S. W. 874.*

(13) Debt due by one member of a partnership can not be set off against a claim due to the firm. *Warder v. Newdigate, 11 B. M. 174; but in a suit by a partner to recover money, the defendant can plead as a counter-claim a debt due by the firm of which he was a member. Owsley v. Bank, 23 R. 1726, 66 S. W. 33.*

(14) Administrator can not set-off debt due to himself, against a debt due by the decedent. *Com. v. Bosley, 5 Bush 221; nor can a demand due by an intestate be pleaded as a set-off to a bond given to his administrator. Cummings v. Williams, 5 J. J. M. 384; and see Lee v. Russell, 18 R. 951, 38 S. W. 874; Hancock v. Hancock, 24 R. 664, 69 S. W. 757; Smith v. Brannon, 21 R. 267, 51 S. W. 178.*

(15) Receiver appointed by the court can not retain funds collected, and pay the person to whom the court directs the funds to be paid, in individual claims held against him. *Johnson v. Gunter, 6 Bush 534.*

(16) Dismissal of action does not prevent defendant from prosecuting set-off, but after plaintiff has entered motion to dismiss petition without prejudice it is too late to file set-off. *N. W. Ins. Co. v. Barbour, 95 Ky. 7, 15. R. 394, 23 S. W. 584;* and if plaintiff dismisses his action or fails to appear, defendant may prosecute his set off. See. 372.

(17) Equitable set-off. A bank having on deposit money due a depositor who makes an assignment for the benefit of creditors may apply deposit to payment of debt due by depositor, although it is not due when assignment is made.

# Title VIIJ

# Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition.

[§ 96]

a claim due him from the bank at the date of its assignment. Finnell v. Nesbit, 16 B. M. 351; and see Chenault v. Bush, 84 Ky. 528, 8 R. 490, 2 S. W. 160; New Farmers Bank v. Young, l00 Ky. 683, 19 R. 35, 39 S. W. 46.

(21) Where it is agreed that money received, or to be received, is to go in satisfaction of a particular demand, the chancellor has power to enforce the contract by decreeing set-off. Landsdale v. Mitchell, 14 B. M. 348.

(22) The trustees of a school being personally liable to a teacher for the amount of his salary, in action by one of them upon a note, he could plead the amount due him as a teacher as a set-off. Harrison v. Slone, 4 Bush 577.

(23) Two persons mutually indebted, executed each to the other notes for the amount of their indebtedness for the purpose of raising money on the notes. In a suit on one of the notes by an assignee, it was held that the payor could not plead the note he held as a set-off. Barbaroux v. Barker, 4 Met. 47; and see Carothers v. Richards, 17 R. 42, 30 S. W. 2211.

(24) A purchaser of land under a void execution who pays the purchase-money to the execution plaintiff can, in a suit against him for trespass by the execution defendant, who is insolvent, set off the amount so paid. Geoghegan v. Ditto, 2 Met. 433.

(25) In an action on a note the defendant who was surety could plead as a set-off the value of the principal's property converted by the plaintiff as executor de son tort. Finnell v. Meaux, 3 Bush 449.

(26) Set-off can only be pleaded in an action on a contract. Brown v. Phillips, 3 Bush 656; but demands arising out of implied contracts may be pleaded as a set-off. Arthurs v. Thompson, 97 Ky. 218, 17 R. 118, 30 S. W. 628.

(27) A sheriff failing to return an execution in favor of a receiver, and against the administrator of an estate, was sued by the receiver for the amount of execution. Held that the sheriff could plead as a set-off the amount paid by the administrator to certain creditors for whose benefit the receiver was collecting the money. Com. v. Bosley, 5 Bush 221.

(28) On a void sale of infant's land the purchaser paid the guardian $190. In a suit to recover the land and rent, the purchaser could plead as a set-off against the rent the money so paid. Anderson v. Lavton, 3 Bush 87; and see further as to set-off of rent. Mathews v. Mathews, 16 R. 788, 29 S. W. 862.

(29) An indorser paid a bill, and sued the acceptor, who pleaded that he accepted the bill for the sole benefit of the drawer, who died insolvent, and that the indorser was indebted to the estate of the drawer in the amount of the bill. Held a good set-off. Bowman v. Wright, 7 Bush 375.

(30) A party proceeded against to recover money paid on a judgment that has been reversed may plead as a set-off a demand against the claimant if he is insolvent or a non-resident. Carson v. Carson, 2 Met. 96.

(31) Judgment that has been superseded cannot be pleaded as a set-off or counter-claim. Yarborough v. Fitzpatrick, 21 R. 208, 51 S. W. 172; and see further as to right to use an assigned judgment as a set-off. McBrayer v. Dean, 100 Ky. 398, 18 R. 847, 38 S. W. 508; and sec. 377 and notes thereto, and Brown v. Lapp, 28 R. 409, 89 S. W. 304; Clark v. Raison, 104 S. W. 342, 31 R. 906, 126 Ky. 486; Bramlett v. Slemp, 108 S. W. 339, 32 R. 1332.

(32) A claim for the amount of taxes voluntarily paid cannot be used as a set-off in action by the person for whose benefit the taxes were paid. Montgomery v. Montgomery, 25 R. 1682, 78 S. W. 465.

(32a) A county may plead as a set-off to an action by a jailer money paid him under previous allowances to which he was not entitled. Hickman County v. Jackson, 153 Ky. 551, 156 S. W. 391.

(32b) A trustee has a right to set-off the beneficiary's indebtedness to the estate against his interest therein; this right will prevail over the right of a creditor who has a lien on the interest of the beneficiary; nor does the taking of additional security from the beneficiary waive this right. (Subsection 2.) Rice v. Bradley's Trus., 159 Ky. 293, 166 S. W. 1012.

(33) Limitation against set-off ceases at commencement of action. Hayes v. Goodwin, 4 Met. 80.

(34) Note payable in another State, and by the laws of that State placed upon the footing of a bill of exchange, is not subject to set-off in this State in hands of an innocent holder, Stevens v. Gregg, 89 Ky. 461, 11 R. 686, 12 S. W. 775.

(35) Pleading-practice. All objections to matter pleaded improperly as a set-off are waived by replication and issue. Boyd v. Day, 3 Bush 617. Answer presenting merely matter of defense cannot

# Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition

be treated as a set-off, though it may be so called. True v. Triplett, 4 Met. 57. (36) The pleading intended as a set-off must be so designated in the caption. Sec. 97-4, Arthurs v. Thompson, 97 Ky. 218, 17 R. 118, 30 S. W. 628. The word action embraces a set-off. Sec. 732-34; 79 Ky. 583, 3 R. 423.

(37) When a set-off is relied on in an answer, which is also made a cross-petition against parties not before the court, the plaintiff is not bound to reply to the set-off until the parties to the cross-petition are before the court, and it cannot be taken for confessed against him. Scott v. Wilson, 2 Bush 603.

(38) Separate action on. Defendant is not required to rely on his claim against plaintiff as a set-off; he may assert it in an independent action against him. See sec. 17; 8 Bush 229; 1 Duv. 321; 2 Met. 92; 3 Met. 274.

(39) Street improvement set-off against claim for. Bodley v. Finley, 111 Ky. 618, 23 R. 851, 64 S. W. 439.

(40) Taxes - Set-off not admissible against demand for unless expressly authorized by statute Anderson v. City of Mayfield, 93 Ky. 230, 14 R. 370, 19 S. W. 598; and see Newport Bridge Co. v. Douglass, 12 Bush 673; Com. v. Rodes, 5 Mon. 318; City v. Somerset Bk. Co., 109 Ky. 549, 22 R. 1129, 60 S. W. 29.

(41) Usury - waiver. Obligor who has executed a new note to an assignee for the debt including usury cannot, when sued on new note, set up the usury as a set-off. Stone v. MeConnell, 1 Duv. 54; Clark v. Rodes, 12 Bush 13.

(42) Waiver of objection to set-off improperly pleaded. It is too late after issue joined to object that set-off could not be relied on. Boyd v. Day, 3 Bush 617; Adams v. Crayeroft, 1 R. 330; Williams v. Porter, 7 R. 527; Lee v. Russell, 18 R. 951, 38 S. W. 874.

# CROSS-PETITION

(1) The provisions of sec. 90 apply to cross-petitions, sec. 97-1, 3.

(2) Lien on land - action to enforce - summons. See secs. 692, 694 and notes thereto.

(3) Pleading - practice. A sued B for the price of a jack. B answered, setting up a writing, signed by A and D warranting the jack, and sought to recover on it as a cross-petition against D; A pleaded non est factum to the writing and was released. Held that B could not recover on his cross-petition against D, as it set up a cause of action not affected by the.

(11) In an action by A against B and others for the recovery and partition of land owned by A and B, and claimed adversely by the defendants other than B, B was held entitled to relief against.

Title VII1         Answer, Counter-Claim, Set-off, Cross-Petition.

# § 97. [Proceedings upon counter-claims, cross-petitions, set-offs—caption.]

1. [Application of sec. 90.] The provisions of section 90 concerning the demand and giving of relief apply to set-offs, counter-claims, and cross-petitions.
2. [Summons not required upon set-off or counter-claim.] No summons is required upon a set-off or counter-claim against a plaintiff.
3. [127, 120] [Proceedings upon against new parties.] Proceedings upon cross-petitions, and upon set-offs and counter-claims against new parties, shall be the same as those upon petitions; but they shall not delay the trial of any issue in the original action, or upon a set-off or counter-claim, concerning which a judgment can be rendered without prejudice to the rights of the defendants to the cross-petition, or to the rights of new parties to a set-off or counter-claim.
4. [Caption to contain nature of action.] A defendant shall not have judgment upon a set-off or counter-claim, unless the caption of the answer contain the words, answer and set-off, or the words, answer and counter-claim; but a misdescription in the caption, of the nature of the defendant's claims, shall not prevent him from having judgment; nor shall a plaintiff have judgment upon a counter-claim unless the caption of his reply contain the words, reply and counter-claim.

See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 430, 459, 468, 475f, 496, 499, 500, 501, 502, 503, 505, 506, 507, 792, 849, 854; Forms N0s. 259-268, 269.

his co-defendants without service of process or making his answer a cross-petition against them. Fritts v. Kirchdorfer, 124 S. W. 882, 136 Ky. 643; see also Dine v. Donnelly, 121 S. W. 685, 134 Ky. 776; Mattingly v. Eversole, 113 S. W. 447; Brackett v. Boreing, 89 S. W. 496, 28 R. 386; Petry v. Petry, 142 Ky. 564, 134 S. W. 922.

Two defendants were sued for a tort resulting in personal injury. A demurrer was properly sustained to a cross petition in the answer of one of them seeking to recover from the other such damages as might be adjudged against it. M. Livingston & Co. v. Philley, 155 Ky. 224, 159 S. W. 665.

Leave of court must be obtained to file a cross-petition making a new party. Howard v. Jones, 147 Ky. 303, 143 S. W. 1058.

Trial of cross-petition-practice action of court in directing trial of original action and cross-petition before same jury will not be disturbed unless prejudicial error shown. Doyle v. Offutt & Blackburn, 122 S. W. 156, 135 Ky. 296.

Practice-third party. Cross-petition not allowed except on cause of action which affects or is affected by original action-cross-petitioner can not.

By replying to answer and counter-claim, the plaintiff.

waived the right to object to the pleading, because it was not designated in the caption as an answer and counter-claim; if there had been no reply, and judgment had gone by default, judgment would have been erroneous. *Cason v. Title VII] Reply and Additional Pleadings. [§ 98

# CHAPTER V.

# REPLY AND ADDITIONAL PLEADINGS

# § 98. Reply may contain—what.

1. [Traverse.] A traverse.
2. [Facts constituting estoppel or avoidance.] A statement of facts which constitute an estoppel against, or avoidance of, a set-off, counter-claim or defense stated in the answer.
3. [Counter-claim.] A counter-claim against a set-off.
4. [Cross-petition.] A cross-petition. (Forms of. Forms 80-88: when not allowed, sec. 112.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 413, 468, 510, 512a, 512b, 512c, 513, 561; Forms Nos. 184-187, 217-318a

# § 98. (1) Answer-cured by.

A defect in the answer may be cured by an averment in the reply. Mitchell v. Ashby, 78 Ky. 254.

# (2) Contributory neglect.

Plea of is admitted by failure to reply. White v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 15 R. 49, 22 S. W. 219; L. & N. R. R. v. Mayfield, 18 R. 224, 35 S. W. 924; Gore v. I. Central R. R., 17 R. 799, 32 S. W. 754; Brooks v. L. & N. R. R., 24 R. 1318, 71 S. W. 507.

But defendant may, by failing to raise question in proper time and manner, waive his right to judgment because of failure to file reply. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Copas, 05 Ky. 460, 16 R. 14, 26 S. W. 179; L. & U. R. Co. v. Paynter, 26 R. 761, 82 S. W 412; Lou. Ry. Co. v. Hibbett, 139 Ky. 43, 129 S. W. 319; Shulte v. L. & N. R. Co., 128 Ky. 633, 108 S. W. 941.

# (3) Denial in—sufficiency of.

Reply to a set-off, denying generally the correctness of each and every item, is not a sufficient traverse of the items in the set-off. Whitaker v. Sandifer, 1 Duv. 261; Thruston v. Oldhain, 6 Bush 16; and see note 13.

# (4) Denial of any knowledge or information.

Denial of any knowledge or information as to whether or not the various items mentioned in the account pleaded as a set-off, and alleged to have been sold and delivered to plaintiff, were delivered, is not good; there should be a specific denial. Wing v. Dugan, 8 Bush 583.

# (5) The petition alleged that the debt was unpaid.

With a statement as to how it was created. The denial in the answer that anything was due, and an allegation of payment, made an issue. Robinson v. Williamson, 7 Bush 604.

# (6) In an action against a vendor.

To recover damages for failing to convey land and deliver possession, the defendant by counter-claim sought a specific execution of the contract. The plaintiff could not in his reply demand a rescission, the reply being restricted to matters of defense. Spalding v. Alexander, 6 Bush 160.

# (7) Infancy-ratification.

The plaintiff in a reply may plead a ratification of the contract by the infant where infancy is relied on as a defense. Stern v. Freeman, 4 Met. 309.

# (8) Limitation.

If limitation is pleaded, the plaintiff may in a reply rely upon a new promise made before the limitation expired; but if he depends on a new promise made after limitation has run he must set it up in an amended petition. Gilmore v. Green, 14 Bush 772.

# (9) No consideration-reply is necessary to plea of.

Brown v. Ready, 14 R. 583, 20 S. W. 1036.

# § 99. [Rejoinder may contain—what.]

1. [Traverse.] A traverse.
2. [Facts constituting estopel or avoidance.] A statement of facts which constitute an estoppel against, or avoidance of—
1. Facts stated in the reply in support of the plaintiff's original cause of action.
2. A defense stated in the reply to a set-off or counter-claim.
3. A counter-claim stated in the reply. (When not allowed, see § 112.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 413, 431, 515; Forms Nos. 278, 281, 283, 286, 288, 290, 295, 311.

# § 100. [Surrejoinders and subsequent pleadings.]

Surrejoinders and subsequent pleadings may be of the same character as replies and rejoinders.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 431, 517; Forms Nos. 283a, 291.

(10) Non est factum-reply is not necessary to plea of. Brown v. Ready, 14 R. 583, 20 S.W. 1036.

(1) Payment. Answer pleading does not require a reply. Logan Co. Bank v. Barclay, 104 Ky. 97, 20 R. 773, 46 S.W. 675; Ermert v. Deitz, 19 R. 1639, 14 S.W. 138.

(12) Pleading-practice. Matters of defense alleged in a petition to be made a party, such petition being taken as an answer, need not be denied by reply. Graves v. Ward, 2 Duv. 301.

(13) Every material affirmative allegation in an answer should be replied to (sec. 126) and an issue formed as to each cause of controversy. Sec. 114.

(14) If reply contain matter inconsistent with the petition, the defendant should object to the filing of it, or move to strike out the inconsistent matter, or else the objection is waived. Barbaroux v. Barker, 4 Met. 47; sec. 101; sec. 113-4.

(15) If the matter alleged in the answer as constituting a counter-claim involve only a denial of what is averred in the petition, or an affirmance of what is therein denied, no reply is necessary. Davis v. Dycus, 7 Bush 4; 11 Bush 228.

(16) While a reply cannot contain a set-off against a set-off set up in the answer, a defense that the goods for which it was sought to recover in the set-off were sold in payment of an account due plaintiff, can be pleaded in the reply. Williams v. Jones, 1 Bush 621.

(17) Where a reply is treated by the lower court and parties as an amended petition, it will be so regarded by the Court of Appeals. Ruffner v. Ridley, 81 Ky. 165, 4 R. 958. A cause of action was set up in a reply which should have been set up by amended petition. Judgment was given thereon upon hearing after issue joined. The error was not prejudicial. Rounds & Jesse v. Cloverport F. & M. Co., 159 Ky. 414, 167 S.W. 384.

(17a) In an action by a factor against his principal, the defendant pleaded as a counter-claim a failure of the factor to sell certain tobacco when ordered to sell. A ratification by the principal of this departure from instructions could not be given in evidence under a traverse in the reply. It should have been specially pleaded in an estoppel. Louisville Tob. W. H. Co. v. Lee, 172 Ky. 171, 189 S.W. 16.

(17b) After a third party has intervened, the plaintiff cannot by reply set up against such third person a cause of action not contained in the petition. Hodge Tob. Co. v. Sexton, 166 Ky. 219, 179 S.W. 36.

(18) Departure in reply from cause of action set up in petition is not allowable and judgment by default on a reply that sets up a new cause of action is error. Spiers v. Bartley, 113 S.W. 127, 130 Ky. 277; see also Slone v. Kelly, 143 Ky. 135, 136 S.W. 138; Langan & Taylor Co. v. Fennelly, 122 Ky. 808, 93 S.W. 1, 29 R. 367.

(19) Although a plaintiff cannot in his reply depart from his cause of action, he may rely upon matters of avoidance.

Title VII              Reply and Additional Pleadings.    [9 101

# § 101. [Departure in reply or subsequent pleading forbidden-exception.]

A party shall not in a reply or subsequent pleading, depart from a cause of action or a ground of defense, stated by him in a previous pleading; but the pleading of equitable matter of avoidance or estoppel in an ordinary action, or of legal matter of avoidance or estoppel in an equitable action, shall not, of itself, constitute such departure. (When inconsistent allegations allowed, sec. 113.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 426e, 512a, 573, 580, 902

Form No. 284.

or estoppel. Gibson v. Black, 10 R. 373, 9 S. W. 379.

# §101. (1 Construction of section.

The words "subsequent pleading" refer to a pleading subsequent in its character to a reply-as a rejoinder-and not to amended answer, although it may be filed subsequent to the reply. Bedford v. Graves, 8 R. 262, 1 S. W. 534; and see note 18 to sec. 98.

In an action by the payee of a note, after a plea of non est factum and alteration, the plaintiff may amend and seek a recovery upon the indebtedness for which the note was given. Ramsey v. Utica Deposit Bank, 156 Ky. 263. 160 S. W. 943.

# (2 Departure in pleading-where a counter-claim set up in a reply was repugnant to the cause of action stated in the petition the court should have stricken it out.

Langan & Taylor v. Fennelly, 122 Ky. 808, 93 S. W. 1, 29 R. 367; see further notes 18, 19 to sec. 98.

# (3) Estoppel or avoidance-plea of not a departure.

Dodd v. Pittsburg R. Co., 127 Ky. 762, 106 S. W. 787, 32 R. 1068.

*By the Act of December 30, 1892, c. 124, p. 419, entitled, "An Act concerning practice in circuit courts having continuous sessions," which act is now Sections 980 to 1019, both inclusive, of the Kentucky Statutes, and by the Act of March 29, 1902, c. 122, p. 272, entitled, "An Act to regulate proceedings in civil actions in circuit courts," which is now Section 367a, of the Civil Code, and known as the "practice act," the matters dealt with in Sections 102 to 106, both inclusive, are fully regulated. It should be noted that Section 12 of the Act of March 29, 1902, provides that "this act shall not apply to circuit courts having continuous sessions." Thus the matter dealt with in Section 102 of the Civil Code-time for filing defense in an ordinary action-is governed by Sections 1003 and 1004 as to circuit courts having continuous sessions, and by Civil Code, Sections 367a-2, 367a-3, 367a-6, and 367a-11, by reason of which later acts Section 102 stands repealed by implication, as Sections 706 and 707 govern the same matter in courts inferior to the circuit courts.

For similar reasons Section 103 is impliedly repealed by Section 1005, of the Kentucky Statutes, as to courts having continuous sessions, and by Sections 367a-3, 367a-6 and 367a-11, as to other circuit courts; Section 104 by Kentucky Statutes, Section 1004, and by Section 367a-3, of the Code; Section 105 by Kentucky Statutes, Section 1005, and by Sections 367a-3 and 367a-11; Section 106 by Kentucky Statutes, Section 1005, and by Sections 367a-3 and 367a-11.

For purposes of reference said Sections 102-106 are here inserted:

"102 [87, 135, 137] [Defense-when to be filed.] Defense to an ordinary action must be filed on the day on which the case is set for trial; and to an equitable action on the third day of term, if the summons be served in the county in which the action is brought ten days, or elsewhere in the State twenty days, or out of the State sixty days, before the commencement of the term, or if the defendant be constructively summoned thirty days before the commencement of the term.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 528.

# § 102. (1) Non-resident defendant, as to time when action against stands for trial.

See Harris v. Adams, 2 Duv. 141; sec. 60, and note thereto; and sec. 44, note 2.

Waiver. The benefit of this section is waived by defendant going to trial without seasonable objection as to service of process. Rosenberg v. Dahl, 162 Ky. 92, 172 S. W. 113.

Title VII] Time of Pleading. [§103-109

# § 103 [136] [Ordinary action—time to plead.]

In an ordinary action, all other pleadings must be filed on or before the calling of the case for trial.

# § 104 [138] [Equitable action-time to answer.]

If the summons in an equitable action be served before the commencement of the term, but not the length of time mentioned in Section 102, the defense shall be filed in thirty days after the service.

# § 105 [139] [Equitable action—time to complete pleadings.]

In an equitable action, if the answer be due and be filed on the third day of the term, the reply shall be filed within three days thereafter, and the subsequent pleadings shall be filed within three days after the filing of the reply.

# § 106 [Equitable action-time to complete pleading.]

In an equitable action, if the answer be not due and filed on the third day of the term, the reply shall be filed within ten days after notice of the filing of the answer, and the subsequent pleadings shall be filed within ten days after notice of the filing of the reply.

# § 107 [140] [Time to plead extended.]

The court may for good cause shown, and at the cost of the party applying therefor, extend the time for filing a pleading, but not beyond the next succeeding term.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 531.

# § 108 [141] [Filing in vacation—notice-effect.]

The filing of an answer or subsequent pleading in the clerk's office during vacation, within the time allowed, with notice thereof to the adverse party, or his attorney, shall be equivalent to a filing in court.

# § 109 [Filing demurrer in vacation-curing defect-waiver.]

1. [Demurrer in vacation.] A party who files an answer or subsequent pleading during vacation may file a demurrer therewith.
2. [Amendment to cure defect.] The adverse party may, during that vacation, and after notice to the party filing the demurrer, or his attorney, file an amended pleading to cure any defect suggested by the demurrer.
3. [Waiver of demurrer.] The failure of a party, who so files a special demurrer, to present it for consideration, at the next succeeding term of the court, shall be a waiver of any defect stated in the demurrer, except that to the jurisdiction of the court of the subject of the action.

# CIRCUIT COURTS HAVING CONTINUOUS SESSIONS.

On motion for continuance and time to file an answer the affidavit should disclose the nature of the defense so that the court may judge whether or not it is sufficient. Engleman v. National Bank, 2 Bush 165.

# § 1004. When defendant to answer—warning order--constructive service.

The defense to an action shall be filed within twenty days after the service of the summons in the county where such court sits or within thirty days after such service elsewhere in the State. Every warning order shall warn the defendant to appear and defend the action within sixty days after the making of the warning order, and the defendant shall be considered as constructively summoned in thirty days after the making of such warning order. (Id.. sec. 1004.)

# § 1005. Pleadings, subsequent to answer—filing.

Every pleading subsequent to the answer shall be filed in fourteen days after the pleading is filed to which it responds, but the court may extend the time for pleading. (Id., sec. 1005.)

# § 1007. Filing pleading with clerk-effect of.

The filing of a pleading in the clerk's office within the proper time, and causing it to be noted upon the clerk's memorandum book and rule docket, shall be equivalent to a filing in court. (Id., sec. 1007.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 528, 543, 804.

# § 110 [118, 125] [Caption of pleading—what it shall state.]

1. [Caption, what to be stated in.] The caption of a pleading must state the name of the court in which the action is brought or pending, and the names of the parties, designating who are plaintiffs and who are defendants, coupled with a word, or short phrase, describing the character of the pleading, with this exception: If there be several parties on either side, a statement of the name of the plaintiff, or defendant, first named in the petition, followed by the phrase, "&c.," shall suffice, except in petitions, cross-petitions and answers which make new parties to a set-off or counter-claim.

§ 110. (1). Caption of petition giving the firm name, but not the names of the individuals composing firm, was sufficient, when the body of petition set out in full the names of the members of the firm. Bryant v. Mack, Stadler & Co., 19 R. 744, 41 S. W. 774.

(2) A petition stating a cause of action against the defendant individually is good, although the caption improperly designates him as guardian. Clift v. Newell, 104 Ky. 396, 20 R. 644, 47 S. W. 270; and see Revill v. Claxton, 12 Bush 558.

(4) Defendant sued by wrong name must disclose his correct name if he makes objection on account of the misnomer. L. & N. R. R. v. Hall, 12 Bush 131; Heckman v. L. & N. R. R., 85 Ky. 631, 9 R. 297, 4 S. W. 342; Schmidt v. L.,

# General Rules of Pleading

# § 111-113

# 2. [Petitions, how described.]

Petitions in ordinary actions shall be described by the word, "petition;" those in other actions shall be described by the words, "petition in equity." (See sec. 97.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 197, 397.

# § 111. [Pleading that may be made cross-petition-set-off--counter-claim.]

1. [Cross-petition-pleading that may be.] No pleading, except an answer to an original petition, or the plaintiff's reply to such an answer, shall be made a cross-petition.
2. [Set-off—pleading that may be.] No pleading, except an answer to an original petition, shall present a set-off.
3. [Counter-claim-pleading that may be.] No pleading, except an answer to an original petition, or a reply to a set-off, shall present a counter-claim. (These pleadings defined, sec. 96; proceedings upon, see. 97.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 468, 490, 512b, 512c, 513, 849, 854, 866; Forms Nos. 259-268, 269.

# § 112. [Reply and additional pleading—when not allowed.]

There shall be no reply, nor additional pleading, except to affirmative allegations of an adverse pleading. (That these pleadings may contain, see. 08 to 101.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 429, 511, 516, 888.

# § 113 [124] [General provisions concerning pleadings.]

1. [Demurrer to part of pleading.] A party may demur to part of a pleading, and present an issue of fact as to another part.

C. & L. R. R., 99 Ky. 143; 18 R. 65, 35 S. W. 135.

The omission from the caption of a pleading, of the name of a minor defendant in a proceeding to sell land of an estate and divide the proceeds thereof, was held not fatal to the action. Harrod v. Harrod, 167 Ky. 308, 180 S. W. 997.

# § 111. Cross-petition

A cross-petition can only be asserted in the pleadings mentioned in this section. Fritts v. Kirchdorfer, 136 Ky. 643, 124 S. W. 882.

# § 112.

A certain negative averment in an answer did not need to be controverted by the reply. Tyler v. Woerner, 158 Ky. 712, 166 S. W. 178.

# § 113.

1. Alternative pleas. In action on renewal note, if defense of non est factum is made, plaintiff may, by amendment, ask, if plea is good, to have judgment on original note. First National Bank v. Gaines, 87 Ky. 597, 10 R. 451, 9 S. W. 396.
2. The pleader cannot allege in the alternative that one or the other of two defendants is liable, the alternative plea must relate to facts, not parties. Brown v. Ill. Cent. R. R., 100 Ky. 525, 18 R. 974, 38 S. W. 862; L. & N. R. R. v. Fort Wayne Co., 108 Ky. 113, 21 R. 1544, 55 S. W. 918; but objection must be made before answer. L. & N. R. R. v. Kimbrough, 115 Ky. 512; 24 R. 2409. 74 S. W. 229.
3. A pleading may contain more than two alternative pleas. L. & N. R. Co. v. Wyatt, 29 R. 437.
4. When facts are stated in the alternative with the allegation that the pleader does not know which is true, both of the alternative statements must present a cause of action. Hoffman v. City of Maysville, 123 Ky. 707, 97 S. W. 360, 29 R. 1244.
5. Construction of sub-section two—a party cannot after demurring to the petition raise the question of jurisdiction. McDowell v. C., O. & S. W. R. Co. 90 Ky. 346, 12 R. 331, 14 S. W. 338.
6. Defenses allowed. In an action for negligence against a railroad company

Title VIIj General Rules of Pleading. [§ 113

# 2 [1] [What pleading may contain.]

A pleading may contain statements of as many causes of action, legal or equitable, and of as many matters of estoppel and of avoidance, legal or equitable, total or partial; and may make as many traverses; and may present as many demurrers as there may be grounds for in behalf of the pleader.

# 3 [110, 125] [Paragraphing pleading--duty of court.]

If there be more than one, each must be distinctly stated in a separate, numbered paragraph; and either, which is intended to respond to part only of an adverse pleading, must show to what part it is responsive. It is the duty of the court, upon or without motion, to enforce these provisions; and, for that purpose, to dismiss an action without prejudice, or to strike a pleading, or any part thereof, from the case, or to allow a new pleading.

# 4. [Inconsistent pleading—alternative allegations.]

If, however, a party files a pleading which contains inconsistent statements, or statements inconsistent with those of a pleading previously filed by him in the action, he shall, upon or without motion, be required to elect which of them shall be stricken from his pleading. But a party may allege, alternatively, the existence of one or another fact, if he states that one of them is true, and that he does not know which of them is true.

The defendant has a right under the Code to traverse the petition, plead contributory negligence and a compromise. Jones v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 82 Ky. 610, 6 R. 694; Buford v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 82 Ky. 286, 6 R. 263.

# (7) Demurrer to part of pleading.

A defense insufficiently pleaded in the same paragraph with a defense which is well pleaded may be demurred to. Williams v. Langford. 15 B. M. 566; but the proper practice is to require pleading paragraph, if it can be done, and then demur to defective paragraph, or the defective portion may be stricken out. Sun Ins. Co. v. Crist, 19 R. 305, 39 S. W. 837.

# (8) Denial of knowledge or information as to the existence of an alleged fact

must be specific as to that fact. Ward v. Edge. 100 Ky. 757, 19 R. 59, 39 S. W. 410; and see notes 15-21, sec. 95.

# (9) Inconsistent pleas.

Plaintiff may, by an amended petition, abandon the cause of action set up in his petition, and present a cause that could not have been joined with that relied on in the petition. Hord v. Chandler, J3 B. M. 403, and an objection to a pleading because it contains inconsistent matter cannot be made by demurrer. Ib. See Hancock v. Johnson, 1 Met. 242.

# (10) In an action by the assignee of a bankrupt

to recover certain notes assigned by the bankrupt a short time before his bankruptcy, the petition charged that the bankrupt had fraudulently assigned them; that he was at the time insolvent, and that they were assigned to prefer a creditor. Held not inconsistent pleas, as it is in effect alleged that plaintiff does not know which is true. Beatty v. Dudley, 80 Ky. 381, 4 R. 212.

# (11) In an action by an assignee upon a note,

the defendant pleaded as a set-off a note due to him by the assignor. The plaintiff replied that the defendant executed the note sued on to enable the assignor to raise money, and for that purpose it was assigned to him. Held that the reply was supplemental to, and not inconsistent with, the petition. Barbaroux v. Barker, 4 Met. 47.

# (12) Plea of non est factum and no consideration

are not inconsistent. Smith v. Doherty, 109 Ky. 616, 22 R. 1238, 60 S. W. 380; Spencer v. Shakers, 23 R. 854, 64 S. W. 468; Bank v. Wisdom, 111 Ky. 135, 23 R. 530, 63 S. W. 461; Sparks v. Whittaker, 141 Ky. 484, 133 S. W. 233.

# (13) In an action on a medical account

an answer denying in one paragraph that the service was rendered, and in another paragraph admitting the service, but charging that it was negligently performed, was inconsistent. Black v. Hollo-

# General Rules of Pleading

# 5. [Costs paid by party in fault.]

If a pleading be stricken from the record, re-formed or amended, the party in fault shall pay the cost resulting therefrom.*

* 887. If a party to an action obtains a continuance, or files an amendment that causes a continuance, he shall pay the costs accruing at that term; and the party in error, if an action be dismissed for want of jurisdiction, shall pay the costs; and if a party shall obtain further time to plead or to amend his pleadings, he shall pay the costs occasioned thereby. (Ky. Stat., sec. 887.)

# 6. [Avoidance or estoppel, how pleaded.]

An express admission of a fact is not necessary to render valid a pleading in avoidance or estoppel thereof.

# 7. [Traverse defined—denial of knowledge or information.]

A traverse is a denial; by a party, of facts alleged in an adverse pleading, if they be presumptively within his knowledge; or a denial of them, or a denial that he has sufficient knowledge or information to form a belief concerning them, if they be not presumptively within his knowledge. (Allegations not denied admitted, sec. 126.)

# 8. [Sham pleadings.]

Sham pleadings shall, upon or without motion, be stricken out by the court, at the cost of the parties for whom they are filed and of their attorneys. (What is not departure in pleading, sec. 101; cause of action may be dismissed without prejudice, sec. 371.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 77, 200, 202a, 322, 342, 358, 368, 376, 402, 418, 419, 424c, 426b, 426c, 426d, 426e, 426i, 433, 434, 435, 453, 512a, 543, 550d, 550e, 558, 560, 565, 566, 573, 581, 671, 804, 806, 808, 812, 814, 856, 860, 870, 877, 887, 902; Forms Nos. 30, 31, 85, 155, 173, 174, 183, 188-256, 257, 258, 271.

way, 19 R. 694, 41 S. W. 576; and see further as to ruling on inconsistent pleas. M. & C. Turnpike Co. v. Maupin, 79 Ky. Elias v. Gill, 92 Ky. 569, 13 R. 798, 18 S. W. 454.

(14) A party will not be permitted to sue on contract or for tort by averring that he does not know on which claim he is entitled to recover. Southern Lumber Co. v. Wireman, 19 R. 585, 41 S. W. 279.

(15) If a reply contain matter inconsistent with the petition, the defendant should object to its being filed, or move to strike out the inconsistent matter. 4 Me. 47.

(16) When the petition shows that several paragraphs relate to the same cause of action, the plaintiff should be required to elect to prosecute one of the paragraphs, and the others should be stricken out; unless the plaintiff shall allege that the fact, as stated in one or the other of the paragraphs, is true, but he does not know which of them is true.

(19) In an action for negligence allegations that injury was caused by being struck by car or by falling in an effort to prevent being struck are not inconsistent. Owensboro R. R. v. Hill, 21 R. 1638, 56 S. W. 21.

Title II1 General Rules of Pleading.

(20) In an action under the statute for maliciously killing another person—an answer stating that the shooting was in self-defense and that it was accidental was inconsistent. Hollingsworth v. Warnock, 112 Ky. 96, 23 R. 1395, 65 S. W. 163.

(21) A defendant will only be required to elect between defenses when they are so inconsistent that if the truth of one defense were admitted it would disprove the other. Caruso v. Brown, 142 Ky. 76, 133 S. W. 948; Jones v. Whittaker, 141 Ky. 484, 133 S. W. 223.

(2la) Where, in a personal injury action, a cause of action is stated under the State law and also under the Federal act of April 22, 1908, the plaintiff should be required to elect under which law he will proceed. Thompson v. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co., 165 Ky. 256, 176 S. W. 1006.

(2lb) A petition in an action against a telegraph company set up certain negligence; an amendment set up additional acts of negligence. These pleadings were not inconsistent. (Subsection 4.) W. U. Tel. Co. v. Reed, 158 Ky. 552, 165 S. W. 656.

(2lc) A plea of non est factum and a charge of fraud and undue influence in obtaining the execution of the conveyance sought to be set aside were inconsistent. (Subsection 4.) Fletcher v. Nichols, 157 Ky. 23, 162 S. W. 544.

(2ld) The objection that defenses set up in an answer are inconsistent should be taken by motion. It is not available on demurrer. Brashears v. Combs, 174 Ky. 344, 192 S. W. 482.

(2le) This section does not allow in an action for negligence a joinder of two parties upon an allegation that one or the other of them committed the negligent act. (Subsection 4.) Louisville Gas Electric Co. v. Wall, 178 Ky. 33, 198 S. W. 745.

(22) Paragraphing-waiver. The fact that distinct defenses are not set out in separate paragraphs is not cause of demurrer, but of a motion to paragraph. Mullikin v. Mullikin, 15 R. 600, 23 S. W. 352; Williams v. Langford, 15 B. M. 566. And an objection to a pleading because not properly paragraphed is waived by answering. Noel v. Hudson, 13 B. M. 205.

(23) Paragraphing - practice. Each paragraph must be complete in itself; the averments in one paragraph cannot be connected with or relied on in aid of another, and where separate paragraphs contain inconsistent matter one should be stricken out; it is not good practice to strike out merely the inconsistent matter. Black v. Holloway, 19 R. 694, 41 S. W. 576; L. & N. R. Co. v. Adams, 148 Ky. 513, 147 S. W. 384.

(24) If several acts of negligence are charged as causing the death complained of, it is not necessary to state them in separate paragraphs. Figg v. L. & N. R. R., 111 Ky. 30, 23 R. 383, 63 S. W. 580.

(25) In an action to recover damages it is not necessary to state each item of damages in a separate paragraph. Bartram v. Ohio R. Co., 141 Ky. 100, 132 S. W. 188.

(26) Sham pleading. Stating amount in controversy in a sum sufficient to give the court jurisdiction will not be considered. Cumberland Tel Co. v. Logsdon, 142 Ky. 639, 134 S. W. 1159. If upon the facts set up in the pleadings the amount in controversy is less than $200, the Court of Appeals will not take jurisdiction of an appeal from the judgment, although the prayer for damages in the petition is for a much larger sum. Barton-Parker Mfg. Co. v. Wheeler, 164 Ky. 452, 175 S. W. 980.

(27) Redundancy. If a petition contains irrelevant or redundant matter, but does not present two causes of action, a motion to strike out (sec. 121) or to make more specific (sec. 134) is the way to reach the defect. Bonney v. Reardon, 6 Bush 34.

(27a) Striking insufficient pleading. Where defendants failed to file their answer within the time prescribed by law, and additional time was given, and defendants tendered and filed an answer which was insufficient, the court had power to strike it from the record. (Subsec. 8.) Combs v. Frick Co., 162 Ky. 42, 171 S. W. 999.

(28) Traverse necessary. In action against carrier for damages to stock transported over its line, denial of knowledge or information as to its negligence is not sufficient, it must make a specific denial. N., C. & St. L. R. R. v. Carrico, 95 Ky. 480, 16 R. 66, 26 S. W. 177.

(29) Traverse of record will put in issue affirmative matter the same as a written pleading. Ky. Union R. R. v. Conner, 17 R. 426, 31 S. W. 467.

(30) See further notes to sec. 95 as to necessity for and insufficiency of traverse.

(31) Two or more obligations sued on. Each should be set out in a separate paragraph, although the same parties are on each instrument, but where the obligations sued on are for the performance of a public duty running through several terms and the plaintiff is unable to state amount of defalcation for each term sep-

$ 14-116 General Rules of Pleading. [Title VII]

# § 114. [Material issue to be formed-duty of court.]

Parties must, before trial, form a material issue concerning each cause of controversy; and it is the duty of the court, upon or without motion, to compel them to do so; and, for that purpose, they may be required to reform their pleadings or the pleading of a party who is in fault may be stricken from the case.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 6, 431, 509.

# § 115 [142] [Written in English language and signed—exception.]

Pleadings must be in the English language, and as concise as possible consistently with clearness; and, excepting those mentioned in section 705, must be in writing, and be signed by the parties who file them, or by their attorneys.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 192, 198, 207, 208a, 383, 432, 525, 526, 883, 905; Form No. 41.

# § 116 [142, 148, 588] [Verification—when not required.]

Every pleading, which this Code requires to be written, must be verified by an affidavit to the effect that the affiant believes that the statements of the pleading are true, excepting (See further as to verification secs. 137-13). Forms of verification, Forms 102-104.)—

Separately, it is sufficient to set out in paragraphs the obligations and state at conclusion amount of defalcation. Com. v. Tate, 89 Ky. 608. 12 R. 9. 13 S. W. 117.

(32) Waiver-Objection that pleading is inconsistent is waived by failure to make objection before answer. L, & N. R. R. v. Kimbrough, 115 Ky. 512, 24 R. 2409, 74 S. W. 229.

Failure to properly sign a pleading must be taken advantage of by rule, or the objection will be waived. Voorheis v. Eiting, 15 R. 161, 22 S. W. 80.

# § 114. Material issue.

(1) The court should compel the parties to form a material issue and strike out all redundant or irrelevant matter so that the real issue will not be confused. Hale v. Grogan, 99 Ky. 170, 18 R. 46, 35 S. W. 282; Brashear v. Letcher Co., 22 R. 1763, 61 S. W. 285.

(2) Burden of proof. In an action on a note the defendant claimed that a certain sum of money had been paid upon the note. The reply filed alleged that the money was paid, but was paid on another debt. The burden of proof was on the defendant. Ditto v. Hopkins, 164 Ky. 412, 175 S. W. 658.

# § 115. (1) Signing pleadings.

The answer of the defendant was not signed at the end of it, as it should have been; the signature of the attorney on the back below the indorsement is not a signing in the meaning of the Code; but the court having permitted it to be filed, over the objection of plaintiff, should not have disregarded it on the hearing because not signed. Ashbrook v. Roberts, 82 Ky. 298, 6 R. 317; Abernathy v. Meyer-Bridges Co., 99 S. W. 942, 30 R. 844; see sec. 732-7.

# § 116. (1) Verification.

Pleadings in actions for injuries to person or character - as for libel - must be verified. Berea College v. Powell, 25 R. 1220, 77 S. W. 382; and see as to waiver of objection.

Title IJ

# General Rules of Pleading

# § 116

1. Demurrers. Demurrers.
2. Defensive pleading for persons under disability or constructively summoned. Defensive pleadings filed by a guardian ad litem, curator or committee of a defendant who is under disability, or by an attorney for a defendant who has been constructively summoned and has not appeared to the action.
3. Pleading founded on writing filed with pleading. A pleading which states a cause of action that is founded on a written contract, or upon a written indorsement or assignment thereof, if it be filed with the pleading.
4. Defense founded on writing that is filed. A pleading which states a defense that is founded on a writing executed by the adverse party, and filed with the pleading.
5. Allegations concerning value or damages. Allegations concerning value or damage, not accompanied by an allegation of an express promise, or by a statement of facts showing an implied promise to pay such value or damage.
6. Pleadings in name of Commonwealth. Pleadings in actions brought in the name of the Commonwealth, pursuant to Chapter XIII of Title X.

*For "chapter XIII," the enrolled bill has "chapter XIV," the reference in the text as given above evidently being that intended.—Ed.
7. Allegation that might subject party to criminal prosecution. When the admission of an allegation of a pleading might subject a party to a criminal or penal prosecution. (Verification not required in actions for alimony, or divorce, sec. 421; nor in actions of divisions of land, sec. 499; waiver of, sec. 138; rehen amendments allowed without, sec. 139; how and before whom made, secs. 551, 549.)

See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 451, 526, 526c, 526f, 526g, 526h, 526j, 694, 709, 741, 886; Form No. 164.
