Plea of non est factum must be verified. Ky. Stat., sec. 473; as must plea of no consideration, Ky. Stat., sec. 472.
Objection for want of verification should be made by rule requiring party to verify, and upon his failure to do so, pleading should be stricken from record. Wheeler v. Wales, 3 Bush 225; but not until rule to verify has been awarded and time given. Baxter v. Knox, 17 R. 489, 31 S. W. 284; Payne v. Trigg, 19 R. 801, 41 S. W. 4.
A pleading that is required to be verified will not be considered as filed until it is verified, on a motion to submit the case. Park v. McReynolds, 111 Ky. 651, 23 R. 894, 64 S. W. 517; but see City of Dayton v. Hirth, 27 R. 1209, 87 S. W. 1136, 121 Ky. 42; nor is a party required to respond to a pleading until it is verified or until motion to verify is overruled. Ewell v. Tye, 25 R. 976, 76 S. W. 875.
Failure of court to require, not prejudicial error, when. Ball v. Sandlin, 176 Ky. 537, 195 S. W. 1089.

# § 117

Verification. It may now be regarded as settled that the oaths of the plaintiff and the defendant to the respective pleadings are equivalents, leaving the issue to be adjudicated upon.

# General Rules of Pleading

# § 117 [142, 143] [Verification—by whom to be made.]

Pleadings, the verification of which is required by this Code, must be verified as follows:

1. [Pleadings for Commonwealth.] Pleadings of the Commonwealth must be verified by the attorney who brings the action, or by the officer or agent who is authorized by law to have it brought.
2. [County or corporation.] That of a county, or of a municipal or private corporation, must be verified by its chief officer or agent upon whom a summons in the action is lawfully served, or might be lawfully served if it were a defendant; or if it have no such officer or agent residing in the county in which the action is brought, or is pending, it may be verified by its attorney.
3. [Party must verify—exceptions.] That of any other party must be verified by him, with these exceptions:
- a. If he be absent from the county, or mentally incapable of taking an oath, or physically unable to attend before an officer, it may be verified by his agent or attorney.
- b. If it be a joint pleading of several parties, who are united in interest, it may be verified by either of them.
- c. If the party be under disability, excepting coverture, or infancy and coverture combined, it may be verified by the person who is authorized to file it.
4. [Party may be required to verify in person.] But, on motion of a party who files his affidavit, stating his belief that an adverse party, whose pleading has been verified by a person other than himself, knows that a statement thereof, in the affidavit mentioned, is untrue, and that the motion is not made for delay, the court, if such statement be material, shall require such adverse party to verify the pleading; and, if he fail to do so, within a reasonable time, shall treat it, with regard to him, as if it had not been filed.
5. [Effect of verification.] Verification of a pleading does not make other or greater proof necessary on the side of the adverse party than the evidence. Worley v. Tuggle, 4 Bush 168; Enders v. Williams, 1 Met. 346; 17 B. M. 642; 16 B. M. 174. Suit by county. County judge is to treat pleading as a nullity as to those who refuse to verify; but failure of one to verify does not authorize court to treat pleading as a nullity as to those who do. Harrison v. Lebanon Water Works, 91 Ky. 255, 12 R. 822, 15 S. W. 522.

(4) Corporation. When a pleading of a corporation is verified by an agent or attorney, the affidavit should state that the officer required to verify it by subsection 2 is absent from the county. Northern Ice Co. v. Orr, 102 Ky. 586, 19 R. 163, 44 S. W. 216; Dengler v. Krell-French Co., 119 S. W. 757; First Nat.

Title VIIJ General Rules of Pleading.

would have been necessary if the pleading had not been verified. (See further, as to verification, secs. 137-139. How and before whom made, secs. 551, 549; when by agent or attorney, sec. 550; form of affidavit to require party to verify in person, Form 111.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 223, 424a, 451, 526c, 526e 810; Forms Nos. 1, 2, 152, 331b.

# § 118 [128] [Special demurrer may be presented by pleading—waiver.]

A party may, by an answer or other proper pleading, make any of the objections mentioned in section 92, the existence of which is not shown by the pleading of his adversary; and failure so to do is a waiver of any of said objections, except that to the jurisdiction of the court of the subject of the action. (See further as to special demurrer, sec. 92.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed., secs. 20b, 52, 52b, 151a, 190a, 387, 392b, 393, 393a, 396a, 544, 799, 800, 803, 905; Forms Nos, 165-170, 408.

Bank v. Sanders Bros., 162 Ky. 874, 172 S. W. 689.

Waiver of verification, see sec. 138.

# (6) Pleading must be verified as required by laws of this State, although it be executed in another State.

Crane & Breed Co. v. Stagg, 122 S. W. 225, 135 Ky. 428.

# § 118. (1) Defect of parties -how reached.

If defect of parties appear in petition it should be taken advantage of by special demurrer, as provided in sec. 92.4; if defect does not appear, it must be taken advantage of (in the Circuit Court, New Bell Jellico Co. v. Braznell's Admr., 149 Ky. 418, 149 S. W. 888) by answer, or the error is waived. McAlister v. Saving Bank, 80 Ky. 684, 4 R. 652; Albro v. Lawson, 17 B. M. 642; 10 Bush 763; 3 Bush 200; 14 B. M. 246; 4 Met. 385; Rudd v. Deposit Bank, 105 Ky. 443; 20 R. 1497, 49 S. W. 207; Puckett v. Jameson, Sheriff, 157 Ky. 172, 162 S. W. 501. A voluntary unincorporated association cannot be sued in the name of the corporation. The point is waived unless raised by special demurrer or by answer where the facts do not appear upon the face of the petition. United M. Workers of A. v. Cromer, 159 Ky. 605, 167 S. W. 891.

# (2) Garnishee may show.

A garnishee may show by affidavit that there is a defect of parties. Forepaugh v. Appold, 17 B. M. 625.

# (3) Jurisdiction -objection made by answer-that is also a plea to the merits will not enter appearance.

Louisville Home Tel. Co. v. Beeler, 101 S. W. 397, 31 R. 19, 125 Ky. 366. The general appearance of the defendant in a bastardy proceeding, before making objection to the jurisdiction of the court, does not waive such objection. Com. v. Adkins, 169 Ky. 487, 184 S. W. 372.

# (4) Jurisdiction-objection to must be pointed out in pleading if it does not appear on the face of the petition that court has not jurisdiction.

Richardson v. L. & N. R. Co., 112 S. W. 582, 129 Ky. 449; and unless made in proper time and manner will be waived. Gillen v. I. C. R. Co., 125 S. W. 747, 137 Ky. 375; L. & N. R. R. v. Mitchell, 162 Ky. 253, 172 S. W. 527; Fentzka's Admr. v. Warwick Const. Co., 162 Ky. 580, 172 S. W. 1060. A petition did not show whether the county in which the action was brought, was or was not the proper county in which to bring it. The question of jurisdiction could not be raised by special demurrer. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Stewart, 163 Ky. 164, 173 S. W. 757.

# (5) Jurisdiction — appearance.

When the court has no jurisdiction of the subject-matter consent cannot give it. Fiddler v. Hall, 2 Met. 461; Barton v. Barton, 80 Ky. 212, 3 R. 746; 18 B. M. 259.

# (6) When the court has jurisdiction of the subject-matter, the objection for want of jurisdiction over the person may be waived by consent.

Hughes v. Hardesty, 12 Bush 364; Barton v. Barton, 80 Ky. 212, 3 R. 746. Defense to the merits of an action is waiver of the right to object for want of jurisdiction over the person of defendant. Baker v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 4 Bush 619; and so is filing a general demurrer to the petition.

General Rules of Pleading. [Title VII]

# § 119 [144] [Facts that need not be stated — private statute.]

1. Neither the evidence relied on by a party, nor presumptions of law, nor facts of which judicial notice is taken, excepting private statutes, shall be stated in a pleading.
2. [Private statute, how pleaded.] In pleading a private statute, it shall be sufficient to refer to it by stating its title and the day on which it became a law.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 205, 210f, 211, 427, 551d, 694, 719, 741, 878, 872, 875, 876; Forms Nos. 52, 60, 135.

Dowell v. C., O. & S. W. R. R., 90 Ky. 346, 12 R. 331, 14 S. W. 338.

See further as to what steps a defendant may take without entering his appearance, and when his appearance is entered, note 9, sec. 39.
Non-residents. The provision that a defect of parties appearing in petition is waived by failure to demur applies to actions against persons constructively summoned. Gill v. Johnson, 1 Met. 649.
Practice. Where the fact that plaintiff has not legal capacity to sue is relied upon in the answer and proved on the trial, the plaintiff cannot have judgment even if defendant fails to insist upon the defect by motion. Petty v. Malier, 14 B. M. 246.
When the answer discloses a defect of parties, the action should not be dismissed or abated, but an order entered requiring the additional parties to be made on pain of dismissal without prejudice, and the action postponed until necessary parties are made. Carpenter v. Miles, 17 B. M. 598; 3 Met. 133.
Pendency of another action. An action will be abated by reason of the pendency of another; but they must both be prosecuted for the same cause of action. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Ohio Val. Tie Co., 161 Ky. 212, 170 S. W. 633.

# § 119.

Judicial notice. The courts take judicial notice of all acts and resolutions of the General Assembly. Kentucky Statutes, secs. 1624, 1625. As to what facts judicial notice is taken of, see Newman's Pleading and Practice; and as to presumptions of law, see Ibid.; Gregory v. McFarland, 1 Duv. 50; Williams v. Gordon, 11 Bush 693.
It is unnecessary to prove facts of common knowledge or the meaning of words in the vernacular language. Pedigo v. Com., 24 R. 1029, 70 S. W. 659.
Private statutes. As to what are private statutes in the meaning of this section, see City of Cov. v. Voskotter, 80 Ky. 219, 3 R. 749; City of Cov. v. Hoadley, 83 Ky. 444, 7 R. 487; Board of Ed. v. L., H. & St. L. R. R., 110 Ky. 932, 23 R. 376, 62 S. W. 1125. The Court of Appeals will take judicial notice of private acts when they have been relied on in the lower court, although that fact is not formally stated in the bill of exceptions. Lackey v. R. & L. T. P. Co., 17 B. M. 43; Bixler v. Parker, 3 Bush 166.
(In pleading a private statute it is necessary to state its title and the day on which it became a law. Zable v. Lou. Baptist Home, 92 Ky. 89, 13 R. 385, 17 S. W. 212; Nichols v. Bardwell Lodge, 105 Ky. 168, 20 R. 1236, 48 S. W. 426; Rudd v. Deposit Bank, 105 Ky. 443, 20 R. 1497, 49 S. W. 207; Gaertner v. Lou. Stone Co., 114 Ky. 160, 24 R. 940, 70 S. W. 293.)
City ordinances that court is required by statute to take judicial notice of, may be pleaded the same as private statutes. Gaertner v. Lou. Stone Co., 114 Ky. 160, 24 R. 940, 70 S. W. 293; Tenn. Brick Co. v. Barker, 22 R. 1069, 60 S. W. 690; Cabell v. City of Henderson, 28 R. 89, 88 S. W. 1095.
Charter of town is not a private statute that need be pleaded as required by this section - it is a public act. Town v. Weighans, 19 R. 1979, 44 S. W. 985.
A stipulation in a pleading that a private statute has not been repealed will be construed to mean that it has not been repealed by a special act. City of Lou. v. Vreeland, 131 S. W. 195, 140 Ky. 400.
A petition for the removal of an action which did not state the title of a private statute but only the pleader's conclusion as to its contents was defective. Illinois Cent. R. Co. v. Shegog, 103 S. W. 323, 31 R. 691, 126 Ky. 252.
Objection that a private statute is not well pleaded must be made by special demurrer. L. & N. R. Co. v. Robbins, 111 S. W. 283; Rudd v. Deposit Bank, 105 Ky. 443, 49 S. W. 207, 20 R. 1495.

Title VIIj General Rules of Pleading.

# § 120 [145] [Writing on which action founded to be filed.]

If an action, counter-claim, set-off or cross-petition be founded on a note, bond, bill or other writing, as evidence of indebtedness, it must be filed as a part of the pleading, if in the power of the party to produce it: and if not filed, the reason for the failure must be stated in the pleading; if upon an account, a copy thereof must be filed with the pleading. (Action on lost zeriting, sec.7; other writings may be filed as evidence, sec. 128; rehen read as genuine, sec. 527; meaning of "zeriting," sec. 732.)

# General Rules of Pleading

# § 121 [147] [Irrelevant or redundant matter stricken out.]

Irrelevant or redundant matter in a pleading shall be stricken out, upon or without motion, at the cost of the party whose pleading contains it.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 383a, 383b, 384, 550d, 550g, 872, 880.

# (4) Record - writing filed part of.

Notes sued on and filed with the petition constitute part of the record. Dodd v. King, 1 Met. 430; Totten v. Cooke, 2 Met. 275; Harmon v. Wilson, 1 Duv. 322; Day Lumber Co. v. Mack, 24 R. 640, 69 S. W. 712; and see Gilbert v. Bamberger, 19 R. 1833, 44 S. W. 421, pointing out difference between this section and sec. 128.

# (5) An account not filed with and as part of the petition, but simply lodged in the papers before appearance or answer, is not part of the record, and need not be noticed in the answer.

Webb v. Jeffries, 2 Bush 221; and see Dodd v. King, 1 Met. 430.

# (6) In an action against a common carrier to recover the value of baggage lost, a schedule filed with the petition and made part thereof is a part of the case, and the jury had a right to regard the statements of fact it contained as admissions upon the part of the plaintiff.

L., C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Mahan, 8 Bush 184.

# (7) Rule to file-practice.

If a writing which is the evidence of the indebtedness or liability constituting the cause of action be not filed, the adverse party may ask a rule to have it filed, and upon failure to file it, or to offer a sufficient reason for not so doing, may have action dismissed; but if he fail to take steps requiring it to be filed, and judgment is rendered on the pleading, it cannot be reversed because the writing was not filed.

Preston v. Roberts, 12 Bush 570.

# (8) A rule to file title papers, or papers of any kind where they are not referred to in the pleadings, is unauthorized, except upon sufficient grounds made out by affidavit, showing that the filing of the papers is necessary to the substantial ends of justice.

Ruggles v. Moore, 18 B. M. 821; and see Marion Nat. Bank v. Abell, 88 Ky. 428, 10 R. 980, 11 S. W. 300; for a full statement of practice regarding production of books and documents.

# (9) Writing—to be filed—waiver.

Although a writing upon which action is founded is not filed, the adverse party cannot complain of it after judgment.

McGhee v. Sutherland, 84 Ky. 198, 8 R. 87, 1 S. W. 5; 12 Bush 570; especially if the question has not been raised by demurrer.

# (10) Open account sued on need not be set out in the body of the pleading if it is referred to and made a part of the pleading.

Snowden v. Snowden, 96 S. W. 922, 29 R. 1112.

# (11) Generally.

A party who introduces a writing executed by his adversary in support of an issue is entitled to an instruction based upon it, although it may not have been filed under this section.

Edge v. Ott, 151 Ky. 672, 152 S. W. 764.

# (12) An ordinance for a street improvement is a writing on which an action is founded within this section.

City of Richmond v. Madison Female Institute, 153 Ky. 301, 155 S. W. 371.

# (13) A writing which does not evidence the indebtedness is not required to be filed under this section.

Kraver v. City of Henderson, 155 Ky. 633, 160 S. W. 257.

# (14) In an action by the Commonwealth against the secretary of the State Board of Health to recover money alleged to be misappropriated, a report of the State Examiner and Inspector is not an exhibit under Code, sec. 120, or Code, sec. 128. It has no evidential value.

Com. v. McCormack, 177 Ky. 474, 197 S. W. 977.

# Title VIIJ

# General Rules of Pleading.

# § 122 [148] [Judgment—how pleaded.]

In pleading a judgment or determination of a court or officer, it is not necessary to state the facts conferring jurisdiction; but it shall be sufficient to state that the judgment or determination was duly given or made.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 207, 208, 208a, 220, 240, 426f, 467, 700, 703, 800, 825; Forms Nos. 10, 38, 39, 40, 41, 43, 45, 46, 47, 48, 49, 55, 61, 62, 75, 82, 93, 103, 113, 134, 140, 142, 146, 153, 186, 241.

# § 123 [150] [Libel or slander—unnecessary allegations.]

In an action for libel or slander, it is not necessary to state any extrinsic facts for the purpose of showing the application to the plaintiff of the alleged defamatory matter.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 207, 208, 230; Forms Nos. 94-99.

Buckles v. Lambert, 4 Met. 330; and see Bonney v. Reardin, 6 Bush 34, but the court should require all redundant or irrelevant matter to be stricken out, so that the real issue may not be obscured or confused. Hale v. Grogan, 99 Ky. 170, 18 R. 46, 35 S. W. 282; Brashear v. Letcher Co., 22 R. 1763, 61'S. W. 285.

# § 122.

(1) Administrator - appointment of, how pleaded. The allegation in the petition, that appellees were by an order of the Hardin County Court appointed administrators of A. S. Gardner, deceased, and qualified as such, is a substantial compliance with section 1:22 of the Civil Code. Warfield v. Gardner, 79 Ky. 583, 3 R. 423.

(2) Bankruptcy-pleading discharge. In pleading discharge in bankruptcy, it is not necessary to allege that the court granting the discharge had jurisdiction, or to state the facts conferring jurisdiction; but it is necessary to state in substance that discharge was duly granted. Laidley v. Cummings, 83 Ky. 606, 7 R. 616.

(3) Foreign judgment. In action on foreign judgment it is necessary to allege facts showing that the court rendering the judgment had jurisdiction of the subject-matter and the person of the defendant. Gebhard v. Garnier, 12 Bush 321; Wood v. Wood, 78 Ky. 624, I R. 358; 10 Bush 160; 12 Bush 274; Montgomery v. Consolidated Boat Co., 115 Ky. 157, 24 R 2004, 72 S. W. 816.

(4) Any defamatory words calculated to degrade or injure the reputation of a person in society, when written and published, are libelous and actionable. Allen

# General Rules of Pleading

# § 124 [151] [Slander or libel-what answer may state.]

In the actions mentioned in section 123, the defendant may state the truth of the alleged libel or slander, and any mitigating circumstances; and, whether he prove the justification or not, he may prove the mitigating circumstances to reduce the amount of damages.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 207, 424g, 426e, 569, 829, 856; Forms Nos. 243, 244.

# § 124a. [Request for retraction, proof of--lack of malice punitive damages denied—mitigation of damages, plea allowed in.]

That in any civil action for libel, charging the publication of an erroneous statement, alleged to be libelous, it shall be relevant and competent evidence for either party to prove the fact that the plaintiff requested retraction or omitted to request retraction. The defendant may also allege and give proof that the matter alleged to have been published, and to be libelous, was published without malice, and that the defendant in the next regular issue of the newspaper or publication, after receiving demand in writing or within seven days if no such demand is made to correct and to retract said statement, or in the next regular issue of the newspaper or publication did publish a sufficient correction, retraction, explanation or rectification, as conspicuously and publicly as that in which said alleged libelous statement was published in the same type and in the same place in at least two successive issues of the same periodical publication accompanied by editorials in which the alleged slander is specifically repudiated. Upon proof of such facts, the plaintiff shall not be entitled to punitive damages; and the defendant shall be liable only to pay actual damages. And upon the publication of such correction, retraction, explanation or rectification, the defendant may plead same in mitigation of damages. (March -, 1010; c. 104, p. 294.)

# §124. (1 Inconsistent defenses.

The defendant can not plead inconsistent defenses in slander suit, as in one paragraph admitting and in another denying the speaking of the words. Rooney v. Tierney, 82 Ky. 253, 6 R. 231; Lane v. Pryant, 100 Ky. 138, 18 R. 658, 37 S. W. 177.

(6) Privileged communications - see Stewart v. Hall, 83 Ky. 375. 7 R. 323; Nix v. Caldwell, 81 Ky. 293, 5 R. 275; Morgan v. Booth, 13 Bush 480; Campbell v. Bannister, 79 Ky. 205, 2 R. 72; Tanner v. Stephenson, 138 Ky. 578, 128 S. W. 879.

(7) Innuendo. An innuendo can not extend the meaning of words beyond their natural import. Spears v. McCoy, 155 Ky. 140.

Title II] General Rules of Pleading. [§124b-125

# § 124b. [Assault and battery-defense--provocation.]

In all civil actions for damages inflicted by an assault, or by an assault and battery, the defendant shall have the right to plead as a defense to the claim for punitive damages, and to introduce in evidence in mitigation of damages, any matter of provocation which preceded the assault or assault and battery. If the matter of provocation prompted the assault or assault and battery, and was of a nature as to cause a person of ordinary prudence and judgment to take the action taken by the defendant. (March 16, 1906, c. 32, p. 262.)

# § 125 [152] [Land-pleadings in action for—burden of proof-costs.]

1. [Petition to describe.] A petition for the recovery of land, or for its subjection to a demand of the plaintiff, must describe it so that it may be identified.
2. [Answer—what it may contain—burden of proof-costs.] In an action for the recovery of land, the answer of the defendant must state whether or not he claims it, or any part of it; and, if he claims part of it, his answer must so describe such part that it may be identified. The making of such statement, or of such statement and description, shall not, of itself, throw on the defendant the burden of proving his right to the land claimed by him; but, if the plaintiff recover any part thereof, the defendant shall pay the costs of the action.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 363a, 424b, 424n, 437b, 706, 719; Forms Nos. 76, 238.

# Pleading - evidence.

When the only answer is a denial of the speaking of the words, nothing is admissible in evidence which tends either to prove the truth of the charge or to establish a defense on the ground that they were spoken on a justifiable occasion. A plea of justification must admit the truth of the words spoken. Harper v. Harper, 10 Bush 418. This case, in so far as it holds that inconsistent defenses may be pleaded, is overruled in Lane v. Bryant, 100 Ky. 138, 18 R. 658, 37 S. W. 584; and see Shipp v. Patton, 93 S. W. 1033, 29 R. 481, 123 Ky. 65.

1. A denial of knowledge or information as to speaking the words charged to be slanderous is not sufficient, a positive denial must be made. Macauley v. Elrod, 16 R. 291, 27 S. W. 867.
2. Rumor. A corporation engaged in the publication of a newspaper was sued for libel for having published an article which implied a rumor that the plaintiff had committed murder. It was competent for the defendant to plead that the rumor referred to was current in the city in which the publication was made and in the neighborhood where the plaintiff lived. Reid v. Sun Pub. Co., 158 Ky. 727, 166 S. W. 245.

# § 125.

1. Pleading. In action to recover land in possession of defendant it is only necessary that he shall deny title in plaintiff; it is not necessary to set up title in himself. Simms v. Simms, 88 Ky. 642, 11 R. 131, 11 S. W. 665; as to evidence under traverse, see Brent v. Long, 99 Ky. 245, 18 R. 137, 35 S. W. 640.
2. Petition for recovery of land must so describe it that it may be identified and defendant, by asserting claim to the land, does not assume burden of proving it. Howard v. Lock, 15 R. 154, 22 S. W. 332; Owensboro R. R. v. Barker, 15 R. 175, 22 S. W. 444; and see Bryant v. Strunk, 28 R. 556, 89 S. W. 549.
3. Answer of defendant, failing to allege that land claimed by him is any part of land described in petition, is defective. Bailey v. McConnell, 12 R. 473, 14 S. W. 337; the answer must state what part of land if any defendant claims and describe it. Brent v. Long, 99 Ky.

General Rules of Pleading

§ 126 [153] [Allegations not denied admitted—exception.] Every material allegation of a pleading must, for the purposes of the action, be taken as true, unless specifically traversed, excepting these, which must be proved, though not traversed. (Material allegation defined, sec. 127.)

1. [Allegations against defendant under disability.] Allegations of a petition, or cross-petition, against a defendant who is under any disability except coverture.

245, 18 R. 137, 35 S. W. 640; Hackworth v. Layne, 22 R. 185, 56 S. W. 817. An answer described certain lands. Defendants showed title to a part of said lands. Defendants were entitled to a judgment as to that part. Foster v. Roberts, 179 Ky. 752, 201 S. W. 334.

(4) Description of land sought to be recovered or subjected must be sufficient to identify it or else a sale under the judgment will not pass title. Blackwell v. Townsend, 91 Ky. 609, 13 R. 291, 16 S. W. 587; McNew v. Martin, 22 R. 1275, 60 S. W. 412; but a sufficient description in an exhibit filed with petition will answer. DeHaven v. DeHaven, 104 Ky. 41, 20 R. 663, 46 S. W. 215; Day Lumber Co. v. Mack, 24 R. 640, 69 S. W. 712; and see Downing v. Thompson, 92 S. W. 290, 28 R. 1182; Quigley v. Bean, 125 S. W. 727, 137 Ky. 325.

(5) In action to recover land the plaintiff need not set out his title and he may recover on a possessory title without pleading the character of title he has. Asher v. Howard, 91 S. W. 270, 28 R. 1097, 122 Ky. 175; L. & N. R. Co. v. Taylor, 138 Ky. 437, 128 S. W. 325.

(6) Where the answer claims the whole of the land described in the petition it is not necessary to describe it. Holland v. Coleman, 114 S. W. 305; Paducah Cooperage Co. v. Paducah Veneer Co., 121 S. W. 986, 135 Ky. 53.

(7) A defective answer may be cured by a verdict. Bryant v. Prewitt, 117 S. W. 343, 132 Ky. 799.

(8) Defendant is not required to plead title by adverse possession in order to prove such title. Asher v. Howard, 91 S. W. 270, 28 R. 1097, 122 Ky. 175.

(9) A petition which failed to describe the real estate on which a trespass was charged to have been committed was fatally defective. Hackney v. Dillan, 9 Ky. Opinions 38.

(10) An action to quiet title is an action for the recovery of land within the meaning of this section. Patton v. Stewart, 173 Ky. 220, 190 S. W. 1062.

(11) Where a petition seeks to enforce a vendor's lien it should describe the property. (Subsec. 1.) Baird v. Prewitt, 158 Ky. 793, 166 S. W. 771.

(12) Under this section and Statute, sec. 889, plaintiff in ejectment is entitled to recover costs where he obtained judgment for a portion of the land in question. Shanahan v. McIntire, 169 Ky. 160, 183 S. W. 529.

§ 126. (1) Assessment of recovery without evidence - allegations concerning value arising under contract, or the amount of damage claimed in actions sounding in tort need not be proven if accompanied by a promise to pay or a statement of facts showing an implied promise to pay, but in actions in trespass or tort for damages only proof must be heard. Burchett v. Herald, 98 Ky. 530, 17 R. 918, 33 S. W. 85.

(2) A recovery without proof may be had either in actions, in contracts, or tort, where there is either an express or an implied promise to pay the amount sought to be recovered. Harris v. Mertz, 82 Ky. 200, 6 R. 65; Merchants' Dispatch Co. v. Hoskins, 19 R. 799, 41 S. W. 31; 80 Ky. 61, 3 R. 562; and see sec. 379 as to manner of assessment when it is necessary.

(3) In an action to recover for use and occupation of land, where the petition stated facts raising an implied promise to pay, its allegations could be taken as true unless traversed. 80 Ky. 61, 3 R. 562; but in an action for damages caused by trespass the damages must be proven unless there is an allegation to pay or facts showing an implied promise. Mize v. Jackson, 17 R. 750, 32 S. W. 467.

Title I General Rules of Pleading. [§ 126]

2. Allegations of answer—seeking recovery against new party. Allegations of an answer, or reply, so far as it states a set-off or counter-claim against a new party who is under any disability except coverture.

3. Against defendant constructively summoned. Allegations against a defendant constructively summoned, who has not appeared in the action.

4. Concerning value or amount of damages. Allegations concerning value or amount of damage, not accompanied by an allegation of an express promise, or by a statement of facts showing an implied promise, to pay such value or damage; such allegations, so accompanied, need not be proved unless traversed. (Traverse defined, sec. 113; judgment upon failure to plead, sec. 379; trial by court, sec. 312; by jury, sec. 313; petition for divorce not taken as true by failure to answer, sec. 422.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 230, 402, 407, 408, 419, 419a, 422, 426c, 453, 509, 512, 512a, 861; Form No. 411.

It was held even under Code of 1854 that in an action on a medical account, or for goods sold and delivered, the averments of petition would be taken as true if not traversed. Harris v. Ray, 15 B. M. 628; Mills v. Brown, 2 Met. 404; and see Harris v. Doughitt, 141 Ky. 645, 133 S. W. 550.

(5) In an action for assault and battery the jury have a right, in the absence of answer and proof, to render a judgment for more than nominal damages. Rogers v. Aulick, 2 Duv. 419; and see Adkins v. Kendrick, 115 S. W. 814, 131 Ky. 779.

In an action to recover for the services of a negro boy for one year, the law and facts were submitted to the court, and without hearing proof the judge assessed the amount of recovery. The court says "that in an action of assumpsit for work and labor, the jury have a right, in the absence of all proof of the value of such labor, and from their own knowledge, to find a verdict for the price of such labor, and the court has same power when case is submitted to it." Baum v. Winston, 3 Met. 127. And see further, sec. 379.

(6) Infants—in an action against to correct a mistake in a deed the allegations of petition should be proven. Dever v. Dever, 19 R. 1988, 44 S. W. 996; and so when conveyance is attacked as fraudulent. Choates v. Long, 96 S. W. 554, 29 R. 942; Isom v. Holcomb, 110 S. W. 249; Anderson v. Anderson, 98 S. W. 281, 30 R. 250.

(7) Interrogatory—failure to answer. Judgment cannot be given against a defendant because he failed to answer an interrogatory propounded in the petition, when the material allegations thereof are denied. Burnett v. Garnett, 18 B. M. 68.

(8) Interest should be allowed on cash advances, not as a matter of discretion in the jury or court, but as legal damages. Field v. Burnam, 3 Bush 518; and is allowable on an account as matter of law from the time when it becomes payable. Henderson Mfg. Co. v. Lowell Machine Shops, 86 Ky. 668, 9 R. 831, 7 S. W. 142.

LAND, ACTIONS TO SELL.

(9) In an action to sell land of a decedent to pay his debt if an heir of the decedent is an infant defendant, a judgment directing a sale is erroneous unless it appear affirmatively, both by pleading and evidence that the decedent's personalty is not sufficient to pay his debts. A settlement of decedent's administrator referred to in the petition as an exhibit, but not filed until after the sale will not sustain the judgment or the sale. Luscher v. Julian's Admr., 173 Ky. 150, 190 S. W. 692.

(10) In an action to sell land for indivisibility no evidence was introduced, although one defendant was constructively summoned. A sale under the judgment in the action was valid.

General Rules of Pleading. [Title VII]

Huber v. Johnson, 174 Ky. 697, 192 S. W. 821.

1. In actions under Code, sec. 490, subsec. 2, evidence of indivisibility cannot be made as to infants by affidavit. This rule cannot be waived by the statutory guardian. A ruling on the question does not seem to have been necessary to a decision of this case. (Subsec. l.) Pollard v. Hamilton, 179 Ky. 284, 200 S. W. 621.
2. Non-resident. Allegations against a defendant constructively summoned must be proven. Com. v. Vanderbilt, 26 R. 716, 82 S. W. 426; but there are exceptions to this rule and in an action against a defendant constructively summoned, it is not necessary to prove the execution of the note sued on. Gill v. Johnson, 1 Met. 649; Ball v. Poore, 81 Ky. 26, 4 R. 746. In an action to sell indivisible land the court held that proof was necessary; but as land had been sold, the purchaser would not be disturbed. Perkins v. McCarley, 97 Ky. 43, 16 R. 801, 29 S. W. 867.
3. Payment-plea by one of several obligors. In an action against several obligors on a note, a plea of payment by one presents a defense for all, and there can be no judgment against those not answering until plea is disposed of. Rouse v. Howard, I Duv. 31.
4. Practice. In an action for the conversion of property the defendant failed to answer, and a judgment was rendered pro confesso except as to the allegation of the value of the property, that being tried at a subsequent term by a jury. The judgment pro confesso concluded all the litigation except as to value. Kendrick v. Fields, 2 Bush 153.
5. An order taking a petition in equity for confessed should not be made before the process is served upon all the defendants concerned in interest. Alexander v. Quigley, 2 Duv. 399. See secs. 366, 367, as to trial of equity actions.
6. The provision that allegations of value or amount of damage cannot be taken as true by failure to answer them applies to equitable as well as ordinary actions. Clark v. Seaton, 18 B. M. 226.
7. If the jury in an action for damages simply find a verdict "for the plaintiff" and fail to assess amount of recovery, the court cannot render judgment and should set verdict aside. C. O. & S. W. R. R. v. Maddox, 19 R. 966, 42 S. W. 1124; and see L. & N. R. R. v. Hartwell, 99 Ky. 436, 18 R. 745, 36 S. W. 183. Dehoney v. Sanford, 2 Bush 169; Mead v. Nevill, 2 Duv. 280; Marrs v. Prather, 3 Met. 196; Smith v. Curtis, 1 Duv. 281.

Title VII General Rules of Pleading.

# § 127 [154] [Material allegation defined.]

A material allegation is one which is necessary for the statement or support of a cause of action or defense.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 402, 512.

# § 128 [15s] [Writings relied on as evidence may be filed—when part of record.]

1. [Filing writing relied on as evidence.] In addition to the writings which a party is required by section 120 to file as the foundation of his action or defense, he may file, as an exhibit, with his pleading, or with leave of court at any time pending the action, any writing upon which he may intend to rely as evidence.
2. [When part of record in equitable action.] In an equitable action, such exhibits shall constitute part of the record, unless it show that they were not used on the trial.

Gould v. Bonds, 1 Bush 189; 14 B. M. 393; 18 B.M. 227; 5 Bush 426.

A private sale of this land afterward made by him was void. The devisees in this case were infants. Schlickman v. Dusing, 180 Ky. 506, 203 S. W. 295.

# § 127.

(1) The provisions of this section that material allegations of a pleading must be taken as true if not specifically traversed, are mandatory. Rose v. Cecil, 167 Ky. 608, 181 S. W. 170.

Under this section, averments, by a contestant of an election, that the votes cast for him were legal, is not material, and do not excuse his failure to reply to an affirmative defense. Rose v. Cecil, 167 Ky. 608, 181 S. W. 170.

# § 128.

1. (1) Change in section. This section is materially different from sec. 155 of Code of 1854. For cases construing sec. 155 see Harman v. Wilson, 1 Duv. 322; Haney v. Tenpest, 3 Met. 95; Vaughn v. Mills, 18 B. M. 633; Ruggles v. Moore, 18 B. M. 821.
2. (2) Construction of section-this section does not relate to writings which a party is required by sec. 120 to file. Gilbert v. Bamberger, 19 R. 1833, 44 S. W. 421. Beeler v. C. Tel. Co., 150 Ky. 257.
3. (3) A party is not required to file bonds, deeds or title papers on which he relies, and that he intends to offer as evidence, but may do so. Burkhart v. Loughridge, 116 Ky. 604, 25 R. 815, 76 S. W. 397.
4. (4) See note 18, sec. 90, as to effect of exhibits filed with a pleading.
5. (5) Writings-genuineness of in an equitable action may be proved orally; see sec. 552; Cosby v. Wickliffe, 7 B. M. 121; Talbott v. Bedford, 21 R. 897, 53 S. W. 294.
6. (6) Transcript of evidence in another case may be filed with a pleading. Blocker v. City of Owensboro, 110 S. W. 369, 129 Ky. 75.
7. (7) A writing mentioned in a pleading but not filed with it was held incompetent without proof of execution. Beeler's Ex. v. Cumb. Tel. & Tel. Co., 150 Ky. 257, 150 S. W. 335.
8. (8) A party who introduces a writing executed by his adversary in support of an issue is entitled to an instruction based upon it, although it may not have been filed under this section. Edge v. Ott, 151 Ky. 672, 152 S. W. 764.

General Rules of Pleading

# 3. When part of record in ordinary action.

In an ordinary action, such exhibits shall not constitute part of the record, unless it show that they were used on the trial. (Meaning of "writing," sec. 732; when read as genuine, sec. 527. See further as to exhibits, sec. 552.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 204b, 204c, 204d, 204f; Forms Nos. 10, 38, 39, 55, 72, 117, 419. See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 65.

(9) In an action by the Commonwealth against the secretary of the State Board of Health to recover money alleged to be misappropriated, a report of the State Examiner and Inspector is not an exhibit under Code, sec. 120, or Code, sec. 128. It has no evidential value. Com. v. McCormack, 177 Ky. 474, 197 S. W. 977.

Title VIIj    Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments.    [§ 129

# CHAPTER VIII.

# MISTAKES IN PLEADINGS-AMENDMENTS.

# § 129. Variance when material-amendment.

# § 130. Immaterial variance-practice-amendment.

# § 131. Variance when fatal-action on contract.

# § 132. Amendment before answer-notice.

# § 133. Demurrer overruled, party may plead.

# § 134. Amendments generally—trivial error.

# § 135. Supplemental pleading-action to enforce lien.

# § 137. Verification of petition before trial sufficient.

# § 138. Verification not necessary after trial begins.

# § 139. Verification—amendment allowed without.

§ 129 [15e] [Variance when material—amendment.] No variance between pleadings and proof is material, which does not mislead a party, to his prejudice, in maintaining his action or defense upon the merits. A

§ 129. (1) Material variance. Where petition alleged that certain property was purchased from defendant and proof showed that it was a purchase of double the amount from defendant and another the variance is fatal. Gray v. Garrison, 2 R. 218; and see further, Bannister v. Weatherford. 7 B. M. 271; Howard v. Chiles, 8 B. M. 377.

Judgment can not be rendered against a defendant as an individual when the petition shows that he executed the obligation sued on in his fiducial capacity as administrator. Lusk v. Anderson, 1 Met. 426.

(3) Plaintiff instituted suit upon a note executed by the defendant to another person, and alleged that the note had been executed to himself; it had not been assigned to plaintiff. Held that the variance was fatal, and plaintiff could not recover. Dodd v. King, 1 Met. 430.

(4) In an action upon an express contract no recovery can be had on proof of an implied contract in the absence of an amendment conforming the pleadings to the proof. Newton v. Field, 98 Ky. 186, 17 R. 769, 32 S. W. 623; and see further, Bull v. McCrea, 8 B. M. 422.

(5) In an action for injuries sustained by being thrown against a mail crane, the plaintiff alleged that he was a workman on the train. In fact he was not a workman on the train, but a section hand. Upon the entire record, however, this error was not prejudicial nor was there a fatal variance between the pleading and the proof. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Jesse, 159 Ky. 450, 167 S. W. 407.

(6) In a certain action for personal injuries there was no such variance between the pleading and proof as to render the variance fatal. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Spatig, 158 Ky. 263, 104 S. W. 811.

(7) Negligence. The allegation of willful negligence necessarily includes all inferior grades, and, upon failing to establish his right to punitive damages, plaintiff may recover compensatory damages, if the defendant is a railroad company and the person injured was not at the time an employe. L., C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Case, 9 Bush 728; Claxton v. L. & B. S. R. R. Co., 13 Bush 636. And a general allegation that injury was caused by negligence and carelessness of defendant is sufficient. Chiles v. Drake, 2 Met. 146.

(8) Negligence-where plaintiff specifies the character of negligence he relies on he cannot recover by showing a different state of negligence. McCain v. I. & N. R. R. Co., 13 R. 809, 18 S. W. 537; Thomas v. L. & N. R. R., 18 R. 164, 35 S. W. 910; nor can he prove a different state of negligence from that set out in petition. Greer v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 34 Ky. 169, 14 R. 876, 21 S. W. 649.

# § 129

# Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments.

party who claims to have been so misled must show that fact to the satisfaction of the court; and, thereupon, the court may order the pleading to be amended, upon such terms as may be just. (See further, secs. 130, 131.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 622, 639, 885.

# (9) Practice.

The proper mode to take advantage of a variance between allegation and proof is a motion to instruct the jury as in case of a non-suit. Mitcherson v. Grays, 4 B. M. 400.

# (10)

A party who claims to have been misled by a variance must show that fact to the satisfaction of the court and then the court may order or permit the pleadings amended on such terms as may be just. East Jellico Co. v. Golden, 25 R. 2056, 79 S. W. 291; Kearney v. City, I Met. 339; Simpson v. Carr, 25 R. 849, 76 S. W. 346; Chicago R. R. Co. v. Wilson, 25 R. 525, 76 S. W. 138; Henderson Brew. Co. v. Folden, 25 R. 969, 76 S. W. 520; Tyler v. Coleman, 29 R. 1270, 97 S. W. 373; Vaught v. Hogue, 107 S. W. 757, 32 R. 1061.

# (11)

Variance—not material. If the variance between the answer and proof is not such as to mislead the plaintiff in maintaining his action, and does not affect his substantial rights, it will not be ground for reversal. Gains v. Deposit Bank, 19 R. 171, 39 S. W. 438; I. C. R. v. Smith, 118 S. W. 933, 133 Ky. 732; Central Coal Co. v. Thompson, 102 S. W. 272, 31 R. 276; Continental Co. v. Hunt, 90 S. W. 1056, 28 R. 1010. If the variance is material, the attention of the court should be called to it, and an effort made to have pleadings corrected. Woodcock v. Farrell, 1 Met. 437; see cases cited in preceding note and sec. 130.

# (12)

In an action against a carrier on a live stock contract where a verbal contract was alleged and a written contract proven the variance was held not material. Baltimore R. Co. v. Wood & Co., 114 S. W. 734, 130 Kv. S39; Illinois Cent. R. Co. v. Curry, 106 S. W. 294, 32 R. 513, 127 Ky. 643.

# (13)

In an action upon a writing purporting to be an unconditional guaranty, but which did not express the consideration upon which it was executed, the averments of the petition as to the consideration showed that the contract was conditional, depending for validity upon the performance of certain acts by the promisor. Held that there was no variance between the contract sued on and the allegation of the petition. Steadman v. Guthrie, 4 Met. 147.

# (14)

In an action to recover for services alleged to have been performed in 1863, proof that they were performed in 1864 is not a material variance. Gentry v. Doolin, I Bush 1. For other illustrations of immaterial variances, see cases cited in note 8.

# (15)

A bill of exchange payable in "current exchange" was declared on as a bill for money. Held that the variance was immaterial. Morrison v. Tate, 1 Met. 569.

# (16)

In a suit by a surety against a co-surety, to recover money paid for their principal, the petition failed to allege the insolvency of the principal, and no proof of his insolvency could be admitted. Bolling v. Doneghy, 1 Duv. 220.

# (17)

The allegation in the petition that the note sued on was executed and delivered to the plaintiffs, being admitted by failure to deny it, the variance between the petition and note in the name of one of the plaintiffs was waived, being immaterial. Anderson v. Rogers, I Bush 200.

# (18)

The variance between an allegation of the petition, in an action for injury by collision of plaintiff's horse with an automobile, causing the horse to run away, and proof that the horse became frightened and ran away because the automobile ran too close to the horse, is immaterial. Weil v. Hagan, 166 Ky. 750, 179 S. W. 835.

# (19)

Under this section the variance between the petition and the proof as to the time when the acts complained of occurred, was held not a material variance, where defendant knew the year in which the action was laid, and was not taken by surprise. Merritt v. Cravens, 168 Ky. 155. 181 S. W. 970.

# (20)

Where negligence is alleged in general terms, by a petition charging that plaintiff was struck by defendant's car, the variance caused by evidence that plaintiff was not struck by the car but slipped and fell under it while attempting to board the car, was held immaterial. Ky. Trac. & Terninal Co. v. Waits, 167 Ky. 236, 180 S. W. 356.

# (21)

The rules as to variance laid down by Civil Code, secs. 129 and 131, are applicable in a prosecution for false swear-

Title VIIj Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments. [§ 130-131]

# § 130 [157] [Immaterial variance-practice-amendment.]

If such variance be not material, the court may direct the fact to be found according to the evidence, and may order an immediate amendment.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 584, 622, 885.

# § 131 [158] [Variance when fatal-action on contract.]

If, however, the allegation of the claim or defense, to which the proof is directed, be unproved, not in some particular or particulars only, but in its general scope and meaning, it is not to be deemed a case of variance within the last two sections, but a failure of proof. [In an action on a contract alleged to have been made by several defendants, in the event the evidence shall show the contract to have been made with less than all those defendants by whom it is alleged to have been made, this shall not be deemed either a variance or failure of proof, but judgment may be rendered against the party or parties shown to be bound, and in favor of those shown not to be bound.] (Words in brackets added by Act of March 24, 1888, c. 667, p. 58)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 622, 639, 641, 885.

# Case References

(22) Plaintiff alleged that she was a passenger in defendant's automobile and was injured by the car turning over. Defendant claimed that the proof showed her to be a licensee only and that the car did not turn over but only collided with the obstruction, tilted on one side and stopped suddenly, thereby causing plaintiff's injury. Held, the variance is immaterial. Beard v. Klusmeier, 158 Ky. 153, 164 S. W. 319.

(23) Under secs. 129-131 and sec. 134, it was held error, in an action for false imprisonment, to deny the defendant the right to withdraw his denial of the arrest, and justify the arrest so as to conform to the proof. Griffin v. Russell, 161 Ky. 471, 170 S. W. 1192.

# § 130. Pleadings may be amended

during trial when variance is not material and even if it is material, court may permit amendments to cure variance. See cases cited in note 8, sec. 129.

# § 131. (1) Failure of proof-what is.

In Glossom v. Badgett, 6 Bush 97, it was held that in an action against two upon an alleged joint undertaking, a judgment against one upon proof that the contract was made alone with him could not be sustained. But since section was amended in an action upon a contract alleged to have been made by several, judgment may be rendered against any of the defendants who made the contract. Curran v. Stein, 110 Ky. 99, 22 R. 1575, 60 S. W. 839.

# (2) In an action against one,

the evidence disclosed that the defendant made the contracts sued on as a member of a firm, and for the firm. Held that a judgment against him was proper. Waits v. McClure, 10 Bush 763.

# (3) The rules as to variance

laid down by Civil Code, secs. 129 and 131, are applicable in a prosecution for false swearing. Fugate v. Com., 177 Ky. 794, 198 S. W. 240.

# (4) Where the petition in an action

against a telephone company alleged a contract to transmit and deliver a message, but the evidence shows merely an undertaking by defendant to furnish reasonable facilities for conversation by telephone, the variance is fatal. Gainesboro Tel. Co. v. Buckner, 160 Ky. 604, 169 S. W. 1000.

# (5) In an action for damages for personal injuries

which had been received through negligence a certain variance was not a failure of proof. Prestonsburg Coal Co. v. Wallen, 159 Ky. 369, 167 S. W. 395.

# (6) In a certain action

the variance of the evidence from the allegations amounted to a total failure of proof. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Collinsworth, 152 Ky. 197, 153 S. W. 241.

# (7) Certain evidence

was held not to be a failure of proof nor even a variance. Weirich's Exr. v. Weirich, 153 Ky. 630, 156 S. W. 370.
