# § 132-133] Mistakes in Pleadings—Amendments.

# [Title VII]

# § 132 [150] [Amendment before answer—notice.]

The plaintiff may, at any time before answer, amend his petition without leave; but, unless the amendment be filed five days before the term at which the defendant is summoned to answer, he shall give to the defendant notice, of one day, of his intention to amend. (May be filed in vacation to cure defect, sec. 109.)

# $ l0ol.

The plaintiff may, without leave, amend his petition at any time before answer is filed. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1001.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 576.

# § 133 [160] [Demurrer overruled, party may plead.]

Upon a demurrer being overruled, the party demurring may file an answer, reply or additional pleading.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 581.

# § 132.

(1) Amendment-notice of filing. One day's notice is only required where the plaintiff amends his petition without leave, and less than five days before term. When the amendment is made by leave of court, and in presence of defendant, no notice is necessary. Hunt v. Semonin, 79 Ky. 270, 2 R. 334; Bryant v. Cooney, 19 R. 423, 40 S. W. 918.

(2) New cause of action -summons necessary. Summons is necessary on an amended petition which states a new or distinct cause of action. Cecil v. Sowards, 10 Bush 96; Cope v. Slayden, 24 R. 1734, 72 S. W. 284; although it is filed after summons has been executed on petition, and before answer. Rutledge v. Vanmeter, 8 Bush 354; and see note 1 to sec. 40.

(3) If amendment does not present a new or distinct cause of action, summons is not necessary. Joyes v. Hamilton, 10 Bush 544; L., C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Case, 9 Bush 728; McKay v. Maycs, 16 R. 862, 29 S. W. 327; Adams Oil Co. v. Christmas, 101 Ky. 564, 19 R. 760, 41 S. W. 545.

(4) Where the original petition sought to make defendant liable as indorser of a bill, and an amended petition charged him with the conversion of the bill, the amendment set up a new cause of action. So, if A should institute an action against B upon a note alleged to have been executed for goods sold and delivered, and it appeared B was not liable on the note, if A should file an amended petition upon an account for the same goods, it would present a new cause of action. Hyatt v. Bank of Kv., 8 Bush 193.

(5) In an action to sell land owned by infants summons on petition was properly executed, afterward an amended petition was filed asking the sale of town lots owned by them also. Summons on amended petition was necessary. Howard v. Singleton, 94 Ky. 336, 15 R. 309, 22 S. W. 337.

(6) Practice. The plaintiff has a right to file an amended petition before answer filed, and the court has no judicial discretion to reject it. Champion v. Robertson, 4 Bush I7; L. & N. R. R. v. Hall, 115 Ky. 567, 24 R. 2487, 74 S. W. 280; but after answer is filed leave of court must be obtained to file an amended petition. Mount v. L. & N. R. R., 2 R. 221; Petry v. Petry, 142 Ky. 564, 134 S. W. 922; and see further sec. 134 and notes thereto.

(7) Construction of section-this section applies when no answer has been filed and no demurrer sustained, sec. 94 applies when a demurrer has been sustained. Quinn v. Cincinnati R. Co., 97 S. W. 379, 30 R. 15.

# § 133. Answer after demurrer overruled.

A defendant was permitted to file a demurrer after the time allowed by law. It was overruled. He then tendered an answer which set up a defense. It was error to overrule the motion to file the answer. Chicago Bonding & Surety Co. v. Johnson, 180 Ky. 589, 203 S. W. 317.

# §134.

(1) Amendments-- departure. In an action by a ward against her guardian, to recover on an assignment of a note, an amended petition alleging that the guardian had failed to account

Title II Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments.

# 134 [161] [Amendments generally—court may require—trivial error.]

The court may, at any time, in furtherance of justice, and on such terms as may be proper, cause or permit a pleading or proceeding to be amended, by adding or striking out the name of a party; or, by correcting a mistake in the name of a party, or a mistake in any other respect; or by inserting other allegations material to the case; or, if the amendment do not change substantially the claim or defense, by conforming the pleading or proceeding to the facts proved. And, if a proceeding taken by a party fail to conform in any respect to the provisions of this Code, the court may permit an amendment of such proceeding, so as to make it conform.

for money in his hands was a departure. and should not have been allowed; but, if filed, plaintiff should have been required to elect which action she would prosecute. Humphrey v. Hughes, 79 Ky. 487, 3 R. 273.

An amended petition seeking to recover on claim originally sued on, but setting up a different evidence of the claim, is not a departure. Adams Oil Co. v. Christmas, 101 Ky. 564, 19 R. 760, 41 S. W. 545.

# (2a)

In an action for recovery for services alleged to have been rendered to a decedent, an amended petition setting up a promise by decedent to devise property to plaintiff as compensation was properly allowed to be filed. Benge's Admr. v. Fouts; Same v. Creech, 163 Ky. 796. 174 S. W. 510.

# (2b)

A certain amended petition in an action for damages which specified items of damage was no departure, nor was it ground for continuance in the absence of an affirmative showing that by reason of same the opposite party could not continue with the trial in consequence of the amendment. Phoenix-Jellico Coal Co. v. Grant, 159 Ky. 95, 166 S. W. 812.

# (2c)

In an action by the payee of a note after a plea of non est factum and alteration, the plaintiff may amend and seek a recovery upon the indebtedness for which the note was given. Ramsey v. Utica Deposit Bank, 156 Ky. 263, 160 S. W. 943.

# (3)

Appeal from inferior to circuit court. On an appeal to the circuit court the defendant has a right to make all the defenses he has, and the court may allow the defense to be changed or new defenses to be made, as if the case had been originally brought in that court. Willis v. McNeal, 8 R. 411; and see further, notes to 726.

# (4)

The plaintiff in justice's court filed an affidavit showing defendant's indebtedness to him in the sum of $59. and obtained an attachment; no petition was filed. On an appeal to the circuit court, the plaintiff was allowed to file an amended petition setting up his cause of action. Puff v. Hutcher, 78 Ky. 146; and see further, notes to 726. Sec. 705 requires pleadings, when amount is over $50, to be in writing.

# 5

Attachments. As to amendment of petition, or affidavit for, see sec. 268 and notes thereto.

# 6

Bastardy warrant may be amended. Com. v. Cantrell, 20 R. 24, 45 S. W. 72.

# (7)

Contested election—amendment of notice. Wilson v. Hines, 99 Ky. 221, 18 R. 233, 35 S. W. 627.

# (7a)

In the trial of a contested election an amendment to the petition setting out additional names of illegal voters was rightly rejected. The power to allow amendments applies to such amendments as undertake to perfect and make more definite the grounds set out in the original petition, and an amendment should not be allowed to be filed which brings in the names of other voters or charges additional irregularities. Clark v. Robinson, 159 Ky. 25, 166 S. W. 801.

# (8)

Definite and certain—court may require pleading to be made—but a party will not be obliged to furnish in detail facts already known to his adversary or that are equally accessible to both parties—as the number of a train or the names of the train men. Bogard v. I. C. R. R., 116 Ky. 429, 25 R. 624, 76 S. W. 170; Howard v. W. U. Tel. Co., 25 R. 828. 76 S.W. 387.

# (8a)

The remedy for indefiniteness is by motion to make more specific. Daniel v. Daniel, 166 Ky. 182, 179 S. W. 5.

# (8b)

Where in an action for slander, the name of a person in whose presence the alleged slanderous publications were made, is stated in the petition, it is error

# § 134] Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments.

formable thereto. And, if the allegations of a pleading be so indefinite or uncertain that the precise nature of the claim or defense is not apparent, the court may require the pleading to be made definite and certain by amendment. The court must, in every stage of an action, disregard any error or defect in the proceedings, which does not affect the substantial rights of the adverse party; and no judgment shall be reversed or affected by reason of such error or defect. (Party in fault must pay cost of, sec. 113; Ky. Stat., sec. 887; amendment of affidavit for attachment, sec. 268.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 104, 200d, 219b, 222, 314, 396g, 509, 531, 550g, 558, 580, 582, 584, 586, 616, 621, 636, 648, 706, 769, 798, 817, 883, 902; Form No. 175.

to require the plaintiff to file a bill of particulars specifying the name of other persons who were present at the same time and place. McCollum v. Langdon, 165 Ky. 244, 176 S. W. 990.

(8c) In an action for the collection of taxes an amendment may be filed giving a more accurate description of the property levied on in order to identify the property in the judgment. Ludlow v. City of Ludlow, 152 Ky. 545, 153 S. W. 783.

(10c) It was not error to permit the filing of three amended petitions setting up different acts of negligence relating to the same wrongful act and causes of action. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Greenwell's Admr., 155 Ky. 799, 160 S. W. 479.

(10d) It was not error to overrule a motion to file an amended answer in view of delay in offering same. City of Louisville v. Lausberg, 161 Ky. 361, 170 S. W. 962.

(10e) An amended answer set up a complete defense in an action for services; the refusal to allow it to be filed was error. Bd. of Ed. of City of Ludlow v. Ritchie, 149 Ky. 674, 149 S. W. 985.

(10f) The filing of an amended rejoinder after evidence was heard was not an abuse of discretion and was not ground for a continuance in this case. L. & N. R. Co. v. Tuggle's Admr., 151 Ky. 409, 152 S. W. 270.

(10) Discretion of court. The only limitation upon the discretion of the court in allowing amended pleadings is that they must be in furtherance of justice, and must not change substantially the claim or defense. With few exceptions the action of the trial court in permitting or rejecting amendments will be approved. Greer v. City of Cov., 83 Ky. 410, 7 R. 419; L. & N. R. R. v. Pointer, 113 Ky. 952, 24 R. 772, 69 S. W. 1108.

(10a) It was proper for the trial court to allow an amended petition in an action for negligence setting up additional acts of negligence. W. U. 'Tel. Co. v. Reed, 158 Ky. 552, 165 S. W. 656.

(10b) Where an amendment to a pleading is tendered, an objection to the filing of the same raises the question of insufficiency. The objection goes to each paragraph separately, and the court may permit defense by one paragraph and reject another. C. N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Smith & Johnston, 165 Ky. 235, 176 S. W. 1013.

(11) An amended answer denying a fact admitted by mistake in the original answer should be permitted to be filed. The allowance or rejection of an amendment is not usually a cause for reversal, unless there has been a manifest abuse of the discretion of the court. Downing v. Bacon, 7 Bush 680; and see cases cited in note 22.

(11a) A public administrator brought suit for damages for death of an intestate under a void appointment. Afterwards he obtained a valid appointment as

Title VII] Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments. [5 134

administrator more than a year after the death of the intestate and tendered an amended petition showing that fact. The court properly refused to permit it to be filed. *Fentzka's Admr. v. Warwick Con. Co., 162 Kv. 580, 172 S. W. 1060.*

# (12) During trial.

In an action against a city for work done at its instance, the petition alleged the performance of the work and presented a cause of action. The proof was that the work was done under a special contract, by which plaintiff was to look to the lot-owners for payment. The court instructed the jury to find for the defendant. after which, but before verdict was rendered, plaintiff offered an amended petition alleging facts sufficient to render city liable on special contract. Held that the amendment should have been permitted to be filed. *Kearney v. City of Cov., I Met. 339; I. C. R.R. v. Jackson, 25 R. 2087, 79 S. W. 1187; but in Chun v. K. & I. Bridge Co., 23 R. 1092, 64 S. W. 649.* the court approved the ruling of lower court in refusing an amendment during trial setting up a different cause of negligence.

# (13) The chancellor may.

even after the trial is commenced, permit the pleadings to be amended when it is apparent that the ends of justice require it. *Rogers v. Rogers, 15 B. M. 364.*

# (13a)

This section gives the courts a broad discretion in the matter of allowing amendments to be filed, but this discretion should not be stretched to the extent of permitting the filing of an amended answer which is not tendered or offered to be filed, until after the evidence is closed, instructions given, the case argued, and the jury have retired to their room for the purpose of arriving at a verdict. *Moore v. Damron, 157 Ky. 799, 164 S. W. 103.*

# (14)

In an action upon a note the defendant alleged that it contained $160 usurious interest; the plaintiff by reply admitted that the note contained $36 usury. During the trial the plaintiff was permitted upon the vague statement of a witness that there was no usury in the note, to file an amendment denying that it embraced any usury. Held that it was error to allow the amendment. *Hubble v. Murphy, 1 Duv. 278.*

# (15)

After the issues are made up and the cause ready for hearing, it is not an abuse of discretion to refuse to permit an amendment pleading the statute of limitation to be filed. *Yocum v. Foreman, 14 Bush 494; Donnelly v. Pepper, 91 Ky. 363, 13 R. 82, 15 S. W. 879.*

# (16)

When an amended answer conforms to the proof and does not substantially change the defense, and the filing of it is in furtherance of justice, it is not error to set aside submission and permit it to be filed. *Nat. Mutual Association v. Jones, 84 Ky. 110, 7 R. 750; and the court may permit an amendment filed after jury is sworn. Wade v. Moore, 3 R 392; or a reply, Washington Mfg. Co. v. Barnett, 19 R. 958, 42 S. W. 1120.*

# (16a)

In this case it was error to refuse to admit an amended answer to be filed to conform to the proof. *Griffin v. Russell, 161 Ky. 471, 170 S. W. 1192.*

# (16b)

It was held error, in an action for false imprisonment, to deny the defendant the right to withdraw his denial of the arrest, and justify the arrest so as to conform to the proof. *Griffin v. Russell, 161 Ky. 471, 170 S. W. 1192.*

# (16c)

In an action to settle a decedent's estate the circuit court may at a term after judgment has been rendered fixing the amount of advancements, permit the filing of amended and supplemental pleadings for the purpose of procuring a sale to satisfy the debts. *Aylor v. Aylor, 158 Ky. 713, 166 S. W. 216.*

# (16d)

In an action for injuries resulting from the fright of a horse, it was held not error to permit plaintiff, at the close of the evidence, to amend his petition to conform to the proof, so as to allege that his horse at the time of the injury was an ordinarily gentle one and was being carefully driven. *Cincinnati, N. O. & T. P. Rv. Co. v. Dungan, 162 Ky. 36, 171 S. W. 1007.*

# (17) Duty of court.

If the court permits a defective pleading to be filed over the objection of the adverse party, the court should in some way notify the party of the defect, and give him an opportunity to correct it, before disregarding it on the final hearing because of such defect. *Ashbrook v. Roberts, 82 Ky. 298, 6 R. 317.*

# (18)

Forcible detainer. Warrant for may be amended before trial. See notes to sec. 454.

# (19)

Judgment. After final judgment it is too late for either party to file amendments. *Meadows v. Goff, 90 Ky. 540, 12 R. 495, 14 S. W. 535; Brown v. Vancleave, 86 Kv. 381, 9 R. 593, 6 S. W 25; and see Johnson v. Johnson, 88 Ky. 275, 10 R. 860, 11 S. W. 5.*

# (20)

Proceedings upon an amended petition filed after judgment are nullities. *Brown v. Vancleave, So Kv. 351, 9 R. 593, 6 S. W. 25.*

# §134 Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments

# (21)

After a petition has been dismissed an amended petition can not be filed at the next term of the court, as there is no petition in court to amend, and even if it be considered as an original petition, if it sets up same matter as was contained in original petition, the judgment dismissing first petition is a bar. *Huston v. Kidwell, 12 R. 3S6, 6 S. W. 25.*

# (2la)

In a certain action judgment was rendered against the garnishee subjecting to plaintiff's demand a debt which had been owing to an ancestor of defendant upon an averment that he had purchased the interest of all the heirs except one. Afterwards, and before the money was paid, certain persons claiming to be heirs of said decedent offered to file a pleading which denied that they had sold their shares to defendant. The court held that they were too late. The pleading, however, was insufficient. *Combs v. Cardwell, 164 Ky. 542, 175 S. W. 1009.*

# (2lb)

A judgment directing the sale of land in which a person of unsound mind had an interest was void for failure to serve process on his committee. This could not be cured by the filing of a pleading by the committee after a sale under the judgment. *Barry v. Fair's Admr., 172 Ky. 308, 189 S. W. 220.*

# (2lc)

Local option election petition. This section permits a person signing a petition for a local option election order by the county court to withdraw his name from the petition and also to revoke or withdraw such withdrawal before the entry of such order. Under it also a supplemental petition adding new names may be filed. *Horton v. Botts, 158 Kv. 11, 164 S. W. 352.*

# (22)

Misnomer in name of defendant may be corrected by amendment, and when mistake in name is discovered after cause of action is barred, defendant who has answered will not be allowed to rely on plea of limitation. *Heckman v. L. & N. R. R., 85 Kv. 631, 9 R. 297, 4 S. W. 342; and see Schmidt v. L. & N. R. R., 99 Ky. 143, 18 R. 65, 35 S. W. 135; City v. Com., 106 Ky. 434, 21 R. 42, 50 S. W. 815; and where defendant is sued as a corporation an amendment may be filed setting up that it is a partnership. Teets v. The Snider Mfg. Co., 27 R. 1061, 87 S. W. 803.*

# (23)

When sued by the wrong name, the defendant must disclose his true name in making objection to the misnomer by plea in abatement or otherwise. *L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Hall, 12 Bush 131; and see Schmidt v. L. & N. R. R., 99 Ky. 143, 18 R. 65, 35 S. W. 135; Heckman v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 85 Ky. 631, 9 R. 297, 4 S. W. 342.*

# (24)

Practice. An amended petition not noted of record, but treated by the parties as filed, and certified by the clerk as part of the record, will be regarded as part of it. *Bowler v. Lane, 3 Met. 312; and where a reply was treated by parties, and lower court as an amendment to the petition it will be so regarded by Court of Appeals. Ruffner v. Ridley, 81 Ky. 165, 4 R. 958.*

# (24a)

After an answer filed traversing the allegations of the petition, an objection that the petition was not sufficiently specific is waived and for the court, on defendant's motion thereafter to require the petition to be made more specific and on failure to strike same from the petition, to disregard the waiver is error. *Eaton v. Green River Coal & Coke Co., 157 Ky. 159, 162 S. W. 807.*

# (25)

Petition failing to state cause of action—the rule allowing amendment to cure defective petition is very liberal and if the amendment will fill out and perfect the petition, however scant it may be, the court should permit it to be filed. *L. & N. R. R. v. Pointer, 113 Ky. 952, 24 R. 772, 69 S. W. 1108.*

# (26)

The court should not permit an amended answer to be filed over the objection of the adverse party when it presents no defense. *Patrick v. Swinney, 5 Bush 421.*

# (27)

In an action on an alleged contract to pay $50 annually for the use of a bridge, the testimony established, not an express, but an implied, contract to pay the amount, and the court should have permitted plaintiff to amend its petition to enable it to recover on the implied promise if it failed to establish an express agreement to pay. *Frankfort Bridge Co. v. Frankfort, 18 B. M. 41.*

# (28)

If a petition presents in substance a cause of action, but is defective in form, the remedy is by motion to make more specific, and not by demurrer; and failure to make motion before answer is waiver of objection. *Pugh v. White, 78 Ky. 210; Posey v. Green, 78 Ky. 162; 4 Met. 330.*

# (29)

Prayer of petition. Amendment to make it conform to the cause of action set forth in the petition is not a new cause of action. *Durrett v. Stewart, 88 Ky. 665, 11 R. 172, 11 S. W. 773.*

# (29a)

If the uncertainty in a pleading is such that it states no cause of action or defense, a demurrer is proper. *Reid v. Lyttle, 150 Kv. 304, 150'S. W. 357.*

Title Ij Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments.

(30) Rejected pleading made part of record. See MeCain v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 13 R. 809, 18 S. W. 537; and notes to sec. 335.

(31) Return of case from Court of Appeals. In an action where there was no answer and no motion to vacate an attachment obtained upon insufficient allegations and reversed by the Court of Appeals, the plaintiff on the return of the case should have leave to amend his petition. Clark v. Seaton, 18 B. M. 226. The right to amend after reversal because of defective pleadings is the same as before trial. Vest v. Norman, 1 R. 317.

(32) Time amendment takes effect. Matter set up in an amended pleading will not relate back to the time of filing the original, so far as the rights of persons not parties to the suit are concerned; and the action as to the matters set up in the amendment will be considered pending, as to them, only from the time the amendment is filed. Hawes v. Orr, 10 B. 431.

(33) Verdict—after. The court should not, after trial and verdict, set aside the verdict for the purpose of allowing a pleading or an amended pleading to be filed. Evans v. Stone, 80 Ky. 78, 3 R. 751; see Mount v. Tappey, 7 Bush 617.

(39) This section has been construed in a great many cases and the court has uniformly held that the trial court has a wide discretion in allowing new pleadings, that correct errors and omissions, and the rulings of the trial court will not be disturbed when the ends of justice have been promoted by its action and the parties have had a fair trial. Barron v. City of Lex., 105 S. W. 395, 32 R. 92; Glinn v. L. & N. R. Co., 105 S. W. 437, 32 R. 346; Staton v. Byron, 105 S. W. 928, 32 R. 246; Vaught v. Hogue, 107 S. W. 757, 32 R. 1061; Title Guaranty Co. v. CoM., 141 Ky. 570, 133 S. W. 577.

(40) Amendment during trial. The court may refuse an amendment when it appears that the purpose of it is to delay the case, or get some unfair advantage, or it changes in a substantial way the issues. Langan v. Louisville Dry Goods Co., 129 S. W. 111, 138 Ky. 798; Randolph v. Snyder, 129 S. W. 562, 139 Ky. 159; Robards v. Robards, 110 S. W. 422; Harrison v. Pace, 121 S. W. 671.

(41) When an amendment is offered during the trial, the party offering it should show by affidavit why it was not sooner tendered. Cincinnati R. Co. v. Crabtree, 100 S. W. 318, 30 R. 1000; see further, Ford v. Providence Coal Co., 90 S. W. 609, 30 R. 698, 124 Ky. 517.

(42) Omitted words in pleading will be supplied when their omission is clearly an inadvertence and their insertion not hurtful to rights of other party. Young v. Chesapeake R. Co., 125 S. W. 241, 130 Ky. 784.

(43) Second trial-amended pleading may be filed if ends of justice require it. Illinois Cent. R. Co. v. Houchins, 101 S. W. 924, 31 R. 93, 125 Ky. 483; Asher v. Uhl, 87 S. W. 307, 27 R. 938.

(44) Verdict will cure defective pleadings unless the defects prejudiced the substantial rights of the adverse party. Drakeboro Coal Co. v. Jernigan, 99 S. W. 235, 30 R. 477.

# §134 Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments

(45) New parties-Court should allow new parties to be made when the ends of justice and the rights of the parties require it. Brackett v. Boreing, 59 S. W. 496, 28 R. 386.

(46) Harmless error. Although the trial court may commit error, yet if upon the whole case it does not appear to have prejudiced the rights of the complaining party it will not constitute reversible error. Chesapeake R. Co. v. Conley, 124 S. W. 861, 136 Ky. 601; Bell v. Hatfield, 89 S. W. 544, 28 R. 515, 121 Ky. 560; Phoenix Ins. Co. v. Flowers, 124 S. W. 403; Pate v. Berry, 140 Ky. 102, 130 S. W. 815; Kilcoyn v. Chicago R. Co., 141 Ky. 237, 132 S. W. 438; Claypool v. Continental Co., 112 S. W. 835, 129 Ky. 682; Baltimore R. Co. v. Wood, 114 S. W. 734, 130 Ky. 839; Lindsey v. Smith, 131 Ky. 176, 114 S. W. 779; L. & N. R. Co., 142 Ky. 468, 134 S. W. 899; Petry v. Petry, 142 Ky. 564, 134 S. W. 922; Illinois Cent. R. Co. v. Moss, 142 Ky. 658, 134 S. W. 1122; Morgan v. Sparkman, 143 Ky. 27, 135 S. W. 408.

(47) The omission of a fact essential to the cause of action was not cured by a verdict. There was no admission or proof of the fact nor submission of the question. Ct. Fire Ins. Co. v. Moore, 154 Ky. 18, 156 S. W. 867.

(48) In an action for damages for personal injury loss of time was defectively pleaded; but evidence on the question was heard without objection. The error of the circuit court in instructing on that matter was thereby waived. L. & N. R. Co. v. Miller, 154 Ky. 236, 157 S. W. 8.

(49) A petition alleging damage by the defendant having negligently thrown from its mine, slack, copperas, "and other deleterious substances," into the creek, the plaintiff should, on defendant's motion, be required to make the phrase "other deleterious substances" more specific. Eaton v. Green River Coal & Coke Co., 157 Ky. 159, 162 S. W. 807.

(50) Where an amendment that is tendered to a pleading merely amplifies a defense sufficiently set up in a previous pleading, the refusal to allow the same to be filed is not error. L. & N. R. Co. v. Parker's Admr., 165 Ky. 658, 177 S. W. 465.

(51) Where a petition pleading a contract is too general, error of the court in refusing to require plaintiff to make the petition more specific is not prejudicial where the precise terms of the contract as contended for by each party are developed by the evidence and the instructions of the court are predicated on the complaining party. Cincinnati, N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Smith & Johnston, 165 Ky. 235, 176 S. W. 1013.

(52) One of several defendants who answered and participated in a trial without objection to the failure of the plaintiff to dismiss the action as to the defendants who were not summoned was not allowed to insist that such rendition without a dismissal as to the others was a clerical misprision. Harbison-Walker Ref. Co. v. McFarland's Admr., 156 Ky. 44, 160 S. W. 798.

(53) A defective petition in an action for personal injuries was cured by verdict. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Burch, 155 Ky. 245, 159 S. W. 782.

(54) A personal judgment was warranted against an assessment company which denied all liability on a policy. Inter. Bus. Men's Acc. Assn. v. Ford, 161 Ky. 163, 170 S. W. 525.

(55) In view of this section, failure of the court, in an action on a fire policy covering tobacco in bulk as well as in a barn, to limit recovery for a loss of tobacco to the amount specified in the policy, was held not error, where the total recovery for tobacco and barn exceeded the insurance on the tobacco alone by less than $200, the barn being insured for $900. Henry Clay Fire Ins. Co. v. Barkley, 160 Ky. 153, 169 S. W. 747.

(56) The substantial rights of a party were held not invalidated by denial of a motion to transfer the case to the equity docket. Interstate Business Men's Accident Assn. v. Ford, 161 Ky. 613, 170 S. W. 525.

(57) Error, if any, in overruling a demurrer to a petition on a benefit policy alleging that the deceased was in good standing at the time of his death, was held not prejudicial under this section where defendant pleaded that deceased was not in good standing, and evidence thereto was offered on both sides of the question, and instructions relating thereto were properly given. National Council J. O.U. A. M. v. Thomas, 163 Ky. 364, 173 S. W. 813.

(58) A defendant carrier was held not prejudiced in its substantial rights by refusal of the court to allow amendment to its plea setting up limitations of its liability on the contract of shipment.

(59) Under this section and secs. 338, 756, a verdict will not be disturbed because of trivial errors which could not have prejudiced the substantial rights of the complaining party. Consolidated Coal

Title VII] Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments. [§ 135

# § 135 [162] [Supplemental pleading—action to enforce lien.]

A party may be allowed, on motion, to file a supplemental pleading, alleging material facts occurring after the filing of the former pleading; but if a plaintiff, having a lien for a debt due and a debt not due upon property which he seeks to subject, state both claims in his petition, he may, upon a suggestion of record that one of them has become due, pendente lite, have judgment for a sale of the property therefor.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 582, 614a, 614c; Form No. 116.

# Case References

Co. v. Baldridge, 166 Ky. 187, 179 S. W. 18.

(60) A correct judgment will not be reversed because it is based on erroneous reasonings. Com. v. McCauley's Exr., 166 Ky. 450, 179 S. W. 411.

(61) Where a petition in an action against a corporation improperly named a corporation, the petition may be corrected by amendment which will relate back to and become a part of the original petition, where service was had on the corporation's duly constituted agent. Imperial Jellico Coal Co. v. Neff, 166 Ky. 722. 179 S. W. 829.

(62) In view of this section and secs. 338. 756, testimony by plaintiff, in an action for personal injuries, that he had fallen off the car, was held not to affect substantial rights, where the issue was whether he was caused to fall by a violent and unusual jerk of the car, due to the negligence in its operation. Roberts v. Louisville Ry. Co., 168 Ky. 230, 181 S. W. 1131.

(63) Under this section and sec. 756, error in an instruction in an action for damages for breach of contract in sale of timber with privilege of mill site, in not limiting the findings to damages shown by the evidence, was not reversible error, where the jury acted on that theory and the verdict was sustained by the evidence. Baker v. Morris, 168 Ky. 168. 181 S. W. 943.

(64) In view of this section and sec. 756, a judgment for an employee in an action under federal employers' liability act will not be reversed because of improper instruction on contributory negligence, in the absence of any evidence relating thereto. Chesapeake & O. Ry. Co. v. Cooper, 168 Ky. 137. 181 S. W. 933.

(65) Where the petition, in an action for personal injuries, alleges that plaintiff suffered internal injuries, and defendant did not move to make the petition more definite, the error was waived. Cassidy v. Berkovitz, 169 Ky. 785, 185 S. W. 120.

(66) Under this section and secs. 338, 756, error, in order to be reversible, must affect the substantial rights of the appellant. Watson Contract Co. v. Trumbo, 170 Ky. 95, 185 S. W. 518.

(67) A judgment will not be reversed because of error in the form of one instruction if the instructions taken together are simple and easily understood. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Troxell, 150 Ky. 185, 150 S. W. 41.

(68) A judgment was rendered upon a lost note; the bond required by sec. 7 of the Code was given before judgment. The judgment was affirmed although the bond should have been given before the bringing of the action. Kimbrough v. Lex. City Nat. Bk., 150 Ky. 336, 150 S. W. 325.

(69) A guardian ad litem for three infants filed a report in which he omitted their names, but by mistake inserted the names of three other infant defendants; afterwards he was appointed for the last named three and filed a report setting out their names. The court declined to set aside a sale by reason of this omission. Thomson's Gdn. v. Thomson, 151 Ky. 296, 151 S. W. 658.

(70) Where negligence is alleged in general terms, by a petition charging that plaintiff was struck by defendant's car, the variance caused by evidence that plaintiff was not struck by the car but slipped and fell under it while attempting to board the car, was held immaterial. Ky. Trac. & Terminal Co. v. Waits, 167 Ky. 236, 180 S. W. 356.

# 135. (1 Supplemental pleadings.

Where a mortgagee institutes suit to recover possession of mortgaged property before forfeiture, and the forfeiture occurs pending the action, he may by supplemental petition set up the forfeiture. This section applies to ordinary as well as equitable actions. Brookover v. Hurst, 1 Met. 665.

# § 136-137] Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments.

# § 136 [168] [Continuance on amendment.]

If a party amend a pleading or proceeding, and the court shall be satisfied, by affidavit or otherwise, that the adverse party cannot be ready for trial in consequence thereof, a continuance may be granted to some day in the same term or to another term of the court. (Costs of continuance to be paid by party filing amendment, Ky. Stat., sec. S87.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 646; Forms. Nos. 470, 471.

# § 137 [164] [Verification of petition before trial sufficient.]

A petition filed without verification shall not be dismissed for that reason, if the verification be made on or before the calling of the action for trial. (See further as to verification, secs. 116, 117.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 526j.

(2) The facts which this section allows to be presented in the supplemental petition must be material to the case as presented in the original petition; they must be such as serve to explain or perfect the cause of action originally stated. Taylor v. Moran, 4 Met. 127.

(3) The plaintiff in an action for slander cannot, by an amended petition, set up slanderous words spoken pending the action as a distinct ground of recovery or to enhance the damages; but may set them up to show the intent with which the words charged in the original petition were spoken. 4 Met. 127.

A plaintiff who holds lien notes due and not due may set up all of them in his petition, and, by suggestion of record, have judgment for notes not due as they mature, but this rule only applies to lien notes, and a supplemental pleading is required as to other notes. Dant v. Head, 90 Ky. 255, 12 R. 153, 13 S. W. 1073; and see Wheeler v. Bleakley, 18 R. 254, 35 S. W. 1031; Moshell v. Reed, 97 S. W. 372, 30 R. 10; Rittenhouse v. Swango, 128 S. W. 299; and secs. 692, 694 and notes thereto.

5 In an action upon three lien notes judgment was rendered ordering sale of land to satisfy two of them that were due; afterward the third note fell due, and the former judgment was set aside and judgment rendered directing sale to pay all the notes. Held that the court had power to set aside first judgment after the term, and its action was proper. Carr v. Watkins, 10 R. 342, 9 S. W. 218. See further as to lien notes secs. 692, 694 and notes thereto.

6) Leave of court must be obtained to file a supplemental pleading. Petry v. Petry, 142 Ky. 564, 134 S. W. 922.

7) In a proceeding for the entry in the county court of an order calling a local option election a supplemental petition adding new names may be filed before the entry of such order. Horton v. Botts, 158 Ky. 11, 164 S. W. 352.

8) In view of this section and secs. 335, 756, an instruction allowing recovery for ordinary negligence is harmless, although the petition avers and the evidence shows that the negligence was gross. Chesapeake & O. Ry. Co. v. Shamblen, 166 Ky. 789, 179 S. W. 837.

9) Under this section, plaintiff in an action on a vendor's lien notes some of which were not due when the action was instituted, is entitled to judgment on the notes which mature after the petition was filed, without further process. Stone v. Daniels, 166 Ky. 719, 179 S. W. 831.

(10) This section does not authorize a sale to satisfy a debt, a portion of which is not due when judgment is rendered. Durham v. Elliott, 180 Ky. 724, 203 S. W. 539.

# § 136. (1 Continuance on amendment

is addressed to sound discretion of court. City of Paducah v. Johnson, 93 S. W. 1035, 29 R. 518; and if adverse party desires a continuance he should file affidavit showing why. Troendle v. Morgan Coal Co., 114 S. W. 312.

(2) When several causes of action are set out, or the same cause is set forth in different paragraphs, the striking out of any of the causes of action or paragraphs is not such an amendment as will authorize a continuance. Branshaw v. Berry, 2 R. 58.

(2a) An amended petition was filed which did not materially change the cause of action. Refusal of a continuance because of the amendment was held

Title VII] Mistakes in Pleadings-Amendments. [§ 138-139

# § 138 [165] [Verification not necessary after trial begins.]

No objection shall be taken, after the commencement of the trial, to any pleading for the want of, or a defect in, the verification. (See further as to verification, secs. 116, 117; when trial begins, sec. 590.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 526j.

# § 139 [166] [Verification—amendments allowed without.]

Courts may, in their discretion, permit the amendments authorized by this chapter to be made without being verified, unless a new and distinct cause of action or defense be thereby introduced.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 526h, 580, 596, 597.

not to be error. Moore's Adm. v. Pierce, 160 Ky. 107, 169 S. W. 620.

(2b) The court is not required to grant a continuance because of the amendment of a petition, which makes no material change in the cause of action. Moore's Admr. v. Pierce, 160 Ky. 107, 169 S. W. 620.

# § 138.

(1) Objection—how taken. Objection to pleading for want of verification should be by rule against the party failing to verify it, and upon his failure to have it stricken from the record. Wheeler v. Wales, 3 Bush 225; Harris v. Ray, 15 B. M. 628; and see notes to secs. 116, 117.

(2) To take advantage of error in overruling motion to require party to verify his pleading, the decision must be excepted to at the time. Cobb v. Stewart, 4 Met. 225; Mason v. Mason, 5 Bush 187.

(3) Waiver. Right to require plaintiff to verify petition is waived by answer, and the right cannot be regained by withdrawing answer. Butler v. Church of Conception, 14 Bush 540; Meador v. Turpin, 4 Met. 93.

(4) Although an answer denying the execution of a note must be verified, the objection for want of verification is waived unless made before commencement of trial. Meyer v. Douglass, 99 Ky. 267, 18 R. 169, 35 S. W. 917.

(5) If a pleading impeaching the consideration of a deed is verified, no other verification is needed. If the omission to verify the pleading is not objected to it is waived. Hite v. Reynolds, 163 Ky. 502, 173 S. W. 1108.

(6) The want of verification of the petition alleging a mistake in a deed will be treated as waived unless objected to. Hite v. Reynolds, 163 Ky. 502, 173 S. W. 1108.

# § 139.

(1) Verification of amendment. Although an amended petition which the lower court refused to permit filed was not verified, its rejection was held to be error, as it appeared that it was not rejected for this cause. Lebus v. Poindexter, 18 R. 740, 33 S. W. 9.

(2) An amended answer set up a new and distinct defense, such as under Code, sec. 116, requires a verification. The refusal of the court to require verification was not in this case prejudicial error. Ball v. Sandlin, 176 Ky. 537, 195 S. W. 1089.

(3) A judgment was reversed for a refusal of the circuit court to permit the filing of an amended pleading. The amendment was not verified. It did not appear from the record that lack of verification was the reason for the refusal. Caldwell Co. v. Durret Construction Co., 180 Ky. 594, 203 S. W. 291.

# § 140 [167] [Equitable actions in-answers read as deposition.]

In equitable actions, a party may annex to his pleading written interrogatories to the adverse party, concerning any material allegation thereof; and answers thereto, on oath, may be read by either party, as a deposition between the party interrogating and the party answering.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f.

# § 141 [16s] [Party answering not confined to mere response.]

The party answering shall not be confined to responding merely to the interrogatories, but may state any facts concerning the cause of action to which the interrogatories refer, and they may likewise be read as a deposition. (If answers state mere matter; how controverted, sec. 608.)

# Circuit Courts Having Continuous Sessions.

§ 1006. Interrogatories annexed to a pleading shall be answered when the responsive pleading is required to be filed. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1006.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f.

# § 142 [16o] [Time to answer-effect of delay on trial.]

Interrogatories annexed to a petition shall be answered when the party is required to answer the petition: if annexed to any other pleading, they shall be answered in twenty days after notice of the filing thereof shall be given to.

# § 140. Interrogatories. Party answering interrogatories may, on the trial of the action, read his answers as a deposition.

Ecklar v. Galbreath, 5 Bush 617; Short v. Tinsley, 1 Met. 397. But they cannot be so read unless authenticated, as required by the Code. Ford v. Thompson, 1 Met. 580; see Musick v. Ray, 3 Met. 428. Defendant is not bound to answer interrogatory contained in body of petition nor unless annexed to it. Burnett v. Garnett, 18 B. M. 68; McRae v. Gunther, 14 R. 5, 18 S. W. 1034; nor to answer any question which would subject him to criminal or penal prosecution. Cole v. Wilson, 18 B. M. 212.

Title VIIj Interrogatories. [§ 143-147

# § 143 [170] [Ordinary actions-when allowed in.]

In ordinary actions, a party may annex to his pleading written interrogatories to an adverse party concerning any material allegation—

1. [If party resides more than twenty miles from court.] If the party interrogated do not reside within twenty miles from the place where the action may be pending.
2. [If party unable to attend court.] If the party interrogated be unable to attend court on account of infirmity or imprisonment, or be a female.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f.

# § 144 [171] [Ordinary actions—time to answer.]

In ordinary actions, interrogatories annexed to the petition shall be answered when the petition is required to be answered; if annexed to any other pleading, they shall be answered at or before the calling of the cause for trial, if the party interrogated had reasonable notice of their being filed.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f, 210.

# § 145 [172] [Trial not postponed because of failure to answer.]

The trial of an ordinary action shall not be postponed on account of the failure to answer the interrogatories, if the party interrogated be present in court at the trial, so that he may be orally examined; nor, in case of his absence, without an affidavit showing the facts which the party believes will be proved by the answers thereto, and that the party has not filed the interrogatories, nor omitted to file them, for the purpose of delay; nor if the party will consent that the facts stated in the affidavit shall be considered as admitted by him.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f.

# § 146 [178] [Application of section 141.]

The provisions of section 141 shall apply to the answers to interrogatories in ordinary actions. (If answers state new matter; here controverted, sec. 609.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f.

# § 147 [174] [Answers to distinguish between knowledge and information.]

A party, in answering such interrogatories, shall distinguish clearly between what is stated from his personal knowledge and what is stated from information or belief merely. An unqualified statement of a fact shall be considered as made from his personal knowledge.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f, 420.

161

# Interrogatories

# § 148 [175] [Verification of answers-what to be stated in.]

Answers to such interrogatories shall be verified by the affidavit of the party answering, to the effect that the statements in them made of his personal knowledge are true, and those made from the information of others he believes to be true.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f.

# § 149 [176] [Party—when compelled to appear as witness.]

If a party reside within twenty miles from the place in which the action may be pending, he may be summoned by the adverse party, and be compelled to testify on the trial of an ordinary action as any other witness.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f.

# § 150 [177] [Claim or defense when sustained by failure to answer.]

If a party, filing interrogatories, file an affidavit that he verily believes that any subject of the interrogatories is in the personal knowledge of the adverse party, and that his answers thereto, if truly made, will sustain the affiant's claim or defense, or any part thereof, and the adverse party shall fail, after reasonable time, to answer them, the claim or defense, or the part thereof mentioned in such affidavit, shall be deemed to be sustained.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f.

# § 151 [178] [Court may compel answers--penalty for failure.]

The court may compel answers to interrogatories by process of contempt, and may, on the failure of the party to answer them, after reasonable time, dismiss the petition, or strike out the pleading, of a party so failing. (In action on constructive service court may propound, sec. 412.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 208f.

# § 150. Construction of section.

Where and this section does not apply. Huntsberry v. Smith, 90 S. W. 601, 28 R. 877, 90 Ky. 601.

# § 152 [179] [Defendant in civil action—how arrested.]

A defendant in a civil action can be arrested and held to bail, only upon the conditions and in the manner prescribed in this chapter.

# § 153 [150] [Order of arrest—grounds of—who may issue.]

An order for the arrest of the defendant shall be made by the clerk of the court in which the action is brought or pending, at its commencement, or at any time before judgment, if an affidavit of the plaintiff be filed in his office showing.

# § 154 [181] Bond to be executed before order issues.

The order of arrest shall not be issued by the clerk until a bond is executed in his office, with good sureties, to the effect that the plaintiff shall pay to the defendant all damages which he may sustain by reason of the arrest, if the order be wrongfully obtained, not exceeding double the amount of the plaintiff's claim stated in the affidavit. (Form of bond, Form 117. If defective, a new one may be executed, sec. 682. Sureties may be sworn; qualification of, secs. 683, 684.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 343.

# (2) Arrest - insufficient grounds for.

The plaintiff filed his affidavit showing that defendant owed him $450, and obtained an order for his arrest on the ground that he was about to depart from the State, without leaving enough property therein to satisfy plaintiff's claim. It was proved on the trial that defendant was about to leave the State, and that he had, at the time the order of arrest was issued, cash notes amounting to about $20,000 on deposit in a Richmond bank, and that he owned turnpike stock of the value of $1,000. The court says: "Whether the evidences of debts deposited in the bank should be regarded as property within the meaning of the Code, we are satisfied the turnpike stock should be; and as it was amply sufficient to satisfy plaintiff's claim, the order of arrest was properly vacated." Curtis Field v. Montmollin, 5 Bush 455.

# (3) An intention of a resident of this State to leave the State temporarily

# (4) No appeal lies from an order vacating an order of arrest and discharging the defendant.

# § 157 [184] [Order—how executed.]

The sheriff shall execute the order of arrest by arresting the defendant and delivering to him a copy thereof. (When may execute on holiday, sec. 665. May break doors to execute, sec. 676. When defendant privileged from arrest, sec. 666.)

# § 158 [185] [Defendant to be committed to jail unless bailed—how discharged.]

The defendant, when arrested, shall be committed by the

# § 157. Officer--duty of to execute

damages for refusing to execute in the night time an order of arrest. he being informed at the time it was delivered to him that the next morning would be too late. Phillips v. Ronald, 3 Bush 244.

# Arrest and Bail

# [Title VIII]

The sheriff to the jail of his county, a copy of the order of arrest being delivered to the jailer; and, unless bailed, shall be kept in custody in the manner provided by law in the case of a defendant taken in execution upon a judgment in a civil action, until discharged in the mode and upon the conditions applying to such defendant. (Mode of discharge, see Ky. Stat., sec. 2180.)

# § 159 [188] [May deposit money in lieu of bail.]

The defendant may, before or after giving bail, deposit in the hands of the sheriff, or in court, the sum of money mentioned in the order of arrest; whereupon he shall be discharged, or his bail, if any be given, shall be released.

# § 160 [187] [Sheriff to pay into court money received by him.]

The sheriff shall pay into the court the money received by him in vacation, in lieu of bail, on the first day of the next term. If it be received during a term, he shall pay it into the court immediately.

# § 161 [188] [Court to make orders for its keeping and disposition.]

The court shall make proper orders for the safe-keeping of money deposited in lieu of bail. It may direct the sheriff to keep the money, and, after judgment in the action, shall order it to be paid to the party entitled thereto, according to the result.

# § 162 [189] [Sheriff liable for money deposited.]

Money deposited in the hands of the sheriff, in lieu of bail, or directed by the court to be kept by him, shall be held upon his official responsibility; and he and his sureties shall be liable, and may be proceeded against, for any default in relation thereto, as in other cases of delinquency.

# § 163 [190] [Bail of defendant--requisites and effect of bond.]

Bail may be given by the defendant on his arrest, or at any time afterward before judgment, upon giving bond with good surety to the plaintiff, in the presence of the sheriff, or of the jailer if the defendant have been committed to jail, to the effect that, if judgment shall be rendered in the action against the defendant, he will render himself amenable to the process of the court thereupon. The bond, when accepted, shall be returned to the clerk's office, and the defendant shall be discharged. (Form of bond, Form 119; if defective, a new one may be executed, sec. 682.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 47, 346.

# § 162. Sureties of officer-liability of.

The sureties of a sheriff who has received money in lieu of bail are responsible, first, when the sheriff has failed to account for the money to the court; and second, when the court directs the sheriff to keep it. They are not responsible when the court directs the sheriff to loan out the money, if, having done so, he fails to make the required report concerning it. Sanders v. Parrott. 1 Duv. 292.

# § 163.

(1) Bail bond. It was held in Shuttleworth v. Levi, 13 Bush 195, that an unnecessary stipulation in a bond rendered it invalid; but see now Ky. Stats., sec. 4569.

(2) Bond taken under an order of arrest issued before affidavit made is invalid. Pauer v. Simon, 6 Bush 514.

Title VIII Arrest and Bail. [§ 164-168]

# § 164 [191] [Qualifications of bail.]

The bail must be a resident of this State, and be worth double the sum specified in the order of arrest beyond the amount of his debts, and have property in this State, subject to execution, at least equal in value to that sum. If two or more become bail, they must, in the aggregate, possess those qualifications. The bail, if so required by the sheriff, shall make affidavit of their qualification before him. Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 347.

# § 165 [192] [Objections by plaintiff to sufficiency of bail.]

Objection to bail, for insufficiency, may be made in court, during the term at which the bail bond is returned, and not afterward, unless the bond be returned within the last three days of the term; in which case, the motion may be made on the first day of the succeeding term. The motion cannot be made without notice thereof to the officer who accepted the bond. And if the notice be adjudged unreasonable, the court may continue the motion to some day in the same or the next succeeding term.

Circuit Courts Having Continuous Sessions. 1013. An objection to bail in civil cases for insufficiency shall only be made by motion within ten days after the return of the bail bond. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1013.)

# § 166 [198] [Officer exonerated from liability—when.]

If the motion be not made, or the bail be not thereupon adjudged insufficient, the officer shall be exonerated from liability by reason of the insufficiency of the bail.

# § 167 [194] [Proceedings if bail adjudged insufficient.]

Judgment that bail is insufficient shall not release them, but an order shall forthwith be made to rearrest the defendant; and, thereupon, the same proceedings may be had, and with the like effect, as are directed upon an order of arrest.

# § 168 [195] [Insolvent debtor's oath—defendant may take.]

Upon judgments in actions in which the defendant has been arrested and held to bail, and in which the order of arrest has not been vacated, an execution against the body of the defendant may be issued, upon which the same.

# (3) Requisites of bond.

It is essential to the validity of the bond that it should appear to have been executed in the presence of the sheriff or other officer authorized to take bail. Jones v. Bunn, 2 Met. 490. But the failure to return bond to clerk's office will not defeat or impair its validity. Ib.

A bond stipulating that the defendant "will render himself amenable to the court thereupon," the words "process of" being omitted, is valid. Abbott v. Daniel, 3 Met. 340.

The bond must follow the substantial requirements of this section or it will be invalid. Cole v. Regan, 107 S. W. 756, 32 R. 1059.

can only be effected in the manner pointed out in the Ky. Stat., sec. 2180. Sowle Mfg. Co. v. Bernard, 100 Ky. 658, 18 R. 1106, 39 S. W. 239; McGovern v. Maloney, 30 R. 801, 99 S. W. 935.

# § 168

Proceedings shall be had, and the defendant shall be dealt with in the same manner, as is provided by law in the civil actions in which executions may be issued against the body of the defendant; except that the oath of insolvency, upon reasonable notice, may be taken at any time after the arrest, by delivering to the plaintiff, his agent or attorney, a schedule of the property intended to be surrendered, although said schedule may not have been delivered ten days before the taking of such oath. This exception shall also apply to section 158 of this Code. (See Ky. Stat., sec. 2180, as to oath of insolvency; form of execution against body of defendant, Form 152.)

# INSOLVENT DEBTORS—PROCEEDINGS CONCERNING

# [ACT OF JUNE 10, 1892, C. 58, P. 87.]

# § 2180

Application for discharge—notice--duty of jailer-schedule and oath—papers preserved. Any person taken or charged in execution in any civil case may, by petition, apply for a discharge to a justice of the peace or the presiding judge of his county, or the police judge of the county seat, who shall by warrant, under his hands, require the keeper of the jail to bring the body of the petitioner before him at the court-house on a specified day, and a list of the several executions with which he may stand charged. (Con., sec. 18.)

1. Ten days' notice of the time and place of such application, and a copy of the schedule of the property he intends to surrender, must be given to the adverse party, or his agent or attorney, if in the county.
2. The keeper of the jail shall obey the warrant.
3. The petitioner, when brought before the judge or justice, shall subscribe and deliver a schedule of his whole estate liable for his debts, and take an oath or affirmation in substance as follows:

I, A. B., do swear (or affirm) that the schedule now delivered contains, to the best of my knowledge and belief, a full, true and perfect account and discovery of all the estate and effects unto me in anywise belonging and of such debts as are owing to me or in trust for me, which are liable for my debts; and that I have not, directly or indirectly, sold, assigned or otherwise disposed of, in trust or otherwise, for my use or the benefit of another, or concealed any part of my effects whereby to secure the same, to receive or expect any profit or advantage from the same, to defraud any creditor to whom I am in anywise indebted.

Upon the delivery of such schedule, properly verified, the judge or justice (unless it be made to appear that the petitioner has acted fraudulently) may, by warrant, command the keeper of the jail to discharge the petitioner forthwith; and the warrant shall be a justification to the keeper of the jail for such discharge.
The schedule so subscribed and verified, with the petition and warrant, shall be returned to, and kept safely on file by, the clerk of the county court of the county in which the prisoner was confined. (Ky. Stat., sec. 2180.)

# § 2181

Property held for creditors--sale—prosecution of actions. The right of the petitioner to the effects described in the schedule (saving his wife's dower in the lands, if any) shall be vested in the sheriff of the county in which...

Title VIIIJ, Arrest and Bail. [§ 169
the prisoner was confined, in trust for the use of the creditors under whose executions he stood charged, and the sheriff shall perform the trust, though his term of office expires.

1. The sheriff shall sell such effects and pay over the proceeds to the execution creditors, retaining his commission.
2. He shall, for the use of the execution creditors, at their instance and costs, prosecute any necessary action to recover the effects of a person discharged under this chapter. (Id., sec. 2181.)

§ 2182. Execution of fieri facias may issue after discharge. A fieri facias may be issued after the discharge of a prisoner to have execution of the unsatisfied portion of the creditor's judgment. No revival of the judgment shall be necessary. (Id., sec. 2182.)

§ 2183. Property exempt from surrender. An insolvent debtor shall not be held to surrender such of his effects as are exempted by law from execution. (Id., sec. 2183.)

§ 2184. Applies to one imprisoned by order of chancellor. The provisions of this chapter shall apply to a person imprisoned by order of a court of chancery to compel the payment of money under a judgment of such court. (Id., sec. 2184.)

§ 2185. Choses in action-petition for discovery--sale. Where choses in action are brought into court by virtue of a petition for discovery of assets instituted against an insolvent debtor, the court may order their sale upon the same terms as are prescribed for sales of other personal property; and the court shall make all needful orders for the transfer of the title thereto to the purchaser. (Id., sec. 2185.)

# LIABILITY AND DISCHARGE OF BAIL, AND OF OFFICER.

§ 169 [196] [Surrender of defendant discharges bail.] A surrender of the defendant to the sheriff of the county in which he was arrested, with a delivery to him of a certified copy of the order of arrest and of the bail bond by the defendant, or by his bail, at any time before the liability of the bail is fixed, shall discharge the bail. The sheriff shall give to the bail a written acknowledgment of the surrender, and commit the defendant to the jail of his county, with his order indorsed upon the copy of the bail bond delivered to the jailer.

# Arrest and Bail

# § 170 [187] [Bail may arrest or have defendant arrested.]

For the purpose of surrendering the defendant, the bail may, at any time or place, arrest him, or authorize in writing, indorsed upon a copy of the order of arrest and bail bond, the sheriff or any constable to do so.

# § 171 [198] [Bail—liability how fixed and amount of.]

A return of "not found" upon an execution against the body of the defendant, placed in the hands of the sheriff of the county in which he was arrested, within twenty days after it might have issued upon the judgment, shall be necessary to fix the liability of the bail, which shall be to pay the amount of the judgment and costs.

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 47.

# § 172 [190] [Action against bail.]

The bail can be proceeded against in a separate action only.

# § 173 [200] [Exoneration of bail-what will cause.]

He will be exonerated by the death of the defendant, or his removal from this State under process of law as a fugitive, before the return-day of the summons served upon the bail in the action to enforce his liability; or by the imprisonment of the defendant in the penitentiary; or his legal discharge from the obligations to render himself amenable to the process of the court; or by his surrender to the sheriff of the county in which he was arrested in execution thereof, on or before the return-day of the summons in the action against the bail, or within such further time as the court, in which the action is pending, may allow. If the defendant be confined in any jail of this State, the bail will be exonerated by delivering to the jailer thereof, at any time before the return-day of the summons in the action against the bail, a certified copy of the order of arrest and of the bail bond, with a written order thereupon to detain the defendant in custody, until discharged by law from the action in which the bond was given. The jailer shall give a written acknowledgment of the receipt of the order, which shall be filed with the original bond.

# 171. (1) Bail-liability of, how fixed.

The law requires that the execution shall be placed in the hands of the officer within twenty days after it might have issued, but does not require that the execution shall be returned within twenty days after judgment, nor within twenty days after it is issued, in order to fix the liability of the bail. An execution commanding the sheriff to take the body of the defendant to "satisfy and pay the Commonwealth of Kentucky the sum of $125" is insufficient to fix the liability of the bail. It should be to satisfy and pay the plaintiff in the judgment. Abbott v. Daniel, 3 Met. 340.

The liability of the bail, when fixed by this section, shall be to pay the amount of the judgment and costs, without regard to the solvency or insolvency of the principal. Abbott v. Daniel, 3 Met. 340.

That execution upon the judgment against the principal was returned "not found" before the return day thereof does not prejudice the bail. Jones v. Bunn, 2 Met. 490.

# 170

Title VIII1           Arrest and Bail.

# § 174 [201] [Officer—when liable as bail.]

If, after being arrested, the defendant, without fault on the part of the sheriff, be rescued or escape, or bail be not taken or the adjudged insufficient, or a deposit be not made, the sheriff shall be liable as bail, unless he shall have committed the defendant to jail, and obtained a written acknowledgment thereof from the jailer; in which case, the jailer shall be so liable. Either officer may discharge himself from such liability by putting in sufficient bail at any time before judgment.

# § 175 [202] [Officer—liability of as bail how fixed.]

The liability of the officer, as bail, shall be fixed in the manner provided in section 171, and can be enforced only in a separate action against him, or against him and his sureties in his official bond, as in other cases of delinquency.

# § 176 [205] [Bail—liability of to officer.]

Bail, judged insufficient shall be liable to the officer for the damages he may sustain by reason of such insufficiency.

# ARTICLE 4.

# MOTION TO VACATE AN ORDER OF ARREST.

# § 177 [204] [Application for reduction of bail or vacation of order.]

A defendant, against whom an order of arrest has been obtained, may, at any time before judgment in the action, and before a decision of a motion on account of the insufficiency of the bail, apply to the court, or, in vacation and before executing a bail bond, to the judge thereof, or to any circuit judge, or to the presiding judge of the county court, to vacate the order of arrest, or to reduce the amount of bail. Reasonable notice of the application shall be given to the plaintiff. If satisfied that the bail ought not to have been required, or that the sum for which it was demanded is too large, the court, or judge, may vacate the order of arrest, or reduce the amount of bail. The decision of the motion shall be final in the action, but shall not affect the rights of the parties in any other action. Upon the vacation of the order of arrest, the defendant shall be discharged, or the bail bond, if any be given, shall be canceled. (Notice may be served on attorney, sec. 631.)

# § 178 [205] [Motion supported or opposed by affidavits.]

If the motion supported or opposed by affidavits. Appeal does not lie from an order vacating an order of arrest and discharging the defendant. Porter v. Griffin, 143 Ky. 138, 136 S. W. 130.

# § 179 [206] [Scire facias against bail abolished.]

The writ of scire facias against bail is abolished.

# § 180 [207] [Plaintiff may claim immediate delivery.]

The plaintiff in an action to recover the possession of specific personal property may, at the commencement of the action, or at any time before judgment, claim the immediate delivery thereof, as is herein provided. (Jury to assess value, sec. 330; form of judgment, sec. 388, Form 193.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 235; Forms Nos. 77, 241, 265, 402, 417, 428-431.

# § 180.

(1) Action may be maintained against person who had the possession of property, although he may not have the possession when suit is brought. Gentry v. Barnett. 0 Mon. 113: Scott v. Hughes, 9 B. M. 104.

(2) Demand is not necessary to maintain action. Easley v. Easley, 18 B. M. 86.

(3) Destruction of property wrongfully possessed by a defendant in detinue or a plaintiff in replevin pending the action is the loss of the wrongful possessor. Carrel v. Early, 4 Bibb 271;

(4) Fraudulent purchase of goods- vendor may elect to treat contract as a nullity, and bring an action for the recovery of the property. Dietz v. Sutcliffe, 80 Ky. 650, 4 R. 567.

(5) Identity of property sought to be recovered is question of fact for jury not court, to determine. Sawyer v. Middlesborough, 13 R. 550, 17 S. W. 444.

(6) Joint owners must all join in an action for the recovery of property. Bush v. Groom, 9 Bush 675.

# Title III

# Claim and Delivery of Personal Property.

§ 181 [Order of delivery—clerk to issue-affidavit for.] An order for the delivery of property to the plaintiff shall be made by the clerk, when an affidavit of the plaintiff is filed in his office, showing--

1. Description of property. A particular description of the property claimed.
2. Value of and damages for detention. Its actual value and the damages which the affiant believes the plaintiff ought to recover for the detention thereof.
3. Ownership of--right to possession. That plaintiff is the owner of the property, or has a special ownership or interest therein—stating the

(7) Judgment-form of. Where the plaintiff was in possession of a stray horse, which he had never posted and which he had bailed to defendant, the plaintiff was entitled, in an action for the recovery of the horse, to a judgment for the horse, if to be had; if not, for its value. Borron v. Landes, 1 Duv. 299.

(8) Judgment should be in the alternative for the return of the property, or its value in case a return can not be had, and damages for the taking and withholding of the property. Rogers v. Bradford, 8 Bush 163; Reid v. King, 89 Ky. 388, 11 R. 615, 12 S. W. 772; but judgment for value of property can not exceed amount claimed. Anderson v. Hiele, 23 R. 1115, 64 S. W. 849; and plaintiff may have execution for the specific property or its assessed value. Atkinson v. Fitzpatrick, 22 R. 1363, 60 S. W. 516; and see secs. 330, 388 and notes thereto.

(9) The plaintiff having obtained possession of the property, upon the trial the jury returned a verdict for the defendant. Thereupon the court rendered judgment for a return of the property or for ninety dollars, being the value of the property proved upon trial. The judgment was held erroneous, as the jury should have fixed in their verdict the value of the property, sec. 330. Young v. Parsons, 2 Met. 499; 2 Bush 117.

(10) Owner of property which has been stolen from him may maintain action for its recovery against purchaser from thief. Bassett v. Green, 2 Duv. 560.

(11) Plaintiff can not dismiss his action. The plaintiff can not, after having obtained possession of the property by the execution of the required bond, dismiss his action, and thereby prevent a judgment; the defendant is entitled to a trial, and if proper, to a judgment for the return of the property and damages for its detention. Rogers v. Bradford, 8 Bush 163; and if the plaintiff dismisses the action the defendant can sue on the bond. Kv. L. & E. Co. v. Crabtree, 118 Ky. 395, 26 R. 283, 80 S. W. 1161. Mounts v. Murphy, 126 Ky. 803, 31 R. 1192; 104 S. W. 978.

(12) Pleading. The defendant from whom a horse had been taken filed an answer claiming to be the owner of the horse, and praying to be dismissed with a judgment for his costs. On the trial the jury found for defendant, fixing the value of the horse at one hundred and twenty-five dollars, and the damages at $35, and judgment was rendered in accordance with the verdict. Held that the answer was sufficient, and that defendant was entitled under it to the judgment rendered. Bates v. Buchanan, 2 Bush 117.

Can counter-claim be set up in answer? The Burt Lumber Co. v. Crawford, 27 R. 798, 86 S. W. 702.

(13) Right to maintain action. The absolute property and right to immediate possession will enable one to maintain action, although he has never had actual possession. Tunstall v. McClelland, 1 Bibb 186; McDowell v. Hall, 2 Bibb 610; 5 Litt. 254.

(14) Description of property-verdict. Where the petition sought the recovery of several articles of different values the failure of the verdict to specify the value of each article was not prejudicial. Black Raven Coal Co. v. Edmonson, 108 S. W. 955.

(15) Tort may be waived. One whose timber has been wrongfully cut and converted may sue for the recovery of the specific property or its value. Dennis Bros. v. Strunk, 108 S. W. 957, 32 R. 1231.

(16) Receiver not allowed. Where the plaintiff may obtain relief by recovering the property as provided in this section a receiver should not be appointed.

3. [Other facts to be stated.] That the plaintiff's cause of action has accrued within one year. If the action be brought to recover property taken under an execution or distress-warrant or attachment, the affidavit must state the fact of the taking and the nature of the process under which it was done. (Form of affidavit, Forms 120, 121; how and before whom made, secs. 551, 549; when by agent or attorney, sec. 550; when may issue on holiday, sec. 665; trial of, and judgment in action, secs. 330, 388.)

# § 182 [209] [Value of each article to be stated.]

If the delivery of several articles of property be claimed, the affidavit must state the value of each.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 328, 769; Form No. 78.

# § 183 [210] [Requisites of order—when returnable.]

The order for the delivery of the property to the plaintiff shall be addressed and delivered, with a copy thereof, to the sheriff. It shall state the names of the parties to the action, and the court in which it is brought or pending, and direct the sheriff to take the property—describing it, and stating its value, as in the affidavit of the plaintiff—and deliver it to him, and to make return of the order on a day to be named therein. The order shall be made returnable as an order of arrest is directed to be returned.

(Form of.

Clure v. McGee, 108 S. W. 341, 32 R. 1318, 128 Ky. 464.

(1) In an action for the delivery of specific personal property in which no defense was made, it was error to give judgment against the defendant for a greater amount than the alleged value of the property. Hibbard v. Est- ridge, 156 Ky. 122, 160 S. W. 746.

(2) This section and sec. 182 limit recovery for the value of property replevied to a sum not exceeding the amount claimed in the affidavit or petition. Kentucky Portland Cement & Coal Co. v. Steckel, 164 Ky. 420, 175 S. W. 663.

Dismissal of action on motion of defendant. No recovery can be had on bond where the action is dismissed for want of prosecution on motion of the defendant. Howard v. Wyatt, 145 Ky. 424, 140 S. W. 655.

# § 184 [211] [Bond to be executed by plaintiff—form of action on by claimant.]

The order shall not be complied with by the sheriff until there has been executed in his presence, by one or more sufficient sureties of the plaintiff, a bond to the defendant, to the effect that the plaintiff shall duly prosecute the action, and that he shall perform the judgment of the court therein, by returning the property, if a return thereof shall be adjudged, and by paying such sums of money as may be adjudged against him in the action, not exceeding double the value of the property and the costs of the action. Any person establishing a claim to the property, pursuant to the provisions of section 29, shall be entitled to the benefit of the provisions of said bond. (Form of bond, Form 123. If defective, new one may be executed, 682. Sureties may be sworn; qualifications of, secs. 683, 684.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 351.

# § 185 [212] [Bond in action against officer—provisions of.]

If the action be brought against a sheriff, or other officer, to recover the possession of property taken by him under an attachment or execution against a person other than the plaintiff, or under a distress warrant, the bond provided for in section 184 shall be to the effect that the plaintiff shall duly prosecute the action, and that he shall perform the judgment of the court therein, by returning the property, if a return thereof shall be adjudged, and by paying to the defendant, or to the plaintiff in the attachment or execution, or party who sued out the distress warrant, as may be directed by the court, such sums of money as may be adjudged against the plaintiff in the action, not exceeding double the value of the property and the costs of the action. (Form of bond, Form 124. Tenant may bring action for delivery against plaintiff in distress warrant, sec. 33.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 241, 352.

# § 184. (1) Bond-action on.

In an action against B, A obtained an order of delivery for a horse, and upon the execution of the bond required by this section the horse was delivered to him. On the trial of the action C, who claimed to be interested, was made defendant, and a judgment rendered in his favor against A for two hundred and fifty dollars, the value of the horse. Held that B and C for the benefit of C, could maintain an action on the bond executed by A to B, and recover against the surety in the bond the amount of his judgment against A. MeGlasson v. Bradford, 7 Bush 250; sec. 874 of the Code of 1854 mentioned in this opinion is not in this Code.

(2) No recovery can be had for attorney fees or any costs or expense except the ordinary costs allowed by the statute. Ky. L. & E. Co. v. Crabtree, 26 R. 283, 80 S. W. 1161, 118 Ky. 395.

(3) When action is dismissed on motion of defendant for want of prosecution no recovery can be had on bond. Howard v. Wyatt, 145 Ky. 424, 140 S. W. 175.

# Claim and Delivery of Personal Property

# § 186 [218] [Order—how executed.]

The sheriff shall execute the order by taking the property therein mentioned, if it be found in the possession of the defendant or of his agent, or of any other person who obtained possession thereof from the defendant, directly or indirectly, after the order was placed in the sheriff's hands. He shall also deliver a copy of the order to the defendant, or to the person from whose possession the property is taken; or, if neither can be found, he shall leave it at the usual place of abode of either, with some person of the age of sixteen years or over. (When may be executed on holiday, sec. 665. May break and enter building to execute, sec. 675.)

# § 187 [214] [Disposition of property by officer.]

If the affidavit of the plaintiff state that the property was taken under an attachment, execution or distress warrant, the sheriff shall deliver it to the plaintiff. In every other case he shall retain the property for two days, unless the bond mentioned in the succeeding section be sooner executed.

# § 188 [215] [Defendant may retain property by executing bond.]

Within two days after the taking of the property by the sheriff, the defendant, or any one for him, may, if the property was not taken under an attachment, execution or distress warrant, cause a bond to be executed to the plaintiff, in the presence of the sheriff, by one or more sufficient sureties, in double the value of the property, to the effect that the defendant shall perform the judgment of the court in the action; whereupon the sheriff shall restore the property to the defendant, or to the person in whose possession it was found. If such bond be not executed within the time above limited, the sheriff shall deliver the property to the plaintiff. He shall return the bonds with the order. (Form of bond, Form 125: see references under sec. 184.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 353.
