# § 237 [259] Action before debt matures—when allowed.

Before a debt or liability upon a contract becomes due or matures, an equitable action for indemnity may be brought by a creditor against his debtor; by a surety against his principal; or by one who is jointly liable with another for such debt or liability, against the latter:

1. Grounds upon which action allowed. If the defendant be about to depart from this State, and, with intent to defraud his creditors, have concealed or removed from this State his property, or so much thereof that the process of court, after judgment, cannot be executed.
2. Grounds upon which action allowed. If there exist against the defendant any of the grounds for an attachment which are mentioned in subsections 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 and 8, section 194. (Form of judgment, secs. 243, 244. Action by joint debtor or surety after debt matures, secs. 661, 662.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 162c.

# § 237.

1. Defective affidavit—although the affidavit was defective, as the debt matured before judgment, the plaintiff had the right, after its maturity, to amend his affidavit and make it conform to the Code. Hey v. Harding, 21 R. 771, 53 S. W. 33.
2. Fraudulent conveyance—attachment may be obtained against debtor who is about to make a fraudulent conveyance of his property although other persons who are solvent are liable for debt. Marks v. Gauze, 24 R. 1949, 72 S. W. 732.
3. Issual before execution of bond. The county judge having directed clerk to issue attachment for debt not due upon execution of proper bond, an attachment issued before bond was executed is void. Kleine v. Nie, 88 Ky. 542, 11 R. 583, 11 S. W. 590.
4. Surety may maintain action against his principal for indemnity before debt falls due. Bamberger v. Moayon, 91 Ky. 517, 13 R. 102, 16 S. W. 276.
5. Surety may sue before debt matures to set aside fraudulent transfer made by his principal. Walters v. Akers, 101 S. W. 1179, 31 R. 259; Samples v. Rogers, 107 S. W. 222, 32 R. 784.

Title VIII]  Attachment-Order of Arrest in Certain Actions. [§ 238-242

# § 238 [260] [Attachment or order of arrest—how obtained.]

In such action, if the petition, verified by the oath of the plaintiff, show the nature and amount of the demand and when it will mature, the court in which the action is pending [or the clerk thereof], or any circuit judge, or the presiding judge of the county court, may grant—

1. [Order for arrest—how obtained.] An order for the arrest of the defendant, if the petition also state the facts mentioned in subsection 1 of section 237.
2. [Attachment—how obtained.] An attachment against the property of the defendant, if the petition also show the existence of any of the grounds specified in subsection 2 of section 237. (Words in brackets inserted by Act. of April 5, 1888, c. 847, p.74; when two justices may grant sec. 689.)

# § 239 [261] [Requisite of attachment or order.]

The order of arrest, or attachment, shall specify the sum for which it is allowed, not exceeding a sum sufficient to satisfy the plaintiff's claim and the probable costs of the action. (Forms of orders, Forms 130 and 131 will answer.)

# § 240 [262] [Bond to be executed before issuance.]

Such order of arrest, or attachment, shall not be issued by the clerk until, if it be an order of arrest, bond be executed pursuant to section 154; nor, if it be an order of attachment, until bond be executed pursuant to section 198. (When: may issue on holiday, sec. 665.)

# § 241 [268] [Sections of Code applicable.]

1. [For order of arrest.] The provisions of chapter I, of Title VIII, subsequent to section 154, shall, so far as applicable, regulate arrests authorized by this article.
2. [For attachment.] The provisions of the first article of this chapter subsequent to section 198, shall, as far as applicable, regulate attachments authorized by this article.

# § 242. [Judgment before debt due costs-creditor to be party.]

1. [Judgment before debt matures.] Judgment may be rendered, in an action brought pursuant to section 237, concerning the alleged liability of the defendant, though it may not have matured.

# § 238. (1) Construction of Section.

Ourbacker v. Clafflin, 96 Ky. 235, 16 R. 436, 28 S. W. 506.

# (2) Specific attachment.

Clerk may issue general but not specific attachment under this section. Samples v. Rogers, 107 S. W. 222, 32 R. 784; see further Green v. Leet, 128 S. W. 78.

# § 239. Statement of amount in order.

The order did not state the amount for which it was allowed, but stated that it "was according to the prayer of the petition." The petition specified the amount of the debt, and the prayer was for judgment for the debt. "Held sufficient. Burnam v. Romans, 2 Bush 191. And an order directing clerk to issue an attachment for amount claimed in petition, without specifying amount, is sufficient. Kleine v. Nie, 88 Ky. 542, 11 R. 583, 11 S. W. 590.

# § 242. Creditor is necessary party, but failure to make him a party does not render void attachment issued in the action.

Attachment-Order of Arrest in Certain Actions. [Title VIII]

2. [Creditor—when to be defendant.] Unless the creditor bring the action, he must be made defendant, but shall not be liable for costs.

# § 243. [Form of judgment in action by creditor-duty of court.]

If such action be brought by the creditor, and judgment be rendered against the defendant, it shall be that he pay to the plaintiff the amount of the judgment, or, if the demand have not matured, that he pay said amount at the time of such maturity; and the court shall apply, in satisfaction of the judgment, moneys of the defendant under its control, though the demand have not matured.

# § 244. [Form of judgment in action by surety against principal.]

If such action be brought by a surety against his principal, and judgment be rendered against such defendant, it shall be that he pay to the creditor the amount of the judgment; or, if the demand have not matured, that he so pay said amount at the time of said maturity. (See further, sec. 247.)

# § 245. [Form of judgment in action by joint debtor against co-surety.]

If such action be brought by one who is jointly liable with another, against the latter, and judgment be rendered against such defendant, it shall be, that he pay to the creditor so much of his demand as such defendant is equitably liable for, as between him and the plaintiff; or, if the demand have not matured, that he pay said amount at the time of said maturity. (See further, secs. 247, 248.)

# § 246. [Money of defendant in court—how applied.]

In actions mentioned in section 244, or section 245, moneys of the defendant under control of the court shall be applied in satisfaction of the judgment when the demand matures, or, with the creditor's consent, before it matures; and, until so applied, such moneys, and property of the defendant attached in the action, shall be under the control of the court.

# § 247. [General provisions concerning judgments.]

1. [Judgment against defendant.] A judgment against the defendant shall include a judgment for costs in favor of the plaintiff.
2. [Judgment-how enforced.] Such judgments may be enforced as in equitable actions generally, except that executions upon judgments rendered pursuant to section 244, or section 245, shall be in favor of the plaintiff for the benefit of the creditor, and shall be so indorsed by the clerk.

# 3. Judgment before debt matures.

In rendering judgment before maturity of the demand, the court shall make an abatement, on account of interest; and shall make the judgment bear interest from the day of its rendition until it shall be satisfied, according to the rate of such abatement.

# § 248. Joint debtor to pay his share before judgment in his favor.

No judgment shall be rendered pursuant to section 245, until the plaintiff shall have paid into court money enough to satisfy the residue of the creditor's demand; or shall have filed in court the creditor's receipt therefor, or his release or assignment to the plaintiff of his right to the demand or to said residue.

# § 249 [278] Actions in which specific attachment allowed, and grounds for.

In an action to enforce a mortgage of, or lien upon, personal property; or, for the recovery, partition or sale of such property; or, by a plaintiff having a future estate or interest therein for the security of his rights, if it satisfactorily appear, from a verified petition, or from affidavits, or the proofs in the cause, that the plaintiff has a just claim, and

§ 249. (1) Affidavit is sufficient that follows the language of this section in stating grounds of attachment, and if petition states amount of debt, it is not necessary to repeat amount that plaintiffs ought to recover. Bell v. Mansfield. 12 R. 89, 13 S. W. 838; and see Hillman v. Hall. 147 Ky. 845, 146 S. W. 31.

(2) Debt not due. Although the debt may not be due, the mortgagee may obtain an attachment under this section. Schnabel v. Jaeobs, 105 Ky. 774, 20 R. 1596, 49 S. W. 774.

(3) Mortgaged personal property. When the mortgage gives the debtor the right to hold and use the property, it can not be taken under a general attachment.

(4) Buyer of personal property is not entitled to specific attachment when the title of the property has not passed to him. Quisenberry v. Rucker, 124 S. W. 274.

(5) Lien creditor may have a general attachment and levy it on property on which he has a lien. Browning v. Crawford, 145 Ky. 279, 140 S. W. 530.

(6) Sufficiency of order as to description of property and requisites of bond. See Green v. Leet, 128 S. W. 78.

that the property is about to be sold, concealed or removed from the State; or if the plaintiff state on oath that he has reasonable cause to believe and does believe that, unless prevented by the court, the property will be sold, concealed or removed from the State, an attachment may be granted against the property. (Hor and before whom affidavit made, secs. 551, 549; when by agent or attorney, sec. 550.)

# § 250 [274] Action by vendor to cancel fraudulent purchase.

In an action by a vendor of property fraudulently purchased, to vacate the contract and have a restoration of the property or compensation therefor, if the petition show such fraudulent purchase and the amount of the plaintiff's claim, and be verified by his oath, an attachment against the property may be granted.

# § 251 [275] Court or judge that may grant.

The attachments in the cases mentioned in sections 249 and 250 may be granted by the court in which the action is brought, or by the judge thereof, or any circuit judge, or the presiding judge of the county court, upon such terms and conditions as to security, on the part of the plaintiff, for the damages which may be occasioned by them, and with such directions as to the disposition to be made of the attached property, as may be just and proper under the circumstances of each case. (When two justices may grant, sec. 689.)

# § 252 [276] Plaintiff to execute bond--retention of property by defendant.

In every case, the plaintiff shall be required to give bond, with surety, for the damages to the defendant, in an adequate sum to be specified in the order granting the attachment; and, if proper, the court or judge may direct that the defendant, or person in possession of the attached property, shall be permitted to retain it, upon giving such bond with surety, and for such sum, as the court or judge may prescribe. (Forms of bonds, Forms 129, 136, 138 will answer.)

# § 253 [277] Attachment not to issue until bond executed.

No order of attachment shall be issued by the clerk until the bond on the part of the plaintiff, required by the order of the court or judge, be executed in his office by one or more sufficient sureties of plaintiff. (When may issue on holiday, sec. 665; sureties may be sworn, qualifications of, secs. 683, 684.)

# § 254 [278] Order of attachment-requisites and execution of.

The order of attachment shall describe the specific property against which it is

§ 251. Clerk has no authority to issue an order of specific attachment. Samples v. Rogers, 107 S. W. 222, 32 R. 785.

§ 252. Bond-liability on. In an action to enforce a lien on a boat, the defendant executed a bond for the forthcoming of the property, and it appearing that the boat was mortgaged, and that the equity of redemption in the property was all that could be subjected, to the extent only of the value of that equity could the obligors be held liable. Halbert v. McCulloch, 3 Met. 456; and see MeKee v. Pope, 18 B. M. 548.

# § 255 [279] [Disposition of attached property.]

The sheriff shall make such disposition of the attached property as may be directed by the court or judge; or, if there be no direction upon the subject, he shall safely keep the property subject to the order of the court.

# § 256 [281] [Officer may pursue and retake in another county.]

If, after an order of attachment against specific property has been placed in the hands of the sheriff, such property be removed from the county, the sheriff may pursue and attach it in another county within twenty-four hours after such removal.

# § 257 [282] [Defendant to give information concerning property.]

If it appear from the return of the sheriff, or the affidavit of the plaintiff, that any specific property, against which an order of attachment is issued, has been concealed or removed by the defendant, the court may require him to attend and be examined on oath respecting such property, and may enforce its orders in this respect as in cases of contempt.

# § 258 [283, 284] [Sections of Code applicable.]

The provisions of the first article of this chapter, not inconsistent with, nor inapplicable to, the foregoing sections of this article, shall regulate the proceedings in cases of attachments against specific property.

# § 263 [289] [Affidavit controverting grounds -how regarded-time to file.]

The defendant may file his affidavit denying all or any of the material statements of the affidavit on which the attachment is issued; and, thereupon, the attachment shall be considered as controverted, and the affidavits of the plaintiff and defendant shall be regarded as the pleadings concerning the attachment, and have no other effect. If the attachment was obtained at the commencement of the action, the defendant may file his affidavit at such time after the levy of the attachment as is given by this Code for filing his defense after the service of the summons—which period may be extended by the court for sufficient cause. (When defense must be filed, sec. 102.)

Title III Attachment-Discharge and Reinstatement Of

# § 264 [290] [Trial of attachment—evidence.]

The trial of the attachment shall be by the court; and the witnesses shall be orally examined, except in cases wherein depositions might be read in an ordinary action. (When depositions may be read, sec. 554; person on whose affidavit attachment issued may be examined, sec. 548.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 62.

# § 265 [291] [Motion to discharge before attachment sustained--notice.]

At any time before the attachment is sustained the defendant, upon reasonable notice to the plaintiff or his attorney may move for its discharge; and the hearing of such motion may be postponed by the court, upon sufficient cause, from time to time; and upon the hearing, if the court be of opinion that the attachment was obtained without sufficient cause, or that the grounds of the attachment, being controverted, are not sustained, it shall be discharged.

# § 266 [202] [Order discharging or sustaining at or after judgment is final-appeal.]

An order for the discharge of an attachment, made at or after the rendition of judgment in the action, shall be final; and cannot be reinstated as is authorized by section 270; but shall be the subject of appeal if the amount in controversy be such as to authorize an appeal. An order sustaining an attachment shall, in like manner, on the rendition of judgment in the action, be the subject of appeal.

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 44. See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 127, 128.

# (3) Pleading.

The defendant can controvert in his answer the grounds of attachment. Talbot v. Pierce, 14 B. M. 195.

# (4)

If the defendant appears and fails to contest the grounds of attachment, the court will regard it as valid. Spalding v. Simms, 4 Met. 285.

# (5) Rights of attaching creditors.

Creditors attaching mortgaged property can make all the defenses against the mortgagee the mortgagor could make, so far as may be necessary for the protection of their attachment liens. Pearce v. Hall, 12 Bush 209.

(1) Evidence on trial. Affidavits can not be read on the trial of attachments. No other proof than oral testimony or depositions is admissible. Newton v. West, 3 Met. 24; but party may waive his right to have oral examination of witnesses by failing to object in proper time and question can not be made for first time in Court of Appeals. Rice v. People's Bank, 21 R. 292, 51 S. W. 441.

2 Practice-motion for new trial or separation of law, and facts not necessary. Kassell v. Snead, 21 R. 777, 52 S. W. 1058; Francis v. Burnett, 84 Ky. 23, 7 R. 715.

# § 265.

Court may discharge at any time. Although the court refused to discharge an attachment on a motion made for that purpose, it may, without additional evidence, discharge it on final hearing. Talbot v. Pierce, 14 B. M. 195; Cabell v. Patterson, 98 Ky. 520, 17 R. 836, 32 S. W. 746; 17 B. M. 536. And it may, after the trial is commenced and part of the evidence given to the jury, entertain a motion to, and discharge the attachment. Taylor v. Smith, 17 B. M. 530.

(2) Practice-although an order or motion to discharge an attachment is not final, it may after a final judgment in the case be reviewed in appeal. Cabell v. Patterson, 98 Kv. 520, 17 R. 836, 32 S. W. 746. See further as to practice Standard Hay & Grain Co. v. Ratlife, 144 Ky. 161, 137 S. W. 1035.

# Attachment-Discharge and Reinstatement Of

# § 267 [203] [Reinstatement-effect of—motion to discharge.]

The reinstatement of attachments, as provided in section 270, shall only be binding on the inferior court until judgment; and when judgment is rendered in the action the inferior court shall make a disposition of the motion to discharge which shall, in like manner, be final.

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 44.

# § 268 [294] [Motion to discharge in vacation--practice--amendment of affidavit]

1. [Motion to discharge in vacation—practice.] The defendant, on reasonable notice to the plaintiff, may, at any time before the term next after the levy of the attachment, move the judge of the court, in vacation, to discharge the attachment, on the ground of its having been issued contrary to the provisions of article I of this chapter; and the judge, on an inspection of the papers in the action, or of authenticated copies of them, shall have the same power of discharging the attachment, by his written order, filed by the clerk, as he would have in court; and shall have like power to permit amendments by the plaintiff.
2. [Amendment of affidavit-effect of.] A plaintiff may, by an amended affidavit, conforming to section 196, cure a defect in the affidavit upon which he obtained an attachment; or may state a ground of attachment not mentioned in his first affidavit, whether it may have existed when he filed the first affidavit, or may have arisen afterward; and may thus acquire a lien upon the attached property, if the proceedings conform to law in other respects; but such lien shall not affect a bona fide right to, or lien previously acquired upon, the property, by attachment or otherwise.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 902.

appealed from (Hanson v. Bowyer, 4 Met. 108); nor can an appeal be taken from a preliminary order discharging an attachment. Leet v. Lockett. 4 Met. 55; Overby v. Gay, 17 B. M. 144; see secs. 269, 270.

(Practice-appeal. A motion for a new trial is not necessary in order to bring a judgment sustaining an attachment before the Appellate Court; nor is it necessary to state separately the law and the facts. Francis v. Burnett, 84 Ky. 24, 7 R. 715; Kassell v. Snead, 21 R. 777, 52 S. W. 1058.

(3) The defendant may appeal from an order sustaining the attachment, and not from the judgment for the debt. Such appeal will not prevent the plaintiff from collecting by execution his judgment but will stay a sale of the attached property if a supersedeas be executed. Hawkins v. Baldauf, 10 Bush 624; and upon such appeal the amount in controversy is the amount prayed for in the petition. Schnabel v. Jacobs, 105 Ky. 774, 20 R. 1596, 49 S. W. 779.

§267. See notes to sec. 266.

268. (1 Amendment to cure defects. Defective petition may be cured by an amendment which does not state a new cause of action, and the amendment will relate back to the filing of petition and give plaintiff priority over another lien created after petition is filed and before amendment; the provision as to amendment of affidavit for attachment does not apply to the petition. Bamberger v. Moayon, 91 Ky. 517, 13 R. 102, 16 S. W. 276; and see Moore v. Harrod, 101 Ky. 248, 19 R. 406, 40 S. W. 675.

(2) Amendment of affidavit for attachment does not relate back to original affidavit or give plaintiff priority over creditors who have obtained attachments before amendment. Harbour-Pitt Shoe Co. v. Dixon, 22 R. 1169, 60 S. W. 186;

Title VIII] Attachment-Discharge and Reinstatement Of.

# § 269 [205] [Time allowed to have reinstated.]

A plaintiff who desires a reinstatement of an attachment may have an entry made upon the record, of leave to apply therefor within a reasonable time, not exceeding twenty days; whereupon, the order of discharge shall be made, to take effect at the end of the period so limited, unless the attachment be reinstated in the meantime.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 32, 127, 128.

# § 270 [20] [Appellate judge may reinstate-effect.]

A judge of the Court of Appeals, if the plaintiff have secured the right to apply for the reinstatement of an attachment, may, upon an inspection of a copy of the record, including the evidence read upon the motion to discharge, reinstate the attachment; and, if the order of reinstatement be filed in the clerk's office of the court in which the action is pending, within the time limited as provided in section 269, the execution of the order of attachment shall proceed; otherwise, it shall stand discharged, and restitution shall be made of any property taken under it. (See further, secs. 266, 267.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs, 32, 127, 128.

Bamberger v. Moayon, 91 Ky. 517, 13 R. 102, 16 S. W. 276; it is good as against other attaching creditors only from date of filing. Bell v. Hall, 2 Duv. 288; Peters v. Conway, 4 Bush 565; and a purchaser of property after it had been attached under a defective affidavit, but before the amendment curing defect was filed, will be protected in his purchase. Northern Ice Co. v. Orr, 102 Ky. 586, 19 R. 1634, 44 S. W. 216.

# § 269. Attachment erroneously discharged.

Order discharging may be reviewed by Court of Appeals upon appeal after final judgment, although no application is made to have attachment reinstated. Cabell v. Patterson, 98 Ky. 520, 17 R. 836, 32 S. W. 746.

# § 270.

(1) Application for reinstatement. The record referred to in this section includes the evidence; so far as the evidence is by deposition, a transcript thereof should be prepared and certified as is cases of equitable actions upon appeal; so far as it is oral, it should be preserved and certified as bills of exceptions in ordinary actions in cases of appeal. Buck v. Watson, 161 Ky. 169, 170 S. W. 509.

(4) Where an attachment obtained under sec. 237 was defective, but debt matured before judgment, the plaintiff could amend his affidavit and perfect his grounds of attachment. Hey v. Harding, 21 R. 771, 53 S. W. 33.

(5) Construction of section. The only authority for a motion to discharge an attachment, is not complied with by offering a copy of an order of the circuit court discharging the attachment, together with the original papers in the action. Buck v. Watson, 161 Ky. 169, 170 S. W. 509.

# § 271. [Injunction defined-mandatory injunction.]

An injunction may be the final judgment in an action, or may be allowed as a provisional remedy, and, if so allowed, it shall be by order. When any mandatory injunction is issued by proper authority, it must be obeyed by all persons to whom it is directed, or who have notice that it has been granted.

Keith v. Wilson, 3 Met. 202, or Bartram v. Ohio R. Co., 141 Ky. 100, 132 S. W. 188.

(1) Effect of injunction. The Civil Code has not diminished or changed the force or effect of an injunction. When issued by proper authority, it must be obeyed by all persons to whom it is directed.

(2) Grounds for mandatory injunction. The only grounds upon which courts of equity may grant injunctions are not specified in the Code. The provisions of the Code upon this subject were not intended to take away other well-recognized grounds of equity jurisdiction which may be found necessary for the full and proper administration of justice.

79 Ky. 295, 2 R. 318. Mandatory injunction, Hager v. New South Brewing Co., 28 R. 895, 90 S. W. 608.

(3) Injunction-in what court to be obtained. An injunction is merely incidental to the action in which it is sought, and can only be obtained in the court in which the action is pending.

Moore v. Sheppard, 1 Met. 97.

(3a) An injunction was improperly granted by a circuit court to restrain the enforcement of a judgment of a police court in a city of the third class as the circuit court had jurisdiction of an appeal under Ky. Stat., sec. 3367.

Polsgrove v. Moss, 154 Ky. 408, 157 S. W. 1133.

Circuit Court has no jurisdiction to enjoin the use of a ferry privilege granted by the Ohio County Court. *Stabl v. Brown*. 84 Ky. 325, 8 R. 279, 1 S. W. 540.

(5) Mandamus not stayed. A railroad company obtained a writ of mandamus to compel the county judge to issue certain bonds. The taxpayers could not enjoin the judge from complying with the writ. *C. & O. R. R. CO. v. Washington Co.*, 10 Bush 564.

(6) Verdict-effect of, may be preserved by injunction; as where a party sued to recover damages for obstructing passway and to enjoin future obstructions, and after a jury returned a verdict determining his right to passway he filed amended petition setting up verdict, and case being transferred to equity, injunction was granted. *Hunt v. Kemper*, 10 R. 593, 9 S.W. 503.

(7) Court of Appeals has no original jurisdiction to grant an injunction. *City of Newport v. Veith*, 144 Ky. 501, 139 S. W. 774.

(8) Classes of cases in which injunction will issue. An injunction will issue in favor of a justice of the peace to restrain another justice of the peace from holding court in the district of the first named justice of the peace. *Wheeler v. Schulman*, 165 Ky. 185, 176 S. W. 1017.

(9) A life tenant may be enjoined at the suit of a remainderman from using minerals and timber on the land for commercial purposes. *MeCoy v. Ferguson, Jr.*, 164 Ky. 136, 175 S. W. 23.

(10) An injunction lies against the commissioner of insurance on behalf of a life insurance company to enjoin him from requiring it to compile a written list of names and addresses of former policy holders with information respecting the policies. *Metropolitan Life Ins. Co. v. Clay*, 158 Ky. 192, 164 S. W. 968.

(11) An injunction may be granted to a city against the owner of property to restrain him from erecting a building made of inflammable material within a designated fire district where the infliction of the penalty prescribed by ordinance does not furnish a full, complete and adequate remedy. *City of Monticello v. Bates*, 163 Ky. 38, 173 S. W. 159.

(12) Ordinarily an injunction will not lie to restrain criminal proceedings. In this case proceedings by way of prosecution for obstructing a passway were enjoined, as a property right was involved and irreparable injury would follow unless the prosecution should be enjoined. *Sweigart v. C. & O. Ry. Co.*, 161 Ky. 463, 170 S. W. 1194.

(13) Relief may be had by injunction against an additional assessment which is void for want of notice. *Boske v. Louis Marks & Bros.*, 161 Ky. 460, 170 S. W. 1175.

(14) A number of persons brought separate suits in an inferior court against a defendant on causes of action growing out of the same transaction. The defendant was allowed to maintain an equitable action in the circuit court to enjoin the prosecution of these suits and compel the plaintiffs to assert what claims they had in the injunction suit. *Illinois Cent. Ry. Co. v. Baker*, 155 Ky. 512, 159 S. W. 1169.

(15) An injunction enjoining a party from interfering with the possession of an infant until there could be in the circuit court a determination of the rights of the parties was sustained. An attempt had been made to change the custody of the child by action in a county court without jurisdiction. *Cullins v. Williams*, 156 Ky. 57, 160 S. W. 733.

(16) An injunction to prevent the collection of an illegal tax upon personalty was sustained. *City of Lancaster v. Pope*, 156 Ky. 1, 160 S. W. 509.

(17) An injunction lies on behalf of justices of the peace to restrain persons claiming wrongfully to exercise powers of which the justices of the peace are in rightful possession. *Hutchinson v. Miller*, 158 Ky. 363, 164 S. W. 961.

272. (1) Amount in controversy—injunction may be obtained without re-

# § 272] Injunction-When and How Obtained.

or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of an act which would produce great or irreparable injury to the plaintiff; or if, during the litigation, it appear that the defendant is doing, or threatens or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, an act, in violation of the plaintiff's rights, respecting the subject of the action and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, a temporary injunction may be granted to restrain such act. It may also be granted in any case in which it is specially authorized by statute. (May be granted in action to settle estates, sec. 436; in forcible entry and detainer, sec. 467; in proceedings to stay and vacate judgments, secs. 378, 523; to quash execution, sec. 406; as to judgments, secs. 284, 285.)

gard to. Bourne v. Beck, 22 R. 792, 58 S. W. 690.

Fee bill-court has jurisdiction to enjoin collection of illegal fee bill. Shackelford v. Phillips, 112 Ky. 563, 24 R. 154, 66 S. W. 419; 68 S. W. 441.

(3a) Ferry privilege is a franchise and the right to its enjoyment may be protected from disturbance by injunction. Hazelip v. Lindsey, 93 Ky. 14, 13 R. 913, 18 S. W. 832; and see Combs v. Sewell, 22 R. 1026, 59 S. W. 526.

(4) Injury to property constantly recurring may be restrained by injunction. Greenup Co. v. M. & B. S. R. R., 88 Ky. 659, 11 R. 169, 11 S. W. 774.

(5) Illegal tax or assessment. The right to have an injunction to restrain the collection of an illegal tax is unquestioned. Gates v. Barrett, 79 Ky. 295, 2 R. 318; Allison v. L. & H. R. R. Co., 9 Bush 247; L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Warren Co., 5 Bush 243; and notes to sec. 25, and an illegal assessment, final in its character, may be enjoined. Baldwin v. Shine, 84 Ky. 502, 8 R. 496, 2 S. W. 164.

(6) Labor unions-may be enjoined from interfering with non-union laborers. Underhill v. Murphy, 25 R. 1731, 78 S. W. 482.

(7) Laws of other States. Injunction may be granted here against a citizen of this State to prevent him from selling property exempt from execution owned by a citizen of this State and temporarily in another State, where it is sought to be subjected. Stewart v. Thompson, 97 Ky. 575, 17 R. 381, 31 S. W. 133.

(8) Nuisance. As to restraining and suppressing a nuisance, see Hain v. Thornberry, 7 Bush 403; Dumesnil v. Dupont, 18 B. M. 800; Louisville Coffin Co. v. Warren, 78 Ky. 400; Seifried v. Hays, 81 Ky. 377, 5 R. 369; and injunction will lie to prevent erection of house that will do great injury. Miley v. O'Hearn, 13 R. 834, 18 S. W. 529; but injunction against a threatened nuisance will not be granted when the thing complained of is not per se a nuisance. Pfingst v. Senn, 94 Ky. 556, 15 R. 325, 23 S. W. 358; Alexander v. Tebeau, 24 R. 1305, 71 S. W. 427; Boyd v. Board of Councilmen, 25 R. 1311, 77 S. W. 669.

(Prize-fighting—may be prevented by injunction. Com. v. McGovern, 116 Ky. 212, 25 R. 411, 75 S. W. 261; see also Louisville Athletic Club v. Nolan, 119 S. W. 800, 134 Ky. 220.)

(10) Prosecutions under invalid ordinance. Numerous warrants having been issued against the defendant, and the amount of the fine for violating the ordinance not being sufficient to authorize an appeal, an injunction was the only remedy. Shinkle v. City of Cov., 83 Ky. 420, 7 R. 412; and see Brown v. Trustees, 11 Bush 435; and prosecutions under a void ordinance may be enjoined. City of Newport v. Bridge Co., 90 Ky. 193; 12 R. 39, 13 S. W. 720; South Cov. R. v. Berry, 93 Ky. 43, 13 R. 943, 18 S. W. 1026; Boyd v. Board of Councilmen, 25 R. 1311, 77 S. W. 669.

(11) Remedy at law. If a party has a good defense at law and fails to present it, or it is disallowed by the verdict, he can not obtain relief by injunction. Talbot v. Bank, 2 J. J. M. 548; Harrison v. Lee, 7 J. J. M. 171; McCoun v. Macklin, 7 Bush 308; Walker v. Thomas, 88 Ky.

# § 273 [3oo] [Officers who may grant.]

The injunction may be granted at the commencement of the action, or at any time before judgment by the court, or by any circuit judge, or by the clerk of the court, or the county judge if the judge of the court be absent from the county, or by two justices of the peace, if the judge and the clerk of the court and the county judge be absent from the county. No injunction shall be granted by any of the foregoing officers, unless it appear from the affidavit of the party applying therefor that it has not been refused by the court; nor shall such injunction be granted by a clerk or county judge or justice unless it appear from such affidavit that it has not been refused by the court or any circuit judge. No injunction or temporary restraining order shall be granted until after the petition seeking the injunction shall be filed. No injunction or temporary restraining order shall be granted by any circuit or other judge of similar jurisdiction in any action pending outside of the.

1. The rule that an injunction will not be granted where there is an adequate remedy at law refers to legal remedies and not criminal proceedings. *Underhill v. Murphy*, 25 R. 1731, 78 S. W. 482.
2. Restraint of trade. Violation of valid contract concerning may be enjoined. *Sutton v. Head, 86 Ky. 156, 9 R. 453, 5 S. W. 410; Stovall v. McCutchen*, 21 R. 1317, 54 S. W. 969.
3. Threatened breach of contract may under some circumstances be restrained by injunction. *Duncan v. Central Pass. R. R.*, 85 Ky. 525, 9 R. 92, 4 S. W. 228.
4. Title to property-to protect. A purchased from B a tract of land in Illinois, and paid him the purchase-price, accepting a deed. The acknowledgment of the deed was so defective as not to pass the title against purchasers from B and B being insolvent and about to dispose of the property, A could enjoin him from so doing. *Frank v. Peyton*, 82 Ky. 150, 6 R. 8.
5. Tolls. Individual can not enjoin a corporation from collecting toll on the ground that its road is out of repair. *Shewmaker v. M. W. & L. T. P.*, 18 R. 221, 35 S. W. 1040.
6. Trespass. An injunction will lie to restrain a trespass if the threatened trespass will destroy the very substance of the estate 'in the character in which it has been enjoyed,' or if so permanent and continuous that it can never be said to be complete, so that the injury can be computed; or if the injury can not be estimated in money; or if so vexatiously persisted in that a multiplicity of suits must result; or if committed by one who is insolvent and against whom a verdict will be valueless. *Hillman v. Hurley, 82 Ky. 626, 6 R. 682; Musselman v. Marquis, 1 Bush 463; Peak v. Hayden, 3 Bush 125; Ellis v. Wren, 84 Ky. 254, 8 R. 285, 1 S. W. 440; O'Hara v. Johns, 7 R. 296; Preston v. Preston, 85 Ky. 16, 8 R. 633, 2 S. W. 501; Chambers v. Haskel*, 25 R. 1707, 78 S. W. 478.
7. Void judgment-proceedings under may be enjoined. *Combs v. Sewell*, 22 R. 1026, 59 S. W. 526; and see notes to sec. 285.
8. Waste may be restrained. Ky. Stat., sec. 2336.
9. Common carrier-wrongful and discriminating acts of may be enjoined. *L. & N. R. Co. v. Central Stock Yards Co.*, 97 S. W. 778, 30 R. 18.
10. Mandatory injunction will be granted to compel removal of thing that is a continuing trespass. *Cum. Tel. Co. v. Barnes*, 101 S. W. 301, 30 R. 1291.
11. Criminal proceedings can not be stayed by injunction. *Smiser v. City of Cynthiana*, 29 R. 1244, 97 S. W. 360; see further cases cited in note 10.
12. Municipal council will not be enjoined from passing an ordinance. *Slade v. City of Lexington*, 121 S. W. 621.
13. Court of Appeals has no original jurisdiction to grant an injunction. *City of Newport v. Veith*, 144 Ky. 501, 139 S. W. 774.

# § 274 [802] [Granted before judgment-court may control.]

Injunctions granted before judgment shall be subject to the further order of the court.

# § 275 [804] [Defendant who has answered to be notified.]

An injunction shall not be granted against a defendant who has answered, unless he have had notice of the application therefor.

# § 276 [805] [Notice of application for-when not required-restraining order—bond-practice.]

An injunction shall be granted only upon reasonable notice, in writing, to the party sought to be enjoined, of the time and place of the application therefor, and of the court or officer to whom the application is to be made. Where the court or officer to whom the application for an injunction is made shall be satisfied by the facts set forth in the affidavit of the applicant, or by other evidence, that irreparable injury will result to the applicant from the delay of giving notice, the court or officer may enter a temporary order restraining the act or acts sought to be enjoined, or it may be mandatory in its nature, if the case so require.

W. 413; St. Bernard Coal Co. v. Pittsburg Coal Co., 112 Ky. 418, 23 R. 52, 64 S. W. 288; Jones v. Walters, 24 R. 878, 70 S. W. 191.

(3) County judge may grant if circuit judge be absent from county. Renshaw v. Cook, 111 S. W. 377, 129 Ky. 347.

(4) A plaintiff dismissed without prejudice an action in which a temporary restraining order had issues. A circuit judge was not thereby deprived of authority to grant a temporary injunction in a new suit on the same cause of action. Howard v. Simpson, 175 Ky. 237, 194 S. W. 105.

(5) The refusal of an injunction by a judge of a circuit court other than the court in which the action is pending will not preclude the issual of the injunction by another circuit judge. Petrey v. Holliday, 178 Ky. 410, 199 S. W. 67.

(6) In 1876 a temporary injunction granted by a clerk of a court by an order indorsed on the summons and not entered elsewhere was sustained. McKay v. Blackwell, 9 Ky. Opinions 18.

(7) Under this section, jurisdiction to grant a mandatory injunction is conferred on the judge of the circuit court, and in his absence on a judge of a similar jurisdiction. Scott v. Singleton, 171 Ky: 117, 188 S. W. 302.

# § 274. Order refusing temporary injunction is not final.

when a temporary restraining order or a mandatory order is final in its nature, and in itself furnishes all the relief that can be obtained, or when a party knows in time to give notice the relief that he intends to ask and he delays until it is too late to get the relief if notice is given. Title VIII. Injunction-When and How Obtained.

except that no mandatory restraining order shall be entered by any clerk, county judge or two justices in any case. Such restraining order shall in no case be granted until the applicant shall have given bond, as prescribed by subsection 3 of section 278 of the Civil Code, and a copy of the order shall be forthwith served upon the party or parties sought to be enjoined.

The restraining order, if made by the court, or any circuit or other judge of similar jurisdiction, shall set forth a reasonable place and time, not to exceed ten days from the day upon which the order is made, at which the applicant shall move the court or judge to grant the injunction; and the order shall remain in force until the motion is heard and determined, but not for a greater length of time than ten days after the day fixed in the order for hearing the application. Such motion for injunction shall be tried as provided in this Code for the trial of applications for injunctions of which notice has been given in the first instance. The restraining order, if made by the clerk of the court, the county judge or two justices of the peace, shall remain in force until set aside or modified, or until the injunction is granted as provided in section 290 of the Civil Code as amended by this act. (Section as amended by Act of March 19, 1894, c. 87, p. 200.)

# § 277 [806] [Affidavit may be read on application for, when notice has been given.]

On the hearing of an application for an injunction of which notice has been given, each party may read affidavits. (How and before whom affidavit made, secs. 551, 549.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 129.

# § 278 [807] [Bond to obtain—requisites of.]

1. [Bond to stay proceedings under an execution—appraisement.] If an injunction be granted to stay proceedings upon a levy made under an execution, and the party applying for the injunction desire to discharge the levy, he shall execute a bond to the effect that he will satisfy the execution to the extent to which South Brewing Co., 28 R. 895, 90 S. W. 608. petual injunction. Rallihan v. Motschmann, 179 Ky. 180, 200 S. W. 358.

Rogers, 107 Ky. 236, 21 R. 905, 53 S. W. 413; St. Bernard Coal Co. v. Pittsburg Coal Co. 112 Ky. 418, 23 R. 52, 64 S. W. 288; Jones v. Walters, 24 R. 878, 70 S. W. 191.
3. Restraining order dissolved by judge granting it can not be reinstated by judge of Court of Appeals. Kelly v. Pulaski Stave Co., 105 S. W. 153, 31 R. 943, 127 Ky. 155. (Where an execution has been stayed by injunction and bond executed as provided in subsec. 1, the effect of it.

# §279 Injunction-When and How Obtained.

The injunction may be dissolved, not exceeding the value of the property released; and upon the execution of such bond, and the issuing of the injunction, the levy shall be discharged. If he do not desire to release the levy, he may execute a bond to the effect that, upon a dissolution of the injunction, in whole or in part, he will have the property, or its value, forthcoming and subject to the order of the court; and, upon the execution of such bond, and the issuing of the injunction, the property levied on shall be delivered to him. For the purpose of ascertaining the value of the property, it shall be appraised as directed in section 215.

# 2. [Additional bond in cases of executions.]

If such injunction extend further than the stay of proceedings upon the levy, the party shall give such additional bond as the court, judge or officer granting the injunction may direct.

# 3. [Judge to fix amount of bond in other cases.]

In all other cases, the court, judge or officer granting the injunction shall, in the order granting it, fix the amount of the bond to be given, and may prescribe its terms. If the terms of the bond be not so prescribed, it shall be to the effect that the party giving it will pay to the party enjoined such damages as he may sustain, if it be finally decided that the injunction ought not to have been granted.

# 4. [Bond where judgment is suspended.]

If the injunction be to stay proceedings upon a judgment, the amount of the bond shall be sufficient to cover, with other damages, the sum of money for which judgment was rendered, with five years' interest thereon, and the rent, hire or value of the use, for two years, of property for which judgment was given; and, in case of personal property, its value also. (Form of bond, Forms 143-146; if defective, new one may be executed, sec. 682. See further as to enjoining judgments, secs. 284, 285.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 708, 715; Forms Nos. 45, 46, 385, 386, 387, 390.

# §279 [308]

[Bond must be executed before order issues.] The order of injunction shall not be issued by the clerk until a bond, as is required by is to release the levy and discharge the lien created thereby, and it is the duty of the officer handling the execution to restore the property levied on to the defendant.

Keith v. Wilson, 3 Met. 202; Lockridge v. Biggerstaff, 2 Duv. 281.

(3) When the levy of an execution against a debtor who has ample property to satisfy it is enjoined and, pending injunction, property is so diverted that debt cannot be made, sureties on bond are liable for amount of execution.

Moore v. Wilson, 1 R. 351.

(5) Bond that left amount blank was good and bound sureties for amount of damage sustained.

Alexander v. Gardner, 113 S. W. 906, 130 Ky. 785.

# §279. (1) Action on bond.

An action can not be maintained upon an injunction.

# Title VIII] Injunction—When and How Obtained.

# § 280 [300] [Sureties in bond to be accepted by clerk.]

Sureties in injunction bonds shall be accepted by the clerk under the same responsibilities as in other cases of sureties accepted by him.

# § 281 [310] [Order of injunction-requisites and service of.]

W. 5.55.

(3) Where a party is prevented by injunction from performing an illegal or invalid contract he cannot upon dissolution of the injunction recover on the bond. E. Tenn. Tel. Co. v. Anderson Tel. Co., 115 Ky. 488, 24 R. 2358, 74 S. W. 218.

(4) In all cases except where proceedings upon a judgment have been stayed, the remedy is on the injunction bond, and no assessment of damages by the court upon the dissolution of the injunction is necessary. When a judgment has been stayed, the judge is empowered to assess the damages. Logsden v. Willis, 14 Bush 183; Rankin v. Estes, 13 Bush 428. It is only where the enforcement of a judgment has been enjoined that the court must at the time ascertain the damages. Eastern Ky. R. R. v. Brown, 99 Ky. 540, 18 R. 825, 36 S. W. 555; Alexander v. Gish, 88 Ky. 13, 10 R. 989, 9 S. W. 801.

(5) In an action upon an injunction bond the plaintiff can only recover such damages as resulted to him from the operation of the injunction; he cannot recover the costs and expense incurred in defending the action in which the injunction was obtained and which were not occasioned by the injunction. Burgen v. Sharer, 14 B. M. 497.

(6) Defective bond—may be good as a common law bond and recovery had thereon. Hardin v. Barlow, 6 Mon. 396; Hopkins v. Morgan, 7 Mon. 3; 9 Bush 748.

(7) Numerous parties. One or more may sue upon bond for benefit of all. Alexander v. Gish, 88 Ky. 13, 10 R. 989, 9 S. W. 80l; and see notes to sec. 25.

# § 282 [811] [Service of order when unnecessary-party when bound.]

If notice of the application for an injunction have been given to the party sought to be enjoined, it shall not be necessary to serve the order upon him; he is bound by the injunction as soon as the bond required of the adverse party is executed.

# § 283 [812] [When binding on defendant.]

An injunction binds the party from the time he is informed thereof. (See sec. 282.)

# § 284 [818] Judgment that cannot be enjoined.

No injunction shall be granted to stay proceedings upon a judgment of a justice of the peace, or of a county court, if the value of the matter in dispute do not exceed twenty-five dollars.

# § 285 [814] [Judgment to be enjoined in court rendering.]

An injunction to stay proceedings on a judgment shall not be granted, in an action brought by the party seeking the injunction, in any other court than that in which the judgment was rendered. Nor shall such injunction be granted, unless the party applying therefor make affidavit that no injunction has been previously granted to stay the proceedings on such judgment. (See further as to enjoining judgment, secs. 378, 523, 524.)

§ 282. Defendant bound as soon as he has notice of issuance of injunction if bond has been executed. Bartram v. Ohio & B. S. R. Co., 141 Ky. 100, 132 S. W. 188; see further cases cited in note to sec. 286.

§ 283. Parties bound by injunction. Injunctions do not interfere with the rights of third parties who have acquired them in good faith, and who are not parties or privies to the proceedings. They must be respected while in force, although improperly granted, but bind only parties and privies. Jolly v. City of Hawesville, 89 Ky. 270, 11 R. 477, 12 S. W. 313; Green v. Quisenberry, 118 S. (2) The circuit court has no jurisdiction to enjoin the sale of property under an execution upon a judgment rendered in a justice's court. *C. & O. R. R. Co. v. Reasor, 84 Ky. 369, 8 R. 374, 1 S. Patterson, 110 Ky. 863, 23 R. 316, 62 S. W. 1040; and see Shackelford v. Phillips, 112 Ky. 563, 24 R. 154, 66 S. W. 419, 68 S. W. 441.*

(3) Where a judgment upon a note given for land is rendered in the quarterly court, a petition for an injunction must be filed in that court; the case can then be transferred to the circuit court if the title to the land is involved. *Kelly v. Kelly, 2 Duv. 363.*

(4) The provision that an injunction to stay proceedings on a judgment must be brought in the court in which the judgment was rendered applies not only to the party against whom the judgment was rendered, but to all who seek to enjoin it. *Mallory v. Dauber, 83 Ky. 239, 7 R. 243.*

(4a) A suit to enjoin an execution issued under a judgment in a justice's court should be brought in such court. *Kentucky River Hardwood Co. v. Noble, 168 Ky. 773, 182 S. W. 941.*

# § 286. [Disobedience of-how punished.]

Disobedience of an injunction may be punished by the court, or by the judge thereof, or any circuit judge in vacation, as a contempt. Where the case has been appealed such disobedience may be punished by the Court of Appeals, or by any judge thereof during a vacation, recess or adjournment of said court. Upon production of evidence by affidavit of the breach of an injunction, the court, or any judge thereof during vacation, may proceed by rule and attachment against the party committing the breach, who may introduce like evidence in his behalf. Should the party be held to be in contempt, unless he purge the contempt, the judge in vacation may commit him to jail until the sitting of the court, or take a bond with security for his appearance to answer for the contempt at the next term of the court, and, in the meantime, to obey the injunction. (Cection as amended by Act of March 19, 1894, c. 87, p. 200.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 131.

# § 287 [817] [Affidavits read on application, to be filed.]

Affidavits read upon an application for an injunction shall be filed with the papers of the case.

# § 288 [818] [Surety in bond—additional may be required.]

A party enjoined may, at any time before judgment, after reasonable notice to the adverse party, move the court for additional security; and, if it appear on such motion that the surety in the injunction bond has died or removed from this State, or is insufficient, the court may vacate the order of injunction, unless in a reasonable time sufficient security is given.

Proceedings thereunder may be enjoined in Co. v. Krieger, 91 Ky. 625, 13 R. 219, 16 S. W. 824; Borrone v. Mosely, 143 Ky. 812, 137 S. W. 531; Smith v. Miller, 28 R. 1205, 91 S. W. 1140, 122 Ky. 289; Stratton v. Meriweather, 147 Ky. 577, 144 S. W. 1083; and see further sec. 747.

(1) Circuit court may enjoin judgment of justice's court obtained without service of process. Robinson v. Carlton, 29 R. 876, 123 Ky. 419, 96 S. W. 549.

(2) A common carrier can not excuse its violation of an injunction upon the ground that its duty to the public demanded such violation. Kv. & I. Bridge Co. v. Krieger, 91 Ky. 625, 13 R. 219, 16 S. W. 824.

# § 286. (1) Disobedience of injunction.

Violation of an injunction pending an appeal from judgment perpetuating injunction is a contempt of the Appellate Court and punishable. K. & I. Bridge Co. v. Krieger, 91 Ky. 625, 13 R. 219, 16 S. W. 824.

# §290.

[Motion to dissolve or modify injunction or restraining order - notice evidence.] A motion to dissolve or modify an injunction, or to set aside or modify a restraining order, granted by the clerk of the court, the county judge or two justices of the peace, may be made before the court, or any circuit or other judge of similar jurisdiction, who is competent to grant an injunction in the case under section 273, of the Civil Code, at any time after reasonable notice, in writing, to the plaintiff or applicant for the injunction or restraining order of the time and place of the motion and of the judge before whom it is to be made. Upon the hearing of such motion, either party may read affidavits, and the court or judge shall, within ten days from the hearing, sustain or overrule the motion to dissolve or modify the injunction, or shall set aside or modify the restraining order, or grant an injunction in lieu of the same, as the case may require, and shall try either of such motions as if it were an application for an injunction de novo. The order of the judge overruling the motion to dissolve or modify an injunction, or dissolving or modifying an injunction; or setting aside or modifying a restraining order, or granting an injunction, with the notice, shall be returned to the office of the clerk of the court, and the order obeyed as if made by the court. (Section as amended by Act of March 19, 1894, c. 87, p. 200.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 129.

# § 292 [822] [Postponement of motion-grounds for.]

The motion, upon the whole case, to dissolve or modify the injunction, may be postponed, on the application of either party, to a subsequent day, or to the next term, if the court be satisfied that, under the circumstances of the case, the delay is proper. If delay be asked in order to obtain the testimony of a witness, it must appear by affidavit what facts the witness is expected to prove, that the affiant believes his testimony will be true, and that it could not have been obtained by due diligence.

After hearing the motion, the court or judge shall overrule it, or dissolve or modify the injunction, according to the right of the case.

Only one motion to dissolve or modify an injunction, upon the whole case, is allowed.

# CHAPTER V.

# RECEIVER.

# 298. Court may appoint—appeal from order.

§ 299. Mortgaged property to take charge of.

§ 300. Persons not to be appointed.

301. Oath and bond of receiver.

§ 302. Powers of receiver.

# 298 [328] [Court may appoint-appeal from order appointing or refusing to appoint.]

On the motion of any party to an action who shows that he has, or probably has, a right to, a lien upon, or an interest in, any property or fund, the right to which is involved in the action, and that the property or fund is in danger of being lost, removed or materially injured, the court, or the judge thereof during vacation, may appoint a receiver.

# 298.

1. Action against receiver cannot be brought without leave of court by which he was appointed. Spalding v. Com., 88 Ky. 135, 10 R. 714, 10 S. W. 420; May v. Ball, 24 R. 875, 70 S. W. 196.
2. Appointment of powers. In an action under the act of 1856, the statute does not authorize the appointment of a receiver except to take charge of property in the possession, or under the control, of the transferee, or the person to whom the conveyance is made; and there is no law authorizing a receiver to take charge of property in the possession of the transferrer, until it has been first adjudged that the conveyance operated as an assignment. Griffith v. Cox, 79 Ky. 562.
3. Appointment of receiver is largely in the discretion of the court. L. & V. R. R. v. Eakin, 100 Ky. 745, 19 R. 54, 39 S. W. 416; 12 Bush 608.
4. As the Banking Act of 1912, chapter 4, page 8, provided a method for the liquidation of insolvent banks a court of equity cannot appoint a receiver therefor unless the commission fails to perform the duties required by the act. Cartmell v. Com. Bk. & Tr. Co., 153 Ky. 798, 156 S. W. 1048.
5. A certain order removing a trustee and directing the funds to be turned over to the receiver of the court was not erroneous. Holbrook v. Fyffe, 164 Ky. 435, 175 S. W. 977.
6. Court has authority to appoint a receiver at any state of the proceedings, whenever the facts justify it, and an application denied may be renewed upon a new or different state of facts. Columbia F. & T. Co. v. Morgan, 19 R. 1761. 44 S. W. 389, 628, 45 S. W. 65.
7. When a receiver has been appointed and is in possession, his possession is the possession of the court, and may not be disturbed without leave of the court; nor will the court, without its sanction being first obtained, permit any one to intercept or prevent payment to its receiver of anything which he has been appointed to receive, although it may not be actually in his hands. Hazelrigg v. Bronaugh, 78 Ky. 62.
8. Under sec. 299 a receiver may be appointed to preserve the property, but not to sell it. Wilson v. A. & T. Co., 91 Ky. 299. 12 R. 881, 15 S. W. 783.
9. See further, Kentucky Statutes, sec. 392, as to receivers.
10. Where a person has wrongfully in his possession money belonging to another, the court may require him to pay money to a receiver. Rebhan v. Fuhrman, 21 R. 17, 50 S. W. 976.
11. Receiver of insolvent corporation may repudiate illegal transfer made by corporation and sue to recover assets wrongfully diverted. Industrial Deposit Co. v. Taylor, 26 R. 802. 82 S. W. 574.
12. A joint owner of land may have it under certain circumstances placed in hands of a receiver to prevent the other joint owner from cutting and removing.

The order of a court, or of the judge thereof, appointing or refusing to appoint a receiver, shall be deemed a final order for the purpose of an appeal to the Court of Appeals. Provided, That such order shall not be superseded.

(Receiver to take charge of attached property, sec. 218. Receivers in action involving waste, see Ky. Stat., sec. 2336.)

# Liability and Mortgages

(12) Liability for funds. The receiver of a fund having loaned it to a firm of which he was a member, the other members of the firm are liable, although the money was repaid to the receiver by the firm. Ryan v. Morrill, 83 Ky. 352, 7 R. 339.

(12a) Mortgages. An insolvent corporation sued for the appointment of a receiver and a sale of its property; a holder of some of its mortgage bonds being a defendant filed an answer and asked for the appointment of a receiver and showed good grounds for same. The receivership was properly granted. Lewis' Admr. v. Bowling Gr. Ry. Co., 155 Ky. 681, 160 S. W. 242.

(12b) A mortgagor made an insolvent assignment. The mortgage did not appropriate rents to the mortgage debt. A motion by the mortgagee for a receiver was properly overruled. Deposit Bank v. Kirby, 175 Ky. 700, 194 S. W. 929.

(18) Taxation of funds in hands of circuit court should direct receiver to list property and pay taxes. Spalding v. Com., 88 Ky. 135, 10 R. 714, 10 S. W. 125.

# Title VIII

# § 299 [320] [Mortgaged property to take charge of.]

In an action by a mortgagee for the sale of the mortgaged property, a receiver may be appointed, if it appear that the property is in danger of being lost, removed or materially injured or, that the condition of the mortgage has not been performed, and that the property is probably insufficient to discharge the mortgage debt. Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 116.

# § 300 [8so] [Persons not to be appointed.]

Excepting personal representatives, guardians, curators and committees of persons of unsound mind, neither a party to an action, nor his attorney, nor any person interested therein, shall be appointed receiver.

# § 301 [831] [Oath and bond of receiver.]

Before entering upon his duties, the receiver must be sworn to perform them faithfully; and, with one or more sureties approved by the court or judge, must execute a bond to such person and in such sum as the court shall direct, to the effect that he will faithfully discharge the duties of receiver in the action, and obey the orders of the court therein. (Form of bond, Form 96.)

(22) Appeal from quarterly or other inferior court can not be taken direct to the Court of Appeals. Brown v. Crump, 96 S. W. 1112, 29 R. 1227, 123 Ky. 685.

299. (1) Appointment in discretion of court. The appointment of a receiver is a matter of sound legal discretion, to be made or refused on the circumstances of each particular case. Douglass v. Cline, 12 Bush 608; and see Goldsmith v. Fletcheimer, 16 R. 432, 28 S. W. 21.

A receiver may be appointed when the title to land is involved, or there is a proceeding to enforce a lien, and the party in possession claiming title is insolvent, and is committing waste, or when the plaintiff is entitled to the rents under his contract and the party in possession is insolvent and the property insufficient to pay the claim. Collins v. Richart, 14 Bush 621; Hounshell v. Insurance Co., 81 Ky. 304, 5 R. 267; and see Columbia Trust Co. v. Morgan, 19 R. 1761, 44 S. W. 389, 628; 45 S. W. 65; Ky. Stat., sec. 2336.

(3) Construction of section. In a suit to enforce a mortgage lien the court or judge in vacation has no power to order a sale of the mortgaged property in advance of a decision upon the merits, and can only appoint a receiver to preserve, not to sell the property. Wilson v. A. & T. Co., 91 Ky. 299, 12 R. 881, 15 S. W. 783; Hurst v. Nicola Bros., 23 R. 1406, 65 S. W. 364.

Title MII

# § 302 [Powers of receiver.]

The receiver has, under the control of the court, power to bring and defend actions, to take and keep possession of the property, to receive rents, collect debts and generally to do such acts respecting the property as the court may authorize. (May sue in his own name, sec. 21.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 117.

The bond was given. Rowlett v. Eubank, 1 Bush 477.

(3) Although no statute authorizes a general receiver to be appointed by the court, if for convenience the receiver by the consent and approval of the court shall execute a general bond, it will be good as a common law obligation and the sureties bound for the acts of the receiver under it. Ellis v. Carr, 1 Bush 527; see further, Ky. Stat., sec. 392.

(4) Sureties of sheriff on his official bond are not liable for his acts as receiver. Heeter v. Jewell, 6 Bush 510.

(5) Receiver can not be sued until after court has made an order directing him to pay the money over and he has failed to do so. May v. Ball, 24 R. 875, 70 S. W. 196.

# § 302.

(1) Powers of receiver. A receiver can not bring an action involving the title to real estate, or submit a controversy concerning real estate to arbitration and bind without their consent the parties in interest by the judgment which may be rendered. Caldwell v. McWhorter, 84 Ky. 130, 8 R. 79.

(2) A receiver has no right to retain funds collected by him, and pay with his individual claims against the party to whom he is directed to pay money, the amount due to him. The possession of funds collected by the receiver is the possession of the court. Johnson v. Gunter, 6 Bush 534; see further, notes to sec. 298.

(3) Power of court to punish one for contempt for failing to deliver property to the receiver. Rebhan v. Fuhrman, 21 R. 17, 50 S. W. 976.

(4) An action can not be maintained against a receiver without leave of court. Hazelrigg v. Bronaugh, 78 Ky. 62.

(5) Receiver can not speculate in property under his control. Titherington v. Hodge, 81 Ky. 286, 5 R. 211.

See further as to rights and liabilities of sureties. Farmers' Bank v. Sherley, 12 Bush 304, and notes to sec. 301.

(7) Receiver appointed under Code, or by virtue of sec. 409, Ky. Stat., may when so directed by the court maintain an action. Mitchell v. Chenault, 112 Ky. 267, 23 R. 1544, 65 S. W. 447.

(8) A receiver appointed under Ky. Stat., sec. 616, may, without first obtaining the consent of the court, purchase property as receiver at a sale under a judgment in his favor when the purchase-price does not exceed the amount of the indebtedness. The court has power to withhold its approval of what its receiver has done, but this power will not be exercised in an arbitrary manner or without grounds. Williams v. Owensboro Sav. Bk. & Tr. Co. Receiver, 153 Ky. 789, 156 S. W. 899.

(9) Negligence and misconduct. If a receiver is negligent or guilty of misconduct, the court may refuse him compensation. He is liable for negligent failure to collect rents: if he mingles the trust fund with his own he may be charged interest. Higgins v. Shields, 151 Ky. 227, 151 S. W. 391.

# CHAPTER VI.

# DEPOSIT IN COURT.

# § 303. Deposit of money or property may be ordered.

If a party admit by his pleading, or upon examination, that he has in his possession, or under his control, any money, or other thing capable of delivery, which, being the subject of the litigation, is held by him as trustee for another party, or which belongs or is due to another party, the court may order the same to be deposited in court, or to be delivered to the party who is thus admitted to be entitled thereto, with or without security, subject to the further order of the court.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 454, 454b.

# § 304. Court may enforce order.

If, in the exercise of its authority, a court order the deposit or delivery of money or other thing, and the order be disobeyed, the court, besides punishing the disobedience, may make an order requiring the sheriff to take the money or thing, and deposit or deliver it in conformity with its direction.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 454, 454b.

# § 305. Sheriff may be directed to keep property—costs.

The court may direct the sheriff to keep safely any property delivered to him pursuant to the provisions of this chapter; and may allow him the necessary expenses attending the same, to be paid by such party as the court shall direct, and taxed in the costs of the action.

# § 306. Sheriff may be directed to keep money.

It may confide to the sheriff money deposited or paid into court—which shall be kept by him under the same requirements and responsibilities of himself and his sureties as are provided by this Code in respect to money deposited in lieu of bail.

# § 307. Money may be placed in bank.

# § 308. Money may be loaned.

# § 303. Clerks not authorized to receive money on replevin bonds, judgments or executions, and the sureties in the bond of the clerk are not liable for his failure to pay over money thus received by him.

Hardin v. Carrico. 3 Met. 290; Chinn v. Mitchell, 2 Met. 92; Durant v. Gabby, 2 Met. 91.

Title VIIIj Deposit in Court. [§307-308]

# § 307 [8s7] [Money may be placed in bank—how drawn out.]

A court sitting in a county in which, or in any county adjoining which, there is a bank, or branch of a bank, created by the laws of this State, or of the United States, transacting regular banking business, may order money paid into court to be deposited in such bank or branch, to the credit of the court in the action or proceeding in which the money was paid. Money so deposited shall be paid only upon the check of the clerk of the court, annexed to its certified order for the payment, and in favor of the person to whom the order directs the payment to be made.

# § 308 [8s8] [Money may be loaned.]

Money deposited or paid into court, in an action, shall not be loaned by the court, unless with the consent of all the parties having an interest therein or making claim thereto; or, unless the party moving to have it loaned, execute a bond to the Commonwealth of Kentucky, with good surety, to be approved by the court, for the benefit of the parties interested, conditioned that the borrower of the money will pay it according to the contract loan.

§ 309. Definition-two kinds.

§ 310. Issue of fact—when arises.

§ 309 [350] [Definition—issues of law and fact.] Issues arise on the pleadings when a fact, or conclusion of law, is maintained by one party and controverted by the other. They are—

1. Of law.
2. Of fact.

§ 310 [340] [Issue of fact—when arises.] An issue of fact arises—

1. [When a pleading is traversed.] Upon a traverse in an answer or other pleading of a material allegation in a petition or other pleading.
2. [When law controverts it.] Upon an allegation of a pleading which is controverted by law. (Traverse defined, sec. 113-7.)

# § 311 [341] [Trial defined.]

A trial is a judicial examination of the issues of law or of fact in an action or proceeding.

# § 312 [342] [Issues-how tried.]

1. [Trial of law issues.] Issues of law must be tried by the court.

2. [Issues of fact in ordinary actions.] Issues of fact in ordinary actions, except for injuries to person or character, shall be tried by the court, unless a jury trial be demanded by a party. (Court may try actions for injuries to person and character, sec. 331; court to state separately law and facts, sec. 332.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 901; See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 46.

# § 311. (1) Trial of two cases together, when prejudicial.

It was error to hear together by a jury trial two cases in each of which the plaintiff was a competent witness for the plaintiff in the other, but not for herself. Benge's Admr. v. Fouts: Same v. Creech, 163 Ky. 796, 174 S. W. 510.

# § 312. (1) Judgment of chancellor.

As to the weight and effect of the judgment of the chancellor in a common law action, see note 1 to sec. 10.

(2) Laws and facts submitted to court, admission of incompetent evidence not reversible error. Andrews v. Hayden, 88.

# § 313-314] Trial in General. [Title IX

# § 313 [848] [Trial of facts by court—submission of issue to jury.]

All other issues of fact shall be tried by the court, subject to its power to order any issue to be tried by a jury. (Practice in trials by court, sec. 346. As to transferring actions from equity to ordinary, and vice versa, and submitting issues to jury, see secs. 8 to 16.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 98, 102, 396d.

# § 314 [344] [Order in which actions shall be tried.]

The trial of each action shall be according to the order in which it stands upon the docket, unless for good reason appearing the court shall order otherwise.

(4) Practice. Assessment of damages by the court. See notes to sec. 126; and see notes to secs. 11, 12, as to right to trial by jury.

# § 313.

(1) Trial by court-when the law and facts are submitted in an ordinary action to the court the decision of the court is entitled to the same weight as the verdict of a properly instructed jury. See cases cited in note 1, sec. 10.

A motion and grounds for a new trial are as necessary as if case was tried by a jury and either party is entitled to demand that the judge shall state separately his findings of fact and conclusions of law as provided in sec. 332. See sec. 332 and notes.

(2) Verdict on issue out of chancery. The court may disregard the verdict of a jury on an issue out of chancery. Sellers v. Sellers, 162 Ky. 9, 171 S. W. 449.

# § 314.

(1) Continuance on ground other than absence of evidence. An action was continued because the defendant was unable by reason of disease to attend the trial or to give his deposition. It was not error to refuse a continuance asked for the same reason at the next term. Rose v. Monarch, 150 Ky. 129, 150 S. W. 56.

(2) Defendant asked a continuance because of the absence of an attorney who was not an attorney of record in the action; defendant was himself an attorney and was represented by competent attorneys. The continuance was

Title [X] Trial in General.

# § 315 [845] [Postponement of trial—affidavit for and effect of.]

A motion to postpone a trial on account of the absence of evidence can be made only upon affidavit showing the materiality of the evidence expected to be obtained, and that due diligence has been used to obtain it; and, if it be for an absent witness, the affidavit must show what facts the affiant believes the witness will prove, and not merely the effect of such facts in evidence; and that the affiant believes them to be true. If, thereupon, the adverse party will consent that, on the trial, the affidavit shall be read as the deposition of the absent witness, the trial shall not be postponed on account of his absence. (When witness liable for cost of continuance, sec. 536. How personal attendance of witness secured, sec. 556. See Ky. Stat., secs. 887, 889, 1734.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 469.

Attorneys, defendants had no right to assume, merely because the trial court discharged the common law jury for the term, that there would be no further trials during that term. As there were to be no further trials until the following Thursday, it was not only the right but the duty of the court to discharge the jury and thus save the Commonwealth the unnecessary expense of its attendance." Gahren v. Parkersburg Nat. Bank, 157 Ky. 266, 162 S. W. 1135.

# 315. (1) Affidavit for continuance.

The refusal to grant a continuance is not an abuse of discretion when the statement of what the absent witness will prove is admitted as evidence. Hutton v. First Nat. Bank, 20 R. 225, 45 S. W. 668; M. & L. R. R. Co. v. Herrick, 13 Bush 122.

# (2)

Affidavit for continuance may be made by a person not a party to action if he is informed concerning and can state necessary grounds for continuance. Hardesty v. Com., 88 Ky. 537, 11 R. 43, 11 S. W. 589.

# (3)

A party is not entitled to a continuance on account of the absence of a witness who cannot be required to attend unless he shows that he has paid or tendered the witness fees, or that the witness has waived the payment. Thurman v. Virgin, 18 B. M. 785. See secs. 534, 536 of this Code as to attendance of witnesses; and Ky. Stat., sec. 1734, as to fees.

# (4)

The defendant was not entitled to a continuance on account of an absent witness by whom he could prove that a witness who would testify for plaintiff was not present when the assault complained of in the petition occurred, because he did not set out in his affidavit what the plaintiff expected to prove by his witness. Slater v. Sherman, 5 Bush 206.

# (5)

The application for a continuance is addressed to the sound discretion of the court, and unless this discretion has been abused the action of the court will not be disturbed, nor is the court bound to accept the statements in the affidavit as true, but may hear other affidavits contradicting it when the judge considers this proper under all the circumstances of the case; but when the sole ground for a continuance is the absence of material witnesses it seems that counter affidavits are not admissible, and that the continuance must be granted unless adverse party consents that affidavit may be read as a deposition. McClung v. Igleheart, 17 R. 913, 33 S. W. 80.

A party to a suit who is prevented from attending court by the serious illness of a near relative is entitled to a continuance. Langdon Creasy Co. v. Rouse, 24 R. 2095, 72 S. W. 1113.

# (6a)

Certain grounds for a continuance were insufficient. May v. May, 150 Ky. 522, 150 S. W. 685.

# (6b)

An application for a continuance was accompanied by the affidavit of counsel that the just and proper effect of the evidence of the absent witnesses could not in a reasonable degree be had without a personal examination in court. The circuit court refused a continuance. Upon the whole of the record it was held that the substantial rights of the party asking the continuance were prejudiced. Mad., H. & E. R. Co. v. Allen, 152 Ky. 706, 154 S. W. 5.

# (6c)

A continuance was asked on ac-
