Life Ins. Co. of Am. v. Williamson*, 152 Ky. 818, 154 S. W. 409.

(6d) An affidavit for a continuance was admitted to be read as a deposition for defendant; plaintiff's counsel referred to it in the argument as the affidavit of opposing counsel. This was improper and the court should have instructed the jury verbally that the affidavit should be treated as the deposition of the absent witnesses and would have the same force and effect as if they personally made the statements contained in it. *Mad., H. & E. R. Co. v. Allen*, 152 Ky. 706, 154 S. W. 5.

(6e) A circuit court's refusal to grant a continuance on account of the absence of witnesses was not error. *C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Stapleton*, 154 Ky. 351, 157 S. W. 702.

(6f) Due diligence was shown in this case; it was not necessary that the address of the witness be stated in the subpoena. *Louisville Ry. Co. v. Vessels Admx.*, 159 Ky. 664, 167 S. W. 924.

(6g) It is not error to refuse a continuance on affidavit filed if the judge gives the party applying for the continuance an opportunity to procure the attendance of the absent witness and the party declines to take advantage of the offer. *Whitaker v. First Nat. Bank*, 163 Ky. 623, 174 S. W. 47.

(6h) An affidavit which failed to show that a subpoena was issued for the absent witness and placed in the hands of the sheriff was insufficient to sustain a motion for a continuance. *North River Ins. Co. v. Dyehe*, 163 Ky. 271, 173 S. W. 784.

(6i) An affidavit as to what an absent witness would prove was admitted as evidence. It was not error to refuse a continuance. *Bon Jellico Coal Co. v. Murphy*, 161 Ky. 450, 171 S. W. 160.

(6j) It was not error to refuse to permit a corporation to read as a deposition an affidavit as to what its general agent would testify in the absence of diligence on the part of the company to procure the presence of such witness. *Masonic Life Assn. v. Robinson*, 156 Ky. 371, 160 S. W. 1078.

(6k) It is not an abuse of discretion to deny a continuance because of the absence of a witness, where the party seeking to produce him makes affidavit as to the facts he will testify to, which affidavit is read to the jury as the witness's testimony. *Bon Jellico Coal Co. v. Murphy*, 161 Ky. 450, 171 S. W. 160.

(6l) Under this section and sec. 556, the postponement of a trial because of the absence of a witness is within the sound discretion of the trial court. *Crane v. Hall*, 165 Ky 827, 178 S. W. 1096.

(6m) A request for postponement of the trial in order to procure the personal attendance of a witness was properly denied, where an affidavit as to what the witness would testify to was read as the witness' deposition. *Crane v. Hall*, 165 Ky. 827, 178 S. W. 1096.

(6n) Refusal to grant a continuance because of the absence of witnesses was held not error in the absence of the required showing by affidavit. *Bolar v. Browning*, 168 Ky. 273, 181 S. W. 1109.

(7) Affidavit-amendment of, is not permitted after court has expressed an opinion, so the court held in *Singleton v. Carr, 1 Bibb 554; Smalley v. Anderson, 4 Mon. 367; but see Comn. v. Hourigan*, 89 Ky. 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550; where it is held that in criminal cases, after a motion for a continuance is overruled, court may allow amended affidavit to be filed and motion renewed.

(8) Amendment of pleading. Grounds of continuance, see sec. 136; and see *Watts v. McKenney, 1 Mar. 561; Ewing v. Beauchamp, 4 Bibb 496; Eldridge v. Duncan*, 1 B. M. 101, holding that an amendment entitling party to continuance must be material, and not a mere verbal one.

(8a) Counter affidavit. The court does not decide whether a counter affidavit is permissible to be read in opposing an application for a continuance. *Ford v. House-Hasson Hard. Co.; Same v. Jellico Gro. Co.*, 159 Ky. 669, 169 S. W. 879.

(8b) Deposition of adverse party, failure to get. The fact that the defendant did not succeed in getting the deposition of the plaintiff as if under cross-examination prior to the trial, is not of itself grounds for a continuance. *Cumb. Tele. & Tele. Co. v. Laird*, 161 Ky. 800, 171 S. W. 386.

(8c) Inability of defendant to obtain the testimony of plaintiff by deposition as if under cross-examination before the trial was not ground for continuance, where he was not taken by surprise, and there was nothing in the testimony which he was not in every way ready to meet. *Cumb. Tel. & Tel. Co. v. Laird*, 161 Ky. 800, 171 S. W. 386.

(Sub-sec. 8.) Home Ins. Co. of N. Y. v. Crowder, 164 Ky. 792, 176 S. W. 344.

(8e) The refusal of a defendant to give his deposition under Code, sec. 606, sub-sec. 8, is not ground for reversal in the absence of a motion for a continuance for the purpose of taking the deposition. Christian's Admx. v. Ennis, 173 Ky. 101, 190 S. W. 675.

# SUBDIVISION 1. FORMATION OF JURY.

# § 316. Formation of jury.

§ 316 [346] [Formation of jury.] The general mode of summoning, impaneling, challenging and swearing the jury is not changed by this Code (For provisions concerning, see Ky. Stat., sec. 2264.)

# JURIES.

# SUMMONING, IMPANELING, CHALLENGING AND SWEARING OF.

[See Chapter 74, Kentucky Statutes.]

# Selection of Jury.

- § 2252. Twelve persons constitute in circuit court—six in inferior courts.
- A petit jury in the circuit court shall consist of twelve persons, and in all trials held in courts inferior to the circuit court, or by any county, police, or city judge, or justice of the peace, a jury shall consist of six persons; but the parties to any action or prosecution, except for felony, may agree to a trial by a less number of persons than is provided for in this section. (Ky. Stat. sec. 2252.)

§ 2264. Panel to be called—how deficiency supplied.

Title IX] Trial by Jury.

# § 316

such as attend, and are not excused by the court, shall be entered of record; and any deficiency in the panel shall be supplied as directed in this chapter. (Id., sec. 2264.)

# $265. Names to be placed in box and jury drawn from.

The clerk shall write the names of the jurors entered of record on separate slips of paper, as near the same size and appearance as may be, and when a jury is wanted for the trial of a case the same shall be drawn from a box after the papers shall have been deposited therein and well mixed. The clerk shall provide and keep for that purpose a suitable box with a sliding lid. (Id., sec. 2265.)

# $ 2267. Civil cases-hor jury selected-deficiency supplied.

In all civil cases of jury trial the clerk shall draw from the box the names of eighteen of the jury, and write them, as drawn, on two slips of paper, and deliver one to each party, from which plaintiff and defendant may each strike three and return the list to the clerk, who shall call the first twelve names not erased and swear them as a jury to try the case. But before either party shall be required to strike, those on the list may be challenged for cause, and others drawn and placed on the list in the place of as many as may be set aside for cause. In all cases where the jury shall be completed, the names of persons not on the jury shall be returned to the box, from which another jury may be drawn, and whenever, from any cause, it may be necessary to make up a jury, if there be not enough of the standing jury remaining or in attendance, the court shall order for the occasion a sufficient number to be summoned, as directed in this chapter, to make up a panel as aforesaid. If a standing juror, for any cause, shall be discharged or excused from the jury, his place shall be supplied as directed in this chapter. (Id., sec. 2267.)

# § 2258. Challenges-number of peremptory—for cause.

Each party litigant in civil actions shall have the right of peremptory challenge to three jurors, and the right to challenge as now allowed by law. (See Criminal Code, secs. 197-216, for challenges in criminal cases.)

# 2259. Oath to jury.

The following, in substance, shall be the oath to be administered to jurors in a civil suit: "You do swear that you will well and truly try the issue joined between A B, plaintiff, and C D, defendant (or if there be no issue, 'try the case'), and a true verdict give, according to evidence, unless dismissed by the court or withdrawn by the parties."

# $2254. Juries de medictate linguae.

Juries de medictate linguae may be directed by the court.

# § 2255. Juror may testify.

Jurors knowing any fact material to the issue shall disclose the same in open court, upon oath, as evidence.

# § 2268. Three-fourths of jury may make verdict.

That in all trials of civil actions in the circuit courts, three-fourths or more of the jurors concurring may return a verdict, which shall have the same force and effect as if rendered by the entire panel. But where a verdict is rendered by a less number than the whole jury, it shall be signed by all the jurors who agree to it. (Id., sec. 2268.)

# § 316. (1 Juror-practice-waiver.

Objection that one of the jury who signed the verdict was not sworn as a juror comes too late unless made at close of trial or as soon as fact is discovered, and so when a juror is known by a party to be hostile to him he should move to dis- charge the jury or the objection will be waived. Bickel v. Kraus, 100 Ky. 728, 18 R. 1054, 39 S. W. 414.

(2) See notes to sec. 340 as to irregularity or misconduct of jury.

(3) Court may excuse on its own motion a juror for cause. Shellman v. 245

# SUBDIVISION 2.

# CONDUCT OF THE TRIAL.

# § 317 [847] [Order of proceeding in trial.]

When the jury has been sworn, the trial shall proceed in the following order, unless the court, for special reasons, otherwise direct:

1. [Statement of case for plaintiff.] The plaintiff must briefly state his claim and the evidence by which he expects to sustain it.
2. [Statement for defense.] The defendant must then briefly state his defense and the evidence he expects to offer in support of it.
3. [Introduction of evidence.] The party on whom rests the burden of proof, in the whole action, must first produce his evidence; the adverse party will then produce his evidence. (Who has burden of proof, sec. 526; see further as to the order of proof, sec. 592.)

Louisville R. Co., 147 Ky. 526, 144 S. W. 1060. the hearing of testimony. This was not a substantial error. Ada Coal Co. v. Linville, 152 Ky. 2, 153 S. W. 21.

(4) Presumption that jury was sworn. Where a reversal was urged on the ground that the order impaneling the jury does not show that the jury was sworn, held, although the record does not expressly show that the jury was sworn, yet as there was nothing in the record to show that they were not, the court will assume that they were. Sword v. Bowen, 157 Ky. 615, 163 S. W. 733.

(2) It is improper argument for an attorney to go outside of the evidence and the law of the case, and when it appears that the improper argument may have influenced the jury the court will reverse for this cause, but the argument must be objected to when it is made; if not the error will be waived. L. & N. R. R. v. Earl, 94 Ky. 368, 15 R. 184, 22 S. W. 607; but to prohibit counsel from arguing case is error. Be- hume v. Caldwell, 2 Bibb 76; Wilken v. Exterkamp, 102 Ky. 142; 19 R. 1132, 42 S. W. 1140. L. H. & St. Louis R. R. v. Morgan, 110 Ky. 740, 23 R. 121, 62 S. W. 736; I. C. R. R. v. Whittaker. 22 R. 395. 57 S. W. 465; in Warren v. Nash, 24 R. 470, 68 S. W. 658, the court holds that error in respect to permitting

# § 317

# 4. Evidence in rebuttal.

The parties will then be confined to rebutting evidence, unless the court, for good reasons in furtherance of justice, permit them to offer evidence in chief.

# 5. Instructions—kinds of verdict.

When the evidence is concluded, but before the argument to the jury, either party may require the court to direct the jury to find a separate-general verdict with the general verdict or to find a special verdict. If a special verdict be so required, the questions of law may be reserved by the court until after verdict, but if a general verdict be required, either party may ask written instructions to the jury on points of law, which shall be given or refused by the court before the commencement of the argument to the jury.

# 6. Order of argument.

The parties may then submit or argue the case to the jury. In the argument, the party having the burthen of proof shall have the conclusion and the adverse party the opening. If there be more than one speech on either side, or if several defendants having separate defenses appear by different counsel, the court shall arrange the relative order of argument. (Burden of proof, sec. 526; order of proof, sec. 592.)

*Words in italics in brackets were stricken out by the Act of May 15, 1886. c. 1170, p. 120, reading as follows:

§ 1. That so much of chapter 2, title 9, of the Civil Code of Practice as authorizes and provides for a separate-general or special verdict be, and the same is hereby, repealed: Provided, That the court in its discretion may direct the jury to find a separate-general verdict.

Improper argument may be shown by affidavits filed with motion for new trial that are made part of the record.

# (3) Pleadings cannot be read to the jury or commented on in argument unless introduced as evidence.

L. & N. R. R. v. Hull, 113 Ky. 561, 24 R. 375, 68 S. W. 433.

See further notes to sec. 227, Criminal Code, as to improper argument.

# (5) Burden of proof-party having is entitled to conclusion of argument.

O'Connor v. Henderson Bridge Co., 95 Ky. 633, 16 R. 244, 27 S. W. 251, 983; Tarvin v. Timberlake, 18 R. 807, 38 S. W. 491; and denial of this right is reversible error.

# (5a) An erroneous ruling of the circuit court awarding the burden of proof to the wrong party was not in this case a prejudicial error.

Home Ins. Co. of N. Y. v. Crowder, 104 Ky. 792, 176 S. W. 344.

# (5b) Under a traversed plea of payment, the defendant had the burden of proof.

Fornash v. Antrobus, 178 Ky. 621, 199 S. W. 781.

# (5c) Where it is admitted that the applicant for life insurance was accepted, the premium paid, and the policy issued and delivered to the applicant, and that his death took place while the policy was in force, the burden is upon the insurance company to establish the defense that certain answers contained in the application were false and material.

Reserve Loan Life Ins. Co. v. Boreing, 157 Ky. 730, 163 S. W. 1085.

# (6) As to which party has burden of proof, see notes to sec. 526.

# INSTRUCTIONS.

# (7) Abstract principles of law.

It is error to give instructions which contain mere abstract principles of law; as to say to the jury "that where there is a conflict in the testimony of witnesses, the one side being of an affirmative and the other of a negative character, the affirmative character of testimony is preferred and is entitled to the greater weight by the jury in making up their verdict."

Lou. Chem. Works v. Com., 8 Bush 179.

# (7a) An instruction should not be

# Trial by Jury-Conduct Of

given unless there is evidence on which to base it. McCoy v. Com., 149 Ky. 447, 149 S. W. 903; Worley v. MeCurd, 153 Ky. 104, 154 S. W. 909.

# (7)

An instruction should not be given on an issue not presented by the pleadings. Forrest v. Winter, 149 Ky. 759, 149 S. W. 981.

# (7b)

Neither party is entitled to have the jury instructed on a subject not put in issue by the pleadings nor upon an issue unsupported by evidence. Edge v. Ott, 151 Ky. 672, 152 S. W. 764.

# (7c)

It was not error to refuse an instruction as to plaintiff's aggravation of damages by her own negligence in the absence of a pleading presenting that issue. Cumberland R. R. Co. v. Baird, 156 Ky. 255, 160 S. W. 919.

# (7d)

Instructions should be predicated upon the issues made by the pleadings and the facts appertaining to such issues, furnished by competent evidence introduced on the trial; and in writing them brevity and clearness of expression should be used by the court, in order that their meaning may be readily understood by the jury. The refusal by the trial court of an instruction predicated upon an issue not made by the pleadings is not error. Moore v. Damron, 157 Ky. 799, 164 S. W. 103.

# (7e)

The trial court should not instruct upon an issue not made by the pleadings. Turner & Frazer v. Frazier, 157 Ky. 388, 163 S. W. 245.

# (7f)

A jury after a case had been submitted came into court and asked for further instructions. The court gave an instruction on the point inquired of, which was correct. This was not ground for reversal. L. & E. Ry. Co. v. Boatwright, 164 Ky. 374, 175 S. W. 648.

# (7g)

It is improper to give an instruction announcing a naked legal proposition, however correct it may be, unless it bears upon and is connected with the issues involved; and, unless, further, there has been received some competent evidence to which the jury may apply it. Bowlin v. Archer, 157 Ky. 540, 163 S. W. 477.

# (7h)

Where an arresting officer was sued for conspiring to bring about the killing of his prisoner, the petition relying solely upon the alleged conspiracy, it was error for the trial court to instruct the jury to find for the plaintiff, if they believed from the evidence that the officer had failed to protect his prisoner from assault. Bowlin v. Archer, 157 Ky. 540, 163 S. W. 477.

# (7i)

Adverse possession. For a possession to ripen into a title by time it must be actual open, notorious, continuous.

Trial by Jury-Conduct Of

court may instruct the jury that certain material allegations of the pleadings which are not denied are true; but the allegations thus admitted should be pointed out in the instruction. Hartley v. Hartley, 3 Met. 57; Becker v. Crow, 7 Bush 198; Tipton v. Triplett, 1 Met. 570; Allard v. Smith, 2 Met. 297.

# (10) Burden of proof.

It is error to instruct the jury as to which party the burden of proof is on or to use the expression, "preponderance of evidence." Ragsdale v. Ezell, 99 Ky. 236, 18 R. 146, 35 S. W. 629, 1130.

# (11) Compensatory damages.

See L. & N. R. R. v. Clark, 105 Ky. 571, 20 R. 1375, 49 S. W. 323; Canton C. & H. T. P. Co. v. McIntire, 105 Ky. 185, 20 R. 1107, 48 S. W. 980; L.& N. R. R. v. Taaffee, 106 Ky. 535, 21 R. 64, 50 S. W. 850; L. & N. R. R. v. Hall, 115 Ky. 567, 24 R. 2487, 74 S. W. 280.

# (11a) In an action for personal injuries

where the pleadings claim compensation for loss of time and for impairment of the power to earn money, it is proper to instruct the jury that the allowance for the impairment of power to earn money should begin when the allowance for loss of time ends. McHenry Coal Co. v. Taylor, 165 Ky. 144, 176 S. W. 976.

# (12) Contributory negligence

—see L. & N. R. R. v. Tow, 23 R. 408, 63 S. W. 27; Canton T. P. Co. v. McIntire, 105 Ky. 185, 20 R. 1107, 48 S. W. 980; Bevis v. Vanceburg Tel. Co., 113 S. W. 811.

# (12a)

If an injury to the plaintiff would not have been sustained without the plaintiff's own negligence, he has no cause of action for such injury. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Cooper, 164 Ky. 489, 175 S. W. 1034.

# (12b)

Certain instructions as to contributory negligence were held sufficiently concrete. N.. C. & St. L. Ry. Co. v. Banks, 156 Ky. 609, 161 S. W. 554.

# (12c)

A refusal of certain instructions offered on contributory negligence was not error. Bon Jellico Coal Co. v. Murphy, 161 Ky. 450, 171 S. W. 160.

# (13) Credibility of witnesses.

It is error to give an instruction that jury are judges of the credibility of witnesses and of the weight to be attached to their evidence; but the court will not reverse on this ground if such an instruction was not prejudicial. Transatlantic Ins. Co. v. Bamberger, 11 R. 101, 11 S. W. 595; Peoples v. Com., 87 Ky. 487; 10 R. 517, 9 S. W. 509, 810; Barnard v. Com., 10 R. 143; Barnett v. Com., 84 Ky. 449, 8 R. 448, 1 S. W. 722; Anderson Distilling Co. v. Hair, 103 Ky. 196, 19 R: 1822, 44 S. W. 658.

# (14)

It is error to instruct the jury "that if they believe any witness has sworn falsely and knowingly to any material fact, they are bound, or have the right, to disregard all his testimony." The jury are the exclusive judges of the testimony. Letton v. Young, 2 Met. 558; Hall v. Renfroe, 3 Met. 52.

# (14a) Damages.

In an action for damages arising out of a collision, an instruction was held to be erroneous because it failed to furnish the jury a guide for determining the amount of damages. The court lays down rules to the measure of damages for injury to personal property and for personal injuries. Weil v. Hagan, 161 Ky. 292, 170 S. W. 618.

# (14b)

An instruction on the measure of damages in a suit for personal injury sustained by negligence, was approved by the Court of Appeals as not being prejudicial, although not aptly worded. Ky. & Tenn. Ry. Co. v. Hogue, 161 Ky. 439, 170 S. W. 1187.

# (14c)

In condemnation proceedings for a railroad right of way instructions as to damages were approved. West Ky. Coal Co. v. Dyer, 161 Ky. 407, 170 S. W. 967.

# (14d)

In an action for damages for breach of a contract, an instruction as to the measure of damages, which gave the jury no standard by which to fix the amount of damages was erroneous. Adams v. Hambrick, 161 Ky. 797, 171 S. W. 398.

# (14e)

In a certain action for injury to land, it appeared that the cause of damage was permanent, and could not be remedied at a reasonable expense. It was error not to instruct the jury to make its finding of damages once for all. W. & B. M. Ry. Co. v. Blanton, 160 Ky. 134, 169 S. W. 580.

# (14f)

In an action on a contract for the sale of an engine to operate a stave manufactory there was a breach of the contract arising from the failure to deliver the engine on board the cars at Cincinnati at the time fixed by the contract of sale. The instruction should have confined the recovery of damages by the purchaser, to such as were sustained by it, resulting from the delay of the seller in delivering the engine beyond the day fixed by the contract of sale for its delivery. Fairbanks, M. & Co. v. Carson-Muse L. Co., 160 Ky. 346, 169 S. W. 731.

# (14g)

An instruction which permits the jury to fix damages, without giving

# Trial by Jury—Conduct Of.

(15e) Unless an instruction is requested in writing on a particular issue it is not error for the court to fail to instruct the jury upon that issue. *Branham's Admr. v. Buckley*, 158 Ky. 848, 166 S. W. 618.

(15f) A party can not complain of the failure of the court to give an instruction which was not asked. *Wood v. Rigg*, 152 Ky. 242, 153 S. W. 214.

(15g) A party can not avail himself of error in an instruction which is to the same effect as one asked by himself. *l. & N. R. Co. v. WooDford*, 152 Ky. 398, 153 S. W. 722.

(15h) It is not error to refuse an instruction when it is in substance embraced in an instruction already given. *L. & N. R. Co. v. Kemp's Admr.*, 149 Ky. 344, 149 S. W. 835.

(15) Duty of court. In civil cases the court is only required to give such instructions as are offered by the parties, but if an instruction offered is defective in form or substance the court should prepare or direct the preparation of a proper instruction on this point, and if the court on its own motion undertakes to instruct the jury it is better to cover the entire case. *L. H. & St. L. R. Co. v. Roberts, 144 Ky. 820, 139 S. W. 1073; L. & N. R. R. v. Harrod, 115 Ky. 877, 25 R. 250, 75 S. W. 233; C. N. O. & T. R. R. v. Curd, 22 R. 1222, 60 S. W. 297; Swope v. Schafer, 9 R. 160, 4 S. W. 300; Nicola Bros. v. Hurst, 28 R. 87, 88 S. W. 1081; South Cov. Ry. Co. case*, 29 R. 836, 96 S. W. 562.

(15a) It was not error to refuse to give an instruction in this case which was merely the converse of an instruction that was given. *Glasgow E. L. & I. Co. v. Clark's Admx.*, 158 Ky. 734, 166 S. W. 214.

(15b) Under this section the court is not required to give any instructions in a civil case, unless written instructions are requested. *Charles Taylor Sons Co. v. Hunt*, 163 Ky. 120, 173 S. W. 333.

(15c) It is not the duty of the court in a civil case to give instructions to the jury unasked, but if a party offers an instruction upon any point of the case the court should give a correct instruction upon that point. *Chas. Taylor Sons Co. v. Hunt*, 163 Ky. 120, 173 S. W. 353.

(15d) The refusal of the circuit court to give a proper instruction which was offered was not prejudicial, as the matter was fully covered in another instruction given which was correct. *Reasor v. Paducah & I. Ferry Co.*, 164 Ky. 411, 175 S. W. 632.

(15i) Where the trial court attempts to instruct the jury upon an issue involved, but if an instruction given does not correctly state the law, the error therein, if prejudicial to the substantial rights of the party complaining of it, and if such instruction was objected to by him when given, will, on appeal, authorize a reversal, although the latter may not have offered or asked an instruction in lieu of such erroneous one containing a correct statement of the law. *Chesapeake & Ohio Ry. Co. v. Dwyer's Admx.*, 157 Ky. 590, 163 S. W. 752.

(15j) An instruction on contributory negligence, objectionable in form, was cured by another instruction. *C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Troxel*, 150 Ky. 185, 150 S. W. 41.

(15k) Failure to give a certain correct instruction was not prejudicial error as all it contained was embraced in the instructions which were given. *Tyler v. First Nat. Bank of Winslow*, 150 Ky. 515, 150 S. W. 665.

(16) Et cetera. In *L. & N. R. R. v. Berry*, 96 Ky. 604, 16 R. 722, 29 S. W. 449, it was held error to use the expression "etc." in an instruction fixing the measure of damages.

(16a) How made part of record. Instructions not made part of the record by an order or a bill of exceptions will not be considered, although copied into the transcript by the clerk. *Payne's Gdn. v. Nicholson & Co.*, 155 Ky. 94, 159 S. W. 672.

(16b) Harmless error. The giving of an erroneous instruction which is not prejudicial to an appellant is not ground for reversal. *C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Case*, 158 Ky. 594, 165 S. W. 968.

# Title IX

# Trial by Jury-Conduct Of

# § 317

(16c) Indemnity. A judgment was rendered against a city for damages by reason of the act of a contractor in placing logs in a street. An action was afterward brought by the city against the adjacent property owner and the contractor for indemnity. The city had a cause of action against the contractor and none against the owner of the property. City of Georgetown v. Cantrill, 158 Ky. 378, 164 S. W. 929.

(16d) Influence, undue. Upon the evidence in this case, it was proper for the circuit court to give an instruction on undue influence. Holliday v. Holliday, 161 Ky. 500, 171 S. W. 156.

(17) Instructions must be in writing when so requested by either party. Traders' Bank v. Henry, 105 Ky. 707; 20 R. 1506, 49 S. W. 530; Ferguson v. Fox, 1 Met. 83; L. & N. R. R. v. Banks, 17 R. 1065, 33 S. W. 627; Webster v. Green, 22 R. 1456, 60 S. W. 714.

(17a) Land, cutting timber from. In an action to recover for timber cut from land, the jury should have been instructed that if the timber was cut from the land embraced within the boundaries of the patent sued on, they should find for plaintiff unless they believed that the land was covered by the older patent. Louisville Prop. Co. v. Lawson, 163 Ky. 633, 174 S. W. 29.

(18) Law to be decided by the court. The court should decide all questions of law, and only submit the facts to the jury. Maltus v. Shields, 2 Met. 553.

(18a) If a person injured by two negligent acts relies upon both acts, the jury may properly be instructed as to the law applicable under both states of fact. L. & N. R. Co. v. Gaines, 152 Ky. 255, 153 S. W. 216.

(18b) An instruction was rightfully granted on matter appearing in evidence that was admitted without objection, but which the pleading strictly construed did not authorize; an exception was saved to the instruction. The giving of the instruction was not error. Lex. & E. Ry. Co. v. Fields, 152 Ky. 19, 153 S. W. 43.

(18c) A judgment was reversed because of an erroneous instruction although the appellant did not ask a correct instruction on the same point. L. & E. Ry. Co. v. Crawford, 155 Ky. 723, 160 S. W. 267.

(18d) If in a common law action there is any evidence to support the plaintiff's claim, the issue should be submitted to the jury. Royal Neigh. of Amer. v. Laufman, 158 Ky. 358, 104 S. W. 966.

(18e) A pupil was expelled from a certain school for violating a rule of the school against hazing. In an action by his father to recover tuition paid by him, it was the duty of the court to decide whether the rule was or was not reasonable; it was error to submit that question to the jury. Ky. Military Inst. v. Bramlet, 158 Ky. 205, 164 S. W. 808.

(18f) Libel. Where words libelous per se have been published punitive damages may be awarded, although there be no actual malice. Reid v. Sun Pub. Co., 158 Ky. 727, 166 S. W. 245.

(19) Malice defined - Ohio Valley Tel. Co. v. Meyer, 22 R. 36, 56 S. W. 673; Ahrens & Ott Mfg. Co. v. Hoeher, 106 Ky. 692, 21 R. 299, 51 S. W. 194.

(19a) Malicious prosecution. An instruction on the subject of malice in an action for damages for malicious prosecution was disapproved. The court states what instructions should have been given. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Cecil, 164 Ky. 377, 175 S. W. 654.

(19b) An instruction as to the meaning of the word "malice" in an action for malicious prosecution was disapproved. L. & N. R. R. Co. and Curt Jones v. Owens; Same v. Parsons, 164 Ky. 557, 175 S. W. 1039.

(19c) In an action for damages for malicious prosecution the court should tell the jury what facts constitute probable cause; damage to reputation need not be specially pleaded nor proved. Schwartz v. Boswell, 156 Ky. 103, 160 S. W. 748.

(19d) In an action for malicious prosecution the court should tell the jury what facts constitute probable cause, and let them determine whether these facts are proved. (Subsec. 5.) Keiner v. Collins, 161 Ky. 696, 171 S. W. 399.

(20) Negligence—gross neglect—ordinary care—ordinary negligence. Proper instructions defining. L. & N. R. R. v. McCoy, 81 Ky. 403, 5 R. 397; Needham v. L. & N. R. R., 85 Ky. 423, 9 R. 61, 3 S. W. 797; 11 S. W. 306; L. & N. R.R. v. Greer, 16 R. 667, 29 S. W. 337; C. & R. R. v. Lang, 100 Ky. 221, 19 R. 65, 38 S. W. 503, 40 S. W. 451, 41 S. W. 271; L. & N. R. R. v. Kelly, 100 Ky. 421, 19 R. 69, 38 S. W. 852; 40 S. W. 452.

(20a) In an action for personal injury, an instruction as to the care required of deceased for his own safety should be in the same terms as the instruction as to care required of the wrongdoer. L. & N. R. Co. v. Parks' Admr., 154 Ky. 269, 157 S. W. 27.

(20b) In an action for injuries the

Trial by Jury-Conduct Of

petition alleged gross negligence; upon the facts stated an instruction as to negligence, not gross, should have been given. L., H. & St. L. Ry. Co. v. Armes, 158 Ky. 676, 166 S. W. 190.

(20c) Where an alleged ground of negligence is not supported by any evidence, the court should not instruct the jury upon that issue; but an unnecessary instruction which is not prejudicial to any of the substantial rights of the complaining party will not authorize a reversal. McKee v. C., F. & S. R. R. Co., 161 Ky. 711, 171 S. W. 425.

(20d) Where upon the trial of a personal injury action the facts are admitted, or are shown by uncontradicted evidence, it is the duty of the court to declare to the jury whether they constitute negligence; and is error to leave to the jury to determine whether there has been negligence. Williams v. Louisville Ry. Co., 158 Ky. 50, 164 S. W. .-.

(20e) Nuisance. Where, by a temporary nuisance, damage is done to property occupied by plaintiff, the measure of damages to the property occupied by plaintiff is the diminution in the value of the use of the property during the continuance of the nuisance or injury. If the property be rented, it is the diminution in its rental value during the continuance of the nuisance or injury. Southern Ry. Co. v. Routh, 161 Ky. 196, 170 S. W. 520.

(21) Peremptory instructions. When there is evidence tending to establish a matter in issue, the court should not grant a peremptory instruction, although the judge should be of the opinion that if the jury found adversely to the request for such instruction, he would be compelled to grant a new trial. Buford v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 82 Ky. 286, 6 R. 263; Curran v. Stein, 110 Ky. 99, 22 R. 1575, 60 S. W. 839; Thompson v. Thompson, 17 B. M. 22; Payne Clothing Co. v. Payne, 21 R. 1226, 54 S. W. 709; 11 Bush 265; and after a motion for a peremptory instruction has been made the court may allow the plaintiff to introduce other evidence. Ballowe v. Hillman, 18 R. 677, 37 S. W. 950.

(22) When the court gives a peremptory instruction it is the duty of the jury to make a verdict in accordance with it. Curran v. Stein, 110 Ky. 99, 22 R. 1575, 60 S. W. 839.

(23) To authorize an instruction as in case of a non-suit, it should appear that, admitting the testimony to be true, and every inference that is fairly deducible from it, the plaintiff has still failed to support his claim. Shay v. R. & L. T. P. Co., 1 Bush 108.

(24) While it is usual for the defendant, in moving for a peremptory instruction, to do so upon the plaintiff's evidence alone, yet the court may, after all the evidence has been heard upon both sides, direct the jury to find for the defendant, if all the evidence is in his favor. Wilsey v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 83 Ky. 511, 7 R. 498.

(25) If the court overrules a motion for peremptory instructions at conclusion of plaintiff's testimony, and afterward becomes satisfied that this was error, it may, upon conclusion of all the evidence, set aside former ruling and instruct jury to find for defendant. Nance v. Newport News R. R., 13 R. 554, 17 S. W. 570; or may reserve decision on motion made at conclusion of plaintiff's evidence until all the evidence is in. Owens v. Meredith, 25 R. 1485, 78 S. W. 145.

(26) The rule is, "that a party may move upon the evidence of his antagonist, assuming that evidence and every fact which it conduces to prove as true, and ask the court for a peremptory instruction; but he cannot make such a motion on his own evidence alone, or on his evidence mingled with, or added to, that of the opposite party, unless his own evidence is composed of record, or uncontested unofficial documents or writings." United Shakers v. Underwood, 11 Bush 265.

(26a) It was not error to refuse to give a peremptory instruction in a case where there was some evidence authorizing the submission of the case to the jury. L. & I. Ry. Co. v. Roemmele, 157 Ky. 84, 162 S. W. 547.

(26b) On the trial of an appeal from an order probating a will the due execution of the will was proved; the burden of showing that undue influence was exercised was upon the contestants, and in the absence of some evidence showing that such influence was exercised in fact, a peremptory instruction was proper to find for the propounders. Crump v. Chenault, 154 Ky. 187, 156 S. W. 1053.

(26c) The action of the circuit court in giving a peremptory instruction will not be reviewed by the Court of Appeals in the absence of a motion for a new trial. Witt v. L. & E. Ry. Co., 158 Ky. 401, 165 S. W. 399.

(26d) Upon an appeal it was held that a certain verdict for a plaintiff was flagrantly against the evidence. On the next trial the evidence was not improved. It was proper for the circuit court to let

Title IX] Trial by Jury-Conduct Of. [§ 317

the ease go to the jury and afterward to set aside a verdict for the plaintiff as flagrantly against the evidence. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Johnson, 151 Ky. 809, 152 S. W. 962.

(26e) If a circuit court erroneously overrules a motion for a peremptory instruction at the close of plaintiff's evidence, and the defects in the evidence are supplied by defendant's testimony, it is not error to overrule a motion for a peremptory instruction at the end of all the evidence. L. & N. R. Co. v. Peck, 152 Ky. 6, 153 S. W. 30.

(26f) In an action for personal injury from negligence, a peremptory instruction is proper if there be a failure to show negligence. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Goode, 153 Ky. 247, 154 S. W. 941.

(26g) In these actions for the killing of horses by a railroad train there was evidence sufficient to take the case to the jury. The giving of peremptory instructions was error. Campbell v. Mobile & O. R. R. Co., 162 Ky. 58, 171 S. W. 1002.

(26h) If in reversing a judgment the Court of Appeals states that a peremptory instruction should have been given, the trial court should give a peremptory instruction unless the evidence is materially different from what it was on the former trial. L. & N. R. Co. v. Murphy, 150 Ky. 176, 150 S. W. 79.

(26i) To authorize a directed verdict for the defendant it must appear that, admitting the plaintiff's testimony to be true, and every inference fairly deducible therefrom, he has failed to support his cause of action. Helton's Admr. v. C. & O. Ry. Co., 157 Ky. 380, 163 S. W. 224.

(26j) Where the proof upon an issue of fact is contradictory, it is for the jury to decide the issue. Turner & F'razer v. Frazier, 157 Ky. 388, 163 S. W. 245.

(26k) In an action against a city to recover for the death of a horse alleged to have resulted from the negligent failure of the city to maintain its streets in a reasonable safe condition, the evidence failed to show what was the proximate cause of the death of the horse. A verdict for the defendant was rightfully directed. Russell v. City of Ashland, 159 Ky. 223, 166 S. W. 971.

(26l) On the trial of a contested will case a peremptory instruction directing a verdict for the propounders should not be given if there is any evidence tending to show a want of testamentary capacity on the part of the testator, or that the will was the result of undue influence exercised upon the testator by others. Bramel's Exr. v. Crain's Gdn., 157 Ky. 671, 163 S. W. 1125.

(26m) In an action by a servant against his master for injuries received from becoming overheated while at work it clearly appeared that the injuries were not caused by the negligence of the master. It was error to refuse a peremptory instruction for the defendant. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Williams, 165 Ky. 386, 176 S. W. 1186.

(27) Prominent facts. Instructions should not be given which give prominence to certain portions of the evidence, when the whole of it should have been left to be considered and weighed by the jury. Flood v. Pragoff, 79 Ky. 607, 3 R. 372; Stokes v. Shippen, 13 Bush 180.

(27a) In an action to recover damages for death occasioned by negligence, an instruction giving the measure of damages was erroneous, because it specifically called the jury's attention to certain facts. It was not sufficiently prejudicial of itself to justify a reversal. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Shoemake's Admr., 161 Ky. 746, 171 S. W. 383.

(27b) Railroad - spark-arrester. An instruction as to the kind of spark-arrester which a railroad company should have, was sustained. Ils. Cent. Ry. Co. v. Scheible, 162 Ky. 469, 172 S. W. 910.

(27c) Railroad-effect of announcement of station. The announcement of a station and the opening of the vestibule door is not an invitation to the passengers to alight before the train stops. Gayle's Admr. v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 163 Ky. 459, 173 S. W. 1113.

(27d) Railroad trainmen, duty to obey rules. An instruction imposing on trainmen the duty of using ordinary care to obey certain rules of the company was erroneous. The instruction should have imposed on them an imperative duty of obeying those rules. L. & I. Ry. Co. v. Kraft, 156 Ky. 66, 160 S. W. 803.

(28) Written instructions. Counsel can not complain of the action of the court in refusing an instruction unless it was offered in writing. Bell v. Louisville R. Co., 148 Ky. 189; 146 S. W. 383.

(28a) After a case was submitted to a jury the jury came into court for information as to a matter of law; the court gave the instruction, as to which they made inquiries orally. This was not ground for reversal. L. & E. Ry. Co. v. Boatright, 164 Ky. 374, 175 S. W. 648.

(29) Jury should obey instructions and if they do not it will be reversible.

# Trial by Jury-Conduct Of

error. Lynch v. Snead Iron Works, 116 S. W. 693, 132 Ky. 241.

# (30) On a retrial after a reversal

the instructions given on the former trial except so far as they have been criticised or condemned by the Court of Appeals should be given. Wall v. Deinint, 141 Ky. 515, 133 S. W. 768.

# (30a) If on an appeal to the Court of Appeals

in the opinion certain instructions given below are not criticised, they are thereby tacitly approved and become the law of the case; but the court is not precluded from giving on a return of the case a proper instruction on another subject which was not covered by an instruction given on the other trial. Standard Oil Co. v. Marlow, 162 Ky. 1, 171 S. W. 436.

# (31) Uncontradicted issue.

Where a material issue is admitted the court may instruct the jury on it as a matter of law. Central Consumers Co. v. Booker, 107 S. W. 198, 32 R. 795.

# (32) Personal injury cases.

Louisville Gas Co. v. Fuller, 92 S. W. 566, 29 R. 131, 122 Ky. 614.

# (32a) A certain instruction allowed a recovery for mental pain, or nervous, or physical suffering.

The words "or nervous" should have been omitted. Southern Ry. Co. in Ky. v. Owen, 156 Ky. 827, 162 S. W. 110.

# (32b) A certain instruction as to the measure of damages

in a case of personal injury arising from negligence was criticised and approved. Ills. Cent. Ry. Co. v. Williams, 163 Ky. 831, 174 S. W. 741.

# (32c) Certain instructions given in an action against a railroad company

for injuries to one riding in an automobile which was struck by a train were held not erroneous. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Nethery, 160 Ky. 369, 169 S. W. 883.

# (32d) In a suit to recover for personal injury

the financial circumstance of either party is not a matter for consideration. Andonique v. Carmen, 162 Ky. 154, 172 S. W. 112.

# (33) Waiver of right to object.

A party cannot object to an instruction if he has asked a similar one. I. & N. R. Co. v. Wilson, 148 Ky. 250, 146 S. W. 422.

# (33a) Will-execution of.

An instruction that the jury should find a paper to be the will of the deceased unless when it was "signed" by the testator he was of unsound mind, or under undue influence, was not prejudicial, although the word "executed" would have been better than the word "signed." Gernert v. Straeffer's Exr., 162 Ky. 605, 172 S. W. 1044.

# (34) Punitive and compensatory damages.

When instruction on punitive damages is given jury should be told to find separately the amount allowed for compensation and exemplary damages. L. & N. R. Co. v. Scott, 141 Ky. 538, 133 S. W. 800.

# (34a) An instruction erroneously authorized the jury

to find punitive damages. It was plain from the amount of damages awarded that the damages were merely compensatory. The judgment was affirmed. St. Bernard Mining Co. v. Ashby, 164 Ky. 416, 175 S. W. 626.

# (35) Punitive damages.

Instruction authorizing should not be given unless the evidence shows a wanton and reckless disregard of the rights of the complaining party. L. & N. R. Co. v. Wilkins, 143 Ky. 572, 136 S. W. 1023.

# STATEMENT OF CASE.

(36) The purpose of a statement is to enable each party to present to the jury the facts expected to be proven, and it is not proper to discuss the law applicable to the case or to read from law books. Hughes v. General Electric Co., 107 Ky. 485. 21 R. 1202, 54 S. W. 723.

(37) It is optional with counsel to make a statement or not but the court may require a statement. Cincinnati R. Co. v. Evans, 110 S. W. 844, 129 Ky. 152.

(38) In will contest statement should first be made by propounders. Rust v. Rust, 84 S. W. 1152, 27 R. 275.

(39) Evidence in rebuttal.

Discretion of court in allowing evidence in rebuttal will not be disturbed unless abused. Rogers v. Flick, 144 Ky. 844, 139 S. W. 1098.

(40) The fact that the court permitted parties to introduce evidence in rebuttal which should have been introduced in chief, will not be ground for reversal, in the absence of abuse of discretion, where the ruling was manifestly in the interest of justice. Jonas v. South Cov. & C. St. Ry. Co., 169 Ky. 807, 185 S. W. 117.

(41) Rebuttal evidence is not necessarily confined to proving or disproving facts testified to by the witnesses on the other side: it is none the less rebuttal evidence if it tends to overcome the.

# Title IX] Trial by Jury-Conduct Of

# § 318 [84s] [View of property or place by jury.]

Whenever, in the opinion of the court, it is proper for the jury to have a view of real property which is the subject of litigation, or of the place in which any material fact occurred, it may order them to be conducted in a body, under the charge of an officer, to the place, which shall be shown to them by some person appointed by the court for that purpose. While the jury are thus absent no person, other than the person so appointed, shall speak to them on any subject connected with the trial.

# § 319 [849] [Duty of jury and officer after submission-separation of jury.]

When the case is finally submitted to the jury, they may decide in court or retire for deliberation. If they retire, they must be kept together in some convenient place, under the charge of an officer, until they agree upon a verdict or are discharged by the court-subject to the discretion of the court to permit them to separate temporarily at night and at their meals. The officer having them under his charge shall not suffer any communication be made to them, nor make any himself, except to ask them if they have agreed upon their verdict, unless by order of the court; and he shall not, before their verdict is rendered, communicate to any person the state of their deliberations, or the verdict agreed upon.

# § 320 [350] [Admonition by court to jury.]

If the jury be permitted to separate, either during the trial or after the case is submitted to them, legal effect of the evidence for the other side. Reserve Loan Life Ins. Co. v. Bore- 646, 58 S. W. 502; Todd v. Cooke, 23 R. 1528, 64 S. W. 908; Cohankus Mfg. Co. v. Rogers, 96 S. W. 437, 29 R. 748; City of Louisville v. Caron, 90 S. W. 604, 28 R. 844; Crane v. Congleton, 116 S. W. 341.

318. (1) View of premises. Court may permit jury to view place where an accident occurred, even after they have retired to consider their verdict, when the jury so request. I., N. A. & C. R. R. v. Schick. 94 Kv. J91, 14 R. 833. 21 S. W. 1036; and see Ky. C. R. R. v. Snith, 93 Kv. 449, 14 R. 455, 20 S. W. 392; where it is held that where jury was sent to view premises at close of testimony it was not error to refuse to permit them to go at a particular time during trial.

(2) In M. & B. S. R. R. v. Trustees, 18 R. 1i11, 39 S. W. 35. it was held not reversible that jury viewed premises after dark, it appearing that they could see well with the aid of lanterns furnished.

(3) Whether or not the jury may view the premises is a matter largely in the discretion of the trial court. H. & C. Gravel R. v. Cosby, 103 Ky. 182, 19 R. 1851, 44 S. W. 639; and its action will not be disturbed unless the discretion has been abused. Central Asylum v. Hauns, 21 R. 22, 50 S. W. 978; Green v. M. & B. S. R. R., 25 R. 1623, 78 S. W. 439; Valley T. P. Road v. Lyons, 22 R. 255.

§ 319. The code does not require that the jury be kept together at any time before the final submission. Smith's

# Trial by Jury--Conduct Of.

they must be admonished by the court that it is their duty not to converse with, nor suffer themselves to be addressed by, any other person on any subject of the trial; and that, during the trial, it is their duty not to form or express an opinion thereon, until the cause is finally submitted to them.

# § 321 [851] [Information as to law or evidence after submission——how given.]

After the jury have retired for deliberation, if there be a disagreement between them as to any part of the testimony, or if they desire to be informed as to any point of law arising in the case, they may request the officer to conduct them into court, where the information required shall be given in the presence of, or after notice to, the parties or their counsel.

# § 322 [852] [Discharge of jury-causes for.]

The jury may be discharged by the court on account of the sickness of a juror, or other accident or calamity requiring their discharge; or, by consent of both parties, or, after they have been kept together until it satisfactorily appears that there is no probability of their agreeing.

# § 323 [358] [Retrial of cases in which jury discharged.]

Cases in which the jury are discharged without making a verdict shall be tried again at such time as the court may direct.

# § 324 [354] [Verdict—manner of rendering—poll of jury.]

When the jury have agreed upon their verdict, their names shall be called by the clerk and the verdict be rendered by their foreman. When the verdict is announced, either party may require the jury to be polled—which is done by the clerk or court asking each juror if it is his verdict. [If any one answers in the negative, the jury must again be sent out for further deliberation.*]

Admx. v. Middlesboro E. Co., 164 Ky. 46, 174 S. W. 773.

# § 321. (1) Jury-how informed as to law or evidence after retiring.

After the jury have been instructed and have retired for consultation, any information, written or oral, sent to them by the judge is a violation of the Code. Goode v. Campbell, 14 Bush 75.

# (2)

A jury may be allowed after retiring to return into court to inquire of a witness what he had testified to, or said, in any part of his testimony, and cross-examination would not be allowed. In rare cases a jury might after retirement be permitted to re-examine a witness respecting a fact not before testified to, where the court is satisfied that probable injustice would otherwise be done, but in such case cross-examination would be proper. Thompson v. Poston, 1 Duv. 389.

After the jury had retired, a witness who had testified was sent for by them, admitted into the jury room and re-examined without the knowledge or consent of the court or parties; held sufficient grounds for a new trial. Luttrell v. M. & L. R. R. Co., 18 B. M. 291.

# (4)

The jury may by permission of the court take to their room or examine a model used as evidence after they have retired to deliberate. L. & N. R. R. v. Berry, 96 Ky. 604, 16 R. 722, 29 S. W. 449; but in L. H. & St. Louis R. R. v. Morgan, 110 Ky. 740, 23 R. 121, 62 S. W. 736, the court held it to be reversible error to permit jury to take a deposition to their room over objection of adverse party, ruling that this section does not permit jury to take any written evidence over objection except writings that are foundation of action.

# (5)

In Milward Co. v. Luigart, 19 R. 701, 41 S. W. 568, it was held error to refuse to instruct the jury after they.

Title IX] Trial by Jury-Conduct Of. [§ 325

# § 325 [855] [Verdict to be written and signed—clerk to read.]

The verdict shall be written, signed by the foreman, and read by the clerk to the jury, and the inquiry made, whether or not it is their verdict. If *any* juror disagree, the jury must be sent out for further deliberation; but if no disagreement be expressed, and neither party require the jury to be polled, the verdict is complete and the jury discharged from the case. (Provisions when three-fourths of jury render, sec. 2268 Ky. Stat.)

*So much of sections 324 and 325 as requires a unanimous verdict in civil actions in circuit courts is repealed by section 248 of the Constitution as carried into effect by section 2268 of the Kentucky Statutes:*

# § 2268. Three-fourths of jury may make verdict.

That in all trials of civil actions in the circuit courts, three-fourths or more of the jurors concurring may return a verdict, which shall have the same force and effect as if rendered by the entire panel. But where a verdict is rendered by a less number than the whole jury, it shall be signed by all the jurors who agree to it. (Ky. Stat., sec. 2668; see Constitution, sec. 248.)

# (6) The jury are entitled to take the pleadings.

C. & O. R. R. v. Dupee, 23 R. 2349, 67 S. W. 15.

# §325.

# (1) Verdict-sufficiency of.

The provision of the Code that the verdict shall be signed by the foreman is merely directory, and a motion to set aside a verdict because not signed comes too late after the jury has been discharged. Berry v. Pusey, 80 Ky. 166, 3 R. 656; and see further, Bickel v. Kraus, 100 Ky. 728, 18 R. 1054, 39 S. W. 414; Ky. D. & W. Co. v. Leonard, 27 R. 1055, 87 S. W. 509; and notes to sec. 255, Criminal Code, as to manner of rendering verdict and correction thereof.

# () Jury may return to their room and perfect their verdict if they have brought in an imperfect or irregular one.

Tarlton v. Briscoe, 1 Mar. 67; or the judge may, if verdict is a substantial response to issue, correct informalities in it. Worley v. Isbel, 1 Bibb 247; Crozier v. Gano, 1 Bibb 257; Bergen v. Siears, 24 R. 80, 67 S. W. 1002.

# (2a) Where a verdict is informal it may be corrected by the judge.

Gernert v. Straeffer's Ex., 162 Ky. 605, 172 S. W. 1044.

# (3) Affidavit of jurors can not be received to impeach their verdict for misbehavior in themselves or their fellow jurors.

Allard v. Smith, 2 Met. 297; and if affidavits of jurors can be received to show that their verdict was not what they intended it to be, they should be received with great caution and the verdict remain unchanged unless the mistake is clearly made out. Alexander v. Humber, 86 Ky. 565, 9 R. 734. 6 S. W. 453; and see notes 25-33, sec. 340.

# (4) A verdict is good if its meaning may be understood in the light of the record.

Buckeye Engine Co. v. Buckwalter, 22 R. 1706, 61 S. W. 263; Ky. Dis. Co. v. Leonard, 27 R. 1055, 87 S. W. 809; Pittsburg R. Co. v. Darlington, 111 S. W. 360, 129 Ky. 266.

# (5) Defects in pleading cured by verdict-see note 12, sec. 95.

# (6) Judgment against verdict - see notes to sec. 386.

# (7) Verdict finding "for plaintiff the debt in the petition mentioned" is sufficiently certain.

Brannin v. Force, 12 B. M. 509; or "for plaintiff." Cooper v. Poston, 1 Duv. 92; Jackson v. Hill, 22 R. 563, 58 S. W. 434; nor does surplusage in a verdict vitiate it. Kouns v. Grayson, 2 Bibb 237, and see notes to sec. 326.

# (8) Three-fourths or more of a jury may make a verdict, and when a verdict is so made by less than the whole jury, it must be signed by all who agree to it.

Ky. Stat., sec. 2268.

# (9) Third verdict for same party will not be set aside on the ground that it is not sustained by or is clearly against the evidence.

Wall v. Dimmitt, 141 Ky. 715, 133 S. W. 768.

# § 326] Trial by Jury-Verdict. [Title IX

# SUBDIVISION 3.

# VERDICT.

# § 326. Verdicts described and defined.

# 327. Verdicts-kind that jury may find.

# § 328. Judgment, if general and special inconsistent.

# 329. Assessment of recovery by jury in cases of general verdict.

# § 330. When jury to assess value of property and damages.

§ 326 [856] [Verdicts described and defined.] The verdict of a jury is general or separate-general [or special.*]

1. [General verdict.] A general verdict is that by which the jury pronounces generally, upon all the issues, for the plaintiff or for the defendant.
2. [Separate-general verdict.] A separate-general verdict is the finding, upon any of the issues, in favor of the plaintiff or of the defendant.
3. [Special verdict.] A special verdict is the finding of facts by a jury, as shown in their answers to questions submitted to them in writing.*

*Words in brackets repealed. See editorial note to section 317.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 396d; Forms Nos. 397, 399, 374-406, 406a.

# GENERAL VERDICT.

326. (1) Verdict-defects in pleading cured by. Where there is any defect, imperfection or omission in any pleading, whether in substance or form, which would have been fatal on demurrer, yet if the issue joined be such as necessarily required on the trial proof of the facts, so defectively stated or omitted, and without which it is not presumed that either the judge would direct the jury to give, or the jury would have given, the verdict, such defect, imperfection or omission is cured by the verdict. Fuqua v. Mullen, 13 Bush 467.

(4) Verdict-how construed. When a question between A and B as to the ownership of a horse is submitted to a jury, and the verdict is that the property was owned by B, it will be regarded as meaning that B was the owner at the time of the levy. Schweim v. Sims, 2 Met. 209; see Noel v. Hudson, 13 B. M. 205, and notes to sec. 325.

(4a) A verdict for plaintiff did not state against which of two defendants it was rendered. It was held to be a verdict against both. Atkinson v. Lawrence, 161 Ky. 166, 170 S. W. 528.

(4b) A finding by a jury in these words: "We, nine of the jury, agree for each party to pay their cost," was void and no verdict. The failure of a litigant to object to such a verdict at the time of its rendition waives nothing. The objection is sufficient if made in the grounds for new trial. Davis v. Stone, 172 Ky. 696, 189 S. W. 937.

(3) Verdict is not a judgment-the court must render judgment upon it: and the court cannot refuse to render judgment upon the verdict upon proper notice and motion made after the expiration of the time to move for a new trial.

(5) Verdict in absence of proof. In an action for the value of work and labor, the jury have a right, in the absence of all proof of the value of such labor, to find a verdict for the price of the work.

Title [X] Trial by Jury-Verdict.

§ 327 [357] [Verdicts—kind that jury may find.] Unless otherwise directed, the jury may find a general, or a general and separate-general, verdict [or a special verdict, with or without a general*] or a separate-general verdict; but the court may without motion, or upon the motion of a party shall, direct the jury to find—

1. [Separate-general verdict-general verdict—effect of inconsistency.] A separate-general verdict, as to any issue; and with such finding the jury shall also return a general verdict; and, if the separate-general verdict be inconsistent with the general verdict, judgment shall be rendered pursuant to the former; or, done as charged in the petition. Baum v. Winston, 3 Met. 127.
2. In an action for assault and battery where the defendant fails to answer, the jury may properly, without proof, render a verdict for the plaintiff for more than nominal damages. Rogers v. Aulick, 2 Duv. 419.
3. In action for damages in a specified sum, the court could not render judgment for the amount claimed upon a verdict of "we, the jury, find for the plaintiff." C., O. & S. W. R. R. v. Madox, 19 R. 966, 42 S. W. 1124; L. & N. R. R. v. Hartwell, 09 Ky. 436, 18 R. 745, 36 S. W. 183; 38 S. W. 1041. If a separate-general verdict is asked for, the court shall grant it, and with it shall require the jury to return a general verdict.
4. Joint defendants in tort -verdict as to one good although there is no finding as to other. C. & O. R. Co. v. Booth, 149 Ky. 245.
5. Separation of compensatory and exemplary damages. If a party desires a separation in a verdict of compensatory and exemplary damages he must request such separation. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. John's Admx., 155 Ky. 264, 159 S. W. 822.

# SEPARATE-GENERAL VERDICT DEFINED.

"The meaning of the expression 'separate-general verdict' is that the verdict is separate as to the particular issue as distinguished from any other issue in the case and general as to the particular issue. That is it was intended to apply in cases where there is more than one issue. For instance, an action upon an alleged contract when the issues presented are first, was the contract procured by fraud or duress? And, second, if it was so procured, and therefore voidable, was it subsequently ratified by the defendant after a full knowledge of the fraud and the removal of the duress? If, in such case, the jury should find a general verdict for the defendant, the special findings permissible. In Tinsley v. Ross, 15 R. 44, 22 S. W. 313, special findings were submitted to the jury, and on their findings the court rendered judgment. In answer to the objection that the law permitting special findings had been repealed, and it was therefore error to submit them, the court held the objection not available, as the judgment was correct. The provisions of the Code permitting special verdicts have been repealed. See amendment to sec. 327; but as occasionally, as in this case or by agreement, special findings may be submitted, I have inserted cases following.-Ed.

Special findings—practice. When in an ordinary action both legal and equitable.

Trial by Jury—Verdict

# § 328

# [Special verdict.]

A special verdict; and, on such finding, the jury shall return a special verdict only; and the court shall render judgment upon it.*

[Amendment of May 15, 1886, c. 1170, p. 120.] So much of chapter 2, title 9, of the Civil Code of Practice as authorizes and provides for a separate-general or a special verdict be, and the same is hereby, repealed: Provided, That the court in its discretion may direct the jury to find a separate-general verdict.

*Words in brackets repealed. See editorial note to Section 317. Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 396d; Form No. 406a.

# § 328 [858]

[Judgment, if general and special inconsistent.] If a general and a special verdict be inconsistent, judgment shall be rendered pursuant to the latter.] (This section is repealed by Act of May 15, 1886, c. 1170, p. 120.)

If defenses are relied upon, the court may submit to the jury for its decision such matters of fact as are involved in the equitable issue, when such issue has not been transferred to the equity docket, and where either party may be entitled to recover money, or where damages are to be assessed, the court should direct the jury to assess the amount of recovery. A special verdict is a finding of facts without reference to their relation to any issue. Witty v. C. & O. R. R. Co., 83 Ky. 21, 6 R. 321; Berry v. Pusey, 80 Ky. 166, 3 R. 656; Imperial Ins. Co. v. Kiernan, 83 Ky. 468, 7 R. 542; Jones v. Moore, 99 S. W. 286, 30 R. 603.

Special verdict to control. Where special findings are directed, it should be to every fact necessary to make out a cause of action or defense; if they are complete they must control, and the court will not look to the general verdict or the instructions that may have been given to the jury. The questions of law may be reserved by the court until after verdict. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Brice, 84 Ky. 298, 8 R. 271, 1 S. W. 483. "The rule is imperative that the court shall direct the jury to find upon such issues or facts as are embraced by the motion or interrogatories, but the court may reject the form proposed by counsel and put the question or prescribe the mode of eliciting the special verdict according to its sound discretion." Louisville R. R. v. Weams, 80 Ky. 420, 4 R. 287.

In directing a special verdict the court should confine the question propounded to the controlling facts in the case, and they should be such as to enable the court on the return of the verdict to apply the law and enter judgment without anything further from the jury.

328. General and special verdict. If a general and a special verdict are inconsistent, judgment shall be rendered pursuant to the latter, but the judgment should be rendered on the general verdict when the special findings are not inconsistent with the general verdict. A bill of evidence is not necessary to enable the court to determine whether or not a general and a special verdict are inconsistent. Quaid v. Cornwall, 13 Bush. 601; see Gailbraith v. Arlington Ins. Co., 12 Bush 29.

Title [X] Trial by the Court.

# § 329 [859] [Assessment of recovery by jury in cases of general verdict.]

If, by a general verdict, either party be entitled to recover money of the adverse party, the jury, in their verdict, must assess the amount of recovery. Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 397, 398, 399.

# § 330 [seo] [When jury to assess value of property and damages.]

In actions for the recovery of specific personal property, the jury must assess its value, and the damages for the taking or detention of it, if their verdict authorize a judgment for the recovery or return of the property. (Form of judgment, Form 193 and sec. 388. Form of execution for specific property, Form 150.) Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 126a, 770; Forms Nos. 402, 417.

# ARTICLE 3.

# TRIAL BY THE COURT.

# § 331. Actions in tort-effect of trial by court.

# § 332. Law and facts to be separately stated.

# § 331 [861] [Actions in tort—effect of trial by court.]

1. [Trial by jury to assess amount of recovery-exceptions to rule. Questions of value and damages are questions of fact, and as a rule in cases involving such questions the jury must assess the amount of recovery, and a verdict "finding for plaintiff" is not sufficient. L. &: N. R. R. v. Hartwell, 99 Ky. 436, 18 R. 745, 36 S. W. 183; 38 S. W. 1041; but in an action on a note where the only defense was usury and verdict was, "We of the jury find for the plaintiff," a judgment for the amount of the note and interest was proper. Cooper v. Poston, I Duv. 92; and see Brannin v. Force, 12 B. M. 506, and see notes 1-5, to sec. 126.]
2. (2) To authorize a jury to assess damages for property illegally taken and detained, there should be some evidence of the value, or some description of the property, to enable the jury to determine the value from their own knowledge. Pharis v. Carver, 13 B. M. 237.
3. (3) The value and damages must be fixed by the jury. 2 Met. 499, 78 Ky. 481, 1 R. 25; and where there are several articles in controversy the verdict should find the value of each article, and the judgment should be for each article or its value. Buckner v. Haggin, 3 Mon. 60, 6 Mon. 52; and see further, notes to sec. 181 and Black Rayen Coal Co. v. Edmonson, 108 S. W. 955.

# § 330.

1. (1) Verdict and judgment in action for recovery of property. In an action for the recovery of specific personal property, the jury should fix in their verdict the value of the property and the damages for its detention; and the judgment should be in the alternative for the return of the property or its value. Buckner v. Haggin, 3 Mon. 60, 6 Mon. 52; and see further, notes to sec. 181 and Black Rayen Coal Co. v. Edmonson, 108 S. W. 955.
2. (2) If in an action for recovery of personal property defendant has delivered the property to plaintiff the jury in finding a verdict for plaintiff need not assess the value of the property. Rogers v. Bradford, 8 Bush 163; oYung v. Parsons, 2 Met. 499; Bates v. Buchanan, 2 Bush 117; Strubbee v. C. R. W. Co., 78 Ky. 261.

# § 331. (1) Court, trial by - motion for new trial.

In a common law action,

# § 331 Trial by the Court.

In an action for an injury to the person or character of the plaintiff, the parties may, with the consent of the court, waive a jury trial, by written consent, in person or by attorney, filed with the clerk; or, by oral consent in court, entered on the record.

# 2. Effect of trial by court.

If a jury trial be not demanded, or be waived, the court shall decide the issues, and make such assessments of value, damage or amount of recovery as it would have been the duty of a jury to make; and the finding of the court shall have the same effect as that of the jury would have had. (See further as to trial by court, secs. 312, 346. Form of capias ad satisfaciendum, Form 152.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 102, 901; Form No. 418. See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 47.

Where the law and facts are submitted to the court, a motion and grounds for a new trial are necessary in order to bring before the Appellate Court any errors committed by the court during the progress of the trial. Helm v. Coffey, 80 Ky. 176, 3 R. 677; Henderson v. Dupree, 82 Ky. 678, 6 R. 702; Harper v. Harper, 10 Bush 447; Beeler v. Sandidge, 20 R. 1581, 49 S. W. 533; Albin Co. v. Ellinger, 103 Ky. 240, 19 R. 1886, 44 S. W. 655; and this rule applies to an action in equity that is tried as an ordinary action. Sims v. Lanehart, 19 R. 1439, 38 S. W. 490; but where an ordinary action is transferred to equity and heard, or where only legal questions are presented, no motion for a new trial or separation of law and facts is necessary. Com. v. Railroad Cos., 95 Ky. 60, 15 R. 449, 23 S. W. 868; Keaton v. Sublett, 109 Ky. 106, 22 R. 631, 58 S. W. 528.

# 3. Judgment-how regarded.

When the law and facts are submitted to the court, every fact which goes to support the judgment must be assumed to have been found in favor of the party for whom judgment was given, and every fact as to which the evidence is conflicting must be assumed to have been found against the adverse party. Coleman v. Meade, 13 Bush 358; and see note 1, sec. 10.

# 4. Weight given to judgment.

Where the law and facts are submitted to the court, its findings of facts are to be treated as the verdict of a properly instructed jury, and will not be disturbed unless palpably against the evidence. Bell v. Wood, 87 Ky. 56, 9 R. 917, 7 S. 262.

# 5. Waiver of jury trial.

Waiver of a trial by jury should appear in the entry submitting the facts to the court; or it may be inferred from an entry showing that the court heard testimony and pronounced judgment thereon; but such inference cannot be indulged in where nothing appears but a judgment by default. Clarke v. Seaton, 18 B. M. 226.

# 6. Submission of law and facts to court-motion for new trial-separation of law and facts-practice.

In the absence of a motion and grounds for new trial and a separation of the law and facts, nothing is before the Court of Appeals except the sufficiency of the pleadings and the evidence to support the verdict or decision. Albin Co. v. Ellinger, 103 Ky. 240, 19 R. 1886, 44 S. W. 655.

Title X] Trial by the Court. [§ 332
# § 332 [862] [Law and facts to be separately stated, if requested.]

Upon trials of questions of fact by the court, it shall not be necessary for the court to state its finding, except, generally, for the plaintiff or defendant, unless one of the parties requests it, with the view of excepting to the decision of the court upon the questions of law involved in the trial; in which case the court shall state in writing the conclusions of fact found, separately from the conclusions of law. (Form of judgment, Forms 189, 190.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 418.

See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 46.

W. 550; Kahn v. Rogers, 19 R. 1806, 44 S. W. 431; and see note 1 to sec. 10.

1. The finding of the court on a trial where the law and the facts are submitted without intervention of a jury is equivalent to the verdict of a properly instructed jury, and may cure defects in the petition. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Williams, 156 Ky. 114, 160 S. W. 769.
2. (1) Stating separately law and facts practice. The section of the Code requiring the court to state separately the law and facts does not relate to the trial of provisional remedies. Francis v. Burnett, 84 Ky. 23, 7 R. 715; Haynes v. Wiley, 12 R. 299, 2 S. W. 681; Crouch v. The Meguiar-Harris Co., 19 R. 819, 42 S. W. 91; KassEl v. SneaD, 21 R. 777, 52 S. W. 1058. See further, Com. v. R. R. 95 Ky. 60, 15 R. 449, 23 S. W. 868; 56 S. W. 993.
3. (When the court upon request states separately its conclusions of law and fact, there must be an exception to the conclusions of law; a motion for a new trial, in the absence of such exception, will not cure omission. American Aid Society v. Bronger, 91 Ky. 406, 12 R. 971, 15 S. W. 1118; Beeler v. Sandidge, 20 R. 1581, 49 S. W. 533.)
4. (3) Request for separation of law and facts need not be made until after judgment is rendered but must be made within three days thereafter. Albin v. Collier, 181 Ky. 319, 204 S. W. 74.
5. (6) Separation of law and facts-motion for a new trial—practice. See The Albin Co. v. Ellinger, 103 Ky. 240, 19 R. 1886, 44 S. W. 655; Beeler v. Sandidge, 20 R. 1581, 49 S. W. 533; Day v. Adams, 20 R. 1827, 50 S. W. 2.
6. (7) Criminal trials. This section does not apply to criminal trials. Com. v. Collier, 181 Ky. 319, 204 S. W. 74.
