§ 333 [3es] [Exception -how taken—objection when necessary.]

1. [Exception defined.] An exception is an objection taken to a decision of the court upon a matter of law.

§ 333. (1) Amendment. An exception taken to the filing of an amendment that is allowed to be filed without objection is not available; objection should have been made to the filing. Helburn v. Mofford, 7 Bush 169.

Demurrer. An error in overruling a demurrer must be excepted to at the time. Beaven v. Phillips, 83 Ky. 88, 7 R. 9.

Depositions. When an exception goes to the whole of a deposition, and some of it is competent, it should be overrulED. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Graves, 78 Ky. 74; see further, sec. 586.

(2) When depositions are excepted to, it must appear that the exception was acted on, or it will not avail. Corn v. Sims, 3 Met. 391; Lewis v. Wright, 3 Bush 311; Walrath v. Viley, 1 Bush 266.

(5) Evidence. A party must object to the introduction of incompetent evidence and, if it is allowed over his objection, must except to it at the time, or the error is waived. Crabb v. Larkin, 9 Bush 154; Helburn v. Mofford, 7 Bush 169; Russell v. Marks, 3 Met. 37; L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Graves, 78 Ky. 74, 1 Met. 450; where an objection was entered to the admission of evidence, and at the close of the evidence the bill of exceptions stated "to all of which the defendant excepted," held sufficient exception. L. & N. R. R. v. Ritter, 85 Ky. 368, 9 R. 22, 3 S. W. 591.

(5a) An error in excluding testimony offered on the jury trial cannot be considered on appeal unless the ruling of the court was excepted to; an avowal as to what the witness would state is not sufficient to bring up the question. Posey v. Cont. Ins. Co., 149 Ky. 631, 149 S. W. 984.

(6) Final orders. Final judgments, whether upon issues of law or fact, need not be objected or excepted to. Where a demurrer to an answer is sustained and judgment rendered against the defendant, or where a peremptory instruction is given, no exception is necessary. Coffman v. Wilson, 2 Met. 542; Craycraft v. Duncan, 6 R. 651; Loving v. Warren Co., 14 Bush 316; Meacham v. L. N. R., 20 R. 112, 45 S. W. 363. Nor is it necessary to except to a void order. Smith v. Blakeman, 8 Bush 476.

(7) Instructions - offered by adverse party must be objected to at time and if objection is overruled and instruction given an exception must be taken at time; if this is not done any error in giving instruction will be waived; there must be both an objection and an exception; neither of itself is sufficient. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Graves, 78 Ky. 74; Cox v. Winston, 3 Met. 577; Reed v. Com., 7 Bush 641; Garrot v. Ratliff, 83 Ky. 384, 7 Ky. 463; L. & N. R. Co. v. Bocock, 107 Ky. 223, 21 R. 896, 51 S. W. 580; 53 S. W. 262; 14 Bush 316; 1 Duv. 387; 81 Ky. 51, 4 R. 596; but if the instruction is given by the court on its own motion, no objection is necessary; an exception is sufficient; and so if the court instructs the jury by direction of both parties. L. S. R. R. v. Hooe, 18 R. 521, 35 S. W. 266; 38 S. W. 131.

(8) The circuit court cannot adopt any rule of practice in contravention of the provisions of the Code requiring ex-

# Trial-Exceptions

2. Exception without objection. A party may, without a previous objection, except to a decision against him, unless it be made at the instance of the adverse party.

3. Objection and exception, when necessary. But a party cannot except to a decision made at the instance of the adverse party, unless objection shall have been made to the motion, offer or request of the adverse party. (Exceptions to depositions, how taken, secs. 586, 587.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 42, 43, 44.

§ 334 [364] Exception, when to be taken—time to prepare bill. The party objecting must except when the decision is made; and time may be given to prepare a bill of exceptions, but not beyond a day in the succeeding term, to be fixed by the court.

[Absence of trial judge extends time to file bill. If the judge of said court, for any cause, does not preside at the said term of the court, or no court is held, then the party offering the bill of exceptions shall have until the next term of the court to perfect and prepare the bill of exceptions.]

(Words in brackets added by Act of May 12, 1886, c. 1058, p. 101. Form of bill, Forms 182-186.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 44, 67, 75.

Exceptions and objections to be taken and made as therein directed. Kennedy v. Cunningham, 2 Met. 538; Letton v. Young, 2 Met. 558; I. C. R. R. v. Glasscock, 24 R. 1937, 72 S. W. 769.

(9) Neither an objection nor exception is necessary to enable a party to complain of a peremptory instruction. Loving v. Warren Co., 14 Bush 316; Meacham v. L. & N. R. R., 20 R. 112, 45 S. W. 363; 2 Met. 542.

(9a) Where no objection was offered to an instruction at the time it was given, complaint of it upon appeal cannot be considered. Chesapeake & Ohio Ry. Co. v. Kelley, 157 Ky. 724, 163 S. W. 1088.

(10) Waiver of error. Neither party can rely for reversal upon an erroneous decision made at the instance of the adverse party unless he objected to the motion or offer of the adverse party at the time it was made, and then excepted to the decision; but where the court acts upon its own motion an exception alone is sufficient. Branson v. Com., 92 Ky. 330, 13 R. 614, 17 S. W. 1019.

(11) Objection and exception both necessary when decision is made at instance of adverse party. Moss Tie Co. v. Myers, 116 S. W. 255; Smalling v. Shaw, 144 Ky. 458, 139 S. W. 779.

(11a) The provision that objections and exceptions must be taken by a complaining party does not apply so as to bind the court in which the order is made; a party cannot avail himself of erroneous rulings in such cases in the Court of Appeals without complying with those provisions in the circuit court. First Nat. Bk. of Louisville v. Bickel, 154 Ky. 11, 156 S. W. 856.

(11b) The fact that appellee's counsel used certain language will not be considered on appeal unless it be authenticated by bill of exceptions; an affidavit as to the language used is insufficient. So. Ry. Co. of Ky. v. Thacker's Admr., 156 Ky. 483, 161 S. W. 236.

(11c) In the absence of a bill of evidence the court was unable to say whether a certain argument by the attorney of the successful party was improper. Fish v. Welch's Admr., 156 Ky. 541, 161 S. W. 512.

(11d) The overruling of a motion to dismiss and remand a case to the county court cannot be assigned as error, where the defendant did not except to the ruling at the time it was made. Delk v. Com. 166 Ky. 39, 178 S. W. 1129.

(12) See further notes to secs. 737, 742.

334. (1Bill cannot be filed in vacation. A bill of exceptions cannot be filed in vacation in the clerk's office, nor

# § 334] Trial-Exceptions. [Title IX

at any time except during term time. Nor can this rule be changed by an order of the court; and it is questionable if by agreement of parties, entered of record, a bill filed in vacation can be considered. *Allard v. Snith, 2 Met. 297; Freeman v. Brenham, 17 B. M. 603; and see Vandever v. Grithith, 2 Met. 425; Corley v. Evans, 4 Bush 409; Com. v. Lewis, 19 R. 170, 39 S. W. 438; Craft v. Allen, 21 R. 243, 51 S. W. 169; Adkins v. Comn., 102 Ky. 94, 19 R. 1300, 42 S. W. 834; 44 S. W. 132; Knecht v. Louisville Home Tel. Co., 121 Ky. 492, 28 R. 456, 89 S. W. 508; Dupoyster v. Fort Jeff. Imp. Co., 28 R. 504, 89 S. W. 509.*

(1a) A bill of exceptions can not be filed in vacation. *Beckett v. Com., 154 Ky. 209, 157 S. W. 12.*

Bill filed by agreed order-at the term at which judgment was rendered an order was made stating that bill of exceptions and evidence was filed, approved and made part of record, but as a matter of fact the bill was not filed or approved until next term, the court held that the bill could not be considered. *I. C. R. R. v. Glasscock, 24 R. 1937, 72 S. W. 769; N. N. & M. V. R. R. Co. v. Stavig, 98 Ky. 533, 17 R. 1113, 33 S. W. 620.*

(3) Death of party before bill filed. The death of the appellee before the time allowed for filing the bill of exceptions will not prejudice the appellant if his bill be tendered within the time allowed: although there may have been no revivor. *Hayden v. Ortkeiss, 83 Ky. 396, 7 R. 399.*

(4) Jefferson Circuit Court. The Jefferson Circuit Court has the same power over its judgments for sixty days that the circuits courts have during the term. If under any circumstances the court is authorized to extend the time to file a bill to a day beyond a period of sixty days from the judgment, it can not be filed after the day so fixed if no notice is taken of the matter on that day. *Bailey v. Villier, 6 Bush 27. If time be extended by that court beyond sixty days from date of judgment, and the bill is filed within the time allowed without objection, the error, if any, will be regarded as waived, Downing v. Bacon. 7 Bush 680; see further, Johnson v. Stivers, 95 Ky. 128, 15 R. 477, 23 S. W. 957; holding that not more than one hundred and twenty days can be allowed after judgment to file bill. The court can not by an order fix a less time than sixty days from judgment in which to file bill, and a bill will be considered if it is filed within the sixty days. Connelly v. Adams, 19 R. 1084, 42 S. W. 1133; Metz v. L. & N. R. R., 102 Ky. 225, 19 R. 1200, 43 S. W. 215; but if time is not extended within the sixty days the bill filed after that time will not be considered. City v. Wilson, 23 R. 1722, 66 S. W. 8; Knecht v. L. Home Tel. Co., 28 R. 456. 121 Ky. 492, 89 S. W. 508; Zehe v. City of Louisville, 123 Ky. 621, 96 S. W. 918, 29 R. 1106; Louisville Ry. Co. v. Wellington, 137 Ky. 719, 128 S. W. 1077.*

(5) Nunc pro tunc order that "the parties came by their attorneys, and it appearing at the trial of this cause at the last term of this court the court allowed defendants time to prepare a bill of exceptions," and thereupon "the bill was filed as part of the record," was invalid, as no time had been given at a former term to file the bill. *Vandever v. Griffith. 2 Met. 425; Blue Grass Traetion Co. v. Crossdale, 143 Ky. 196, 136 S. W. 204; but where an indorsement on bill showed that it had been filed on a certain day, although no order filing it was made, it was proper afterward to enter a nunc pro tunc order filing bill as of day endorsement was made. Washington Life Ins. Co. v. Menefee, 107 Ky. 244, 21 R. 916, 53 S. W. 260; smalling v. Shaw, 144 Ky. 458, 139 S. W. 779.*

(6) Pendency of motion for new trial. The time for filing a bill of exceptions is reckoned from the overruling of a motion for a new trial, and not from the rendition of the judgment on the verdict, and the court has no power pending the motion for a new trial to prescribe the time within which the bill of exceptions shall be filed. *10 Bush 447.*

(6a) A motion for a new trial in a civil case suspends the judgment, and the cause may be continued and passed upon a subsequent term. *Stearn's Coal & Lumber Co. v. Com., 163 Ky. 837, 174 S. W. 771.*

(7) Special judge. When a special judge, who has tried a case, extends time for filing bill to a day in next term, and is not present on that day, the party who then tenders bill in open court is entitled to have offer to file continued to succeeding term, and then have bill signed by special judge. *McFarland v. Burton, 89 Ky. 294, 11 R. 499, 12 S. W. 336; Phillips v. Beattyville M. & T. Co. 100' S. W. 244, 30 R. 1103; the special judge who tries case can sign bill at a subsequent term. Murray v. East End Imp. CO., 22 R. 1477, 60 S. W. 648; and the bill must be signed by special, not regular judge, L. S. R. Co. v. Lewis, 101 Ky. 296, 19 R. 570, 41 S. W. 3.*

# Title IX

# Trial-Exceptions

# (8)

Time to file bill - When time is given it must be to a certain day. *8 Bush 476: Johnson v. Stivers, 95 Ky. 128. 16 R. 477, 23 S. W. 957; and when time is given until a day in the next term to file a bill an order may be made on the day specified extending the time to another day certain in the term; and for sufficient reason, further extensions may be given from one day certain to another day certain during the term. Smith v. Blakeman, 8 Bush 476.*

# (9)

When time is given to file bill of exceptions, it may be filed during that term and before the day specified. *Newport News R. R. v. Thomas, 96 Ky. 613, 16 R. 706, 29 S. W. 437; L. & N. R. R. v. Turner, 81 Ky. 489, 5 R. 647; Metz v. L. & N. R. R., 102 Ky. 225, 19 R. 1200, 43 S. W. 215. When a bill is tendered in court and taken for consideration by the judge, it is to be regarded as filed on the day it is tendered, and may be filed by him on a subsequent day, but bill must be tendered on or before day specified. Toner v. South Cov. Ry. Co., 109 Ky. 41, 22 R. 564, 58 S. W. 439; 81 Ky. 489, 5 R. 647; Meaux v. Meaux; White v. Allen, 10 R. 1025, 11 S. W. 364; and the orders should state the facts. Nance v. Newport News R. R., 13 R. 554, 17 S. W. 570.*

# (10)

A bill of exceptions filed at a term later than the next succeeding term after judgment is rendered will not be considered by the Court of Appeals, although it is filed pursuant to an order of court. *Combs v. Combs, 19 R. 439, 41 S. W. 7; Johnson v. Stivers, 95 Ky. 128, 15 R. 477, 23 S. W. 957; Knecht v. Lou. Home Tel. Co., 28 R. 456, 121 Ky. 492, 89 S. W. 508; Dupoyster v. Fort Jeff. Imp. Co., 28 R. 504, 89 S. W. 509; Woolsey v. Kennon, 117 S. W. 943.*

# (11)

A bill of exceptions showing by a memorandum of the clerk, but not an order of court, that it was filed in time, will not be considered. *Padget v. Mays, 2 R. 213.*

# (12)

Giving time to file bill of exceptions does not operate to stay proceedings on the judgment. *Wright v. Woolfolk, 14 Bush 308.*

# (13)

If time is given in which to prepare and file a bill of exceptions, the record must show that the time was given, or it cannot be filed, although by neglect or oversight the order giving time was not made. *Blue Grass Traction Co. v. Crossdale, 143 Ky. 190, 136 S. W. 204; L. & A. Coal Co. v. Morris, 116 S. W. 330, 132 Ky. 223; Vandever v. Griffith, 2 Met. 425; Linch v. Reynolds, 6 Bush 547. Code provision that exception must be saved and time allowed to prepare bill is mandatory. Dalton v. Dalton, 146 Ky. 18, 141 S. W. 371; Smalling v. Shaw, 139 S. W. 779, 144 Ky. 458.*

# (14)

Bill cannot be filed in clerk's office or signed or approved in vacation, nor can time in which to file bill be extended in vacation. *Forsythe v. Huey, 25 R. 147, 74 S. W. 1088; Adkins v. Coni., 102 Ky. 94, 19 R. 1300, 42 S. W. 834; and see Proctor Coal Co. v. Strunk, 28 R. 241, 89 S. W. 145.*

# (15)

Where time is given to a day in next term to file a bill it means in absence of anything to the contrary next regular term, and the holding of a special term in the interval does not affect the order. *Mayes v. Lane, 116 Ky. 566, 25 R. 824, 76 S. W. 399.*

# (16)

Where no objection is made in lower court to the filing of bill an error in respect to the time in which it is filed will be considered waived. *Walling v. Eggers, 25 R. 1563, 78 S. W. 428; L. & N. R. Co. v. Setser, 138 Ky. 476, 128 S. W. 341, 7 Bush 680.*

# (17)

If parties consent that bill may be filed at second succeeding term or fail to object to the order giving this time they will be estopped from raising any objection on appeal. *Hill v. Penn. Life Ins. Co., 27 R. 567, 85 S. W. 759; 120 Ky. 190; Vertrees v. Head, 138 Ky. 83, 127 S. W. 523.*

# (18)

Delay without excuse from week to week, for six months in preparing bill of exceptions, is sufficient reason for refusing to consider it over objection of adverse party. *Schneider v. Hesse, 11 R. 433, 12 S. W. 271.*

# (18a)

Time was given on the thirteenth day of a certain term of court to file a bill of exceptions. Court was not in session on that day. The filing in the office of the clerk was sufficient. *City of Henderson v. Ky. Peer. Dist. Co., 101 Ky. 1, 170 S. W. 210.*

# (18b)

Where time is given until a day in a succeeding term to file a bill of exceptions, further extension may be given from a day certain in the term to another day certain within the term for presentation of the bill. *U. S. Fidelity & Guaranty Co. v. Cole's Admr., 165 Ky. 823, 178 S. W. 1057.*

# (19)

Retention of bill by judge - effect of. Where time is given to succeeding term to file bill and within the time the bill is given to the judge who holds it until the next term it may be then filed. *Smalling v. Shaw, 144 Ky. 458, 139 S. W. 779.*

# § 335] Trial-Exceptions.

# [Title IX]

# § 335 [865] [Bill of exceptions—how prepared.]

1. [How bill prepared.] No particular form of exception, or bill of exceptions, is required. A party must not, except as hereinafter provided, state the evidence, but must state the material facts which the evidence conduced to prove; and, if the exception be to a decision admitting or excluding testimony, or concerning its meaning or effect, so much of the evidence as is necessary to explain the exception, and no more, shall be stated.

# (20) Transcript of evidence must be filed in time.

If a bill of exceptions is filed in time but the transcript of the evidence is not, the evidence will not be considered. Knecht v. Louisville Home Tel. Co., 89 S. W. 508, 28 R. 456, 121 Ky. 492; Zehe v. City of Louisville, 96 S. W. 918, 29 R. 1107, 123 Ky. 621.

# (21) Equity cases.

When oral evidence is heard in an equity case the evidence must be made a part of the record by a bill of exceptions prepared in the same time and manner as in ordinary cases. Dupoyster v. Fort Jefferson Imp. Co., 89 S. W. 509, 28 R. 504, 121 Ky. 518; Knecht v. Home Tel. Co., 89 S. W. 508, 28 R. 456, 121 Ky. 492; Shannon v. Stratton, 144 Ky. 26, 137 S. W. 850.

# (22) Time how counted.

The day on which the judgment was rendered must be counted in computing the time within which a bill of exceptions must be filed. Louisville Ry. Co. v. Wellington, 137 Ky. 719, 128 S. W. 1077.

# (23) Extension of time to file bill may be granted before the time allowed to file it has expired.

L. & N. R. Co. v. Setser, 138 Ky. 476, 128 S. W. 341.

# (24) Must be filed within time allowed or bill will not be considered.

City of Catlettsburg v. May, 140 Ky. 367, 131 S. W. 15; L. & N. R. Co. v. Turner, 81 Ky. 489, 5 R. 647; L. & A. Coal Co. v. Morris, 116 S. W. 330, 132 Ky. 223; Nave v. Riley, 146 Ky. 276, 142 S. W. 388.

# (24a)

A certain order extending the time to file a bill of exceptions was afterward set aside by the court. The court had power during the term to set aside the second order and leave the first order in force. City of Henderson v. Ky. Peer. Dist. Co., 161 Ky. 1, 170 S. W. 210.

# (24b)

Upon the overruling of a motion for a new trial, time may be granted until the next term to file a bill of exceptions, although the motion for a new trial was not acted upon at the term at which the judgment was rendered. Stearns Coal & L. Co. v. Com., 163 Ky. 837, 174 S. W. 771.

# (24c)

The extension of time to file a bill of exceptions to a day in the next term may be made at any time during the term at which the judgment become final. Carter Coal Co. v. Clouse, 163 Ky. 337, 173 S. W. 794.

# (24d)

A bill of exceptions not filed in time will be stricken from the record on motion. Bauer Cooperage Co. v. Ewell, 149 Ky. 838, 149 S. W. 1137.

# (24e)

A bill of exceptions containing the evidence, which is not tendered within a definite time fixed by the court or within a definite extension thereof, cannot be considered, and will be stricken from the record. U. S. Fidelity & Guaranty Co. v. Cole's Admr., 165 Ky. 823, 178 S. W. 1057.

# § 335. (1) Absence of bill of exceptions—only sufficiency of pleadings to support the verdict can be considered

and every presumption will be indulged that the pleadings authorize. Martin v. Richardson, 94 Ky. 183, 14 R. 847, 21 S. W. 1039. A motion for new trial or bill of exceptions is not necessary to take an appeal, but in their absence only the sufficiency of the pleadings to support the verdict will be considered. See note 37, sec. 340.

# la)

In the absence of a bill of exceptions the Court of Appeals will consider only the sufficiency of the pleadings. Tyler v. Woerner, 158 Ky. 710, 166 S. W. 178.

# lb)

In the absence of a bill of exceptions the only question considered was whether the pleading supported the verdict. Clark v. Wallace Oil Co., 155 Kiy. 836, 160 S. W. 506.

# lc)

A judgment in ejectment was affirmed as the bill of exceptions had been struck from the record and the pleadings were sufficient to sustain the judgment. Bobbitt v. Blakemore, 153 Ky. 170, 154 S. W. 941.

# (2) Affidavits

in Warren v. Nash, 24 R. 479, 68 S. W. 658, it is held that the court will consider affidavits filed with motion for new trial and made part of record although the error pointed out in

Trial-Exceptions
2. [Refusal of judge to sign—verdict against evidence.] If the judge refuse to sign the bill of exceptions, as presented; or if the party wish to appeal upon the ground that the verdict is not sustained by the evidence, it shall be stated in full, and the bill of exceptions be presented to the judge. (See sec. 339.)

3. [Copy of one of several similar writings.] But if a number of instruments of substantially the same character be used upon a trial it shall be sufficient to furnish a copy of one of them in the bill of exceptions, accompanied by a statement showing its resemblance to the others, and how many such others were used on the trial; and such copy and statement shall have the same effect as if all of said instruments had been copied in the bill of exceptions. (When writings filed as exhibits are part of record, sec. 128; form of bill, Forms 182-186.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 466, 467, 468.

See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 70, 71, 75.

Affidavits do not appear in bill of exceptions.

(2a) An affidavit which was offered to be filed was not made a part of the record by order of the court, or by a bill of exceptions. The Court of Appeals struck it from the transcript. Crews v. Com., 155 Ky. 122, 159 S. W. 638.

(2b) Amendment. The Court of Appeals will not consider an amendment tendered and not made part of the record by order or bill of exceptions. Koke's Admr. v. Andrew Steel Co., 149 Ky. 627, 149 S. W. 368.

(3) Bill of exceptions-when not necessary. Where a verdict for the plaintiff is set aside and a judgment entered for defendant on the ground that the petition does not state a cause of action, a bill of evidence is not necessary to enable the Court of Appeals to consider the sufficiency of the petition, and reverse the case if a reversal is proper. Johnson v. Louisville R. W. Co., 10 Bush 231.

(5a) Circuit court conclusions of fact. In the absence of a bill of exceptions, the Court of Appeals was unable to say that any of the conclusions of fact found by the circuit court were not supported by the evidence. Daniels, Exr., v. Compton, 151 Ky. 714, 152 S.W. 753.

(5b) Counsel, remarks of. This court will not consider an objection to improper argument of counsel where the same is not certified in the bill of exceptions. City of Louisville v. Hehemann, 161 Ky. 523, 171 S. W. 165.

(5c) Improper and prejudicial remarks of counsel in argument will not be heeded by the Court of Appeals unless the same is certified in the bill of exceptions.

# Trial-Exceptions

be incorporated in the bill of exceptions. St. P. Fire & Mar. Ins. Co. v. Kendle, 163 Ky. 146, 173 S. W. 373.

1. A judgment will not be reversed on account of improper argument of counsel unless the objectionable argument is set out in a bill of exceptions showing that objection was taken. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Stapleton, 154 Ky. 351, 157 S. W. 702.
2. Depositions used as evidence upon trial can not be considered upon appeal unless embraced in bill, or identified by order of court. L. & N. R. R. v. Finley, 86 Ky. 294, 9 R. 660, 5 S. W. 753; nor will depositions copied into the record, but not embodied in bill of exceptions or read on trial, be considered. Harlan v. Howard, 79 Ky. 373, 2 R. 368.
3. When depositions offered as evidence are rejected, the record should show that an exception was taken to the ruling rejecting them, and the grounds of objection, as well as the rejected depositions, should be incorporated into the bill of exceptions. Walrath v. Viley, 1 Bush 266; sec. 589.
4. When two depositions are given by a witness and one only is read, if both are copied in the record, it should show which one was read. Crabb v. Larkin, 9 Bush 154.
5. When depositions have been regularly taken and are lodged with record and read on trial, they should be copied as part of record, although never indorsed as filed. Long v. Kerrigan, 15 R. 65, 21 S. W. 99.
6. A bill of exceptions showed that a certain deposition was read to the jury upon the trial, and showed the ruling of the court upon whatever exceptions were interposed. It was not necessary that the deposition be copied into the bill of exceptions. Harrison v. Ford, 158 Ky. 467, 165 S. W. 663.
7. Entries on order book-when a motion, order, objection, or exception appears on the order book-as where there is objection to the filing of an amendment and exception taken to order allowing it to be filed-it is not necessary that it should appear in bill of exceptions-the orders copied into record will show it. MeAlister v. Ins. Co., 78 Ky. 531.
8. Evidence rejected --how made part of record. When objection is made and sustained to a witness answering a question, the party offering the witness should make an avowal of what he expected the witness would state if permitted to answer the question, and the record should show it, or else the alleged error in refusing the evidence can not be considered. Manion v. Lambert, 10 Bush 205; Bowler v. Lane, 3 Met. 312; Dickerson v. Talbot, 14 B.M. 60; L C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Sullivan, 81 Ky. 624, 5 R. 722.
9. When a party objects to the introduction of evidence the record should show his objections and exception, and so much of the evidence as is necessary to explain the grounds of it. Easley v. Enxley. 18 B. M. 86; and see as to statements in bill as to evidence, note 4.
10. A bill of exceptions which shows that it contains all the evidence is sufficient, although it does not state in so many words that it contains all the evidence. Bracken Co. v. Robertson Co., 6 Bush 69; and see 83 Ky. 384, 7 P. 463; 83 Ky. 88, 7 R. 9.
11. In absence of any part of evidence court will not consider any question relating to the testimony offered or introduced on trial. L. & N. R. R. v. Finley, 86 Ky. 294, 9 R. 660, 5 S. W. 753.
12. Facts appearing upon face of papers, no bill of exceptions is necessary. Collins v. Richart, 14 Bush 621; 78 Ky. 531.
13. Form of bill of exceptions-see Forms 182-186.
14. Instructions. Express statement in bill that it contains all the instructions is not required. If it shows that certain instructions were asked by plaintiff and defendant, and either given or refused, in the absence of anything showing that others were given or refused, bill will be sufficient in this respect. L. & N. R. R. v. Finley, 86 Ky. 294, 9 R. 660, 5 S. W. 753; Warren v. Nash, 24 R. 479, 68 S. W. 658.
15. An alleged error in giving or refusing instructions can not be considered in the absence of the testimony. Beaven v. Phillips, 83 Ky. 88, 7 R. 9; and where the bill does not contain all the instructions given the court can not reverse for an error in instructing the jury. Higgins v. L. & N. R. R., 18 R. 899, 38 S. W. 876.
16. Instructions should be made a part of record either by an order of court or by embodying them in bill of exceptions, the proper mode is to include them in bill of exceptions, if not made part of record in one of these ways they will not be considered by the court, although the clerk may copy them in the record. Huffman v. Charles, 97 S. W. 775, 30 R. 197; Rogers v. Zumbiel, 114 S. W. 323; Nave v. Riley, 146 Ky. 276, 142 S. W. 388; 81 Ky. 475, 5 R. 548; 81 Ky. 51, 4

Title [X] Trial-Exceptions.

R. 596; 4 Met. 94; Tinsley v. White, 21 W. 400; 12 Bush 119; Com. v. P., C., C. & St. L. Ry. Co., 103 Ky. 644, 174 S. W. 29; Holtman v. Bullock, 154 Ky. 634, 157 S. W. 933; Green v. Com., 152 Ky. 239, 153 S. W. 242; Burke Hollow Coal Co. v. Lawson, 151 Ky. 305, 151 S. W. 657.

(19a) An instruction not made part of the record by order of the court or by a bill of exceptions, will not be considered on appeal. Gooch v. Collins, 156 Ky. 282, 160 S. W. 1038; Mudd v. Shroader, 152 Ky. 696, 154 S. W. 21; Gardner v. Alexander, 159 Ky. 713, 169 S. W. 466.

(20) Judge must sign bill. Berea College v. Powell, 25 R. 1235, 77 S. W. 381; Brineger v. I. & N. R. Co., 24 R. 1973, 72 S. W. 783, and sec. 337.

(20n) A bill of exceptions including the evidence was filed, approved and signed by the judge and made part of record. It was not necessary that the judge should again attach his official signature to the bill of exceptions included in the typewritten transcript made by the clerk for the Court of Appeals. Lee v. Com., 155 Ky. 62, 159 S. W. 648.

(20b) Jury trial, suspension of. In the absence of a bill of exceptions the Court of Appeals will not review an alleged error of the court in suspending the trial of a case and using some of the jurors sworn in that case to try other cases. Sams v. Gray, 156 Ky. 615, 161 S. W. 553.

(20c) Map. A map attached to the record by the clerk in making up the transcript which was not shown by a bill of exceptions was struck from the record. Ferrell v. Bauer Coop. Co., 156 Ky. 740, 161 S. W. 1120.

(20d) Ordinances. The Court of Appeals will not strike out a bill of exceptions on the ground that a motion for a new trial was not made in time. Witt v. L. & E. Ry. Co., 155 Ky. 382, 159 S. W. 789.

(21) Pleadings not filed by an order of court but lodged in clerk's office and endorsed filed by clerk that were treated as filed by lower court and parties will be considered as filed. Shields v. Lewis, 24 R. 822, 70 S. W. 51; Day v. Mack, 24 R. 640, 69 S. W. 712.

(22) Pleadings rejected-how made part of record. When the record shows that an amended pleading was not allowed to be filed, unless it is made a part of the record by an order of court, or by the bill of exceptions, it cannot be considered, although copied into the record. Horstman v. C. & L. R. R. Co., 18 B. M. 218; 7 Bush 474; Johnson v. Miller, 12 R. 82, 13 S. W. 879; Sutton v. Pollard; 13 R. 85, 16 S. W. 126; MeCain v. L. & N. R. R., 13 R. 809, 18 S. W. 537; Stafford v. Dyer, 19 R. 155, 39 S. W. 708; Dehoney v. Bell, 17 R. 76, 30 S. W. 271.

(25a) It is the better practice to include the stenographer's transcript in the bill of exceptions, yet the bill of exceptions may be prepared in the narrative form instead of a transcript of the evidence.

# Trial-Exceptions

# § 336 [866] [Entry of exception on record-when sufficient.]

If the decision objected to be entered on the record, and the grounds of objection appear in the entry, the exception may be taken by the party causing to be noted, at the end of the decision, that he excepts.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 65.

# § 337 [867] [Bill of exceptions--time to prepare -bystander's bill.]

1. [Bill necessary unless exception appears on record.] If the decision be not entered on the record, or the grounds of objection do not appear in the entry, the party must prepare his bill of exceptions and present it to the judge for his signature.
2. [Bill of exceptions—time to prepare-what it must show.] Exceptions taken during the trial need not be noted of record, nor reduced to writing, unless by order of court, until after the trial. [During the term at which the judgment becomes final] the party excepting shall, unless further time be given him, prepare his bill of exceptions, which shall include all the decisions of the court excepted to, in consecutive order; and, if he except to a decision of the court in granting or refusing any instruction, all the instructions given and refused shall be also included. (Words in brackets inserted by Act of February 27, 1878, c. 263, p. 24. Form of bill, Forms 182-186.)

U. S. Fidelity & Guaranty Co. v. Travelers' Ins. Mach. Co., 167 Ky. 382, 180 S. W. 815.

# (26) Writings -how made part of record.

Deeds, wills and other writings introduced as evidence should be made part of bill by entry on order book. Baker v. Gilbert, 4 R. 621; or be copied into the bill of exceptions, and it should show that they were read as evidence. Vaughn v. Mills, 18 B. M. 633; Haney v. Tempest, 3 Met. 95; Duncan v. Brown, 15 B. M. 186; Shannon v. Stratton, 144 Ky. 26, 137 S. W. 850; see sec. 128 and note thereto, as to when writings filed to be relied on as evidence become part of the record.

# (27) Bill--how to be prepared-stenographer's transcript-instructions.

See full cases of Sinclair v. L. & N. R. Co., 100 S. W. 236. 30 R. 1040; Postal Tel. Co. v. Louisville Cotton Oil Co., 122 S. W. 852, 136 Ky. 843.

# (28) Equity cases.

When oral evidence is heard in an equity case, the evidence must be made a part of the record in the same time and manner as in ordinary cases. Dupoyster v. Fort Jeff. Imp. Co., 89 S. W. 509, 28 R. 504, 121 Ky. 518. Shannon v. Stratton, 144 Ky. 26, 137 S. W. 850; Roemer v. Mottley, 161 Ky. 316, 170 S. W. 649.

# (2) When the judge who presides at trial presides when motion for new trial is disposed of, a bystander's bill cannot be prepared as provided in subsec. 5.

# (3) Bill of exceptions signed by bystanders will not be considered where it is apparent that if bill had been prepared in time judge would have signed it.

# Title IX

# Trial--Exceptions.

# 3. [Bystander's bill if judge refuse to sign.]

If the bill of exceptions be approved by the judge, he shall sign it, and it shall be filed as part of the record, but not spread at large on the order book. If not approved, he shall correct it, or suggest the correction to be made, and sign it. A party objecting to the judge's correction of an exception which purports to state the evidence may, within five days after the bill is signed, file the exception as written by him, if its truth be attested by the affidavits of two bystanders; but its truth may be controverted and maintained by other affidavits filed in the clerk's office, not exceeding five on either side.

# 4. [Affidavits in case of bystander's bill.]

Affidavits controverting an exception not signed by the judge must be filed in the clerk's office, and notice of the filing given, within fifteen days after the filing of such exceptions; and affidavits sustaining such exception must be filed within fifteen days after such notice.

# 5. [Bystander's bill if trial judge absent.]

If the judge who presided at the trial do not preside when a motion for a new trial is overruled, the bill of exceptions may be certified by bystanders, and be controverted and maintained, pursuant to the provisions of subsections 3 and 4 of this section.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 44, 65, 67, 70, 75.

In this case preparation of bill was delayed from time to time for six months. Schneider v. Hesse, 11 R. 433, 12 S. W. 271. Where the trial judge refuses to sign any bill of exceptions the appellant may make up a bill and have it certified by bystanders. Com. v. Hourigan, 89 Ky. 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550.

(5d) In the opinion in this case, it is said that if the trial judge arbitrarily refuses to permit counsel to make a part of the bill of exceptions his timely objections or exceptions to an action or ruling, the proper course is to prepare a bystander's bill and file it in the Court of Appeals as part of the record. (Subsec. 3 Carter Coal Co. v. Love, 173 Ky. 49, 190 S. W. 481.

(5a) Bills of exceptions attested by bystanders, on account of the death of the judge before expiration of the time for settling the bills, were held sufficiently attested by four disinterested bystanders. U. S. Fidelity & Guaranty Co. v. Travelers' Ins. Mach. Co., 167 Ky. 382, 180 S. W. 815.

(5b) A bystander's bill was authorized when the judge who presided at the trial died after hearing a motion for a new trial before the time for filing a bill of exceptions. Co. Bd. of Ed. of Mercer Co. v. Rankin, 160 Ky. 352, 172 S. W. 1026.

(5c) A bystander's bill may be prepared and used in certain states of case if a transcript of testimony taken by the official stenographer cannot be procured within the time allowed. Helm v. Hoke Co., 173 Ky. 525, 191 S. W. 269.

(6) Judge must sign bill of exceptions or it will not be considered on appeal. Stanford v. Parker, 12 R. 878, 15 S. W. 784; 16 S. W. 268; Lou. Bridge Co. v. Neafus, 110 Ky. 571, 23 R. 183, "62 S. W. 2; 63 S. W. 600; and it must be signed by judge who tried case, regular judge cannot sign bill in case tried by special judge. L. S. R. R. v. Lewis, 101 Ky. 296, 19 R. 570, 41 S. W. 3.

(6a) A bill of exceptions signed and approved by the judge who tried the case and by his successor after the term of office of the judge who tried the case had expired was struck from the transcript.

# § 338 [868] [Errors not substantial disregarded.]

No exception shall be regarded, unless the decision to which it relates be prejudicial to the substantial rights of the party excepting. (Same provisions in secs. 134, 756.) Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 45.

# § 339. [Certificate of judge to bill-what it shall state.]

In cases in which, by subsection 2 of Section 335, the evidence is required to be stated in full, the judge shall certify in the bill of exceptions that it contains all the evidence. In all other cases he shall certify that the bill of exceptions is true. Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 71, 75.

City of Pikeville v. Dils, 175 Ky. 697, 194 S. W. 918.

(10) Death of trial judge. Where the trial judge dies before signing bill of exceptions it can not be signed by his successor it must be certified by bystanders.

Pittsburg R. Co. v. Austin, 143 Ky. 70, 135 S. W. 412; Bradford v. Jones, 148 Ky. 50, 145 S. W. 1105; Board of Education v. Rankin, 134 S. W. 157, 142 Ky. 324.

A motion to strike the bill from the record was overruled. (Subsec. 5.) Sandy Valley & Elkhorn Ry. Co. v. Bentley, 175 Ky. 736, 194 S. W. 906.

(7) Time to prepare bill-see notes to sec. 334.

(8) Bystander's bill must be filed within five days. Asher v. Brashear, 90 S. W. 1060, 28 R. 1012; Smalling v. Shaw, 144 Ky. 458, 139 S. W. 779.

(8a) The extension of time to file a bill of exceptions to a day in the next term may be made at any time during the term at which the judgment becomes final. Carter Coal Co. v. Clouse, 163 Ky. 337, 173 S. W. 794.

(8b) Revivor, need of. One of several appellees died within the time given to the appellants to make a bill of exceptions. The bill of exceptions was properly tendered within the time allowed; it could not be made a part of the record until after the revivor of the action in the circuit court. It was proper to continue the case for that purpose. Gish Banking Co. v. Leachman's Admr. 159 Ky. 769, 169 S. W. 481.

(Successful party can not defeat right of unsuccessful party to have time allowed in which to prepare a bill of exceptions. Louisville Home Tel. Co. v. City of Lou., 117 S. W. 315, 130 Ky. 159.)

Title IX] New Trials. [§ 340

# ARTICLE 5.

# NEW TRIALS.

# § 340. New trial defined—causes for.

# 341. When not to be granted.

# § 342. Application for—when to be made.

# 343. Motion and written grounds-affidavits.

# § 344. Grounds discovered after term-practice-divorce cases.

# § 340 [seo] [New trial defined--causes for.]

A new trial is a re-examination in the same court of an issue of fact after a verdict by a jury or a decision by the court. The former verdict or decision may be vacated and a new trial be granted, on the application of the party aggrieved, for any of the following causes affecting materially his substantial rights. (See further provisions as to new trial, sec. 518.)

1. [Irregularity that prevents a fair trial.] Irregularity in the proceedings of the court, jury or prevailing party, or any order of the court, or abuse of discretion, by which the party was prevented from having a fair trial.
2. [Misconduct of jury or party.] Misconduct of jury, of the prevailing party, or of his attorney.
3. [Accident or surprise.] Accident or surprise which ordinary prudence could not have guarded against.

# § 340. (1) Accident-surprise.

Known defenses must be pleaded, and the court will not grant a new trial to allow parties to avail themselves of a defense known to them when judgment was rendered. Dickinson v. Trout, 8 Bush 441. See further, sec. 518 and notes thereto.

# (2)

Although one of the appellant's counsel was prevented by sickness from attending the trial, if there was a lack of diligence on the part of appellant in not attending the trial, or providing other counsel, he will not be heard to complain. Landrum v. Farmer, 7 Bush 46. As to unavoidable absence of attorney, see further, Triplett v. Scott, 5 Bush 81; of party, see Yowell v. Gaines, 2 Bush 211.

# (3)

Mistake of counsel in the preparation of a case, or as to its standing on docket resulting in a judgment against a party is not such "casualty or misfortune" as will authorize a new trial. Prather v. Campbell, 110 Ky. 23, 22 R. 1510, 60 S. W. 918; 6 Bush 662; and see notes to sec. 518.

# (4)

After judgment by default has been rendered, if the defendant desires a new trial on account of accident or surprise, he should tender an answer presenting a good defense. Hayman v. Hallam, 79 Ky. 389, 2 R. 419; Prather v. Campbell, 110 Ky. 23, 22 R. 1510, 60 S. W. 918.

# (5)

When a party seeks to have a judgment set aside he should show that he has not been guilty of any lack of diligence. Alexander v. Lewis, 1 Met. 407. "When a party or his counsel are taken by surprise, whether by accident or fraud, on a material point or circumstance which could not reasonably have been anticipated, and where want of skill, care or attention cannot be justly imputed, and injustice has been done, a new trial will be granted." MeCall v. Hitchcock, 9 Bush 66; L. & N. R. R. v. Bickell, 67 Ky. 222, 17 R. 107, 30 S. W. 600; Kirk v. Grover, 96 S. W. 824, 29 R. 1047.

# (6)

The fact that a witness testifies contrary to what he did on a former trial...

# New Trials.

1. Excessive damages. Excessive damages, appearing to have been given under the influence of passion or prejudice.
2. Error in assessment of amount of recovery. Error in the assessment of the amount of recovery, whether too large or too small, in an action upon a contract, or for the injury or detention of property.
3. Verdict contrary to evidence or law. That the verdict or decision is not sustained by sufficient evidence, or is contrary to law.
4. Newly discovered evidence. Newly discovered evidence, material for the party applying, which he could not, with reasonable diligence, have discovered and produced at the trial.
5. Errors of law occurring at trial. Error of law occurring at the trial and excepted to by the party making the application. (Form of motion and grounds, Form 180.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 709; Form No. 458.

See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 46, 51, 54, 57.

is not ground for a new trial. Ivers v. Avery. 6 R. 220; but in L. & N. R. R. v. Bickel, 97 Ky. 222, 17 R. 107, 30 S. W. 600, it was held that a new trial was proper where a party was absent at second trial because misled by testimony of adverse party in a former trial and changed on second trial; and see McFarland v. Clark, 9 Dana 134.

(7) Where an affidavit is read as the testimony of an absent witness it is not cause for new trial that after trial was over it was learned that the witness if present would have made statements different from his affidavit. Gibson v. Sutton, 24 R. 868, 70 S. W. 188; Lou. R. Co. v. DeGore, 27 R. 54, 84 S. W. 326.

(8) When a party institutes an ordinary action it is his duty to exercise ordinary diligence to inform himself when it is set for trial and to be in attendance, and neither accident nor surprise is cause for setting aside judgment in the absence of such diligence. Ross v. L. & N. R. R., 92 Ky. 583, 13 R. 801, 18 S. W. 456; and see Chaffin v. Fulkerson, 95 Ky. 277, 15 R. 635, 24 S. W. 1066.

(9) A party who has prepared his case for trial, but is prevented from attending by the sudden and serious illness of his child or himself, is entitled to a new trial. Steel v. Seale, 4 R. 42; Langdon Creasy Co. v. Rouse, 24 R. 2095, 72 S. W. 113; Ray v. Arnett, 106 S. W. $26, 32 R. 562; and so where the defendant is absent because he has entered into an agreement to arbitrate the matters in controversy. Perry v. Fisher, 19 R. 1733, 44 S. W. 378.

(13) A deed was not properly indexed. Failure to discover it and produce it at trial.

# Title IX

# New Trials

The trial was excusable. A new trial was granted upon the discovery of the deed. Smith v. Chapman, 153 Ky. 70, 154 S. W. 915.

(13b) Certain facts (including a mistake of a stenographer of defendant's attorney) constituted an accident which ordinary prudence could not have guarded against. Bentley v. Ben Williamson Hdw. Co., 177 Ky. 799, 198 S. W. 232.

(13c) A new trial will not be granted on the ground of surprise, unless the party surprised ask that the swearing of the jury be set aside and the trial postponed. The Liverpool & L. & Globe Ins. Co. v. Wright & Allen; Old Colony Ins. Co. v. Same; Citizens F. Ins. Co. v. Same; People's Nat. F. Ins. Co. v. Same, 158 Ky. 290, 164 S. W. 952.

(13d) A defendant failed to ask for a continuance because of surprise at the statement of a witness: a new trial was properly refused. Howard v. Strawbridge & Clothier, 165 Ky. 88, 176 S. W. 977.

(13e) The inability of the stenographer, who had taken down the evidence at the trial, to transcribe his notes is not good ground for the granting of a new trial. Crouch v. O'Banion, 163 Ky. 581, 174 S. W. 3.

(13f) Appellant moved for a new trial on the ground that it was surprised because its witness failed to testify as she indicated she would before the trial commenced. Held, "While there might be cases in which this character of surprise would be ground for a new trial, this is not one of them." City of Louisville v. Lee, 157 Ky. 285, 162 S. W. 1141.

(13g) Agreement of parties will not authorize new trial. A new trial will not be granted simply because both parties agree that a new trial shall be granted. John B. Carter Co. v. Cox, 159 Ky. 710, 169 S. W. 472.

(13h) The default of defendant's attorney to answer within the time required was held excusable neglect where the attorney did not know of a change in the terms of court made by a Session Act subsequent to the last compilation of the statutes, which act was not indexed. Callahan Const. Co. v. Thomas, 160 Ky. 496, 169 S. W. 828.

(14) Cross appeal will not be considered in absence of a motion and grounds for new trial. L. & N. R. R. v. Whitehead, 24 R. 2315, 73 S. W. 1128.

(15) Discretion of court in granting a new trial will be interfered with only in exceptional cases. C. & O. R. R. v. Hickey, 15 R. 112, 22 S. W. 441; Ivers v. Avery, 6 R. 220; Hurt v. L. & N. R. Co., 116 Ky. 545, 25 R. 755, 76 S. W. 502; Taylor & Sons v. Lou. Pub. W. H. Co., 24 R. 1656, 72 S. W. 20; Brown v. L. & N. R. Co., 144 Ky. 546, 139 S. W. 782; and only when there is a flagrant abuse of discretion in granting a new trial will the court reverse second judgment and direct that first judgment be entered. L. & N. R. R. v. Coniff, 16 R. 296, 27 S. W. 865; Couadeau v. Am. Accident Co., 95 Ky. 280, 15 R. 667, 25 S. W. 6; Caldwell v. Bright, 8 B. M. 525; Miller v. Ashcraft, 98 Ky. 314, 17 R. 894, 32 S. W. 1085.

In the following cases the Court of Appeals held that new trials had been erroneously granted and directed that former judgment set aside by lower court be entered. Rinchards v. L. & N. R. R., 20 R. 1478, 49 S. W. 419; L. & N. R. R. v. Ricketts, 21 R. 662, 52 S. W. 939; Crowley v. L. & N. R. R., 21 R. 1434, 55 S. W. 434.

(15a) The Court of Appeals refused to reverse a judgment rendered on a verdict on a new trial, granted because of excessive damages. Snyder v. Louisville Ry. Co., 150 Ky. 816, 150 S. W. 980.

(15b) The Court of Appeals will not reverse because the court below granted a new trial unless there was an abuse of discretion. Nantz v. Sizenore, 156 Ky. 539, 161 S. W. 552.

(15c) A judgment will not be reversed by the Court of Appeals because of the granting of a new trial below unless such granting was an abuse of discretion. Chenoa-Hignite Coal Land Co. v. Philpot's Admr., 152 Ky. 385, 153 S. W. 457; Conley v. Cent. Ky. Trac. Co., 152 Ky. 764, 154 S. W. 41; Pack v. Camden Interstate Ry. Co., 154 Ky. 535, 157 S. W. 906.

(15d) The denial of permission to call witnesses and examine them orally under an application for a new trial is not an abuse of discretion. Smith's Admx. v. Middlesboro Elec. Co., 164 Ky. 46, 174 S. W. 773.

(15e) A judgment setting aside a verdict and granting a new trial will not be reversed unless it appears that there was an abuse of discretion on the part of the trial court. Thus, where there are several grounds for a new trial, one of which is insufficient, and it does not appear from the record that the trial court, in awarding the new trial, acted upon the insufficient ground, but may have awarded the new trial upon other grounds which were sufficient, the order granting the new trial will not be set aside. Norvell v. Paducah Box & Basket Co., 157 Ky. 703, 163 S. W. 1106.

# § 340 New Trials.

# (10) Equity action-motion for new trial not necessary in.

MeCormick Mch. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Hoskins' Amdr., 164 Ky. 575, 176 S. W. 29.

Salyer v. Arnett, 23 R. 321, 62 S. W. 1031; List v. List, 26 R. 691, 82 S. W. 446.

# (17) Errors must be specified.

The grounds relied on for a new trial must be specifically set out. The general ground, "because of errors of law occurring at the trial and excepted to by the party making the application," or "that verdict is contrary to law," provided in subsecs. 6, 8, not sufficient. The specific error to which an exception was taken during the trial must be mentioned.

American Credit Co. v. National Clothing Co., 122 S. W. 840; McLain v. Dibble, 13 Bush 297; Slater v. Sherman, 5 Bush 206; Com. v. Williams, 14 Bush 297; 7 Bush 235; Ohio Valley Co. v. Kuhn, 9 R. 467, 5 S. W. 419; Jones v. Worden, 90 Ky. 230, 12 R. 105, 13 S. W. 911.

# (18) All that is necessary in any case.

is to use such plain and intelligible language in the grounds for new trial as indicates, points out or shows to the court, with reasonable and ordinary certainty, the particular errors which are complained of, so as to enable the court, by the exercise of proper attention, to understand what errors are meant, and to reconsider the facts or law out of which they are alleged to have grown.

L. & N. R. R. Co. v. MeCov, 81 Ky. 403, 5 R. 397; Meaux v. Meaux, 81 Ky. 475, 5 R. 548.

# (19) While the general statement that verdict is against law and evidence is not sufficient.

yet when the lower court has granted a new trial upon such a statement the court will not reverse the second judgment and direct first to be entered for the reason that the grounds upon which new trial was granted were insufficient.

L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Coniff 16 R. 296, 27 S. W. 865.

# (19a) Evidence, admission and rejection of.

The improper admission of books of a corporation to contradict a witness was ground for reversal. Louisville Ry. Co. v. Kritzky, 162 Ky. 652, 172 S. W. 1051.

# (19b) Errors in the rejection of evidence will not be considered on appeal.

unless embraced in the grounds for a new trial. Gooch v. Collins, 156 Ky. 282, 160 S. W. 1038.

# (19c) A minister of the gospel was killed by negligence of a railway company.

It was proper to refuse to admit testimony as to the amount of his salary as a minister. There was other evidence.

Lingar, 152 Ky. 87, 153 S. W. 6; Humble v. Humble, 152 Ky. 100, 153 S. W. 249; L. & N. R. Co. v. Miller, 154 Ky. 236, 157 S. W. 8; C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Reed, 154 Ky. 380, 157 S. W. 721; L. & N. R. Co. v. Parks' Admr, 154 Ky. 269, 157 S. W. 27; Bdwy. Coal Mining Co. v. Robinson, 150 Ky. 707, 150 S. W. 1000; Cent. Ky. Nat. G. Co. v. Salyer, 164 Ky. 718, 176 S. W. 183; L. & N.R. R. Co. v. Mitchell, 162 Ky. 253, 178 S. W. 527; L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Nethery, 160 Ky. 369, 169 S. W. 883; City of Cov. v. Westbay, 156 Ky. 839, 162 S. W. 91; Ky. Tr. & Ter. Co. v. Peel, 160 Ky. 239, 169 S. W. 689; Ky. & Tenn. Ry. Co. v. West, 160 Ky. 280, 169 S. W. 728; L. & I. Ry. Co. v. Roemmele, 157 Ky. 84, 162 S. W. 547; L. Ry. Co. v. Smock, 157 Ky. 11, 162 S. W. 546; Bd. of Coun., City of Frankfort, v. Kirby, 156 Ky. 741, 161 S. W. 1115; Big Branch Coal Co. v. Sanders, 159 Ky. 125, 166 S. W. 813; Ky. Midland Coal Co. v. Vincent, 159 Ky. 100, 166 S. W. 800; L., H. & St. L. Ry. Co. v. Armes, 158 Ky. 676, 166 S. W. 190; C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Jesse, 159 Ky. 450, 167 S. W. 407; C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Dwyer's Admx., 162 Ky. 427, 172 S. W. 918; New Bell Jellico Coal Co. v. Sowders, 162 Ky. 443, 172 S. W. 914; N. & W. Ry. Co. v. Thompson, 161 Ky. 814, 171 S. W. 451; Cumb. Tele. & Tele. Co. v. Laird, 161 Ky. 800, 171 S. W. 386; M. Livingston & Co. v. Philley, 155 Ky. 224, 159 S. W. 665; City of Newport v. Lewis, 155 Ky. 832, 160 S. W. 507; Compton v. Wilkins, 164 Ky. 634, 176 S. W. 36; L. & E. Ry. Co. v. Boatright, 164 Ky. 374, 175 S. W. 648; C. & O. Ry. Co. v. John's Admx., 155 Ky. 264, 159 S. W. 822; Ky. Trac. & Ter. Co. v. Wilson, 165 Ky. 123, 176 S. W. 991; Ky. Tr. & Ter. Co. v. Downing's Admr., 159 Ky. 502, 167 S. W. 683; Schmid's Admr. v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 155 Ky. 237, 159 S. W. 786; New Bell Jellico Coal Co. v. Oxendine, 155 Ky. 840, 160 S. W. 737; L. & E. Ry. Co. v. Crawford, 155 Ky. 723, 160 S. W. 267; St. Bernard M. Co. v. Ashby, 164 Ky. 416, 175 S. W. 620; Continental Coal Co. v. Cole's Admr., 155 Ky. 139, 159 S. W. 668.

(20e) An allowance for $1,200 as damages in certain condemnation proceedings was excessive. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Wilson, 165 Ky. 151, 176 S. W. 980.

(20f) In the following cases verdicts were held to be excessive: Benge's Admr. v. Fouts, Same v. Creech, 163 Ky. 796, 174 S. W. 510; Trosper Coal Co. v. Crawford, 152 Ky. 214, 153 S. W. 211; C., St. L. & N. O. R. Co. v. Rowell, 151 Ky. 313, 151 S. W. 950; So. Ry. Co. in Ky. v. Owen, 156 Ky. 827, 162 S. W. 110.

(20g) Where in an action on contract a part of the amount awarded is due to an erroneous instruction, the Court of Appeals may set aside so much as is assessed under the erroneous instruction and sustain the amount properly awarded. Sturgeon's Admr. v. MeCorkle, 163 Ky. 8, 173 S. W. 149.

(21) When a verdict is flagrantly against the evidence, the court cannot refuse a new trial because party remits part of judgment; but if error in amount recovered is caused by an erroneous instruction, so much of the judgment as was due to such instruction, if it can be ascertained, may be remitted and remainder allowed to stand. Merrick v. Holt, 7 R. 758; City of Iayton v. Gardner, 19 R. 302, 40 S. W. 779; and see Johnson v. Johnson, 104 Ky. 714, 20 R. 890, 47 S. W. 883; Masterson v. Hagan, 17 B. M. 325.

(22) In an action for malicious prosecution the plaintiff recovered a verdict for $4,000; the defendant filed grounds and moved for a new trial; the judge announced that he would not grant a new trial unless the plaintiff would accept $1,000; the plaintiff agreed to do this, and the motion for a new trial was overruled. The court says: In this instance the court virtually assessed the damages, and thereby deprived each party of his right to an assessment by the jury. This seems to be error to the prejudice of the defendant if he was entitled to a new trial, and to the plaintiff's prejudice if the defendant was not entitled to a new trial. Brown v. Morris, 3 Bush 81; L. & N. R. R. v. Earl, 94 Ky. 368, 15 R. 184, 22 S. W. 607; Merrick v. Holt, 7 R. 758; Johnson v. Johnson, 104 Ky. 714, 20 R. 890, 47 S. W. 883.

(20d) A judgment for $56,971.56 was not held excessive for damages resulting from injury to business inflicted by withholding accommodations furnished other shippers, and by imposing extortionate freight rates.

Note.—This judgment was reversed by the Supreme Court of the United States in December, 1916. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. ...

# New Trials

of the record when copied into the tran- script, and it is not necessary to include them in the bill of exceptions. McAllister v. Ins. Co., 78 Ky. 531; and see notes to sec. 342.

# (23a) Harmless error.

The admission of the opinions of non-expert witnesses as to the condition of an injured person will not be ground for a new trial where on the whole case it was not prejudicial. Louisville Ry. Co. v. Sweeney, 157 Ky. 620, 163 S. W. 739.

# (24) Improper argument

is ground for new trial and when it appears that the jury was unduly influenced by the improper argument it is cause for re- versal. McHenry Coal Co. v. Suedden, 98 Ky. 684, 34 S. W. 228, 17 R. 1261; L. & N. R. Co. v. Hull, 113 Ky. 501, 24 R. 375, 68 S. W. 433; and notes to sec. 317.

# (24a) The fact that appellee's counsel used certain language

will not be considered on appeal unless it be authenticated by a bill of exceptions; an affidavit as to the language used is insufficient. So. Ry. Co. of Ky. v. Thacker's Admr., 156 Ky. 483, 161 S. W. 236; Fish v. Welch's Admr., 156 Ky. 541, 161 S. W. 512.

# (24b) Improper questions.

The circuit court should have awarded a new trial because counsel for the successful party during the trial persisted in asking questions which had been adjudged incompetent by the trial court and which might influence the jury. Shield's Admr. v. Rowland, 151 Ky. 136, 151 S. W. 408.

# (24c) Improper statements.

A statement made by counsel in his argument may be of such a prejudicial nature that the trial court should set aside any verdict in favor of his client. The Liverpool & London & Globe Ins. Co. v. Wright & Allen; Old Colony Ins. Co. v. Same; Cit. F. Ins. Co. v. Same; People's Nat. F. Ins. Co. v. Same, 158 Ky. 290, 164 S. W. 952.

# (24d) A judgment was reversed

because of improper statements made in argument by counsel for successful party, which were not supported by the record. Kt. of Maccabees of the World v. Shields, 162 Ky. 392, 172 S. W. 696.

# (24e) The court having excluded a statement made by the attorney

and cautioned the jury to disregard it, a new trial will not be granted for this misconduct. Louisville Ry. Co. v. Sweeney, 157 Ky. 620, 163 S. W. 739.

# (25) Irregularity of judge, jury or party.

After the jury had retired for consultation, one of the witnesses, who had been examined on the trial, was sent for by them, admitted into the jury room and re-examined without the knowledge of the court or parties. Held that, although he was the witness of the party seeking the reversal, the misconduct of the jury authorized a new trial. Lutrell v. M. & L. R. R. Co., 18 B. M. 291; and see 3 Mon. 411.

# (26) That juror was on jury that had tried the case at a former term

is not cause for a new trial. The objection to him should have been made before the jury was sworn, or before the jury had retired, if not discovered before that time. Fitzpatrick v. Harris, 16 B. M. 561; Throckmorton v. Com., 20 R. 1508, 49 S. W. 474; Taylor v. Combs, 20 R. 1828, 50 S. W. 64; and although the jury, or some of them, had been guilty of improper conduct, the verdict will not be set aside unless it appears that the successful party, or some one in his behalf, was a party to it. Drake v. Drake, 107 Ky. 32, 21 R. 636, 52 S. W. 846. If the complaining party knows of the misconduct of a juror, he must make the objection at the time, or his right to do so will be waived. Drake v. Drake, 107 Ky. 32, 21 R. 636, 52 S. W. 846.

# (27) When a juror during the trial expresses an opinion

as to how case should be decided it is ground for new trial for the party against whose interest the remark is made. Rice v. Wyatt, 25 R. 1060, 76 S. W. 1087.

# (28) New trial will not be granted because of relationship of juror to party

when the relation is remote. Rice v. Wyatt, 25 R. 1060, 76 S. W. 1087; Northcut v. Jouett, 18 R. 327, 36 S. W. 179; or is not known. Miraele v. Com., 148 Ky. 453, 146 S. W. 1136.

# (28a) The mere fact that a juror in a civil case had served as juror upon a former trial of the case

does not of itself require the verdict to be set aside. McKee v. C., F. & S. R. R. Co., 161 Ky. 711, 171 S. W. 425.

# (29) Affidavits of jurors can not be received

to impeach their verdict for misbehavior in themselves or their fellow jurors. Allard v. Smith, 2 Met. 297; as to when jurors will be allowed to explain their verdict or correct mistake in it, see Alexander v. Hunber, 86 Ky. 505, 9 R. 734, 6 S. W. 453; Johnson v. Davenport, 3 J. J. M. 390; Eversole v. White 112 Ky. 193, 23 R. 1435, 65 S. W. 442.

# (30) An affidavit of a juror is not competent evidence

to show that the verdict was the result of an agreement that such a verdict should be rendered as was favored by a majority of the jury. Lucas

Title IX] New Trials. [§ 340
v. Cannon, 13 Bush 650; 3 Mon. 411; and see Cain v. Cain, 1 B. M. 214, as to when partiality of juror is grounds for new trial

(31) It is a well-settled principle, applicable to the conduct of all juries, that the testimony of the jurors is not competent to explain the grounds of their decision, or to impeach the validity of their findings. Coin. v. Skeggs, 3 Bush 19; Johnson v. Davenport, 3 J. J. M. 390; but is competent to sustain the verdict and show there was no misconduct. Howard v. Com., 24 R. 612, 69 S. W. 721; Gleason v. Com., 145 Kv. 128. 140 S. W. 63, and see 149, Ky. 632.

(32) Where the defendant, in passing near one of the jury during the trial, said in his hearing, "Don't hang," it was an insult to the juror, a contempt of court and cause for a new trial. Campbell v. Bannister. 79 Ky. 205, 2 Ki. 72; and so where, during a recess of the court, a witness related to the defendant and the defendant explained to the jury a point in the case. Wooldridge v. White, 20 R. 1144, 48 S. W. 1081.

(33) As to correspondence between the judge and jury, after they had retired for consultation, concerning a point at issue in the case, see Cloode v. Campbell. 14 Bush 75; as to improper statement by judge to jury to induce them to make a verdict, see Randolph v. Lampkin, 90 Kv. 551, 12 R. 517, 14 S. W. 538; Asher Lumber Co. v. Lunsiord, 17 R. 559, 30 S. W. 968; City v. Bestwick, 26 R. 780, 82 S. W. 569.

(33a) Certain irregularities in empaneling a jury were held grounds for reversal of a judgment. L. & N. R. R. Co. and Curt Jones v. Owens; Saine v. Parsons, 164 Ky. 557, 175 S. W. 1039.

(33b) In this case the limitation of an argument to ten minutes was held reversible error. Murphy v. Ray, 161 Kv. 384, 170 S. W. 946.

(33c) A bystander was summoned on the jury by a name differing from his own. The fact that the party who was defeated in the action would not have accepted him had it not been misled by the misnomer was not ground for a new trial Louisville Ry. Co. v. Smock, 157 Ky. 11, 162 S. W. 546.

(33d) The affidavit of a juror is not competent to prove misconduct of the jury. Smith's Admx. v. Middlesboro E Co., 164 Ky. 46, 174 S. W. 773.

(33e) In this case certain language had been improperly used by counsel for plaintiff in argument, and the court admonished the jury to disregard the same. It was not error for the court to overrule defendant's motion to discharge the jury. Glasgow E. L. & Ice Co. v. Clark's Admx., 158 Ky. 734, 166 S. W. 214.

(34) A party who failed to make defense because the adverse party told him case was settled is entitled to a new trial. Head v. Ayer, 24 R. 728, 70 S. W. 55.

(34a) The fact that plaintiff had said to defendant that he should like to settle the matter out of court was not sufficient reason for a failure on the part of the defendant to make defense. Stamper v. Forman-Earle Co., 158 Ky. 324, 164 S. W. 937.

(35) Instructions. "Grounds which merely state that the court erred in giving or refusing instructions is sufficient to raise any and all questions upon this point. Helfrich v. Éverly, 17 R. 795, 32 S. W. 750; American Ins. Co. v. Austin, 18 R. 632, 37 S. W. 678; Meaux v. Meaux, 81 Ky. 475, 5 R. 548; L. & N. R. R. v. McCov, 81 Kv. 403, 5 R. 397; but where the party expressly indicates the giving or refusal of certain instructions as error and names none others, either expressly or in a general way, he will be confined to them. Johns v. L. & N. R. R., 10 R. 757, 10 S. W. 417; Bland v. Caither, 10 R. 1033, 11 S. W. 423; and if no complaint is made about instructions in motion they will not be considered. Lawson v. Lightfoot, 27 R. 217, 84 S. W. 739.

(36) Where one ground for new trial was that court erred in giving certain designated instructions, and another ground was that court erred in giving an instruction as to compensatory damages, this latter instruction can be considered on appeal, although it was omitted on first ground. L. & N. R. R. v. Morris, 14 R. 406, 20 S. W. 539.

(36a) Errors in instructions will be waived unless objections and exceptions are taken to them in the trial court. Sword v. Bowen, 157 Ky. 615, 163 S. W. 733.

(36b) Where no objection was offered to an instruction at the time it was given, complaint of it upon appeal cannot be considered. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Kelley, 157 Ky. 724, 163 S. W. 1088.

(36c) An instruction not complained of in the grounds for a new trial can not be complained of on appeal. Asher v. Metcalf, 152 Ky. 632, 153 S. W. 987.

(36d) A judgment was reversed because of an erroneous instruction, although the appellant did not ask a correct instruction on the same point.

# New Trials

§ 340] New Trials. [Title II

& E. Ry. Co. v. Crawford, 155 Ky. 723, 160 S. W. 267.

(36e) Negligence is a question for the court, where the facts are admitted or are established by undisputed testimony. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Cooper, 164 Ky. 489, 175 S. W. 1034.

(36f) An instruction requiring only ordinary care of a street railway company maintaining in the public street a trolley carrying a high power of electricity was erroneous. Owensboro City R. R. Co. v. HaDEn; L., H. & St. L. Ry. Co. v. Same, 155 Ky. 283, 159 S. W. 792.

# (37) Motion for new trial-purpose and effect of.

Neither a motion for a new trial nor bill of exceptions is necessary to enable the unsuccessful party to maintain an appeal. The only office of a motion for a new trial and a bill of exceptions is to bring before the court for revision errors occurring during the trial which would not otherwise appear. When an appeal is prosecuted from a judgment on a verdict, or the judgment of the court when the law and facts are submitted to it, without a motion for a new trial and a bill of exceptions, nothing is before the court, except the pleadings, verdict and judgment. Harper v. Harper, 10 Bush 447; McAllister v. Ins. Co., 78 Ky. 531, 80 Ky. 176, 3 R. 677, 82 Ky. 678, 6 R. 702; Owensboro R. R. v. Barker, 15 R. 175, 22 S. W. 444; nor is motion for necessary when pleadings are not sufficient to support verdict. Owensboro R. Co. v. Barker, 15 R. 175, 22 S. W. 444.

(37a) An alleged error on a trial cannot be reviewed unless it be made a ground for a new trial. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Culbertson, 159 Ky. 561, 165 S. W. 681.

# (38) Motion for-suspends judgment.

A motion for a new trial suspends the judgment, and may be continued and passed on at the subsequent term. But a motion to set aside an order overruling a motion for a new trial does not suspend the judgment. There is no authority for a motion to rehear a motion for a new trial. Louisville R. & L. Co. v. Kerr, 78 Ky. 12; Harper v. Harper, 10 Bush 447; Turner v. Johnson, 18 R. 202, 35 S. W. 923.

# (39) Newly discovered evidence.

The general rule governing applications for new trials upon the ground of newly discovered evidence are: 1. That 'the names of the witnesses who have been discovered' must appear. 2. That the party has been vigilant in preparing his case for trial. 3. That the new facts were discovered after the trial and would be important. 4. That the evidence discovered will tend to prove facts which were not directly in issue on the trial or were not then known, nor investigated by the proof. 5. That the new evidence is not merely cumulative. Price v. Thompson, 84 Ky. 219, 8 R. 201, 1 S. W. 408; Cahill v. Mullins, 101 S. W. 336, 31 R. 72; I. C. R. Co. v. Wilson, 103 S. W. 364, 31 R. 793.

(40) The fact that a party does not know of the evidence by which he can establish his defense does not excuse him from presenting the defense. A defense of which the defendant must have had knowledge, if it was true, not relied on, or presented on the trial, cannot be made available on a motion for a new trial. Mason v. Mason, 5 Bush 187.

(41) Where a party discovers testimony, even after the argument has commenced, and it can then be given, it is his duty to ask that it be heard, and if he fail to offer it, it is no ground for a new trial; nor can a party get a new trial on the ground that he has discovered testimony that would strengthen his case. Fleet v. Hollenkemp, 13 B. M. 220.

(42) Cumulative evidence is not sufficient to authorize a new trial. Mason v. Mason, 5 Bush 187; Allen v. Perry, 6 Bush 85; Bell v. Offut, 10 Bush 632; nor is evidence that only tends to discredit or impeach an opposing witness. L. & N. R. R. v. Tinkham, 19 R. 1784, 44 S. W. 439; L. & N. R. Co. v. Ueltschi, 104 S. W. 320, 31 R. 933, 126 Ky. 556.

(42a) The circuit court rightly refused to grant a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence, where this evidence was only corroborative. Mulloy v. City of Louisville, 161 Ky. 596, 171 S. W. 190.

(42b) A new trial in a proceeding will not be granted for merely cumulative evidence discrediting a witness. In re T. A. McDonald, 157 Ky. 92, 162 S. W. 566.

# (43) When the ground for a new trial is newly discovered evidence, affidavit of newly discovered witness must be filed to sustain it.

Slone v. Slone, 2 Met. 339; Bowling v. Com., 148 Ky. 9, 145 S. W. 1126; American Central Ins. Co. v. Hardin, 148 Ky. 246, 146 S. W. 418.

(43a) A judgment was reversed for failure of the circuit court to grant a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence. Goddard v. Latta, 152 Ky. 538, 153 S. W. 737.

(43b) A new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence will not be granted unless the evidence be of such a

Title [X] New Trials.

character as to render a different result hart v. Stalzenberger, 7 Bush 209; Rice reasonably certain. Weaks v. McDowell v. ClaRK, 25 R. 1060, 76 S. W. 1087; Const. Co. and L. & N. R. Co., 158 Ky. 691, 156 S. W. 127; S. Cov. & C. St. Ry. Co. v. Lee, 153 Ky. 621, 156 S. W. 99.

# (44)

To entitle a party to a new trial on the grounds of newly discovered evidence, it must not only have been discovered too late to have been used upon the trial, but must have been of such a character or so concealed that it could not have been previously discovered by the use of reasonable diligence. The discovery of a deed on record in the proper office will not entitle a party to a new trial. Denny v. Wickliffe, 1 Met. 216; Howton v. Roberts, 20 R. 1331, 49 S. W. 340; nor will the fact that a party did not know of a city ordinance. 3 Bush 703, 7 Bush 308; but if the failure to find a deed or record is due to the fact that it is not on index a new trial should be granted. Elliott v. Harris, 81 Ky. 470, 5 R. 499; Hall v. Com., 17 R. 231, 30 S. W. 877.

# (49)

The newly discovered evidence that will authorize a new trial is not confined to the evidence that may be given by living witnesses, but is any character of evidence that is admissible to illustrate the point in issue. L. & N. R. R. v. Whitley Co., 100 Ky. 413, 18 R. 868, 38 S. W. 678.

# (49a)

On an application for a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence or of misconduct of the jury, oral testimony may be heard. Barnes v. Com., 179 Ky. 725, 201 S. W. 318.

# (49b)

Certain newly discovered evidence justified the granting of a new trial in an action against a surgeon for malpractice. Mason, Evans & Keys v. Meloan, 165 Ky. 582, 177 S. W. 435.

# (49c)

When newly discovered evidence is of such a permanent and unerring character as to preponderate greatly, a new trial will be granted. Dunn v. Blue Gr. Realty Co., 163 Ky. 384, 173 S. W. 1122.

# (49d)

Under this section plaintiff was held not entitled to a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence, where no effort was made to discover the facts sought to be established. J. D. Hughes Lumber Co. v. Knuckles, 168 Ky. 29, 181 S. W. 935.

# (49e)

A motion for a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence which was practically decisive of the case should have been sustained. (Subsec. 7.) C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Cecil, 164 Ky. 377, 175 S. W. 654.

# (50)

New trial on payment of costs. The Ky. Stat., sec. 899, provides that a new trial may be granted upon condition that the costs of former trial are paid by the party applying therefor within forty days. But the court is vested with a sound legal discretion in each case, and may refuse to set aside the order granting a new trial, although the costs are not paid within forty days. The time should not be less than forty days. Myers v. Iummis, 80 Ky. 456, 4 R. 301; and see Mattingly v. L. & N. R. R. Ky. 463, 13 R. 666, 25 S. W. 830; Galbraith v. Galbraith, 5 R. 859.

# (51)

The court can not order all costs to be paid, but only costs of former trial, but when court granted a new trial upon payment of all costs, the party should have offered to pay costs of former trial. Carbon v. Stout, 7 Bush 609;

# § 340] New Trials.

and when a new trial is granted because of error of the court in instructing the jury over the objection of a party, he should not, when granted a new trial, be required to pay any costs. Ullman v. Abraham, 9 Bush 738.

- (52) Peremptory instruction. Motion for new trial necessary. Kistler v. Slaughter, 20 R. 1937, 50 S. W. 529; Con. v. Standard Oil Co., 112 S. W. 632, 129 Ky. 546; Witt v. L. & E. Ry. Co., 158 Ky. 401, 165 S. W. 399.
- (53) Practice-the proper method of bringing to Court of Appeals a case to correct errors of court during trial is by appeal and not by petition for a new trial. Beckham v. Morrison, 14 R. 241, 20 S. W. 197.
- (54) After getting a new trial, the party obtaining can not appeal from the original judgment. McCall v. Hitchcock, 9 Bush 66.
- (55) Order granting a new trial is a final order and court has no control over it after expiration of term. Snyder v. Cox, 21 R. 796, 53 S. W. 263; Williams v. Williams, 107 Ky. 496; 21 R. 1208, 54 S. W. 716.
- (56) Provisional remedies-motion for new trial not necessary in. Francis v. Burnett, 84 Ky. 23, 7 R. 715; Crouch v. The Meguiar-Harris Co., 19 R. 819, 42 S. W. 91.
- (57) Reversal with directions to enter judgment set aside. The Court of Appeals may, when a new trial has been granted and there is an appeal from the second judgment, direct the entry of first judgment if it has been erroneously set aside. Couadeau v. American Accident Co., 95 Kv. 280, 15 R. 667, 25 S. W. 6; L. & N. R. Co. v. Ricketts, 21 R. 662, 52 S. W. 939; Crowley v. L. & N. R. Co., 21 R. 1434, 55 S. W. 434; Brown v. L. & N. R. Co., 144 Ky. 546, 139 S. W. 782; Ross v. Kohler. 163 Ky. 583, 174 S. W. 36, and see note 15.
- (57a) A new trial was granted and an appeal was taken from the verdict entered upon the second trial. The ground for sustaining the motion for a new trial did not appear. The court refused to reinstate the former verdict. Greenberg v. Hyman & Oppenheim, 159 Ky. 618, 167 S. W. 914.
- (58) Tort. In action for tort against several, where a joint verdict is returned a new trial may be had as to some, and the full amount of judgment left standing against the others. Loving v. Coin., 103 Ky. 534, 20 R. 229, 45 S. W. 773; Lou. S. R. R. v. Tucker, 105 Ky. 492, 20 R. 1303, 49 S. W. 314.
- (59) Trial by court--motion for new trial. In a common law action, where the law and facts are submitted to the court, a motion and grounds for a new trial made within three days are necessary in order to a review by the Appellate Court of any alleged errors committed during the trial. In the absence of a motion and grounds for a new trial, nothing is before the court except the inquiry as to whether the pleadings state any cause of action or defense, and whether the evidence authorizes the judgment. Helm v. Cofey, 80 Ky. 176, 3 R. 677; Henderson v. Dupree, 82 Ky. 678, 6 R. 702; Harper v. Harper, 10 Bush 447; Jenne v. Matlack, 19 R. 503, 41 S. W. 11; The Albin Co. v. Ellinger, 103 Ky. 240, 19 R. 1886, 44 S. W. 655; and this rule applies, although the conclusions of law and fact are separately stated. 82 Ky. 678, 6 R. 702; and see notes to sec. 331, 332.
- (60) Verdict not sustained by evidence. Verdict will not be disturbed because not sustained by the evidence, unless it is clearly and palpably against the weight of it. Thompson v. Thompson, 93 Ky. 435, 14 R. 513, 20 S. W. 373; Urso v. Unverzagt, 2 R. 228; McClain v. Esham, 17 B. M. 146; L. &. N. R. R. Co. v. Graves, 78 Ky. 74; Bell v. Keach, 80 Ky. 42, 3 R. 520; Kincaid v. Bull, 159 Ky. 527, 167 S. W. 903; L. & N. R. R. Co. v. McArthur, 163 Ky. 291, 173 S. W. 770; Stewart & Whitesides v. Mackin, 159 Ky. 727, 169 S. W. 469; Denker Transfer Co. v. Pugh. 162 Ky. 818, 173 S. W. 139; Adams Express Co. v. Tucker, 161 Ky. 741, 171 S.W. 428.
- (61) If the plaintiff fails to set out a good cause of action, and the defect is not cured by answer (Fible v. Caplinger, 13 B. M. 464); or if the plaintiff shows no right to sue, a verdict for the plaintiff should be set aside. Petty v. Malier. 14 B. M, 246; and see sec. 386 and notes thereto as to when judgment may be rendered against verdict.
- (6la) If a verdict be contrary to the instruction given by the court a new trial should be granted, whether the instructions were or were not erroneous. Yellow Poplar Lumber Co. v. Bartley. 164 Ky. 763, 176 S. W. 201; St. P. F. & M. Ins. Co. v. Kendle, 163 Ky. 146, 173 S. W. 373.
- (6lb) In an action against a railroad company for damages resulting from fire alleged to have been caused by the emission of sparks and cinders from its locomotive by reason of failure to provide a proper spark arrester, a verdict
