based wholly upon circumstantial evidence in favor of the plaintiff may be sustained. T. P. Ry. Co. v. Hill, 161 Ky. 237, 170 S. W. 599; Aetna L. Ins. Co. v. Sullivan, 9 Ky. Opinions 43.

(61) A new trial may be granted under subsec. 6, although there was evidence sufficient to take the case to the jury. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Johnson, 151 Ky. 809, 152 S. W. 962.

(62) Appeal from order granting new trial may be prosecuted to Court of Appeals. Brown v. L. & N. R. Co., 1H Ky. 546, 139 S. W. 782.

(63) County court—new trial in county court is regulated by these provisions of the Code unless controlled by other sections or statutes. Broadway C. M. Co. v. Smith, 136 Ky. 725, 125 S. W. 157.

(64) Alimony - new trial may be granted in action for. Sebastian v. Rose, 122 S. W. 120, 135 Ky. 197.

(65) Waiver of right to have a party must act promptly if he is taken by surprise during the trial. He will not be allowed to take the chance of getting a verdict and then if he loses to demand a new trial. Lesser v. Jefferson Fire Ins. Co., 141 Ky. 667, 133 S. W. 551; Louisville Ry. Co. v. Masterson, 96 S. W. 534, 29 R. 829.

(66) Verdict is contrary to law-when it is contrary to the instructions whether they are right or wrong. Lynch v. Snead Iron Works, 116 S. W. 639, 132 Ky. 241.

(66a) Where the damages can be measured and the jury in fixing the damages has evidently disregarded instructions of the court a new trial must be granted. Dunn v. Blue Grass Co., 163 Ky. 384, 173 S. W. 1122.

(66b) In an action to recover specific personal property, a verdict which valued the property at an amount exceeding that named in the petition and affidavit for an immediate delivery was contrary to law. Ky. Port. Cement & Coal Co. v. Steckel, 164 Ky. 420, 175 S. W. 663.

(66c) A certain verdict was too vague to support a judgment. Its ambiguity could not be explained by an affidavit of a juror. Romans v. McGinnis, 156 Ky. 205, 160 S. W. 928.

(66d) A certain verdict was sufficiently definite and specific. Walter v. Louisville Ry. Co., 150 Ky. 652, 150 S. W. 824.

(66e) A verdict for plaintiff did not state against which of two defendants it was rendered; it was held to be a verdict against both. Atkinson v. Lawrence, 161 Ky. 166, 170 S. W. 528.

(67) Default judgment—action of court in setting aside is not controlled by these Code provisions but rests in the exercise.

# New Trials

# § 341 [870] [When not to be granted—two new trials.]

A new trial shall not be granted on account of the smallness of damages in an action for an injury to the person or reputation, or in any other action in which the damages equal the actual pecuniary injury sustained; nor shall more than two new trials be granted to a party upon the ground that the verdict is not sustained by the evidence.

of a sound discretion. Southern Ins. Co. v. Johnson, 140 Ky. 485, 131 S. W. 270.

(67a) If a judgment be taken by default a new trial may be granted at any time during the term upon the offering of a sufficient answer and showing of good reasons why the same was not filed in time. Strader v. Strader, 163 Ky. 356. 173 S. W. 793.

(68) Division of land-motion for a new trial not necessary. Comett v. Burchfield, 142 Ky. 357, 134 S. W. 466; Sayler v. Arnett, 62 S. W. 1031, 23 R. 321.

(69) Pending appeal or after appeal has been granted the unsuccessful party may file petition for a new trial. L. & N. R. Co. v. Ueltschi, 104 S. W. 320, 31 R. 933, 126 Ky. 556.

(70) Petition after term. A petition for new trial filed after term need not be accompanied by supporting affidavits. Mason, Evans & Keys v. Meloan, 165 Ky. 582, 177 S. W. 435.

# 341. (1) Smallness of damages.

When in an action for any injury to the person special damages are alleged and proved, and the jury disregard the evidence and the law and find for the plaintiff only one cent, a new trial should be awarded. This section does not apply to the actual pecuniary damages resulting directly from the wrong. These damages can be measured, and the rule that a new trial shall not be granted on account of the smallness of the damages does not apply. Ray v. Jeffries, 86 Ky. 367, 9 R. 602, 5 S.W. 867; Taylor v. Houser, 12 Bush 465; Jesse v. Shuck, 11 R. 463, 12 S. W. 304.

(1a) Where no iterus of special damages are alleged and proved, but a recovery is sought for general damages, such as mental and physical suffering and permanent impairment of power to earn money, this section applies with full force, and a new trial will not be granted on account of the smallness of the damages. Rossi v. Jewell Jellico Coal Co., 157 Ky. 332, 163 S. W. 220.

(1b) This section does not apply to actual, pecuniary damages resulting directly from a wrong, which are capable of being measured, such as special damages.

(9) Presumption that new trial was for other grounds than inadequacy. Where, in a motion for a new trial, other grounds than inadequacy of the verdict are relied upon, and such grounds or some of them are sufficient to justify a new trial, the Court of Appeals will not assume that the new trial was granted solely because of inadequacy of the verdict. Paducah Trac. Co. v. Walker's Admr., 169 Ky. 721, 185 S. W. 119.

§ 342. (1) Motion for new trial-practice. A motion for a new trial must be made within three days after the verdict is returned, whether the verdict be general or special. Imperial Ins. Co. v. Kiernan, 83 Ky. 468, 7 R. 542; and although a motion for judgment, notwithstanding verdict, may be pending. Ruhrwein v. Gebhardt, 90 Ky. 147, 11 R. 969, 13 S. W. 447.

(2) The requirement that the motion shall be made within three days means three judicial days, and the day on which the verdict or decision is rendered, and the day on which the motion is made, are both computed. Long v. Hughes, 1 Duv. 387; White v. Crutcher, 287.

# § 997. New trial in equity—when application to be made.

Any application for a new trial in an equitable action or proceeding, except for newly-discovered evidence, shall be made within fifteen days after the judgment is rendered; and if the application be for cause mentioned, it shall be made as provided by general laws as to courts having terms, sixty days being counted as a term. Title IX] New Trials.

# § 343 [372] [Motion and written grounds-affidavits sustaining and controverting.]

The application must be by motion upon written grounds filed at the time of making the motion. The grounds mentioned in section 340, subsections 2, 3, and 7, must be sustained by affidavits showing their truth; and may be controverted by affidavits.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 51, 54, 57.

# 344 [s78] [Grounds discovered after term -practice-—divorce cases.]

If grounds for a new trial be discovered after the term at which the verdict or decision is rendered, the application may be made by a petition filed with the clerk not later than the second term after the discovery.

# § 343. (1) Grounds relied on-how stated.

See notes 17, 18 to sec. 340; and when it is necessary to support grounds by affidavits they should be filed, stating names of witnesses and facts that they will prove, and that these facts came to the knowledge of the party after the trial. Adams v. Ashby, 2 Bibb 287; Richardson v. Finney, 6 Dana 320; Brown v. Green, 2 Duv. 234; Dixon v. Wood, 23 R. 1004, 64 S. W. 724; Soper v. Crutcher, 96 S. W. 907, 29 R. 1080; grounds must be filed and motion made in court. Boyle v. Stivers, 109 Ky. 253, 22 R. 793, 58 S. W. 691.

# (la) Certain grounds for a new trial did not raise the question whether a bill of lading violated the provisions of the interstate commerce act.

The Court of Appeals declined to consider this federal question. L. & N. R. Co. v. Woodford, 152 Ky. 398, 153 S. W. 722.

# (lb) Affidavits, when necessary.

Affidavits as to misconduct of counsel in argument are not necessary, where it occurred before the court, to which the judge certified and concerning which there is no dispute. Carter Coal Co. v. Hill, 166 Ky. 213, 179 S. W. 2.

# (lc) When a motion for a new trial is made under this section on the ground of newly discovered evidence within the term the motion must be supported by affidavit.

Mason, Evans & Keys v. Melan, 165 Ky. 582, 177 S. W. 435.

# (ld) On an application for a new trial on account of newly discovered evidence, the affidavit of the proposed witness should be introduced setting out what he will testify to.

Powell v. Com., 149 Ky. 433, 149 S. W. 891.

# (le) A motion for a new trial based upon a claim of newly discovered evidence must be sustained by affidavits, one of which must be the affidavit of the party applying, saying that the facts were unknown to him in time to be used at the trial.

Chas. Taylor Sons Co. v. Hunt, 163 Ky. 120, 173 S. W. 333.

# (lf) A motion for a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence must be sustained by an affidavit showing that diligence was exercised to secure the evidence at the former trial.

Russell v. City of Ashland, 159 Ky. 223, 166 S. W. 971.

# (lg) Under this section plaintiff was held not entitled to a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence, where no effort was made to discover the facts sought to be established.

# ARTICLE 6.

# GENERAL PROVISIONS.

# § 345 [874] [Amount of damages recoverable.]

If damages be recoverable, the plaintiff may claim and recover any rate of damages to which he may be entitled for the cause of action established.

# § 346 [875] [Provisions of title apply to trials by court.]

The provisions of this title, respecting trials by jury, apply, so far as they are in their nature applicable, to trials by the court.

# (8) Filing grounds at subsequent term.

When a motion for a new trial made in due time is continued to next term other grounds may be filed at that term. Million v. Million, 104 S. W. 768, 3l R. 1156.

# (8a) Finality of judgment.

At the expiration of the term at which a judgment is rendered, the court loses control of the judgment, except in suits brought pursuant to this section and sec. 5l8. Algee v. Algee, 168 Ky. 362, 182 S. W. 197.

# (8b) A judgment regularly entered.

After adjournment of court at which it was rendered becomes final and cannot be vacated, set aside, or modified except on petition filed pursuant to this section. Noe v. Davis, 171 Kv. 482, 188 S. W. 457.

# (9) Casualty-misfortune.

Where a new trial is sought after term on grounds of casualty or misfortune the application should be made under sec. 5l8, and not under 344. Ray v. Arnett, 106 S. W. 828, 32 R. 562.

# (10) Criminal cases.

This section has no reference to convictions for crime. The rules as to obtaining new trials in criminal cases are embraced in Criminal Code 271 to 274. Wellington v. Com., 159 Ky. 462, 167 S. W. 427.

# § 345. Vindictive damages.

The right of a plaintiff to recover vindictive damages for personal injuries, where the commission of the act complained of is accompanied with circumstances of aggravation, has been repeatedly recognized, and must now be regarded as a settled rule of law. Punitive, vindictive and exemplary damages are in legal contemplation synonymous. Chiles v. Drake, 2 Met. 146.

§ 1015. When ordinary actions placed on trial docket. In civil actions or special proceedings other than equitable, after an issue of law or fact is formed, or after the time for pleading has elapsed without a pleading being filed, either party may place the action or proceeding on the common law trial docket of the court or the branch thereof in which same is pending, which shall be kept and called as the court shall, by rule, direct. (Ky. Stats., sec. 1015.)

# § 350 [379] [Jury trial-time of.]

The trial of any issue of fact or assessment of damages by a jury in any case shall be on or after the day set on the docket for its trial.

On the equity docket shall be entered all equitable actions in the order in which they are brought (Action may be transferred to ordinary, secs. 8 to 13.)

§ 993. Equity trial docket—cases against cities. On the equity trial docket shall be entered the actions that are ready for trial. Said docket shall be called at such times as the court may deem proper, when the actions thereon shall be heard, or the trial be postponed to a future day, or the action be remanded. No case in which any city is a party shall be submitted on motion or trial, unless the name of the said city shall appear in the style of case on the docket in which said case is set for motion or trial. (Ky. Stat., sec. 993.)

§ 994. Motions placed on equity docket. Any motion made and assigned to a future day for hearing may be placed upon such docket. (Ky. Stal., sec. 994.)

§ 995. Pleadings admitted true-action docketed. When a party consents that the pleadings of the opposite side may be taken as true, the action may be at once placed on such trial docket. (Ky. Stat., sec. 995.)

§ 996. Failure to plead—action docketed. Any party may place the action on such trial docket, when the time allowed to the opposite party to plead has elapsed.

# § 352 [881] [Motion docket—entries on.]

On the motion docket may be entered any motion relating to proceedings in the court. (Motion defined, sec. 623.)

# § 353 [882] [Dockets to show names of parties and attorneys.]

The entry on all the dockets shall give the names of the plaintiff and defendant; or, if there be more than one, those of the first named in the pleadings, with the words "and others" added thereto, and the names of the attorneys. (Officer's return on summons to be entered, sec. 570.)

# § 354 [883] [Common docket—what it shall show.]

The entry on the common docket shall also show whether or not the summons has been fully served in due time for trial, and whether or not the issues have been formed.

# § 355 [884] [Motion docket—what it shall show.]

The entry on the motion docket shall show the date of the entry and the object of the motion.

# § 356 [885] [First hour each day for motions.]

The first hour of each day of the term shall be devoted to hearing motions—in which the court shall first call the motion docket; and then, upon the attorneys present, for motions.

# § 357 [886] [Equity docket may be called.]

If the hour be not consumed in motions, the court may proceed, during the remainder thereof, to call the equity docket.

# § 358 [887] [Proceedings after the motion hour.]

At the expiration of the motion hour, the court, except on the third day of the term, shall proceed to the call of the common docket and the trial of the issues until the call be completed; and then to the call of the equity docket.

# CIRCUIT COURTS HAVING TERMS.

Unless otherwise directed by the parties, the clerk shall issue subpoenas for witnesses to the first day of the term, and they shall attend at such days as the case in which they are subpoenaed shall be set for trial, and until the same is disposed of by trial or continuance: Provided, however, The court may by rule entered upon its order book, direct the clerk to set actions.

§ 352. Motion. In proceeding by motion. plaintiff may, on day specified in notice, make motion in court and have it entered of record and cause it to be placed on the motion docket, and in either case the motion will be regarded as regularly pending in court. Bent v. Maupin, 86 Ky. 271, 9 R. 469, 5 S. W. 425.

# § 359 [as8] [Ordinary actions called after motion hour.]

On each day of the term the court shall, immediately after the hearing of motions, call all the cases on the common docket set for that day [in which the summons has been served in due time, as provided in section 102, and in which no issue of fact has been formed; and, upon failure to defend, may render judgment;*] and at the same time may hear and determine all issues of law in such actions and render judgment, or give further time for hearing and deciding them.

# § 360 [389] [Equity docket called on third day.]

[On the third day of each term the court shall call all cases on the equity docket in which the summons has been served, as provided in section 102, and upon failure to defend may give judgment.*]

# § 361 [soo] [Issues of law in actions tried third day—judgment.]

On the third day the court may hear and decide all issues of law in the actions, as they are called; and render judgment, or give further time for hearing or deciding them.

# § 362 [8o1] [Motion made by entering it on docket.]

The entry of a motion on the motion docket shall be considered as making the motion.

# § 359. Trial of cases.

There is no provision of the Code or rule of practice which authorizes the court, before an action is ready for trial, to hear and try it, and render a judgment in favor of one of the parties whose right to recover anything on the final hearing 'is not admitted. "The court has no right to take a case up and hear it, except as provided for in the Code, in opposition to the wishes of either party. Mattingly v. Bosley, 2 Met. 443.

# § 363] Trial-Time of Trial.

[Title IX]

§ 363 [392] [Ordinary actions—when stand for trial-different rule in cases of contract and tort.] [Ordinary actions shall stand for trial at the first term after process has been served on the defendant, as specified in section 102.*] An action upon contract, wherein the summons has been served in due time, as provided in section 102, upon part only of the defendants, shall stand for trial at the first term as to those so summoned, and may be continued as to the others for further proceedings. In other ordinary actions the plaintiff can only demand a trial at any term as to part of the defendants upon his discontinuing his action on the first day of such term as to the others. (See further. secs. 370, 373. See note.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 173d, 329a, 438, 439.

*Section 367a-4 reads as follows: "§ 367a-4. Actions at law shall stand for trial at the first term of court after summons has been served in the county ten days, and elsewhere in the State twenty days before the commencement of said term." (Civil Code.)

This section seems clearly to repeal so much of section 363 as is enclosed in brackets.-Ed.

# § 363. (1) Change of venue-time of trial.

If the case would stand for trial at the succeeding court of the county from which it is removed, and the papers be filed in the clerk's office of the court to which it is removed ten days before the ensuing term, it will stand for trial at that term. Dale v. Hays, 14 B. M 315; Ky. Stat., sec. 1100.

(2) Contract-action against several may be tried as to those before the court. L. & A. R. R. v. Bennett, 25 R. 834, 76 S. W. 408; Crump v. J. I. Case Co., 123 S. W. 333, 136 Ky. 339.

(2a) Appeals to circuit court. An appeal to a circuit court is pending from the time the record is filed. It may be heard at a special term called to begin before the regular term to which the summons on the appeal is returnable. Young v. Trimble; Hiter v. Waddy; Brannon v. Myall; Lancaster v. Thacker, 164 Ky. 177, 175 S. W. 366.

(3) Pleadings must be verified before party who is in default in this respect can demand a trial. Park v. McReynolds, 111 Ky. 651, 23 R. 894, 64 S. W. 517.

(4) Plea of payment by one of several obligors-no judgment should be rendered against any of them until the issue is disposed of. Rouse v. Howard, 1 Duv. 31; Williams v. Rogers, 14 Bush 776.

(5) Tort-trial as to some of defendants-judgment. In an ordinary action not founded on a contract, the plaintiff can only demand a trial as to part of the defendants, when he has dismissed his action against those not summoned on the first day of the term, and judgment rendered against any of the defendants, unless this requirement is complied with, is erroneous.

(6) If several persons jointly commit a tort, the plaintiff in general has his election to sue all or some of the parties, and if several are jointly sued, and a joint verdict is rendered against them, the court may set aside the verdict as to one or more and let it stand as to the others. Buckles v. Lambert, 4 Met. 330.

(7) Joint trespassers—joint or several judgment against is proper. Alexander v. Humber, 86 Ky. 565, 9 R. 734, 6 S. W. 453; Cent. P. Ry. Co. v. Kuhn, 86 Ky. 578, 9 R. 725, 6 S. W. 441.

# § 364 [308] [Equity actions-when stand for trial.]

Equitable actions shall stand for trial at any term, if the pleadings have been, or, by the provisions of sections 102, 104, 105, and 106, should have been completed [sixty*] days before the commencement of such term. But, if they have not been so completed, though they should have been, by those sections, the party in default as to time shall not be entitled to demand a trial.† Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 534.

"Sixty" substituted in place of "ninety" by Act of July 6, 1893, c. 254, p. 1389.

This section seems to be repealed by section 367a-5, reading as follows:

367a-5. Suits in equity shall stand for trial at the first term of court after the issue shall be completed or, by the provisions of this act, shall have been completed, thirty days before the commencement of the term.

Quaere: Is the last sentence in section 364 repealed by sections 367a-3, 367a-5, 367a-10, and 367a-11, read together?--Ed.

# PARTIES CONSTRUCTIVELY SUMMONED.

# § 367a-6.

* As to parties constructively summoned and not answering as herein provided, the action, whether at law or equity, shall stand for trial at the first term of court, which commences not less than sixty days after the said warning order is entered. (Civil Code.)

# § 365 [394] [Party interrogated, when entitled to trial.]

If interrogatories be annexed to a pleading in an equitable action, the party interrogated shall not be entitled to demand a trial, unless he have answered the interrogatories served on the first day of the term, the objection is waived. Rosenberg v. Dahl, 162 Ky. 92, 172 S. W. 113.

# 364.

(1) Non-resident. An equitable action against a non-resident does not stand for trial until ninety [now sixty] days after the pleadings ought to have been completed. Harris v. Adams, 2 Duv. 141.

(1a) Equitable actions. In a suit on a note and to enforce a lien on mortgage bonds held as collateral, in which defendant's petition was taken as an answer, and denied ownership of the bonds, and that they were taken as collateral with knowledge that they did not belong to defendant, it was held that the answer raised a question of fact, and that it was reversible error to submit the case at the appearance term. Mayfield Water & Light Co. v. Graves County Bank & Trust Co., 170 Ky. 56, 185 S. W. 485.

(2) In equitable actions in the Louisville Chancery court, "when the order of warning is entered, the defense must be filed in sixty days thereafter." Irish B. & L. Association v. Cleinons, 78 Ky. 79, and see sec. 10oit. in appendix, chap. 4.

(3) Pleadings must be verified before party in default in this respect can demand a trial. Park v. McReynolds, 111 Ky. 651, 23 R. 894, 64 S. W. 517.

(Time of trial. An action does not stand for trial at the term at which a reply to a counter-claim is filed, the reply being necessary to complete the pleadings. Smith v. Ferguson, 3 Met. 424; Coldiron v. Combs, 22 R. 1187, 60 S. W. 386.

(5) Trial of issues raised by cross-petition will not be permitted to delay trial of the original action except as provided in sec. 97. Edwards v. Grimes, 23 R. 2055. 66 S. W. 515.

(6) This section only applies to cases in which an issue of fact is made. Hazelwood v. Webster, 25 R. 1388, 78 S. W. 123.

(7) This section applies to courts in continuous session and was not repealed by the act regulating practice in such courts. Hornick v. Holtsup, 25 R. 1030. 76 S. W. 874.

# § 366 [ass] [Equitable action--when plaintiff may demand trial.]

The plaintiff shall be entitled to a trial in an equitable action, at the first term after the summons has been served on all defendants as provided in section 1o2, if no issue of fact be made by the pleadings: or, if the plaintiff consent that the statements of the answer may be taken as true.

# § 367 [89e] [Equitable action-trial as to part of defendants.]

If the summons in an equitable action have been served in due time on part only of the defendants, the plaintiff may dismiss his action as to those not summoned, and proceed to trial as to the others, if he could have maintained his action against them without joining those not summoned.

# § 366.

1. Trial of equitable action at term answer is filed. on motion of defendant, is not error when the plaintiff getn judgment for all he is entitled to under his petition. Iowe v. Lowe, 13 Bush 6ss; as to trial in courts having continuous sessions, see statute regulating, in appendix, ch. 4, art. 5.
2. When there is no objection to the hearing nor motion for a continuance, nor for a rehearing it can not be adjudged that the hearing was premature or erroneous, although the case was submitted a few days after the filing of an amended answer. Moses v. Rowland, 3 Bush 505.
3. An equitable action does not stand for trial at the term at which an answer making an issue is filed, unless the plaintiff consents that the statements may be taken as true. Gruel v. Smalley, 1 Duv. 358.

6. It was held that the submission and judgment on both the petition and cross-petition in an equitable action, at the term succeeding the institution of the action, was premature and unauthorized, where there is no consent that the answer be taken as true, and the answer was not filed in time for the issues to be completed thirty days before the commencement of the first term of court succeeding the institution of the suit. Jones v. Hazard Dean Coal Co., 169 Ky. 588, 184 S. W. 1131.
7. This section does not apply to a suit against the secretary and general manager of an insolvent corporation, where all the issues submitted and tried were by consent referred to the master commissioner, and where defendant not only consented to the order of reference, but filed no exceptions to the master's report, and made no objections to the submission upon the plaintiff's exceptions thereto. Bastin v. Given's Admx., 170 Ky. 201, 185 S. W. 835.
8. In a suit on a note and to enforce a lien on mortgage bonds held as collateral, in which defendant's petition was taken as an answer, and denied ownership of the bonds, and that they were taken as collateral with knowledge that they did not belong to defendant, it was held that the answer raised a question of fact, and that it was reversible error to submit the case at the appearance term. Mayfield Water & Light Co. v. Graves County Bank & Trust Co., 170 Ky. 56, 185 S. W. 485.

Title IX] Trial-Time of Trial. [§ 367a-1-367a-3
# PRACTICE ACT.

AN ACT TO REGULATE PROCEEDINGS IN CIVIL ACTIONS IN CIRCUIT COURTS. [Act of March 29, 1902, c. 122, p. 272.]

# § 367a-1. [Rule day.]

That the first Monday in each month shall be rule day in the office of the clerk of each circuit court in this Commonwealth, except when said court is actually in session.

# § 367a-2. [Summons-when answers due.]

That all summonses issued in actions, both at law and in equity, brought in said courts, shall command the officer to whom it is directed to summons the party or parties against whom it is issued to answer in ten days after the service of the summons, if directed to the county where issued, and in twenty days after the service of the summons, if directed to any other county.

# § 367a-3. [Pleadings demurrers motions-when court fixes time.]

At the first rule day after the summons has been served ten days in the county or twenty days in any other county, the defendant so served shall file his answer or other plea he may have, whether of law or of fact. On the next rule day the opposite party shall file his reply and any other plea he may have, whether of law or fact; and so on at each succeeding rule day until the issue shall be fully made up. If any party shall fail to file any plea at the rule day at which the same is due, he may file the same at a subsequent rule day, and as soon as practicable and he shall file therewith his affidavit or other sworn statement as to the cause of his delay, and if the court shall consider his excuse insufficient, it may, at the next regular term, strike such plea from the files.

# 367a. (1 Practice act-when case stands for trial.

Board of Councilmen v. Brislan. 104 S. W. 311, 31 R. 867, 126 Ky. 477; Combs v. Virginia Coal Co., 98 S. W. 1013, 30 R. 408.

# (la) A petition remonstrating against the annexation of certain territory to a town of the sixth class was filed on September 4, 1914, and the answer was filed September 10, 1914.

The case stood for trial at the next November term of the court, although a demurrer to the petition had not been passed upon. Preston v. Town of Paintsville, 158 Ky. 700, 166 S. W. 188.

# (Answer after demurrer.

A defendant was permitted to file a demurrer after the time allowed by law. It was overruled. He then tendered an answer which set up a defense. It was error to overrule the motion to file the answer. Chicago Bonding & Surety Co. v. Johnson, 180 Ky. 589, 203 S. W. 317.

# (2n) Answer, insufficient excuse for not filing in time.

Time until a certain rule day was given a defendant to file an answer setting up his claim. Before that rule day he lodged his answer with the clerk of the court to be filed on that rule day, but did not file it with the clerk and give notice thereof. The clerk failed to file the answer. A judgment barring the defendant's claim was affirmed. (Subsee. 3.) Kohlman v. Moore, 175 Ky. 710, 194 S. W. 933.

# (2b) Removal of cause.

A petition for removal of a cause to the federal court filed within three days after defendant's appearance and answer entitled him to removal to the federal court. Fearon Lumber & Veneer Co. v. Lawson, 166 Ky. 123, 178 S. W. 1121.

Trial-Time of Trial [Title IX]

If any demurrer, motion to elect or like pleading is filed the court shall dispose of same as soon as possible and before the case is called for trial or submitted and the court in such cases shall direct when subsequent pleadings should be filed.

# § 367a-4. [Ordinary actions when stand for trial.]

Actions at law shall stand for trial at the first term of court after summons has been served in the county ten days, and elsewhere in the State twenty days before the commencement of said term.

# § 367a-5. [Equity actions-when stand for trial.]

Suits in equity shall stand for trial at the first term of court after the issue shall be completed or, by the provisions of this act, shall have been completed, thirty days before the commencement of the term.

# § 367a-6. [Warning orders-when actions stand for trial.]

All warning orders both at law and in equity, shall warn the party against whom the same is issued to answer in thirty days after the entry of the warning order, and the answer and other plea shall be due at the first rule day after the expiration of said thirty days. As to parties constructively summoned and not answering as herein provided, the action whether at law or equity, shall stand for trial at the first term of court, which commences not less than sixty days after the said warning order is entered.

# § 367a-7. [Interlocutory orders.]

Any interlocutory order necessary for the preparation of the action for trial, whether at law or in equity, may be entered by any party at any rule day; but no order shall be entered which deprives a person of the possession of property.

# § 367a-8. [Clerk enters and signs orders-court may set aside.]

The clerk shall enter upon the minute book of the court all orders, filing pleas and otherwise, except warning orders and shall sign same. He shall, as soon thereafter as practicable, enter said orders in full upon the order book of the court, and shall sign same; and all such orders shall constitute a part of the record in the case, for all purposes, as fully as if made in open court. The court shall at the first ensuing term, have power to set aside any such order.

# § 367a-9. [Ordinary actions—docketing-trial-subpoenas.]

The clerk shall docket all actions at law for the first day of the term at which, under the provisions of this act, they stand for trial. On said day the court shall call said docket and enter default judgments where no defense is made. In all such actions where defense is made, the parties shall be deemed to have waived a trial by jury, unless a jury is demanded upon said call, and the court shall then proceed to set such actions down for trial at some convenient day in the term.

# Title IX

# Trial-Time of Trial

# [§ 367a-10-367a-14]

The clerk shall issue subpoenas for witnesses to the first day of the term, and they shall attend at such days as the case in which they are subpoenaed shall be set for trial, and until the same is disposed of by trial or continuance: Provided, however, The court may by rule entered upon its order book, direct the clerk to set actions at law for convenient days during the term instead of on the first days of the term, as provided above; and to issue subpoenas to said days, instead of to the first day, as provided above.

# § 367a-10. [Equity actions-docketing-trial.]

The clerk shall docket all suits in equity for the first term of court which does not commence less than ten days after the same is filed. On the third day of the term the court shall call the equity appearance docket and enter default judgments where no defense is made: Provided, All parties to the action affected thereby have been summoned; those in the county, ten days before the commencement of the term; those out of the county twenty days before the commencement of the term; and those constructively summoned, thirty days before the commencement of the term.

# § 367a-11. [Pleading—time of.]

When the issue has not been completed before the commencement of the term, the answer or next pleading due shall be filed on the first day of the term, in law actions, and on the third day of the term, in equity actions. In law actions, subsequent pleadings shall be filed on or before the day on which the action is set for trial. In equity actions each pleading, subsequent to the answer, shall be filed not less than three days after the filing of the pleading to which it is responsive. The court may, in its discretion, allow a pleading to be filed after it is due under the provisions of this act.

# § 367a-12. [Courts in continuous session.]

This act shall not apply to circuit courts having continuous sessions.

# § 367a-13. [Repealing clause.]

All provisions of the Code of Practice in conflict with this act are hereby repealed in so far as in conflict.

# § 367a-14. [When act takes effect.]

This act shall take effect and be in force from its passage.

# § 368 [807] [Judgment defined.]

A judgment is a final determination of a right of a party in an action or proceeding. (Forus of judgments, Forms 187-195; rehat are final orders, sec secs. 266, 267, 298.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 419-423.

# § 368.

(1) Default judgment in ordinary action sec. 359, in equity action sec. 360, for uncontested part of claim sec. 380, against part of defendants secs. 370, 373.

Enforcement of judgment by rule requiring party to bring money to satisfy judgment into court is not allowable. Cofer v. Woodyard, 5 R. 858; and see Ky. Stat., secs. 1650, 1663, providing that execution may issue on judgment at law or in equity.

(2a) Enjoining tax. A judgment enjoining a certain tax was sufficiently certain. City of Lancaster v. Pope, 156 Ky. 1, 160 S. W. 509.

(3) Erroneous judgment cannot be assailed in a collateral proceeding. Green v. Ball, 4 Bush 586; Newcomb v. Newcomb, 13 Bush 544; Myers v. Pedigo, 24 R. 1923, 72 S. W. 734; and see as to void judgment, note 27.

Final order defined. Final judgments "are such as at once put an end to the action by declaring that the plaintiff has either entitled himself, or has not, to recover the remedy he sues for." And final order "either terminates the action itself, decides some matter litigated by the parties, or operates to divest some right in such manner as to put it out of the power of the court making the order, after the expiration of the term, to place the parties in their original position." Helm v. Short, 7 Bush 623. See M. & L. R. R. Co. v. Punnett, 302.

Title IX] Judgment--In General. [§ 368

15 B. M. 47; Turner v. Browder, 18 B. M. 825; and a decree is a judgment. Hughes v. Shreve, 3 Met. 547; and see further, as to final orders, notes to sec. 734.

# (4a)

An order which merely sustains a demurrer to a petition is not a final order. Harrison v. Stroud, 150 Ky. 797, 150 S. W. 993.

# (4b)

An order made by the circuit court directing that allegations of a petition be taken as confessed is merely interlocutory and may be set aside by the circuit court for cause at a subsequent term. So much of the opinion in 149 Ky. 482 as seems in conflict with this proposition was withdrawn. Duf & Oney v. Rose, 151 Ky. 96, 151 S. W. 22.

# (4c)

On an appeal by a wife, plaintiff in an action for alimony, from an order fixing the alimony to be paid pending the action, the amount of temporary alimony was increased. Kelly v. Kelly, 179 Ky. 586, 200 S. W. 925.

# (4d)

A circuit judge cannot grant a writ of mandamus in vacation. A refusal of a judge in vacation to grant a mandamus is not a final order. Bennett v. Blankenship, 177 Ky. 499, 197 S. W. 967.

# (4e)

An order refusing to allow a certain answer to be filed was not a final order. Wilhelm v. Hendrick, 177 Ky. 296, 197 S. W. 836.

# (4f)

A commissioner's report in an action to settle a decedent's estate disallowed a claim. An order confirming this report was a final judgment against the claim. The circuit court after the term had no power to vacate or modify the same in the absence of some of the grounds in Code, sec. 518. Culver v. Lutz, 171 Ky. 691, 189 S. W. 240.

# (4g)

Forcible detainer. A vendee who was ejected in a proceeding of forcible detainer brought by the vendor had no cause of action against the vendor, the magistrate or the constable. Ackerson v. Semple, 163 Ky. 375, 173 S. W. 1153.

# (4h)

Homestead. A judgment sustained an attachment; it was adjudged a lien upon a house and lot which had been levied on. Before the judgment was entered the defendant offered an amended answer pleading homestead; on the trial they declined to read that portion of the deposition which bore on the question of homestead. On appeal the judgment was reversed as to certain rents of the property. A new action claiming a homestead was rightly dismissed. Wren v. Cooksey, 155 Ky. 620, 159 S. W. 1167.

# (9)

Judgment against administrator not binding upon heirs in action to subject property descended to them. Willis v. Roberts, 90 Ky. 122, 11 R. 929, 13 S. W. 358; Jones v. Com. Bank, 78 Ky. 413.

# (9a)

Judgment after continuance. A circuit court may render final judgment in an action, although it has previously entered an order continuing the action to.

(12) Judgment obtained by fraud will not be enforced in an action based on the judgment. Duerson v. Semonin, 17 R. 51, 29 S. W. 635.

(13) Nunc pro tunc judgment. Although neither the minutes nor order book showed that judgment had been rendered at a previous term, the filing by defendants of supersedeas bond and assignment of errors were sufficient admissions of record to authorize the court to enter a nunc pro tunc judgment. Wade v. Bryant, 9 R. 875, 7 S. W. 397.

The mere recollection of the judge as to what took place at a former term is not sufficient to authorize an addition to or amendment of the record in regard to any order or judgment." Boyd Co. v. Ross, 95 Ky. 167, 15 R. 520, 25 S. W. 8; and see Raymond v. Smith, 1 Met. 65; see further as to judgments, sec. 390, and notes.

The 12th day of December, 1905," signed by the circuit judge, was a sufficient basis for entering an order nunc pro tunc after the death of one of the petitioners for the adoption of a child. Chester v. Graves, 159 Ky. 244, 166 S. W. 998.

(10f) There is no priority of estate between the executor or administrator of a decedent and his heirs at law or devisees; and a judgment at law against the former, while it may bind the latter as far as it concerns the personal estate, and be prima facie evidence against them as to the realty, is not a conclusive estoppel upon them in respect to lands devised or descended to them, either on the administrator's application for leave to sell real estate to pay the decedent's debts, or on a bill by a judgment creditor against the heirs or devisees to subject the land to his judgment; and under ordinary circumstances the heirs or devisees may set up any meritorious defense against the judgment when it is sought to be enforced against their interests. Davis v. Davis, 157 Ky. 530, 163 S. W. 468.

(15) Parol evidence is admissible to show what was determined in the former action, if it does not appear from the record. Maize v. Bowman, 93 Ky. 205, 14 R. 121, 19 S. W. 589.

(16) Personal judgment should not be rendered when none is asked. Cavanaugh v. Fried, 3 R. 253; Howland v. Brown, 13 Bush 681; and see further as to judgment that may be rendered under prayer of petition, notes 49-53 to sec. 90.

(37) Res judicata. The burden is upon the party relying upon the bar to show that the merits of the case were conclusive.

# Judgment-In General

(§ 368)

Considered. Pepper v. Donnelly, 87 Ky. 259, 10 R. 140, 8 S. W. 441.

# (18)

The rule is well settled that to constitute the former judgment a bar to another suit founded on the same cause of action it must appear to have been a decision upon the merits. But if the trial went off on a technical defect, or because the debt was not due, or because the court had not jurisdiction, or because of the temporary disability of the plaintiff to sue, or the like, the judgment will be no bar to a future action. Birch v. Funk, 2 Met. 514; Potter v. Benge, 24 R. 24, 67 S. W. 1005.

# (18a)

An order of the circuit court that a certain case be filed away was treated as a judgment dismissing same without prejudice. Phillips v. Arnett, Admr., 164 Ky. 426, 175 S. W. 660.

# (18b)

A judgment sustaining a demurrer to a petition is a bar to the bringing of another action upon the same cause of action unless other facts be set up in the second action. West v. King, 163 Ky. 561, 174 S. W. 11.

# (18c)

The finding of a probate court of another State in an ex parte proceeding, that the decedent resided in a certain county or State, was not binding on any one not a party to the proceedings. Baker v. Baker, Eccles & Co., 162 Ky. 683, 173 S. W. 109.

# (18d)

In this case it was held that a judgment in a former action between the same parties was not an estoppel as to matters which were not in issue nor determined in the former action. Campbell v. Mims, 161 Ky. 530, 170 S. W. 1176.

# (18e)

A judgment in a habeas corpus proceeding is not a judgment of a court. Bedford v. Hamilton, 153 Ky. 429, 155 S. W. 1128.

# (18f)

The proposition was laid down that where the question of jurisdiction is raised and disposed of in a judgment, that question is res judicata and cannot be questioned in a collateral proceeding. The question does not seem to be involved in this record, as the jurisdiction in the case referred to was clearly shown. Stone v. Winn, 165 Ky. 9, 176 S. W. 933.

# (18g)

A judgment on a note merges the note; but if after the judgment another suit be brought on the same note and judgment be rendered therein, the second judgment is valid and cannot be collaterally attacked. Daniels v. Runyon, 164 Ky. 309, 175 S. W. 338.

# (18h)

In an action for trespass to land the title of the land was put in issue. The judgment in that action is a bar to any future action between the parties involving the ownership of the land. Benge v. Martin, 159 Ky. 302, 166 S. W. 1003.

# (18i)

A judgment does not determine matters which could not have been determined under the pleadings and evidence; a judgment in an action for the construction of a deed did not bar an action seeking the same relief on the ground that the deed after delivery had been altered. Phillips v. Big Sandy Co., 149 Ky. 555, 149 S. W. 957.

# (18j)

An action for the statutory penalty imposed for disobedience to a writ of habeas corpus which writ was but a copy of the original writ was barred by a judgment in an action between the same parties for a penalty for alleged disobedience to the writ. Stewart v. Fuson, 152 Ky. 137, 153 S. W. 247.

# (18k)

A refusal of the Court of Appeals or a judge thereof to dissolve a mandatory injunction requiring election commissioners to canvass votes at a certain election is not an adjudication that the election was valid. Morgan v. Goode, 151 Ky. 284, 152 S. W. 584; Hogue v. Myrick, 151 Ky. 284, 152 S. W. 584.

# (18l)

A judgment in favor of two defendants against the plaintiff is not an adjudication between the two defendants. Martin v. Hall, 152 Ky. 677, 153 S. W. 997.

# (18m)

A judgment dismissing a petition for specific performance of a contract to compromise claims to certain mineral rights was not a bar to another action to quiet title to the mineral rights. Kentenia Corp. v. Boreing L. & M. Co., 159 Ky. 61, 166 S. W. 780.

# (18n)

In a suit to settle a decedent's estate an order confirming the report of a commissioner which disallows the claim is a final judgment against such claim, and is a bar to a subsequent assertion of such a claim in the same action. Wood v. Sharp's Admr., 159 Ky. 46, 166 S. W. 787.

# (18o)

To constitute a former judgment a bar, it must have been on the merits, and an adjudication of the vital issue involved. Rockport Coal Co. v. Carter, 157 Ky. 555, 163 S. W. 734.

# (18p)

It is a well-recognized rule of law that a judgment, necessarily affirming the existence of any fact, is conclusive upon the parties or their privies whenever the existence of the fact is again in issue between such parties or privies, not only when the subject is the same, but when the point comes incidentally in question in relation to a different matter in the same or any other.

# Judgment-In General

court. Hoskins v. Hoskins' Admrs., 157 Ky. 738, 164 S. W. 77.

(18) To constitute a judgment a bar or estoppel there must be an identity of parties, as well as the subject-matter; it being necessary that the parties as between whom the judgment is relied on as an estoppel, must have been parties to the action in which it was rendered, in the same capacities and in the same antagonistic relation, or else they must be in privity with the parties in such former action. Davis v. Davis, 157 Ky. 530, 163 S. W. 468.

(19) "Ordered that this cause be dismissed at defendant's costs, and leave given to withdraw note sued on, by leaving a copy in the papers," can not be pleaded in bar of a subsequent action on the same note. Hibler v. Shipp, 78 Ky. 64; and see notes to sec. 371.

(20) When demurrer to petition is overruled and case heard upon law and facts and petition dismissed, it will be presumed in the absence of rebutting circumstances that the trial was upon the merits, but the presumption is not conclusive. Pepper v. Donnelly, 87 Ky. 259, 10 R. 140, 8 S. W. 441; see further, notes to sec. 371.

(21) Judgment dismissing petition is a bar to another action for the same relief, provided the determination has reached the merits of the case, and whether the facts upon which court acts are shown by evidence or averred in petition and admitted by demurrer. Maize v. Bowman, 93 Ky. 205, 14 R. 121, 19 S. W. 589.

(22) When a judgment which has been affirmed upon appeal is relied upon in bar of another action the ground of affirmance must be taken to be the same upon which judgment was rendered, and resort had to opinion to determine whether there was a decision upon the merits. Pepper v. Donnelly, 87 Ky. 259, 10 R. 140, 8 S. W. 441. Where an appeal is improperly dismissed the judgment of dismissal can not be pleaded in bar of a second appeal. Bitzer v. O'Bryan, 107 Ky. 590, 21 R. 1307, 54 S. W. 951.

(23) The judgment of the court having jurisdiction of the subject-matter and the parties is conclusive, not only as to all matters determined by it, but as to all incidental matters which might have been properly litigated and decided in the same suit, until it has been reversed or vacated in some of the modes known to the law. Honaker v. Cecil 84 Ky. 202, 8 R. 188, 1 S. W. 392; Crabb v. Larkin, 9 Bush 154; Francis v. Wood, 81 Ky. 306, 16, 4 R. 616; Sears v. Sears, 95 Ky. 173, 15 R. 510, 25 S. W. 600; Harpending v. Wylie, 13 Bush 158; Clark v. Rodes, 12 Bush 13; 81 Ky. 16, 4 R. 616; 14 Bush 746; Malona v. Schwing, 101 Ky. 56, 19 R. 145, 39 S. W. 523; Bridges v. McAlester, 106 Ky. 791, 21 R. 428, 51 S. W. 603.

(24) See further, notes to secs. 390 and 734.

(25) Statement in judgment that summons has been executed is not proof of that fact. Robinson v. Mobley, I Bush 196; Long v. Montgomery, 6 Bush 394; and see note 1, sec. 47.

(26) Suit to settle estate-effect of creditor's action in filing his claim. See Story v. Story, 18 R. 97, 35 S. W. 540.

(27) Void judgment may be resisted in any court in which it is attempted to be enforced, and in order to obtain relief from it, neither an appeal nor a direct proceeding to vacate judgment is necessary. Spencer v. Parsons, 89 Ky. 577, 11 R. 769, 13 S. W. 72; Stevens v. Deering, 10 R. 393, 9 S. W. 292; Willis v. Tomes, 141 Ky. 431, 132 S. W. 1043; and see Jacobson v. Wernert, 19 R. 662, 41 S. W. 281; but when the court has jurisdiction, and the defendant appears, or is warned according to law, the judgment is not void, although it may be erroneous in all its parts, nor can it be assailed collaterally. Derr v. Wilson, 84 Ky. 14, 7 R. 701; Newcomb v. Newcomb, 13 Bush 544.

(28) Unsigned orders-where the judge on the last day of the term signs the minutes, the court on the first day of next term and before the minutes are spread on the order book and signed has full power to correct or disapprove them. Fristoe v. Gillen, 26 R. 149, 80 S. W. 823.

(28a) It is essential to the validity of a judgment that it be entered on the order book and signed by the judge. Farris v. Matthew, 149 Ky. 455, 149 S. W. 896.

(28b) An unsigned judgment is no judgment; an appeal taken within two years from the time a judgment was signed was in time, although it had been spread upon the order book long before. Interstate Petroleum Co. v. Farris, 159 Ky. 820, 169 S. W. 535.

(28c) In an action for divorce the judge signed the minute on the minute book and a judgment of divorce was drawn and put in the papers, but not entered by the clerk on the order book nor signed by the judge. The judgment was void; the status of the parties continued as before, although each of them married under the belief that there had been a valid judgment.

Title IX] Judgment-In General. [§369-370

# § 369 [so8] [Judgment for or against one or more of parties.]

Judgment may be given for or against one or more of several parties. (If on contract, it will not bar proceedings against others, sec. 27.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 105, 124e, 136, 314, 329a, 438.

# § 370 [899] [Judgment as to some, continuance as to others.]

In an action against several defendants, the court may, in its discretion, render judgment against one or more of them, leaving the action to proceed against the others, if a several judgment be proper. (When several judgments not proper, sec. 80; and see secs. 363, 373.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 105, 173d, 329a, 438, 439.

been a divorce. A deed made by the wife in which the husband who was supposed to have been divorced did not join was void. Hellard v. Rockcastle Min., Lum. & Oil Co., 153 Ky. 259. 154 S. W. 401.

(29) Entry out of term by clerk. Judgment entered by clerk out of term time and without authority of judge is void. Shepherd v. Shepherd, 107 S. W. 273, 32 R. 943, 128 Ky. 87.

(30) Judgment conclusive on parties. In an action to enforce satisfaction of judgment, defenses that might have been made when the judgment was rendered cannot be set up. Raymer v. Trustees, 30 R. 332, 98 S. W. 323, 124 Ky. 96.

(31) Collateral and direct attack on judgment - distinction between. Crown Real Estate Co. v. Rogers, 117 S. W. 279, 132 Ky. 790.

(32) A petition for adoption filed in the Jefferson Circuit Court did not give the name of the court nor did it state that the petitioners were residents of Jefferson County. The validity of the judgment was in nowise affected by these omissions. Chester v. Graves, 159 Ky. 244, 166 S. W. 998.

(33) If on a collateral attack the record of the judgment attacked shows certain steps taken to acquire jurisdiction, it will not, unless the record be an old one, be presumed that jurisdiction was acquired in some other way. Baker v. Baker, Eccles & Co., 162 Ky. 683, 173 S. W. 109.

(34) A judgment of a court in this State cannot be attacked directly except by appeal or by the proceedings of secs. 340, 414 or 518. All other attacks are collateral. Upon a collateral attack every presumption must be indulged in favor of the judgment. Baker v. Baker, Eccles & Co., 162 Ky. 683, 173 S. W. 109.

(5) Joint or several judgment may be rendered in action against trespassers. Alexander v. Humber, 86 Ky. 565. 9 R. 734. 6 S. W. 453; Central Pass. Co. v. Kuhn, 86 Ky. 578, 9 R. 725, 6 S. W. 441; and notes to sec. 373.

§ 369. Judgment in favor of one or two parties. Where a railroad company leased its track to another railroad company and the latter is sued for personal injuries, a verdict and judgment in favor of the lessee is no bar to a prosecution of the action against the lessor, although sued jointly. Louisville Bridge Co. v. Sieber, 157 Kv. 151, 162 S. W. 804.

307

# § 371 Judgment-In General.

# § 371 [400] [Dismissal of action without prejudice.]

An action, or any cause of action, may be dismissed without prejudice to a future action—

- [By plaintiff.] By the plaintiff, before the final submission of the case to the jury, or to the court, if the trial be by the court.
- [By court.] By the court—
- a. If the plaintiff fail to appear at the trial.
- b. For the want of necessary parties.
- c. On the application of a defendant, if there be others whom the plaintiff fails to prosecute with diligence.
- d. For disobedience by the plaintiff of an order concerning the proceedings in the action. In all other cases, upon the trial of the action, the decision must be upon the merits. (Upon failure of plaintiff to secure costs, sec. 617; see further as to dismissal, sec. 85.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 177, 190c, 377, 393c, 587.

# (6) Judgment-how construed.

Where an action is dismissed agreed it will be understood as a judgment against "the defendants," without naming them, will be a bar to a recovery on the same cause of action between the same parties. Hibler v. Shipp, 78 Ky. 64.

(Dismissal without prejudice-effect of. A dismissal without prejudice leaves the parties as if no action had been instituted, and when, on issue formed and proof heard, the court "dismisses the action without prejudice," it will be presumed that the case was not disposed of on its merits. Magill v. Mercantile Co., 81 Ky. 129, 4 R. 927.

# (7) In an action against several,

one of them being a nominal and unnecessary party between whom and the plaintiffs there was no litigation, a judgment against "the defendants," without discrimination, although erroneous, is a clerical misprision. C., H. & D. R. R. Co. v. Spratt, 2 Duv. 4; see further, notes to sec. 373.

(8) Replevying judgment by part of defendants. Where several persons are sued and judgment is rendered against some of them and replevied, it does not bar proceedings against the others. Burrus v. Anderson, 3 Met. 500; Nat. Bank v. Bryant, 13 Bush 419.

# (9) Judgment against part in action on contract.

Crump v. J. I. Case Machine Co., 123 S. W. 333, 136 Ky. 60.

# 371. (1) Counter-claim or set-off not affected by dismissal of action;

but after plaintiff has made motion to dismiss it is too late to file counter-claim. Sec. 372; Northwestern Ins. Co. v. Barbour, 95 Ky. 7, 15 R. 394, 23 S. W. 584.

# Defect of parties.

When there is a defect of parties the action should be dismissed without prejudice, unless within a reasonable time the defect is cured. Turpin v. Thompson, 2 Met. 420; Carpenter v. Miles, 17 B. M. 598.

# (3) Dismissed agreed-effect of.

When

308

Title [X] Judgment-In General. [§ 371

(5) Dismissal-when not a bar. Dismissal of a cross-bill as to one of the causes of action set up, with an express reservation as to all others matters, is no bar to another action for the matters reserved. Harrow v. Johnson, 3 Met. 578; and see notes 17-21 to sec. 368.

(5a) An order dismissed an action on a forthcoming bond brought on an unsigned judgment sustaining an attachment; it was not a bar to an action on the judgment when signed. Farris v. Matthew, 149 Ky. 455, 149 S. W. 896.

(5b) Plaintiff in an action on contract assigned its claim for value and on its motion the action was dismissed settled; no satisfaction was made by defendant. This order was not a bar in an action by the assignee to collect the claim. New Bell Jellico Co. v. Braznell's Admr., 149 Ky. 418, 149 S. W. 888.

(6) If a trial went off on a technical defect, or because the debt was not due, or because the court had no jurisdiction, or because of the temporary disability of the plaintiff to sue, or the like, the judgment will not be a bar to a future action. Birch v. Funk, 2 Met. 544; Pepper v. Donnelly, 87 Ky. 259, 10 R. 140, 8 S. W. 441; Yankey v. Sweeney, 85 Ky. 55, 8 R. 944, 2 S. W. 559; see notes 17-21, sec. 368.

(7) Dismissal of a petition absolutely, when it should have been dismissed without prejudice, will not bar another action on the same cause when the plaintiff in the action dismissed had no right of action; the legal effect is the same as if the petition had been dismissed without prejudice. Bracken Co. v. Robertson Co., 6 Bush 69; Hilton v. Hilton, 110 Ky. 522, 22 R. 1934, 62 S. W. 6.

(8) Where this order was made, "ordered that this cause be dismissed at defendant's cost, and leave is given the plaintiff to withdraw the note sued on by leaving a copy in the papers," it did not bar another action on the note. Hibler v. Shipp, 78 Ky. 64.

(9) Filing away case for want of prosecution is not a final order or bar to a subsequent action on the cause. Nickel v. Fallen, 15 R. 389; 7 R. 758.

(10) The practice of filing away cases is not authorized by the Code, but if a case has been filed away with leave to reinstate, the plaintiff is entitled upon notice and motion to that effect to have case redocketed. Whailey v. Myers, 7 R. 758.

(11) Final order. When an action is dismissed without prejudice because the plaintiff neglects or fails to have a guardian ad litem appointed for the infant defendants, it is the duty of the court to see that a guardian ad litem is appointed, and where the case is submitted before the appointment of the guardian the submission should be set aside.

309

# § 372 Judgment-In General.

# § 372 [401] [Trial of set-off or counter-claim.]

A defendant is entitled to a trial of a set-off or counter-claim, although the plaintiff dismiss his action or fail to appear. (As to judgment on, see sec. 387.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 377, 500, 849.

aside and a guardian appointed. C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Bowler, 9 Bush 468.

(18) In an action against an administrator and others on a bond, if the affidavit and demand required by law have not been made, the action should be dismissed without prejudice as to the administrator, but not as to the others. Rogers v. Mitchell, I Met. 22.

(19) In an action for the recovery of land and damages for its detention, the plaintiff dismissed his action for damages up to the filing of the suit. The averments of the petition did not claim damages after that time, and a judgment for $500 damages was erroneous. Shean v. Cunningham, 6 Bush 123.

(20) Right to dismiss without prejudice at any time before submission belongs to the plaintiff absolutely, and court has no discretion in the matter. Northwestern Ins. Co. v. Barbour, 95 Ky. 7, 15 R. 394, 23 S. W. 584; but a plaintiff cannot dismiss his petition without prejudice after an answer, and set-off or counter-claim has been tendered, although not filed, and thereby deprive the defendant of the right to a trial of his set-off. Ringo v. New Fariners' Bank, 101 Ky. 91, 19 R. 91, 39 S. W. 701; Glenny Glass Co. v. Taylor, 99 Ky. 24, 17 R. 1331, 34 S. W. 711; and see notes 11 and 19, and sec. 372.

(21) Time when motion may be made. Plaintiff has a right to dismiss without prejudice even after motion for peremptory instruction has been sustained, but before instruction is given to jury. Vetrree v. N. N. & B. V. R. R., 95 Ky. 314, 15 R. 680, 21 S. W. 1; Wilson v. Dupree, 24 R. 1456, 71 S. W. 645; Wilson v. Sullivan, 112 S. W. 1120: and see notes 12, 20.

(21a) A plaintiff has a right to dismiss the action, without prejudice, at any time before final submission. Ray v. Ellis, 162 Ky. 517, 172 S. W. 951.

(21b) A cause is finally submitted to a jury when all questions of law have been disposed of by the court and the instructions and papers have been delivered to the jury and they are authorized to proceed to a decision. Ohio Valley Elec. R. Co. v. Lowe, 167 Ky. 132, 180 S. W. 61.

(21c) After the court has verbally indicated its intention to sustain a motion for a peremptory instruction, and while the attorney for the plaintiff is discussing the propriety of such ruling, and before delivery of such instructions to the jury for the signing thereof, the court may properly sustain plaintiff's motion to dismiss without prejudice.

(22) Trespassers. Any one or all of joint trespassers may be sued, and a dismissal as to, or release of, one or more who are sued does not release the others. Sellards v. Zomes, 5 Bush 90; Buckles v. Lambert, 4 Met, 330. See further, notes to secs. 370, 373.

(23) Dismissal for failure to pay cost is error. Hobbs v. L. H. & St. L. R. Co., 102 S. W. 818. 31 R. 452, 126 Ky. 1; Wilson v. Sullivan, 112 S. W. 1120.

(24) After submission to the court it is too late to demand right to dismiss without prejudice, but the court may allow it to be done. Welhelm v. Bains, 147 Ky. 832, 145 S. W. 1125; Williamson v. American Bond Co., 140 Ky. 215, 130 S. W. 1072.

(25) Court may dismiss case set down to a day certain for trial although the attorneys agree on a continuance. Conrad Fruit Co. v. Bondurant, 121 S. W. 482, 134 Ky. 568.

(26) Striking case from docket without any other order is in effect a dismissal without prejudice. Wilson v. De Loach, 96 S. W. 514, 29 R. 823, 123 Ky. 393; Porter v. Redmond, 96 S. W. 529, 29 R. 846, 123 Ky. 400.

(27) Finality of judgment. Under subsec. 2, a trial court may not dismiss an action absolutely, except upon motion of the plaintiff, or upon final hearing on the merits. Tandy & Farleigh Tob. Co. v. Briggance & Price, 181 Ky. 13.

§372. (1 Set-off-counter-claim. Although the plaintiff may dismiss his petition, the defendant is entitled to a trial of his set-off and counter-claim.

Title IX] Judgment-In General.

# § 373 [402] [Judgment may be against part of defendants.]

Though several defendants are summoned, judgment may be rendered against any of them, if the plaintiff would have been entitled to judgments against them in an action against them alone. (When several judgments not allowed, sec. 80; see further as to when actions stand for trial, secs. 363, 370, 367, 367a.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 173d, 439.

# § 374 [408] [Enforcement of lien not to be postponed.]

In an action to enforce a mortgage or other lien, a sale of the property may be ordered without giving time to pay money or do other act. (Proceeding if several liens on same real property, sec. 694.)

shears v. Letcher Co., 19 R. 478, 41 S. W. 22; but this right only exists where the counter-claim or set-off has been fled in court before the plaintiff has made a motion to dismiss his petition. Northwestern Ins. Co. v. Barbour, 95 Ky. 7, 15 R. 394, 23 S. W. 584; but see Ringo v. New Farmers' Bank, 101 Ky. 91, 19 R. 91, 30 S. W. 701, modifying this rule.

(1a) In an action on a note by an indorsee fraud was pleaded and a cancellation was prayed. This was a counter-claim. Defendant was entitled to a trial of same, notwithstanding a dismissal of the petition without prejudice. (Subsec, Sparr v. Fulton Nat. Bank, 179 Ky. 755, 201 S. W. 310.)

(1b) An insolvent corporation sued for the appointment of a receiver and a sale of its property; a holder of some of its mortgage bonds being a defendant filed an answer and asked for the appointment of a receiver and showed good grounds for same. The receivership was properly granted. Lewis' Admr. v. Bowling G. Ry. Co., 155 Ky. 681, 160 S. W. 212.

(2) Dismissal of petition because plaintiff had no capacity to sue carries with it a counter-claim. McClelland v. Troendle, 09 S. W. 329, 30 R. 611.

# § 373.

(1) Answer by one of several obligors. In an action against several obligors on a contract, a plea of payment or release, which extinguishes the demand sued on, inures to the benefit of all, and no judgment can be rendered against any of them until the plea is disposed of. But a personal plea, such as infancy, non est factum, or coverture, by one does not prevent judgment from being taken against the others. Rouse v. Howard, 1 Duv. 31.

(2) Judgment against one of several obligors. In an action upon a joint un-

# § 374.

(1) Mortgage, security for debt only. A mortgage is a mere security for the debt, and the mortgagor is the real owner of the property mortgaged, and is entitled to the use and profits thereof. The rents can only be claimed by the mortgagee by virtue of a contract, or when a receiver has been appointed.

311

# Judgment-In General

# §375 [404] [Foreclosure of mortgage forbidden.]

Foreclosure of a mortgage is forbidden.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 155, 176c, 184f.

# § 376 [408] [Enforcement of lien and personal judgment.]

In an action to enforce a mortgage or lien, judgment may be rendered for the sale of the property and for the recovery of the debt against the defendant personally. (Form of judgment, Form 195.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 25d, 392g; Form No. 116.

Woolley v. Holt, 14 Bush 78s. See further, notes to sec. 299; and secs. 692, 694 and notes thereto, as to enforcement of liens and practice concerning.

# 2) Sale by mortgagee.

Provision in mortgage permitting sale of property by mortgagee is void. Wilson v. Aultman, 91 Ky. 299, 12 R. 881, 15 S. W. 783; The Aultman-Taylor Co. v. Meade, 28 R. 208, 121 Ky. 241, 89 S. W. 137.

# §375. (1) Foreclosure defined.

"A proceeding in chancery by which the mortgagor's right of redemption of the mortgaged property is barred forever. This takes place when the mortgagor has forfeited his estate by non-payment of the money due on the mortgage at the time appointed, but still retains the equity of redemption; in such case the mortgagee may file a bill calling on the mortgagor in a court of equity to redeem his estate presently, or in default thereof to be forever barred from any right of redemption." Bouvier Law Dict.

# (2) Status of mortgagee who undertakes in violation of this section to foreclose a mortgage.

Aultman & Taylor Co. v. Meade, 89 S. W. 137, 28 R. 208, 121 Kv. 241.

# (3) Mortgaged personalty.

Where personal property is sold, the seller reserving title until payment of the purchase-price, and the transaction is construed as a sale passing the title to the buyer with a mortgage back for the purchase-price, the seller may, after lawfully taking possession as provided by the contract, sell the property at a fair public or private sale, upon adequate notice to the purchaser. Montenegro-Riehm Music Co. v. Beuris, 160 Ky. 557, 169 S. W. 896.

# § 376 (1) Judgment in personam and in rem.

Any rule of practice by which the assignee of notes secured by lien on land is prevented from proceeding at the same time to enforce his personal judgment and judgment in rem should not be adopted in case where the rights of the assignee may be jeopardized. Chambers v. Kecne, 1 Met. 289. An assignee, to entitle himself to recover against the assignor on the contract of assignment, must exhaust his remedies, both legal and equitable; and this section of the Code does not lessen the responsibility of the assignee. Rives v. Brown, S1 Ky. 636, 5 R. 745. See Carlisle v. Chambers, 4 Bush 268; 1 Met. 289.

# (2) Mortgagee of personal property—rights of.

"On a mortgage of personal property, after condition broken, the mortgagee may take possession of the mortgaged property, if he can peaceably get it, as authorized by the common law, which right has never been repealed by any statute." Brown v. Phillips, 3 Bush 656; and see Brookover v. Hurst, 1 Met. 665; The Aultman-Taylor Co. v. Meade, 28 R. 208, 121 Kv. 241, 89 S. W. 137.

# (3) Personal judgment.

In an action to enforce a lien on land personal judgment may be rendered against the defendant, although he has not been summoned in the county where action is pending. Collins v. Park, 93 Ky. 6. 13 R. 90.5, 18 S. W. 1013; Mitchell v. Fidelity Trust Co., 20 R. 713, 47 S. W. 446.

# (4) Practice.

The court may render a personal judgment and reserve the right to later pass on the question of a judgment in rem. Woolley v. Miller, 24 R. 1542, 71 S. W. 856.

# (5) Rents-practice as to when mortgagee entitled to rents, see sec. 299 and notes; as to practice, see numerous notes to 692, 694.

# (6) Stock, enforcement of lien on.

In an action on a note secured by lien on certain stock of a corporation, which had been taken over by another corporation, under the agreement of the latter corporation to issue its own stock, in lieu of the stock of the first corporation, the lien should have been enforced by sale. Ray v. Ellis, 162 Ky. 517, 172 S. W. 051.

Title IX] Judgment-Upon Failure to Plead.

# §377 [407] [Set-off of judgments for money.]

Judgments for the recovery of money may be set off against each other, with due regard to the legal and equitable rights of all persons interested therein. The set-off may be ordered upon motion, after reasonable notice to the adverse party, if both judgments are in the same court; or in an equitable action in the court which rendered the judgment sought to be annulled by the set-off.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 58a, 475d, 846; Forms Nos, 155, 156, 271.

# §378 [408] [Enjoining collection of judgment.]

During the pendency of an action, the judgment in which, when recovered, could be used as a set-off against a judgment in favor of the defendants or either of them, the court, to prevent loss by insolvency, non-residence or otherwise, may enjoin the collection of the judgment in favor of such defendants, according to the provisions of chapter IV of title VIII.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 475b; Forms Nos. 155, 156.

(1) Set-off of judgments-at- attorney's lien -equitable rights. The attorney's lien attaches to the claim in litigation at the commencement of the action, and cannot be defeated by setting off one judgment against another, as provided in this section. Robertson v. Shutt, 9 Bush 659; and see Ky. Stat. sec. 107.

(2) A judgment in the quarterly court in the form of a replevin bond may be set off against a judgment in the circuit court in an equitable action in the latter court. Smith v. Bohon, 12 Bush 418.

(3) The right to set off judgments can only be exercised in cases where the legal and equitable rights of all those interested in both judgments can be upheld; and where, pending an action on a note, the plaintiff assigned his claim, the assignment being filed when judgment in his favor was rendered, a judgment against the plaintiff, assigned to the defendant, could not be set off against the judgment obtained, and assigned by the plaintiff. Pfeiffer v. Harris, 11 Bush 400;

(4) A judgment debtor may obtain by assignment a judgment under a contingent agreement to pay for it and use it as a set-off against the judgment against him if the judgment creditor is insolvent. MeBrayer v. Dean, 100 Ky. 398, 18 R. S47, 38 S. W. 508; and see to same effect Bush v. Monroe, 20 R. 547, 47 S. W. 215; and cases cited in note 3.

(5) In an action to recover personal property which was exempt from execution, judgment went for the value of the property and damages for its detention. A judgment in favor of the defendant could not be set-off against this judgment. Grant v. Phoenix-Jellico Coal Co., 155 Ky. 585, 159 S. W. 1161.

# Judgment for uncontroverted part of claim.

If only a part of a claim be controverted, judgment may at any time be rendered for the part not controverted.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 564, 566, 567.

# § 381 [411] Parties to appear in person.

Any person may personally appear in a court of competent jurisdiction and, with the assent of a person having a cause of action against him, confess judgment therefor; whereupon judgment shall be entered accordingly. (Offer to confess— effect of, sec. 640; see Ky. Stat., sec. 416.)

# CONFESSION OF JUDGMENT.

# § 416. Power to confess judgment before action brought, void.

Powers of attorney to confess judgment, or to suffer judgment to pass by default, or otherwise, and every release of errors given before an action is instituted, are declared void. (Ky. Stat., sec. 416.)

# § 417. Penalty for attorney appearing under void power.

If any attorney, or other person, shall appear under such power for any defendant, in any court in this State, such attorney, or other person, shall forfeit and pay one thousand dollars to such defendant. (Ky. Stat., sec. 417.)

# § 418. Liability of attorney for damages.

He shall be, moreover, liable to damages at the suit of the party aggrieved. (Ky. Stat., sec. 418.)

# § 380. Judgment for uncontested amount.

A party has a right to ask judgment for the amount of his claim uncontested, without precluding himself from prosecuting his action to recover the residue claimed. See Sears v. Henry, 13 Bush 413.

Title IX] Judgment-Manner of Giving and Entering [9 382-386

# § 382 [412] [Cause of action to be stated.]

The cause of action shall be briefly stated in the judgment, or in a writing to be filed as pleadings in an action.

# § 383 [418] [Effect of judgment.]

Such judgment shall authorize the same proceedings as judgments rendered in actions, and the confession shall operate as a release of errors.

# § 384 [414] [Judgment in conformity to verdict-exception.]

After a jury-trial, the clerk shall enter judgment in conformity to the verdict, unless it be special as to one or more questions of fact, or unless the court reserve the case for consideration.

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 397, 414, 415, 416, 417

# § 385 [415] [Court to direct entry in certain cases.]

After such special verdict or such reservation the clerk shall enter such judgment or order as the court may direct.

# § 386 [416] [Judgment notwithstanding verdict.]

Judgment shall be

§ 384. (1) Verdict - judgment on. The verdict of a jury is not a judgment, and does not terminate the action. The court must render judgment, and can not refuse to render judgment in accordance with the verdict, under proper notice and motion made after the expiration of the time when a motion for a new trial can be made. Dunn v. Blue Grass Realty Co., 163 Ky. 384, 173 S. W. 1122.

(3 A certain judgment which did not conform to the verdict was erroneous. It seems in this case also that the judgment was not sustained by the pleadings. See further as to verdicts, notes to secs. 325, 326.

(2) If, in a state of a case where the amount of damages is fixed by law, a jury renders a verdict for the plaintiff without specifying the sum, the verdict is sufficient; but if the jury specifies the amount, which is erroneous, the court must conform the judgment to the verdict. The remedy is by granting a new trial. Fuqua v. Mullen, 13 Bush 467.

§ 386. (1) Judgment against the verdict. In an action on a note, the defendant pleaded that the note was executed without consideration, and was obtained by fraud, covin and misrepresentation.

Judgment-Manner of Giving and Entering
