# § 428 1465] [Who may institute action-necessary parties.

1. Who may institute action. A representative, legatee, distributee or creditor of a deceased person may bring an action in equity for the settlement of his estate provided that no such suit shall be brought by any of the parties named except the personal representative until the expiration of six months after the qualification of such representative.

§ 428. (1) Action to settle estate may be brought within less than six months after appointment of personal representative, and at any time after his qualification. Holland v. Lowe, 101 Ky. 98, 19 R. 97, 39 S. W. 834; 41 S. W. 9; Maddox v. Williams. 5 R. 696; Brand v. Brand. 109 Ky. 721. 22 R. 1366, 60 S. W. 704; Faukner v. Tucker, 26 R. 1130, 83 S. W. 579; Alderson v. Alderson, 26 R. 1260, 83 S. W. 1129: Ky. Stat., sec. 3847.

By Act of 1918 (Sess. Acts, p. 657), no person can bring this action within the six months except the personal representative.-Ed.

(la) An action to settle an intestate's estate was brought within six months from his death; a purchaser from an heir pending the suit acquired no interest in the land, as claims against the estate consumed the property. It does not appear in the opinion that the notice of action mentioned in Ky. Stat., sec. 2358a, was filed. Robertson v. Hines, 154 Ky. 364. 157 S. W. 704.

(2) When the time in which a settlement shall be made is fixed by the will, creditors but not heirs or devisees can institute an action before that time. Linthicum v. Vowels, 26 R. 221, 80 S. W. 1090.

(3) Administrator-action by not favored unless the personal estate that comes into his hand is insufficient to pay the debts; if no personal estate has come into his hands he should not be allowed to burden the estate with costs and attorney fees. Holburn v. Pfanmiller, 114 Ky. 331, 24 R. 1613, 71 S. W. 940; Hall v. Metcalfe. 114 Ky. 886, 24 R. 1660, 72 S. W. 18; Thumb v. Gresham, 2 Met. 306.

(4) Lis pendens created by institution of action. Alderson v. Alderson, 26 R. 1260, 83 S. W. 1129; Ky. Stat., sec. 2087.

(5) Parties. In action to settle an estate no persons except those mentioned in this section are necessary or proper parties. Debtors of the estate are not necessary or proper parties. Citizens Nat. Bank v. Boswell. 93 Ky. 92. 14 R. 17, 19 S. W. 174; Hilton v. Hilton, 110 Ky. 522, 22 R. 1934, 62 S. W. 6. While all creditors are not necessary parties, they are entitled to be heard, and in some cases the general creditors are necessary parties, as where some of the

Settlement of Trust Estates

2. [Who must be made parties.] The representatives of the decedent and all persons having a lien upon or an interest in the property left by the decedent, or any part thereof, and the creditors of the decedent, so far as known to the plaintiff, must be parties to the action as plaintiffs or defendants. (Words in brackets added to subsection 1 by Act of March -, 1918, c. 155, p. 657. Venue of action, secs. 65, 66. Meaning of "representative," sec. 732-18, 19. See further sec. 438.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 27a, 78, 121, 180c, 180g, 468a; Form No. 142.

Creditors are claiming liens upon property that the testator has charged with the payment of his general debts. Payne v. Johnson, 95 Ky. 175, 15 R. 522, 24 S. W. 238, 609.

(5a) An action for settlement brought by a widow of a decedent in which she did not make his heirs or distributees parties was properly dismissed on special demurrer. Hackett v. State Bank & Tr. Co. and Ira Scudder, Ex. of the will of B. C. Hackett, deceased, and Ira Scudder, Trus., 155 Ky. 392, 159 S. W. 952.

(5b) A statutory guardian of an infant died; in an action brought by his successor and by his personal representative for the purpose of settling his estate and his accounts as guardian, and for the purpose of recovering from his surety and from a bank that had received the proceeds of a certain note, the amount due the ward, the surety and the bank and a person who was alleged to have assisted the deceased guardian to appropriate fraudulently to the guardian's use a portion of those proceeds, were properly made defendants. Taylor v. Harris' Admr., 164 Ky. 654, 176 S. W. 168.

(5c) In an action to settle a decedent's estate seeking to sell his land to pay his debts, the failure to make the wives of his heirs parties is cured, if after judgment, said wives become parties by pleading, and confirm what has been done upon privy examination, and their right to compensation for inchoate dower is preserved. These steps may be taken after exceptions to the sale are filed. Barry v. Fain's Admr., 172 Ky. 308, 189 S. W. 220.

(10) Fraudulent conveyance. If the decedent or his heirs have in fraud of the rights of creditors conveyed property, the conveyances may be attacked in an action to settle the estate. Elliott v. Scoville, 144 Ky. 584, 139 S. W. 806.

(11) Guardian of infant creditor may bring suit to settle estate. Beddow v. Wilson, 90 S. W. 228, 28 R. 661.

Title X] Settlement of Trust Estates. [§ 429

# 429 [468] Averments of petition-sale of real estate.

In such an action the petition must state the amount of the debts and the nature and value of the property, real and personal, of the decedent, so far as known to the plaintiff; and, if it shall appear that the personal estate is insufficient for the payment of all debts, the court may order the real property descended or devised to the heirs or devisees who may be parties to the action, or so much thereof as shall be necessary, to be sold for the payment of the residue of such debts.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 25, 27a, 468a.

# 3882. Choses in action—sale of-compromise of claims.

In actions for the settlement of decedents' estates, the court may direct the sale of choses in action, including judgments, and may authorize the personal representative to compromise claims growing out of contract or tort due the estate, as well as claims on contract or tort against the estate; and a personal representative may compromise and settle any claim or demand for damages growing out of injury to or the death of the decedent. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3882.)

# (12) Partial distribution in kind.

In a settlement suit under this action, where administratrix's answer showed that there are no debts due by the estate, it was held proper for the court to make an order directing her to make a partial distribution by delivering to plaintiff certain designated bonds. Stratton v. Wilson. 168 Ky. 699, 182 S. W. 858.

# (13) Preference-not allowed to creditor obtaining return of nulla bona.

A creditor of a decedent obtained a judgment against the administrator and a return of nulla bona. He could not by action under this section obtain a preference over other creditors. Benge's Admr. v. Creech, 163 Ky. 810, 174 S. W. 517.

# (14) Subrogation.

An executor will be subrogated to the rights of creditors whose debts he has paid. Franzell's Exr. v. Franzell, 153 Ky. 171, 154 S. W. 912.

# (15) Exceptions to sale.

An exception of a purchaser at a judicial sale, that the depositions on which the judgment for sale was rendered were taken without notice, was rightfully overruled. Rothchild v. Wallace, 155 Ky. 676, 160 S. W. 170.

# (16) Misnomer-amendment.

A sheriff to whom the estate of a decedent was committed brought suit to settle the decedent's estate styling himself "eurator." Afterward he amended showing the facts. The sale was sustained. Pierce v. Marrs, 153 Ky. 748, 156 S. W. 404.

# (17) Judgment-finality of.

In a suit to settle a decedent's estate an order confirming the report of a commissioner which disallows the claim is a final judgment against such claim, and is a bar to a subsequent assertion of such a claim in the same action. Wood v. Sharp's Admr., 159 Ky. 46, 166 S. W. 787.

# (18) In an action to settle a decedent's estate.

The circuit court may at a term after judgment has been rendered fixing the amount of advancements, permit the filing of amended and supplemental pleadings for the purpose of procuring a sale to satisfy the debts. Aylor v. Aylor, 158 Ky. 713, 166 S. W. 216.

# (19) Void judgment-not curable after sale-lunatic.

A judgment directing the sale of land in which a person of unsound mind had an interest was void for failure to serve process on his committee. This could not be cured by the filing of a pleading by the committee after a sale under the judgment. Barry v. Fair's Admr., 172 Ky. 308, 189 S. W. 220.

# §429.

(1) Judgment to sell more land than necessary. In an action to settle the estate of a decedent, a judgment ordering a sale of the whole tract, when it is only necessary to sell a portion of it to pay the debts, is void if there are infant defendants. Only a sufficiency of the land to pay the debts should be sold. Gill v. Given, 4 Met. 197; Auxier v. Clarke. 26 R. 890, 82 S. W. 605. See Thornton v. MeGrath, 1 Duv. 349, and note 16. sec. 489.

(2) Petition-requisites of. See Holland v. Lowe, 101 Ky. 9S, 19 R. 97, 39 S. W. 834; 41 S. W. 9; Rodgers v. Rodgers, 17 R. 358, 31 S. W. 139; Meyer v. Zotel, 96 Ky. 362, 16 R. 506, 29 S. W. 28; Faulkner v. Tucker, 26 R. 1130, 83

# Settlement of Trust Estates

# § 430 [467] [Reference to commissioner—notice to creditors.]

In all such actions the court shall make an order for the creditors of the decedent to appear before a commissioner, to be appointed by the court, and prove their claims before a certain day to be named in the order, notice of which shall be given by advertisement in a newspaper; or, if none be published in the county, then by such other modes as the court may judge best.

S. W. 679: Tabb v. Wortham, S9 S. W. 191, 28 R. 260; Harris v. Harris, 147 Ky. 713, 145 S. W. 369; Carter v. Crow, 112 S. W. 1098, 130 Ky. 41; Staton v. Byron, 105 S. W. 928, 32 R. 246; and notes sec. 428.

# (2a) In a suit to settle the estate of a decedent

a sale of real estate was not erroneous although there was no testimony showing that the deceased left no personal property or that the land was indivisible. It clearly appeared from the record that he left no personal property, and that the land was indivisible. Pierce v. Marrs, 153 Ky. 748, 156 S. W. 404.

# (3) Sale of real estate may be ordered

before an order is made referring case to a commissioner for proof of claims, and before there is any report of claims if court is satisfied that the personal estate is not sufficient to pay the debts. Harlammert v. Moody, 15 R. 830, 26 S. W. 2; Rodgers v. Rodgers, 17 R. 358, 31 S. W. 139; Union T. & S. Co. v. Marshall, 113 S. W. 73, 130 Ky. 206; and see further, as to sale of infant's real estate to pay debt of ancestor, sec. 489.

# (3a) Undivided interest.

A sale of an undivided interest of a decedent in land for payment of his debts was valid. His heirs were infants. Auxier v. Auxier; Auxier v. Williams Coal Co., 180 Ky. 518, 203 S. W. 310.

# (3b) Purchase by administrator as such-effect of.

In a suit to settle the estate of a deceased testator and the estate of another decedent the administrator of said testator bought land of said testator and paid out of the assets of testator the share of the heirs of the other decedent, and took a deed to himself as administrator of said testator. A private sale of this land afterward made by him was void. The devisees in this case were infants. Schlickman v. Dusing, 180 Ky. 506, 203 S. W. 295.

# (4) Title papers-not required to be filed in proceeding under chapter.

Rodgers v. Rodgers, 17 R. 358, 31 S. W. 139. Indivisible property. If it is necessary to sell more property than will pay the debts and there are infant defendants their rights should be protected in the manner pointed out in secs. 490-46 of Code. Foley v. Graham, 110 S. W. 38; Carter v. Crow, 112 S. W. 1098, 130 Ky. 41; Foley v. Graham, 110 S. W. 838, 32 R. 762.

# (5a) The homestead of a person in indivisible realty

attaches for a reasonable time to the funds arising from a sale for indivisibility. Staun v. Proctor, Jr., 152 Ky. 142, 153 S. W. 196.

# (6) Sale of land when not necessary to pay debts is erroneous.

Davidson v. Marcum, 89 S. W. 703, 28 R. 562.

# (6a) In an action to sell land of a decedent to pay his debt

if an heir of decedent is an infant defendant a judgment directing a sale is erroneous unless it appear affirmatively both by pleading and evidence that the decedent's personalty is not sufficient to pay his debts. A settlement of decedent's administrator referred to in the petition as an exhibit, but not filed until after the sale will not sustain the judgment or the sale. Luscher v. Julian's Admr., 173 Ky. 150, 190 S. W. 692.

# (7) Proof of averments of petition is necessary

before land of infants can be sold. Tabb v. Wortham, 89 S. W. 191, 28 R. 260; Carter v. Crow, 112 S. W. 1098, 130 Ky. 41.

# (8) Proceeds a fund in court.

The proceeds of a judicial sale to enforce a lien, so long as they are represented by uncollected sale bonds payable to the commissioner who made the sale are a fund in court, and cannot be subjected to a debt of the lien creditor by an attachment served on the commissioner; service must be made on the clerk of the court. Patton v. Com., 176 Ky. 180, 195 S. W. 455.

# (9) Proceeds of sale of realty of a decedent for division among his heirs,

which proceeds are represented by uncollected sale bonds payable to the commissioner who made the sale, are a fund in court within Civil Code, sec. 207. Burdine v. White, 173 Ky. 158, 190 S. W. 687.

# 430. (1 Claims-when to be filed.

After an action to settle an estate is

Title X] Settlement of Trust Estates.

# § 431. [Order of reference in vacation-clerk may make—publication of notice.]

The clerk of the court shall, during vacation, have the powers conferred on the court by section 430, except that the creditors shall be required to appear before the master commissioner in chancery for the county; and, if no newspaper be published in the county, the clerk shall cause the notice to be posted at the courthouse door and at two or more other public places in the county.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 27a, 18Oc, 468a.

# § 432 [468] [Creditor presenting claim becomes party—effect.]

A commenced, it is in the sound discretion of the chancellor to prescribe the time within which creditors may present their claims. Gray v. Lewis, 79 Ky. 453, 3 K. 234.

1. Duty of creditor to file claim. When a creditor is made defendant to a suit to settle an estate, and served with process, he should be compelled to either set up or abandon his claim; and if he fails to present it, its recovery against the administrator, legatees and distributees is barred. Hood v. Hood, 80 Ky. 39, 3 R. 515.
2. When the provisions of the Code are substantially complied with, a creditor who has failed to present his claim until after estate is settled and funds distributed, can only look to the legatees and distributees, as provided in sec. 434. Hill v. Mayes, 25 R. 2023, 79 S. W. 276.
3. Order of reference. As the Code imperatively requires an order of reference to be made in all cases for the settlement of estates of deceased persons, such orders are ministerial, and not judicial acts. Smith v. Cochran, 7 Bush 5548.
4. Limitation. Filing claim with the administrator of the principal, in a suit to settle his estate, did not prevent the statute of limitation from running as to the surety, although the claim was filed by request of the surety. Harris v. Moberly, 5 Bush 556.
5. Limitation, pleaded by exception. In an action to settle a decedent's estate, the five-year statute of limitations was pleaded by exceptions filed to the report of the commissioner allowing a claim of the administrator. It was sustained. The action was brought in 1911 and the petition stated that the decedent died in 1906. Johnson's Admr. v. Johnson, Gdn., 155 Kv. 9, 159 S. W. 606.
6. Practice regarding presenting of claims. The usual practice in actions for the settlement of the estates of deceased persons is for creditors to present their claims, verified and proved as required by statute. Upon exceptions to the master's report, the sufficiency of the verification and proof can always be inquired into; and in such cases it seems allowable to investigate and pass upon the merits of the claim. But a creditor has a right to file his petition setting up his claim, and when it is controverted he has the right to demand that the grounds of defense be stated, according to the rules of pleading, in the form of a verified answer, or by exception containing the denials or allegations necessary to present a defense, Horner v. Harris, 10 Bush 357; Callender v. Callender, 24 R. 1145, 70 S. W. 844. See Francis v. Smith, 1 Duv. 121.
7. Finality of judgment. A commissioner's report in an action to settle a decedent's estate disallowed a claim. An order confirming this report was a final judgment against the claim. The circuit court after the term had no power to vacate or modify same in the absence of some of the grounds in Code, sec. 518. Culver v. Lutz, 171 Ky. 691, 189 S. W. 240.

# § 432. (1) Creditor presenting claim before commissioner becomes a party to the action and is concluded by final judgment.

# Settlement of Trust Estates

creditor appearing before the commissioner and presenting his claim becomes thereby a party to the action, and is concluded by the final judgment of the court allowing or rejecting his claim.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 27a, 151g, 180c, 468a, 508a.

# § 433 [469] [Creditor failing to present claim—effect.]

Creditors failing to appear and prove their claims, agreeably to such order, shall have no claim against the executor or administrator who has actually paid out the estate in expenses of administration, and to creditors, legatees or distributees.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 27a, 468a.

# § 434 [470] [Legatees and distributees liable to creditor.]

Legatees and distributees shall be liable to a direct action by a creditor to the extent of estate received by each of them, notwithstanding the failure of the creditor to appear and the discharge of the personal representative as prescribed in the preceding section; and that liability shall continue during the same period that the liability of the personal representative would have continued but for said discharge. (See further Ky. Stat., sec. 2084.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 27a, 160l, 468a.

ment. Heidrich v. Silva, 9 Ky. 422, 11 R. 645, 12 S. W. 770; and see further as to rights of creditors, Duff v. Combs, 117 S. W. 260, 132 Ky. 710.

# (2) Practice in presenting claims.

See note 6, sec. 430.

# § 433. Failure to present claim.

Creditor being made a party to a suit for the settlement of an estate, and having failed to present his claim, has no claim against the administrator after he has paid out the entire estate. The judgment of distribution is binding as long as it is in force. Stull v. Davidson, 12 Bush 167; see Story v. Story, 18 R. 97; 35 S. W. 540; and notes 2, 3, sec. 430.

# § 434. (1) Action by infant ward against creditors.

An infant ward failed to present her claim in the settlement and distribution of the estate of her guardian, under the act of 1856. After arriving at age, she could sue the creditors who had received the estate, and require them to contribute pro rata to the payment of her claim. Roberts v. Phillips, 11 Bush 11.

# (2) Creditor to present claim - bar.

When devisees, as well as creditors, are made parties, and one of the creditors holds a claim which, if allowed, would alter the division of the estate, such creditor should be required to present his claim, or have it barred against legatees and distributees, as well as the administrator. Hood v. Hood, 80 Ky. 39, 3 R. 515; Story v. Story, 18 R. 97, 35 S. W. 540; Benson v. Simmers, 21 R. 1060, 53 S. W. 1035; Hill v. Mayes, 25 R. 2023, 79 S. W. 276.

# (3) Legatee or distributee, who has received assets to the extent of the creditor's claim, may be sued for the whole amount of claim and required to look to his co-distributees for contribution.

Rubell v. Bushnell, 91 Ky. 251, 12 R. 816, 15 S. W. 520; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 2084, for further provisions concerning liability of heirs.

# (4) It is error in action against heirs to render a joint judgment against them.

The amount each is liable for should be ascertained and judgment rendered therefor. Ransdell v. Threlkeld, 4 Bush 347.

# (5) It is suggested in this opinion that this section as to the liability of legatees and distributees applies to the case of one who has received assets of an intestate or testator in another State, if there has been no administration in Kentucky.

Bennett v. Miller, 159 Ky. 104, 166 S. W. 805.

# (6) It is immaterial to the liability of heirs or devisees that the assets received by them were lands in another State.

Taylor's Extrx. v. Jefferson, 167 Ky. 454, 180 S. W. 801.

Title X] Settlement of Trust Estates. [9 435-437

# § 435 [471] [Refunding bond required of distributees and legatees.]

[The court may, in its discretion, without motion, and shall, on motion of any party in interest, require the distributee or legatee, before receiving his distributable share or legacy, if received within five years after the grant of administration, to execute bond, with good surety to the Commonwealth, conditioned to pay his proportion, not exceeding the amount received by him, of any debt which may appear against the estate of the decedent within said period.] (Section as amended by Act of April 13, 1880, c. 973, p. 77. Form of bond, Form 99; and see as to bond, Ky. Stat., sec. 3843.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 27a, 468a; Form No. 396.

# § 436 [472] [Creditors may be enjoined from suing.]

Upon the institution of the action mentioned in this chapter, an order may be made enjoining the prosecution of actions against the representatives of a decedent, by creditors, for their demands. But such enjoining shall not be made, unless the action for the settlement of the estate be commenced within three years after the qualification of the personal representative.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 27a, 468a.

# § 437 [478] [Affidavit and demand necessary—exception.]

The provisions of sections 35 to 39, both inclusive, of article 2. chapter 39,* General Statutes, concerning decedent's estates, are adopted as part of this Code, except that the demand therein mentioned need not be made if the personal representative of the decedent be a non-resident of this State or shall have been absent therefrom four months. (These sections of the Statutes require creditor to make affidavit and demand payment. Form of affidavit, Forms 112, 113.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 27a, 78, 468a; Form No. 38.

# § 435. (1 Refunding bond.

It was held under the Code of 1854, sec. 471, to be error to render judgment even by default in favor of heirs or distributees against an administrator, without requiring a refunding bond. Mountjoy v. Pearce, 4 Met, 98; but observe amendment, and see Fleming v. Jones, 12 Bush 503; and Ky. Stat., sec. 3843.

Injunction granted under this section did not prevent a ward from bringing an action against estate of county judge to recover damages for failure to take a sufficient bond from guardian. Grigsby v. Wilkinson, 9 Bush 91; but see Wilkinson v. Rosser, 104 S. W. 1019, 31 R. 1264.

# 437. (1 Affidavit-sufficiency of.

It is necessary that the affidavit of the claimant should state that there is no "offset or discount" against the claim. "Offset" alone is not sufficient. If the debt is an account it must be proved by.

# Settlement of Trust Estates

Said sections are now, respectively, superseded by that part of the Act of March 3, 1893, now compiled as Sections 3870 to 3874, both inclusive, of the Kentucky Statutes, as follows:

# § 3870. Claims against decedent's estate-how verified.

All demands against the estate of a decedent shall be verified by the written affidavit of the claimant, or, in his absence from the State, by his agent, or, if dead, by his personal representative, stating that the demand is just, and has never to his knowledge or belief been paid, and that there is no offset or discount against the same, or any usury therein; and if the demand be other than an obligation signed by the decedent or a judgment, it shall also be verified by a person other than the claimant, who shall state in his affidavit that he believes the claim to be just and correct, and give the reasons why he so believes. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3870.)

The affidavit of a competent witness, stating that he knows it to be just and correct. Trabue v. Harris, 1 Met. 597; and must set out the reasons or facts why he believes claim to be just. Dewhurst v. Shepherd, 102 Ky. 239, 19 R. 1260, 43 S. W. 253; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 3870.

1. The affidavit must state that there is no offset or discount; that there is no just offset or discount will not do. Leach v. Kendall, 13 Bush 424; Prewitt v. Wortham, 79 Ky. 287, 2 R. 282; see Greer v. Powell, 1 Bush 489; if the debt be evidenced by note or other writing, the presentation of the note, accompanied with the affidavit of the claimant, is all that is necessary; but if the claim is an account, it must be proved by the claimant and another person, unless it cannot be proved by any person except the personal representative; and if he refuses to give an affidavit, the affidavit of the claimant alone will be sufficient. Trabue v. Harris, 1 Met. 597. See Ky. Stat., sec. 3870, and note requirement as to verification of accounts, and Crane & Breed Mfg. Co. v. Stagg, 122 S. W. 225, 135 Ky. 428.
2. Witness in verifying claim must speak of his own personal knowledge, and when he states facts unqualifiedly it will be presumed that he does so, Sherley v. Sherley, 13 R. 565, 17 S. W. 628.
3. Agent may make affidavit--waiver. The affidavit may be made by an agent if the creditor is absent from the State; and the demand may be waived by the administrator before or after suit. See this case for a statement of facts evidencing a waiver. Howard v. Leavell, 10 Bush 481.
4. Claimant should file copies of records with his claim, when they are necessary to support it. Sherley v. Sherley, 13 R. 565, 17 S. W. 628.
5. Demand is only necessary when the suit is against the personal representative, but before judgment is rendered.

Title X] Settlement of Trust Estates. [§ 437

# 3871. Affidavit—what it shall state—proof of claim not dispensed with.

If any part of the demand has been paid, or there be any set-off or discount against the same, or any usury therein, the affidavit shall state the amount of the payment or usury, when the payment was made, and when the offset or discount was due, to the best of the affiant's knowledge and belief. This verification shall not be held to dispense with other proof of the demand as required by law. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3871.)

# 3872. Demand and verification before suit brought.

Before such affidavit is made, no action shall be brought or recovery had on any such demand, nor until demand of payment thereof has been made of the personal representative, accompanied by the required affidavit. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3872.)

# § 3873. Action—when may be brought without additional proof.

When any person has such a demand, and in his affidavit states he is unable to prove it otherwise than by the personal or other representatives of the decedent, and that they refuse to make affidavit, he may bring an action therefor, after proper demand made of said personal representatives, without proof or affidavit other than his own. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3873.)

# 3874. Claim not to be paid or allowed unless verified.

No demand against a decedent's estate shall be paid by his personal representative, or allowed as a credit by any commissioner or court, which is not verified by affidavit as required herein. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3874.)

(17) Objection to be made by rule. If affidavit and demand have not been made, the proper time to make the objection by rule is before answer is filed. Thomas v. Thomas, 15 B. M. 178. But in Nuttall v. Cockrill v. Mize, 11 R. 637, 12 S. W. 1040.

# § 438. [Provisions of this chapter applicable to trust estates.]

The foregoing provisions of this chapter shall, so far as applicable, regulate proceedings for the sale of property held in trust, by virtue of the provisions of article 2, of chapter 44, of the General Statutes, or assigned by a debtor for the payment of debts generally; but subsection 2 of section 428 shall not be so construed as to require all creditors holding liens upon the property by virtue of the statute, or under the deed of assignment, to be made parties.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 27a, 185c.

(22) Affidavit must conform to laws of this State if made in another State. Crane & Breed Mfg. Co. v. Stagg, I22 S. W. 225, 135 Ky. 428.

# § 438. (1) Parties to action-practice - attorney fees.

In an action to settle a trust estate, all persons having an interest in the property, or any part thereof, conveyed by the deed, and the creditors of the assignee, so far as known, except creditors holding liens upon the property, should be made parties to the action, either as plaintiffs or defendants. Dobyns v. Dobyns, 79 Ky. 95, 2 R. 274; and see as to voluntary assignments, Ky. Stat., sec. 74.

# Attorney fees-costs commission - practice.

See Ky. Nat. Bank v. Louisville Bagging Co., 98 Ky. 371. 17 R. 983, 33 S. W. 101; Courier-Journal v. Columbia Fire Ins. Co., 21 R. 1258, 54 S. W. 966; Columbia Trust Co. v. Morgan, 19 R. 1761, 44 S. W. 389, 628; 45 S. W. 65; Farmers Bank v. Norton, 19 R. 1773, 44 S. W. 428.

# (3) Action to settle estate may be instituted at any time by a creditor without alleging that assignee is in default in any respect.

Bohmer v. Louisville D. Bank, 17 R. 16, 30 S. W. 874.

# (4) Suit by one creditor inures to the benefit of all.

A suit by one creditor under the act of 1856, if commenced in time and prosecuted to a successful termination, inures to the benefit of all the creditors, whether they sue or not. Roberts v. Phillips, 11 Bush 11; Heidrich v. Silva, 89 Ky. 422, 11 R. 645, 12 S. W. 770; and a creditor who has filed his action in time cannot dismiss it nor can it be filed away by the court so as to defeat the claims of other creditors who had filed their claims and made themselves parties to the action. Sawyers v. Langford, 5 Bush 539; nor can a creditor by refusing to appeal from judgment dismissing his claim deprive other creditors of the right to prosecute an appeal. Heidrich v. Silva, 89 Ky. 422, 11 R. 645, 12 S. W. 770; and see Linthicum v. Fenley, 11 Bush 131.

# (4a) Injunction - to what extent granted.

In an action for settlement of an insolvent assignment a creditor will not be enjoined from seeking in another action a personal judgment. The injunction will go to prevent an enforcement of a lien and to any proceedings affecting the assignee. Deposit Bank v. Kirby, 175 Ky. 700, 194 S. W. 929.

# (5) Verification of claims is now required by Ky. Stat., secs. 90, 1917.

Title X] Enforcing the Satisfaction of Judgments. [§ 439

# CHAPTER IV.

# ENFORCING THE SATISFACTION OF JUDGMENTS.

# § 439. Proceedings to enforce—when allowed—parties.

After an execution of fieri facias, directed to the county in which the judgment was rendered, or to the county of the defendant's residence, is returned by the proper officer, either as to the whole or part thereof, in substance, no property found to satisfy the same, the plaintiff in the execution may institute an equitable action for the discovery of any money, chose in action,

# § 439. (1) Action in circuit court on judgment of inferior court.

The circuit court has jurisdiction of an action to subject an equitable interest in real property to the satisfaction of a judgment rendered in the quarterly court, upon which an execution had been returned, no property found, both from the quarterly and the circuit court. Jones v. Jeffress, 11 Bush 636; but return from both courts is necessary. Clenments v. Waters, 90 Ky. 96, 11 R. 880, 13 S. W. 431; Behan v. Warfield, 90 Ky. 151, 11 R 960, 13 S. W. 439; and see Burns v. Cade, 10 Bush 251; Counts v. Howes, 98 Ky. 397, 17 R. 1071, 33 S. W. 395. Action may be brought in circuit court to subject personalty, upon a return of no property in a justice's court. Austin v. Payne, 7 Bush 480; but not land that can be levied on under an execution. Behan v. Warfield, 90 Ky. 96, 11 R. 880, 13 S. W. 431; Mansfield v. Wilkinson, 16 R. 276; Weatherford v. Myers, 2 Duv. 91.

# (3) Bastardy case-to enforce judgment in.

In a bastardy proceeding, judgment was rendered in the county court against the father. On a return of no property on this judgment, the circuit court had jurisdiction to set aside a fraudulent conveyance made by the father, and subject the property. Brightwell v. Com., 79 Ky. 537, 3 R. 310.

# (4) Conclusiveness of judgment.

Until reversed or vacated as provided by law, or attacked for fraud, judgment upon which action on return of no property is based is conclusive, and defendant cannot show he was not before court in contradiction of the record. Stevenson v. Flournoy, 89 Ky. 561, 11 R. 745, 13 S. W. 210; but judgment obtained by fraud may be attacked in action to enforce satisfaction of it. Fort Jeff. Imp. Co. v. Greene, 112 Ky. 85, 23 R. 1342, 65 S. W. 161; Duerson v. Semonin, 17 R. 51, 29 S. W. 635; and see as to rights of trustee and heirs to contest judgment against cestui que trust or personal representative. Roberts v. Yancey, 94 Ky. 243, 15 R. 101, 21 S. W. 1047; Willis v. Roberts, 90 Ky. 122, 11 R. 929, 13 S. W. 358.

# (4a) Corporation, judgment against firm sued as.

An action may be brought on a judgment which purports to be rendered against a corporation to impose liability on the members of a firm which was erroneously sued as if a corporation. Heavrins v. Jack Malleable Iron Co., 153 Ky. 329, 155 S. W. 729.

# (5) Creditor may maintain action upon return of no property.

In such action acquire a lien upon and subject property that was and is subject to execution. He has a right to rely upon officer's return, although it be false. Clements v. Waters, 90 Ky. 96, 11 R. 880, 13 S. W. 431.

# (6) Decedent's estate - interest of judgment defendant in may be attached by service on administrator.

Wilkerson v. Phillips, 26 R. 440, 81 S. W. 691.

# (7) Execution must be directed to and executed by an officer authorized to execute it, or the return will not authorize proceedings under this chapter.

(Vcnuc of action, sec. 70.)

# § 210. Action to subject land adversely held.

A petition in equity by a judgment creditor, after a return of execution of no property found, may be filed to subject the title of the defendant to any real estate to which he may have title, legal or equitable, though the same may be in the adverse possession of another, to which procedure the person in possession shall be made a defendant. (Ky. Stat., sec. 210.)

v. Elkin, 90 Ky. 163, 1l R. 967, 13 S. W. 448; when there are several execution defendants, residing in different counties, see as to proper proceeding, Proctor v. Bell, 97 Ky. 198, 16 R. 823; 30 S. W. 15; see notes to section 667 as to what officers must execute executions.

# (8) Garnishees.

As to proceedings against, see notes to secs. 223, 225.

# (9) Interest on costs of former action should not be allowed.

Cockrill v. Mize, 11 R. 637, 12 S. W. 1040.

# (10) Judgment-action on.

The only action that can be maintained on a judgment of a court of this State is the equitable action to enforce its satisfaction, provided in this section. Davidson v. Simmons, 11 Bush 330; and see Ritchey v. Buricke, 21 R. 1120, 54 S. W. 173.

# (11) Judgment superseded.

After a judgment has been superseded and the bond executed, no action can be brought to enforce its collection. Johnson v. Williams, 82 Ky. 45, 5 R. 733.

# (12) Lis pendens.

Lis pendens is created by filing a petition in which the property sought to be subjected is described. Caldwell v. Deposit Bank, 109 Ky. 197, 22 R. 684, 58'S. W. 5S9: Wilkerson v. Phillips, 26 K. 440. 81 S. W. 691; Nat. Bank v. Johnson, D6S. W. 433. 29 R. 728. and see cases cited in note 1, sec. 442.

# (13) Officer's return on execution.

A return by a deputy sheriff of "no property found," without adding "in my bailiwick," is sufficient. Evans v. Wait, 5 J. J. M. 110.

# (14) A return of "no property found."

A return of "no property found," made two days after the issuance of the execution, was held sufficient. Dana v. Banks, 6 J. J. M. 219.

# (15) Petition--necessary allegations in.

The petition must allege that the execution was directed to the county of

# (16) "The failure to aver in terms that judgment was still unsatisfied, if a material defect, was cured by an amendment alleging that fact, and did not vitiate the levy or lien created by the attachment."

Lewis v. Quinker, 2 Met. 284.

# (17) In an action upon a return of "no property found"

on an execution against an administrator to subject property voluntarily conveyed by the decedent after the creation of plaintiff's demand, it is necessary to allege in the petition against the fraudulent vendees the facts constituting the original cause of action. Alexander v. Quigley, 2 Duv. 399, and see note 4.

# (17a) The requisites of a petition under this section are set out in this case.

Adkins v. Meadows, 9 Ky. Opinions I24.

# (17b) Parties.

An attorney employed under a contract for a contingent fee was discharged before the action was tried; he had no lien upon a judgment afterward recovered in another action in which he was not attorney; he was not a necessary or proper party to a creditor's bill to collect the judgment. Harbison-Walker Ref. Co. v. McFarland's Admr., 156 Kv. 44. 160 S. W. 798.

# (17c) Preference not obtained against

Title X] Enforcing the Satisfaction of Judgments. [5 439

A creditor of a decedent's estate obtained a judgment against the administrator and a return of nulla bona. He could not by action under this section obtain a preference over other creditors. *Benge's Admr. v. Creech*, 163 Ky. 810, 174 S. W. 517.

# (18)

Personal judgment on which an execution may issue is the only judgment that can be enforced under this section. *Ritchey v. Buricke, 21 R. 1120, 54 S. W. 173; and see Goodin v. Wilson*, 114 Ky. 716, 24 R. 1521, 71 S. W. 866.

# (19)

Property exempt from attachment.-See note 11 to sec. 212.

# (20)

Property fraudulently conveyed. -How subjected, see Ky. Stat., sec. 1907a and *Campbell v. T'rosper, 108 Ky. 602, 22 R. 277, 57 S. W. 245; O'Kane v. Vinnedge*, 108 Ky. 34, 21 R. 1551, 55 S. W. 711.

# (20a)

In 1876 it was necessary in order to set aside a fraudulent conveyance by one who had died to have personal judgment and the return of no property against the personal representative if there was a personal representative. If it were desired to reach land as estate descended, the provisions of Ky. Stat., sec. 2085, or Ky. Stat., sec. 2089, should be followed. *Pearce v. Brown*, 9 Ky. Opinions 166.

# (21)

Second personal judgment in action on return of no property is erroneous. *Smith v. Belmont, 11 Bush 390; Farmer v. Porch*, 5 R. 933.

# (22)

Waiver of error. It is too late after an issue and trial on the merits in lower court to raise question that there was no return of "no property." *Behan v. Warfield*, 90 Ky. 151, 11 R. 960, 13 S. W. 439.

# (23)

What may be attached. Any debt or demand that the judgment defendant can enforce collection of may be attached. *Merriwether v. Bell*, 22 R. 844, 58 S. W. 987.

# (24)

Appeal -supersedeas- when a judgment discharging attachment is superseded the lien continues until appeal is disposed of. *Nat. Bank v. Johnson*, 96 S.W. 433, 29 R. 728.

# (25)

Equitable contingent interest in land should be subjected under this section and not under sec. 1691, Ky. Stat. *The Peoples Trust Co. v. Deweese*, 143 Ky. 730, 137 S. W. 201.

# (26)

Stock of non-resident debtor in a foreign corporation that has its chief office and place of business in this State may be attached in this State. *Bowman v. Breyfogle*, 145 Ky. 443, 140 S. W. 694.

# (27)

Creditor cannot compel husband who is judgment debtor to renounce will so that he may subject estate that husband would have taken if no will had been made. *Pike County v. Sowards*, 147 Ky. 37, 143 S. W. 745.

# (28)

Land--venue. In action to enforce satisfaction of judgment land in another county cannot be subjected when the title is in dispute. *Bramlett v. Couch, 105 S. W. 460, 32 R. 312; Metcalfe v. Com. L. & L. Co.*, 113 Ky. 751, 68 S. W. 1100.

# (29)

Jurisdiction of debtors of judgment debtor. Action may be brought in county in which judgment was rendered and the judgment debtor as well as debtors to him may be brought before the court although they reside in and are summoned in other counties. *Parks v. O. K. Jellico Coal Co.*, 124 S. W. 868, 136 Ky. 622.

# (30)

Guardian's bonds-question of validity of appointment and right of guardian to sue. *Havens v. Ahlering*, 87 S. W. 344, 29 R. 1265, 123 Ky. 713.

# (31)

Limitation. Failure to have execution issued within fifteen years did not bar an action under this section that was brought within fifteen years after last execution issued. *Thierman Co. v. Wolfe*, 102 S. W. 843, 31 R. 376, 125 Ky. 832.

# (31a)

An action under this section may be kept on the docket until the judgment is satisfied, and attachments may be had from time to time, although fifteen years may have elapsed since the last execution was issued on the judgment or the last attachment issued in the action. *Slaughter v. Mattingly*, 155 Ky. 407, 159 S. W. 980.

# (31b)

In an action to enforce the satisfaction of a judgment the plaintiff must allege and prove that the execution was issued before the judgment was barred by limitation; the statute of limitation need not be pleaded. *Gotee v. Graves*, 153 Ky. 26, 154 S. W. 386.

# (32)

Parties. Court should bring into the case all parties who have an interest in the fund in controversy. *Sanders & Walker v. Herndon*, 108 S. W. 908, 32 R. 1362, 128 Ky. 437.

# (33)

Bail-money deposited in lieu of may be attached to satisfy judgment. *Landy v. Moritz*, 109 S. W. 897.

# (34)

Priority between attaching creditors-notice of lien. *Brackett v. Boring*, 110 S. W. 276.

# (35)

Executor who has in his hands money due him as compensation may be summoned as garnishee to collect a debt due by him as an individual. Sanders

Enforcing the Satisfaction of Judgments

# § 440 [475] [Answer to be verified-court may require full disclosure.]

The answer of each defendant shall be verified by his oath, and not by that of an agent or attorney, and the court shall, if necessary, enforce full and explicit discoveries in such answers by process of contempt.

# § 441 [476] [Attachment without affidavit or bond.]

In such action the plaintiff may have an attachment against the property of the defendant in the execution, similar to the general attachments provided for in chapter 3, of title 8, without either the affidavit or bond therein required.

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 134.

# § 442 [477] [Lien on property of defendant-how created.]

A lien shall be created on the property of the defendant by the levy of the attachment; or by service of the summons, with the object of the action indorsed thereon, on the person holding or controlling his property. (Except against defendant constructively summoned, sec. 418; form of indorsement, Form 133.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 134, 382.

# § 443 [478] [Surrender of property compelled by court.]

The court shall enforce the surrender of the money or securities therefor, or of any other property of the defendant in the execution, which may be discovered in the action; and for this purpose may commit to jail any defendant or

& Walker v. Herndon, 93 S. W. 14, 29 R. 322, 122 Ky. 760.

§ 440. Garnishee. Where the amount the garnishee owes is specifically stated, judgment by default may be taken against him, but not if it is simply alleged that he owes more than the plaintiff's claim. Crabb v. Hill, 17 R. 44, 30 S. W. 415; and see notes to sec. 225.

§ 441. Neither affidavit nor bond necessary to obtain an attachment in an action upon a return of no property. Lewis v. Ouinker, 2 Met. 284.

§ 442. (1) Attachment- lis pendens. An attachment is not necessary to create a lien if the property sought to be subjected be specifically described in the petition. A lien is created on such property when the petition is filed. Trabue v. Conners, 84 Ky. 283, 8 R. 288, 1 S. W. 470; Parsons v. Meyburg, 1 Duv. 206; Ward v. Robinson, 1 Bush 294; Murphy v. Cochran, 80 Ky. 239, 3 R. 727; Caldwell v. Deposit Bank, 100 Ky. 197, 22 R. 684, 58 S. W. 589; and see cases cited in note 12, sec. 439.

2 When the action is for the discovery of and subjection of any property owned by the defendant, no lien is created on his property unless an attachment is obtained, or summons with indorsement is executed on the person holding his property. Bryant v. Bryant, 14 R. 358, 20 S. W. 270; and see note 1.

# § 444 [479] [Judgment obtained on motion—against whom.]

A judgment may be obtained, on motion, by a surety against his principal or co-surety for money paid; by a client against his attorney for money collected or property received; by a party or officer against a surety for costs; and by a party against an officer for money collected or property received, and for the damages which such party is entitled to recover; and in all other cases specially authorized by statute; and the service of the notice shall be regarded as the commencement of the proceeding.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 162c; Forms Nos. 69, 319-325.

# § 444.

1. Summary proceedings. The collection of appeal bond cannot be enforced under the provisions of this chapter. Stephens v. Miller, 80 Ky. 47, 3 R. 523.
2. A surety in the bond executed by the defendant to retain possession of property, as provided in sec. 188, cannot be proceeded against by motion. Gay v. Morgan, 4 Bush 606; nor can the surety in a bond to keep the peace. Combs v. Com., 24 R. 1310, 71 S. W. 504.
3. A trustee appointed by the court to invest funds, and his sureties in his bond as such trustee, in case of his failure to invest the funds as required by the court, may be proceeded against by rule, and required to pay such funds back into court. Such proceedings by rule may be instituted by the court on its own motion. Dickinson v. Trout, 8 Bush 441.
4. A purchaser of land sold under the judgment of a court may recover rent accruing after the confirmation of his purchase by motion against the tenant, upon notice duly executed. Cooper v. Baker, 2 Bush 244.
5. The provisions of this chapter have not repealed the right of a surety who has paid a judgment to have it transferred to him as provided in the Gen. Stat., chap. 104, sec. 9 [Ky. Stat., sec. 4666]. Alexander v. Lewis, 1 Met. 407.
6. The collection of costs which a party binds himself to pay by a recognizance entered upon the record may be enforced by summary proceedings. Kinney v. O'Bannon, 6 Bush 692.
7. By mistake a commissioner in executing the court's order overpaid one of the litigating parties under an agreement that if he was overpaid he would refund; the court had authority summarily to compel a refunding. Boreing's Adm'r v. MeHargue, 152 Ky. 360, 153 S. W. 463.

# § 445 [480] [Notice of motion.]

Notice of such motion shall be served on the party against whom the judgment is sought, at least ten days before the motion is made. (By whom and how served, secs. 624 to 630.)

The notice shall state the nature and grounds of the motion, and the day on which it will be made.

Unless the motion be made or entered on the motion docket on the day specified in the notice, it shall be considered as abandoned.

# § 448 [483] [Motion may be made any day of term.]

Such motion mentioned in this chapter may be made on any day of the term of the court.

The motion may be heard and determined upon or without written pleadings, and judgment shall be given according to law and the rules of equity.

Notice need not be given ten days before beginning of term, it may be given at any time, but cannot be acted on until ten days have expired. McChesney v. Hager, 104 S. W. 714, 31 R. 1038.

# § 446

(1) Notice-sufficiency of. In determining the sufficiency of a notice on motion for judgment on a bond executed under sec. 645 of the Code, it should be considered in connection with the bond. Smith v. Wells, 4 Bush 92; and see further as to sufficiency of notice. Bunnell v. Thompson, 12 Bush 116; Mooar v. Cov. City Nat. Bk., 80 Ky. 305, 3 R. 674.

(2) When a party appears to a notice defective in form, and without objection makes defense to the merits, he waives the right to object to it because not sufficient. Brown v. Gibson, 78 Ky. 602, 1 R. 335; Fleming v. Sinclair, 22 R. 499, 58 S. W. 370.

# § 447

(1) Motion to be entered on day specified. The motion must be made or entered on the day specified in the notice, and if this is not done a judgment rendered pursuant to the notice will be a nullity. Notice was given to a sheriff and his sureties that on the fourth day of the June term a motion would be made for judgment. On the third day of the term the notice was filed and ordered to lie over; no further step was taken at that term. A judgment rendered the next term by default was a nullity. Foster v. Wade, 4 Met. 253; see Mershorn v. Com., 2 Met. 371.

(2) Motion may either be made in open court on day specified in notice, or on that day may be entered on the motion docket. Bent v. Maupin, 88 Ky. 271, 9 R. 409, 5 S. W. 425.

# § 449

(1) Pleadings. Proceeding by motion on a claimant's bond may be heard and determined upon or without written pleadings. Couchman v. Maupin, 78 Ky. 33; Borches v. Bellis, 110 Ky. 620, 23 R. 37, 62 S. W. 486; Fritz v. Kirchdorfer, 124 S. W. 882, 136 Ky. 643.

Title X] Summary Proceedings. [5 449

# RECORDS-HOW LOST SUPPLIED.

# § 3991. Judgment or order—lost or mutilated supplied-evidence-costs.

When any judgment or final order of any court of record of this State remains unexecuted, and the record thereof has been lost, mutilated, defaced or destroyed, it shall be lawful for any person interested therein, upon ten days' notice, in writing, to the adverse party, to move the court in which such judgment was rendered or final order was made to re-enter the same of record; and, upon satisfactory proof that such judgment or final order had been there-to-fore entered of record, that the same had been mutilated, defaced or destroyed, and the purport thereof, it shall be the duty of the court to re-enter the same of record; which judgment or final order so entered shall have the same effect as the original to all intents and purposes, and official copies thereof shall be received as evidence for all purposes for which like copies of the original, if in existence, would be competent. No judgment for costs shall be rendered against the defendant in such motion, unless he shall controvert the plaintiff's right, and fail in his defense. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3991.)

# § 3992. Bond having force of judgment—lost or mutilated supplied—costs.

When any delivery, sale, forthcoming, replevin or other bond, having the force and effect of a judgment, has been lost, destroyed, mutilated or defaced, it shall be lawful for any person interested therein, upon ten days' written notice to the adverse party, to move the court in which said bond was on file to award execution thereon; and upon satisfactory proof that such bond had existed, that the same had been lost, destroyed, mutilated or defaced, the purport thereof, and that the same remains undischarged, it shall be the duty of the court to award execution thereon, endorsed as though execution were issued on such bond, if in existence; but no judgment for costs shall be rendered against the defendant in such motion, unless he shall controvert the plaintiff's right to an execution and fail in his defense. (Id., sec. 3992.)

# § 3993. County court-lost or mutilated order or bond in—supplied.

If an order of the county court, appointing an administrator or guardian, or appointing any person to any place, station, or trust; or, if the bond executed in the county court by any such person, or by an officer shall be destroyed, obliterated, or lost, the court shall, by the appropriate proceeding, due notice thereof being given, supply the record, and require such person to execute a new bond, with good and sufficient sureties: and on his failure to do so, shall enter up an order vacating his office or station. (Id., sec. 3993.)

# § 3994. Records or papers of any court supplied-commissioner.

If the records or papers of any court shall be lost, destroyed, defaced, or obliterated, such court shall appoint a commissioner, who shall have power and authority to fix on a convenient place to meet and sit, from time to time, giving reasonable public notice thereof. (Id., sec. 3994.)

(2) If parties see proper to proceed by pleadings, the rules applicable to pleadings in action obtain. 'Sargent v. Farrar, 2 R. 212.

(4) Motion and grounds for a new trial are necessary in order to a review of the evidence. Carr v. Brownlee, 102 Ky. 160. 19 R. 1163, 42 S. W. 1099.

(5) Motion for possession of land under see, 169, Kiv. Stat., is tried under these provisions of the Code. Scott v. Mitchell, 19 R. 218, 39 S. W. 507; and defense need not be in writing.

# § 449

# Summary Proceedings.

# § 3995. Commissioner-powers and duties of.

The commissioner may, at the instance of any person, issue a summons and cause the attendance of witnesses, and take evidence, in writing, of such witnesses, relative to any record or paper so destroyed, defaced, or obliterated; which deposition shall be legal evidence, and shall be returned to the clerk of the court and safely kept by such clerk. Before any such proof is taken, the party offering it must make and file with the commissioner an affidavit that there is no attested copy of such record or papers in existence known to him. If such affidavit is not made, no testimony taken shall be evidence. The commissioner shall not remain in office longer than one year. (Id., sec. 3995.)

# Lost RecordsJustices' Courts—Restoration.

# § 1091. Destruction or loss of records-how supplied—appeals.

If the records of a justice of the peace be destroyed or lost, and a judgment so destroyed or lost remain unsatisfied, the justice may, on reasonable notice to the adverse party, render a new judgment for whatever is due. The justice shall state in his record of such trials that the proceedings are founded on a judgment which has been lost or destroyed. An appeal lies from such judgment as in other cases. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1091.)

# NAME—HOW CHANGED.

# § 3717. Persons whose name may be changed.

Any person of the age of twenty-one years, and not a married woman, may have his or her name changed by the county court in which he or she resides. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3717.)

# § 3718. Infant—name of may be changed.

The father, or if he be dead, or shall have abandoned his infant child or children, the mother and guardian, or, if no mother is living, the guardian alone of an infant, may, in like manner, have its name changed. (Id., sec. 3718.)

# § 3719. Facts to be entered of record.

The original name, age and place of birth, and the name to which the change is made, and the names of an infant's father and mother, if known, and of the person on whose motion the change is made, must be entered on the order-book of the court. (Id., sec. 3719.)

# § 3720. Index to be kept by clerk.

The clerk of every court shall keep an alphabetical index for each order-book, referring to the proper page in which each person's name is changed, giving the name from and to which it is changed. (Id., sec. 3720.)

# ATTORNEYS-AT-LAW-MOTION AGAINST FOR MONEY COLLECTED.

# § 104. Motion for failing to pay money collected—limitation.

If any attorney-at-law shall collect the money of his client, and, on demand, wrongfully neglect or refuse to pay over the same, the circuit court of the county in which the money may be collected, shall, after notifying the attorney to show cause against the same, suspend him from practice in any court for twelve months, and until the money shall be paid. Before any such motion shall be entertained, a demand of the money shall be made of such attorney in the

Title 1] Civil Proceedings On Behalf of Commonwealth. [§ 450

county of his residence, and no such proceeding shall take place unless it is commenced within two years next after the collection of the money. (Ky. Stat., sec. 104.)

# 105. Commonwealth—county attorney and Attorney-General to prosecute.

It shall be the duty of the Commonwealth's attorney for the district, or county attorney for the county, to attend to the proceedings in the circuit court under the preceding section, and the duty of the Attorney-General to attend to the same in the court of appeals. (Id., sec. 105.)

# § 451 [490] [Controversies that may be submitted, and how.]

1. Any controversy which is or might be the subject of an action may, at the request of those interested therein, be submitted to the decision of one or

§ 451. (1 Amendment of award cannot be made after award is reduced to writing as specified in submission, and where such an attempt is made the second award will be disregarded and the original enforced. Martin v. White, 1 K. 347; Eddy v. Northup, 15 R. 434, 23 S. W. 353; I Met. 434.

(2) Appeal does not lie from an award under a statutory submission although by entry of record it is made the judgment of the court, unless some exception is taken affecting the validity of the award. Payne v. Payne, 8 R. 591.

(3) Common law submissions-parties may stipulate as they please as to notice or other conditions. Martin v. White, 1 R. 347.

(3a) The common law respecting arbitrations and awards is still in force. Woolfork v. Calloway, 9 Ky. Opinions 161; Jones v. Hatchett, 9 Ky. Opinions 164.

(3b) Condemnation proceeding. Parties to a condemnation proceeding agreed of record to refer the question of damages to arbitrators. The award was filed and no exceptions to it were filed; it was conclusive as to the question of damages. Hensley v. The Wasiota & Black Mt. R. Co., 153 Ky. 17, 154 S. W. 385.

# Duty of arbitrators and parties.

The arbitrators are to act upon the evidence before them, and it is the duty of each party to bring before them all of his evidence. Adams v. Ringo, 79 Ky. 211, 1 R. 251.

Title X] Arbitrations and Awards. [5 451

more arbitrators, or to two and their umpire, by the order of any court having jurisdiction of the subject. The parties making such submission shall, if there be no action pending, by written agreement filed and noted on the record, or by an entry on the record, state what question is submitted. The order of court shall state the time in which the award is to be made and returned, but the court may enlarge the time.

# 2. Oath of arbitrators and umpire.

Arbitrators and the umpire, if there be one, before they proceed to act shall take an oath to decide the controversy to them submitted according to law and evidence and the equity of the case, to the best of their judgment, without favor or affection; and a certificate of such oath shall be returned to the court with the award.

# 3. Witnesses-how attendance of coerced.

Any one of the arbitrators shall have power to issue subpoenas for witnesses to attend their sittings and give evidence touching the questions referred to them, to which all sheriffs and other like officers shall give obedience. Witnesses failing to attend before arbitrators at the time and place designated, or who shall refuse to give evidence when they do attend, shall be reported to the court by the arbitrators, and be proceeded against and punished as if the case had been pending in court.

# 4. Fiduciaries may submit controversy.

The personal representative of a decedent, the guardian, committee or curator of a person under disability, or any trustee, may agree to such submission. Upon such a submission, made in good faith, the award shall be binding, and be entered as the judgment of the court. No such fiduciary shall be responsible for any loss resulting from an award adverse to the interest he represents, unless caused by his fault or neglect.

Whittaker v. Wallace, 1 R. 271; Cook v. McRoberts, 5 R. 764.

# (9) Fraud-to set aside award for.

In an action to set aside an award upon the ground of fraud, there must be averments of the specific fraud relied on. Phillips v. Phillips. 81 Ky. 147, 4 R. 941.

# (10) Judgment on award.

It is error to render judgment upon an award not delivered to the parties ten days before the commencement of the term at which judgment is rendered. But this is a clerical misprision, and motion must be made to correct it in the circuit court. Carson v. Carson, 1 Met. 434.

# (11) Majority of persons to whom a question is submitted may act.

Hewitt v. Craig, 86 Ky. 23, 9 R. 232, 5 S. W. 280.

# (12) Notice--waiver of.

Upon an arbitration made under common law rules, either party may waive notice of the meeting of the arbitrators or decline to be present. When the award is made the arbitrators have no power over it, and a promise by them to reopen the arbitration and hear additional testimony will not affect the award. Whitlock v. Ledford, 82 Ky. 390, 6 R. 387.

# (13) When the parties are present and heard by the arbitrators.

It is no objection to the award that it does not state that the parties had notice of the time and place the arbitrators met and acted. Newton v. West, 3 Met. 24.

# (14) Oath of arbitrators.

The officer's certificate accompanying the award stated that the arbitrators appeared "and made oath according to law that they would faithfully discharge the duties devolved upon them as arbitrators."

Arbitrations and Awards

# 5. Arbitrator refusing to act—proceedings.

If any arbitrator fail or refuse to act, the court may appoint another, or set aside the order of reference.

# 6. Meetings—powers—award.

The arbitrators and umpire, if there be one, shall meet at such convenient times and places as may be necessary, of which the parties shall have reasonable notice, and hear such evidence as either party may adduce. They shall have power to administer oaths. They shall make their award in writing, stating therein when it is made, and sign it; and shall deliver a copy of it to each of the contending parties, and return the original to the court with indorsements showing the time of delivering a copy to each party.

# 7. Award entered as judgment—when.

The award shall be entered as the judgment of the court at the first term, ten days before which copies of the award are delivered to the parties, unless, on exceptions filed, it be set aside by the court.

# 8. Power of court over award.

No award shall be set aside for the want of form. But courts shall have power over awards on equitable principles as heretofore.

# 9. Arbitration of little controversies—practice—appeal.

Controversies within the jurisdiction of a justice of the peace may be submitted for arbitration in the same manner, and the proceedings shall be the same as are above authorized and required. Either party may appeal to the circuit court from the judgment of the justice on the award, as in other cases. (Controversy may without suit be submitted to court, sec. 637.)

and umpire." Held sufficient. Snyder v. Rouse, 1 Met. 62ã; and, if court is satisfied by parol evidence that oath was taken, it will be sufficient. Rhodes v. Carson, 17 R. 875. 32 S. W. 590.

(15) In the absence of proof to the contrary it will be presumed that the arbitrators were sworn, and that they complied with all the requirements of the law. Carson v. Carson, 1 Met. 434.

(16) Parol submission. The rule of the common law that any matter in controversy might be submitted by parol agreement to arbitration unless it divested the parties of some right that could only pass by a written agreement has not been changed by the Code or Statutes. Thonasson v. Risk, 11 Bush 619; Royse v. McCall, 5 Bush 695.

(17) Statutory arbitration and award. See Ky. Stat., sec. 69.

(18) Submission by order of court. To authorize the reference of a controversy to arbitration by rule of court, when there is no action pending, it is necessary that there be an agreement in writing.

(19) Time in which award to be made. When a time is fixed within which the award is to be returned, it must be made within the time; and the arbitrators should decide all questions submitted; or a failure to comply with either of these requirements, the award will be set aside. Burnam v. Burnam, 6 Bush 389.

(20) Umpire -selection and duties. Umpire may be selected at any time after arbitrators are chosen. Whittaker v. Wallace, 1 R. 271; and see as to duties of umpire and part he should take in deliberations, Couchman v. Maupin, 78 Kv. 33; 1 R. 271.

(21) The umpire should only act when the arbitrators differ, but the fact that he occasionally takes part in the discussion of questions will not invalidate the award. 79 Ky. 211, 1 R. 251; Whittaker v. Wallace, 1 R. 271.

Title X] Arbitrations and Awards. [§ 451

# § 69. Controversies which may be submitted-how submission made.

All controversies which might be the subject of a suit or action may be submitted to the decision of one or more arbitrators, or two and their umpire. Said submission may be in the mode specified in the Civil Code, or it may be in writing, and the agreement of submission shall be binding on the parties thereto, if it states the matter submitted and who are to be the arbitrators. (Ky. Stat., sec. 69.)

# § 70. Covenant of parties.

The parties to the submission may enter into a mutual covenant, substantially as follows: "We, A B and C D, having a controversy respecting (here insert the matter in controversy), for the purpose of settling the same, have mutually chosen and arbitrators, who shall choose an umpire in case of disagreement, whose award we do hereby bind ourselves to perform, provided it is in writing. In witness whereof, we have hereunto set our hands this — day of —" (Id., sec. 70.)

# § 71. Arbitrators-duty-notice of time and place of meeting-selection of umpire.

The arbitrators shall meet at a time and place to be fixed by themselves, of which reasonable notice in writing shall be given to each party, and shall take an oath fairly and impartially to decide the controversy to them submitted according to law, justice and the equity of the whole case. If they select an umpire, he shall, in like manner, be sworn before the hearing begins. They shall hear all testimony offered by either party relative to the matters in controversy, and shall have power and authority to summon witnesses, compel their attendance, and punish for contempt by fine or imprisonment to the extent justices of the peace may do; shall have authority to administer oaths to parties and witnesses, and to preserve order and decorum while engaged in the determination of the matter. (Id., sec. 71.)

# § 72. Award to be in writing-copies delivered to parties—action on award.

The award of the arbitrators must be in writing, signed by them and the umpire, if any, and shall be a final settlement of the controversy between the parties. Copies of the award must be given within reasonable time to each party, and the same shall be binding upon both. For a failure to perform the award, an action by ordinary proceeding may be maintained by either party against the other. (Id., sec. 72.)

# § 73. Award not set aside for want of form-equity jurisdiction.

No award shall be set aside for want of form; but courts of equity shall have power over awards on equitable principles as heretofore. (Id., sec. 73.)

(22) On a submission to two arbitrators, who in case of disagreement are to choose an umpire, the umpire is only authorized to act when they disagree; and if they all act together the award will be set aside. Royse v. MeCall, 5 Bush 695.

(23) A county court may submit to arbitration matters in controversy between it and an individual. Remington v. Harrison County, 12 Bush 148.

(24) A personal representative may submit matters to arbitration with the same effect and the same freedom from liability out of court, as with the consent of court. Overly v. Overly, I Met. 117.

Allen-Bradley Co. v. Anderson, 99 Ky. 311, 18 R. 216, 35 S. W. 1123.
