[For thorough discussion of this Chapter see Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate," of Infants and Other Persons Under Disability.—Ed.]

# 489. · [Sale of real estate of infants and persons of unsound mind.]

A vested estate of an infant or of a person of unsound mind, in real property, may be sold by order of a court of equity-

1. Depositions must be taken on interrogatories if all parties against whom they are to be read are infants or non-residents. Womble v. Trice, 112 Ky. 533, 23 R. 1939, 66 S. W. 370; 67 S.W. 9.
2. Foreign guardian can not prosecute action for infants unless authorized in the manner provided by, sec. 204l, Ky. Stat. Williams v. Duncan, 92 Ky. 125, 13 R. 389, 17 S. W. 330; Watts v. Wilbon, 93 Ky. 495, 14 R. 463, 20 S. W. 505; Shelby v. Harrison, 84 Ky. 144, 8 R. 83; Woolridge v. Woolridge, 26 R. 97, 80 S. W. 775.
3. Grounds of sale. Under this section the real estate of an infant can only be sold for his debts or a liability with which he is chargeable, or under a decree in an action brought by his statutory guardian to obtain means for his support or education or a reinvestment of the proceeds. Tyler v. Jewell, 10 R. 887, 11 S. W. 25. In Lyn v. Lyn, 17 R. 1364, 34 S. W. 1065; the court under the facts of the case refused to sell infants' land at the instance of their guardian for their maintenance and education.
4. (Guardian ad litem-service of process-as to guardian ad litem, see secs. 36-38; service of process, secs. 52-53.)
5. Improvements. Sale or mortgage of portion of infant's land for the purpose of improving remainder is void, because not authorized by statute. Hays v. Bradley, 15 R. 387, 23 S. W. 372; Posey v. Dugan, 22 R. 1104, 59 S. W. 862.
6. Purchaser under void sale of infant's real estate, who has in good faith erected improvements, is entitled, as against the infants, to recover the amount that improvements have enhanced the value of the land. Wornack v. Loar, 11 R. 6, 11 S. W. 438; Hays v. Bradley, 15 R. 387, 23 S. W. 372.
7. Lunatic's land can not be sold under an execution issued on a judgment rendered before he was adjudged to be of unsound mind. Buckler v. Reese, 100 Ky. 336, 18 R. 959, 38 S. W. 492.
8. Mortgage of infant's land-is not allowable even under decree of court, except as provided in sec. 498. Posey v. Dugan, 22 R. 1104, 59 S. W. 862.
9. Powers of courts of equity to sell and reinvest infant's real estate, or that of persons under disability, are statutory, and not inherent. Walker v. Smyser, 80 Ky. 620, 4 R. 662; Henning v. Harrison, 13 Bush 723; Liter v. Fishback, 25 R. 260, 75 S. W. 232; Elliott v. Fowler, 112 Ky. 376, 23 R. 1676, 65 S. W. 394.

Title X] Sales of Real Property, Etc. [§ 489

1. [Debt of ancestor, to pay.] For the payment of any debt or liability of his ancestor or devisor with which he may be legally chargeable, in an action brought against him pursuant to section 428; or in an action brought against him by a creditor of the ancestor or devisor, unless it be enjoined pursuant to section 436.

2. [Debt of creditor, to pay.] For the payment of his debt or liability, in an action brought against him by his creditor.

3. [Maintenance and education of ward.] In an action by a guardian against his ward, for a sale of the estate for the maintenance and education of the ward. (See Ky. Stat., sec. 2034.)

849; and the chancellor has no inherent power to order a sale. The statute regulating sales must be followed. Meddis v. Bull, 13 R. 767, 18 S. W. 6.

(9a) Exchange of lands is not authorized. Powers of courts of equity to sell and reinvest infants' real estate are statutory, and not inherent, and courts have no power to divest infants of their title to land except in the manner pointed out by the statute. There is no statute in this State giving to courts of equity authority to approve an exchange of land made by the guardian of infants, or to divest infants of their title for the purpose of perfecting such an exchange, and a judgment approving such an exchange is void, and not simply erroneous. Ford v. May, 157 Ky. 830, 164 S. W. 88.

(10) Court can not confirm a private sale of infants' real estate to carry out a contract made by an unauthorized person. Kinslow v. Grove, 98 Ky. 266, 17 R. 845, 32 S. W. 933; and see Ogden v. Stevens, 98 Ky. 564, 17 R. 1115, 33 S. W. 932.

(10a) Sale of minerals under land. A court of equity under this section may order a sale of the minerals under infants' land without selling the surface when it is to the interest of the infants so to sell. In so selling the mineral under the land, unreasonable rights as to the surface should not be conferred. Hays v. Wicker, 161 Ky. 706, 171 S. W. 447.

(11) Practice. A purchaser can not, after the confirmation of the sale, and after the expiration of the term at which confirmation was made, resist the payment of the purchase-money because of irregularities in proceedings under which the sale was made. Todd v. Dowd, 1 Met. 281.

(11a) Bond and lien as to excess. If by reason of indivisibility land is sold to pay the debts of an infant or his ancestor or devisor, which brings more than enough to pay the debts and costs, the provisions of Code, sec. 493 and 497, as to bond and as to lien apply to the excess. (Subsecs. 1 and 2.) Carpenter v. Julian, 180 Ky. 580, 203 S. W. 323.

(11b) Defect which can not be cured after judgment. Defects in the preparation of a statutory guardian's action for a sale of land for maintenance and education of the ward can not be cured by amendments or affidavits filed after judgment. Clay's Gdn. v. Rice, 172 Ky. 164, 189 S. W. 11.

(12) Proceeds of sale ordered under subsec. 5 of this section, or under sec. 491, may be applied if necessary to education and maintenance of ward. Sawyer v. Guscurth, 3 R. 592.

(12a) The proceeds of a sale of real estate under subsec. 3 of this section, are not required to be kept intact. They may be used for the maintenance and education of the ward. Farris v. Bingham, 164 Ky. 444, 175 S. W. 649.

(13) Process on infants-correction of errors. In an action to settle an estate summons was not executed on the infants over fourteen, but guardian ad litem for another infant filed answer for them and after the sale was made the statutory guardian of infants not served asked that proceedings be adjudged valid but the court held that the sale was void as to infants and error could not be cured. Hulsewede v. Churchman, 111 Ky. 51, 23 R. 487, 63 S. W. 1.

(14) Reinvestment - in action to sell for reinvestment, facts must be stated in the petition and proved showing that sale will benefit the infants, and the title papers must be filed. Womble v. Trice, 112 Ky. 533, 23 R. 1939, 66 S. W. 370; 67 S. W. 9; Code, sec. 492; Siler v. Archer, 26 R. 557, 82 S. W. 256.

Sales of Real Property, Etc. [Title X]

4. [Maintenance of lunatic and family.] In an action by a committee of a person of unsound mind against him, for a sale of the estate for his maintenance, or for the maintenance of himself and his family, including the education of his infant children. (See further, Ky. Stat., sec. 2150.)

5. [Reinvestment—sale for.] In an action against a person of unsound mind by his committee; or against an infant by his guardian; or, if the infant be a married woman, by her husband, if he be twenty-one years of age, if not, by her next friend, for a sale of the estate and investment in other property. (Venue of action, secs. 62, 65; as to defense by, and service of summons on, see secs. 36, 52, 53.)

See Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate," secs. 61, 64, 66, 68, 73, 75-80. Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 90, 441; Form No. 153.

(15) An action for reinvestment can only be brought against an infant by his guardian and the bond required by sec. 493 must be executed. Hicks v. Jackson, 24 R. 218, 68 S. W. 419; Cornell v. Cornell, 115 S. W. 795, 131 Ky. 650; Phillips v. Spalding, 102 S. W. 1193, 31 R. 581.

(16) Sale to pay debts-settlement of estate. In an action to settle the estate of a decedent in which infants are interested, the whole property may be sold if it is indivisible, although it may not require all the proceeds to pay the debts, but in such a case facts must be alleged and proven showing the necessity for the sale and the proceeds going to the infant be protected as provided in the Code, otherwise the sale in excess of the amount necessary to pay debts will be void. Elliott v. Fowler, 112 Ky. 376, 23 R. 1676. 65 S. W. 849: Zehnder v. Schoenbachler, 24 R. 947, 70 S. W. 278; Lou. Bk. Co. v. Pranger, 24 R. 408, 68 S. W. 632; Oldham v. McElroy, 121 S. W. 414, 134 Ky. 454.

(17) Strict compliance with provisions of Code is necessary to divest the infant of title to land. Bullock v. Gudgell, 25 R. 1413, 77 S. W. 1126; Hulsewede v. Churchman, 111 Ky. 51, 23 R. 487, 63 S. W. 1.

(18) Venue of action. In an action for the sale of land and division of proceeds among some of the heirs, and reinvestment as to others, land situated in other counties than that in which action is pending may be sold, when the ancestor died in and part of the land was located in county where action was brought. Phalan v. Louisville Trust Co., 88 Ky. 24, 10 R. 663, 10 S. W. 10.

(Void sale.) Judgment directing sale of infants' real estate without any person having been summoned as required by sec. 52 is void. Wornack v. Loar, 11 R. 6, 11 S. W. 438.

(19a) A sale made in an action by the guardian of two infants for sale of their real estate for reinvestment is void unless the bond of sec. 493 is given before the sale. The sale cannot be sustained by a bond given after the sale; nor can it be sustained under the theory that the sale was necessary because of indivisibility; neither the pleadings nor the evidence showed facts within Civil Code 490. Melcher v. Yager's Gdn., 159 Ky. 597, 167 S. W. 871.

(19b) If, in a suit for the sale of an infant's land under sec. 489 the bond required by sec. 493 of the Code is not executed, the sale is void. Jackson v. Haake, 163 Ky. 486, 175 S. W. 1127.

(19c) In an action to sell land of infants under Code, sec. 489, subsec. 3, unless the record shows the execution of the bond required by Code, sec. 493, a judgment ordering a sale and the sale thereunder are void for want of jurisdiction. (Subsec. 3.) Nunnelly v. Nunnelly, 173 Ky. 372, 191 S. W. 85.

(19d) A judgment directed the sale of certain mineral products and timber belonging to an infant. The judgment was erroneous, not void. (Subsec. 3.) Webb v. Webb's Gdn, 178 Ky. 152, 198 S. W. 736; Mays v. Beaver Creek Coal & Coke Co., 178 Ky. 149, 198 S. W. 743.

(20) Sale beneficial to infants. This fact does not cure errors that would otherwise invalidate sale. Bayne v. Stratton, 115 S. W. 728, 131 Ky. 494.

(21) Infant over fourteen must be served with process, his guardian cannot enter his appearance. Hartman v. Fast, 145 Ky. 402, 149 Ky. 707.

(22) Maintenance of infant. Title pa-

Title X] Sales of Real Property, Etc. [§ 490
# § 490 [542, 548]

[Joint owners—real estate of may be sold.] A vested estate in real property jointly owned by two or more persons [or owned as set out in subsection 3 of this section], may be sold by order of a court of equity, in an action brought by either of them, though the plaintiff or defendant be of unsound mind or an infant—

1. [Shares worth less than $100 each.] If the share of each owner be worth less than one hundred dollars.
2. [Property in possession and indivisible.] If the estate be in possession and the property can not be divided without materially impairing its value, or the value of the plaintiff's interest therein. (Partition between joint tenants, Ky. Stat., sec. 2348.)
3. [Property of decedent passing with life estate in widow.] [If the estate shall have passed by devise or descent to the widow and heir or heirs of a decedent, and the widow shall have a life right in a portion thereof, either as homestead or dower or by devise and the said property can not be divided without materially impairing its value, or the value of the plaintiff's interest therein.

pers need not be filed when it is shown that infant received land by inheritance. Father will not be allowed to sell for maintenance the whole estate unless it clearly appears he is unable to support infant. *Campbell v. Goodin, 108 S. W. 248, 32 R. 1119.*

(22a) There is no authority to sell an infant's land for maintenance and education if his parents are able to maintain and educate him. *Taylor v. Taylor's Gdn., 149 Ky. 707, 149 S. W. 1000; Luscher v. Julian's Admr., 173 Ky. 150, 190 S. W. 692.*

(22b) In a suit to sell lands of an infant for his education and maintenance, the evidence was held insufficient to show the father's inability to support and educate the ward. *Damron v. Damron, Gdn., 169 Ky. 678. 184 S. W. 1129.*

(22c) In a suit to sell a tract of land of an infant for his education and maintenance, it was held that there was no necessity shown for the sale of an interest in the tract sought to be sold, where there were other smaller tracts which could be sold for sufficient amounts required for education and maintenance of the ward, at less sacrifice to the estate. *Damron v. Damron's Gdn., 169 Ky. 678, 184 S. W. 1129.*

(22d) Void sale. An action was brought to sell land belonging to infants to pay the debts of their mother and for their maintenance and education. The infants were made plaintiffs. The judgment and sale were void. *Wyatt's Trus. v. Grider, 158 Ky. 440, 165 S. W. 420.*

(22e) A sale which apart from a certain power in a will under this section would have been void was not validated in this case by reason of the power in the will. *Wyatt's Trus. v. Grider, 158 Ky. 440, 165 S. W. 420.*

(23) Bond with only one surety is invalid and sale will be void--bond must be accepted by court to render it valid. *Hughes v. Saffell, 119 S. W. 804, 134 Ky. 175.*

(24) Land of an infant was sold under this section and purchased by the plaintiff; on a reversal of the judgment the sale was ordered to be set aside. *Turner v. Hamlin, 152 Ky. 469, 153 S. W. 778.*

# 490. (1) Amended petition—process.

In an action for the sale of infant's real estate, process is necessary on an amended petition asking sale of land not mentioned in petition. *Howard v. Singleton, 94 Ky. 336, 15 R. 309, 22 S. W. 337.*

Appraisement of land is not necessary in an action by devisees to have it sold on account of its indivisibility. *Southwick v. Greuzenbach, 12 R. 263, 13 S. W. 918.*

(3) Assignee of joint owner may maintain action to have indivisible real estate sold. *Hill v. Cornwall, 95 Ky. 512, 16 R. 97, 26 S. W. 540.*

# Sales of Real Property, Etc.

# [Title X]

If any real property held by a widow and heirs of a deceased person as provided in subsection 3 of this section has heretofore been sold by decree of a court of equity and said sale has been confirmed and a deed made by orders of such court, and possession of said property has been taken under said sale and deed, the purchaser thereof or said widow or heir may file a petition in equity in the court in which said land was sold, asking the confirmation of said sale, and making all other persons interested in said property parties thereto; and said court on the hearing thereof shall have the power to confirm said former sale, if it shall appear that no party under disability was prejudiced thereby.

(Words in brackets added by Act of March, 1916, c. 119, p. 707.)

See Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate," secs. 81, 82, 83, 87, 88, 90-98, 101-106, 108, 109, 186, 191.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 109a, 178, 441; Form No. 153b.

# (4) Bond to infant.

Where real estate is owned by persons some of whom are infants and others married women, and a proceeding is instituted to sell it under subsec. 2 of sec. 490, the execution of the bond provided by sec. 493 is not necessary, but the purchase price shall not be paid by the purchaser, but shall remain a lien on the land, as provided in sec. 497. Kendall v. Briggs, 81 Ky. 119, 4 R. 854; and see Shelby v. Harrison, 84 Ky. 144. 8 R. 83.

# (4a) A sale made in an action by the guardian of two infants for sale of their real estate for reinvestment is void unless the bond of sec. 493 is given before the sale.

The sale cannot be sustained by a bond given after the sale; nor can it be sustained under the theory that the sale was necessary because of indivisibility; neither the pleadings nor the evidence showed facts within Civil Code. sec. 490. Melcher v. Yager's Gdn., 159 Ky. 597. 167 S. W. 871.

# (5) Contingent interest.

Land devised to a mother and "her children," they being in possession, may be sold, and if order directs investment of fund to be held in same manner as land sold, the purchaser takes a good title, although other children may be born. Tyler v. Jewell, 10 R. 887, 11 S. W. 25; Newman v. Eeton, 100 Ky. 653, 14 R. 793, 21 S. W. 526; Sheirich v. Maxwell, 28 R. 173. 89 S. W. 4; Harting v. Milward, 28 R. 776, 90 S. W. 260.

# (6) Creditor of joint owner can not have whole of land sold because it can not be divided.

Hill v. Cornwall, 95 Ky. 398 512, 16 R. 97, 26 S. W. 540; Marcum v. Marcum. 177 Ky. 186, 197 S. W. 655.

# (6a) Curtesy-peculiar circumstances.

A husband who was entitled to curtesy in land purchased before 1894 sold his life estate claiming that it was only one-third; a son of the wife sold his interest to the same purchaser. The purchaser could maintain a suit for sale under this section against the remaining child who did not sell. Wallace v. Wallace's Extrx., 149 Ky. 636, 149 S. W. 988.

# (7) Decree necessary.

Infants' real estate can only be sold and conveyed in the manner pointed out in the Code. Where a father who was life tenant, his children owning the remainder, sold the land, a court of chancery had no power to confirm the sale. Bill v. Burgess, 15 R. 41, 22 S. W. 84; and see notes 9, 17, sec. 489.

# (8) Dower interest of wife can not be divested by a sale under this section, unless the provisions of sec. 495 are complied with.

Woman's Club v. Reed, 111 Ky. 806, 23 R. 1346, 04 S. W. 739, 65 S. W. 862.

# (8a) In 1914 lands could not be sold as indivisible where owned by one person, subject to dower in another.

Van Meter v. Van Meter, 160 Ky. 163, 169 S. W. 592.

# (8b) Joint heirs and dowress.

Four infant children were joint owners in fee of an indivisible house and lot in which their mother had dower. A sale under this section upon a petition in which their mother joined was valid. Hatterich v. Bruce, Gdn., 151 Ky. 12. 151 S. W. 31.

Sales of Real Property, Etc. [§ 490]

(9) Improvements-owned by one of the joint owners-how value of fixed when property sold under this section. Gray v. Cornwall, 95 Ky. 566, 16 R. 228, 26 S. W. 1018.

(10) Indivisible real estate owned by infants may be sold upon allegation and proof that the interest of the infants will be benefited without reference to any other provision of the Code or necessity of the infants. Bacon v. Bills, 6 R. 217; all that is necessary to show is, that property is owned jointly and in possession, is a vested estate and can not be divided without materially impairing its value. Burns v. Ingersoll, 6 R. 741. Infants' lands can not be sold as a part of or in conjunction with land in which they have no interest. It must be the property owned jointly that is indivisible without reference to other property. Kelley v. Muir, 17 R. 167, 30 S. W. 653; and the fact that it is indivisible must be proven. Gill v. Lane, 26 R. 267, 80 S. W. 1176; Talbot v. Campbell, 23 R. 2198, 67 S. W. 53; ZehnDEr v. SchoenbachlEr, 24 R. 947, 70S. W. 278.

(IOa) It is not necessary in an action under this section to allege or prove that a sale would be to the interest of an infant joint owner. Scott v. Graves, 153 Ky. 221, 154 S. W. 1084.

(10b) A woman owned one-fourth of an indivisible tract of land and her husband owned the residue thereof. By his will he gave her a life interest in one-third of his real estate. The cause of action for sale under this section existed. Eldridge v. Embry, 158 Ky. 707, 166 S. W. 223.

(10c) In actions under Code, sec. 490, subsec. 2, evidence of indivisibility can not be made as to infants by affidavit. This rule can not be waived by the statutory guardian. A ruling of this question does not seem to have been necessary to a decision of this case. (Subsec. Pollard v. Hamilton, 179 Ky. 284, 200 S. W. 621.

(10d) Burden of proof. In an action to sell for indivisibility a tract of 159 acres owned by three persons, the burden of proof was held to be upon the party contending that it was not divisible. Cherry v. Cherry. 162 Ky. 245, 172 S. W. 505.

(11) Infants may sue as plaintiffs by their statutory guardian, and no defense is required to be made for them, nor is bond required when the proceeds are to be invested under order of court. Powers v. Powers, 12 R. 793, 15 S. W. 523; Bacon v. Bills, 6 R. 217; Henning v. Baringer, 10 R. 674, 10 S. W. 136; Smith v. Leavill, 16 R. 609, 29 S. W. 319.

(11a) Under this section, it is the joint ownership which gives the right to a sale of the property, and an action therefor may be brought by infants, through their guardians as well as by adult parties. Moore v. Potter-Matlock Trust Co., 167 Ky. 201, 180 S. W. 789.

(11b) Infants-non-resident. A non-resident guardian of non-resident infants may bring suit for sale of land for indivisibility under Code, sec. 490, subsec. 2, if thereto authorized by the county court of the county in which the land lies. The failure to file before judgment a copy of the order of appointment or of the order authorizing the foreign guardian to act may be cured by filing such copies after a sale and exceptions thereto. Baker v. Weaks, 178 Ky. 515, 199 S. W. 53.

(11c) Need of guardian or guardian ad litem. A judgment of sale under subsec. 2 of this section is erroneous if one of the infant owners has no guardian and no guardian ad litem is appointed for her. Wade v. Wade, 153 Ky. 618, 156 S. W. 138.

(11d) Joint owners-certain persons were not. Under this section, where an infant owns an undivided two-thirds interest in fee in land and owns the remainder in the other third of a house and lot, and defendant owns a life estate in the third interest, plaintiff and defendant are not joint owners in the meaning of the statute, and the court has no power to order a sale of the property, at the instance of the infant, for division. Van Meter v. Van Meter, 160 Ky. 163, 169 S. W. 592.

(12) Joint tenants-tenants in common. Joint tenants, tenants in common and coparceners are entitled to have a division of their real estate as contemplated by this section, and a sale of it can not be prevented by the objection of one of the joint owners when the property can not be divided without materially impairing the value. Subsec. 7, sec. 494, only applies when the property can be divided. Kean v. Tilford, 81 Ky. 600, 5 R. 655, and see Harting v. Milward, 28 R. 776, 90 S. W. 260.

(12a) Where there are two joint owners of real estate to which dower interest attaches, such real estate may be sold in partition. Vanderpool's Heirs v. Vanderpool's Heirs and Creditors, 171 Ky. 381, 188 S. W. 461.

(13) Jurisdiction. Although an action

# Sales of Real Property, Etc.

# § 490

for the division of land and allotment that his debt be paid out of the interest of one of the owners, shall make the children of the mortgagor parties, they being parties to original action. *Southwick v. Greuzenbach, 12 R. 263, 13 S. W. 918.*

# (13a) A tract of land may be sold for indivisibility

although there is a life estate in one of the undivided interests. *Stovall v. Oates, '153 Ky. 81, 154 S. W. 914*

# (14) Indivisible property in some shares of which is life-tenancy with remainder.

Real property jointly held in possession, if indivisible without materially impairing its value, may be sold under this section, although some of the shares are held by life-tenants with remainder in fee to adults or infants. *Atherton v. Warren, 120 Ky. 15l, 27 R. 632, 85 S. W. 1100; Cradock v. Smythe, 30 R. 455, 99 S. W. 216; Stine v. Goodman, 29 R. 221, 92 S. W. 612; Harting v. Milward, 28 R. 776, 90 S. W. 260; Walsh v. Parr, 110 S. W. 300. These cases overrule the case of Dineen v. Hall, 112 Ky. 273, 23 R. 1615, 65 S. W. 445, 66 S. W. 392.*

# (14a) Under this section, the land of an infant, subject to a life estate, cannot be sold in partition proceedings,

where the land is in two tracts and one of them has been surveyed into town lots. *Walton Bk. & Tr. Co. v. Glinn, 16l Ky. 60, 170 S. W. 511.*

# (14b) An infant remainderman was held estopped to claim that the sale of his remainder interest was void.

*Sudduth v. Rowland, 164 Kv. 351, 175 S. W. 646.*

# (14e) Property held in remainder by one person cannot be sold under this section upon petition of the owner of the life estate.

*Walton Bk. & Tr. Co. v. Glinn, 161 Ky. 60, 170 S.W. 511.*

# (15) Lunatic. Creditor may have estate sold to pay debts.

*German Bank v. Engeln, 14 Bush 708.*

# (16) Money to equalize.

In a suit for partition of land owned jointly by two or more persons, one of the joint owners cannot be compelled to accept his interest partly in money in order to equalize the division. *Wrenn v. Gibson, 90 Ky. 189, 12 R. 26, 13 S. W. 766.*

# (17) Objection of one joint tenant to sale will not avail if the property cannot be divided without material impairment of its value.

*Gill v. Lane, 26 R. 267, 80 S. W. 1176.*

# (18) Parties-mortgagee.

In an action to sell land because indivisible it is not necessary that a mortgagee who asks.

# (20) Parties-service of process-bond.

In action under this section it is not necessary that infant be served with process if his guardian answers for him nor is the execution of a bond necessary before sale is ordered - see sec. 497.

Sales of Real Property, Etc. [§ 490]

(21) Partition of land between joint owners - see Ky. Stat., sec. 2348; Civil Code, sec. 490. See further note 45.

(22) Possession of joint owners necessary before sale can be ordered under this section. Life tenant cannot sue remaindermen. Malone v. Conn., 95 Ky. 93, 15 R. 421, 23 S. W. 677; Liter v. Fishback, 25 R. 260, 75 S. W. 232; Swearingen v. Abbott, 99 Ky. 271, 18 R. 184, 35 S. W. 925.

(23) Possession of one joint tenant is possession of all where there is no adverse holding. Stone v. Burge, 26 R. 1060, 83 S. W. 139.

(23a) Possession, adverse - issue as to. A suit for the sale of land in partition may be transferred to the ordinary docket for jury trial, where there is an issue as to adverse possession. Winchester v. Watson, 169 Ky. 213, 183 S. W. 483.

(24) Power of alienation - where the deed under which parties hold provides that land shall not be sold until youngest child arrived at twenty-one, it cannot be sold before that time. Young v. Young, 20 R. 1741, 49 S. W. 1074; and see Kean v. Tilford, 81 Ky. 600, 5 R. 655.

(24a) Process may be constructive. Land may be sold for indivisibility although a defendant is brought before the court by constructive service; no attachment is necessary. Krieger v. Sonne, 151 Ky. 739, 152 S. W. 936; Huber v. Johnson, 174 Ky. 697, 192 S. W. 821.

(25) Purchase by guardian indirectly of ward's land at decretal sale is constructively fraudulent. Morrison v. Garrott, 15 R. 305, 22 S. W. 320; and see Faucett v. Faucett, 1 Bush 511.

(25a) Several indivisible tracts. An action was brought under Civil Code, sec. 490, subsec. 2, for sale of three tracts of land as indivisible. A person of unsound mind, a defendant, was the owner of an undivided interest in fee in two of the tracts and of an undivided interest for life in the third tract. She was entitled to have the value of her entire interests in all the tracts set apart to her in that one of the tracts, which adjoined other lands of hers; this could be done without impairing the value of the tracts. There seems to have been no owelty of partition. (Subsec. 7.) Prewitt v. Hurt, 178 Ky. 526, 199 S. W. 33.

(26) Summons is not necessary against a lunatic when his committee enters his appearance. Finzer v. Nevin, 13 R. 773, 18 S. W. 367.

(27) Value of shares is to be determined at time of sale and the share of each owner must then be worth less than $100. Berry v. Lewis, 26 R. 530, 82 S. W. 252; Tichenor v. Rock, 140 Ky. 86, 130 S. W. 989.

(28) Vested interest in real estate, whether held in joint tenancy or by tenants in common or coparceners, if incapable of division, may be sold. Smith v. Upton, 12 R. 27, 13 S. W. 721.

(29) Vested estate - estate in possession - meaning of terms as used in this section. Ward v. Edge, 100 Ky. 757, 19 R. 59, 39 S. W. 440; Crutcher v. Rodman, 26 R. 294, 81 S. W. 252; Adams v. De Domengue, 112 S. W. 663, 129 Ky. 599; Fullenwider v. Johnson, 145 Ky. 19, 139 S. W. 1096.

(30) Venue of action brought under subsec. 2 of this section is fixed by sec. 62, subsec. 3. Perkins v. McCarley, 97 Ky. 43, 16 R. 801, 29 S. W. 867; Goldsmith v. Hieatt, 28 R. 741, 90 S. W. 259.

(30a) An action brought by devisees of a decedent against the other devisees for sale of land devised under the will and division of the proceeds must be brought in the county where the personal representative qualified. Boreing v. Melcon, 159 Ky. 14, 166 S. W. 612.

(31) Void proceedings cannot be cured by subsequent compliance with provisions of Code. Hulsewede v. Churchman, 111 Ky. 51, 23 R. 487, 63 S. W. 1.

(32) Homestead. Land in which infants have a homestead may be sold under subsec. 2. Jenkins v. McVaw, 145 Ky. 205, 140 S. W. 150.

(33) Advertisement - survey. Where advertisement is waived by consent of court failure to advertise will not invalidate sale, nor is survey of land necessary. Finney v. Finney, 144 Ky. 114, 138 S. W. 257.

(34) Dower of married woman may be sold in conformity to sec. 495. Finney v. Finney, 144 Ky. 114, 138 S. W. 257.

(35) Land cannot be sold under this section if a life estate in the whole land, which life estate is worth more than $100, be owned by one person, although the remainder be owned by several persons and although the land is indivisible. Berry v. Lewis, 26 R. 530, 82 S. W. 252; Ericson v. Martin, 145 Ky. 19, 138 S. W. 262.

(35a) Prior to Act of 1916 (Sess. Acts 707) indivisible land owned by one person in fee, subject to a life estate in an indivisible part of the land owned by one.

§ 490 Sales of Real Property, Etc. [Title X

other person, could not be sold under this section. Goldsmith v. Hieatt, 90 S. this section. Fullenwider v. Johnson, 145 W. 259, 28 R. 741.

(36) Bond not required when sale of indivisible property is made for purpose of dividing proceeds. Powell v. Baer, 143 Ky. 282, 136 S. W. 629; Ellis v. Smith, 147 Ky. 99, 143 S. W. 776.

(37) Interest of owners need not be fixed before a sale is ordered. Powell v. Baer, 143 Ky. 232; 136 S. W. 629.

(38) Title papers need not be filed in suit for division of proceeds. Powell v. Baer, 143 Ky. 282, 136 S. W. 629.

(39) Guardian may sue in his own name without making infants either parties plaintiff or defendant. Ellis v. Smith, 147 Ky. 99, 143 S. W. 776.

(40) Lien holder on one interest in indivisible property cannot have the whole property sold under this section - only the interest on which the lien is can be sold. Greenbaum v. Com., 144 S. W. 45, 147 Ky. 450.

(41) Whole of the property must be sold if proceeding is had under this section. The remainder subject to life estate cannot be sold. Williamson v. Mann, 119 S. W. 232, 134 Ky. 63.

(41a) One joint owner cannot procure a valid judgment directing a sale of the shares of other joint owners and not a sale of the entire title. Carpenter v. Moorelock, 151 Ky. 506, 152 S. W. 575; Strode v. Carpenter, 151 Ky. 506, 152 S. W. 575.

(41b) Section does not apply to minerals and gases. An owner of an undivided interest in minerals, gases, &c., cannot in an action against infants who own the remaining undivided interest in the fee, enforce sale of all the minerals and gases on the ground of indivisibility. Scott v. Pond Creek Coal Co., 152 Ky. 67, 153 S. W. 23.

(42) Bond not required to be executed before sale when action is brought to settle estate and property is sold under secs. 694 and 490, as the interest of the infant remains a lien on the land. Foley v. Graham, 110 S. W. 838.

(43) Settlement of estate. In action to settle estate interest of infants may be sold although not required to pay debts if property is indivisible but provisions relating to sales under sec. 490 must be observed. Catlin v. United States F. & G. Co., 125 S. W. 297, 137 Ky. 208.

(52) Sale forbidden by will. Sale under this section cannot be made if prohibited to all the joint owners by the will under which they hold title. Moore v. Potter-Matlock Trust Co., 167 Ky. 201, 180 S. W. 789.

(53) As the Code does not prohibit the sale of land for indivisibility, if a sale

Title X] Sales of Real Property, Etc. [9 491

# § 491. [Reversion or remainder may be sold.]

In an equitable action by the owner of a particular estate of freehold in possession, or by his guardian or committee if he be an infant or of unsound mind, against the owner of the reversion or remainder, though he be an infant or of unsound mind, and against the owner of the particular estate if he be an infant or of unsound mind; or, if the remainder be contingent, against the person, if in being, in whom it would have vested if the contingency had happened before commencement of the action, though he be an infant or of unsound mind, and against the owner of the particular estate if he be an infant or of unsound mind—real property may be sold for investment of the proceeds in other real property.

See Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate," secs. 112, 114, 115, 117-121, 123, 124, 125, 193, 194.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 115.

Thereof is prohibited in a will under which the land is held, a court having jurisdiction has the power to determine whether or not the will forbids the sale, and its judgment, if erroneous, is not void and cannot be attacked collaterally. *Houser v. Paducah Lands Co., 157 Kv. 252, 162 S. W. 1113.*

# (54) Sale under petition for partition.

A petition for partition was held to state a cause of action under this section. *Cherry v. Cherry, 162 Ky. 245, 172 S. W. 505.*

# § 491.

1. Bond not required when sale is made under this section for purpose of reinvestment. *Luttrell v. Wells, 97 Ky. 84, 16 R. 812, 30 S. W. 10; Haggins v. Rogers, 97 S. W. 362, 29 R. 1236; and subsec. 5, sec. 493.*
2. Contingent interest-sale for investment. Where land was devised to children, with provision that if either should die without issue his share should go to survivor, the interest of one may be sold and proceeds reinvested in land, to be held in same manner as land sold. *Harris v. Anderson, 3 R. 237; Tyler v. Jewel, 10 R. S87, 11 S. W. 25; Newman v. Ecton, 100 Kv. 653, 14 R. 793, 21 S. W. 526; Sheirich v. Maxwell, 28 R. 173, 89 S. W. 4.*
3. Land in which the present holder has a defeasible fee may be sold for reinvestment. *Owens v. Burks, 151 Ky. 162, 151 S. W. 369.*
4. Decree is necessary to pass title to real estate of infants; it cannot be sold or conveyed in any other manner than as provided in the Code. *Bill v. Burgess, 15 R. 41, 22 S. W. 84, and notes 9, 10, sec. 489.*
5. On a collateral attack on a sale of land of an infant for reinvestment as void, the pleadings were too indefinite to sustain the judgment. There was a reversal with directions to plead further. *Maiden v. Stewart, 163 Ky. 551, 174 S. W. 5.*
6. Guardian ad litem-error in report. A guardian ad litem appointed for three infants filed a report in which he omitted their names, but by mistake inserted the names of three other infant defendants; afterward he was appointed for the last named three and filed a report setting out their names. The court declined to set aside a sale by reason of this omission. *Thomson's Gdn. v. Thomson, 151 Ky. 296, 151 S. W. 658.*
7. Proceeds of sale under this section may be applied to the education and maintenance of the infant if necessary for that purpose. *Sanger v. Guscurth, 3 R. 592.*
8. Remainder interests can only be sold under this section and for reinvestment. *Dineen v. Hall, 112 Ky. 273, 23 R. 1615, 65 S. W. 445, 66 S. W. 392; Crutcher v. Rodman, 26 R. 294, 81 S. W. 252; Liter v. Fishback, 25 R. 260, 75 S. W. 232; Luttrel v. Wells, 97 Ky. 84, 16 I. 812, 30 S. W. 10.*
9. Sale bonds in proceedings under this section should be made payable to the commissioner and the fund remain under the control of the court until reinvested; sec. 493, subsec. 5. *Crutcher v. Rodman, 26 R. 294, 81 S. W. 252.*
10. Unconstitutional. So much of sec. 491 "as authorizes the sale of real property upon the petition of the life tenant in opposition to the wishes of the owner.

Sales of Real Property, Etc.

# Sale or Mortgage of Land Held in Trust Subject to Life Estate, Etc.

[Trust estates with remainder—sale of—investment of proceeds—improvements—tures—insurance. That when lands are held in trust by one person for the life of another, with remainder over to a class of persons, or to any person not ascertained or to be ascertained until the death of the person upon whose life such estate for life is made to depend, or with power on the part of such person for whose life such life estate is held by the trustee, to dispose by a last will and testament, or by an instrument in the nature of a last will and testament, it shall be competent for the circuit courts or courts of like jurisdiction in the county in which such land or a part thereof is situated, in an action to which all persons having a present or vested interest in such land are parties, to direct the trustee to either sell or mortgage such land; but in all actions it must be averred and proven to the court that such sale or mortgage would be beneficial to all the parties concerned, and facts showing such benefits must be alleged and proven. Any deed or mortgage executed under authority, or in pursuance of the fee, where the latter is not laboring under the disability of infancy or of unsound mind, is unconstitutional.]

Gossom v. McFerran, 79 Ky. 236, 2 R. 243; but the sale may be ordered if all parties in interest consent. Robinson v. Fidelity Trust Co., 11 R. 313, 11 S. W. 806; and see Harris v. Anderson, 3 R. 237.

# (8) Wife's land-sale of.

Husband and wife brought an action against their infant children asking for the sale of two tracts of land in which it was alleged the wife owned a life estate and the children the remainder; it appeared that the wife owned the fee in one of the tracts and it was held that the sale under the judgment did not pass the wife's title in the tract in which she owned the fee, but did as to the other. Munnell v. Orear, 84 Ky. 452, 8 R. 669, 1 S. W. 725.

# (9) Buildings to repair or erect.

Land cannot be sold under this section and the proceeds used in the erection or repair of buildings. Globe Realty Co. v. Lentz, 142 Ky. 497, 134 S. W. 882.

# (10) Right to have the rent of property.

Right to have the rent of property does not confer right to have it sold under this section. Kalfus v. Dane, 110 S. W. 871.

# (11) Reinvestment of part of proceeds not allowable.

The whole must be reinvested. Lilly v. Clay, 102 S. W. 278, 31 R. 318; and see Bethshares v. Allison, 141 Ky. 643, 133 S. W. 555.

# (12) Proceeds may be invested in another State.

Contingent remainders sale of. McClure v. Crume, 141 Ky. 361, 132 S. W. 433; Riley v. Dedman, 134 Ky. 146, 119 S. W. 756.

# (12a) The proceeds of land in Hart County sold under this section.

may be reinvested in land in Louisville. Owens v. Burks, 151 Ky. 162, 151 S. W. 309.

# (13) Contingent remainders-sale of.

McClure v. Crume, 141 Ky. 361, 132 S. W. 433.

# (14) Sale in connection with other land.

Sale in connection with other land in which infants have no interest is not authorized by the Code. Bennett v. Bennett, 143 Ky. 380, 136 S. W. 878.

# (15) Sale estoppel to question.

One whose land was sold under a void decree under this section, while he was an infant, estopped himself from claiming the land by collecting his part of the purchase-money after he arrived at full age and allowing the grantee to hold for many years. Sudduth v. Rowland, 164 Ky. 351, 175 S. W. 646.

# (16) Sale-void for want of bond.

A sale of an infant's land without bond, where a bond is required, is void unless the proceeds have been retained. Maiden v. Stewart, 163 Ky. 551, 174 S. W. 5.

# (17) Erroneous proceedings.

It is error to adjudge a sale under this section of infant's land in connection with another tract in which the infant has no interest; it is also error to adjudge a sale if the title papers or copies of same are not filed. Conrad v. Conrad, 152 Ky. 422, 153 S. W. 740.

# (18) Necessary parties.

A sale for re-

Title X] Sales of Real Property, Etc. [§ 491
of any judgment rendered in any such action, shall be held and construed and have the same effect as if executed by every person having a vested or contingent interest in or ownership of such land, and as if executed by all persons and classes who could take under the limitations or provisions of said deed, or as devisees under the exercise of such power to devise or appoint, and as if every claimant, present or future, under such deed or power, was under no disability whatever. The proceeds of the sales authorized by this section shall be paid into court, and shall be reinvested by the court after first having, by appropriate order, provided for the payment of the costs and taxes, if any, in other property to be conveyed and held subject to the same limitations and trusts as the land sold was held. The proceeds of all mortgages of such lands as are mentioned in this section shall be paid into court, and shall be appropriated under the order of the court in the construction of permanent improvements on the land mortgaged. But before appropriating such money in the construction of such improvements, the court shall provide for the payment of all unpaid taxes on said land, and the costs of the action. The court shall require the life-tenant, out of the rents, to pay the taxes and insurance on such improvements, and residue of the rents to be paid into court for disposition by the court until such mortgage debt is fully paid off; and such court may provide for semi-annual reports to be made by such life-tenant; and in the event of the disobedience of any order of such court by such life-tenant, it shall be the duty of the court to appoint a receiver, who shall take possession of such land and hold the same as the court may direct, and collect and receive all the rents and profits of such land, and pay the same into court for such disposition thereof as the court may take.] (Words in brackets are an Act of April 15, 1882, c. —, p. 90, a special act which, by an Act of April 24, 1882, c. -, p. 128, made the former a general act.*)

For uniformity it is now so compiled in this edition.—Ed.

investment where an infant owner of a reversion or remainder is not made a party can not be sustained under this section. Walton Bk. & Tr. Co. v. Glinn, 161 Ky. 60, 170 S. W. 511.

(19) In an action brought under this section to sell for reinvestment real estate devised to one for life and at his death to go to the heirs of his body but in the event the life tenant died without issue to go to three brothers of the life tenant or the living descendants of those who might be dead the life tenant had an

(20) In an action to sell for reinvestment land devised to a woman for life with remainder to her children and if she died without issue with remainder to certain other persons, those of the remote contingent remaindermen who were in being were made parties. The opinion says that they were not necessary parties.

Sales of Real Property, Etc. [Title X]

# Sale of Remainder and Contingent Interest.

# § 491a. [Sale of remainder and contingent interests.]

1. Remainder and contingent interest in real estate may be sold upon petition of any person having a present or vested interest, all persons in being having any interest in such estate being made parties to the action. If the court shall be satisfied that the interest of all concerned would be subserved by such sale, it shall adjudge accordingly; which judgment and sale thereunder shall invest the purchaser with all title of the present and future contingent claimants to the said real estate. (General Statutes, Chapter 63, Article VI., sec. 1.*)

2. [Proceedings regulated.] The proceedings in the case provided for in the preceding section shall be the same in all respects, as far as necessary, as those provided for in article iv. of this chapter; * and any defendant refusing to have the sale made may have partition according to his interest, if he so desire, unless in a case such as is provided for in section 6, article y., of this chapter.* (Id., sec. 2.)

3. [Proceeds to be reinvested.] The proceeds of sale shall be by the court reinvested in the same kind of property, to be conveyed and held in the same manner, subject to like limitation, trust, and conditions, as the property which was sold: Provided, however, That the court shall cause all just claims for taxes, or other lawful assessments for public improvements upon the property, to be first paid, and only the balance of the proceeds be invested; or, upon a sale of a part of such property, the proceeds may be invested in suitable improvements upon the remaining land, under the direction of the court; or it shall be lawful for the court to cause the proceeds to be reinvested as provided in section ?, article v., of this chapter.* (Id., sec. 3.)

See Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate," chap. Xv.

ties. Walden v. Smith, 179 Ky. 829, 201 S. W. 302.

(21) Finality of judgment that sale is not forbidden by will. This section does not apply to actions brought under sec. 490 to sell land for indivisibility and if a sale is prohibited in a will under which the land is held, a court having jurisdiction has the power to determine whether or not the will forbids the sale and its judgment, if erroneous, is not void and cannot be attacked collaterally. Houser v. Paducah Lands Co., 157 Ky. 252, 162 S. W. 1113.

(23) Land held by a woman for life with remainder to her son if living at her death or remarriage, otherwise reverting to grantor, is within the Act of April 24. 1882, part of this section. Lowry v. Berry, 178 Ky. 656, 199 S. W. 809.

# § 491a.

This provision of the General Statutes is yet in force and land may be ordered sold under it. Sheirich v. Max. well, 28 R. 173, 89 S. W. 4; Newman v. Ecton, 100 Ky. 653, 14 R. 793. 21 S. W. 526; and see Kalfus v. Davie, 110 S. W. 871.

Title X] Sales of Real Property, Etc. [§ 491a
*Articles 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 of the General Statutes of 1883 were by the compilers of the General Statutes of 1888 believed to have been repealed by Sections 489 to 498, inclusive, of the Civil Code and were accordingly omitted from the last edition of the General Statutes.

However, in the case of Newman v. Ecton, 100 Ky. 653, 14 R. 793, 21 S. W. 526, it was held that the statutes conferring the right given in Article VI., of Chapter 63, of the General Statutes of 1883, were not repealed by the Code.

Article VI. is given in full above as Section 49la of the Civil Code. It will be noted that in subsection 2 of section 49la, reference is made to Article IV., and to Section 6 of Article V. In subsection 3 reference is made to section 2 of Article V. In view of these references in such statute which has been held still to be in force, the possibility that such other sections so referred to may also be in effect so far as they are brought into relation with Section 49la would seem to require that such other sections be included in this edition of the Code.

Said statutes so referred to are as follows:

# ARTICLE IV.

# Sule of Lands of Infant Marricd Women.

1. The lands of a married woman under twenty-one years of age, or any interest therein, may be adjudged to be sold on the petition of her husband, if he be twenty-one years of age, or her next friend, she being made a defendant to the same; and if the husband be not the petitioner, he must also be made a defendant.
2. The petition must set forth and exhibit her evidence of title and interest in the lands, and state that a sale thereof will be to her interest, and be verified by the affidavit of the petitioner.
3. No judgment of sale shall be rendered until she files an answer to the petition, consenting to the sale, and shall acknowledge the same on privy examination by the court, or by the judge of the court, or by a commissioner appointed by the court to take the same. (Sertion as amended by Act of January 25, 1876, c. 28, p. 3.)
4. All parties interested in the estate, and the trustees of the married woman, if any, and the donor of the estate, or his personal representative, if it was a donation, must be made parties to the petition.
5. When the land is held jointly with others, a joint sale may be ordered, if assented to by the others; if not, a partition may be made, and the infant married woman's part may be sold.
6. No judgment shall be entered until the petitioner shall enter into a bond, to the Commonwealth, for the benefit of such married woman and her heirs, with surety in number and value and in substance as provided for in the second and third sections of the preceding article, which shall be examined and proceeded with as provided for in the fourth section thereof.
7. If no such covenant is given, the judgment, sale, and conveyance made under it, shall be void.
8. The proceedings shall, in other respects, be the same as are required to procure the sale of infant's real estate; and no sale of land shall be made under this article if, in the opinion of the court, such sale would be in contravention of the deed or will under which the same is held, and unless the court believes that such sale will be beneficial to the owner thereof.

407

# § 492] Sales of Real Property, Etc. [Title X

# ARTICLE V. Miscellaneous Provisions.

§ 2. The court ordering a sale of an infant, idiot, lunatic, or infant married woman's real estate, may cause the proceeds of the same to be reinvested in lands in or out of this State, or in United States bonds or bonds of this Commonwealth, or in the stock of a bank of the latter, subject to the same uses, limitations, and trusts as the estate sold was subject to; if not so reinvested, the proceeds of the sales of an infant's real estate shall be paid to his guardian; and that of an infant married woman to the petitioner, who has executed bond as required herein; and that of an idiot or lunatic shall be paid to his committee, and kept at interest, under the control of the court; and shall, with the interest, be paid to the person entitled thereto, when ordered by the court, or at the death of the idiot or lunatic. But the court may order such part of the proceeds of sales to be laid out for the maintenance or education of an infant, as is authorized by the chapter on Guardian and Ward; and such part of the proceeds of the sales of an idiot's or lunatic's real estate to be laid out for his maintenance, as is authorized by the chapter on Idiots and Lunatics.

6. If lands be held by joint tenants, tenants in common, or coparceners, and the court shall be satisfied from the proof that the same is not susceptible of division, or that a division thereof would materially impair its value, may, upon petition of any one interested, order a sale of the entirety, and make division of the proceeds among those entitled. If infants or persons of unsound mind are interested, it shall be the duty of the court, before ordering such sale, to take like steps and require like proceedings as are provided in article 3 of this chapter; and in case a married woman hath an interest, whether adult or infant, the cause shall proceed as prescribed in article 4 of this chapter, so far as regards her share: Provided, That if the married woman be an adult, she may, upon privy examination, in her answer waive or dispense with the necessity of such proceedings. The share of such infant or person of unsound mind shall not be paid by the purchaser, but remain a lien upon the land, bearing interest, until the person entitled becomes capable in law of receiving the same, or until the appointment of a statutory guardian, or the committee, as the case may be, who shall give bond, as required by law, or until the infant, if a female, shall marry.

See, also, Section 4706, Kentucky Statutes, and Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate," Chapter xv.-Ed.

# § 492 [541] [Sales under sections 489, 491.]

In the actions mentioned in subsections 3, 4 and 5 of section 489, and in section 491—

§492. (1) Allegation and proof-title tories. Womble v. Trice, 112 Ky. 533, 23 R. 1939, 66 S. W. 370; 67 S. W. 9; Siler v. Archer, 26 R. 557, 82 S. W. 256; Conrad v. ConrAD, 152 Ky. 422, 153 S. W. 740; National Surety Co. v. Rives' Gdn., 164 Ky. 201, 175 S. W. 351.

Sales of Real Property, Etc.

# 1. [s s44] [Sale not made if forbidden by deed or will.]

No sale shall be ordered if forbidden by the deed, will or contract under which the property is held.

# 2. [Title papers to be filed.]

The title papers, or copies of them, under which the property is held, must be filed with the petition.

# 3. [s 541] [Wife and children of lunatic to be parties.]

The wife and children, if any, of the person of unsound mind must be made defendants to the action.

# 4. [Allegation and proof that sale will be beneficial.]

Facts must be stated in the petition, and must be proved, showing that the sale will benefit the defendant; or, in the action mentioned in section 491, facts must be stated in the petition, and must be proved, showing that the sale will benefit the parties interested in the property. (Evidence to be taken on interrogatories, sec. 574.)

See Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate," secs. 127, 129, 130, 131, 132.

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 153.

# § 493. [Bond to be executed—exception-provision as to married women—funds held for reinvestment.]

Subject to the provisions of sections 491, 496 and 497, and except the cases mentioned in subsections 1 and 2 of section 489.

# (1a)

A judgment for sale of interests of an infant in land was erroneous, because no title papers had been filed. It was not void. Hays v. Beaver Creek Coal & Coke Co., 178 Ky. 149, 198 S. W. 743.

# (2)

Sale forbidden by deed. Subsection 1 of this section, providing that no sale shall be ordered if forbidden by the deed or will, has reference to sales of infant's real estate on the petition of the guardian or committee, and was not intended to be applied to sales of real estate held jointly, when the property so held cannot be divided without materially impairing its value or the plaintiff's interest therein; and in such cases the property may be sold, although the sale of the interest of one of the joint owners is forbidden by the deed. Kean v. Tilford, 81 Ky. 600, 5 R. 655; Warfield v. English, 11 R. 263, 11 S. W. 062; and see Young v. Young, 20 R. 1471, 49 S. W. 1074.

# (3)

The provisions of this section inhibiting a sale if forbidden by the deed are mandatory and must be obeyed. Moore v. Thompson, 80 Ky. 424, 4 R. 303; Morton v. Morton, 27 R. 661, 85 S. W. 1188; Lee v. Lee, 141 Ky. 62; 132 S. W. 154. See further, as to what provisions in a will do not forbid sale. Lindemeier v. Lindemeier, 91 Ky. 264, 12 R. 766, 15 S. W. 524; McGraw v. Minor, 12 R. 687, 15 S. W. 6; Luttrell v. Wells, 97 Ky. 84, 16 R. 812, 30 S. W. 10.

# (3a)

A provision in a will that one life tenant shall not sell his interest in the land devised does not prevent a court of equity from selling the entire land for reinvestment. Rousseau v. Page, 150 Ky. 812, 150 S. W. 983.

Proof that sale will benefit parties interested must be made. Lilly v. Clay, 102 S. W. 278, 31 R. 278.

# (5)

Civil Code, sec. 489, subsec. 3, does not authorize the sale of an infant's land to pay a debt, a portion of which was not created in the maintenance or education of the infant. Nunnelly v. Nunnelly, 173 Ky. 372, 191 S. W. 85.

# 493. (1) Bond to be executed.

When the provisions of this chapter require it, bond must be executed by the guardian, with at least two sureties, and it is not a bond unless there are two sureties; and unless this is done the sale will be void. Barnett v. Bull, 81 Ky. 127, 4 R. 939; Fritsch v. Klausing, 11 R. 788, 13 S. W. 241; Isert v. Davis, 17 R. 686, 32 S. W. 294; Loeb v. Struck, 19 R. 935, 42 S. W. 401; Hicks v. Jackson, 24 R. 218, 68 S. W. 419; Elliott v. Fowler, 112 Ky.

Sales of Real Property, Etc.

1. [Bond—form of.] The guardian of each infant, the committee of each person of unsound mind, and the husband or next friend of each married woman, must, before the sale is ordered, execute a bond to the infant, infant married woman, or person of unsound mind, with at least two sureties, worth not less than double the value of the estate to be sold, in substance as follows: "We,                     principal, and                 sureties, bind ourselves to            that the said                   as guardian (or committee or husband or next friend), will faithfully discharge all his duties as such, and will comply with the judgment and orders of the court in the action, and will account for, pay and deliver to the said -                     all money or property due or belonging to him (or her) when required."

2. [Court to approve.] The court shall indorse its approval on said bond, which shall be recorded with the order of sale, and certified by the clerk of said court, who shall deliver it to the clerk of the county court, and it shall, by him, be recorded and properly indexed.

3. [Sale void if not executed.] If the bond be not given, any order of sale, and any sale or conveyance made under such order, shall be absolutely void and of no effect.

376, 23 R. 1676, 65 S. W. 849; Phillip v. Spalding, 31 R. 581, 102 S.'W. 1193; Carpenter v. Julian, 180 Ky. 580, 203 S. W. 323. Cornell v. Cornell, 115 S. W. 795, 131 Ky. 650.

(le) The execution of the bond required by this section in a suit by a committee of a lunatic is indispensable, but it is not essential to the validity of the bond that it be signed by the principal. It is the sufficiency of the sureties to make the bond and not the solvency of the principal that is looked to. The principal is already bound as fully as he could be by joining in the execution of this bond by the former bond that he executed when he qualified as committee. Nor did the failure of the committee to sign the bond deprive it of its character as a statutory bond or convert it into a common law bond. It complied with all the substantial requirements of this section, and the sureties were as fully bound as if the principal had signed it. The form of the bond given in this section contemplates that it shall be signed by the principal, but this form is not of the substance of the bond. It is the signatures and sufficiency of the sureties that make the bond valid, and when they have signed it their liability is fixed by the terms of the obligation. "Allen v. Jenkins, 157 Ky. 406, 163 S. W. 234.

(ld) If by reason of indivisibility land is sold to pay the debts of an infant, or his ancestor or devisor, which brings more than enough to pay the debts and costs, the provisions of Code, sees. 493 may.

Sales of Real Property, Etc. [Title X]

# General Provisions.

# § 494. [Concerning sales-proceeds of sales-parties.]

1. The court shall appoint a suitable person, as commissioner, to execute its judgment.
2. [No warranty of title.] The court shall cause the title of the property to be conveyed by a commissioner to the purchaser, without warranty.
3. [Deed to be acknowledged and approved.] The conveyance must be acknowledged before and approved by the court, and be certified by its clerk to the clerk of the county court for record.
4. [Investment of proceeds.] In the actions for investment authorized by section 489, the court shall order the proceeds of sales to be invested, subject to its approval, in real estate in or out of this State, or in bonds of the United States or of this State, or in the stock of a bank of this State, subject to the same uses, trusts and limitations as the estate sold was subject to.
5. [Preservation and disbursement of proceeds.] In the actions mentioned in subsections 3 and 4 of section 489, the court shall make proper orders for the preservation and disbursement of the proceeds of the sale.
6. [Descent of proceeds.] If the owner of real estate which may have been sold under the provisions of this chapter die during infancy; or, being of unsound mind, die intestate; or, being an adult married woman, die without having received the proceeds of sale upon her written request, and upon privy examination as is authorized by this chapter, or without disposing in the manner authorized by law of the property in which the proceeds may have been invested, the person who would have been entitled to the property, if it had not been sold, shall be entitled to the proceeds, or to the property in which they have been invested.

Purchaser who paid his purchase-money was not liable to pay the purchase-money a second time because of the failure of the guardian to reinvest the purchase-money properly. Dillingham v. Dillingham, 9 Ky. Opinions 74.

(11) Collateral attack on sale. On a collateral attack on a sale of land of an infant for reinvestment as void, the pleadings were too indefinite to sustain the judgment. There was a reversal with directions to plead further. Maiden v. Stewart, 163 Ky. 551, 174 S. W. 5.

# § 494.

1. Appraisement is not necessary in sales for investment or of indivisible property or for partition of proceeds. Vivion v. Vivion, 21 R. 102, 50 S. W. 984; Graves v. Long Assn., 87 Ky. 441, 10 R. 414, 9 S. W. 297.
2. Burden of proof. In an action for the sale of a tract of land containing 159 acres, objection was made by the defendant. The burden of proof was upon the plaintiff to show that the land was divisible without materially impairing its value. Cherry v. Cherry, 162 Ky. 245, 172 S. W. 505.
3. Conveyance must be by commissioner - court has no power to order conveyance to a third person that he may convey to vendee of purchaser at judicial sale. Kinslow v. Grove, 98 Ky. 266, 17 R. 845, 32 S. W. 933.
4. Descent of proceeds. Construction of subsec. 6. Weisiger v. McDonald, 116 Ky. 862, 25 R. 1053, 76 S. W. 1080; Terrel v. Maupin, 26 R. 1203, 83 S. W. 591.
5. Land of a husband was sold to satisfy an encumbrance created by a deed in which the wife united; she was entitled to dower in the surplus and not to one-half as if it had been personal property. McClain v. McClain, 151 Ky. 356, 151 S. W. 926.

Title X] Sales of Real Property, Etc. [§ 495

# 7. Parties—sale as to some, division as to others.

All persons interested in the property must be made parties; and, if objection to the sale be made by a defendant having a joint interest, his share shall not be sold, but the property may be divided, and a sale of the shares of those desiring it may be ordered, if such division and sale can be made without materially impairing the value of the property or of the plaintiff's interest therein.

# 8. Consent of trustee when necessary.

If a deed or will give to a trustee a discretionary power to sell the property, the court shall not have power to order a sale of it without the consent of the trustee.

See Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate," secs. 137, 138.

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 153.

# § 495. Dower—sale of and compensation for.

1. If a woman have a vested or contingent right to dower in land ordered to be sold pursuant to the provisions of this chapter, the court, with her consent, to be taken upon privy examination if she be married, and of sound mind, or without her consent if she be of unsound mind, may order a sale of the land free from her right; and shall provide for reasonable compensation to her out of the proceeds of sale, or that she shall have the same right in property purchased with the proceeds as she had in the property sold.
2. If a woman have a vested or contingent right to dower in land sought to be sold, under section 490, she shall be made a party to the action, brought to sell such land, and the court may, with or without her consent, order a sale of the land free from her right; and shall provide for a reasonable compensation to her out of the proceeds of sale, or that she shall have the same right in property purchased with the proceeds as she had in the property sold. (Section, as amended by Act of March 17, 1902, c. 18, p. 48. As to the sale and conveyance of inchoate right of dower of married women who are lunatics, see Ky. Stat., sec. 2145.)

See Grider's "Judicial Sales of Real Estate," sec. 140.

# Duty of commissioner.

The commissioner has no power to change the time, or terms, or place of sale specified in the judgment. Cofer v. Miller, 7 Bush 545, and see notes to sec. 696.

# Setting apart in one tract interest of a defendant in three tracts.

An action was brought under Civil Code, sec. 490, subsec. 2, for sale of three tracts of land as indivisible. A person of unsound mind, a defendant, was the owner of an undivided interest in fee in two of the tracts and of an undivided interest for life in the third tract. She was entitled to have the value of her entire interests in all the tracts set apart to her in that one of the tracts which adjoined other lands of hers; this could be done without impairing the value of the tracts. There seems to have been no owelty of partition. Prewitt v. Hurt, 178 Ky. 526, 199 S. W. 33.

# § 495. (l) Dower right.

In a proceeding to sell real estate under sec. 490, the dower right of the married women can only be divested in the manner provided in this section. Woman's Club v. Reed, 111 Ky. 806, 23 R. 1346, 64 S. W. 739; 65 S. W. 862; Reed v. Reed, 25 R. 2324, 80 S. W. 520.

# (la) In an action to sell land for indivisibility, the wife of a joint owner in

# Sales of Real Property, Etc.

# 2146. Dower in insane or infant husband's estate—sale of-when wife an infant.

The wife of an infant husband, or of a husband judicially declared an idiot or lunatic or imbecile, if she be of the age of twenty-one years, or if not, with the approval of the circuit court, on such terms as it may deem equitable, may unite with his guardian or committee, or with the commissioner of the court, in the conveyance of his real estate, so as to release her prospective right of dower, when a sale and conveyance thereof are ordered to be made by the guardian or committee, or by the commissioner or other officer of the court. A wife not of full age may also be permitted by the circuit court to unite with her adult husband in the conveyance of his real estate without terms, or on such as may be deemed equitable, so as to release her prospective right of dower. If, in judicial proceedings to sell the real estate of an infant husband, or of a husband judicially declared an idiot, imbecile or lunatic, his wife is made a party-defendant, and by her answer, and on privy examination in open court, or by a judge of a court wherein such proceedings are pending, or by a commissioner appointed by the court to take the same, she consents to a sale of the property, free from her prospective right of dower, either without terms or on terms by her designated, the court may, if it deems the terms of such consent equitable, order the sale of such property, free from her prospective right of dower, upon the terms of consent proposed by such wife. (Ky. Stat., sec. 2146.)

# 2148. Husband's interest in wife's land--law as to wife's interest applied.

The provisions of this act relating to the wife's dower, or interest in the husband's real estate, shall apply in all cases, so far as may be, to the husband's interest in the wife's real estate. (Id., sec. 2148.)

# § 496. [Bond when not required—sale if share worth less than $100.]

1. [When neither bond nor privy examination required.] In the action mentioned in subsection 1 of section 490, neither the bond nor the privy examination provided for in this chapter shall be required.

# § 497 [548] [Purchase money to remain lien on land until bond executed—payment of proceeds and interest.]

1. [Money to remain lien on land until bond executed.] In the action mentioned in subsection 2 of section 490, the share of an infant, or of a person of unsound mind, shall not be paid by the purchaser; but shall remain a lien on the land bearing interest until the infant become of age, or the person of unsound mind become of sound mind, or until the guardian of the infant, or the committee of the person of unsound mind, execute bond as required by section 493.
2. [Payment of proceeds—bond-married woman.] The infant upon becoming of age, or the person of unsound mind upon becoming of sound mind, or the guardian or committee of the infant or person of unsound mind, upon executing bond, may receive the proceeds; but, if the infant be a married woman, the provisions of subsection 6 of section 494 must be complied with.
3. [Proceeds of married woman her separate property.] The proceeds when received by a married woman shall be her separate property.
4. [Interest may be paid before bond executed.] The court may permit the guardian or committee, without executing the bond above mentioned, or the married woman, though she be an infant, to receive the interest on the money until it is paid.

Two or more persons, either of whom can bring the action mentioned in subsection 5 of section 489, may join therein against defendants who jointly own the property mentioned in the petition.

# (7) Bond-when must be executed before sale.

A sale made in an action by the guardian of two infants for sale of their real estate for reinvestment is void unless the bond of sec. 493 is given before the sale. The sale cannot be sustained by a bond given after the sale; nor can it be sustained under the theory, that the sale was necessary because of indivisibility: neither the pleadings nor the evidence showed facts within Civil Code, sec. 490. Where a father owned a life estate in land and his infant child was the remainderman, and the life estate was sold for debt and the property is subject to liens, a judicial sale of the land at the suit of the guardian of the infant remainderman, under this section, for half its value, will not be confirmed Buckner's Trustee v. # (4)

Under this statute, an action for the sale of real estate held in trust for reinvestment should be brought by the trustee against the party's remaindermen either for judicial sale or for approval of a private sale provisionally made. Noel v. Harper, 170 Ky. 657, 180 S. W. 503.

# (5)

Under this statute the court, on application of a trustee under a will, has the power to direct the sale of parcels of business property for reinvestment of the proceeds. Gillespie v. Winston's Trustee, 170 Ky. 667, 186 S. W. 517.

# (6)

Land held by a woman for life with remainder to her son if living at her death or remarriage, otherwise reverting to grantor is within the Act of April 24, 1882, part of this section. Lowry v. Berry, 178 Ky. 656, 199 S. W. 809.

# (7)

A life tenant borrowed money with which to make repairs and improvements on land in which his children owned the remainder in fee. An action was brought by the lender of the money to subject the land. The court had no jurisdiction to sell the remainder interest of the infants. In this case the life tenant and the trustee appointed by the will under which title was obtained to take charge of the life tenant and the remaindermen, had executed a mortgage to the creditor. Buckner v. Kelly, 151 Ky. 818, 152 S. W. 963.

Validity of the amendment cannot now be questioned. Marshall v. Anderson's Trustee, 157 Ky. 117, 162 S. W. 559.

(That when lands are held in trust by one person for the life of another," includes property held in trust jointly for two or more persons. Marshall v. Anderson's Trustee, 157 Ky. 117, 162 S. W. 559.

# (10)

A provision in a will empowering the trustee named therein to sell and convey the real estate in his discretion, but withholding such power from any subsequent trustee will not be construed as forbidding a sale by the chancellor for reinvestment under Civil Code, Carroll's, sec. 498 (Seymour's, sec. 491), after another trustee has qualified. Marshall v. Anderson's Trustee, 157 Ky. 117, 162 S. W. 559.

# (11)

Where a testamentary trustee holds lands in trust under the terms of a will for the lives of two beneficiaries, with remainder over to a class of persons who cannot be ascertained until the death of the survivor of the two life tenants, it is within the power of a chancellor under the provisions of the amendment of 1882, Civil Code, Carroll's, sec. 498 (Seymour's, sec. 491), to approve the sale by the trustee and reinvest the proceeds. Marshall v. Anderson's Trustee, 157 Ky. 117, 162 S. W. 559.

# CHAPTER XV.

# DIVISION OF LAND AND ALLOTMENT OF DOWER.

499. 1. Circuit or county court has jurisdiction—parties—practice.

2. Persons under disability-provision as to.

3. Division or allotment-when court may order.

4. Three commissioners to be appointed—oath of.

5. Time and place of meeting of commissioners.

6. Duty of commissioners report—practice.

7. Deeds to be made if report confirmed.

8. Report and deeds to be recorded.

9. Two commissioners may act.

10. Allegations of petition may be contested--trial by court.

11. Removal of case from county to circuit court.

12. Appeal may be taken.

13. Costs—how apportioned.

14. Verification of pleadings not required.

15. Compensation of commissioners-taxed as costs.

16. Jurisdiction of courts of equity not affected.

499a. Partition of land between life-tenants and their descendants.

# § 499 [546 to 556] [Circuit or county court has jurisdiction—parties—practice.]

1. [Petition-parties — title papers.] A person desiring a division of land held jointly with others, or an allotment of dower, may file in the circuit court or county court of the county in which the land or the greater part thereof lies a petition containing a description of the land, a statement of the names of those having an interest in it, and the amount of such interest, with a prayer for the division or allotment; and, thereupon, all persons interested in the property who have not united in the petition shall be summoned to answer on the first day of the next term of the court. The written evidences of the title to the land, or copies thereof, if there be any, must be filed with the petition.

# § 499. (1 Allotment of dower.

"By proceeding to have dower assigned and not insisting upon the allotment of homestead, appellant waived her right to homestead." Burch v. Atchinson, 82 Ky. 585, 6 R. 636; and see further, as to dower, Ky. Stat., sec. 2132.

(2) A widow is not entitled to dower unless her husband was beneficially seized of the land during coverture. Fontaine v. Dunlap, 82 Ky. 321, 6 R. 201; 18 B. M. 107. See further, Fritz v. Tudor, 1 Bush 28; Morgan v. Conn, 3 Bush 58 and Ky. Stat., sec. 2132 and notes.

(3) Commissioners' report where the evidence is conflicting will be sustained, unless it appears that they have made a decided error. Chamberlain v. Ballinger, 11 R. 966, 13 S. W. 429; McClanahan v. McClanahan, 12 R. 440, 14 S. W. 496, and see note 21.

(4) (Costs-attorney fees-apportionment of. See Williamson v. Williamson, 1 Met. 303; Lang v. Constance. 20 R. 502, 46 S. W. 693; Ky. Stat., secs. 489, 2080.

(5) Court has no authority to partition land or direct commissioners how.

2. [Persons under disability—provision as to.] The statutory guardian of an infant, committee of a person of unsound mind, and husband of a married woman, may file or unite in the petition, in the names of, and in conjunction with such infant, person of unsound mind, or married woman; and, if the petition be against an infant, person of unsound mind, or married woman, the guardian, committee or husband may appear and defend for them; if they fail to do so, the court shall appoint a discreet person for that purpose.

3. [Division or allotment—when court may order.] Upon such a petition by all interested in the property; or upon the service of a summons on all who have an interest in the property and have not united in the petition, ten days before the commencement of the term, the court may order the division, or allotment of dower, according to the rights of the parties.

4. [Three commissioners to be appointed—oath of.] The court shall appoint three competent persons to make the partition, or allotment of dower, or both, having a due regard to the rights of all parties interested. Before proceeding to act, the commissioners shall take an oath to discharge their duty impartially.
