(6) Division of land—rights of parties. It is proper to order the commissioners to so lay off the share of one as to adjoin other land owned by him, if no detriment would be done to the others; and to lay the parcels of each in different tracts as near together as may be done without doing injustice to the others. Gaithers v. Brown, 7 B. M. 90; Graham v. Graham, 8 Bush 334.

(7) Dower. For tables estimating the value of potential right of dower, see Lancaster v. Lancaster, 78 Ky. 198; and value of present right of dower, see Alexander v. Bradley, 3 Bush 667; 3 Bush 216; see Ky. Stat., sec. 2132, and notes thereto.

(8) Final order. Order appointing commissioners and directing them to divide the land is not a final order and court may afterward set it aside. Salyer v. Arnett, 23 R. 321, 62 S. W. 1031.

(9) Indivisible land. Although land may be indivisible as a whole, yet if the interest of one of the owners can be set apart to him without injury to the interest of the other owners, it should be done. Conner v. Cox, 15 R. 140, 22 S. W. 605.

Title X] Division of Land and Allotment of Dower.

# 5. [Time and place of meeting of commissioners.]

The order of appointment shall fix a time and place for the meeting of the commissioners; who shall meet accordingly; but, if prevented from meeting at the time and place so fixed, they may meet as soon thereafter as convenient, and may adjourn to such other time and place as they may agree upon, until their duty shall be performed.

# 6. [Duty of commissioners—report—practice.]

They shall survey and allot to the parties their respective interests in the land, and make report thereof to the court; which may either confirm, set aside or remand the report to the commissioners for correction.

# 7. [Deeds to be made if report confirmed.]

If the report be confirmed, a commissioner to be appointed for the purpose shall, by deed, convey to each party the land allotted to him.

# 8. [Report and deeds to be recorded.]

Said report and deed shall be recorded in the clerk's office of the county court, in proper books to be kept for that purpose; and, if the report be confirmed by the circuit court, it and the deed shall be certified by the clerk of that court to the clerk of the county court, for record.

# 9. [Two commissioners may act.]

Two of the commissioners may act, if one refuse or fail to do so.

# 10. [Allegations of petition may be contested—trial by court.]

A party summoned may, by answer, controvert the allegations of the petition or contest the rights claimed therein; and, thereupon, the case shall be tried and decided as an ordinary action, but without the intervention of a jury.

Allotment the land should be sold and not partitioned. Wrenn v. Gibson, 90 Ky. 189, 12 R. 26, 13 S. W. 766. Where some of the tenants in common are not before the court is invalid. Burchett v. Clark, 162 Ky. 586, 172 S. W. 1048.

# (15) Oath of commissioners.

Commissioners verbally agreed upon their report before being sworn, but afterward took the required oath and made the same report as that agreed upon, an objection that they were not sworn is not well taken. MeClanahan v. McClanahan, 12 R. 440, 14 S. W. 496.

# (16) Parties.

The persons owning an interest in the land under the same title are the only persons necessary to be made parties. It is not necessary to make those claiming an adverse interest parties. McIntire v. McIntire, 82 Ky. 502, 6 R. 559. Nor will a person claiming an adverse interest be permitted to file his petition setting up his claim; Bacon v. Boyd, 17 R. 1276, 34 S. W. 525. But this rule does not apply to persons in possession who are claiming the land. Logan v. Catron, 19 R. 1200, 43 S. W. 213.

# (16a) Parties -invalidity for defect of.

A partition of land made by proceedings.

# (16b) Pleading.

A petition for partition was held sufficient. Nickles v. Mineral Development Co., 152 Ky. 198, 153 S. W. 235.

# (16c) Possession-when unnecessary.

It is not necessary that the plaintiff in a proceeding for division be in possession of the land. The action should be tried as a common law action. Williams v. Capital Min., Lum. & Oil Co., 153 Ky. 772, 156 S. W. 409.

# (17) Rents.

In Bridgeford v. Barbour, 80 Ky. 529, 4 R. 470, it was held that where one of the heirs, who was also executor, collected rents, the amount so collected was a charge on his portion of the estate.

# (18) One tenant in common or joint tenant has not a lien on the interest of his co-tenant for the payment of the value of the rent received by the latter before partition of the land.

Division of Land and Allotment of Dower

# 11. Removal of case from county to circuit court.

If the action be pending in the county court when such answer is filed, it may be removed, on motion of either party, to the circuit court for trial; and the clerk shall, thereupon, deliver the papers pertaining to the case to the clerk of the circuit court.

# 12. Appeal may be taken.

An appeal may be taken to the Court of Appeals from a final judgment, whether rendered by the circuit court or county court.

# 13. Costs—how apportioned.

The costs of the action shall be apportioned among the parties in the ratio of their interests, except that the costs arising from a contest of fact or law shall be adjudged against the unsuccessful party.

# 14. Verification of pleadings not required.

No verification of the pleadings shall be required.

# 15. Compensation of commissioners taxed as costs.

The commissioners shall be paid a reasonable compensation, to be taxed as costs. (Compensation, Ky. Stat., sec. 1740.)

# 16. Jurisdiction of courts of equity not affected.

This section does not affect the jurisdiction of courts of equity to make partition or allot dower.

(19) In an action to have dower allotted, rents can be recovered from the institution of the suit; and if the plaintiff die her administrator can recover rent for the same time. Magruder v. Smith, 79 Ky. 512, 3 R. 282; Yancy v. Smith, 2 Met. 408.

(20) The widow is entitled to one-third of the rents and profits of her husband's dowable estate from his death until dower is assigned her as against a vendor's lien for the purchase-money. Wilson v. Ewing, 79 Ky. 549, 3 R. 362; but is not entitled to dower as against the vendor's lien. Lee v. Jaines, 81 Ky. 443, 5 R. 492.

(21) Report of commissioners appointed to make partition is subject to exception only upon the ground that they departed from or disregarded the judgment. Beatty v. Beatty, 10 R. 72, 5 S. W. 771; and see note 3.

(22) Transfer from county to circuit court not authorized unless an issue is made and motion made to transfer as provided in subsecs. 10, 11. Salyer v. Arnett, 23 R. 321, 62 S. W. 1031.

(23) Verification of pleadings not required. Hall v. Snipes, 10 R. 435, 9 S. W. 388; Williams v. Capital Min., Lum. & Oil Co., 153 Ky. 772, 156 S. W. 409.

(24) Warranty-limitation. The implied warranty of title which arises from a partition of land between joint devisees survives to an heir of one of them, but such implied warranty does not run with the land so as to give a right of action to a vendee of one of the partners. Jones v. Bigstaff, 95 Ky. 395, 15 R. 821, 25 S. W. 889.

(25) The statute of limitation does not begin to run against a partitioner or his heir for contribution until there has been an actual loss of land by superior title. Jones v. Bigstaff, 95 Ky. 395, 15 R. 821, 25 S. W. 889.

(26) Title papers. Rule to require that they be filed is proper practice. Bartee v. Edmunds, 96 S. W. 535, 29 R. 872; see note.

Title X] Division of Land and Allotment of Dower. [§ 499a

# § 499a. [Partition of land between life tenants and their descendants.]

1. That where the land is held under deed or will vesting a life estate in two or more persons or in trust for their benefit, with remainder as to the share of each to his or her children or descendants, it shall be lawful for a court of equity, on the petition of one of such life tenants and his or her children or descendants who would then be entitled to such remainder, all persons having interests in such lands being made parties, to partition such land so as to set apart to such life tenants and children or descendants so much of said land to which they shall be entitled in severalty; and to that alone shall attach the title or interest of after-born children or descendants in whom, by the terms of said deed or will, such a remainder would vest.

2. Where part of the land so held shall be situated in this State and part in another State, the court may, in making partition, take into consideration the value of all of said land, and set apart to those resident in this State land in this State equal to their full share of all of said land, securing, by proper deeds and orders to the non-residents, release of the interest of such residents in the lands in the other State or Territory.

(Act of May 15, 1886, c. 1171, p. 120.)

# PASSWAYS-PRIVATE.

§ 3779a-l. Places to which may be opened--appointment of commissioners-report. Whenever it shall appear to a county court that it is necessary for a citizen to have a private passway over the land of one or more persons in the county, to enable him to attend courts, elections, a meeting-house, a mill, a warehouse (a regular steamboat landing), ferry or railroad depot, most further as to necessity for filing title papers, Toler v. Toler, 110 S. W. 388.

(26a) A defendant in a proceeding for partition of land set up a counterclaim. It was essential for him to file the title papers. Williams v. Capital Min., Lum. & Oil Co., 153 Ky. 772, 156 S. W. 409.

(26b) Plaintiffs, in an action for partition, are not required to produce other title papers, where defendants filed the record in a suit in which the title papers of the common source of title were filed. Burchett v. Clark, 162 Ky. 586, 172 S. W. 1043.

(27) Parties in interest are those owning an interest in the land and not lien holders. Barry v. Baker, 93 S. W. 1061, 29 R, 573.

(28) Guardian ad litem. When the guardian of the infant is plaintiff and interested in partition suit it is proper to appoint a guardian ad litem. Adams v. De Dominguez, 112 S. W. 663, 129 Ky. 599.

(29) Neither motion for a new trial nor separation of law and facts is necessary in partition proceedings.

(30) Transfer to circuit court. When the right to partition is contested or the title put in issue in a case pending in the county court it should be removed to the circuit court. Hunt v. Phillips, 105 S. W. 445, 32 R. 257.

(31) Report of commissioners is entitled to great weight and should not be set aside as unequal or unfair unless the evidence is convincing. Mead v. Mead, 101 S. W. 330, 31 R. 70.

(32) Appeals. An appeal from a judgment of partition in a county court lies to the Court of Appeals. Howard v. Deems, 178 Ky. 760, 199 S. W. 1079.

(33) A judgment for partition may be reversed so that the heirs of deceased plaintiff may be brought before the court and their interests determined in the partition. Burchett v. Clark, 162 Ky. 586, 172 S. W. 1048.

§ 499a. Construction of section. Eversole v. Combs, 130 Ky. 82, 112 S. W. 1132.

convenient to his residence, the court shall appoint commissioners as in case of a road who, being first sworn to discharge their duties faithfully and impartially, shall go upon the lands of the person through which the passway is proposed, whether arable or not, and shall report, in writing, to the court whether or not a private passway is necessary to the applicant for the purposes aforesaid; and if favorable to the passway, they shall in their report designate the exact route for the same by metes and bounds, course and distance, and the width thereof, which, in no case, shall exceed twenty feet, and they shall determine and assess which will be a just compensation to each owner and tenant, if any, for the land proposed to be taken for a passway, in the same manner as upon an application to open and establish a new road. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3779a-1.)

§ 3779a-2. Notice of application to owner. The applicant for commissioners shall give the person through whose land the passway is proposed ten days' previous notice of the intended application. (Id., sec. 3779a-2.)

§ 3779a-3. Report of commissioners—copy to be given owner. The commissioners shall make out a report of their proceedings and sign the same, and at least ten days before the court is required to act deliver a copy thereof to the proprietor and tenants, if any, of the lands over which the proposed passway is to run. (Id., sec. 3779a-3.)

§ 3779a-4. Trial of application. When the report of the commission is returned to the court it shall proceed to establish the passway or refuse the same as in the case of a road. (Id., sec. 3779a-4.)

§ 3779a-5. Damages paid by applicant before road opened. No passway shall be established without the consent of the owners until compensation and damages to the owner and tenants, if any, of the land shall be assessed, as in case of a public road, which compensation and damages and the cost of the whole proceeding shall be paid by the applicant before the passway shall be established. (Id., sec. 3779a-5.)

§ 3779a-6. Gates to be erected and maintained by applicant. Any gates that shall become necessary by the establishment of a passway shall be erected and kept up at the expense of the applicant for the passway. (Id., sec. 3779a-6.)

§ 3779a-7. Obstructing passway—penalty. Any person who shall put any obstructions in a passway or shall prop open, pull down, injure or leave open a gate erected across the same shall be liable to a fine of ten dollars, recoverable by warrant in the name of the Commonwealth, the fine to be laid out in repairing the passway or gate. (Id., sec. 3779a-7.)

§ 3779a-8. Change of passway--how effected. When the proprietor of lands over which a passway may be established shall wish to change the same he shall give the applicant for the passway ten days' previous notice, and may, thereupon, apply to the county court; and the court shall appoint commissioners to view the proposed change, who shall be sworn as on original applications, and shall go on the grounds and report the conveniences and inconveniences of making the change. Upon the return the court shall, as may seem proper, make the proposed change or not. But before such change shall take effect the proprietor shall open the new route and remove and put up the gates across the same at his own expense and pay the other costs of procedure. (Id., sec. 3779a-8.)

Title X] Division of Land and Allotment of Dower. [5 499a

# § 3779a-9. Discontinuance of--places not to be opened-appeal.

Passways may be discontinued by court in the same manner as roads. The law regulating appeals in road cases shall apply to and govern proceedings concerning passways. No passway shall be established through any town lot, burying ground, buildings or yard (or grounds of any charitable institution of this Commonwealth) without the consent of the owner. (Id., sec. 3779a-9.)

# § 895. Passway or gate-costs of procedure in trial court.

Any person applying for a passway or to erect a gate, or to discontinue a passway or remove a gate, whether successful or not, shall pay the costs of the procedure in the inferior court. (Id., sec. 895.)

# 10. Actions that survive.

No right of action for personal injury or injury to real or personal estate shall cease or die with the person injuring or injured, except actions for assault, slander, criminal conversation, and so much of the action for malicious prosecution as is intended to recover for the personal injury; but for any injury other than those excepted, an action may be brought or revived by the personal representative, or against the personal representative, heir or devisee, in the same manner as causes of action founded on contract. (Ky. Stat., scc. 10.)

§ 500 [557 to 560] [Unnecessary, when—judgment without—order of.] Upon the death of a party to an action, or the cessation of his power as a personal representative or other fiduciary—

500. (1 Death of some of parties will not prevent prosecution of action against survivors if court can properly render judgment against them. Chesnut v. Robertson's Admr., 174 Ky. 836, 192 S. W. S78.

(2b) Deed-commissioners may be made without revivor. It is not necessary to revive an action for the purpose of making a deed on account of the death of a party after the sale has been confirmed. Likens v. Pate, 160 Ky. 319, 169 S. W. 734.

(3) Meaning of survives. It was held under the Code of 1854 that the word "survives," used in sec. 557 (sec. 500 of this Code), "is used in its technical sense, and only applies to cases in which the right survives by operation of law, or operation of the will of contract under which the parties claim or are charged." Smith v. Ferguson, 3 Met. 424.

Title XI] Revivor of Actions. [§ 500

1. [After death of one party action may proceed as to others.] If the right of action survive to or against the remaining parties, the action may proceed, without revivor, after statement, on the record, of such death or cessation of power.

2. [Judgment may be rendered-when.] Though the right of action do not survive to or against the remaining parties, the court may render judgment, as between them, if it can do so without prejudice to others.

(3a) Assault and battery. If a defendant in an action for assault and battery dies after judgment against him and upon an appeal the judgment is reversed the action cannot be prosecuted against the personal representatives of the defendant. Shield's Admrs. v. Rowland, 151 Ky. 136, 151 S. W. 408.

(4) Order reviving not final and no appeal can be taken therefrom. If the order is improper the court can review its action when a final judgment is rendered. Reeves v. Davis, 6 R. 514.

(5) Parties. An order of revivor should be served on the owner of the legal title to land ordered to be sold in the lifetime of the intestate, and where a sale is made, but has not been reported or confirmed, the action should be revived in the name of the personal representative and heirs. Murphy v. Fryer, 1 R. 348.

(5a) Personal injuries. An action was brought in Kentucky under the law of Tennessee for personal injuries sustained in Tennessee; plaintiff died pending the action and the action was revived in the name of his administrator. The widow was rightly permitted to file a petition in said action for damages for the death of her husband and to recover jointly with the administrator a judgment for the personal injuries and the death. Proctor Coal Co. v. Beavers, 151 Ky. 839, 152 S. W. 965.

(6) Practice. In an action to recover land, the defendant died, leaving infant children. The order of revivor was served on those over and under fourteen years of age. As to the latter, the service was void. Cox v. Story, 80 Ky. 04, 3 R. 551.

(7) The plaintiff having died, an order was made reviving the action in the name of his administrators and heirs; but there was no service of the order on defendant, and the record did not show on whose motion the action was revived. It could not be presumed that the order of revivor was made on motion of defendant, and a judgment for the plaintiff was held to be void. Amyx v. Smith, 1 Met. 529.

(8) The death of defendant being suggested and the action continued for revivor, at the next term, without a formal revivor, his executrix filed answer. On a second appeal by the plaintiff from a judgment against him, it was held too late to question her right to defend. Moss v. Rowland, 3 Bush 505.

(9) When a plaintiff dies pending his suit, his death may be pleaded in abatement; but the defendant may waive such plea and permit the cause to be tried upon its merits, without revivor. The death of a plaintiff after judgment in the circuit court, and before the appeal was prosecuted, did not take from the Court of Appeals the right to entertain the appeal prosecuted in the name of the dead plaintiff, and the judgment of reversal was not void. Spalding v. Wathen, 7 Bush 659; and see Hopkins v. Hopkins, 91 Ky. 310, 12 R. 945, 15 S. W. 854.

(10) The death of the appellee after judgment, and before the time allowed in which to file a bill of exceptions, will not prejudice the appellant if his bill be tendered within the time allowed, although there has been no revivor. Hayden v. Ortkeis, 83 Ky. 396, 7 R. 399.

(10a) One of the several appellees died within the time given to the appellants to tender a bill of exceptions. The bill of exceptions was properly tendered within the time allowed; it could not be made a part of the record until after the revivor of the action in the circuit court. It was proper to continue the case for that purpose. Gish Banking Co. v. Leachman's Admr., 159 Ky. 769, 169 S. W. 481.

(11) It was agreed between counsel that the action should be revived against the administrator, but the agreement was not entered of record, and on an appeal the administrator had the action dismissed because there was no revivor. The proper practice in such case would be to have applied in proper time to the

# § 501] Revivor of Actions.

# [Revivor if right to sue or defend passes to a stranger.]

If the right of action survive or pass to or against a person who is not a party to the action; or pass, otherwise than by survivorship, to or against a person who is a party to the action, the action may be revived, by an order of the court, for or against such person. (Hore judgments revived, secs. 401 to 408; provisions of this title apply to cases in Court of Appeals, sec. 767.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 146, 614e. See Miller's Xy. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 116.

# § 501 [561] [Order may be on motion or by action.]

The order may be made on the motion of any party to the action, or of his representative or successor, suggesting the death or cessation of power, which with the name and capacity of the representative or successor shall be stated in the order. Or any party to the action, or his representative or successor, may file in the action a petition against the other parties, stating facts necessary to authorize a revivor, with a prayer therefor, upon which summons may be issued and served, or a warning order may be made under like restrictions and with the like effect as if issued or made upon original petition. (Section as amended by an Act of February 23, 1882, c. 263, p. 27, and an Act of March 15, 1894, c. 72, p. 172.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

circuit court and have the order of revivor entered and the defect in the record supplied. Williams v. Thompson, 80 Ky. 325, 4 R. 9.

(12) Purchaser at judicial sale-death before sale confirmed-sale can neither be confirmed nor set aside unless the heirs of the purchaser are before the court. Gardner v. Roberts, 4 R. 614.

(13) Revivor in Court of Appeals dispenses with necessity for revivor in lower court upon return of case. Howell v. Smith, 7 R. 305; see sec. 767; and Thompson v. Williams, 86 Ky. 15, 9 R. 267, 4 S. W. 914, which holds that a personal representative by making a motion to dismiss an appeal does not enter his appearance so as to dispense with service of the order of revivor in lower court.

(13a) Death pending appeal-when revivor in circuit court is proper. If an appellee dies after submission a revivor of the action in the circuit court may be made if other steps are to be taken in that court. Wallace v. Wallace's Extrx., 150 Ky. 33, 150 S. W. 13.

(14) Waiver of errors. A party by failing to object in due time waives right to complain of errors. City of Lou. v. Hughes, 97 S. W. 1096, 30 R. 231.

(15) Error cured by pleading after. A party by failing to object in due time waives right to complain of errors. Fishback v. Green, 87 Ky. 107, 9 R. 959, 7 S. W. 881.

Title XI Revivor of Actions

# § 502 [562] [Consent revivor-service of order—when action revived.]

If the order be made by consent of the parties, the action shall forthwith stand revived; and, if not made by consent, the order shall be served, in the same manner as a summons, upon the party adverse to the one making a motion. And, at the first term commencing not less than ten days after such service, the party upon whom it is made may show cause against the revivor; and, if sufficient cause be not then shown, the action shall stand revived.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# § 503 [5es] [Revivor after notice.]

If ten days' notice have been given to the representative or successor of the party who died, or whose powers ceased, of the motion by the adverse party for an order to revive the action in his name; or to the adverse party, if the motion be by such representative or successor; and due return be made of the service of the notice, the court may, if sufficient cause be not shown to the contrary, make an order reviving the action in the name of such party: whereupon, the action shall stand revived. (Who is "successor," sec. 732-20.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# CIRCUIT COURTS HAVING CONTINUOUS SESSIONS.

# § 999. Revivor of actions-cause shown against.

Within thirty days after the service of an order to revive an action, the party upon whom it is made may show cause against the revivor; and if sufficient cause be not shown within that time, the action shall stand revived. (Ky. Stat., sec. 999.)

# § 504 [5o4] [Absent defendant-revivor against.]

If it be shown by the affidavit of the person applying for an order of revivor, that the person against whom it is asked is a non-resident of this State; or has been absent from it four months; or has left it to avoid the service of the order; or so

Although an amended pleading asking revivor is filed in vacation, if no objection is made on this ground when case is heard, this court will treat it as part of the record, and hold the revivor in time. Where petition for revivor is filed in time the fact that the order of revivor is not made until time has expired is not material. Sailsberry v. Sailsberry, 131 S. W. 802, 140 Ky. 731.

Objection for failure to serve order of revivor is waived by appearance. Phillips v. Rudy, 143 S. W. 397, 146 Ky. 780.

# CIRCUIT COURTS HAVING CONTINUOUS SESSIONS

# § 1000. Revivor—warning order to allow sixty days

An order warning parties to appear and show cause why an action should not be revived shall allow sixty days for their appearance; and if within the said period sufficient cause be not shown to the contrary, the action shall stand revived. (Ky. Slat. sec. 1000.)

# § 505 [566] [Real representatives—revivor against]

If a year elapse after the death of a defendant, and no person qualify as his personal representative, the action may be revived against his real representatives or any of them. (Who is "real representative," sec. 732-18.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# § 506 [567] [Real representatives—revivor against]

Upon the death of a defendant in an action for the recovery of real property only, or which concerns only his rights or claims to such property, the action may be revived against his real representatives or any of them, and an order therefor may be forthwith made in the manner directed in the preceding sections of this title.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# § 507 [568]

[Six months must elapse before plaintiff can revive, unless by consent.] An order to revive an action against the personal representative of a defendant, or against him and the real representatives of the defendant, can not be made, unless by consent, within six months after the qualification of the personal representative.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# § 505. Heirs-revivor against

Where a defendant dies pending an action, and no personal representative qualifies, the action may be revived against the heirs, but the remedy against the heirs being equitable, if the action is in ordinary, it should be transferred to equity. Hagan v. Patterson, 10 Bush 441.

# § 506.

(1) Death of defendant does not authorize dismissal of action, it only abates until revived. Burchett v. Clarke, 109 S. W. 888.

(2) Infant defendants can not consent to revivor. Action must be revived against them in manner provided in Code. Burchett v. Clarke, 109 S. W. 888.

# § 507.

(1) Death of defendant after sale under decree but before confirmation of sale action should be revived against heirs of deceased. Montz v. Schwabacher, 26 R. 1214, 83 S. W. 509.

(Limitation. The death of the plaintiff abates the action, and if it is not revived within a year limitation will run from the death of the plaintiff. If a personal representative fail to revive, within a year after plaintiff's death, an action brought for the recovery of per-

Title XI] Revivor of Actions. [5508-510

# § 508 [569] [One year in which to revive against defendant.]

An order to revive an action against the representative or successor of a defendant shall not be made, without his consent, unless within one year after the time when it could have been first made.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# § 509 [570] [Plaintiff may revive at once--limitation one year.]

An order to revive an action in the name of the representative or successor of a plaintiff may be made forthwith, but shall not be made without the consent of the defendant after the expiration of one year from the time the order might have been first made; except that, if the defendant shall also have died, or his powers have ceased in the meantime, the order of revivor on both sides may be made in the period limited in the last section.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# § 510 [571] [Action to be dismissed if time expired.]

If it appear to the court, by affidavit, that either party to an action has been dead, or, if personal property, the purchaser of such property, without notice of such suit, will not be affected by it. Hull v. Deatly, 7 Bush 687.

(3) Order made within less than six months from the qualification of the personal representative is a nullity unless made by consent, or unless the representative enters his appearance to it. Thompson v. Williams, 86 Ky. 15, 9 R. 267, 4 S. W. 914; Forst v. Davis, 101 Ky. 343, 19 R. 558, 41 S. W. 27; City of Lou. v. Woolley, 108 Ky. 691, 22 R. 405, 57 S. W. 499: but objection comes too late if made for first time in Court of Appeals. Maupin v. Berkley, 3 R. 617; Galloway v. Craig, 92 S. W. 320, 29 R. 4, 122 Ky. 447.

(2) After expiration of one year action can not be revived without consent of the defendant except in the cases mentioned in last clause of this section. B. & G. T. Co. v. Howell, 13 R. 563, 17 S. W. 481; Apperson v. Fulkerson, 7 R. 452; Hale v. Com., 24 R. 1573, 71 S. W. 902.

(4) Where an action was instituted to sell the estate of a decedent for the payment of debts, it was not necessary to wait six months to revive against one of the heirs who died after judgment but before sale. Wood v. Wood, 103 Ky. 290, 19 R. 2010, 42 S. W. 1100.

(5) Vendee-against. A suit by the vendor against the vendee to enforce a lien on land is not an action for the recovery of real property, and can not be revived as provided in sec. 506. It must be revived under sec. 507. Buford v. Guthrie, 14 Bush 677; City of Lou. v. Woolley, 108 Ky. 691, 22 R. 405, 57 S. W. 499.

(4) Death of plaintiff-effect of revivor. Hull v. Deatly, 7 Bush 687; Amyx v. Smith, 1 Met. 529; Spalding v. Wathen, 7 Bush 659.

(5) Revivor in Court of Appeals. See sec. 767 and note thereto.

# § 508. (1) Order to revive must be entered within twelve months, but it is not necessary that summons or notice be served within that time or action actually revived, but due diligence must be exercised in having service of order.

A defendant died. Within a year thereafter a supplemental petition was filed seeking a revivor against his executrix;

# § 511-512] Revivor of Actions. [Title XI

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# § 511 [572] [Dismissal for failure to revive-notice.]

At any term of the court succeeding the death of the plaintiff, whilst the action remains on the docket, the defendant, having given to the plaintiff's proper representatives, in whose name the action might be revived, ten days' notice of the application therefor, may have an order to strike the action from the docket, and for costs against the estate of the plaintiff, unless the action be forthwith revived.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e.

# § 512 [578] [Trial not postponed if action revived.]

When, by the provisions of the preceding sections, an action stands revived, the trial thereof shall not be postponed by reason of the revivor. (Form of execution to be levied of assets in hands of administrator. Form 151.

Title XII]  Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments.  [§ 513-514

# TITLE XII.

# PROCEEDINGS TO REVERSE, VACATE OR MODIFY JUDGMENTS.

# § 513. Court rendering, or Court of Appeals may.

A judgment rendered in the circuit court may be reversed, vacated or modified, either by it or by the Court of Appeals. (Judgment defined, sec. 368; new trial, sec. 340.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 40, 76, 81.

See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 78

# § 514. Court of Appeals may, for errors in record.

A judgment may be reversed or modified by the Court of Appeals for errors appearing in the record. (Errors not regarded unless prejudicial, secs. 134, 338, 756; how errors shown in the record, secs. 333 to 340.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 40, 81.

See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 78.

# 513. (1) Judgment-what constitutes.

See notes to sec. 368.

(la) An order made by the circuit court directing that allegations of a petition be taken as confessed is merely interlocutory and may be set aside by the circuit court for cause at a subsequent term. So much of the opinion in 149 Ky. 482 as seems in conflict with this proposition was withdrawn. Duff & Oney v. Rose, 151 Kv. 96, 151 S. W. 22.

(lb) Indivisibility of land. The fact that land is directed in the judgment to be sold as a whole is not conclusive in the circuit court as to the fact that it is indivisible. On exceptions to the report of sale the question may be brought up. Should the sale be set aside for this cause the judgment may be so modified as to direct on the resale a sale in parcels. Guest v. Joster, 159 Ky. 1, 166 S. W. 620.

# § 514. (1) Evidence considered on appeal.

In determining the propriety of a judgment, the Court of Appeals will consider only such evidence as is competent. Skidmore v. Harris, 157 Ky. 756, 164 S. W. 98.

(2) When an objection that a city ordinance was not properly adopted is not shown by the record, but merely appears from appellant's brief and exhibits thereto attached, the objection cannot be passed upon by the Court of Appeals. Creekmore v. Central Const. Co., 15 Ky. 336, 163 S. W. 194.

# § 515-517]  Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments.

# Title XII

# § 515 [570] [Proceedings by appeal.]

The proceedings to obtain such reversal or modification shall be by appeal prosecuted as prescribed by law. (See provisions relating to appeals, sec. 734.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 40, 81.

See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 2, 78.

# § 516 [577] [Misprision not ground for appeal—until.]

A misprision of the clerk shall not be a ground for an appeal until the same shall have been presented and acted upon in the circuit court. (See also sec. 763.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 40, 81.

See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 64.

# § 517 [578] [Misprision defined.]

It shall be deemed a clerical misprision-

1. [To render judgment prematurely.] To render judgment before the action stood for trial pursuant to the provisions of this Code.
2. [Premature judgment against person under disability.] To render judgment against an infant—excepting married women—or persons of unsound mind until a defense or report is filed pursuant to the provisions of section 36, subsection 3. (When actions stand for trial, secs. 363, 364, 367a.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 40, 74b, 81.

See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 64.

# § 515. Void judgment-motion must be made to set aside before appeal lies, sec. 763, and judgment to sell infant's land without bond is void.

Baldridge v. Baldridge, 117 S. W. 253.

# § 516. Misprision not ground of appeal until acted on in lower court.

Morrison v. Beckham, 96 Ky. 72, 16 R. 294, 27 S. W. 868; Armendt v. Perkins, 17 R. 1327, 32 S. W. 270; Baldridge v. Baldridge, 117 S. W. 253; Jones v. Hazard Dean Coal Co., 169 Kv. 588, 184 S. W. 1131.

# § 517. (1) Clerical misprision defined.

"The rule seems to be that whenever the error complained of is ascertained to consist in the mistake of the clerk and not in the judgment of the court, and there exists anything in the record by which it can be amended, the application should be made to amend in the court below, and, until thus made and refused, such an error is not available for reversal in this court." Dodds v. Combs, 3 Met. 29.

# (2) "A clerical misprision can only be shown by the record."

This is proved by the familiar rule that a misprision can only be corrected by the record." Bennett v. Tiernay, 78 Kv. 580, 1 R. 312; Boyd Co. v. Ross, 95 Ky. 167, 15 R. 520, 25 S. W. 8.

# (3) Correction of clerical misprision.

Clerical misprision cannot be corrected after the expiration of the term at which the judgment was rendered, except after notice to adverse party, and where court attempts to do so without notice the corrected judgment is void, and no appeal lies from it until motion is made to set it aside. Seiler v. Northern Bank, 86 Ky. 128, 9 R. 497, 5 S. W. 536; and see Treasy v. Moore, 18 R. 421, 36 S. W. 1132, and notes to sec. 519.

(When the record of the trial court is amended to correct a clerical misprision, the effect is to make the amended record the only record on the appeal. Chambers v. Swango, 22 R. 923, 59 S. W. 20.)

# (5) Instances of misprision.

An error in the judgment as to the date from which interest is to be computed. Clark v. Finnell, 16 B. M. 329; Johnson v. Bank, 2 Duv. 521; Wilson v. Barnes, 13 B. M. 330; Wren v. Cooksey, 145 S. W. 1116, 147 Ky. 825; or to render judgment for protest fees not claimed in the petition, 16 B. M. 329, is a misprision, and

Title XII] Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments. [§ 517

so is a judgment for interest inconsistent with the prayer of the petition. *Arinendt v. Perkins*, 17 R. 1327, 32 S. W. 270.

(5a) Adding interest to the face of a note in a judgment, if improper, was a clerical misprision. *Brady v. Equitable Trust Co.*, 178 Ky. 693, 199 S. W. 1082.

(5b) In a certain case the adjudging to plaintiff a sum exceeding the amount prayed for was a clerical misprision. *Jewell v. Cecil*, 177 Ky. 822, 199 S W. 193.

Where the petition admits the payment of a sum, and the credit is omitted in the judgment, it is a misprison. *Dodds v. Combs, 3 Met. 29; Hieronymous v. Mayhall, 1 Bush 508; Long v. Gains, 4 Bush 353; Bell v. Mansfield, 12 R. 89, 13 S. W. 838. See Tong v. Eifort*, 80 Ky. 152, 3 R. 647.

(6a) After a judgment had been entered the improper entry of a second judgment was a clerical misprision. *Green v. Com.*, 152 Ky. 239, 153 S. W. 242.

(6b) The rendition of judgment on a note for more than is claimed in the pleadings to be due is a clerical misprision. *Bratcher v. Ohio Co. Bank's Assignee*, 152 Ky. 458, 153 S. W. 950.

(6c) Failure in a judgment to give credit for the amount of an undisputed counter-claim was a clerical misprision. *Sowders v. Gingell*, 174 Ky. 127, 191 S. W. 896.

(6d) A demurrer to an answer was overruled; afterward an order was entered submitting the same demurrer; afterward the action was submitted and the court dismissed the petition. Without a motion in the lower court to vacate the judgment, the Court of Appeals cannot review this action. *Com. v. Pruden*, 140 Ky. 671, 149 S. W. 921.

(7) In an action upon two notes failure to embrace one of them in a judgment by default. *Smith v. Mullins, 3 Met. 182; or to give judgment in favor of A and B on a note alleged to have been sold by A to B, and asking judgment in B's favor alone, Oldham v. Brannon, 2 Met. 302; or where, pending an action on a note in favor of A, an amended petition is filed setting up that the note has been assigned to B, and asking judgment in his favor alone, giving judgment in favor of A is a clerical misprision. Cooper v. Poston*, 1 Duv. 92.

(8) It is a misprision to render judgment before action stood for trial. *Webber v. Webber*, 1 Met. 18; or to give judgment upon an award not delivered.

433

# Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments

# § 518 [Modification or vacation of judgment after term by court rendering.]

The court in which a judgment has been rendered shall have power, after the expiration of the term, to vacate or modify it—

1. New trial may be granted. By granting a new trial for the cause and in the manner prescribed in section 344.
2. New trial to person constructively summoned. By a new trial granted in proceedings against defendants constructively summoned, as is prescribed in chapter 1 of title x.

brought, Ruby v. Grace, 2 Duv. 540; Dyas v. Lindsey, 4 Bush 349; or against an administrator without revivor, Amyx v. Smith, 1 Met. 529; is not misprision. The judgment is void. See sec. 763 and notes thereto.

If the defendant object to a judgment on process not executed in time (Raymon v. Reed, 16 B. M. 345: Hedger v. Downs, 2 Met. 160), or moves for a continuance, which is improperly refused (Mattingly v. Bosley, 2 Met. 443; Smith v. Ferguson, 3 Met. 424), it is not misprision to overrule the objection or motion.
Judgment for usurious interest prayed for in petition is not a clerical misprision; it is an appealable error. Bunger v. Hart, 3 R. 518.
Nunc pro tunc orders can not be made unless there is something in record to amend by. Boyd Co. v. Ross, 95 Ky. 167. 15 R. 520. 25 S. W. 8; see Monarch v. Brey, 106 Ky. 688, 21 R. 279, 51 S. W. 191, and notes to sec, 368.
Practice. In cases of clerical misprision it is an inviolable rule that no amendment can be made unless there is something in the record to amend by. Boyd Co. v. Ross, 95 Ky. 167. 15 R. 520, 25 S. W. 8.
Failure to enter judgment in conformity to opinion of Court of Appeals is no misprision but error that can be corrected on appeal. Gayheart v. Childers, 137 Ky. 472, 125 S. W. 1085.
Judgment against infant before defense made by guardian ad litem is a clerical misprision and motion to correct should be made in lower court before appeal. Palmer v. Husbands, 131 Ky. 152, 119 S. W. 762.
A judgment against an infant without appointment and defense by a guardian ad litem is not void, but merely erroneous, and does not subject it to collateral attack. An infant may after judgment object in the circuit court that the judgment was rendered without appointment and defense by a guardian ad litem. The rendition of a judgment against an infant without appointment and defense by the guardian ad litem is a clerical misprision, and is not appealable until presented to and acted upon by the circuit court. Revnolds v. Steel 170 Ky. 153, 185 S. W. 820.
Waiver of clerical misprision. One of several defendants who answered and participated in a trial without objection to the failure of the plaintiff to dismiss the action as to the defendants who were not summoned was not allowed to insist that such rendition without a dismissal as to the others was a clerical misprision. Harbison-Walker Ref. Co. v. McFarland's Admr., 156 Ky. 44, 160 S. W. 798.

# § 518. (1) Casualty-misfortune.

If the plaintiff or his attorney, before judgment, either directly or indirectly puts a party who is not liable for the debt sued on off his guard, or prevents him from defending the action, such conduct will entitle the party to relief. Hayden v. Moore, 4 Bush 107. See further, notes 1-10. sec. 340.

# (2) Mistake of counsel in the preparation of a case

is not ground for new trial. Phillips v. Skinner, 6 Bush 662: Prater v. Campbell, 110 Ky. 23, 22 R. 1510, 60 S. W. 918.

# (3) The discovery of a defect in the title to land

after judgment decreeing a specific performance is not sufficient cause to vacate the judgment, when the deed containing the defect was on record in the proper office, and could have been examined. Denny v. Wickliffe, 1 Met. 216. See further, notes 1-10, sec. 340.

# (4) But if the index to the deed-book was lost

and after diligent search the deed could not be found, the party discovering the missing deed after judgment would be entitled to a rehearing. Elliott v. Harris, 81 Ky. 470. 5 R. 499; and so if misfortune is due to failure to index an

1. For clerical misprisions. For misprisions of the clerk.

4. For death of party before judgment. For the death of one of the parties before the judgment in the action.
5. For casualty or misfortune. For unavoidable casualty or misfortune, preventing the party from appearing or defending.
6. For errors in judgment against infant. For errors in a judgment, shown by an infant within twelve months after arriving at full age, as is prescribed in section 391. (When ordinary judgment may be modified in equity, sec. 17. See further provisions as to new trial, sec. 340.)

(5) Where a judgment is rendered by default against a garnishee, he cannot have it vacated on the ground that the note on which he owed the defendant the money garnisheed had been assigned by the defendant to another person before the judgment was rendered, and he was unable, after diligent inquiry, to learn that fact until after the judgment had been rendered. Coburn v. Currens, 1 Bush 242.

(6) Where his principal counsel was prevented from attending the court by sickness, the plaintiff was not entitled to have the judgment against him vacated on that ground, when he did not attend the trial or make an effort to provide other counsel. Landrum v. Fariner, 7 Bush, 46. See Yowell v. Gaines, 2 Bush 211, and Triplett v. Scott, 5 Bush 81; and notes 1-10, sec. 340.

(7) A new trial will not be granted on account of neglect of parties or their attorneys, but where the illness of counsel prevents his attention to case and there is no neglect on the part of the client a new trial should be granted. Bone v. Blankenbaker, 24 R. 1438, 71 S. W. 638.

(8) A new trial should be granted where the casualty or misfortune preventing a party or his attorney from appearing is such as ordinary care could not guard against. Vittelow v. Ames, 21 R. 225, 51 S. W. 1; Snelling v. Lewis, 25 R. 1856, 78 S. W. 1124; French v. Eversole, 17 R. 617, 32 S. W. 211; Cooley v. Barbourville Land Co., 19 R. 1454, 43 S. W. 464.

(9) A non-resident temporarily in this State was sued by several parties, and destroyed the copies of the summons served on him. Afterward he employed counsel and went to the clerk's office to look at the suits, but the clerk could not find one of them, and judgment in that case went against him by default. The defendant under the facts in the case was entitled to a new trial. McCall v. Hitchcock, 9 Bush 66.

(10) That the defendant was of unsound mind (although not judicially found to be), and in consequence thereof incompetent to make an intelligent defense to the action, is such a misfortune within the meaning of sec. 579-7. Code of 1854 (same as sec. 518-7 of this Code), as will authorize the court to vacate or modify a judgment against him. Bean v. Hoffendorfer, 84 Ky. 685, 8 R. 739, 2 S. W. 556; 3 S. W. 138.

(12) Common law judgment-correction of in equity. See sec. 17 and notes thereto.

Lovell, 107 Ky. 676, 2I R. 1433, 55 S. W. 549: Algee v. Algee, 168 Ky. 362, 182 S. W. 197; Noe v. Dnvis, 171 Ky. 482, 188 S. W. 457; Naylor v. Brown, 160 Ky. 617, 169 S. W. 983.

(l4aCourts of continuous session. A circuit court of continuous session can not set aside a judgment rendered br it after sixtr davs from the date of rendi- tion Tritsch v. City of Cov., 16l Ky. 171. 170 S. W. 518.

(l4b) Fiscal court orders. An order of the fiscal court may be attacked under this sertion. Kenton Co. v. Jameson. 150 Ky. 440, 150 S. W. 528.

(15) Fraud. Money collected under a judgment at law fraudulently obtained inay be recovered back in equity without awarding a new trial of the ordinary ac- tion or setting aside the judgment there- in. Ellis v. Kellv, 8 Bush 62l.

(16) A cause once properly adjudged should never be reopened at the instance of one defendant on the ground of im- puted omissions of his attorney and fraud of his co-defendant. Dillingham v. Mudd, 1 Bush l02; and see Baker v. Grundy. 1 Duv. 281.

(l7) As to setting aside judgment ren- dered by consent of an attorney who had no authority to consent, see Smith v. Dixon, 3 Met. 438.

(18) The fact that there is neither alle- gation, exhibits nor proof to sustain the judgment does not autborize its vacation by the circuit court. Anderson v. Ander- son. 18 B. M. 95.

(19) Infants. An infant defendant is entitled to a new trial under this section when the condition of the infant does not appear in the record, nor the error in the proceedings. Jamison v. Petit, 6 Bush 669; Bunnell v. Bunnell, 23 R. 800. 64 S. W. 420; Cox v. Story, 80 Ky. 64, 3 R. 551; Berryhill v. Holland, 90 S. W. 902, 30 R. 832, 124 Kv. 615; Spradlin v. Stanley, 30 R. 928, 99 S. W. 965, 124 Ky. 70l; but if error appears in the rec- ord the remedy is by appeal. Ogden v. Stevens, 98 Ky. 564, 17 R. 1115. 33 S. W. 932; Oliver v. Park, 101 Ky. 1, 19 R. 179, 39 S. W. 423.

(19a) The failure of a petition for the sale of a ward's real estate for his edu- cation to state that the sale was neces- sary for the maintenance and education of the ward, and the failure of the judg- ment order of sale to so state, renders the judgment viodable only, and is bind- ing until remedied by appeal by the ward within one year under this section after removal of disability, or by proceeding under secs, 391. 518. within such time to vacate or modify the judgment. Harris v. Hopkins, 166 Ky. 147, 179 S. W. 14.

(19b) If' an infant under fourteen years of age be served by delivery to her of the summons and not as provided in

Title XII] Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments. [§ 518

this section, and if the record of the action shows untruly that she was then over fourteen years of age, the judgment can not be collaterally attacked. Relief may be had under Code, sec. 518. Fraize v. Walls, 180 Ky. 168, 202 S. W. 310.

(20) See further, notes to sec. 391; and Richards v. Richards, 10 Bush 617.

(21) Judgment can not be attacked or vacated in a collateral proceeding or except in the manner pointed out in the Code, nor will parties be permitted to defeat a judgment relied on as a counter-claim by a collateral attack on it. Couchman v. Bush, 26 R. 1277, 83 S. W. 1039.

(22) Judicial sales. The power of the court to vacate an order confirming a judicial sale, after the expiration of the term, is not dependent upon the existence of a valid defense to the cause of action or claim sued on. The question as to the validity of the sale is distinct from that in regard to the judgment under which it is made, and there may exist grounds for setting aside a sale which do not affect the judgment. Bean v. Hoffendorfer, 84 Ky. 685, 8 R. 739, 2 S. W. 556; 3 S. W. 138; Kincaid v. Tutt, 88 Ky. 392, 10 R. 1006, 11 S. W. 297; Costigan v. Truesdale, 26 R. 971, 83 S. W. 98.

(22a) Under this section, where the answer of a doweress in an action to partition land to which her interest attaches, which is tendered at the next term of court after the sale and confirmation, and which does not constitute a proper application for new trial under this section, should be rejected. Vanderpool's Heirs v. Vanderpool's Heirs and Creditors, 171 Ky. 381, 188 S. W. 461.

(22b) The fact that land is directed in the judgment to be sold as a whole is not conclusive in the circuit court as to the fact that it is indivisible. On exceptions to the report of sale the question may be brought up. Should the sale be set aside for this cause the judgment may be so modified as to direct on the resale a sale in parcels. Guest v. Joster, 159 Ky. 1,166 S. W. 620.

(23) Limitation. There is no limitation except such as applies to the bringing of actions to a proceeding to vacate a judgment for fraud. Lawless v. Sevier, 5 R. 239; L. & N. R. Co. v. Paynter, 101 S. W. 935, 31 R. 163. 125 Ky. 520.

(24) As a judgment against a married woman may be valid, in a proceeding to enforce such a judgment it should not be held conclusively to be void; but the defendant may show that it is void. Parsons v. Spencer, 83 Ky. 305, 7 R. 329; and see Spencer v. Parsons, 89 Ky. 577, 11 R. 769, 13 S. W. 72; Bagly v. Champ, 83 Ky. 13, 6 R. 250. Personal judgment can be rendered against married woman, Ky. Stat., sec. 2128.

(25) Motion for new trial is not necessary by the party whose judgment has been set aside in an action for a new trial before he can appeal. Reinicke v. Morse, 10 R. 767, 10 S. W. 468.

(26) New trial. See sec8. 340-344 and notes thereto for causes authorizing granting of new trial, and rules of practice relating thereto.

(27) Non-residents-vacation of judgments by; see sec. 414 and notes thereto.

(27a) Partition. This section applies to judgments of partition rendered by a county court. Howard v. Deems, 178 Ky. 760, 199 S. W. 1070.

(27b) Pleading. An allegation that the petitioner did not discover that the judgment had been rendered until after the term at which it was entered and could not sooner have discovered it by reasonable diligence is a mere conclusion of law. A certain petition was held to be bad on demurrer. Beasly v. Furr, 154 Ky. 286, 157 S. W. 10.

(27c) Certain pleadings in a case in which a judgment was attacked under subsecs. 5 and 7 were sufficient; a new and independent action was not necessary, although that would be the better practice. Sou. Nat. Life Ins. Co. v. Ford's Admr., 151 Ky. 476, 152 S. W. 243.

(28) Power of court over its judgments. The court has no power to correct its judgment after the term, except for a clerical misprision. Clark v. Anderson, 13 Bush 111; but it may and should disregard its own order, if it is void. Jones v. Com., 2 Duv. 51.

(29) The fact that there is no allegation in the petition, and no proof to uphold the judgment, is not one of the grounds for which a judgment can be vacated. The power of the court to vacate its judgments and award new trials after the term is confined strictly to the causes mentioned in this section. Anderson v. Anderson, 18 B. M. 95; Hocker v. Gentry, 3 Met. 463; Scott v. Scott, 9 Bush 174.

(30) Right to new trial. After expiration of term, defendants who are summoned and failed to answer are not entitled to a new trial merely because of errors in judgment that would authorize its reversal. Coffey v. Proctor, 14 R. 415. 20 S. W. 286.

(30a) In a suit seeking to vacate a judgment and obtain a new trial, the

# § 518] Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments.

The court may set aside the judgment in the original action, and also determine finally the rights of the parties, or the court may set aside the judgment and leave the rights of the parties to be finally determined on the hearing of the original action. Gaar, Scott & Co. v. VanHook, 162 Ky. 332, 172 S. W. 680.

(30b) A defendant in a judgment who sues for a new trial under this section must show that he has a valid defense to the action as well as that he has ground to vacate the judgment. Burks v. Douglas, 156 Ky. 462, 161 S. W. 225.

(31) The "successful party" does not necessarily mean the one in whose favor the judgment is rendered or who is entitled to enforce it, but applies as well to the defendant in the judgment. Lawless v. Sevier, 5 R. 239.

(32) Unsigned orders-minutes or orders entered on last day of term may be corrected or disapproved by the court at next term before the order book containing them is signed. Fristoe v. Gillen, 26 R. 149, 80 S. W. 823.

(33) Vacation after appeal. A judgment may be vacated for any of the causes mentioned in this section, even after an affirmance by the Court of Appeals, provided the error could not have been noticed by the Court of Appeals. In such cases the petition should not be filed until the mandate of the Appellate Court has been carried into effect. McLean v. Nixon, 18 B. M. 768; Scott v. Scott, 9 Bush 174; Speak v. Mattingly, 4 Bush 310; Fugate v. Gill, 99 S. W. 602, 30 R. 731.

(34) Judgment that has been affirmed by Court of Appeals, or entered in pursuance to its mandate, may be vacated in lower court, but not for errors that could have been corrected on the appeal. Maddox v. Williams, 87 Ky. 147, 9 R. 975, 7 S. W. 907.

(34a) If a circuit court misunderstands the mandate of the Court of Appeals, and in consequence of that misunderstanding enters an erroneous judgment the remedy for the error is by appeal. Jellico Hardware Co. v. Pine Mountain R. Co., 180 Ky. 118, 201 S. W. 450.

(35) Void judgment may be resisted in any court, and not merely in the court which rendered it. The provisions authorizing the modification and vacation of judgments apply where a judgment is erroneous, but need not be resorted to when judgment is void. Stevens v. Deering, 10 R. 393, 9 S. W. 292; Spencer v. Parsons, 89 Ky. 577, 11 R. 769, 13 S. W. 438; Francis v. Lilly, 98 S. W. 996, 30 R. 891, 124 Ky. 230; and see L. Iron Co. v. S. Iron & Steel Works, 91 Ky. 191, 12 R 848, 15 S. W. 183.

(35a) A judgment of a court of equity approving an exchange of lands made by the guardian of infants is void, and not simply erroneous. Ford v. May, 157 Ky. 830, 164 S. W. 67.

(36) Sickness that prevents a party from making defense is ground for granting a new trial. Baker v. Owensboro Savings Bank, 140 Ky. 121, 130 S. W. 969; Ray v. Arnett, 106 S. W. 828, 32 R. 502.

(37) Scope of section. In every action or proceeding by whatever name it may be called, a party who by fraud, casualty or misfortune, is prevented from having a hearing may obtain relief under this section. Com. v. Weissinger, 143 Ky. 368, 136 S. W. 875.

(38) Failure to advertise land sold at judicial sale does not render judgment void; it is only erroneous. Bank v. Gardner, 121 S. W. 608, 134 Ky. 632.

(39) Fraud of successful party that results in obtaining judgment is ground for new trial. May v. Vaughn, 91 S. W. 273, 28 R. 1088; Hall v. Wright, 127 S. W. 516, 137 Ky. 39; 138 Ky. 71; Estep v. Estep, 99 S. W. 280, 30 R. 578, 138 Ky. 71.

(39a) Where a defendant is prevented from making defense to an action by the promise of the attorney for the plaintiff to advise him should an offer of compromise be rejected, and that pending said offer, defendant need make no defense to the action; and the attorney for plaintiff, without notice to defendant, takes a default judgment, a new trial will be granted. Kingsley v. Daniels, 157 Ky. 194, 162 S. W. 813.

(39b) A consent or agreed judgment procured by fraud may be vacated under this section. Com. v. Helm, 163 Ky. 69, 173 S. W. 389.

(39c) Though a judgment contained a recital which is false and obtained by fraud, the judgment will not be vacated therefor if it would have been valid without the recital. Anderson v. City Nat. Bank of Cairo, 153 Ky. 268, 155 S. W. 385.

(39d) A defendant who seeks to set aside a judgment on the ground of fraud must plead and prove sufficient grounds to authorize the vacation and the defense which he would have interposed except for the fraud. Gaar-Scott & Co. v. VanHook, 162 Ky. 332, 172 S. W. 680.

(39e) The purchaser of land at a de-

# Title II1

# Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments. [§ 518

A material sale becomes a party to the proceedings from the time the report of sale is made, and relief may be had against the judgment for fraud on his part in procuring the judgment. *Caulder v. Elmore*, 171 Ky. 575, 188 S. W. 666.

(40) Mandate of Court of Appeals directing judgment cannot be modified by amended pleadings, the only remedy is by application for a new trial under this section. *Western Bank v. Coldeway, 94 S. W. 1, 29 R. 65; Fugate v. Gill*, 99 S. W. 602, 30 R. 731; and see note 33.

(41) Infant-proceeding by to vacate judgment must be prosecuted under sec. 391, or this section. *Leavell v. Carter*, 96 S. W. 597, 29 R. 920, 123 Ky. 459.

(42) Relief against erroneous judgment may be obtained by appeal or under this section. *Forrester v. Howard*, 98 S. W. 984, 30 R. 375.

(43) Non-resident who does not proceed under sec. 414 or this portion to have judgment set aside is without remedy unless judgment is void. *Highland L. & B. Co. v. Andas*, 110 S. W. 325.

(44) Infants-when judgment ordering sale of infant's land is set aside the sale should also be set aside. *District of Clifton v. Pferman*, 110 S. W. 406.

(45) Accident that prevents party from attending court is ground for new trial. *Hargis v. Begley*, 129 Ky. 477, 112 S. W. 602.

(46) Married women are excepted from secs. 391 and 517 and occupy the same position as other litigants. *Eversole v. First Nat. Bank*, 136 Ky. 362, 124 S. W. 360.

(47) Final judgment can only be set aside after term under this section. *Williamson v. American Reserve Bond Co.*, 140 Ky. 215, 130 S. W. 1072.

(47a) A commissioner's report in an action to settle a decedent's estate disallowed a claim. An order confirming this report was a final judgment against the claim. The circuit court after the term had no power to vacate or modify the same in the absence of some of the grounds in Code, sec. 518. *Culver v. Lutz*, 171 Ky. 691, 189 S. W. 240.

(48) Land erroneously included in and sold under judgment may be recovered under this section. *Winkler v. Peters*, 142 Ky. 83, 133 S. W. 1144.

(49) Relief under this section cannot be had when the party could have obtained a new trial under sec. 340, or an appeal had he been diligent. *Com. v. Weissinger*, 143 Ky. 368, 136 S. W. 875.

(50) Casualty or misfortune exists.

Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments

# § 519 [580] [Motion to correct misprision or vacate judgment—time to make.]

The proceedings to correct misprisions of the clerk shall be by motion, upon reasonable notice to the adverse party or his attorney in the action. The motion to vacate a judgment because of its rendition before the action regularly stood for trial cannot be made after the expiration of the first three days of the succeeding term.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 40, 81.

# CIRCUIT COURTS HAVING CONTINUOUS SESSIONS.

# § 998. Vacation or modification of judgment.

Proceedings to vacate or modify a final order for grounds for which, in courts having terms, it might be vacated after the term at which it was rendered, may be had in reference to any order or judgment of the court, after expiration of sixty days from its rendition. A motion to vacate a judgment, because of its rendition before the action could regularly be placed upon the trial docket, shall only be entered within three months after its rendition. (Ky. Stat., sec. 998.)

# § 520 [581] [Petition to vacate or modify judgment—proceedings on.]

The proceedings to vacate or modify the judgment on the grounds mentioned in subsections 4, 5, 6, 7 and 8, of section 518, shall be by petition verified by affidavit, setting forth the judgment, the grounds to vacate or modify it, and the defense to the action if the party applying was defendant. On the petition the proceedings shall be the same as those in the action in which the judgment was rendered.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 40, 80, 81, 83; Form No. 157.

# § 519. (1) Misprision-how corrected.

A misprision cannot be corrected by an action; the proper mode is by motion in the court rendering the judgment (McCown v. Macklin, 7 Bush 308); and notice should be given to all parties affected by the correction. Oldham v. Brannon, 2 Met. 302; Seiler v. Northern Bank, 86 Ky. 128, 9 R. 497, 5 S. W. 536; Tracy v. Moore, 18 R. 421. 36 S. W. 1132; and the application should be made in the court rendering the judgment, and if it refuses to make correction an appeal may be taken. Dodds v. Combs, 3 Met. 29; Com. v. Caudill, 121 Ky. 537, 28 R. 520, 89 S. W. 535.

There is no restriction or limitation as to the time in which a clerical misprision, except a premature judgment, shall be corrected. A misprision made in 1856 was corrected in 1859. Smith v. Mullins, 3 Met. 182; Lawless v. Seiver, 5 R. 239; but a misprision occurring in a judgment in another State, upon which suit is brought in this State cannot be corrected here as a misprision. Reynolds v. Powers, 96 Ky. 481, 17 R. 1059, 29 S. W. 299.

# (3) Premature judgment.

Motion to correct must be made within the first three days of the succeeding term. Wingfield v. Cotton, 9 R. 275, 56 S. W. 813; Delker v. Evans, 23 R. 2451. 67 S. W 837; Potter v. Potter, 101 S. W. 905, 31 R. 137.

# (4) Limitation.

Time in which proceedings may be instituted to vacate judgment. Com. v. Caudill, 121 Ky. 537, 89 S. W. 535, 28 R. 520.

# § 520 (1) Judgment-vacation of-appeal.

A judgment vacating a former judgment is final and may be appealed from; and after getting a new trial the defendant cannot prosecute an appeal from the original judgment against him. MeCall v. Hitcheock, 7 Bush 615; MeCall v. Hitchcock, 9 Bush 66; Pague v. O. & K. R. R., I R. 399. A judgment of the Court of Appeals cannot be vacated by a bill filed in that court. Beazley v. Marshon, 6 Bush 424.

Petition to vacate judgment for

Title XII Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments. [§ 521-523]

# § 521 [582] [Conditions on which judgment vacated.]

A judgment shall not be vacated on motion or petition, until it be adjudged that there is a valid defense to the action in which the judgment is rendered; or, if the plaintiff seek its vacation, that there is a valid cause of action; and, if a judgment be modified, all liens and securities obtained under it shall be preserved to the modified judgment.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 40, 81.

# § 522 [588] [Grounds relied on to vacate to be first tried.]

The court may decide upon the grounds to vacate or modify a judgment before deciding upon the validity of the defense or cause of action.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs, 40, 81.

# § 523 [584] [Injunction pending proceedings.]

The party seeking to vacate a judgment obtained by fraud should state the particulars and specify the manner in which judgment was obtained by fraud. McCarty v. Payne, 5 R. 242.

(3) Practice. In an action to vacate a judgment rendered in an equity action if a legal issue is involved the parties so requesting are entitled to have it tried by a jury. Snall v. Reeves, 104 Ky. 289, 20 R. 504, 46 S. W. 726.

The proper practice is to make the application by an independent action, but it may be done by an amended pleading in the pending action. Henry Voght Co. v. Penn. Iron Works, 23 R. 2163, 66 S. W. 734.

(4a) The petition mentioned in this section must be filed in a separate independent action in the absence of waiver. Jeffers v. Taylor; Jeffers v. Vicars, 178 Ky. 392, 198 S. W. 1160.

(4b) Certain pleadings in a case in which a judgment was attacked under Civil Code, sec. 518, subsecs. 5 and 7 were sufficient; a new and independent action was not necessary, although that would be the better practice. Sou. Nat. Life Ins. Co. v. Ford's Adm'r, 151 Ky. 476, 152 S. W. 243.

(5) A party may prosecute an appeal from the judgment and pending appeal file petition to obtain new trial. Duncan v. Allender, 110 Ky. 828, 23 R. 256, 62 S. W. 851.

(6) The record in the original case should be made a part of the petition for new trial. Rice v. Wyatt, 25 R. 1060, 76 S. W. 1087; Weir v. Weir, 19 R. 2005, 45 S. W. 66; Lou. Tob. W. H. Co. v. Martin v. Conley, 99 S. W. 613, 30 R. 728.
