§ 525. Party holding affirmative must prove it.

§ 526. Burthen of proof—who has.

§ 527. Writings—when read as genuine.

# § 525 [58e] [Party holding affirmative must prove it.]

The party holding the affirmative of an issue must produce the evidence to prove it. (And introduce evidence first, sec. 317.)

# § 526 [587] [Burthen of proof-who has.]

The burthen of proof in the whole action lies on the party who would be defeated if no evidence were given on either side.

# § 525. (1 Affirmative of issue.

In an action on a written contract, if the defendant denies the execution and delivery of the contract the burden of proof is on the plaintiff. Thompson v. Ever. sole, 162 Ky. 836, 173 S. W. 165.

# (2) In an action by the health officer against the county to recover for services rendered in an epidemic, on the issue as to whether certain persons were financially able to pay the bills, the burden of proof was on the county. Hickman Co. v. Scarborough, 150 Ky. 1, 149 S. W. 1116.

# (3) In a certain action for trespass to land the burden of proof was on the plaintiff to show that he was rightfully in possession of the premises entered upon. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Collins, 158 Ky. 462, 165 S. W. 680.

# (4) In an action for the sale of a tract of land containing 15 acres, objection was made by the defendant. The burden of proof was upon the plaintiff to show that the land was divisible without materially impairing its value. Cherry v. Cherry, 162 Ky. 245, 172 S. W. 505.

See notes to sec. 526.

# § 526. (1 Argument - introduction of evidence.

Where the court required a party to introduce his evidence first by

# §526 Evidence-By Whom to Be Produced.

# [Title XIII]

ruling that he had burden of proof, and after evidence was all in reversed its ruling and gave adverse party conclusion of argument, the error was prejudicial.

Party who has burden must introduce his evidence first, and is entitled to conclude argument. O'Connor v. Henderson, 95 Ky. 633, 16 R. 244, 27 S. W. 251, 983; Tarvin v. Timberlake, 18 R. 807, 38 S. W. 491.

(1a) An erroneous ruling of the circuit court awarding the burden of proof to the wrong party was not in this case a prejudicial error. Home Ins. Co. of N. Y. v. Crowder, 164 Ky. 792, 176 S. W. 344.

(1b) The circuit court erroneously held that the burden of proof in a certain case was on the plaintiff. This upon the facts of this case was not a prejudicial error and was not grounds for reversal. Ditto v. Hopkins, 164 Ky. 412, 175 S. W. 658.

Alteration of note. The burden of explaining all alterations, erasures or mutilations of a paper upon which a liability is sought to be established is upon the holder. Elbert v. McClelland, 8 Bush 577; 13 Bush 397.

(3) Assault and battery-plea of son assault de mesne or justification, places burden on defendant. Johnson v. 'Strong, 22 R. 577. 58 S. W. 430; Ashland R. W. Co. v. Hoffman, 26 R. 778; but different rule obtains as to officers, see Connelly v. Am. Bonding Co., 113 Ky. 903, 24 R. 714, 69 S. W. 959.

(4) Burden of proof is on party who would be defeated if no evidence was offered on either side. Royal Ins. Co. v. Schwing, 87 Ky. 410, 10 R. 380; 9 S. W. 242.

(5) Contributory negligence. Contributory negligence is purely a matter of defense, and as to it the burden is on the defendant. Bogenschutz v. Smith, 84 Ky. 330, 8 R. 376, 1 S. W. 578; P. & M. R. R. Co. v. Hoehl, 12 Bush 41.

(6) Claimant's bond-in action on court must determine from pleadings or from statement of case if no pleadings are filed who has burden. Borches v. Bellis, 110 Ky. 620, 23 R. 37, 62 S. W. 486.

(7) Client sued attorney who collected money and retained it for fee-question at issue being how much fee attorney was entitled to burden was on client. Tarvin v. Timberlake, 18 R. 807, 38 S. W. 491.

(8) Counter-claim -where defendant admits plaintiff's claim and sets up a counter-claim, burden is on defendant.

# Title XIII

# Evidence--By Whom to Be Produced. [§ 526]

the debt of the husband property standing in the name of the wife the burden is upon the creditor to show that the property belonged to the husband; in this case the facts shown caused the burden to devolve upon the wife to show that there was a valid consideration for the transferring of the property to her. Stix v. Calender, 155 Ky. 806, 160 S. W. 514.

# (13e)

In a suit by a creditor of a husband to set aside as fraudulent a deed made to his wife the burden of proof was upon the creditor. Blackwell v. O'Neal, 152 Ky. 563, 153 S. W. 721; Mount v. Fourth St. Bank, 156 Ky. 503, 161 S. W. 220.

# (14)

Hirer of property. In action against hirer of property to recover damages for injury to property while in his possession, burden is on defendant to account for injury. Craig v. Lee, 14 B. M. 119.

# (15)

Infancy. The general rule is that when a party relies upon a disability, such as infancy, the burden is upon him to establish it. Murrel v. McAllister, 79 Ky. 311, 2 R. 323.

# (16)

Killing stock by railroad. In an action against a railroad company for killing stock, when the plaintiff has proved the killing or it is admitted, the burden is upon the defendant as to the question of negligence, and the plaintiff, after the defendant has concluded its evidence upon this issue, has the right to introduce his evidence bearing upon it. K. C. R. R. Co. v. Lebus, 14 Bush 518; L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Brown, 13 Bush 475; K. C. R. R. Co. v. Talbot, 78 Ky 621, 1 R. 356: L. & N. R. R. v. Kirby, 19 R. 1383, 43 S. W. 441.

# (17)

Legal presumptions. That acceptor of bill had funds of drawer in his possession. Turner v. Browder, 5 Bush 216; that party accepts deed which it is to his interest to accept. Davenport v. Prewitt, 9 B. M. 96; that officers perform their duties in the manner required by law. Long v. Gains, 4 Bush 353; Weber v. Weber, 1 Met. 18; Lewis v. Quinker. 2 Met. 284.

# (18)

The courts take judicial notice of the names and official signatures of all officers charged with the performance of public duties-as clerks, sheriffs, constables and will presume that one who signs his name and follows it by words or letters indicating a particular official character is such officer. Barret v. Godshow, 12 Bush 592, and see Ky. Stat., sec. 1624.

# (19)

That a resident of this State who has been absent and unheard of for seven years is dead. Ky. Stat., sec. 1639; Bank v. Board of Trustees, 83 Ky. 219, 7 R. 185; that person owning property in this State who has not been heard of for seven years and whose heirs are unknown died without heirs. Ky. Stat., sec. 160g. That children are legitimate. Dannelli v. Dannelli, 4 Bush 51; Goss v. Froman, 89 Ky. 318, 11 R. 631, 12 S. W. 387.

# (20)

Libel—where publication is admitted and claimed to be privileged, burden is on defendant. Beiser v. Scripp-McRae Co., 113 Ky. 383, 24 R. 259, 68 S. W. 457; and see Smith v. Com., 98 Ky. 437, 17 R. 1010, 33 S. W. 410.

# (21)

Malicious prosecution. In an action for malicious prosecution, the issue of probable cause or a general traverse does not place the burden upon the defendant. Lucas v. Hunt, 91 Ky. 279, 12 K. 871, 15 S. W. 781; overruling Brown v. Morris, 3 Bush 81; and see Ullman v. Abrams, 9 Bush 738.

# (22)

Negligence - common carrier. Where a passenger on a train is injured by an accident occurring to the train the legal presumption arises that the accident and consequent injury were caused by the negligence of the carrier. L. & N. R. R. v. RiTtEr, 85 Ky. 368, 9 R. 22, 3 S. W. 591.

# (23)

Negligence. Burden of proof is on party seeking to recover damages for injury caused by. P. & M. R. R. v. Hoehl, 12 Bush 41; and as to contributory negligence burden is on party relying on it as a defense. 12 Bush 41; I. & N. R. R. v. Copas, 95 Ky. 460, 16 R. 14, 26 S. W. 179.

# (23a)

In an action for death alleged to have been caused by negligence of the defendant on whose track the deceased was walking, negligence was denied. The burden of proof was on the plaintiff. Caldwell's Admr. v. C. & O. Ry. Co., 155 Ky. 609, 160 S. W. 158.

# (23b)

In an action for damages for killing a person by negligence the evidence was consistent with either view, that is, the existence or non-existence of negligence. The burden of proof was on the plaintiff. Bell's Adnx. v. C. & O. Ry. Co., 161 Ky. 460, 170 S. W. 1180.

# (23c)

In an action against the city to recover for the death of a horse alleged to have resulted from the negligent failure of the city to maintain its streets in a reasonably safe condition the evidence failed to show what was the proximate cause of the death of the horse. A verdict for the defendant was rightly

# Evidence-By Whom to Be Produced

§526

directed. Russell v. City of Ashland, 159 Ky. 223, 166 S. W. 971.

(23d) Office-suit to vacate. If the action mentioned in Code, sec. 483, be brought by a claimant of the office, he must show that he is entitled to same; if the action be brought by the Attorney-General, the incumbent must show that he is entitled to the office. Hermann v. Lampe, 175 Ky. 109, 194 S. W. 122.

(24) No consideration-special consideration. Plea of no consideration to written contract; burden is on defendant. Andrews v. Hayden, 88 Ky. 455, 10 R. 1049, 11 S. W. 428; Trustees v. Fleming, 10 Bush 234; Brann v. Brann, 19 R. 1814, 44 S. W. 424; Keisewetter v. Kress, 24 R. 405, 68 S. W. 633; but where a special consideration is relied on, or the plaintiff undertakes to show what was the consideration, he assumes the burden. Steadman v. Guthrie, 4 Met. 147; L. & N. R. R. v. Literary Societies, 13 R. 5, 15 S. W. 1065.

(25) Non est factum-plea of, places burden upon plaintiff to show execution of writing. Andrews v. Hayden, 88 Ky. 455, 10 R. 1049, 11 S. W. 428.

(26) Payment. If the answer admits the execution of the note and pleads payment, the burden is on the defendant. Clarkson v. White, 3 B. M. 376; Ermert v. Deitz, 22 R. 540, 58 S. W. 442; Chester v. Day, 127 S. W. 794.

(27) Possession of note. The mere possession of a promissory note not assigned is not prima facie evidence of ownership, and when the ownership is contested, the burden is on the holder to show that his possession is rightful. Gano v. McCarthy, 79 Ky. 409, 3 R. 32.

(28) Possession - ownership. Where the petition alleged that the plaintiff was the owner of a slave, and that she had been unlawfully taken from his possession by defendant, and the answer admitted the taking, denied the ownership and alleged that defendant was the owner, the burden of proof was on defendant. Vance v. Vance, 2 Met. 581.

(29) Practice-waiver—where a party who has the burden permits the other party without objection to introduce his evidence first he will not be allowed to conclude the argument. Frey v. Mathias, 18 R. 913, 38 S. W. 871.

(30) Promissory note-denial of credit on. When a payment indorsed on a note is relied on to defeat a plea of limitation, and the payment is denied, the burden of proving it is on the holder of the note. Frazer v. Frazer, 13 Bush 397.

(31) Question as to who has burden of proof must be fixed at beginning of trial from the pleadings and when so settled does not change during the course of the trial. Ky. Wagon Mfg. Co. v. City, 97 Ky. 548, 17 R. 366, 31 S. W. 130.

(32) Receipt in full of amount due or in final settlement is prima facie evidence of payment, and burden is on party disputing its correctness. Robinson v. Williamson, 7 Bush 604; Addyson Pipe Co. v. Copple, 94 Ky. 292, 15 R. 129, 22 S. W. 323.

(33) Settlements of fiduciaries with the county court are prima facie correct. Ky. Stat., sec. 1067.

(34) Several issues. On the trial of a case presenting several issues, that if tried singly would shift the burden, the court has a large discretion in prescribing order of proof, and as a general rule the plaintiff will be required to conclude his entire case on all issues; but as to any issue upon which burden would be on defendant if it was the only one presented, the plaintiff should be allowed to introduce testimony by way of rebuttal, although in chief. Andrews v. Hayden, 88 Ky. 455, 10 R. 1049, 11 S. W. 428.

(35) Where several defendants file separate answers and the burden as to one would be on plaintiff and as to other on defendant the regulation of the burden rests in the sound discretion of the court. Simons v. Pearson, 22 R. 1707, 61 S. W. 259.

(36) In an action on an account, part of which is denied and payment pleaded as to balance, burden is on defendant. Mattingly v. Shortwell, 27 R. 426, 85 S. W. 215, 120 Ky. 52.

(37) Sound mind. Every person is presumed to be of sound mind. Hawkins v. Grimes, 13 B. M. 257; Flood v. Pragotf, 79 Ky. 607, 3 R. 372.

(38) Whole case must be looked to and party who has burden on the whole case although there may be several issues is entitled to conclude the argument. Mattingly v. Shortwell, 27 R. 426, 85 S. W. 215; 120 Ky. 52; Walling v. Eggers, 25 R. 1563, 78 S W. 428; Andrews v. Hayden, 88 Ky. 455, 10 R. 1049, 11 S. W. 428.

(39) Accounts. In an action on an account where part was admitted and part denied the burden was on the defendant. Mattingly v. Shortwell, 120 Ky. 52, 85 S. W. 215, 27 R. 426.

(40) Condemnation proceedings-burden is on party who excepts to commissioner's report. Chicago R. Co. v. Liebel, 86 S. W. 549, 27 R. 716.

(41) Negligence. When sued for gross negligence the defendant cannot get

Title XIII Evidence By Whom to Be Produced. [S 526

burden of proof by admitting it was caused by its negligence; the live stock was not accompanied by the owner or his agent and was delivered in good condition to the carrier. *L. & N. R. Co. v. MeClintock*, 151 Ky. 455, 152 S. W. 253.

(42) Assault and battery. Answer admitting assault but pleading facts in mitigation does not give burden to defendant. *Doerhoeffer v. Shewmaker, 97 S. W. 7, 29 R. 1193, 123 Ky. 646; Torian v. Terrell, 93 S. W. 10, 29 R. 307, 122 Ky. 745; New Ellerslie Club v. Stewart*, 93 S. W. 598, 29 R. 414, 123 Ky. 8.

(42a) Upon the plea of son assault demesne the burden of proof was held to be upon the defendant. *Shirley v. Renick*, 151 Ky. 25, 151 S. W. 357.

(42b) Attached property-claimant of. The burden of proof is on the claimant of attached property. *Daugherty v. Bell Nat. Bank*, 175 Ky. 513, 194 S. W. 545.

(42c) Attached property-exemptions. The burden of proof that attached property is exempt, is on the defendant who sets up the claim. *Daugherty v. Bell Nat. Bank*, 175 Ky. 513, 194 S. W. 545.

(49) Accident policy. An accident policy provided that in the event of death by injuries intentionally inflicted upon the insured by another person (except assaults committed for the purpose of burglary or robbery), the company should pay but one-tenth of the amount otherwise payable. It was not alleged that the death was occasioned by an assault committed for burglary or robbery.

The burden of proof was upon the insurer to show that the assault was not for the excepted purpose. *Aetna L. Ins. Co. v. Rustin, 151 Ky. 103, 151 S. W. 366; Casualty Co. of Am. v. Rustin, 151 Ky. 103, 151 S. W. 366; Fid. & Cas. Co. of N. Y. v. Rustin, 151 Ky. 103, 151 S. W. 366; Travelers' Ins. Co. of Hartford, Conn., v. Rustin*, 151 Ky. 103, 151 S. W. 366.

(50) Suicide. In an action upon a policy of life insurance, the petition averred that the insured came to his death by his own hands, and further that he was at the time so far bereft of reason as not to know what would be the consequence of his acts. This was denied. The burden of proof was on the plaintiff. *Sov. Camp W. of the W. v. Landrum*, 158 Ky. 841, 166 S. W. 598.

(51) Title-divestiture of. When in an action for the recovery of land the pleadings admit that the title was once in plaintiff, the burden is on defendant to show that plaintiff has been divested of title. *War Fork Land Co. v. Spivey*, 162 Ky. 600, 172 S. W. 1042.

(2) Wills-probate of. When the propounder of a will has shown the statutory execution of the paper he may rest without offering evidence as to the soundness of mind of the testator, unless

447

# § 527] Evidence-By Whom to Be Produced.

# [Title XIII]

# § 527 (5ss) [Writings—when read as genuine.]

A writing purporting to have been made by a party, if referred to in, and filed with, a pleading of his adversary, may be read as genuine against him, unless he deny its genuineness by affidavit before the trial is begun. (To same effect is sec. 473, Ky. Stat.; party may file any writing as exhibit, sec. 128; that "writing" includes, sec. 732; when made part of the record, sec. 128.)

# § 473. Answer denying writing must be verified.

The execution of a writing on which a suit or defense is founded, or its assignment, shall only be denied by answer or other pleading verified by oath. (Ky. Stat., sec. 473.)

Something about the paper offered makes the offering of such evidence necessary. Gernert v. Straeffer's Ex., 162 Ky. 605, 172 S. W. 1044.

(53) In a will contest where the execution of the will is clearly proved there may be a directed verdict sustaining the will. Reynolds v. Sevier, 165 Ky. 158, 176 S. W. 961.

# § 527. (1) Writings filed as evidence.

A party is not confined in his evidence to such writings as he has relied on in his pleadings. The only benefit which a party derives from filing with his pleading writings relied on as evidence is that, unless denied as provided in this section, they may be read as genuine. Haney v. Tempest, 3 Met. 96; Barrett v. Coburn, 3 Met. 511; Wells v. Lewis, 4 Met. 269.

# (2) A paper referred to in a pleading and not with it, or otherwise filed so as to allow the adverse party an opportunity to inspect it before trial, cannot be read without proof of its execution.

Beeler's Extrx. v. Cumb. Tel. & Tel. Co. 150 Ky. 257, 150 S. W. 335.

# (2a) A paper purporting to be a receipt given by a decedent was filed as part of defendant's answer in an action brought by the personal representative of decedent.

It was error to admit evidence controverting its genuineness, as no affidavit had been filed denying the genuineness. Robertson v. Robertson's Admr., 174 Ky. 836, 192 S. W. 878.

# 3

The provisions of this section apply to defendants constructively summoned. Buckner v. Bush, 1 Duv. 394; and see note 1 to sec. 409.

# (4) This section applies only to writings that have been executed by the party against whom it is proposed to read them.

Long v. Kerrigan, 13 R. 433, 16 S. W. 708, 17S. W. 441.

# (5) Denial may be made by verified pleading as well as by affidavit.

Harrison v. Rees, 19 R. 658, 41 S. W. 431; Spencer v. Society of Shakers, 23 R. 854, 64 S. W. 468.

Title XIII] Evidence-Means of Production. [9 528-530

# CHAPTER II.

# MEANS OF PRODUCTION.

# 528. Subpoena defined—subpoena duces tecum.

The process by which the attendance of a witness is required is a subpoena. It is a writ directed to the sheriff, requiring him to summon the person named therein to attend at a particular time and place, to testify as a witness. It may, when the court or the judge thereof so directs, require the witness to bring with him any book, writing or other thing, under his control, which he is bound by law to produce in evidence. (Form of, Form 171; when may issue on holiday, sec. 665.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 443.

# 529. Clerk to issue on request of either party.

If the attendance of the witness be required before the court, or on the trial of an issue ordered by it, the subpoena shall be issued by the clerk on the request of either party. (Party may be summoned as witness, sec. 149.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 443.

Evidence-Means of Production

§ 531-536

§ 531 [591] [Officer authorized to take depositions may issue.] If the attendance of the witness be required for the purpose of giving his deposition, the subpoena shall be issued by an officer authorized to take the deposition.

§ 532 [502] [Subpoena—how executed.] The service of a subpoena is made by showing the original and delivering a copy or a ticket containing the substance thereof to the witness.

§ 533 [598] [Subpoena—who may execute-acknowledgment of.] A subpoena may be served by any officer by whom a summons might be served, and his return thereon shall be proof of the service. It may also be served by any person of full age—whose affidavit indorsed thereon shall be proof of the service; or the witness may acknowledge service in writing on the subpoena. (What officers may serve. sec. 667; may be served on holiday, sec. 665.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 443.

§ 534 [594] [Witness—when not bound to appear.] Subject to the provisions of section 556, a witness shall not be compelled to attend for examination upon the trial of a civil action, if he resides more than twenty miles from the place where the court sits in which the action is pending; nor to attend to give his deposition out of the county in which he resides, or in which the subpoena is served on him. (And see further, sec. 554.)

§ 535 [505] [Witness may be punished for contempt.] Disobedience of a subpoena; intentional evasion of a service of it, by concealment, or otherwise; concealment or removal of a minor to prevent service of a subpoena upon him, or preventing his attendance as a witness, by a person having control of him; or a refusal to be sworn or to answer as a witness, or to subscribe an affidavit or deposition when lawfully ordered, may be punished as a contempt of the court or officer by whom the attendance or testimony of the witness is required. (See further, sec. 538.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 446.

§ 536 [50e] [Witness—when liable for costs and damages.] When a witness is brought before a court for contempt by disobedience of a subpoena, and it is shown that the legal fees for travel and one day's attendance were paid or tendered to him when the subpoena was served, and that he failed to attend without reasonable cause, the court may summarily compel to give his deposition. See Seally v. Williston, 117 S. W. 959.

Title XIII] Evidence--Means of Production.
order him to pay the party, on whose behalf he was summoned, the costs occasioned to him by the disobedience of the subpoena, not exceeding twenty dollars; and he shall also be liable for any damages occasioned by the failure to attend. (Witness fees, see Ky. Stat., sec. 1734.)

# § 537 [587] [When of arrest to compel attendance.]

If a witness fail to attend in obedience to a subpoena, the court or officer before whom his attendance was required may issue a warrant for arresting and bringing him before the court or officer, at a time and place to be fixed in the warrant, to give his testimony, and answer for the contempt. If the warrant be not for immediately bringing the witness before the court or officer, a sum shall be fixed in which the witness may give bond, with surety, for his appearance; and if no sum be fixed by the court or officer, it shall be one hundred dollars. (Form of warrant, Form 173.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 444, 445.

# § 538 (508, 50e] [Punishment imposed for contempt.]

The punishment for the contempt mentioned in section 535 shall be by fine, not exceeding thirty dollars, and imprisonment, not exceeding twenty-four hours; but an officer imposing such punishment shall promptly report his action, and the reasons therefor, to the court in which the action is pending; and the court may vacate or modify the order of the officer, and shall render judgment for the fine imposed by the officer, or so much thereof as is approved by the court. If a witness refuse to testify, or to be sworn, or to give a deposition, he shall be imprisoned so long as he refuses; and if the court finally adjourns before he submits, he shall remain in prison until the next term, or until he testifies before an officer who is authorized to take his testimony. The final disposition of the case in which he so refuses shall discharge him from imprisonment. (See further, as to punishment for contempt, Ky. Stat., sec. 1293.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 446.

# § 539 [600] [Warrant of commitment or arrest.]

Every warrant of commitment to prison issued by a court or officer, pursuant to this chapter must specify particularly the cause of the commitment; and, if it be for refusing to answer a question, such question must be stated in the warrant. And every warrant to arrest or commit a witness must be directed to the sheriff of the county where he may be, and be executed in the same manner as process from the court.

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 446.

While his deposition is being taken, he shall remain under the control of the officer having him in custody, who shall afford reasonable facilities for the taking of the deposition.

A witness shall not be liable to be sued in a county in which he does not reside, by being served with a summons in such county while going, returning or attending, in obedience to a subpoena. (And see further, secs. 81, 666.)

§ 543 [604] [Testimony—modes of taking.] The testimony of witnesses may be taken by affidavit, deposition or oral examination.

§ 544 [605] [Affidavit defined.] An affidavit is a written declaration, under oath, made without notice to the adverse party.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 526a

§ 545 [606] [Deposition defined.] A deposition is a written declaration, under oath, made upon notice to the adverse party, for the purpose of enabling him to attend and cross-examine; or, upon written interrogatories.

§ 546 [607] [Oral examination defined.] An oral examination is an examination in the presence of the tribunal which is to decide the fact or act upon it, the testimony being heard by the tribunal from the lips of the witness.

§ 547 [608] [Purposes for which affidavit may be used.] An affidavit

§ 544. (1) Affidavit must be subscribed by affiant and sworn to. Simms v. Simms, 88 Ky. 642, 11 R. 131, 11 S. W. 665; but it is not essential that the name of the affiant should appear in body of affidavit, it is sufficient if he describes himself as "the affiant." Voorheis v. Eiting, 15 R. 161, 22 S. # § 548 [609] [Examination of person obtaining provisional remedy.]

If a provisional remedy be granted upon an affidavit, and a motion be made to discharge or vacate it, the party making the motion may, by written notice to the adverse party, or by order or rule of court, require the production of the affiant for cross-examination; whereupon the party notified shall produce the affiant within ten days, before an officer authorized to take depositions, at a time and place of which he shall give the adverse party three days' notice. If the affiant be not produced, his affidavit shall be suppressed; and, if produced, he may be examined by either party.

# § 549 [610] [Affidavit—before whom to be made.]

An affidavit may be made

1. [In this State.] In this State, before a judge of a court, a justice of the peace, notary public, examiner, clerk of a court or master commissioner.
2. [Out of State.] Out of this State, before any officer or person who may be authorized, pursuant to section 564, to take depositions.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 210i, 526k.

# § 550 [611] [Affidavit—who may make.]

1. [Agent or attorney may make.] Any affidavit which this Code requires or authorizes a party to make may, unless otherwise expressed, be made by his agent, or attorney, if he be absent from the county.

# 2. [Warning order for, if there be several parties.]

If an action be brought by two or more plaintiffs, an affidavit for a warning order may be made by any one of them who may be in the county: Provided, That the affidavit shall state the affiant's belief that the other plaintiffs who reside in the county are also ignorant of such of the facts as are unknown to the affiant. (Subsection 2 as amended April 14, 1880, c. 986, p. 81.)

# 3. [Several parties—one may make for all.]

Every other affidavit in behalf of two or more parties united in interest may, unless otherwise expressed, be made by one of them, subject, with reference to pleadings, to subsection 4 of section 117.

# 4. [Agent or attorney may make.]

No affidavit in behalf of two or more parties united in interest shall, unless otherwise expressed, be made by an agent or attorney, unless all of them be absent from the county.

# 5. [Affidavit of agent or attorney, what to state.]

The affidavit of an agent or attorney must state the absence from the county of the party or parties for whom it is made, and the fact that the affiant is agent or attorney.

§ 552 [618]. [Depositions—when may be read as evidence—proof of exhibits—oral testimony.] 1. [Using depositions on trial of issues or motion or reference.] Depositions or certified copies thereof, if they be lost, may be used upon the trial of any issue of fact in an equitable action, unless such issue be transferred pursuant to Title II., and upon the trial of any issue in an ordinary action, if such issue be transferred pursuant to said title; and upon any motion, or upon a reference to a commissioner of any question, relating to such issue.

§552. (1) Oral proof on trial of issue in equity. Oral testimony may be introduced on the trial by jury of an issue in an equitable action. McMakin v. Stratton, 82 Ky. 226, 6 R. 154. Savings Bank v. Benton, 2 Met. 240; and see Talbott v. Bedford, 21 R. 897, 53 S. W. 297; Morawick v. Martineck, 128 Ky. 155, 107 S. W. 759, 32 R. 973.

(la) An agreement in an equity case that such testimony as had not been previously taken by depositions might be orally heard by the court, was not reduced to writing nor entered of record; a nunc pro tunc entry of the agreement and orders upon the knowledge of the court was authorized. Conrad's Ex. v. Conrad, 156 Ky. 231, 160 S. W. 937.

Practice. If party taking deposition declines to read it upon the trial, it may be read by the adverse party, but, if it impeaches the character of a witness who is not assailed on the trial, the party whose witness it attacks cannot read it. Sullivan v. Norris, 8 Bush 519; St. Bernard Coal Co. v. Southard, 25 R. 638. 76 S. W. 167.

(3) If a party takes a deposition and declines to read it, the adverse party may read such deposition, although the witness would have been incompetent if offered by him. Weil v. Silverstone, 6 Bush 698; and see Crabb v. Larkin, 9 Bush 154.

(3a) Where a deposition has been taken by one party and filed in the cause, it may be read by his adversary as evidence, although the party taking.

Title XIII Evidence Mode of Taking Testimony.

# 2. [In what cases depositions must be used-exhibits.]

Subject to the provisions of section 708, upon any issue of fact which arises upon the pleadings in an equitable action, unless such issue be transferred pursuant to Title II., or upon the pleadings in an ordinary action, if such issue be transferred pursuant to said title, proof must be made by depositions or exhibits: Provided, That the genuineness of exhibits may be proved orally in court, as heretofore allowed by law, and that the court may require oral testimony upon a trial by jury. (Interrogatories may be read as deposition, secs. 140, 141, 146; when exhibits constitute part of record, sec. 128.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 204b. See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 42.

# § 553. [Retaking without leave, except in rebuttal, forbidden.]

The deposition of a witness shall not be retaken without leave of the court [except in rebuttal]. (Words in brackets added by Act of May 15, 1886, c. 1133, p. 110.)

# § 554 [614] [Witnesses whose deposition may be read in any action.]

A deposition may be read upon the trial of an issue in any action, if, at the time of the trial, the witness reside twenty miles or more from the place where the court sits in which the action is pending; or be absent from this State; or be its Governor, secretary, register, auditor or treasurer; or a judge or clerk of a court; or a postmaster, or a president, cashier, teller or clerk of a bank; or a practicing physician, surgeon or lawyer; or a keeper, officer or guard of the penitentiary; or be dead; or be of unsound mind, having been of sound mind when his deposition was taken; or be prevented from attending the trial by infirmity or imprisonment; or be in the military service of the United States or of this State. (And see further, sec. 534.)

it refuses to introduce it in his own behalf; but a portion of such deposition can not be read unless it all be read, if the reading of the whole be demanded. Jonas v. S. Cov. & Cin. St. Ry. Co., 162 Ky. 171, 172 S. W. 131.

Certified copies of depositions may be read, but depositions taken in one suit can not be read in another suit unless they are taken between the same parties or persons claiming in privity with them. Oliver v. L. & N. R. R., 17 R. 840, 32 S. W. 759; Kerr v. Gilson, 8 Bush 129.

(5) It is discretionary with the court to allow oral evidence on an issue out of chancery, and the court may also limit the number of depositions that may be read, as well as the number of witnesses that may testify orally. Talbott v. Bedford, 21 R. 897, 53 S. W. 297; McMakin 457.

# Evidence-Mode of Taking Testimony

# § 555 [615] [Witnesses-when bound to attend court.]

A witness whose deposition might be used shall not be compelled to attend in court for oral examination, unless he failed, when duly summoned, to give his deposition. (When deposition may be read, secs. 534, 554.)

# § 556 [616] [Witness-court may compel personal attendance.]

Upon the affidavit of a party, and the written statement of his attorney, that the testimony of a witness is important, and that the just and proper effect of his testimony cannot in a reasonable degree be obtained without an oral examination in court, the court may, at its discretion, order the personal attendance of the witness to be coerced, although such witness may otherwise be exempt from personal attendance by the provisions of this Code. (Witnesses exempt from personal attendance, see secs. 534, 554.)

(1b) Exceptions to a deposition on the ground that the witness resides within twenty miles of the courthouse should be overruled if submitted before the time of trial; but if thereafter at the time of trial the witness resides within twenty miles, and no other ground for taking the deposition appears, the deposition should be excluded. (2b) De bene esse. One may take the deposition of a witness, although at the time the deposition is taken the witness is not one of the class of persons named in sec. 554. Willis v. Bank of Hardinsburg & Trust Co., 160 Ky. 808, 170 S. W. 188.

Title LLII]    Evidenco-Mode of Taking Testimony.    [ 557-561

# § 557 [617] [Depositions-when parties may begin taking.]

The plaintiff may commence taking depositions immediately after the service of the summons; and the defendant immediately after filing his answer.

# § 558 [618] [Depositions de bene esse.]

A party may take the deposition of any witness de bene esse, and it may be used under the circumstances prescribed in section 554.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 49.

# SUBDIVISION 2.

# OFFICERS AUTHORIZED TO TAKE DEPOSITIONS.

# § 559 [610] [Examiners to be appointed by circuit judges.]

The circuit judges shall appoint two or more examiners in each county of their respective districts, who, before acting, shall be sworn in open court to discharge faithfully and impartially the duties of their office: and who shall hold their offices at the pleasure of the court. (Fees of examiners, Ky. Stat., sec. 1741.

# § 560 [620] [Examiner's office at county seat.]

An examiner's office shall be kept in or near the county seat: and, if there be more than one, the others at such place as the court may direct.

# § 561 [622] [Oaths may be administered by.]

Examiners may administer oaths and give certificates thereof in all cases in which justices of the peace may do so.

# § 558.

Deposition cannot be read unless it appears from proof offered at the trial that witness is unable to be present at trial, although he may not have been able to attend when the deposition was taken. (1) Defendant constructively summoned-when taking of depositions may begin. See sec. 60 and notes thereof. Failure to verify answer before taking depositions is not ground of exception to depositions. Weisiger v. Mills, 91 S. W. 689, 28 R. 1208.

# Evidence-Mode of Taking Testimony

# § 562 [623 to 626] [Depositions in State—who may take.]

Depositions taken in this State, to be used in its courts, shall be taken before an examiner, a judge or clerk of a court, a justice of the peace, or a notary public; and the laws relating to examiners shall apply to each of these officers.

# § 563 [627] [Officer not required to leave office to take.]

It shall not be the duty of an officer to go out of his office to take depositions, but he may take them at any place in the county for which he is appointed.

# § 564 [628] [Depositions out of state—who may take.]

Depositions may be taken, out of this State, before a commissioner appointed by the Governor thereof; or before any other person empowered by a commission directed to him by consent of the parties or by order of the court; or before a judge of a court, a justice of the peace, mayor of a city, or notary public.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 210i, 526k.

Title XIII Evidence-Mode of Taking Testimony. [§ 566-567]

# § 566 [6s0] [Notice to take-requisites of.]

The notice must be in writing, signed by the party giving it, or his attorney; must be addressed to the party on whom it is to be served, and specify the time and place of taking the deposition, and the action of proceeding in which it is to be used; and must also state the name of the proposed witness, if the deposition be taken out of the county where the court sits, unless it be taken to prove a law, custom or usage. (Form of notice, Form 175; how and by whom served, secs. 625, 624; on whom to be served, secs. 626 to 633.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 447.

# § 567 [6s2] [Reasonable notice-what deemed.]

A notice shall be deemed reasonable that allows one day for each thirty miles which the party will have to travel and one day for preparation if the distance be less than one hundred miles, and two days if it be more. If the distance exceed fifty miles and the usual mode of travel for the whole or a part of the distance be by steamboat, railroad cars or other public conveyance, the time ordinarily required by such mode of travel, with the days given for preparation, shall be deemed sufficient in the notice. If the distance be less than thirty miles, a notice which gives to the party a reasonable opportunity to be present shall be sufficient.

is taking. Collins v. Richart, 14 Bush 621; and so is notice which requires party to leave court he is necessarily at. attending, or that requires him to travel on Sunday. Shropshire v. Dickinson, 2 Mar. 21.

(1) Waiver. Where a witness has been cross-examined, an objection for want of notice will be unavailing. Talbot v. Pierce, 14 B. M. 195; and so if adverse party is present when it is taken. Talbot v. Bradford, 2 Bibb 316.

# § 566.

(1) Notice to take on a certain day, and if not then taken, to be taken on a subsequent day, is good. Moore v. Humphreys, 2 J. J. M. 54.

(2) Notice was given in Lexington, Ky., that depositions would be taken at Natchez on the 15th of January, or if not then on the 16th, or if not then on the 17th, or if not then on the 18th, and on the 18th the deposition was taken and the notice held sufficient. Thonas v. Davis, 7 B. M. 227; and see Moore v. Humphreys, 2 J. J. M. 54; May v. Russell, 1 Mon. 224.

(3) Only such certainty in notice as to place where deposition will be taken is required as will enable party by reasonable inquiry to ascertain place and attend taking. Barbour v. Whitlock, 4 Mon. 180; Taylor v. Bate, 4 Dana 198; Crozier v. Gano, 1 Bibb 257; Overstreet v. Phillips, 1 Litt. 120; May v. Russell, 1 Mon. 224; Taylor v. Shemwell, 4 B. M. 575; Moore v. Shannon, 126 S. W. 136, 137 Ky, 604.

# § 567.

(1) Notice-what is reasonable. Notice to take depositions at the same time that the adverse party is taking depositions in the same case is not sufficient, nor does it remove the objection that the party might have obtained leave to cross-examine, and thus have completed the depositions before the case was submitted. Collins v. Richart, 14 Bush 621.

(2) Party should be allowed a reasonable time to notify or find his attorney. Greer v. Ludlow, 7 R. 290; and see notes to sec. 565.

(3) The word "day" in this section does not mean a working day, nor the time from sunrise to sunset. It means from midnight to midnight. Fireman's Ins. Co. v. McGill, 164 Ky. 621, 176 S. W. 27.

# Evidence-Mode of Taking Testimony

# § 568 [6s8] [Adjournment to another time and place.]

If a notice be given to take a deposition, and the attendance of the witness can not be procured at the place specified in the notice, owing to his infirmity or imprisonment, the taking of the deposition may be adjourned to the next day, and to some other convenient place; and a written notice of the adjournment shall be conspicuously posted, at the place specified in the notice, before noon of the day on which the deposition was to have been taken. (See further, sec. 570.)

# § 569 [684] [Adjournment to another place-cause for.]

If a notice be given to take a deposition, but from any cause not mentioned in the last section the deposition can not be taken at the place specified in the notice, an adjournment to the nearest convenient place shall be made; and a written notice of the adjournment shall be conspicuously posted, at the place specified in the notice, for one hour before the deposition is commenced at the place to which the adjournment is made.

# § 570 [635] [Causes for adjourning to be proven.]

1. A deposition taken pursuant to section 568 shall be accompanied by the affidavit of the party taking it, or of his attorney, stating the cause of the non-attendance of the witness, and by the officer's certificate that notice of the adjournment was posted, as is required by said section.
2. A deposition taken pursuant to section 569 shall be accompanied by the officer's certificate stating the cause of the adjournment and that notice thereof was posted as is required by said section.

# § 571 [686] [Interrogatories—when party may require taken on.]

If more than three days' notice to take a deposition be required by section 567, the party to whom the notice is given may, by notice to the adverse party or his attorney, served on the day when the first notice is given, or on the following day, require the deposition to be taken upon interrogatories.

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 448, 449, 450.

# § 568. Adjournment.

When a deposition is commenced too late in the day to finish it, and the witness can not attend the next day, other depositions may be taken the next day, and the deposition of the first witness finished on the next following day, proper notice of the adjournment being given. Jarboe v. Colvin, 4 Bush 70; and see Price v. Caperton, I Duv. 207, for statement of fact showing that an examiner properly adjourned the taking of a deposition to another time and place on account of inability of witness to attend.

# § 571.

1. Under this section a fraction of a day will not be treated as an entire day. Fireman's Ins. Co. v. McGill, 164 Ky. 621, 176 S. W. 27.
2. On the facts of this case it was held that not more than three days' notice to take a certain deposition was required. Fireman's Ins. Co. v. MeGill, 164 Ky. 621, 176 S. W. 27.
3. The party giving the notice has no right to require the deposition to be taken upon interrogatories; but the circuit court had the inherent power to issue a commission to an officer in Maryland.

Title XIII Evidence of Taking Testimony

# §572 [Interrogatories-taken on by consent.]

Depositions may be taken upon interrogatories with the consent of parties who are free from disability; or the consent of the guardian, curator, committee or husband of a party who is under disability.

# §573. [Interrogatories-court may require taken on.]

The court, on motion of either party, may permit or require depositions to be taken upon interrogatories—

1. [On reference to commissioner.] If the ascertainment of a fact or the stating of an account be referred to a commissioner.
2. [Non-residents or persons under disability.] If any of the parties against whom a deposition is to be read be defendants who may have been constructively summoned and have not appeared, or be under any disability other than coverture, or infancy and coverture combined.
3. [Parties numerous.] If the parties against whom the deposition is to be read be numerous, and have not appointed an agent or attorney residing in this State known to the party taking the deposition.

# CIRCUIT COURTS HAVING CONTINUOUS SESSIONS.

# §1008. Depositions to be taken on interrogatories.

All depositions in equitable actions shall be taken upon interrogatories, unless the court shall otherwise provide by rule, or by an order in the cause. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1008.)

# §1009. Depositions—how certified—rules-notice.

Depositions shall be taken and certified in the manner and by the officers directed in this Code, subject to the following modifications: Depositions in cases in the county where such court sits may be taken before the commissioner of the court, and no commission shall be required therefor. Notice to the adverse party shall not be necessary; but the court shall, by rule, prescribe days for the filing of interrogatories, and the time they shall remain in the clerk's office. If a deposition be taken upon interrogatories, neither party nor his agent or attorney shall be present at the examination of the witness, unless he have notified the adverse party of his intentions to be present; and in such case, the party in whose behalf the deposition is to be taken shall notify the adverse party of the time and place of the taking. When it appears by the certificate of the officer that one only of the parties was present at the examination of the witness, the reason for permitting him to be present shall be stated; and when a notice to attend has been given, the evidence thereof shall be annexed to the certificate. Depositions to be read in the court may be taken by the commissioner. The court may prescribe, by rule, that depositions taken on oral examination shall be taken in narrative form, subject to such regulations as may be prescribed in the rule. (Id., sec. 1009.)

# §574. [Interrogatories-when must be taken on-exception.]

If all of the parties against whom a deposition is to be read have been constructively summoned and have not appeared, or be defendants and under disability.

# §575 [638] [Filing and notice of-caption-duty of clerk.]

1. In the cases mentioned in sections 571, 573 and 574, the interrogatories shall be filed in the clerk's office; the caption thereof shall state the name and place of residence of the witness; notice of the filing thereof shall be given pursuant to sections 624 to 633, both inclusive, and shall be filed in the office of the clerk; and until a commission to take the deposition be issued, the party notified may file cross-interrogatories.
2. [Clerk must cross—when.] If no cross-interrogatories be filed, the clerk shall file the following:
1. What is your age, occupation and place of residence?
2. Are all of your statements in the foregoing answers made from your personal knowledge? If not, which of them are made from information or belief, and what is the source of your information, or the foundation of your belief?
3. Have you any interest in this action, direct or indirect? If any, what is it?
4. Have you stated all you know concerning this action? If not, state what you have omitted.

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 451, 452.

# § 576 [esa] [Commission to take on.]

No commission shall issue until ten days shall have elapsed after the giving of notice pursuant to section 575, unless cross-interrogatories be sooner filed.

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 453.

# §577 [640][Commission and interrogatories-—duty of clerk.]

After the interrogatories have remained the due length of time in the clerk's office, one or more commissions, at the request of the party, shall be issued by the clerk, with copies of the interrogatories and cross-interrogatories annexed, authorizing the person to whom the commission is directed to examine witnesses on them; and the commission may be directed generally.

Title III] Evidence-Mode of Taking Testimony.
# § 578 [641] [Subpoena for witness—officer may issue.]

Upon filing with an officer authorized to take depositions a notice duly served, or a commission with interrogatories to take a deposition, it shall be the duty of the officer to issue his subpoena requiring the witness, whose deposition is desired, to appear, at a time and place to be named in the subpoena, for examination. (Form of subpoena, Form 176; officer may have witness arrested and punished for contempt, secs. 535 to 540.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Form No. 454.

# § 579 [642] [Powers of officer taking.]

The officer before whom the deposition is taken shall decide, summarily, all objections to questions; or, if in doubt, shall permit the questions to be answered, noting the objection in the deposition. He shall have power to prevent insulting questions being put to a witness, and to stop a course of interrogation pursued unreasonably long and for mere vexation and delay.

# § 580 [043] [Statements of witness to be written.]

The statements of the witness shall be written by him in the presence of the officer taking the deposition, or by the officer in the presence of the witness.

# § 581 [644] [Party nor attorney allowed at taking on interrogatories.]

If a deposition be taken upon interrogatories, neither party nor his agent or attorney shall be present at the examination of the witness.

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 449, 450.

# § 582 [645] [Certificate of officer—what it shall state.]

The officer's certificate shall state when and where the deposition was taken; that the witness was duly sworn before giving it, and that it was written and subscribed by him in the officer's presence, or was written by the officer in the presence of the witness and read to and subscribed by the witness in presence of the officer; and shall also state whether or not either of the parties, and, if either, which of them, was present in person or by agent or attorney during examination of the witness. (Form of caption and certificate, Forms 177, 178.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 456, 457, 458.

# § 582. (1)

Certificate of officer may be corrected at any time after deposition is filed and without order of court and during vacation. Sec. 588; Dills v. May, 121 Ky. 322, 89 S. W. 3 R. 765.

(4 After exceptions are filed certificate may be amended to show that no one was present at taking. 97 S. W. 363, 29 R. 1262.

# Evidence Mode of Taking Testimony

# § 583 [646] [Transmission to clerk-duty of clerk.]

Excepting depositions taken by a guardian ad litem and by persons suing in forma pauperis - which shall be delivered or sent without prepayment of fees or postage - the officer, upon payment of his fees and necessary postage, shall deliver the depositions to the clerk of the court in which, or the justice before whom the action is pending; or shall send them by mail or by private conveyance, in a sealed envelope, directed to such clerk or justice, with an indorsement showing the style of the action and that it contains depositions; but, if sent by private conveyance, the person by whom they are sent must make oath before the clerk or justice to whom they are delivered that they have not been opened by him or any person, in their transit. The clerk or justice shall indorse on the depositions the time and mode of their reception, and the affidavit, if any, of the person delivering them.

# § 584 [048] [Fees of officers.]

For issuing a subpoena or warrant of arrest, and for summoning or arresting a witness, the same fees shall be paid as are allowed to clerks and sheriffs for similar services. (Fees of clerks, Ky. Stats., sec. 1720; sheriff, Ky. Stat., sec. 1726.)

§583. (1) Delivery to clerk. A deposition taken for a party and by him delivered to the clerk, although sealed in an envelope, was properly rejected. Breeding v. Stamper, 18 B. M. 175. and so if it is sent to commissioner. Sealy v. Williston, 117 S. W. 959. Depositions may be sent by express. Standard Oil Co. v. Doyle, 26 R. 544, 82 S. W. 271.

(3) Deposition should not be rejected because by oversight of clerk it was not marked filed. Burns v. Ingersoll, 6 R. 737.

§585. (1) Depositions against whom read. Depositions cannot be read against a party who was made defendant by an amended petition filed after the depositions were taken. Depositions taken in one suit may be read in another when it appears that they were taken between the same parties, or persons claiming in privity with them. Kerr v. Gibson, 8 Bush 129.

Title XIII] Evidence-Mode of Taking Testimony.

# § 586 [650] [Exceptions to-how taken.]

Exceptions to depositions shall be in writing, specifying the grounds of objection, filed with the papers of the case, and noted on the record.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 49.

# § 587 [651] [Exception-when to be made.]

1. No exception, other than to the competency of the witness, or to the relevancy or competency of the testimony, shall be regarded, unless it be filed and noted.

# Practice-filing.

Party taking deposition can not make it a part of record unless he files it or lodges it with clerk for filing before case is submitted. Long v. Kerrigan. 13 R. 433. 16 S. W. 708; 17 S. W. 441; but where depositions have been lodged in the case and are considered by the chancellor they will be considered on appeal and should be made part of record, although clerk has failed to note or indorse them as filed. Long v. Kerrigan, 15 R. 65, 21 S. W. 99.

(The fact that depositions are endorsed by the clerk as "received" from the examiner does not constitute a filing as required by this section. White v. Movers. 17 R. 402, 31 S. W. 280.

(5) Depositions filed after jury is sworn should not be read over objection of adverse party. Ky. Union Co. v. Lovely, 110 Ky. 295, 22 R. 1742, 61 S. W. 272.

(5a) This section prohibits the reading of a deposition which has not been filed before commencement of the trial, but does not prevent a party from proving any fact in the deposition not filed in time, by other legal method. Ohio Valley Mills v. Lou. Ry. Co., 168 Ky. 758, 182 S. W. 955.

(5b) If an oral hearing be had of an equitable action in which action it has been agreed that affidavits may be read as depositions, an affidavit not filed before the beginning of the hearing can not be read. Rallihan v. Motschinann, 179 Ky. 180. 200 S. W. 358.

(6) Record. As to how depositions made part of record on appeal, see notes 6-9 to sec. 335.

(7) Depositions taken in another case. When two actions growing out of same transaction were brought against same person depositions taken in one case might be read in other. Andricus v. Pineville Coal Co., 121 Ky. 724, 90 S. W. 233, 28 R. 704.

(8) A deposition taken in a former suit can not be read upon the trial unless filed in the papers of the latter suit before the commencement of the trial. Stevenson v. Ill. Cent. R. R. Co., 157 Ky. 561, 163 S. W. 747.

(9) In a suit by A against B, C, and D, a deposition was taken and the action dismissed without prejudice. The right of A to read such deposition in a later suit against B and E over the same subject-matter is doubtful. Stevenson v. Ill. Cent. R. R. Co., 157 Ky. 561, 163 S. W. 747.

# § 586.

(1) Exceptions to depositions are deemed waived when the record does not show they were acted on in lower court. Patterson v. Hansel, 4 Bush 654; Lewis v. Wright, 3 Bush 311; Hon v. Harned, 18 R. 864, 38 S. W. 688.

(2) Although a party may not object to the reading of a deposition on the first trial, he does not thereby waive his right to object at the next trial. Chapeze v. Bane, 1 Bibb 612; and see Kincaid v. Kincaid, 1 J. J. M. 100.

(3) Exception to whole of deposition when part of it is competent should be overruled. Bronston v. Bronston, 133 S. W. 584, 141 Ky. 639.

(4) An exception to a deposition which failed to specify the ground of objection was properly overruled. Harrison v. Ford, 158 Ky. 467, 165 S. W. 663.

(5) The parties stipulated that a certain person, if his deposition had been taken, would have made certain statements; an oral exception to the competency of the statement was rightly sustained. New Bell Jellico Co. v. Braznell's Admr., 149 Ky. 418, 149 S. W. 888.

(6) An exception of a purchaser at a judicial sale that the depositions on which the judgment for sale was rendered were taken without notice was rightfully overruled. Rothchild v. Wallace, 155 Ky. 676, 160 S. W. 170.

# § 587.

(1) Exceptions. An exception that does not go to the competency...

# § 588 Evidence-Mode of Taking Testimony.

on the record before the commencement of the trial and before or during the first term of the court after the filing of the deposition.

# 2. Exceptions as to competency and relevancy.

Exceptions to the competency of the witness, or to the competency or relevancy of the testimony, may be made before or during the trial.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 49.

# § 588 [652] Court to pass on before trial—correction of certificate.

The court on the motion of either party, shall decide upon exceptions, other of the witness, or to the competency or relevancy of his testimony, can not be considered unless filed and noted on the record before the commencement of the trial. Estham v. Curd, 15 B. M. 102; Moore v. Smith, 88 Ky. 151, 10 R. 729, 10 S. W. 380; Western Union Co.v. Cors0, 89 S. W. 212, 28 R. 290, 121 Ky. 322; Andricus v. Pineville Coal Co., 90 S. W. 233, 28 R. 704, 121 Ky. 724; Hall v. Wilson, 116 S. W. 245; Sealy v. Williston, 117 S. W. 959; and before or during the first term of the court after the depositions are filed. L. & N. R. R. v. Shaw, 21 R. 1041; 53 S. W. 1048. See sec. 590, as to commencement of trial.

(1a) Exceptions to a deposition because the witness resided in the county, if filed after the jury was sworn, came too late. Burchett v. Biggs, 9 Ky. Opinions 22.

(1b) An objection was made during the trial to the reading of a deposition of a witness who was then present in court. The objection should have been sustained. The opinion seems to involve the doctrine that an objection to a deposition because the witness does not fall within any of the classes named in sec. 554 of the Code is not too late if made during the progress of the trial; but the decision does not go so far as that. Dailey v. Lexington & Eastern Ry. Co., 180 Ky. 668, 203 S. W. 569.

(2) When an exception to a deposition is general and goes to the whole deposition, if any part of it is competent the exception should be overruled. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Graves, 78 Ky. 74; nor can an objection to one question be raised by an exception or objection to the whole deposition. L. & N. R. R. v. Montgomery, 17 R. 807, 32 S. W. 738. And when exceptions have been overruled it is not proper to present the same exceptions again. Lewis v. Williams, 4 Bush 678.

(3) It is not necessary for a party to file, before the commencement of the trial, exceptions to depositions taken before he was made a party to the suit.

Evidence--Mode of Taking Testimony. [Title XIII]

# § 590 [654] [Swearing of jury commencement of trial.]

If the trial be by jury, the swearing of the jury shall be deemed the commencement thereof.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 49.

# SUBDIVISION 5.

# DEPOSITIONS TO BE USED IN OTHER STATES.

# § 591 [655] [Provisions concerning.]

A party desiring to take depositions in this State, to be used in judicial proceedings in other States, may produce to a judge of the county court of the county where the witnesses reside a commission authorizing the taking of such depositions or a notice duly served; whereupon it shall be the duty of the judge to issue a subpoena requiring the witnesses to attend at a specified time and place for examination, and, in case of their failure to attend or refusal to be sworn or to testify, they may be punished as is provided in section 538.

[By similar proceedings before an examiner, justice of the peace or a notary public, depositions may be taken by such officers in this State, to be used in judicial proceedings in other States; and the officer so taking such depositions shall have the same powers in taking the same as judges of the county courts now have; but in cases of contempt and the infliction of punishment for the same, or a failure upon the part of the officer to punish for contempt, then it shall be the duty of the officer, upon the motion of the witness or either party to the suit, to promptly, in writing, report his actions and the reasons therefor to the judge of the county court for the county in which the deposition is being taken; and the court may change, modify or vacate the order of the officer, and shall render judgment for the fine imposed by the officer or by himself, and he shall, in accordance with the judgment rendered, certify to the officer what further steps shall be taken.] (Words in brackets added by Act of March 26, 1884, c. 471, p.34.)

# § 591. Subpoena duces tecum.

Neither a notary taking a deposition for use in another State nor the judge of the county court to whom such notary reports his action has power to issue a subpoena duces tecum in the proceeding.

E. H. Taylor, Jr., & Sons v. Sandifer, 178 Ky. 463, 199 S. W. 40.

# § 592 [65e] [Order in which evidence introduced on trial.]

The party who begins the case must, ordinarily, exhaust his evidence before the other begins. But the order of proof shall be regulated by the court, so as to expedite the trial, and enable the tribunal to obtain a clear view of the whole evidence. (See further concerning order of proof, sec. 317.)

# § 593 [657] [Court to control mode of interrogation—may limit witnesses.]

The court shall exercise a reasonable control over the mode of interrogation, so as to make it rapid, distinct, as little annoying to the witness and as effective for the extraction of the truth as may be; but, subject to this control, the parties may put such legal and pertinent questions as they may see fit. The court, however, may stop the production of further evidence on a particular point, if the evidence upon it be already so full as to preclude reasonable doubt.

# § 592.

(1) Number of witnesses—reasonable control over examination and court may limit. Montgomery v. Morton, 143 Ky. 793, 137 S. W. 540.

(2) After the making of a motion for peremptory instruction the court permitted the plaintiff to introduce two other witnesses. This was not abuse of discretion. Tenn. Cen. Ry. Co. v. Walker, 155 Ky. 768, 160 S. W. 494.

(3) The action of the court in this case in limiting to five the number of witnesses to be introduced by the defendant to testify concerning a controlling fact was an abuse of discretion. North Jellico Coal Co. v. Trosper, 165 Ky. 417, 177 S. W. 241. See notes to sec. 593.

# § 593.

(1) Court may exercise reasonable control over examination and this discretion will not be revised by the Court of Appeals unless it is manifestly abused.

(3) Where the court announced that...

# Evidence Mode of Taking Testimony

# § 594 [658] [Direct and cross-examination.]

The examination of a witness by the party producing him is the direct examination; his examination, upon the same matter, by the adverse party is the cross-examination. The direct examination must be completed before the cross-examination begins, unless the court otherwise direct.

# § 595 [659] [Leading questions defined-when permitted.]

A question that suggests to the witness the answer which the examining party desires is a leading question. On the direct examination, leading questions are not allowed, except under special circumstances making it appear that the interests of justice require it. On cross-examination, the adverse party may put leading questions, but, if he examine the witness on new matters, such examination is subject to the same rules as the direct examination.

# § 596 [oso] [Party may contradict, but usually cannot impeach his witness.]

The party producing a witness is not allowed to impeach his credit by evidence of bad character, unless it was indispensable that the party should produce him; but he may contradict him by other evidence and by showing that he has made statements different from his present testimony.

Only three witnesses would be allowed to testify on the subject of damage and refused to hear further testimony of the plaintiffs upon that point, the ruling of the court was sufficiently broad to render a specific avowal as to who the witnesses were, or what plaintiffs could prove by them, unnecessary to the review of the ruling. A general avowal upon the part of the plaintiffs that they had other witnesses by whom they could prove their damage was sufficient. Eaton v. Green River Coal & Coke Co. 157 Ky. 159, 162 S. W. 807.

(4) While Ky. Stat., sec. 904, authorizes the court to confine the number of witnesses to not exceeding two on any one point for the purpose of making allowance to witnesses, it in no way affects the right of parties to introduce a larger number, at their own expense, under sec. 593 of the Code. Eaton v. Green River Coal & Coke Co., 157 Ky. 159, 162 S. W. 807.

(5) For the court peremptorily to stop the evidence upon the chief point in the case after only three witnesses have testified thereto is an abuse of discretion under the facts in this case. Eaton v. Green River Coal & Coke Co., 157 Ky. 159, 162 S. W. 807.

# § 596

(1) On cross-examination witness may be inquired of as to new matter. Burton v. Eddington Co., 118 S. W. 472; Georgetown Water Co. v. Neale, 125 S. W. 293, 137 Ky. 197.

(2) A party testified for himself as to transactions with one who was dead; the fact that the attorney for the opposite party cross-examined on these matters did not render the testimony competent. Saylor v. Saylor, 151 Ky. 694, 152 S. W. 763.

(3) In an action for sale an adverse claimant filed an intervening petition denying the validity of the patent under which the parties to the action claimed. He testified in his own behalf. It was not error to permit him to be called upon on cross-examination to identify the patent. Collins v. Flynn, 155 Ky. 717, 160 S. W. 496.

# § 596

(1) Contradiction of witness. A party may contradict his own witness in two ways: First, by other evidence; and, second, by showing that he has made statements different from his present testimony. Either or both of these ways may be adopted, nor is it necessary, in order to enable a party to contradict his own witness by showing that he has made statements different from his present testimony, that he should be able to prove that his testimony is untrue. Champ v. Com., 2 Met. 17; and in a civil case a witness may be contradicted either by witnesses who heard his testimony on a former trial or by his.

Title III Evidence-Mode of Taking Testimony.

# § 597 [es1] [Witness may be impeached by adverse party--how impeached.]

A witness may be impeached by the party against whom he is produced, by contradictory evidence, by showing that he has made statements inconsistent with his present testimony.

Evidence preserved in a bill of exceptions. Boner v. Com., 19 R. 409, 40 S. W. 700.

1. A party may read a deposition taken by the adverse party, and which he declined to read, but when he does he makes the witness his own, and can not impeach him. Musick v. Ray, 3 Met. 428; as to contradiction by proving statements in a former deposition, see Helm v. Hanly, 1 Litt. 220.
2. When a party introduces a witness to prove certain facts, and the witness states that they did not transpire, he can not then introduce other witnesses to prove that the witness had said to them that the facts he had inquired about did transpire. The rule is that where a witness states a fact prejudicial to the party calling him, the latter may be allowed to show that the fact does not exist by proving that the witness had made statements to others inconsistent with his present testimony. Chainp v. Com., 2 Met. 17; Loving v. Com., 80 Ky. 507, 4 R. 457; Blackburn v. Com., 12 Bush 181; Nicholson v. Rust, 21 R. 645, 52 S. W. 933; Left Fork Coal Co. v. Owen's Admx., 155 Ky. 212, 159 S. W. 703; Liverpool & L. & G. Ins. Co. v. Wright & Allen; Old Colony Ins. Co. v. Same; Cit. F. Ins. Co. v. Same; People's N. F. Ins. Co. v. Same. 158 Ky. 290, 164 S. W. 952; Bergman v. Solomon, 143 Ky. 581, 136 S. W. 1010; Duke v. Davis, 101 S. W. 390, 30 R. 1348, 125 Ky. 313; and see for exception to rule. Garrison v. Com., 93 S. W. 594, 29 R. 411, 122 Ky. 882.
3. A witness may be contradicted by proving that he has at another time and place made a different statement from that contained in his present testimony but when evidence of such contradictory statements is admitted the court should instruct the jury that it is only admissible for the purpose of impeaching the credibility of the witness if it does so impeach him and for no other purpose. Asheraft v. Com., 24 R. 488, 68 S. W. 847; White v. Com., 84 S. W. 340, 27 R. 50; Tapscott v. Com., 140 Ky. 573; 131 S. W. 487; Higgins v. Com., 134 S. W. 1135, 142 Ky. 647.
4. On a trial of an indictment for conspiracy it was proper to allow the Commonwealth to contradict its own witness, by showing that he had made statements contrary to his testimony given in court. Rutland v. Com., 160 Ky. 77, 169 S. W. 584.
5. A witness may be contradicted by the stenographic report of his testimony on a former trial and it is proper to introduce as evidence the specific questions and answers to which his attention has been called. The other party may then have his entire evidence read if desired. O'Donnell v. Lou. Electric Co., 21 R. 1362, 55 S. W. 202.
6. See further, notes to sec. 221, Criminal Code.
7. A witness can not be cross-examined as to any fact which is collateral and irrelevant to the issue merely for the purpose of contradicting him by other evidence if he should deny it, thereby to discredit his testimony. If a witness is cross-examined without objection as to collateral and irrelevant matters he can not be contradicted concerning such matters. Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340; Cornelius v. Com., 15 B. M. 839; Crittenden v. Com., 82 Ky. 164, 6 R. 20; Meaux v. Meaux, 81 Ky. 475, 5 R. 548; Hayden v. Com., 140 Ky. 634, 131 S. W. 521.
8. The court may permit a witness to be recalled and examined with a view of laying a foundation for contradicting his testimony in chief by showing that he has made statements different therefrom. Scott v. Com., 4 Met. 227.
9. See further, sec. 598 and notes thereto.
10. Party may contradict his witness on matter brought out on the cross-examination. Southern R. Co. v. Goddard, 89 S. W. 675, 28 R. 523, 121 Ky. 567.
11. Party introducing witness is bound by his statements unless he can contradict or impeach his evidence as provided in this section. Herancourt v. Taylor, 97 S. W. 359, 29 R. 1299.
12. Deposition. Party who reads deposition may contradict evidence therein contained. Chesapeake Stone Co v. Fossett, 100 S. W. 825, 30 R. 1299.
13. Impeached witness should be given an opportunity to explain contradictory evidence. Southern R. Co. v. Stewart, 141 Ky. 270, 132 S. W. 435.

(7) Time to which evidence of character confined. In impeaching a witness his character at the time, as well as before, he is called to testify may be proven, but the evidence of bad character should be directed to the time of the trial, and to this end, under proper circumstances, evidence of previous bad character is competent. Manion v. Lambert, 10 Bush 295; Mitchell v. Com., 78 Ky. 219; Smith v. Com., 140 Ky. 599, 131 S. W. 499; but it is not proper to allow the evidence to go back several years before the trial. (7a) Evidence of the general reputation of a witness at a former residence a year before the trial is admissible to impeach him. North River Ins. Co. v. Dyche, 163 Ky. 271, 173 S. W. 784.

(7b) Reputation of defendant who testifies. By becoming a witness in her own behalf in a criminal proceeding the defendant opened the door for the attack made upon her general reputation, and in such the general moral character of the witness, as well as her general moral character for truth and veracity, was a fair subject of inquiry: but not general character as to any particular acts of ignominy or turpitude. Eversole v. Com., 157 Ky. 478, 163 S. W. 496.

(0) What is not impeachment. Evidence that witness is related to party or interested in result of case, or has made contradictory statements is not impeachment and does not authorize evidence.

§ 598. (1) Witness to be inquired of, before contradicted. Witness may be contradicted by showing that he has made other statements but before other evidence can be offered of the witness.

# Evidence Mode of Taking Testimony

# § 599 [668] [Evidence of good character—when admissible.]

Evidence of the good character of a witness is inadmissible until his general reputation has been impeached.

# § 600 [664] [Re-examination of witness.]

A witness once examined can not be re-examined as to the same matter without leave of the court. But he may be re-examined as to any new matter upon which he has been examined by the adverse party. After the examination on both sides is concluded, the witness can not be recalled without leave of the court.

# § 601 [665] [Exclusion of witnesses—persons rule does not apply to.]

If either party require it, the judge may exclude from the court-room any witness of the adverse party not at the time under examination, so that he may not hear the testimony of the other witnesses.
[But this rule shall not apply to the parties to the action, or the officers of the court.] (Words in brackets added by Act of May 7, 1886, c. 1003, p. 94.)

Having made at another time a different statement, he must be inquired of concerning the same, with the circumstances of time, place and persons present, as correctly as the examining party can present them, and if it is desired to contradict him by a writing, it must be shown to him and he be allowed to explain it.
Murphy v. May, 9 Bush 33; Campbell v. Bannister, 79 Ky. 205, 2 R. 72; and see L. & N. R. R. v. Lawson, 88 Ky. 496, 11 R. 38, 11 S. W. 511; Clay v. Goldstein, 102 S. W. 319, 31 R. 390; Higgins v. Com., 142 Ky. 647, 134 S. W. 1135; Wilson v. Com., 140 Ky. 36, 130 S. W. 793; Redden v. Com., 140 Ky. 94, 130 S. W. 817; and sec. 596 and notes thereto. Gabbard v. Comn., 159 Ky. 624, 167 S. W. 942.

(2) Where the witness is dead, and his testimony given on a former trial is reproduced, evidence that subsequent to the former trial the witness stated that the testimony given by him on that trial was false is not competent. The rule that a witness must be inquired of concerning conflicting statements, before he can be contradicted, applies when the witness is dead.
Craft v. Com., 81 Ky. 250, 5 R. 53; and see further, notes to sec. 221, Criminal Code.

(3) When it is shown that a witness has been convicted of a felony he may introduce evidence to show his good character.
Shields v. Conway, 117 S. W. 341, 133 Ky. 35.

# § 601. (1) Excluding witness from court-room.

The question of excluding witnesses from the court-room is left to the exercise of a sound judicial discretion, and if the presence of a witness is necessary to enable a party to properly present his case, the judge may permit him to remain.
Johnson v. Clem, 82 Ky. 84, 5 R. 793.

Com., 111 Ky. 793, 23 R. 1094, 64 S. W. 846; Greer v. Com., 27 R. 333, 85 S. W. 166; Matthews v. L. & N. R. Co., 113 S. W. 459, 130 Ky. 551.

Dying declaration. Where a dying declaration of a deceased person was introduced on a trial for murder it was competent for the defense to show his statements inconsistent therewith.

Title XIII Evidence-Mode of Taking Testimony. [§ 602-604]

# § 602 [6o6] [Person present may be compelled to testify.]

A person present before a court or before a judicial officer may be compelled to testify in the same manner as if he were served with a subpoena.

# § 603 [eo7] [Judge or juror may be examined.]

The judge or a juror may be called as a witness; but, in such a case, it is in the discretion of the court to suspend the trial and order it to take place before another judge or jury; and, if a party know, when the jury are sworn, that a juror is to be called by him as a witness, he shall then disclose it, and the juror shall be excluded from the jury.

# § 604 [668] [Proof of writing-comparison of handwriting.]

A writing shown to a witness may be inspected by the adverse party, and, if proved by the witness, it must be read to the jury before his testimony is closed; otherwise it can not be read, unless the witness be recalled.

# § 1649. Comparison of handwritings-evidence of genuineness—practice.

In any action, prosecution or proceeding, civil or criminal, which is now pending, or may be commenced hereafter, upon a dispute as to the genuineness of the handwriting of a person, other handwritings of such person, though not in the case for any other purpose, may be introduced for the purpose of comparison by witnesses with the writing in dispute; and such writings, and the testimony of witnesses respecting them, may be submitted to the court or jury as evidence concerning the genuineness of the writing in dispute: Provided, That—

1. [Genuineness to be approved by judge.] The genuineness of such writings shall be proved, to the satisfaction of the judge, by other than opinion evidence.
2. [Must have been written before controversy arose.] It must be proved to the satisfaction of the judge, that they were written before any controversy arose as to the genuineness of the writing in dispute, and that no fraud was practiced in their selection.
3. [Notice to adverse party of intention to introduce.] A party proposing to introduce such writings must give reasonable notice of his intention to the opposite party, or his attorney, with reasonable opportunity to examine them before the commencement of the trial.
4. [Judge may limit number of writings.] The judge may limit the number of such writings.

(2) The rule does not apply to attorneys who have the right to remain in the court-room. Crenshaw v. Gardner, 25 R. 506, 76 S. W. 26; and see Galloway v. Com., 7 R. 165; Allen v. Com., 10 R. 582, 9 S. W. 703; and see further notes to sec. 221, Criminal Code.

(3) Whether a witness who has remained in the court-room and heard all the testimony, notwithstanding an order excluding the witness, shall be permitted to testify, is a matter in the discretion of the trial court; and unless it appear that this discretion has been abused it will not be interfered with. Carlton v. Com., 13 R. 946, 18 S. W. 535;

(2) If the writing itself shows that it is genuine it may be submitted without.

# §605 [669] [Competency of witnesses generally.]

Subject to the exceptions and modifications contained in section 606, every person is competent to testify, except as otherwise provided in this section.
