(1) Crimes that disqualify. Persons convicted of false swearing or perjury or subornation of perjury, or a school officer who makes a false report, or a notary a false statement as to notice in a protest, shall be disqualified from giving evidence. Ky. Stat., sec. 1180. This section is substantially the same as sec. 8, art. 8, chap. 29, Gen. Stat., and construing that section in Com. v. McGuire, 84 Ky. 57, 7 R. 814, the court said that the persons disqualified were limited to those named in the section, and see Combs v. Com., 15 R. 660, 25 S. W. 590.

(5) Expert evidence. When the question does not lie within the range of common experience or common knowledge but requires special training to understand, the opinion of non-expert witnesses is not competent. American Accident Co. v. Fidler, 18 R. 161. 35 S. W. 905; 36 S. W. 528, 124 Ky. 517, 30 R. 698, 99 S. W. 609.

(6) Grand juror is only competent to testify as to what occurred in jury room in the cases mentioned in sec. 113 of Criminal Code. Pritchett v. Frisby, 112 Ky. 629, 23 R. 2035, 66 S. W. 503.

(7) Handwriting. See notes to sec. 604.

(8) Law books are not competent as evidence. L. & N. R. Co. v. Smith, 107 Ky. 178, 21 R. 857, 53 S. W. 269.

(9) Letters. A party may by rule require the production of letters he has written to his adversary for the purpose of using them as evidence of a contract between them. Marion Nat. Bank v. Abell, 88 Ky. 428, 10 R. 980, 11 S. W. 300.

(10) Letter press copies are only competent when the originals cannot be had, and the foundation for their use must be laid the same as in the introduction of other secondary evidence. Seibert v. Ragsdale, 102 Ky. 206, 19 R. 1869, 46 S. W. 653.

(11) Medical books-when competent to discredit expert. Clark v. Com., 111 Ky. 443, 23 R. 1029, 63 S. W. 740.

(12) Photographic copies of signatures are competent in forgery cases. First Nat. Bank v. Wisdom, 111 Ky. 135, 23 R. 530, 63 S. W. 461; Storey v. First Nat. Bank, 24 R. 1799, 72 S. W. 318; 83 Ky. 259, 7 R. 248; as are photographs of places in controversy. Vanarsdall v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 23 R. 1066, 65 S. W. 858; Bowling Green Electric Co. v. Dean, 134 S. W. 1115, 142 Ky. 678.

(13) Physical examination of plaintiff should be permitted on motion of defendant in personal injury actions when it appears necessary to fully elucidate the truth and can be made without danger or pain and does not offend decency. Lou. R. W. Co. v. Hartlege, 25 R. 152, 74 S. W. 742; L. & N. R. Co. v. Simpson, 111 Ky. 754, 23 R. 1044, 64 S. W. 733.

(14) Physician-evidence of skill of is competent in cases where this issue is involved and when attacked may be supported. Clark v. Com., 111 Ky. 443, 23 R. 1029, 63 S. W. 740.

(15) Pleadings are competent when introduced as evidence. L. & N. R. R. v. Mulfinger, 26 R. 3, 80 S. W. 499; L. & N. R. R. v. Hull, 113 Ky. 561, 24 R. 375, 68 S. W. 433; Edwards v. Mattingly, 107 Ky. 332, 21 R. 1045, 53 S. W. 1032.

(16) Prisoner in penitentiary may testify in a criminal prosecution. Com. v. Minor, 89 Ky. 555, 11 R. 775, 13 S. W. 5; Combs v. Com., 15 R. 660, 25 S. W. 590; and the defendant is entitled to compulsory process for his attendance, the expense to be paid by the State. Hancock v. Parker, 100 Ky. 143, 18 R. 622, 37 S. W. 594.

(17) Res gestae. When evidence admissible as part of. L. & N. R. R. v. Shaw, 21 R. 1041, 53 S. W. 1048; Floyd v. Paducah R. W. Co., 23 R. 1077, 64 S. W. 653.

(18) Testimony of absent or dead witness-stenographers' bill of evidence may be read as evidence on a subsequent trial if it appears by affidavit that the presence of the witness cannot be procured or if the witness is dead. Ky. Stat., sec. 4643; Fuqua v. Com., 26 R. 420, 81 S. W. 479.

Evidence-Competency of Witnesses

# § 606

# [Husband and wife-exceptions.]

1. Neither a husband nor his wife shall testify while the marriage exists or afterwards concerning any communication between them during marriage. Nor shall either of them testify against the other. Nor shall either of them testify for the other, except in an action for lost baggage or its value against a common carrier, an innkeeper or a wrongdoer, and in such action either or both of them may testify; and, except in actions which might have been brought by or against the wife, if she had been unmarried, and in such actions either, but not both, of them may testify. [And except that when a husband or wife is acting as agent for his or her consort, either of them may testify as to any matter connected with such an agency.] [And except in an action for divorce where the grounds relied upon are those provided by section 2117, of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, paragraphs 2 and 3, as follows: "Habitually behaving towards her by the husband, for not less than six months, in such cruel and inhuman manner as to indicate a settled aversion to her, or to destroy permanently her peace or happiness.

923; Sievers-Carson Co. v. Curd, 24 R. 1317, 71 S. W. 506; Byrne v. Morel, 20 R. 1311, 49 S. W. 193; Reynolds v. Powers, 96 Ky. 481, 17 R. 1059, 29 S. W. 299; Bonner v. Com., 19 R. 409, 40 S. W. 700; or his testimony on former trial may be shown by persons who heard it, Bonner v. Com., 19 R. 409, 40 S. W. 700; 10 Bush 190; 6 Bush 563.

(19) It is not necessary that a witness introduced to prove what a deceased witness had stated on a former trial should be able to give the precise words, or all the words, used by such witness. When the witness states that he remembers the substance of all the deceased witness testified to, both on the direct and cross-examination, he is a competent witness, and when the evidence is heard, if it does not clearly appear that the witness does not remember the substance of all that the deceased person testified to, the evidence should be permitted to go to the jury; but if it be manifest to the court that he does not so remember, the evidence should be rejected. If the statement appears on its face to cover the substance of what the deceased witness testified to in reference to the material matters in issue, the evidence should be allowed to go to the jury for their consideration. Rush v. Com., 80 Ky. 244. 3 R. 740; Thompson v. Blackwell, 17 B. M. 609; Cave v. Cave, 13 Bush 452.

(20) Corporation—existence of may be shown by parol evidence. Morse v. Com., 111 S. W. 714, 129 Ky. 294.

(21) Person adjudged insane-competency of as witness. City of Cov. v. O'Meara, 119 S. W. 187, 133 Ky. 762.

(22) Life tables admissible. Louisville B. & I. Co. v. Hart, 92 S. W. 951, 29 R. 314. 122 Ky. 731.

(23) Stenographer's report of former trial may be introduced to contradict a witness or to prove what a deceased witness testified to. Austin v. Com., 98 S. W. 295, 30 R. 297; Lake v. Com., 31 R. 1232. 104 S. W. 1003; Fuqua v. Com., 26 R. 421, 81 S. W. 923; Moore v. Com., 143 Ky. 405, 136 S. W. 608.

# § 606.

(1) Husband and wife -- both may testify under amendment to this subsection when either is acting as agent for the other as to facts that come to the knowledge of one in the absence of the other, but not as to facts that occur when both are present. Logsden v. Stern, 25 R. 1649, 77 S. W. 927.

(la) A husband was a competent witness for his wife on the ground of agency and also because she did not testify. Rose v. Monarch, 150 Ky. 129, 150 S. W. 56.

Construction of section. The word "communication" in this section should not be confined to mere statements between husband and wife, but should be construed to embrace all knowledge upon the part of the one or the other obtained by reason of the marriage relation, and which, but for the confidence growing out of it, would not have been known to the party. Com. v. Sapp, 90 Ky. 580, 12 R. 484, 14 S. W. 834; Leucht v. Leucht, 112 S. W. 845, 129 Ky. 700; Wall v.

# 2. [Infant—lunatic or dead person—when party may testify against.]

Subject to the provisions of subsection 7 of this section, no person shall testify for himself concerning any verbal statement of, or any transaction with, or any act done or omitted to be done by an infant under fourteen years of age, or by one who is of unsound mind or dead when the testimony is offered to be given except for the purpose, and to the extent, of affecting one who is living, and who, when over fourteen years of age and of sound mind, heard such statement, or was present when such transaction took place, or when such act was done or omitted, unless-

Dimmitt, 117 S. W. 299, 132 Ky. 747; Allcock v. Allcock, 174 Ky. 665, 192 S. W. 853; Willey v. Howell, 168 Ky. 466, 182 S. W. 619.

# (Divorce-effect of.)

Information obtained by the husband or wife during the marital relation by reason of its existence should not be disclosed even after the relation has been dissolved. Com. v. Sapp, 90 Ky. 580, 12 R. 484, 14 S. W. 834.

# (4)

A divorced wife is a competent witness in behalf of her husband to prove facts which came to her knowledge while the marriage relation existed between them, but which did not come to her confidentially, nor by means of her situation as his wife. Com. v. Sapp, 90 Ky. 580, 12 R. 484, 14 S. W. 834; Elswick v. Com., 13 Bush 155. See Storms v. Storms, 13 Bush 77; Hostetter v. Green, 159 Ky. 611, 167 S. W. 919.

# (5)

In divorce cases neither is competent to testify against the other. Fightmaster v. Fightmaster, 22 R. 1512, 60 S. W. 918. This has been modified by statute.

# (5a)

The wife is a competent witness against her husband, in an action for divorce, only as to the husband's attempt to injure her, where that is the sole ground of divorce. Hester v. Hester, 166 Ky. 544, 179 S. W. 451.

# (5b)

In an action by a wife for divorce on the ground of cruel treatment, both the husband and the wife are competent witnesses. Hayden v. Hayden, 167 Ky. 569, 180 S. W. 961.

# (6) General rules.

Either a surviving husband or wife can testify for the administrator of the other, when not interested in the result of the suit, as to facts known to the witness from other means of information than such as result from the marriage relation. English v. Cropper, 8 Bush 292; Short v. Tinsley, 1 Met. 397.

# (7)

A widow, having no interest in the result of a suit between her husband's executor and another person, is a competent witness for the executor. Booth v. Vanarsdale, 9 Bush 717.

# (8)

Neither husband nor wife can testify in any case against the other. When the wife is the beneficiary of the action she may testify, but both she and her husband cannot; and in such case she has the right to elect to offer herself as a witness and to exclude by her election her husband. Wise v. Foote, 81 Ky. 10, 4 R. 643; Booth v. Vanarsdale, 9 Bush 717; L. & N. R. Co. v. Hall, 143 Ky. 497, 136 S. W. 905.

# (8a)

A wife's testimony as to a separation agreement was incompetent. Cisco v. Cisco, 178 Ky. 745, 199 S. W. 1057.

(8b) The wife of a decedent is not a

A competent witness to establish an agreement that property deeded to her husband alone was so deeded in violation of an agreement by which the land was to have been conveyed to them jointly. Foushee v. Foushee, 163 Ky. 524, 173 S. W. 1115.

(8c) A husband cannot testify that defendant failed to label the bottle in question, in a suit by the wife for injury resulting from the defendant physician's failure to properly label the bottle by reason of which she took the contents by mistake. Nicholson v. Patrick, 160 Ky. 674, 170 S. W. 20.

(8d) It was held error in an action on an insurance policy in which the insured's wife is named as beneficiary, to allow her to testify as to verbal statements of the husband or as to acts done by the husband. Mutual Protective League v. Cole, 160 Ky. 805, 170 S. W. 184.

(9) In an action by creditors of the husband to set aside a conveyance to the wife as fraudulent the husband is a competent witness for the wife. Howard v. Tenney, 87 Ky. 52, 10 R. 94, 7 S. W. 547; Glover v. Suter, 18 R. 1018, 38 S. W. 869; and in such case, where the creditor takes the evidence of the wife, the husband may testify for himself. Truitt v. Curd, 13 R. 118, 16 S. W. 364.

(10) In an action for damages caused by injury to real property owned jointly by the husband and wife, either, but not both, may testify. City of Cov. v. Geyler, 93 Ky. 275, 14 R. 145, 19 S. W. 741; Weber v. Lape, 145 Ky. 769, 141 S. W. 67.

(11) In a case where the individual property of the wife is involved her husband is not a competent witness in her behalf, and where the wife could not testify because the transaction was with a dead person her husband who heard the transaction was also incompetent. Bright v. Swinebroad, 106 Ky. 737, 21 R. 369, 51 S. W. 578; Bright v. Bright, 99 S. W. 901, 30 R. 835.

(11a) Alienation of affections. Notwithstanding this section a divorced wife is a competent witness as to her relations with defendant in an action for alienation of affections. Merritt v. Cravens, 168 Ky. 155, 181 S. W. 970.

(11b) In an action by a husband for alienation of affections, the plaintiff's former wife was held competent to testify that she wrote a letter to her mother, and the time of writing it, since that related only to a transaction of her own during the marriage. Willey v. Howell, 168 Ky. 466, 132 S. W. 619.

(11c) In an action by a husband for alienation of affections, testimony by plaintiff as to statements made to him by his former wife, while the marriage relation still existed, is prohibited by this action. Willey v. Howell, 168 Ky. 466, 182 S. W. 619.

(11d) Indictment for conspiracy. On a trial of an indictment for conspiracy found against one conspirator without joining his fellow conspirator, the wife of the conspirator not indicted, is a competent witness. Rutland v. Com., 160 Ky. 77, 169 S. W. 584.

(11e) Insurance policy. The widow of a decedent was competent to prove that she took out a policy of insurance on

3. *[Party can not testify after introducing other testimony.]* No person shall testify for himself, in chief, in an ordinary action, after introducing other testimony for himself, in chief; nor in an equitable action, after taking other testimony for himself, in chief.

4. *[Attorneys and priests as to communications and confessions.]* No attorney shall testify concerning a communication made to him, in his professional character, by his client, or his advice thereon, without the client's consent; nor shall a clergyman or priest testify concerning any confession made to him, in his professional character, in the course of discipline enjoined by the church to which he belongs, without the consent of the person confessing.

his life and paid the premiums. Bradley v. Bradley's Admr., 178 Ky. 239, 198 S. W. 905.

(llf) Prosecution under Ky. Stat., sec. 1214. The wife is a competent witness in a prosecution of her husband for an offense under Ky. Stat., sec. l2l4, where he has married her pending the prosecution and abandoned her within three years. Miller v. Com., 154 Ky. 201, 157 S. W. 373.

(llg) Harmless error. Husband and wife, neither of whom was competent to testify, were permitted to testify, but as the testimony was not prejudicial, there was no ground for reversal. Moore's Admr. v. Pierce, 160 Ky. 107, 169 S. W. G20.

(llh) Judgment-validity of. Where the court had jurisdiction of the subject-matter and the parties, some of whom were infants, its judgment, even if rendered upon testimony of a witness incompetent to testify under this subsection, although irregular or erroneous, is conclusive so long as unreversed, and can not be attacked collaterally. Cox v. Interstate Coal Co., 157 Ky. 373, 163 S. W. 231.

(12) Several defendants-wife of one may testify in an action against her husband and others--for the others-and the court should by instruction limit the effect of her testimony. Dovey v. Lam, 25 R. 1157, 77 S. W. 383; Schonbachler v. Mischell, 28 R. 460, 89 S. W. 525, 121 Ky. 498.

(12a) When two persons were jointly indicted for housebreaking, the wife of one of them was a competent witness on behalf of the other. Lawson v. Com., 160 Ky. 180. 169 S. W. 587.

(13) Wife for husband. In action by husband as administrator to recover damages for death of his child the wife is a competent witness for him. Board of Trustees v. Moore, 23 R. 1885, 66 S. W. 417; Mitchell v. Brady, 99 S. W. 266, 30 R. 258, 124 Ky. 411.

(14) In an action on a note executed to husband and wife - wife is competent to show circumstances under which loan was made. Smith v. Doherty, 109 Ky. 616, 22 R. 1238, 60 S. W. 380.

(15) In an action where the husband is suing as the next friend of an infant for its benefit his wife may testify. Collins v. Wilson, 18 R. 1040, 39 S. W. 33.

(16) Will cases-wife may testify as to statements made by the testator, her deceased husband. Murphy v. Murphy, 23 R. 1460, 65 S. W. 165; but wife of contestant is not competent for him. Henning v. Stevenson, 26 R. 159, 80 S. W. 1135; nor against him. Milton v. Hunter, 13 Bush 163.

(17) Will contest - contestant not competent to prove declarations of propounder who is dead. Wall v. Dimmitt, 117 S. W. 299, 132 Ky. 747; Grove v. Grove, 18 S. W. 456, 13 R. 807.

(17a) In a will contest, the widow or other devisee, may testify to acts and declarations of testator. Caddell's Heirs v. Caddell's Extrx., 175 Ky. 505, 194 S. W. 541.

(18) Dying declaration-wife is competent to testify as to dying declaration made by her husband. Arnett v. Com., 24 R. 1440, 71 S. W. 635.

(19) Husband or wife may testify as to facts that did not come to them by virtue of the marriage relation. Metropolitan Ins. Co. v. Thomas, 106 S. W. 1175, 32 R. 770; Aetna Ins. Co. v. Bethel, 140 Ky. 609.

(19a) A widow in an action against a third party after her husband's death may testify to a communication made to her before marriage by the man who be-

5. *[Party testifying against dead person or lunatic.]* If the right of a person to testify for himself be founded upon the fact that one who is dead or of unsound mind has testified against him, the testimony of such person shall be confined to the facts or transactions to which the adverse testimony related.

(19b) A widow was held competent to testify in a case between her husband's estate and others; the facts to which she testified did not come to her knowledge by reason of confidential relations with decedent. Cundif v. Cundiff's Admr., 9 Ky. Opinions 97.

(20) Private conversation between husband and wife may be related by witness who heard it. Com. v. Everson, 96 S. W. 460, 29 R. 760, 123 Ky. 330.

(21) Husband and wife when sued may both testify as to facts within their knowledge but they cannot testify as to the same facts. Logsden v. Stern, 25 R. I649, 77 S. W. 927, 117 Ky. 217. Monahan v. Schwartz, 32 R. 1285, 108 S. W. 285; North River Ins. Co. v. Dyche, 163 Ky. 271, 173 S. W. 784.

(2la) Where a nephew and heir at law of a decedent testifies in a suit by administrator against the widow charged with concealing the deceased's property, the nephew being a beneficiary, it would be improper after his testimony to allow his wife to testify to the same facts. Mackey v. Mackey's Admr., 170 Ky. 109, 185 S. W. 494.

(22) Wife may testify as to threats communicated to her by her husband to show her state of mind when she signed note. Ditto v. Slaughter, 92 S. W. 2, 28 R. 1164.

(23) When wife is sued she may testify or her husband may testify for her. E. H. Taylor & Sons v. Johnson, 99 S. W. 320, 30 R. 656.

(23a) In a proceeding to attach the proceeds of two notes payable to a wife upon an allegation that they were transferred to her to prevent them from being subjected to the husband's debt, she was a competent witness for herself. The husband made no claim to the notes. Stix v. Calender, 155 Ky. 806, 160 S. W. 514.

(23b) Joint grantees. In a suit against joint grantees in a deed to cancel a deed, the husband of one of the grantees who is incompetent under this subsection to testify for herself, is incompetent to testify for the other grantee. Dunbar v. Meadows, 165 Ky. 275, 176 S. W. 1167.

(24) Where depositions of both husband and wife are taken the court should require them to elect which they will read. Walker v. Walker, 114 S. W. 338.

(24a) A plaintiff took the deposition of two defendants who were husband and wife. His exceptions to these depositions were overruled. Darby v. Van Meter, 155 Ky. 462, 159 S. W. 940.

Bastin v. Given's Admx., 170 Ky. 201, 185 S. W. 635.

# Evidence-Competency of Witnesses.

6. Entries in books—when party may testify concerning—production of. A person may testify for himself as to the correctness of original entries made by him against persons who are under no disability-other than infancy—in an accounting, according to the usual course of business though the person against whom they were made may have died or have become of unsound mind; but no person shall testify for himself concerning entries in a book, or the contents or purport of any writing, under the control of himself, or of himself and others jointly, if he refuse or fail to produce such book or writing, and to make it subject to the order of the court for the purposes of the action, if required to do so by the party against whom he offers to testify. (5) Depositions. A party can not have read for himself his own deposition in an action against a decedent's estate, although it was taken during the lifetime of the intestate. Hardin v. Taylor, 78 Ky. 593, 1 R. 322; Ferguson v. Staton, 19 R. 979, 42 S. W. 732; Newman v. Blades, 21 R. 1353, 54 S. W. 849; but when a party dies after giving his deposition the adverse party may give his deposition. Powell v. Mead 3 R. 334.

(5a) The Court of Appeals can not consider the question of the competency of a deposition unless the question be raised in the lower court. Williamson v. Justice, 174 Ky. 327, 192 S. W. 13.

(6) Devisees. In a contest about the probate of a will a devisee is a competent witness as to transactions with the decedent. Flood v. Pragoff, 79 Ky. 607, 3 R. 372. See Cave v. Cave, 13 Bush 452; Milton v. Hunter, 13 Bush 163; Phillips v. Phillips, 81 Ky. 328, 5 R. 270; Williams v. Williams, 90 Ky. 28, 11 R. 828, 13 S. W. 250; Murphy v. Murphy, 23 R. 1460, 65 S. W. 165; Swinebroad v. Bright, 116 Ky. 514, 25 R. 742, 76 S. W. 485.

(4ai) Deed-reformation of. In an action by a grantor in a deed against the grantee and her children by which deed the grantee received a life estate and said children the remainder, in which action it was sought to reform the deed to give the grantee the fee simple estate. The grantor testified that such was his intention originally. Held, the interest of the grantor was adverse to that of the children, the object of the action being to deprive the latter of their remainder interest therein; and that fact, in view of their infancy, disqualified him.

(7) Distributees. The distributees of an estate is not a competent witness for the administrator of his intestate in an action in the name of the administrator against another administrator. Manion v. Lambert, 10 Bush 295.

(8) Disqualifying interest must be direct and certain, an uncertain or remote interest will not disqualify. Doty v.

# §606 Evidence-Competency of Witnesses.

# 7. [Assignor of claims when incompetent.]

The assignment of a claim by a person who is incompetent to testify for himself shall not make him competent to testify for another.

# 8. [Adverse party may be compelled to testify.]

A party may be examined as if under cross-examination at the instance of the adverse party, either orally or by deposition as any other witness; but the party calling for such examination shall not be concluded thereby, but may rebut it by counter testimony.

Doty, 26 R. 63, 80 S. W. 803; 118 Ky. 204; New York Life Ins. Co. v. Johnson, 24 R. 1867, 72 S. W. 762. An attorney who has a contingent fee incompetent, Smick v. Beswick, 113 Ky. 439, 24 R. 276, 68 S. W. 439.

# (9)

Executor who has no other interest in the estate may testify as to statements made by the decedent in an action against the estate, Swinebroad v. Bright, 116 Ky. 514, 25 R. 742, 76 S. W. 365; Bannon v. Bannon Sewer Pipe Co., 124 S. W. 843, 119 S. W. 1170, 136 Ky. 556.

# (10) General rules.

A party is a competent witness unless all the adverse parties are persons against whom he could not testify if they were sued singly. Worthly v. Hammond, 13 Bush 519; Allen v. Russell, 78 Ky. 105.

# (11)

Mother of a bastard is a competent witness to prove a contract made by the father with her for the support of the child, when she is not a party to the suit nor interested in the result, the action against his estate being prosecuted for the benefit of the bastard. Stowers v. Hollis, 83 Ky. 544, 7 R. 549; and see Doty v. Doty, 26 R. 63, 80 S. W. 803, 118 Ky. 204.

# (12)

The only inhibition is as to verbal statements of or transactions with or acts done or omitted by the decedent, and a person may testify in his own behalf as to facts that do not appear to have been within the knowledge of the decedent. Hunter v. March, 2 R. 240.

# (13)

A party can not state the consideration paid to a decedent for the execution of a note by him. Mullikin v. Mullikin, 15 R. 609, 23 S. W. 352; 25 S. W. 598; Andrews v. Hayden, 88 Ky. 455, 10 R. 1049, 11 S. W. 428; nor can a party in a suit to recover for services rendered a decedent testify as to the value or the kind of services rendered. Newton v. Field, 98 Ky. 186, 17 R. 769, 32 S. W. 623.

# (13a)

Testimony of the grantee in a deed, which was in possession of the grantor at the grantor's death, to the effect that the deed had been delivered to him and afterward returned, was incompetent. Wilson v. Sutton, 153 Ky. 96, 154 S. W. 394.

# (14)

The interest of a witness may be equipoise or he may be testifying against his interest, but this does not qualify him to testify as to a transaction with a decedent. Alexander v. Alford, 89 Ky. 105, 12 R. 363, 20 S. W. 164.

# (15)

A party may testify concerning transactions with a decedent when a representative of or someone interested in his estate has testified with reference thereto. McHarry v. Irvin, 85 Ky. 322, 9 R. 245, 3 S. W. 374; 4 S. W. 800; Kuhn v. Kuhn, 24 R. 787, 60 S. W. 1077; Carpenter v. Rice, 25 R. 1704, 78 S. W. 458; Schmitt Holtman Co. v. Sweeney, 125 S. W. 180, 136 Ky. 773.

Title XIII] Evidence-Competency of Witnesses. [§ 606
9. [Affidavits—attesting witnesses—provisions of this section do not apply to.] None of the preceding provisions of this section apply to affidavits for provisional remedies, or to affidavits of claimants against the estates of deceased or insolvent persons, or affect the competency of attesting witnesses of instruments which are required by law to be attested. (Evidence as to disputed handwritings, see act, sec. 604.)

The fact that the attorney for the opposite party cross-examined on these matters did not render the testimony competent. Saylor v. Saylor, 151 Ky. 694, 152 S. W. 763.

(16) In an action to recover the amount of a note owned by two persons, each owning one-half—the court, in Beach v. Cummins, 13 R. 881, 18 S. W. 360, held that each might testify for the other; and to same effect is Storey v. Storey, 22 R. 1731, 61 S. W. 279; Schonbachler v. Mischell, 28 R. 460, 89 S. W. 525, 121 Ky. 498.

(17) The beneficiary of a fund is not a competent witness for the trustee as to transactions between the decedent and a former trustee, and where it is claimed that his interest has been removed by the payment of his share in the estate before the transaction he testifies about took place, the burden is upon plaintiff to show this fact. Hopkins v. Faeber, 86 Ky. 223, 9 R. 550, 5 S. W. 749; and see Whitlow v. Whitlow, 109 Ky. 573, 22 R. 1179, 60 S. W. 182.

(18) A party will not be allowed to testify indirectly as to a conversation with a decedent, as by detailing a conversation with the administrator in the course of which what his intestate said is related. Jones v. Jones, Jr., 102 Ky. 450, 19 R. 1516, 43 S. W. 412.

(19) Where the attesting witness to a note, as well as the maker of the note, who signed it by his mark, are dead, the payee who wrote note and signature of maker will not be allowed to testify as to the transaction. Cunningham v. Speagle, 106 Ky. 278, 20 R. 1833, 50 S. W. 244.

(20) It is competent to prove the declarations of a dead person as to a matter of pedigree. Whallen v. Nesbit, 95 Ky. 464, 16 R. 52, 26 S. W. 188.

(21) Where a settlement was made with the mother in the presence of her son and afterward she died and devised a note obtained in the settlement to her son and other persons, the makers of the note were not competent as the son declined to testify. Burton v. Hill, 23 R. 1080, 64 S. W. 736.

(22) W being dead, C could not testify as to a transaction with him so as to affect G, who was not present when it took place and who did not testify. Turner v. Mitchell, 22 R. 1784, 61 S. W. 468.

(22a) In an action for the recovery of money alleged to have been loaned to a decedent, it was not competent for plaintiff to testify that she had a given amount of money in gold at the time of the loan nor to testify what decedent wanted with the money. Fish v. Welch's Admr., 157 Ky. 19, 162 S. W. 533.

(23) Guardian may testify for his ward as to transactions with a decedent. Doty v. Doty, 26 R. 63, 80 S. W. 803.

(23a) Harmless error. In an action against an administrator for services rendered the deceased, the admission of testimony incompetent under this section, was harmless, which served only to confirm defendant's theory of the contract or going to a matter not in issue or to one not seriously disputed. Moore's Admr. v. Pierce, 160 Ky. 107, 169 S. W. 620.

(23b) Insane persons. In an action against a person who had been adjudged of unsound mind, that person testified and plaintiffs testified to transactions with him. It seems that he and they were competent. (Subsee. 2b.) Lamas tus v. Morgan's Committee, 178 Ky. 805, 200 S. W. 32.

(23c) Interest not severable. Under this section one of two joint grantees of deceased grantor cannot testify for the other as to delivery of the deed. Dunbar v. Meadows, 165 Ky. 275, 176 S. W. 1167.

(23d) The interests of certain parties to an action were joint and were so connected that all must succeed or fail together. No one of them was a competent witness for the other as to a verbal promise of a decedent. Giernhert v. Straeffer's Exr., 172 Ky. 823, 189 S. W. 1141.

(23e) Where the interests of two defendants are not severable, neither of them can testify for the other with reference to statements of or transactions.

Evidence-Competency of Witnesses
# §606

with a party who is dead at the time the testimony is offered; nor can the husband of one party in such a case testify for the other. *Dunbar v. Meadows*, 165 Ky. 275, 176 S. W. 1167.

(24) Partnership matters. In an action by the surviving partner, the defendant cannot testify as to what took place between himself and the deceased partner in the absence of the surviving partner. *Lawhorn v. Carter, 11 Bush 7; and see Alexander v. Alford*, 89 Ky. 105, 12 R. 363, 20 S. W. 164.

(25) In an action by an administrator against a firm, one of the partners cannot, by letting judgment go against him, render himself a competent witness for the other partners as to transactions with the decedent. *Worthington v. Miller*, 85 Ky. 320, 9 R. 10, 3 S. W. 532.

(25a) A surviving partner was incompetent in a settlement suit to testify as to transactions evidenced by checks issued by the deceased partner. *Markham's Admr. v. Markham*, 154 Ky. 401, 157 S. W. 1101.

(26) Personal representative. A personal representative cannot, when sued in his representative capacity, testify as to matters occurring between himself and a decedent whose representative is prosecuting the action. *Hobbs v. Russell, 79 Ky. 61, 1 R. 329; and see Alexander v. Alford*, 89 Ky. 105, 12 R. 363, 20 S. W. 164.

(27) An administrator is not a competent witness against the infant children of his intestate in an action by them by their guardian against him to recover rents which he had collected for lands leased by him which had descended to them. *Wilson v. Unselt*, 12 Bush 215.

(28) Persons interested—not only parties to the action but all persons who are directly interested in the recovery are disqualified to testify against a decedent's estate. *Smick v. Beswick, 113 Ky. 439, 24 R. 276, 68 S. W. 439; Apperson v. Exchange Bank*, 10 R. 943, 10 S. W. 801.

(28a) An action was brought against three joint makers of a note. One of them made defense. On the trial, one of the defendants who had not been served with summons was incompetent to testify to verbal statements of a person who was dead at the time the testimony was offered. *Title, Guar. & Surety Co. v. Hay*, 165 Ky. 76, 176 S. W. 957.

(29) Physician—declarations made to by a person who died from injuries as to how the injuries were received are not competent unless necessary to enable him to treat the case properly. *Shade v. *

488

# Title XIII

# Evidence-Competency of Witnesses

# § 606

deed, was incompetent, as the defendant's ancestor was the deceased. However, plaintiff's further statement that his father thereafter placed him in possession of the land of which he had had the uninterrupted adverse possession ever since was competent, "as this section did not exclude plaintiff from testifying in his own behalf as to the facts that do not appear to have been within the knowledge of the decedent." Vanover v. Maggard, 157 Ky. 743, 164 S. W. 94.

(32) Nurse can not testify as to attention he gave deceased. Green v. Teutschman, 97 S. W. 7, 29 R. 1193; Northrip v. Williams, 30 R. 1279, 100 S. W. 1192.

(33) Party can not make himself competent by drawing out on cross-examination statements from an interested party. Crafton v. Inge, 98 S. W. 325, 30 R. 313, 124 Ky. 89.

(34) Parties interested in estate may testify for its protection in a suit against the estate by one who is living. Ratliff v. Daniel, 121 S. W. 1035; Moore v. Moore, 101 S. W. 358, 30 R. 1370.

(35) Purchaser of land can not testify as to what occurred between him and seller, who is dead. Arthur v. Humble, 140 Ky. 56, 130 S. W. 958.

(36) Defendant may testify for co-defendant. Schonbachler v. Mischell, 89 S. W. 525, 28 R. 460, 121 Ky. 498, and see note 16.

(37) Party may testify as to matters that occurred after the death of the adverse party. Huntsberry v. Smith, 90 S. W. 601, 28 R. 877.

(38) Payee of note signed by mark of payor can not testify as to its genuineness. Clark v. Clark, 91 S. W. 284, 28 R. 1069.

(38a) Transaction-surgical operation. The performance of a surgical operation is a transaction with the person upon whom it is performed; in an action against the surgeon after the death of the person for alleged malpractice in the operation, the surgeon is incompetent to testify as to what he saw or did during the operation, although at the time referred to the deceased was under the influence of an anaesthetic and was unconscious. Barnett's Admr. v. Brand, 165 Ky. 616, 177 S. W. 461.

(39) Wife not competent to prove marriage with her deceased husband. Bartee v. Edmunds, 96 S. W. 535, 29 R. 872.

(40) Claimants against estate are not competent to prove declarations of deceased.

(40a) Two actions were brought by different persons against the estate of the same defendant to recover for alleged services and were heard together. Each plaintiff was a competent witness for the other plaintiff. Benge's Admr. v. Fouts; Same v. Creech, 163 Ky. 796, 174 S. W. 510.

(41) Payor of note when sued can not testify he did not sign note or receive proceeds. Black v. McCarley, 104 S. W. 987, 31 R. 1198, 126 Ky. 825.

(42) Party may testify as to nature and extent of injuries received by person who was killed by negligence. C. & O. R. Co. v. Perkins, 105 S.W. 148, 31 R. 1350, 127 Ky. 110; Aetna Ins. Co. v. Bethel, 131 S. W. 523, 140 Ky. 609.

(43) Where an interested party testifies the adverse party can only testify as to matters referred to by him. Foley v. Dillon, 105 S. W. 461, 32 R. 222; Barton v. Barton, 134 S. W. 902, 142 Ky. 487.

(43a) In an action on a note against the executrix and the devisees of the alleged maker of the note, the execution of the note was put in issue. The executrix was also the widow of decedent and was also a devisee; she testified from her knowledge of decedent's handwriting that the signature to the note was not genuine. The payee was not competent to testify that he saw the decedent sign the note. Torian v. Caldwell, 178 Ky. 509, 199 S. W. 35.

§606-3. (1) Testimony in chief. Where the answer of one of two defendants operates for both, the defendant not answering can not testify in chief for his co-defendant after the latter has taken other testimony.

(3) It is too late to raise the question for the first time in Court of Appeals that party took other depositions before he gave his own. *Hampton v. Bailey, 9 R. 423, 5 S. W. 383; and in Barkley v. Bradford, 100 Ky. 304, 18 R. 725, 38 S. W. 432,* it is held that the court will not reverse for this error alone unless it appears that the appellant was prejudiced by the ruling.

Although a party cannot testify in chief after introducing other evidence, it is within the discretion of the court to permit him to be recalled after he has testified in chief, and after other evidence has been heard. *Louisville Ins. Co. v. Monarch, 95 Ky. 578, 18 R. 444, 30 S. W. 563.*

(5) Stockholders in a corporation may testify for it after it has introduced other witnesses. *Western District Co. v. Hayes, 97 Ky. 10, 16 R. 763, 29 S. W. 738.*

The word "person" in this subsection means a party to the action. *Western Dis. Co. v. Hayes, 97 Ky. 16, 16 R. 763, 29 S. W. 738.*

7 Widow may testify after other witnesses in action by administrator of husband to recover damages. *L. & N. R. Co. v. Lucas, 98 S. W. 308, 30 R. 359.*

(8) Deposition may be read after other witnesses testified when it is taken before the witnesses testified. *Cumberland Tel. Co. v. Overfield, 106 S. W. 242, 32 R. 421, 127 Ky. 548.*

(9) Bringing out new matter in cross-examination does not prevent party from thereafter testifying for himself. *Continental Ins. Co. v. Ford, 140 Ky. 406, 131 S. W. 189.*

(10) Party may testify in rebuttal after other witnesses have testified. *Burkhart v. Loughridge, 98 S. W. 291, 30 R. 305, 124 Ky. 48.*

(11) On the trial of an appeal from an order probating a will the testimony of the propounders offered after the contestants rested was in rebuttal, and not within subsection 3 of this section. *Cowan v. Dillon, 163 Ky. 496, 173 S. W. 1160.*

(12) The provisions of subsection 3 of Code, section 606, do not apply to evidence in rebuttal. *Key v. Duffin, 175 Ky. 348, 194 S. W. 355.*

Title XIII] Evidence Competency of Witnesses. [§ 606

# § 606-7.

(1) Assignment of claim by a person who is incompetent to testify will not make him competent for the assignee. Shoptaw v. Ridgway, 22 R. 1495, 60 S. W. 723; Hagins v. Arnett, 23 R. 809, 64 S. W. 430; Neale v. Neale, 18 R. 343, 36 S. W. 526; Alexander v. Alford, 89 Ky. 105, 4 R. 693; Henry v. Kline, 93 Ky. 358, 13 R. 239; Leonora Nat. Bank v. Ragland, 108 S. W. 854, 32 R. 1404; Boering v. Wilson, 108 S. W. 914, 128 Ky. 5iU; Davis v. Strange's Admr., 156 Ky. 420, 161 S. W. 217.

(3a) Entries in account books made by a person who had no personal knowledge of the facts recorded were not admissible. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Smith & Johnston, 155 Ky. 481, 159 S. W. 987.

(3b) The testimony of a person as to original book entries made by him are admissible, where the only book of original entry in his possession was produced at the time of taking his deposition and was uncontradicted. Barnes v. Scott, 168 Ky. 121, 181 S. W. 984.

(4) Mercantile books can only be admitted as affirmative evidence, and are never admissible to establish a negative proposition; and a merchant's account book is incompetent as evidence when it shows on its face that it does not contain the original entries of the items charged in it. Lawhorn v. Carter, 11 Bush 7; partnership books open to inspection of parties are evidence between them. Simms v. Kirtly, 1 Mon. 80.

# § 606-8.

(1) Party may be examined by adverse party who may also introduce testimony to contradict him. Wilson v. Hays, 109 Ky. 321, 22 R. 897, 58 S. W. 773.

(2) Party is not concluded by evidence of adverse party but may rebut it. Sackstader v. Kast, 31 R. 1304, 105 S. W. 435.

(3) Party has unrestricted right to take deposition of adverse party before trial. Western Union Tel. Co. v. Williams, 129 Ky. 515, 112 S. W. 653; Owensboro City Ry. Co. v. Rowland, 152 Ky. 175, 153 S. W. 206.

(4) Diligence required. A party to an action who desires to take a deposition of the adverse party, as if under cross-examination, is held to the same diligence that would apply to the taking of a deposition of a witness not a party to the action. Home Ins. Co. of N. Y. v. Crowder, 164 Kiv. 792, 176 S. W. 344.

(5) Inability of defendant to obtain the testimony of plaintiff by deposition, as if under cross-examination before the trial was not ground for continuance, where he was not taken by surprise, and there was nothing in the testimony which he was not in every way ready to meet. Cumb. Tel. & Tel. Co. v. Laird, 161 Ky. 800, 171 S. W. 396.

(6) Refusal to testify-when not prejudicial. A defendant whose deposition was begun under Code, sec. 606, subsec. 491.

# Evidence Competency of Witnesses

# § 607 [671] [Other objections go to the credit of witnesses.]

All other objections to witnesses shall go to their credit alone, and be weighed by the jury or tribunal to which their evidence is offered.

# § 608 [672] [Interrogator's testimony as to new matter in equitable action.]

In equitable actions, if a party, in answering interrogatories, state new matters not responsive to the inquiries, the party interrogating may, before the trial, file his written statement, under oath, concerning such new matters, which shall be read as a deposition, though the party so answering may have died or have become a prisoner or of unsound mind. (Provisions concerning interrogatories, secs. 140 to 151.)

# § 609 [678] [New matter in answers to interrogatories adverse party may testify.]

In ordinary actions, if a party, in answering interrogatories, or when introduced as a witness, state new matters not responsive to the inquiries of the adverse party, the latter shall be allowed to testify orally concerning such new matter, though the party so answering may have died or have become a prisoner or of unsound mind.

The refusal of defendant to answer the questions was not prejudicial to plaintiff. Netter v. Caldwell, 173 Ky. 200, 190 S. W. 721.

(7) The refusal of a defendant to give his deposition under Code, sec. 606, sub-sec. 8, is not ground for reversal in the absence of a motion for a continuance for the purpose of taking the deposition. Christian's Admx. v. Ennis, 173 Ky. 101, 190 S. W. 675.

(8) Revenue agent. A revenue agent in a proceeding to assess omitted tangible property had a right to examine, as upon cross-examination, the president of a defendant corporation. Com. v. Ky. Heating Co., 176 Ky. 35, 195 S. W. 459.

(9) Taking deposition of adverse party - how far a waiver. In an action for the wrongful death of a child under sixteen years of age by the father as administrator, the defendant did not, by taking the father's deposition, waive its right to object to the father's testimony on the trial concerning his conversation with his deceased son. Ky. Utilities Co. v. McCarty's Admr., 169 Ky. 38, 183 S. W. 237.

# § 610 [67e] [How and by whom perpetuated—petition to be filed-what it shall state.]

A person who resides in this State and expects to be a party to an action in a court hereof; or who, being a non-resident of this State, has an interest in real property herein, concerning which he expects to be a party to an action in a court hereof; and who desires to perpetuate the testimony of witnesses, may file, in the circuit court of the county in which he resides, or in which the property is situated, a petition in equity, verified by his affidavit, stating—

1. That he expects to be a party to an action in a court of this State, and the nature of the expected controversy.
2. The name, age and place of residence of the expected adverse party, if known by the petitioner; or his ignorance thereof, if not known by him.
3. That the evidence of witnesses—whose names and the facts, generally, expected to be proved, shall be stated—is believed by the petitioner to be material to him.
4. The obstacles preventing the commencement of the action or proceeding, if he expects to commence the same. (Perpetuation of evidence relating to land or mineral or oil interests therein, see sec. 1649a, Ky. Stat. copied after section 615.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 526d.

# § 611 [677, 679] [Proceedings against adverse party.]

1. The expected adverse party shall be summoned and proceeded against in the same manner as a defendant to an action.
2. After such expected party is brought before the court, actually or constructively, the court may, in its discretion, make an order for the examination of the witnesses.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 80.

# § 612 [678 to 680] [Depositions, how taken—costs.]

1. If the examination of the witnesses be permitted by the court, their depositions shall

493

# §613-615 Evidence-Perpetuation Of

be taken, certified and delivered or sent to the clerk of the court, and be kept by him, as depositions in equitable actions.

2. The petitioner shall pay all costs incurred.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 80.

# § 613 [681] [Depositions-when may be read.]

Upon a trial of the controversy in the petition mentioned, between the persons therein named as expected parties or their representatives or successors, such depositions, subject to exceptions as if they had been taken pending the action, may be read by either party under the circumstances prescribed in section 554.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 80.

# § 614 [6s2] [Copies may be read-when.]

If the trial be not in the court in which the deposition is filed, it and the affidavit, order, notice and proof of service, or certified copies thereof, may be read as evidence.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 80.

# § 615 [6ss] [Evidence heretofore perpetuated may be read.]

Evidence which has been or may be perpetuated, agreeably to laws heretofore in force, may be read on the trial of any action or suit which has been or may be commenced in any court of this State, subject to exceptions as if they had been taken pending such suit or action.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 80, 526d.

# § 1649a. Perpetuation of evidence concerning land, timber, mineral, oil or gas—method of procedure.

That any and all parties who are the owners of, or may hereafter become the owners of, any land, mineral, oils, gases, timber or any interest of real estate, or the appurtenances thereunto belonging or in anywise appertaining, about which there may arise or has arisen any controversy or dispute, may perpetuate the testimony of any and all living witnesses relative to their said title in the following manner, to-wit:

First. That the party desiring to perpetuate any testimony as aforesaid shall file in the circuit court clerk's office in the county where the real estate is situate, a statement directed in the caption thereof, if known to him, to any party or parties who may be interested, or claim to be interested in said real estate giving the names and addresses, if known, of such claimants or probable claimants, and if non-residents of this State, giving the name and address of such non-resident and State or county in which he resides if known, and if any such claimant or probable claimant be an infant, a lawyer guardian ad litem, shall be designated to represent such infant, by the clerk of the court, and an attorney shall by said clerk be appointed to represent such non-resident interested parties, claimants, or probable claimants, the same lawyer may be appointed to represent as attorney and guardian ad litem non-resident parties whether infants or not.

If one or more of the said parties are unknown to him, he shall give to the parties known to be interested, with the statement in said caption, "to whom it may concern, and to all parties interested in the real estate herein described." If no one, to his knowledge, is interested in the said real estate, the said statement shall be directed, "to any and all parties interested in following described.

# Title XIII

# Evidence Perpetuation Of. [§ 615]

real estate," setting forth by description the real estate about which testimony is to be taken for the purpose of perpetuating the same in accordance with this act. Said statement shall also set forth the time and place said depositions shall be taken and the names of one or more witnesses whose testimony or evidence is to be taken at the time stipulated in said statement. The said statement shall set forth that he has a title to said real estate about which testimony or evidence he desires to take, and the same subscribed and verified by the party filing the same or by his attorney, representative or agent. The said statement shall be filed before the notice shall be given as hereinafter provided. It shall be the duty of the circuit court clerk, upon the filing of the statement aforesaid, to mark the same "filed," and record it in a book kept by him for that purpose, properly labeled and indexed, for which recording he shall receive a fee of $1.00.

Second. The party or parties desiring to take the deposition of any witness shall give ten days' notice in writing to any and all parties known to him to be interested in said land, or is setting up or attempting to set up, claim to said land, mineral, oils, gases, timber or any other interest to the said real estate, stating the time and place the said depositions will be commenced to be taken, and that the same will be continued from day to day, at the same place and between the hours of 8 a. m. and 6 p. m., until the same is completed, and that said depositions are to be taken in behalf of the title to a certain tract of land known as tract, lying on the (here give the local description), in county, in the State of Kentucky, and that the said depositions are being taken for the purpose of perpetuation of the testimony expected to be given, and he shall give further notice as above recited, by having the same published in some newspaper of general circulation published in the county where the real estate lies, at least four weeks, if it be a weekly newspaper, or fifteen days if said paper be a daily paper, if there be a weekly or daily newspapers so published in said county, and by having a like notice posted at the front door of the court house in the county where said real estate is situate, at least fifteen days before the taking of said depositions. If there be no paper published in the county in which such proceeding is filed, printed or written notices shall, in addition to the actual notices served on the known parties as herein provided, be posted on the front door of the court house, one at a public place on the real estate involved, two at public places in the neighborhood thereof and at six other public places in the county, provided that 60 days' notice shall be given to non-residents of the time of taking of such depositions unless they waive such notice through their attorney or otherwise, and it shall be the duty of the attorney or guardian ad litem for such infants and attorney for non-residents to, so soon as notified of their appointment to in writing, if possible, notify the infants and non-residents whom he represents of the fact and nature and purpose of such proceeding, and if no one other than himself appear to represent by special employment those whom he was appointed to represent, he shall appear and, if necessary, cross-examine and do all necessary to protect the interest of his ward or non-resident claimant against any fraud, imposition or injury, and said attorney shall file with said clerk a written report showing what effort he made to give such notice to his ward or non-resident party, and state what, if any, information he has received from them, and such guardian ad litem and attorney for non-resident parties or claimants and probable claimants shall be allowed a reasonable sum for his services to be paid by the party seeking to perpetuate the evidence.

# § 615 Evidence--Perpetuation Of.

Third. When the said depositions are completed, subscribed by the witness, the same shall be certified to the circuit court clerk of the county where the said real estate is situate, by the officer before whom the same were taken, in the same manner as depositions are now certified, and said officer shall take said clerk's receipt therefor.

Fourth. The said clerk, upon receipt of the said depositions, shall immediately mark the same "lodged in my office to be recorded" and give the date of such lodgment, and record the said depositions in a book he shall keep for that purpose, properly labeled and indexed, and upon application deliver to the party on whose behalf the depositions were taken, the original of said deposition for which recording he shall be allowed the sum of $1 for each separate deposition.

Fifth. The original or certified copy of said deposition or of said record shall be evidence in any and all courts of this Commonwealth, having, or which may hereafter have, competent jurisdiction of the subject matter in controversy. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1649a.)

# EVIDENCE.

1624. Judicial notice of legislative acts and resolutions. The several courts of this Commonwealth shall take judicial notice of all acts and resolutions of the General Assembly. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1624.)

1625. Judicial notice of official signatures. All courts, tribunals and officers shall take notice of the official signature of any officer of this State, of the United States, or of any State or Territory in the United States. (Id., sec. 1625.)

§ 1626. Copies of journals of General Assembly—evidence. Copies of the journal of either House of the General Assembly, printed by the State, shall be received as evidence for any purpose for which the original journal could be received. (Id., sec. 1626.)

§ 1627. Public records-copies of evidence. A copy of any record or paper, properly filed or lodged in the clerk's office of any court, or of the Secretary of State, Treasurer, Register or Auditor, of the surveyor of the county, or assessor's books attested by the person having the legal custody thereof, shall, upon proof of the execution of the original, be admitted as evidence in lieu thereof. (Id., sec. 1627.)

§ 1628. Public record-action on copy—answer of defendant. A copy of any such writing as is embraced in the preceding section may be filed in a suit thereon, and the defendant shall plead thereto as if the original were filed. (Id., sec. 1628.)

1629. Records of towns or religious societies—copies. A copy from the mayor's office of any city, or from the official books of any town or religious society, or an ordinance or by-law for the rule of such city, town or society, attested by the keeper thereof, shall be evidence for any purpose for which the original could be received. (Id., sec. 1629.)

§ 1630. Execution—entries of clerk concerning. A copy from an entry in a clerk's or justice's execution book of the date of an execution that issued from such office, and to whom delivered and when returnable, shall be evidence in any proceeding against the officer and his sureties, to whom it shall be entered as delivered. (Id., sec. 1630.)

Title XIII] Evidence-Perpetuation Of. [5 615

# § 1631. Auditor—certificate of concerning sale of land for taxes.

The certificate of the Auditor of Public Accounts of the fact, and of the time of the return of any real estate as delinquent, or of the sale thereof for taxes, or forfeiture for non-payment of taxes, or for other cause, or concerning the redemption thereof, shall be prima facie evidence of the facts therein stated. (Id., sec. 1631.)

# § 1632. Judges may order record books transcribed-compensation-fiscal court to pay for.

In any county having an official indexer the judges of the Circuit Court of said county and the judge of the County Court of said county, sitting jointly, may order any of the books or records in the county clerk's office, or in the office of the clerk of the Quarterly Court, or in the office of the surveyor of the county, or in the circuit clerk's office to be rebound or when obliterated, torn, or in a ruinous condition, to be transcribed in new books, and said transcriptions shall be done by the official indexer without any extra compensation; but when, in the opinion of two-thirds of said judges, said indexer, on account of other pressing work, shall not be able in due time to transcribe said old books into new books, said judges may order said work to be done by the clerk of any court mentioned above as to the books in his own office, and in the order directing said work to be done the amount of said work and the compensation therefor shall be fixed in advance by the vote of two-thirds of said judges; or they may receive bids for the performance of said work and may award the right to do the same to the lowest and best bidder under proper rules and regulations for the proper and safe execution of the same, with due regard to the importance of duly safeguarding the original records. The amount allowed for said work shall be chargeable to the county, and paid by the fiscal court of said county. [Section as amended by act 1912, p. 302.] (Id., sec. 1632.)

# § 1633. Records of courts--preservation of.

All courts having jurisdiction greater than the county court shall have the same power over the books and papers in their respective clerk's offices as is conferred in the preceding section upon the county courts. All expenses incurred by such courts in this regard shall be paid out of the county levy as other claims, except that such expenses incurred by the Court of Appeals shall be paid out of the State Treasury upon the warrant of the Auditor to be issued when the order of the court directing the expense incurred and a statement of the cost of the same is filed in his office. (Id., sec. 1633.)

# § 1634. Records transcribed entitled to full credit.

Any book so transcribed, examined by the judge of the court, and an order made on the records of the court that the same is correct, shall have the same faith and credit that the book or record transcribed was entitled to. (Id., sec. 1634.)

# § 1635. Records of courts of other States and United States.

The records and judicial proceedings of any court of any State, attested by the clerk thereof, with the seal of the court annexed, if there be a seal, and certified by the judge, chief justice or presiding magistrate of the court, to be attested in due form, shall have such faith and credit given to them in this State as they would have at the place whence the said records come. The record and judicial proceedings of any court of the United States, attested by the clerk thereof, with the seal of the court annexed, if there be a seal, shall have such faith and credit given to them in this Commonwealth as they would have in the courts of the United States. (Id., sec. 1635.)

497

# Evidence--Perpetuation Of

# § 1636. Public records of other States or United States.

All records and exemplifications of office books kept in any public office of the United States, or of a sister State, not appertaining to a court, shall be evidence in this State if attested by the keeper of such record or books, and the seal of his office, if there be a seal. (Id., sec. 1636.)

# § 1637. Records of executive department of other State or United States.

Exemplifications from the books of the executive department of the United States, or any papers filed therein, shall be evidence when attested by the President or the chief of either of the departments; or from any State or Territory, of like books or papers when attested by the Governor or the Secretary of State thereof, under his official seal. (Id., sec. 1637.)

# § 1638. Records from foreign countries.

A copy from the record-books of any court, or of any register of births and marriages, or other instruments of writing, or a copy of any law or ordinance of any State, nation, province, colony, city or town out of the United States, if the same shall have been registered in due form, according to the laws of such sovereignty, certified and attested by the keeper of such record or register, and the attestation, certified under his official seal, by any counsel, charge d'affaires or minister of the United States resident within the sovereignty where the record is kept, shall be evidence in this State. (Id., sec. 1638.)

# § 1639. Death presumed after absence of seven years.

If any person, who shall have resided in this State, go from and do not return to this State for seven successive years, he shall be presumed to be dead, in any case wherein his death shall come in question, unless proof be made that he was alive within that time. (Id., sec. 1639.)

# § 1640. Common law of other States—how proven.

The unwritten or common law of any other of the United States may be proved as facts by the parol evidence of persons learned in that law. The printed books of cases adjudged in the courts of a sister State may also be admitted as evidence of such law. (Id., sec. 1640.)

# § 1641. Foreign laws—how proven.

The existence and the tenor or effect of all foreign laws beyond the limits of the United States may be proved by the parol evidence of persons learned in those laws. But if it appear that the law in question is contained in written statute, the court may reject such parol evidence unless it be accompanied by a copy of the statute. (Id., sec. 1641.)

# § 1642. Printed laws of United States or other State, or copy.

The printed laws of the United States, or of any State or Territory thereof, which has been or shall be received in the Secretary's office of this State, and which shall have been printed under the authority of the United States, or such State or Territory, or a copy thereof, when duly certified by the Secretary of State for this Commonwealth, shall be admitted and received as evidence of such laws. (Id., sec. 1642.)

# § 1643. Lost papers in suit may be supplied by attested copies.

When any papers of any suit pending or decided in any court have been lost, destroyed, defaced, or obliterated, and there is in existence an attested copy thereof, such copy may be filed in the office where such papers belonged in lieu of the originals, and shall be prima facie evidence of the contents of such originals, and used as such originals could have been in all after proceedings in the suits to which such papers belonged: Provided, however, That the party whose rights or interests are affected by the filing of such copies, if a resident.

(Id., sec. 1643.)

# 1644. Printed laws of other State evidence of law.

Any printed volume or pamphlet of laws of any State or Territory, showing on its face that it was published by authority of such State or Territory, shall be prima facie evidence of the laws contained therein. (Id., sec. 1644.)

# SECURITY FOR COSTS.

# § 616 [684] [Non-residents and corporations except banks.]

A plaintiff who is non-resident of this State, or a corporation other than a bank created by the laws of this State, before commencing an action, shall file in the clerk's office a bond of a sufficient surety, to be approved by the

# § 616.

1. Costs. A person may, by a recognizance of record, become security for costs, and in such case the liability of the surety may be enforced by summary proceedings. Kinney v. O'Bannon, 6 Bush 692.
2. As to security for costs in Court of Appeals, see sec. 744: and Paducah Hotel Co. v. Dennis Long, 92 Ky. 278, 13 R. 531, 17 S. W. 853.
3. This section does not apply to public corporations; therefore the trustees of a common school district cannot be required to execute bond. Trustees v. City of Flemingsburg, 97 Ky. 702, 17 R. 445, 31 S. W. 722; Horton v. Sherrell-Russell Co., 144 Ky. 508, 139 S. W. 737.
4. New trial upon payment of costs. See Ky. Stat., sec. 899, and Myers v. Lummis, 80 Ky. 456, 4 R. 301; Galbraith v. Galbraith, 5 R. 859; Mattingly v. L. & N. R. R., 92 Ky. 463, 13 R. 666, 25 S. W. 830; Carbon v. Stout, 7 Bush 609.
5. Temporary removal from State does not constitute one a non-resident. Erwin v. Allen, 124 Ky. 458, 99 S. W. 322, 30 R. 607.
6. Poor person cannot be required to pay cost of dismissed action before bringing new action on same cause. Hobbs v. L., H. & St. L. R. Co., 126 Ky. 1, 102 S. W. 818, 31 R. 453.
7. Will contest - this section applies to. Cape v. Cape, 136 Ky. 625, 124 S. W. 869.
8. Injunction. On application for injunction bond for cost is not required. Owen Co. v. Brumback, 128 Ky. 137, 107 S. W. 710, 32 R. 916.
9. Dismissal of action. An action of a non-resident plaintiff will be dismissed on motion of defendant for failure to execute a cost bond within the time allowed by the court. Van Hooser v. Atkinson, 168 Ky. 1, 181 S. W. 610.
