(14) Demurrer. Order overruling demurrer to answer and reciting that plaintiff declined to plead further, excepted to the judgment and prayed an appeal, is final, although it does not expressly order dismissal of petition. Zable v. Lou. Baptist Home, 92 Ky. 89, 13 R. 385, 17 S. W. 212; the general rule is that a judgment must follow the demurrer. 81 Ky. 345. 5 R. 382.

(1ta) Dismissal of appeal-effect of. An appeal was prosecuted from an order which was not final; the dismissal of this appeal was no bar to a subsequent appeal from a judgment entered before the first appeal was prayed. Harding v. Harding, 151 Ky. 398, 152 S. W. 259.

(15) Divided court. "When the case presents two or more questions and the members of this court are equally divided in opinion upon one or more of such questions and the judgment is reversed upon other points upon which all agree, the opinion of the judges who agree with the court below upon the questions about which there is an equal division here becomes the law of the case as to those questions and binding upon this court on a future appeal."

# Appeals to the Court of Appeals

# § 950. 1. Jurisdiction in civil cases-practice when amount under $500.

An appeal may be taken to the Court of Appeals as a matter of right from the judgment of the circuit court in all cases in which the title to land or the right to an easement therein, or the right to enforce a statutory lien thereon is directly involved, but no appeal shall be taken to the Court of Appeals as a matter of right from a judgment for the recovery of money or personal property, or any interest therein, or to enforce any lien thereon, if the value in controversy be less than five hundred dollars, exclusive of interest and costs; nor to reverse a judgment granting a divorce, or punishing contempt; nor from any order or judgment of the county court, except in actions for the division of land and allotment of dower; nor from any order or judgment of the quarterly, police, fiscal or justices' court; nor from a bond having the force of a judgment. In all other civil cases the Court of Appeals shall have

If the court is equally divided the judgment of the lower court stands affirmed. Ky. Stat., sec. 955.

# (16) Divorce

Judgment granting cannot be appealed from. Ky. Stat., sec. 950; although it may be only from bed and board. See note 3, sec. 420.

# (17) Fiduciary-right of appeal

The right of an unsuccessful party, who occupies a fiducial relation, to prosecute an appeal ceases when he resigns or is removed, and so with a bankrupt when his right passes to the assignee. Parks v. Doty, 13 Bush 727.

# (1) Granting in Court of Appeals

Where the appeal granted by the lower court is not taken, or where the record under the appeal in lower court is not filed in time, and the appeal is dismissed, an appeal may be granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals within the time allowed. City v. Elrod, 14 Bush 210; Wearen v. Smith, 80 Ky. 216, 3 R. 708.

# (19) Suing out a summons against the appellee

is not made a requisite to the granting of the appeal, and is not necessary to secure an appeal. The appeal dates from the filing of the record (now copy of the judgment) with the clerk of the Court of Appeals. Jones v. Finnell, 8 Bush 25.

# (19a)

An appeal was granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals within two years after the rendition of the judgment, but without a copy of the judgment being filed. The copy was filed after the expiration of the two years. The appeal was barred by time. It is intimated that a filing of the copy within the two years would have corrected the mistake. Nickels v. Collins, 153 Ky. 219, 154 S. W. 1090.

# (19b)

Where an appeal has been granted by the court below and has been dismissed or the record has not been filed within the time required by law, an appeal may still be granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals within the statutory period. Blalock v. Atwood, 148 Ky. 828, 147 S. W. 748; Blalock v. Atwood, 154 Ky. 394, 157 S. W. 694.

# (19c)

If an appeal be dismissed for failure to file the transcript in time, appellant may sue out a new appeal from the clerk of the Court of Appeals. Edelston v. Edelston, 173 Ky. 252, 190 S. W. 1083.

# (20) Granting by clerk of Court of Appeals

before expiration of term of circuit court at which judgment was rendered is a nullity and may be disregarded. Schmidt v. Mitchell, 95 Ky. 342, 15 R. 768, 25 S. W. 278.

# (21)

Merely filing transcript in clerk's office is not sufficient; the party desiring an appeal must make application to the clerk to grant it. Wright v. L. & E. R. W. Co., 111 Ky. 690, 23 R. 952, 64 S. W. 675.

# (22)

Where an appeal was dismissed because of an error in name of appellant, the dismissal did not affect the right of the parties upon correction of the error to prosecute an appeal. City v. Wemhoff, 116 Ky. 812, 25 R. 995, 76 S. W. 876.

# (22a)

An action for money was brought by heirs of a decedent as his personal representative had refused to sue. They recovered judgment. The defendant upon appeal procured a reversal of the judgment: but by accident or mistake one of the plaintiffs was not made a party to the appeal. An appeal sued out afterward against that plaintiff was entertained by the Court of Appeals, and the judgment was reversed as to her. Halcomb v. Cole, 155 Ky. 615, 159 S. W. 1191.

# (23) Granting by lower court

The lower court has no right to refuse to

2. Practice when amount is uncertain. When the judgment appealed from does not, when construed in connection with the pleadings, certainly fix the value of the amount or thing in controversy, the court shall, upon the request of either party, state in the judgment the actual value in controversy, and this valuation shall be conclusive of the amount in controversy for the purposes of appeal.

(25) Judgments and orders appealable. The rule is "that a judgment to be final must not merely decide that one of the parties is entitled to relief of a final character, but must give that relief of its own force or be enforceable for that purpose without further action by the court or by process of contempt." Bondurant v. Apperson, 4 Met. 30; Hanson v. Bowger, 4 Met. 108; Chorn v. Chorn, 98 Ky. 627, 17 R. 1178, 33 S. W. 1107; Helm v. Short, 7 Bush 625; Christman v. M. 38; judgment that determines the right but does not give the relief is not final. Bank v. Allen, 8 R. 38.

(26) Final order or judgment from which an appeal lies either terminates the action itself or operates to divest some right in such manner as to put it out of the power of the court making the order after the expiration of the term to place the parties in their original condition. Harrison v. Lebanon Water Works, 91 Ky. 255, 12 R. 822, 15 S. W. 522; and see, further, notes to sec. 368.

(27) An appeal may be taken from an order made when the judgment is rendered, sustaining or discharging an attachment; sees. 266-267; or from an order appointing or refusing to appoint a receiver; sec. 298; or from a final judgment in the circuit or county court, allotting dower or dividing land. See 499-12.

Rodes, Trustee, v. Yates, Trustee, 151 Ky. 162, 151 S. W. 359.

# (39e) Refusal to allow the filing of an answer is not a final order.

Pool v. Pool, 182 Ky. 241.

# (40) Jurisdiction-amount in controversy.

Amount in controversy, where the defendant appeals in the absence of a set-off or counter-claim, is amount of judgment against him, and not the amount sued for. L. & N. R. R. v. Wade, 80 Ky. 255, 11 R. 436, 12 S. W. 279; Dunning v. Lacey, 96 Ky. 611, 16 R. 721, 29 S. W. 435; American Accident Co. v. Slaughter, 101 Ky. 260, 19 R. 418, 40 S. W. 675; Guthrie v. Smith, 18 R. 172, 35 S. W. 918.

# (41) Under present law - Ky. Stat. sec. 950, the amount in controversy must be $200, exclusive of interest and costs;

and see construing this statute, Walter A. Wood Co. v. Taylor, 104 Ky. 217, 20 R. 536, 46 S. W. 720; Hale v. Grogan, 106 Ky. 311, 20 R. 1856, 50 S. W. 257;

Appeals to the Court of Appeals. [Title XVIII] 

N., C. & St. Louis R. R. v. Mattingly, 101 Ky. 219, 19 R. 373, 40 S. W. 673.

(42) Where the matter in controversy exceeded fifty dollars (now two hundred dollars) the Court of Appeals had jurisdiction on an appeal by the plaintiffs from a judgment in their favor for three dollars, but the defendant could not appeal as the judgment against him was for less than fifty dollars (now two hundred dollars). Brandeis v. Stewart, 1 Met. 395; Revill v. Pettit, 3 Met. 315; Tipton v. Chambers, 1 Met. 565. Where the judgment was for an amount from which defendant could appeal, the plaintiff by abating enough of it to reduce it below that amount, could not prevent an appeal. Washington Mfg. Co. v. Barnett, 19 R. 958, 42 S. W. 1120.

(43) Where several are jointly suable on the same contract for separate amounts, the aggregate claimed against all determines the jurisdiction. Wilde v. Haycraft, 2 Duv. 309.

(41) When several join in one action to assert distinct interests, the amount of the interest of each is the test, and the amounts claimed cannot be added together to give jurisdiction. Oswald v. Morris, 92 Ky. 48, 13 R. 355, 17 S. W. 167; Fehler v. Gosnell, 99 Ky. 380, 18 R. 238, 35 S. W. 1125; Covington Bro. Co. v. Jordan, 100 S. W. 326, 30 R. 1135, 125 Ky. 73; Sealy v. Combs, 118 S. W. 972.

(45) Although the amount claimed may be more than $200, yet if it clearly appears from the pleadings that the amount in controversy is less than that, the appeal will be dismissed. Evans v. Clearer, 16 R. 199, 29 S. W. 29; and so where the plaintiff sued for $150 and afterward amended his petition and claimed $75 more for the evident purpose of giving court jurisdiction of his case, his appeal will be dismissed. Smith v. C. & O. R. R., 26 R. 758, 82 S. W. 410.

(46) Court has jurisdiction of a case involving only the right to interest where the amount is over $200. Whitehead v. Brothers' Lodge, 24 R. 1633, 71 S. W. 933.

(47) Where the defendant admitted an indebtedness of $1.40 he could not appeal from a judgment of $900 against him. I. C. R. R. v. Landram, 112 Ky. 687, 23 R. 1956, 66 S. W. 599.

(48) Where the amount for which plaintiff obtains judgment when added to amount of counter-claim asserted by defendant is more than $200, court has jurisdiction. Singer Mfg. Co. v. Witt, 26 R. 213, 80 S. W. 1124; and so when the title to land is not involved where the only question is as to priority between lienholders. Pittman v. Wakefield, 90 Ky. 171, 11 R. 972, 13 S. W. 525.

(49) Court has jurisdiction in all cases regardless of amount involved unless it be an action to recover money or personal property--and therefore, jurisdiction of action in which an injunction is sought to restrain collection of less than $200. Shackelford v. Phillips, 112 Ky. 563, 24 R. 154, 66 S. W. 419; 68 S. W. 441; and see 78 Ky. 23.

(50) In cases involving questions of taxation the court has jurisdiction without regard to the amount in controversy. Willis v. Thornton, 25 R. 1521, 78 S. W. 215.

(51) Jurisdiction of "this court only extends to the final orders and judgments of inferior courts, and not to the orders and judgments which judicial officers are authorized to make out of court." Weddington v. Sloan, 15 B. M. 147; Gill on Petition, 92 Ky. 118, 13 R. 351, 17 S. W. 166.

(52) Where the judgment against appellant is not sufficient to give court jurisdiction, will a counter-claim pleaded by him, and which there is no evidence whatever to support and which was dismissed, give jurisdiction? Licking Rolling Mill v. Fischer, 88 Ky. 176, 10 R. 763, 11 S. W. 305.

(53) See as to jurisdiction and powers of Court of Appeals, Ky. Stat., secs. 949, 950.

(54) Land. Where either the legal or equitable title to land is put in issue or a lien is asserted upon it either party may appeal without reference to its value or the amount in controversy. Hughes v. Swope, 88 Ky. 254, 11 R. 783, 1 S. W. 394; Moore v. Boner, 7 Bush 26; Caskey v. Lewis, 15 B. M. 27; Allen v. Long, 19 R. 488, 41 S. W. 17; Smith v. Catlin, 23 R. 381, 63 S. W. 473; Davis v. Ramage, 23 R. 1420, 65 S. W. 340; pending the litigation the land has been converted into money does not affect the right of appeal. Clements v. Waters, 90 Ky. 96, 11 R. 880, 13 S. W. 431.

(55) In action for trespass, where title is brought in issue, appeal lies, although amount in controversy is less than one hundred dollars. Thacker v. Crawford, 598.

# Title XVIII

# Appeals to the Court of Appeals

[§ 734]

5 R. 764; Patterson v. T. J. Moss Co., 24 R. 1571, 71 S. W. 930.

# (56) Mandamus

Court of Appeals will issue writ of to compel circuit court to grant an appeal to a party entitled to it. Kelley v. Toney, 95 Ky. 338, 15 R. 718. 25 S. W. 264; Schmidt v. Mitchell, 95 Ky. 342, 15 R. 768, 25 S. W. 278; Louisville School of Reform v. City of Louisville, 88 Ky. 584, 11 R. 109, 12 S. W. 710; and see, further, notes, sec. 474.

# (57) Office, franchise or freehold

Final order or judgment relating to may be appealed from. Smith v. Cochran, 7 Bush 147; Cheek v. Com., 87 Ky. 429 R. 880, 7 S. W. 403; Johnson v. Com., 90 Ky. 53; 12 R. 20, 13 S. W. 520.

# (58) Parties-privies

Whoever is a party in the lower court, and would be a necessary party to any further proceedings after reversal, must be a party to the appeal, and if not made a party the appeal will be dismissed. Murphy v. O'Reilly, 78 Ky. 263; by privies are meant heirs, executors and ter-tenants. Barr v. Stevens, 1 Bibb 293; South v. Hoy, 3 Bibb 523.

# (59) Payment of judgment

Replevying or satisfying a judgment is not a waiver of the right to prosecute an appeal for its reversal. Kellar v. Williams, 10 Bush 216.

# (60) Prohibition

Power of Court of Appeals to issue writ of is suggested, but not decided, in Preston v. Fidelity Trust Co., 94 Ky. 295, 15 R. 130, 22 S. W. 318; but has been exercised in other cases, see notes to sec. 479 and Constitution, sec. 110.

# (61) Presumption in favor of judgment

The court will presume, unless the contrary appears, that the lower court committed no error. Licking Rolling Mill v. Fischer, 88 Ky. 176, 10 R. 763, 11 S. W. 305.

# (62) Refusal of judge to vacate bench

when proper affidavits are filed is a reversible error. Massie v. Com., 93 Ky. 588, 14 R. 564, 20 S. W. 704; German Ins. Co. v. Landram, 88 Ky. 433, 10 R. 1039, 11 S. W. 367, 592.

# (63) Res judicata

Every question presented upon an appeal must be taken to have been disposed of by the decision upon the appeal, unless it be expressly left open for further litigation. Smith v. Brannin, 79 Ky. 114, 1 R. 414; Davis v. McCorkle, 14 Bush 746.

# (64)

"The plea of res judicata applies, except in special cases, not only to points upon which the court was actually required by the parties to form an opinion and pronounce judgment, but to every point which properly belonged to the subject of litigation and which the parties exercising reasonable diligence might have brought forward." Francis v. Wood, 81 Ky. 16, 4 R. 616; Hardwicke v. Young, 110 Ky. 504, 22 R. 1906, 62 S. W. 10; Elswick v. Matney, 132 Ky. 294, 116 S. W. 718; and see further notes to sec. 368, and Bitzer v. O'Bryan, 107 Ky. 590, 21 R. 1307, 54 S. W. 951; Bridges v. McAlister, 106 Ky. 791, 21 R. 428, 51 S. W. 603; Thompson v. Lou. Banking Co., 21 R. 1611, 55 S. W. 1080.

# (65) Party who is in doubt as to proper remedy

and prosecutes two appeals at same time, will not, after the merits of the litigation have been determined in one of them by the Court of Appeals, be heard to complain of judgment on his other appeal. Masonic Temple v. Com., 11 R. 383, 12 S. W. 143.

# (66) Void judgment

motion to correct must be made in lower court before appeal will lie. Sec. 763 and notes.

# (67) A judgment only binds parties and privies

and the plea of res judicata is only available against parties and privies. County of Henderson v. Henderson Bridge Co., 116 Ky. 164, 25 R. 421, 75 S. W. 239, and see note 58.

# (68) The sufficiency of a petition

to which a demurrer was filed not having been passed on in the first appeal will not be considered on second appeal. L. & N. R. R. v. Mattingly, 22 R. 489, 57 S. W. 620; and the same rule applies to instructions. F. & S. Tob. W. H. Co. v. Gibbons, 24 R. 1670, 72 S. W. 12.

# (69) Appellant against co-appellant

An appellant cannot have any relief against a co-appellant—if he desires relief he should make him an appellee. Ford Lumber Co. v. Cornett, 148 Ky. 25, 145 S. W. 1105.

# (70) Assignor of mortgagor

may appeal from refusal to allow him to redeem—practice. Miller v. Wheeler, 147 Ky. 131, 143 S. W. 1028.

# (71) Party to record

may pray an appeal on record filed by another party. Day v. Amburgy, 143 S. W. 1033, 147 Ky. 123; Allen Co. v. United States F. & G. Co., 93 S. W. 44, 122 Ky. 825.

# (72) Attorney for deceased appellant

may file copy of judgment in clerk's office and pray an appeal and thereafter it may be revived in name of administrator. Davis v. Cattlesburg Water Co., 136 Ky. 596, 123 S. W. 335.

# (73) Injunction

appeal lies from order refusing to grant although amount in controversy may be less than $200.

Appeals to the Court of Appeals

# § 735 [877] [Designation of parties to appeal.]

The party taking the appeal shall be called the appellant; and the adverse party, the appellee.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 78.

# § 736 [878] [Summons when necessary -warning order —appointment of attorney.]

1. No summons of appellee shall be necessary, if the appeal be granted by the inferior court; or if the appellees enter their appearance in the Court of Appeals.
2. In all other cases, the appellees shall be summoned actually or constructively, pursuant to the provisions of chapter 2 of title 4, to appear and answer the appeal.
3. [Warning order-attorney.] For an appellee who is constructively summoned in the Court of Appeals [the clerk of] that court shall appoint attorney, whose duty it shall be to inform the appellee of the pendency of the appeal; and the court may allow him reasonable compensation for his services, to be paid by the appellant and taxed as costs. (Words in brackets inserted by Act of January 13, 1893, c. 131, p. 433.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 84, 96.

# § 737 [879] [Transcripts, how prepared—schedule—subpoena duces tecum—certificate of clerk-index.]

Concerning transcripts of records to be used in the Court of Appeals--(See Ky. Stat., secs. 4637-45 as to transcripts made by official stenographer; Form 133.)

Cincinnati Packet Co. v. Malone, 29 R. 46, 92 S. W. 306; and see note 49.

(74 New trial. Appeal lies from order granting after term under sec. 518. Francis v. Lilly, 124 Ky. 230, 98 S. W. 896, 30 R. 393. and see note 30.

(75 Next friend may prosecute an appeal. Bowman v. Callahan, 137 Ky. 773, 127 S. W. 142.

(76) Refusal to transfer case to Federal court is not a final or appealable order. C. & O. R. Co. v. Helton, 141 Ky. 404. 132 S. W. 1024, but order removing action is.

(77) Appeal must be granted in lower court or prayed in Court of Appeals. Imperial Bank v. Com., 138 Ky. 746, 129 S. W. 95.

# § 735. Error in name of parties.

If an appeal is dismissed because of an error in the name of the parties and the error is afterward corrected a new appeal may be prosecuted. City v. Wehmhoff, 116 Ky. 812, 25 R. 995, 76 S. W. 876; City v. Wehmhoff, 24 R. 438, 68 S. W. 650.

# § 736. Appearance entered by appeal

and on return of case to circuit court the appellant will be before the court, although he may not have been prior to appeal. L. & N. R. R. v. Chesnut, 115 Ky. 43, 24 R. 1846. 72 S. W. 351; Foster Milburn Co. v. Chinn, 134 Ky. 424, 120 S. W. 364.

# § 737.

1. Defective record- how cured. Secs. 472, 473, and notes.
2. Entire record, when only partial necessary. An appeal will not be dismissed because a party brings the entire record up in a proceeding under chapter 3, title 10. If a party sees proper to bring the entire record he can do so, but he must pay the entire costs, although the judgment be reversed. Murrell v. McAlister, 78 Ky. 73.
3. Immaterial record—party who has transcript of made must pay costs although successful. Forest Hill Assoc. v. McEvoy, 24 R. 161. 66 S. W. 1031; Sandy River Coal Co. v. White House Co., 102 S. W. 320, 31 R. 374.
4. The clerk of the circuit court copied the entire record; no specific directions had been given to him. The costs resulting from the copying of immaterial parts of the record were adjudged to be borne by the appellant. Saunders v. Standard Wheel Co., 152 Ky. 238, 153 S. W. 211.

Title XVIIII Appeals to the Court of Appeals.

1. a. *[Clerk to copy record as directed by schedule.]* The clerk of the court which renders a judgment shall copy the entire record, or parts thereof, according to the directions of the judge of the court, or of the parties to the appeal; and the filing of a schedule, as hereinafter authorized, shall be deemed a direction to copy the parts of the record therein mentioned.

b. *[Application of subsections 8 and 9.]* The clerk shall be governed by the provisions of subsections 8 and 9 of this section, in making any transcript to which those provisions apply.

2. *[Assignment of errors.]*

3. *[Entire record to be copied if appellees constructively summoned.] In all [other]* cases in which the appeal is granted by the inferior court if the appellees shall have been defendants in the action, and if any of them shall have been only constructively summoned, and have not appeared, in the action, and be not actually summoned to answer the appeal, the appellant shall order a transcript of the entire record.

(4a) Entire record, when necessary. An appeal was granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals. Certain of the appellants were proceeded against only by warning order. It did not appear that the transcript filed was a transcript of the entire record. The appeal was dismissed. Geo. Anderson & Co. v. Tegart, 164 Ky. 605, 175 S. W. 1011.

(5) Partial transcript. Appellant may bring up so much of the record as he deems necessary to the determination of a question involved, but if it appears that portions of the pleadings or evidence bearing upon the question have been omitted the judgment will be affirmed. Terrell v. Rowland, 86 Ky. 67. R. 258, 4 S. W. 825: Courier-Journal Co. v. Cardoza, 21 R. 1259, 54 S. W. 966; Davis v. Day, 100 Ky. 24, 18 R. 525, 37 S. W. 158; Byrd v. Rose, 19 R. 1898, 44 S. W. 958; Hackney v. Hoover, 23 R. 2061. 67 S.W. 48.

(6) "An appellant who prosecutes an appeal upon a partial transcript does so at his peril, and if it appears that part of the testimony used on the trial in court below is not copied into the transcript it will be presumed in support of the judgment that it would sustain the averments of appellee's pleading." Jones v. Jackson, 13 R. 253. 16 S. W. 458: Hackney v. Hoover, 23 R. 2061, 67 S. W. 601.

(8) Rules of Court of Appeals concerning transcripts. See Rules, Appendix. Chapter XII.

(Schedule-partial record-cross-appeal. So much of the Code as requires the judge to direct what part of the record is to be copied must be regarded as repealed by the act repealing so much of the Code as required an assignment of errors.

4. In all other cases in which the appeal is granted by the inferior court—

1. Schedule—time in which appellant to file—penalty for failure.

The appellant, within ninety days after the granting of the appeal, shall file in the office of the clerk of the inferior court a schedule, showing, concisely, what parts of the record he wishes to have copied. His failure to file said schedule within the time prescribed shall be cause for the dismissal of his appeal.
2. Notice of filing of schedule—service of—appellee may file schedule.

After filing said schedule, the appellant may cause notice of the filing thereof to be served on the appellee, and to be returned to said office, as a summons is directed to be served and returned; and within twenty days after such service; or, if such notice be not served, within one hundred and twenty days after the granting of the appeal; or, whether such notice be served or not, at any time before completion of the transcript ordered by the appellant, and not afterward, the appellee may file in said office a schedule similar to that above described.

The appellant may still bring a part of the record by filing his schedule within ninety days from the time he prays the appeal, and the filing of the schedule with the clerk is all the notice required to be given to the appellee; but he will not be permitted to file a partial record unless he has filed his schedule within ninety days, and this rule applies as well to appeals involving the settlement of estates as in ordinary cases. When the entire record is filed in time no schedule is necessary. If the appellee desires to prosecute a cross-appeal in this court he may file his schedule in the court below, either before or after the schedule is filed by the appellant. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Brice, 83 Ky. 210, 7 R. 180; Harrigan v. The Advance Threshing Co., 25 R. 2116, 80 S. W. 205; but if only a partial record is desired the schedule must be filed in ninety days or appeal will be dismissed. Nelson Co. v. B. & L. T. P. Co., 24 R. 2056, 72 S. W. 1104; and see White v. White, 148 Ky. 492, 146 S. W. 1101, see 150 Ky. 191; 149 Ky. 205.

When appeal is granted by lower court schedule must be filed within ninety days after appeal is granted, or appeal will be dismissed; and where appeal is granted by clerk of Court of Appeals schedule may be filed within ninety days from time appeal is so granted. Adams v. Bement, 96 Ky. 334, 16 R. 676, S. W. 22; and see Wearen v. Smith, 80 Ky. 216, 3 R. 708; Stidham v. Lee Co., 150 Ky. 191, 150 S. W. 10.

A schedule ordering only parts of the record, must order complete parts, and not fragments, such as a certain page of a deposition. Dockins v. Dukes, 151 Ky. 276, 151 S. W. 679.

Schedule must be filed within ninety days from the granting of the appeal, and extending time to prepare and file a bill of exceptions does not extend time in which to file schedule, nor prevent appellant from filing schedule within the prescribed time. Wright v. Woolfolk, 14 Bush 308; and when appellant and appellee each file a schedule the clerk should make his transcript cover each—judge may direct only necessary parts of record copied upon application made to him. Davis v. Day, 100 Ky. 24, 18 R. 525, 37 S. W. 158.

Schedule-when notice of filing unnecessary. A schedule directed the clerk to copy the entire record, with the exception of processes and notices to take depositions; the Court of Appeals refused to dismiss the appeal on the ground that appellants failed to serve notice of the filing of the schedule. Clark's Heirs v. Boyd, 152 Ky. 234, 153 S. W. 227.

(17a) Papers supplied in circuit court. Papers supplied in the circuit court in place of lost papers may take the place of such papers for the purposes of appeal; objections to the correctness of the supplied record must be made by appeal from an order of the circuit court supplying the record. Such objections cannot be considered on the appeal taken on the merits of the case. Turner v. Hamlin, 152 Ky. 469, 153 S. W. 778.

(18) Pleadings, motions and orders that are identified by the order book or copied therein become a part of the record when copied in obedience to the schedule or when clerk certifies that transcript is complete record. Barnard Jeas Co. v. Washburn, 99 S. W. 664, 30 R. 813.

(19) Several parties may prosecute appeal on same record. Allen Co. v. United States F. & G. Co., 122 Ky. 825, 93 S. W. 44.

(20) Presumption that record is complete—when the schedule required by the Code has been filed and transcript is made in accordance therewith it will be presumed that record is complete. See rule 27 of Court of Appeals and Clevinger v. Nunnery, 140 Ky. 592, 131 S. W. 519; White v. White, 148 Ky. 492, 146 S. W. 1101; Braswell v. Hurley, 149 Ky. 205.

(20a) Presumption that omitted portions support judgment. In the absence from the record of certain pleadings and evidence it was presumed that the omitted portions of the record would support the judgment. Smith v. Berry, 164 Ky. 621, 176 S. W. 17.

(20b) One who prosecutes an appeal upon a partial transcript does so at his own peril. It will be presumed that such testimony as was used upon the trial and was not copied into the transcript would sustain the appellee's case. Sublett v. Gardner, Admr., 164 Ky. 385, 175 S. W. 628.

(21) Subpoenas and summons need not be copied into record unless especially directed. Louisville Cooperage Co. v. Fanner, 107 S. W. 770, 32 R. 1110.

(22) Notice of schedule must be given when appeal is granted by clerk of Court of Appeals. Snilsherry v. Sailsberry, 140 Ky. 731, 131 S. W. 802; White v. White, 148 Ky. 492, 146 S. W. 1101.

(23) Photographs should be identified in stenographer's transcript or by order of court. Southern R. Co. v. Schmidt, 118 S. W. 324.

(24) Complete record may be brought up without a schedule. Grubbs v. Fish, 97 S. W. 358, 29 R. 1291; and see note 9.

Title XVIII Appeals to the Court of Appeals.

# 10. [Schedule notice and return to be copied in transcript.]

The above mentioned schedules and notices and returns thereon shall, when filed in the office of the clerk of the inferior court, constitute part of the record; but shall not be spread at large on the order book; and shall be copied in the transcript.

# 11. [Cost of copy of unnecessary papers paid by party ordering.]

A clerk who copies papers which do not constitute part of a record, unless required to do so by [a judge or†] a party; and a party who requires a clerk to copy such papers, or immaterial parts of a record, shall pay the costs resulting therefrom, to be adjudged by the Court of Appeals upon or without motion.

# 12. [Certificate of clerk to record-inde.r-cxhibit.]

At the close of the transcript, the clerk shall certify, in substance, that it contains a true and complete copy of the record, or of such parts thereof as he may have been required to copy, according to the truth; and shall append an index referring, consecutively, to the pages on which the copy of each pleading, or other separate part of the record, commences; and describing briefly the exhibits, if any. (Certificate of clerk—form of, Forms 185, 186.)

# 13. [Transcript made according to this section regarded as complete record-e.rception.]

Subject to the provisions of sections 742 and 743 transcripts and transmissions of parts of records, made pursuant to the foregoing provisions of this section, shall constitute complete records for the purposes of appeal. (Section as amended by act April 4. 1884, as given in the asterisk (*) note belore'; sce as to transcript madc by official stenog- rapher. Ky. Stat., secs. 4637-45.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 41, 93, 94, 95, 100; 101, 103.

# (25) Appeal-schedule.

When a party prays an appeal before clerk of Court of Appeals he may bring up record on a schedule filed with notice before the appeal was prayed. Husbands v. Fidelity & Deposit Co., 144 Ky. 93. 137 S. W. 855.

# (26) Schedule must be filed showing what parts of record are desired unless a complete record is ordered.

Dalton v. Dalton, 146 Ky. 18, 141 S. W. 371.

# (27) Copying unnecessary records.

When the clerk copies unnecessary records—for example, record of case on former appeal the cost cannot be taxed against unsuccessful party. Sandy River Cannel Coal Co. v. White House Co., 102 S. W. 320, 31 R. 374 and see note 3.

# (27a) Papers not part of record below.

If the clerk copies into a transcript a paper not part of the record when the case was heard in the circuit court, it was not considered on appeal. Beavers v. Nelson, 152 Ky. 319, 153 S. W. 42.

# (27b) In making up the transcript the record of another suit which was not considered in the circuit court nor made part of the record was copied into the transcript: it was not considered on appeal.

Dalton v. Dalton, 146 Ky. 18, 141 S. W. 371.

# §738 Appeals to the Court of Appeals.

By an Act of April 4, 1884, c. 600, p. 43, the Legislature provided as follows:

That so much of chapters 1, 2, and 3, of title 18, of an act entitled 'An act regulating practice in civil cases,' as relates to and requires the assignment of errors and cross-errors by parties prosecuting appeal or cross-appeals, be, and the same is hereby, repealed.

The foregoing section as above given appears in form as amended by the foregoing act.

"So much of the Code as requires the judge to direct what part of the record is to be copied must be regarded as repealed."—L. & N. R. R. v. Brice, 83 Ky. 210, 212.

# § 738. [Time in which transcript must be filed.]

The appellant shall file the transcript in the office of the clerk of the Court of Appeals at least

Exceptions will not be considered, although copied into the transcript by the clerk. Payne's Gdn. v. Nicholson & Co., 155 Ky. 04, 159 S. W. 672.

(27e) A map attached to the record by the clerk in making up the transcript which was not shown by bill of exceptions was struck from the record. Ferrell v. Bauer Cooperage (o., 156 Ky. 740, 161 S. W. 1120.

(29) The transcript of the evidence is a record of the circuit court, and if mistakes have been made in it they may be corrected in that court as other clerical errors are corrected. Center Lum. Co. v. Miller, 158 Ky. 130, 164 S. W. 318.

(30) Supplemental record. After a judgment was reversed to the prejudice of infant parties they were permitted to tender a portion of the record which had been omitted by mistake; a supplemental record was filed and the judgment affirmed. Wade v. Wade, 154 Ky. 24, 156 S. W. 873.

# 738. (1 Time to file transcript.

When a transcript has in good faith been filed and is defective or contains only a part of the record, a supplemental record may be filed at any time before submission. Bush v. Lisle, 86 Ky. 504, 9 R. 667, 6 S. W. 330.

# (2) The proper practice is to ask for further time to file transcript when it cannot be filed within time allowed by Code.

Williamson v. Maynard, 135 Ky. 29, 121 S. W. 967: Willis v. Whitt. 124 S. W. 3o?; but when the appellant is ready to perfect the record when motion to dismiss is made, or before dismissal, the court will permit him to do so. Bush v. Lisle, 86 Ky. 504, 9 R. 667, 6 S. W. 330.

# (3) An order giving time to file bill of exceptions does not operate to extend time for filing transcript.

Western Union v. Johnson, 100 Ky. 589, 18 R. 982, 38 S. W. 1043; Willis v. Whitt, 124 S. W. 362.

# (3a) An appeal was dismissed because the record was not filed in time, although the filing of the bill of exceptions was delayed by causes beyond appellant's control.

Wickliffe v. Turner, 149 Ky. 379, 149 S. W. 833.

# (3b) Within the time for filing a bill of exceptions one of the appellees died.

The bill of exceptions was tendered within the time and the transcript was filed in the Court of Appeals in time. The filing of the bill of exceptions was not binding on the representatives of the appellee who had died. But it was allowable that steps be taken in the court below to prepare the case as against the representatives of the person who had died. Gish Banking Co. v. Leachman's Admr., 159 Ky. 769, 169 S. W. 481.

# § 740. [Statement before appeal docketed-penalty for not filing transcript in time.]

No appeal shall be docketed by the clerk until the appellant complies with the provisions of section 739, and if he fail to file upon parties whose names appear in statement, but who were not parties to the record. O. & Mt. Sterling Road Co. v. Hamilton, 21 R. 1150, 54 S. W. 175; and motion to dismiss appeal because of failure to file statement will be waived unless made when case is submitted. City of Lou. v. Clark, 105 Ky. 392, 20 R. 1265, 49 S. W. 1S; City of Lou. v. Cassidy, 105 Ky. 424, 20 R. 134S, 49 S. W. 194; and see Bain v. McAlpin Co., 17 R. 575, 32 S. W. 175; Reinhardt v. Louisville Bk. Co., 17 R. 982, 33 S. W. 198; and sec. 740.

(1) Statement as follows: "C. W. Parsons, &e., appellees." only brings before the court "C. W. Parsons." Brodie v. Parsons, 23 R. 831, 64 S. W. 426; statement that does not show date of judgment or page of record on which it can be found is not sufficient. Tarvin v. Tarvin, 20 R. 730. 47 S. W. 434.

(1a) A party who is not named as an appellant in the statement of appeal, is not before the court as an appellant. Com. v. Columbia Tr. Co., 162 Ky. 825, 173 S. W. 386.

(1b) One can not become an appellant by merely filing his brief where his name is not contained in the statement of appeal. Com. v. Columbia Tr. Co., 162 Ky. 825. 173 S. W. 386.

(1c) In an appeal granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals, no one is named in the statement of appeal is an appellant; the addition of the abbreviation "&e." to the name of the appellant does not affect this rule. Morton v. Young, 173 Ky. 301, 190 S. W. 1090.

(1d) A single appeal can not be prosecuted against judgments in favor of separate parties. The appeal, in such a case, will be treated as if prayed against the appellee first named in the statement. Matney v. Edmonds. 179 Ky. 536, 201 S. W. 465.

(1e) A cross-appeal granted against one who was not mentioned in the statement of appeal was dismissed. Matney v. Edmonds. 179 Ky. 243, 200 S. W. 365.

(1f) A statement of appeal must contain, among other things, the date on which the judgment is rendered and the page of the record upon which it may be found, and if it fails to contain these things, the appeal should not be docketed by the clerk, and if docketed will be stricken on motion made before submission of the case. Stratton, et al., v. Syck, 182 Ky. 78.

(5) Failure to file correct statement is not cause for dismissal of appeal. Buchanan v. Boyd, 131 Ky. 433, 120 S. W. 295.

(6) Error of appellant in failing to give the page of the record on which the judgment appealed from may be found, but giving the page of the order book in the trial court where the judgment is recorded, is waived by failure of the appellee to move to dismiss the appeal for such error. Shelton v. Shelton, 167 Ky. 167, 130 S. W. 83.

# § 741 [850] [Appellee may file copy of record.]

The appellee may file an authenticated copy of the record in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals with the same effect as if filed by the appellant.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 98, 111.

# § 742 [881] [Writ of certiorari—when may be issued.]

If any party to an appeal file in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals a statement of himself, or of his attorney, that the clerk of the inferior court has refused or failed to copy a part of the record which a party pursuant to subsections 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7, of section 737 may have ordered to be copied— which parts of the record shall, in general terms, be described in the statement—the clerk of the Court of Appeals shall, upon the application of such party, issue a writ of certiorari, describing, in general terms, such omitted parts of the record, and commanding the clerk of the inferior court, forthwith, to transmit copies thereof to the Court of Appeals. (Section as amended by Act of April 4, 1884. See asterisk (*) note to section 737.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 102.

(3) Court no authority to extend time after time to file record has expired. Mat- tingly's Ex. v. Brents, 156 Ky. 844, 162 S. W. 109. Langhorne v. Wiley, 120 Ky. 511, 87 S. W. 266, 27 R. 908.

# § 742. (1) Correction of defective record.

When the record is defective by reason of a clerical misprision time will be given by the court to have it corrected and brought up by certiorari. Wil- liams v. Thompson, 80 Ky. 325, 4 R. 9.

(1a) Where a part of the record in the circuit court has been lost since the hearing, time will be given by the Court of Appeals for the supplying of the record by the circuit court. Roberts v. Calhoun, 149 Ky. 731, 149 S. W. 991.

(1b) When a case is decided in the Court of Appeals and the case is then taken from that court by writ of error to the Supreme Court of the United States, and while the case is pending in that court, a mistake in the transcript is discovered, it may be corrected by motion of the Court of Appeals in the same manner as if discovered before the decision of the case in the latter court. The clerk will certify the proceedings on the motion as well as the opinion of the court to the Supreme Court as an additional record. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Wood- ford, 157 Ky. 426, 163 S. W. 238.

Appeals to the Court of Appeals. [Title XVIII]

# § 743 [82] [Court may issue certiorari or order original papers filed.]

1. If the appellees, having been defendants in the original action in the inferior court, be under any disability, other than coverture, or infancy and coverture combined, the Court of Appeals may, by writ of certiorari directed to the clerk of the inferior court, require him to transmit a transcript of such parts of the record as the parties may have failed to require him to copy.
2. [Original record may be brought up-costs.] The Court of Appeals, if satisfied that a view of any part of a record may be important to a correct decision of an appeal; or that the copying of any part of a record would cause great and unnecessary cost or delay, may, by a writ of subpoena duces tecum directed to the clerk of the inferior court, cause him to transmit such part of the record to the clerk of the Court of Appeals. It shall be identified by the clerk of the inferior court, by his certificate endorsed thereon or appended thereto; and, after the cause shall have been decided by the Court of Appeals, shall be returned by its clerk to the clerk of the inferior court, and be kept by him, subject to the order of the inferior court. The cost of such transmission and return shall be paid by the party at whose instance the transmission is made, and be taxed as part of the costs.

# § 743. (1) Commissioner to assist court.

In Gilmour v. Kerr, 18 R. 400, 25 S. W. 270, the court had the commissioner of the lower court appear before it as an aid to understanding the complicated record.

# (2) Subpoena duces tecum.

The court will not grant the writ of subpoena duces tecum that the original bill may be inspected unless there is an affidavit that the instructions embodied in the bill were not those offered, given or refused by the court on the trial. and so with reference to any exhibit made part of the bill of exceptions. Meaux v. Meaux, 81 Ky. 475, 5 R. 548; see further, notes to sec. 742.

# § 744 [88s] [Costs appellant may be required to secure.]

The appellant may be required to give security for costs, as plaintiffs in civil actions may be so required. (See as to security for costs, sec. 616.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 125.

# § 745 [884] [Limitation of right to appeal.]

An appeal shall not be granted except within two years next after the right to appeal first accrued, unless the party applying therefor was then a defendant in the action, and an infant not under coverture; or of unsound mind; or a prisoner who did not appear by his attorney—in which cases an appeal may be brought up unless it is necessary to a correct decision of the case, nor will small records that can be copied with little expense be brought up. Rainey v. Rainey, 144 Ky. 502, 139 S. W. 737.

(4) The transmission of original papers to the Court of Appeals will not be ordered unless it is shown that the case is within this section; in such case the application for the order must identify the papers so that they may be identified in the order. Smith v. Berry, 159 Ky. 721, 169 S. W. 478.

(5) The record of an action not made a part of the record of the appealed case in the circuit court cannot be made a part of the record on appeal. Salyer v. Union Bank, 149 Ky. 847, 150 S. W. 14.

(6) A subpoena duces tecum was awarded for original exhibits filed with certain depositions; but was refused as to the depositions. Yenawine v. Tyerete-Conerete Pro. Co., 156 Ky. 747, 161 S. W. 1127.

# § 744.

(1) Costs. An attorney prosecuting an appeal for a non-resident defendant is not liable for costs. Christmas v. Russell, 2 Met. 112.

(2) Appellants may be required to give security for costs where they would if plaintiffs be so required. Paducah Hotel Co. v. Long, 92 Ky. 278, 13 R. 531, 17 S. W. 853; Horton v. Sherrell Lumber Co., 144 Ky. 508, 139 S. W. 737; Bracken v. Dinning, 140 Ky. 348, 131 S. W. 19.

# § 745.

(1) Appeal granted by clerk of Appellate Court dates from the filing with him of a copy of the judgment. Jones v. Finnell, 8 Bush 25; but party must make application to have appeal granted, merely filing copy of judgment is not sufficient. Wright v. L. & E. R. R., 111 Ky. 630, 23 R. 952, 61 S. W. 675.

May be granted by clerk of Court of Appeals whether or not the appeal granted in lower court is dismissed. Walston v. City, 23 R. 1852, 66 S. W. 385.

(3) Cross-appeal may be granted after expiration of two years from rendition of judgment appealed from. E. L. & B. S. R. R. v. Cattlesburg Water Co., 110 Ky. 175, 22 R. 1632, 61 S. W. 47.

# Infants-limitation.

Where a decree was rendered at the joint instance of several parties, some of whom were adults and some infants, one of the infants could not alone prosecute an appeal when the right of all the others to appeal had been barred. The right of appeal in such case being barred as to one was barred as to all. Helm v. Bentley, 1 Met. 510. Nor can the wife appeal from a joint judgment against herself and husband when his right to appeal is barred. Farlee v. Rodes, 11 Bush 365. See further as to parties who may appeal. Combs v. Combs, 141 Ky. 389, 138 S. W. 629.

(4a) An appeal from a judgment selling under Civil Code, sec. 489, the undivided rights of two infants, and confirming the sale was taken when one of them had been of age more than two years; the appeal was not barred as to the other infant. Hays v. Wicker, 161 Ky. 706, 171 S. W. 447.

(5) Infants-appeal by. An infant may appeal at any time before arriving at the age of twenty-one, but such appeal will be a bar to any appeal after arriving at age. Moss v. Hall, 79 Ky. 40, 3 R. 89; and see notes to sec. 391.

(6) Guardian ad litem may prosecute an appeal in the name of and for the infants, although the statutory guardian has accepted the amount adjudged in their favor by the circuit court. Reed v. Bridge Co., 8 Bush 69.

(6a) An appeal taken in the name of an infant by his guardian ad litem more than two years after the rendition of the judgment appealed from, was dismissed by the Court of Appeals. The question was raised by motion and by answer. Parks v. Barnes, 173 Ky. 589, 191 S. W. 447.

The tax on an appeal shall be [two dollars.]*

(6b) An infant may sue out and prosecute an appeal more than two years after the rendition of the judgment, although the guardian ad litem's right to sue out an appeal in the name of the infant is barred. Shaw v. Shaw's Admr., 174 Ky. 398, 192 S. W. 524.

(Gc) An appeal taken by a guardian ad litem was dismissed. (On notion of the infants by a next friend, the order was set aside and an amended statement filed; and the appeal was prosecuted in the name of the infants by the next friend. Webb v. Webb's Gdn., 176 Ky. 96, 195 S. W. 96.

Limitation must be pleaded. Where an appeal is sought to be dismissed on the ground that it is barred by limitation, the statute must be pleaded. A motion to dismiss will not be considered. Riley v. Reed, 13 Bush 411.

(7a) Limitation. An appeal was granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals within two years after the rendition of the judgment, but without a copy of the judgment being filed. The copy was filed after the expiration of the two years. The appeal was barred by time. It is intimated that a filing of the copy within the two years would have corrected the mistake. Nickels v. Collins, 153 Ky. 219, 154 S. W. 1090.

(7b) An appeal taken on November 17, 1913, from a judgment rendered November 18, 1911, was not barred by limitation. The word "year" embraces the whole of a leap year. Rice v. Blair, 158 Ky. 680, 166 S. W. 180.

(7c) A dismissal of an appeal by the Court of Appeals does not prevent the suing out of another within the two years. Rice v. Blair, 158 Ky. 680, 166 S. W. 180.

(7d) Where an appellant who does not show himself to be within the exceptions contained in sec. 745, fails to file his transcript of record within two years from the overruling of the motion for a new trial, his appeal will be dismissed. Monroe v. Brown, 166 Ky. 89, 178 S. W. 1169.

# (8) Motion for new trial

Suspends judgment and limitation does not begin to run until it is disposed of. Reiser v. Southern P. & L. Co., 112 Ky. 524, 23 R J832, 66 S. W. 286.

# (9) Time-how computed.

It was held under a statute providing that an appeal should not be granted "except in three years after the judgment," that the day on which judgment was rendered must be counted. Chiles v. Smith, 13 B. M. 461; Beavers v. Bowen, 111 Ky. 603, 23 R. 826, 64 S. W. 494; and see for a full discussion of this question, Board of Council v. Farmers Bank, 105 Ky. 811, 20 R. 1635, 49 S. W. 811.

(9a) An unsigned judgment is no judgment; an appeal taken within two years from time a judgment was signed was in time, although it had been spread upon the order book long before. Interstate Petroleum Co. v. Farris, 159 Ky. 820, 169 S. W. 535.

# (10) Transcript—time to file, see sec. 738 and notes.

This section not applicable to. Duf v. Du, 103 Ky. 348, 20 R. 52, 45 S. W. 102.

Extension of time in behalf of infant defendants does not apply to. Harris v. Caperton, 141 Ky. 74, 132 S. W. 157.

# (13) Infant may appeal within one year after reaching twenty-one to have corrected error that appears on face of record.

Leavell v. Carter, 112 S. W. 1118; see further sec. 391, and notes.

# (14) Attorney representing deceased appellant may file copy of judgment and pray an appeal.

Davis v. Catlettsburg Water Co., 136 Ky. 66, 123 S. W. 335.

# (15) Failure of statement to give names of all parties is not ground for dismissal of appeal-practice.

Buchannon v. Boyd, 131 Ky. 433, 120 S. W. 295.

# § 746. (1) Tax on appeal.

It is the duty of the clerk to require the tax to be paid before he grants appeal or files the record, but his failure to require payment of tax will not be cause for dismiss.

# § 747 [8soj ["Supersedeas to stay proceedings—practise as to injunc- tions.]

An appeal shall not stay proceedings on the judgment unless a supersedeas be issued. The provisions of the Civil Code concerning supersedeas on appeals shall not apply to judgments granting, modifying, perpetuating or dissolving injunctions. # §747. (1 Cross-appeal-supersedeas.

Appellee who prosecutes a cross-appeal may supersede the judgment on his cross-appeal, but the bond must be approved by the court and not the clerk. Cov. Transfer Co. v. Piel, 9 R. 665. 6 S. W. 122; and where appellee supersedes judgment and prevents appellant from enjoying benefit of his judgment he is not entitled to the ten per cent damages ordinarily allowed. Cov. Transfer Co. v. Piel, 87 Kv. 267, 10 R. 146, 8 S. W. 449; when judgment is affirmed on original and reversed on cross-appeal, appellee is not entitled to damages. Wade v. First Nat. Bank, 11 Bush 697, but appellant is entitled to damages if judgment on cross-appeal is affirmed. O'Connor v. Henderson Bridge Co., 95 Ky. 633, 16 R. 244, 27 S. W. 251, 983.

(2) Discharge of supersedeas. Where an appeal is prayed in the lower court, and a supersedeas bond executed in that court and an order of supersedeas issued, if that appeal is abandoned and an appeal granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals, the appellee is entitled, upon motion made in the Court of Appeals, to have the supersedeas discharged with damages. City v. Elrod, 14 Bush 216, and so if appeal prayed in lower court is not prosecuted in time. Fuson v. Lambdin, 23 R. 840, 64 S. W. 448; and see L. & N. R. R. v. Lucas, 27 R. 769, 86 S. W. 682.

(3) Effect of supersedeas - a supersedeas is not retroactive; it only preserves the condition of affairs as they existed at the time it was issued. Thompson v. Thompson, 24 R. 645, 69 S. W. 714; and after a judgment has been superseded no further steps can be taken under it. Webber v. Tanuer, 23 R. 1107, 64 S. W. 741; Hey v. Harding, 25 R. 1454, 78 S. W. 136; Townsend v. Gorin, 144 Ky. 671, 139 S. W. 865; Gardner v.

order suspending, modifying or continuing the injunction during the pendency of the appeal, upon such terms as to bond or otherwise as may be proper for the security of the rights of the opposite party. Either party, within twenty days after the entry of such order, may take a transcript of the record, or all parts thereof appertaining to the injunction, and upon reasonable notice in writing to the opposite party, move the Court of Appeals, or, if in vacation, any judge thereof, to revise the order of the lower court, and finally determine how far the injunction shall be suspended, modified or continued pending the appeal. Pending such application to the Court of Appeals or judge thereof, but not longer than for twenty days, the status existing immediately before the entry of the judgment appealed from shall be maintained, and the lower court shall so provide in.

Continental Ins. Co., 3I R. 69, 101 S. W. 9l1; United States F. & G. Co. v. Jones, 111'S. W. 298.

# (3a)

A supersedeas suspends the efficacy of a judgment. Hall v. Smith-McKenney Co., 162 Ky. 159, 172, S. W. 125.

# (3b)

A trustee made payments pursuant to a judgment which was not superseded, but which was afterward reversed. He was protected as to payments made to third parties, but it was held that, under the facts of the case, certain payments made to his brother were in effect made to himself; as to those payments he was not protected. Angel v. Wood, 153 Ky. 195, 154 S. W. 1103.

# (4)

Whether or not the appeal is properly prosecuted is a question for the Court of Appeals to determine, and until it has passed on the question the supersedeas stays proceedings on judgment. Daugherty v. Ringo, 1 R. 272.

# (5)

After a supersedeas bond has been executed and supersedeas issued, the appellee has no power to bring suit on the judgment or otherwise enforce its collection pending the appeal. His remedy is on the bond. Johnson v. Williams, 82 Ky. 45, 5 R. 733.

# (6)

Injunction - mandamus. It was held in Smith v. W. U. T. Co., 83 Ky. 269, 7 R. 255; K. & I. Bridge Co. v. Krieger, 91 Ky. 625, 13 R. 219, 16 S. W. 824: and E. R. R. Co. v. Ashland R. Co., 94 Ky. 478, 15 R. 258, 22 S. W. 855; that where an injunction was dissolved the execution of a supersedeas bond and service of the order left the injunction in full force. See now sec. 747 as amended; and King v. Telford, 100 Ky. 564, 18 R $78, 38 S. W. 888, and note 12; Davis v. Connolly, 104 Ky. 87, 20 R. 411, 46 S. W. 614; and the execution of a writ of mandamus may be stayed by supersedeas. Atchison v. Lucas, 83 Ky. 451, 7 R. 424; Wyatt v. Ryan, 23 R. 1457, 65 S. W. 129. Application to the Court of Appeals to revise order of circuit court must be made within twenty days. Jackson v. Hardin, 27 R. 740, 86 S. W. 530.

# (6a)

Upon an application to a judge of the Court of Appeals under this section, the applicant should produce a transcript of so much of the record as is material to the injunction. Ky. Coal & Timber Dev. Co. v. Carroll Hardwood Lumber, 151 Ky. 281, 151 S. W. 689.

# (7)

Judgment in personam and in rem cross-appeal. Where in an action to enforce a lien on land judgment is rendered for the debt and for a sale of the property, if the entire judgment is superseded the plaintiff is entitled to damages upon the affirmance of the personal judgment, although the judgment for the sale of the property is reversed. Leopold v. Furber, 84 Ky. 214, 8 R. 198, 1 S. W. 404: Laughlin v. Coakley, 19 R. H107, 40 S. W. 248. When the judgment is affirmed on the original and reversed on the cross-appeal, the appellee is not entitled to damages. Wade v. First Nat. Bank, 11 Bush 697.

# (8)

Order of supersedeas must issue. Damages will not be awarded on the affirmance of the judgment unless an order of supersedeas is issued. The judgment is not superseded by the execution of the bond, nor until the order of supersedeas has issued. Reed v. Lander, 5 Bush 598; Jones v. Green, 12 Bush 127; Asher v. Cornett, 108 S. W. 242, 32 R. 1173; and in action on a supersedeas bond it is necessary to allege that a supersedeas was issued. Hoskins v. Southern Nat. Bk., 24 R. 2250, 73 S. W. 786:

the judgment upon the request of either party. # § 748 [887] [Supersedeas bond—conditions of.]

A supersedeas shall not be issued until the appellant cause to be executed before the clerk of the court which rendered the judgment, or the clerk of the Court of Appeals, by one or more sufficient sureties, to be approved by such clerk, a bond to the effect that the appellant shall pay to appellee all costs and damages that shall be adjudged against the appellant on the appeal: also, that

O. & N. R. R. v. Barclay, 102 Ky. 16, 19 R. 997, 43 S. W. 177.

# (9)

The law does not require any record of the issual of the order of supersedeas, and when the bond has been executed, unless it appears that no order of supersedeas was issued, the presumption will be that one was issued. and this presumption will not be overcome by the certificate of the clerk that he does not remember to have issued the order.

Whitchead v. Boorom, 7 Bush 399; United States F. & G. Co. v. Boyd, 99 S. W. 35, 29 R. 599; Stewart v. Blue Grass Canning Co., 133 Kv. 118, 121 S. W. 609.

# (10)

Public officers - In Swceney v. Coulter, 22 R. 399, supersedeas bonds were modified so as to permit the appellee to perform necessary public business pending the appeal.

# (11)

Sureties insufficient - this fact does not authorize supersedeas to be ignored. A rule may be awarded requiring additional surety. Durham v. Straight, 26 R. 1147, 83 S. W. 581; sec. 750. As to liability of sureties in new bond, see Dowling v. Walker, 27 R. 928, 87 S. W. 281.

# (12)

Injunction granted on final judgment is not suspended by execution of supersedeas bond. Barone v. Mosely. 143 Kv. 812, 137 S. W. 531; Stratton v. Merriwether, 147 Ky. 577, 144 S. W. 1083; see further, Bell v. City of Lex., 124 Kv. 463, 99 S. W. 344, 30 R. 609; it can only be suspended in manner pointed out in this section-cases supra.

# (12a)

The provisions of Code, sec. 747, as to judgments granting, perpetuating, modifying or dissolving injunctions, do not apply to other judgments, and do not empower the Court of Appeals to grant an injunction. 'I'hompson v. Haden, 177 Ky. 841, 198 S. W. 231.

# (13)

Duty of clerk to issue supersedeas when bond is executed and damages will be awarded although no order was issued until second appeal was granted as time to file transcript under first appeal had expired. United States F. & G. Co. v. Citizens Nat. Bank, 143 Kv. 699, 137 S. W. 240; and see Torbitt v. Middlesboro Grocery Co., 147 Kv. 343, 144 S. W. 16.

# (14)

Contempt. Power of Court of Appeals to punish for contempt if injunction issued by lower court. Stratton v. Merriwether, 147 Ky. 577, 144 S. W. 1083; Barone v. Moscley, 144 Ky. 294, 137 S. W. 1048.

# (15)

Action on injunction bond can not be brought pending suspension of judgment by supersedeas. United States F. & G. Co. v. Herzig, 124 S. W. 279.

# (16)

Bond must be in record or damages can not go. Monarch Co. v. Farmers & Traders Bank, 20 R. 1275, 106 Ky. 206; Breathitt Coal Co. v. Patrick, 144 Ky. 601, 139 S. W. 790.

# §748.

# (1)

Appellant is not required to sign bond. Gilpin v. Hord, 85 Ky. 213, 8 R. 904, 3 S. W. 143.

# (1a)

Bond executed before granting appeal. A clerk has no power to accept a supersedeas bond before an appeal has been granted, but a supersedeas bond may be executed by a surety before the

Damages. Where a judgment for money which is superseded does not bear interest, the plaintiff, in an action on the bond, is not entitled to interest by way of damages; he is only entitled to the ten per cent damages allowed by sec. 764. L. & N. R. R. v. Sharp, 91 Ky. 411, 12 R. 973, 16 S. W. 86; nor does bond cover attorney fees. Welch v. Welch, 106 Ky. 406, 20 R. 1990, 50 S. W. 687; Dowling v. Walker, 27 R. 928, 87 S. W. 281, 120 Ky. 528; and see further, notes to sec. 761.

(2a) The dismissal of an appeal was held to amount to an affirmance of the judgment appealed from, within the meaning of the appeal bond, rendering the surety liable for the amount of the judgment. III. Surety Co. v. Hendrick, 170 Ky. 347, 183 S. W. 1125.

(3) 'Obligors in bond-liability of. In a contest between two creditors over a fund due their common debtor, the obligors in bond of creditor who superseded judgment directing payment of fund to the other creditor are liable for amount of judgment. Mahlman v. Williams, 89 KV. 282, 11 R. 503. 12 S. W. 335.

(3a) The obligors in a supersedeas bond were held liable on a judgment which the Court of Appeals ordered to be rendered. White v. White, 151 Ky. 96, 151 S. W. 1.

(4) After an execution issued on a replevin bond had been levied on sufficient property of the principal to pay it, the judgment was superseded, and the collection of the execution stayed. The judgment being affirmed, the surety in the replevin bond was compelled to pay it, the principal being then insolvent. Held that he could recover the amount.

Title XVIII Supersedeas on Appeals.
# § 749 [88s, 880] [Clerk who shall take bond and issue supersedeas.]

1. The bond must be executed before the clerk of the court rendering the judgment, if the appeal be granted by that court. In other cases, it must be executed before the clerk of the Court of Appeals.
2. [Provisions as to supersedeas.] The clerk of the court rendering the judgment shall issue the supersedeas, if the bond be executed before him before the expiration of the time for filing a copy of the record in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals pursuant to section 738. In other cases, it must be issued by the clerk of the Court of Appeals.

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms Nos. 49, 464, 465. See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 88, 90, 92.

In the absence of an order allowing damages, the sureties are liable for the judgment, interest and costs. Gilpin v. Hord 85 Ky. 213, 8 R. 904, 3 S. W. 143.

1. Personal representative. A personal representative is not bound personally on a supersedeas bond executed by him in his representative capacity, and is bound only to pay the damages and costs of the appeal and the judgment in case of affirmance out of the assets which have or may come to his hands in the due course of administration. Fitzpatrick v. Todd, 79 Ky. 524, 3 R. 293.
2. Discharge in bankruptcy of principal in bond pending appeal does not discharge sureties. Slusher v. Hopkins, 30 R. 257, 97 S. W. 1128.

# § 749. (1) Issuing of supersedeas.

A supersedeas cannot be issued by the clerk of the court rendering the judgment unless an appeal was granted by that court. Jones v. Green, 12 Bush 127.

# (2) The law does not require that any record be kept of the issuance of a supersedeas.

Whitehead v. Booron, 7 Bush 399.

See further notes to secs. 747, 748 and L. & N. R. R. v. Lucas, 120 Ky. 359, 27 R. 769, 86 S. W. 682.

# (4) Bond and supersedeas-what clerk may take and issue.

Asher v. Cornett, K. 569, 104 S. W. 347; Turner v. Johnson, 106 Ky. 460, 20 R. 2009, 50 S. W. 675; Dowling v. Walker, 27 R. 925, 87 S. W. 281; Maret v. Sanders, 141 Ky. 366, 132 S. W. 552.

# (5) A judgment for money for less than $500 can be superseded only by the execution of a supersedeas bond before the clerk of the Court of Appeals.

Childers v. Ratliff, 164 Ky. 123, 175 S. W. 25.

# (6) A circuit court clerk took a supersedeas bond and issued a supersedeas although no appeal was granted by the circuit court during the term.

The Court of Appeals discharged the supersedeas, but held the supersedeas void and refused to award damages. Dougherty v. Cent. Tr. Co., Ex., 155 Ky. 380, 159 S. W. 777.

# (7) A supersedeas bond executed before the clerk of the court rendering the judgment when no appeal had been granted by that court imposed no obligation.

# §750-752] Supersedeas on Appeals.

# § 750 [8eo] [Supersedeas may be discharged if bond insufficient—new bond.]

The appellee may move the Court of Appeals, if in session; or, on reasonable notice to the appellant, may move a judge of said court, in vacation, to discharge the supersedeas; and, if the court or judge shall consider the sureties insufficient, or the bond substantially defective in securing the rights of the appellee, the court or judge shall make an order discharging the supersedeas, unless a good bond with sufficient sureties be forthwith executed. The order, if made by a judge, shall be in writing and signed by him; upon the filing of which, or of a certified copy of order, if made by the court, in the clerk's office of the inferior court, executions and other proceedings for enforcing the judgment may be immediately taken, as if no supersedeas had been issued. But another supersedeas may be issued by the clerk of the Court of Appeals upon the execution of a new and lawful bond, with sufficient sureties, as hereinbefore provided.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 92.

# § 751 [801] [Part of judgment may be superseded.]

The supersedeas may be issued to stay proceedings on a part of a judgment, and in such case the bond shall be varied so as to secure the part superseded.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 90.

# § 752 [802] [Supersedeas defined.]

The supersedeas is a written order, signed by the clerk, commanding the appellee and all others to stay proceedings on the judgment or order.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 49. See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 88, 90.

The Court of Appeals had no jurisdiction of an appeal from the judgments rendered. Milliken v. Hatten, 177 Ky. 31, 197 S. W. 511.

§ 751. Superseding of part of judgment. An appeal was granted by a circuit court from a judgment for more than $500. A portion of the judgment less than $500 was superseded. On a dismissal of the appeal damages were awarded on the portion superseded. Colvert v. Wilder, 180 Ky. 97, 201 S. W. 449.

§ 752. Effect of supersedeas. A judgment directing the surrender of certain leased premises within a certain time was superseded within that time. Appellant was not guilty of contempt by continuing to occupy and use the premises. Thompson v. Haden, 177 Ky. 841, 198 S. W. 231. See notes to secs. 747.749.

# § 753 [89s] [Appeals-when stand for trial.]

Appeals shall stand for trial during the first term twenty days before which the transcript is filed in the clerk's office, if—

1. The appeal be granted by the inferior court; or,
2. The appearance of the appellee be entered five days before the day on which the case is set for trial on the docket; or,
3. The appellee be summoned actually twenty days, or constructively thirty days, before said day.

4. [In any case where the appeal is granted by the inferior court or where the appellee has been summoned, if the appeal is granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals, the Court of Appeals may, at any time in its discretion, after the transcript is filed, on motion of either party upon reasonable notice to the adverse party, order the appeal docketed, advanced and set for hearing upon a day to be fixed by the court.] (Words in brackets added by Act of March 14, 1912, c. 76, p. 231.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Xy. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 108, 110, 120.

# § 753.

(1) Appeals do not stand for trial unless the transcript is filed twenty days before the commencement of the term. Meacham v. Dem. State Com., 24 R. 1340, 71 S. W. 447.

(2) Election cases will be docketed and advanced. Ky. Stat., sec. 1596a, subsec 12. Graham v. Graham, 113 Ky. 743, 24 R. 20, 67 S. W. 1004.

(3) Prosecution of action pending appeal is not allowable. Ault v. Apperson, 18 R. 472, 37 S. W. 61.

(4) Party is not entitled to have appeal advanced and submitted out of time where other party objects. Stratton v. Merriwether, 144 Ky. 577, 144 S. W. 1083. But see now amendment to this section.

(5) A case will not be advanced merely because parties so consent. Beavers v. Nelson, 151 Ky. 487, 152 S. W. 242.

Trial and Decision

# §754 [804] [Docket arranged, printed, distributed by clerk.]

1. The clerk shall arrange the appeals upon the docket, setting a proper number for each day of the term; and in arranging them may have due regard to the convenience of litigants, in placing together the appeals from the several judicial districts.
2. He shall, at least fifteen days before each term, furnish a copy of the docket to the public printer, who shall immediately print not less than five hundred copies thereof, and send one of said copies to the clerk of each circuit court, chancery court and court of common pleas, for public use; and the claim of the printer shall be approved and certified by the court, and be paid as other claims upon the treasury.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 110.

# §755 [895] [Cross-appeal—how taken.]

1. [Entry on records.] The appellee may obtain a cross-appeal, at any time before trial, by an entry on the records of the Court of Appeals.

(6) Motion of appellant to dismiss without prejudice. The Court of Appeals overruled the motion of appellant to dismiss his appeal without prejudice. The judgment from which the appeal was prayed had been rendered on a new trial after a reversal of a previous judgment. Doherty v. First Nat. Bank, 160 Ky. 64.

# §755.

1. Cross-appeal. If a cross-appeal is desired the appellee may file his schedule in the court below, either before or after the schedule is filed by the appellant. No schedule is required when the entire record is copied. 'L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Brice, 83 Kv. 210. 7 R. 180; but party taking cross-appeal has right to file, schedule and cause entire record copied, and if he fail to do this it will be presumed that omitted record will sustain judgment against him. Adams v. Benent, 96 Ky. 334, 16 R. 676, 29 S. W. 22.
2. A cross-appeal cannot be considered when the parties to be affected by it are not before the court. Daviess Co. v. Howard, 13 Bush 101, and see note 11.
3. In an ordinary action if the appellee desires to avail himself on cross-appeal of errors committed by trial court he must file motion and grounds for a new trial in lower court. L. & N. R. R. v. Whitehead, 24 R. 2314, 73 S. W. 1128.
4. "The legal effect of the affirmance on the original appeal, there being a reversal of the judgment on the cross-appeal, was not to leave the judgment unreversed, but to decide that there was no error to the prejudice of the appellant, and that he was not entitled to a reversal." In such case the appellee is not entitled to damages on the bond executed by the appellant. Wade v. First National Bank, 11 Bush 697.
5. Cross-appeal is only allowable in behalf of the appellee against the appellant and not against a co-appellee. Smith v. Bank, 1 Met. 575; Gaar v. Banking Co., 11 Bush 190; Home Ins. Co. v. Gaddis, 3 R. 159; MeKay v. Maves, 17 R. 827, 32 S. W. 606; Marion Nat. Bank v. Phillips, 18 R. 159, 35 S. W. 910; Asher v. Helton, 31 R. 9, 101 S. W. 350; Matney v. Edmonds, 179 Ky. 243, 200 S. W. 365.
6. When either party appeals from a final judgment his adversary may have a cross-appeal for the purpose of correcting any error to his prejudice or any interlocutory order that controlled or influenced the judgment, but he cannot have a cross-appeal from a judgment wholly distinct from the judgment appealed from. Brown v. Vancleave, 86 Ky. 581, 9 R. 593, 6 S. W. 25; see also note 12.
7. Cross-appeal cannot be granted by circuit court. Murphy v. Blandford, 11 R. 125, 11 S. W. 715; it must be granted by Court of Appeals. Mud v. Mullican, 11 R. 417, 12 S. W. 263, 385; Hancock v. Hancock, 24 R. 664, 69 S. W. 757; Hilton v. Com., 127 Ky. 486, 105 S. W. 956, 32 R. 377; and may be prosecuted within two years from rendition of judgment. Second Nat. Bk. v. Fitzpatrick, 11 Ky. 228, 23 R. 610, 63 S. W. 459.
8. Appellee prosecuting cross-appeal may supersede judgment on his cross-appeal.

A judgment of a chancellor was reversed as against the weight of the evidence. Coomes Bros. v. Grigsby & Co., 151 Ky. 394, 151 S. W. 943.

Costs on appeal. As to discretion of court in awarding, see Ky. Stat., sec. 891, and Counts v. Kitchen, 87 Ky. 47, 9 R. 909, 7 S. W. 538; Hoskins v. Morton, 27 R. 529, 85 S. W. 742. As to effect of tender before appeal taken, see Ky. Stat., sec. 890.
