Error must be substantial. To authorize a reversal the error complained of must affect the substantial rights of the appellant, and that it does so affect them must as clearly appear as the error itself. Williams v. Rodgers, 14 Bush 776; K. & I. Bridge Co. v. Montgomery, 24 R. 167, 67 S. W. 1008, 68 S. W. 1097; Aetna Life Ins. Co. v. Crabtree, 146 Ky. 368, 142 S. W. 690; Garrett v. Creekmore, 121 Ky. 250, 89 S. W. 166, 28 R. 211; Claypool v. Continental Casualty Co., 129 Ky. 682, 112 S. W. 835; C. & O. R. Co. v. Conley, 136 Ky. 601, 124 S. W. 861; I. C. R. Co. v. Mayes, 142 Ky. 382, 134 S. W. 436; L. & N. R. Co. v. Hardy, 142 Ky. 468, 134 S. W. 899.

And the Court of Appeals will presume that the decision of the lower court is correct, unless it affirmatively appears from the record that the decision complained of is erroneous. Hufaker v. Bank, 13 Bush 644; Bowman v. Holloway, 14 Bush 426; and see further, secs. 134, 338, providing that errors not substantial will be disregarded.

# (4b)

Plaintiff brought suit to recover damages for personal injuries and stated.

622

Title XVIIII Trial and Decision. [§ 755

his cause of action in the alternative un-

the Federal Enployers' Liability Act and under the common law; a motion to lect was overruled; but at the conclu-

sion of the evidence the court ruled that the case must be prosecuted under the common law. The error in failing to sus-

tain the motion to elect was not cause for reversal. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Moore, 156 Ky. 708, 161 S. W. 1129.

c Where the record shows that the appellant has no interest in the result of the determination of the question about which the appeal is taken, the ap-

peal will be dismissed. Thomas, Admr. v. Thomas, 162 Ky. 630, 172 S. W. 1054.

(4d) An instruction erroneously authorized the jury to find punitive dam-

ages. It was plain from the amount of damages awarded that the damages were merely compensatory. The judgment was affirmed. St. Bernard Min. Co. v. Ashley, 164 Ky. 416, 175 S. W. 626.

(e) The circuit court erroneously held that the burden of proof in a certain case was on the plaintiff. This upon the facts of this case was not prejudicigl error and was not ground for reversal. Ditto v. Hopkins, 164 Ky. 412, 175 S. W. 658.

(4f) A jury after a case had been sub-

mitted caine into court and asked fur-

ther instruetions. The court gave an in-

struction on the point inquired of, which was correct. This was not ground for reversal. L. & E. Ry. Co. v. Boatright, 164 Ky. 374, 175 S. W. 648.

(4g) The refusal of the regular judge to vacate the bench on the trial of a contested election in this case was not prejudicial error. Potter v. Campbell, 159 Ky. 328. 167 S. W. 404.

(4h) A guardian ad litem appointed for three infants filed a report in which he omitted their names, but by mistake inserted the names of three other in-

fant defendants; afterward he was ap-

pointed for the last named three and filed a report setting out their names. The court declined to set aside a sale by reason of this omission. Thomson's Gdn. v. Thomson, 151 Ky. 296. 151 S. W. 658.

(4i) If in an action by two plaintiffs a judgment is rendered in favor of one of them for the amount owing by the defendants, the judgment will not be re-

versed at the instance of the defendant on the ground that a part of the sum adjudged should have been adjudged in favor of the other plaintiff. Olive Hill Fire Brick Co. v. Mullins, 153 Ky. 293, 155 S. W. 372.

(4j) If, in an action against two de-

fendants, one has not been brought be-

fore the court in any way, and a judg-

ment is rendered against both defend-

ants, the one who is before the court is not prejudiced thereby. The judgment against the other defendant is void Rosenberg v. Dahl, 162 Ky. 92, 172 S. W. 113.

(4k) In an action for injuries sus-

tained by being thrown against a mail crane, the plaintiff alleged that he was a workman on the train. In fact, he was not a workman on the train, but a sec-

tion hand. Upon the entire record, how-

ever, this error was not prejudicial nor was there a fatal variance between the pleading and the proof. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Jesse, 159 Ky. 450, 167 S. W. 407.

(5) A judgment against a divorced wife, and also against her late husband, for her debt; as to him, to be lovied on any property received from her during marriage, although erroneous as to him is not prejudicial to her. Joyes v. Hamilton, 10 Bush 544.

(6) An error in not directing the com-

missioner to sell only enough land to pay the debt will not be available when the entire tract sold for less than the amount of the debt. Doughty v. Moss, 1 Bush 161.

(7) Although a deposition was errone-

ously rejected, the court will consider the effect of it, and if, when admitted, it would not change the result, the case will not be reversed on that ground. Wal-

rath v. Viley, 1 Bush 266; Millett v. Parker, 2 Met. 608; Tudor v. Tudor, 17 B. M. 383. So with evidence rejected. Smith v. Bank, 1 Met. 575.

(8) The refusal of the court to allow a party to testify will not authorize a reversal when it does not appear that he would have stated any fact beneficial to himself. Francis v. Com., 3 Bush 4.

(9) Refusal of court to pass upon ex-

ceptions to depositions on the ground that they are incompetent and irrelevant will not warrant a reversal, when the depositions are competent and relevant. Root v. Merriwether, 8 Bush 397, Nor is an error in overruling a demurrer to a pleading prejudicial when no instruc-

tion based on the pleading is given. Beaven v. Phillips, 83 Ky. 88, 7 R. J.

(10) An error in refusing to strike out irrelevant matter in a pleading will not warrant a reversal unless it appear that the party was prejudiced by the error. Buckles v. Lambert, 4 Met. 330.

(11) An error of five dollars in amount of judgment is too small to au-

thorize a reversal. German Ins. Co. v. Landram, 88 Ky. 433, 10 R. 1039, 1F S.

# Trial and Decision

§756] W. 367, 592; as is an error of thirty-five dollars in a case involving many thousands. Seiler v. Northern Bank, 86 Ky. 128. 9 R. 497. 5 S. W. 530. Nor will an error of six dollars and fifty cents in the judgment against the appellant justify reversal. Moore v. Estes, 79 Ky. 282, 2 R. 256; C., N. O. & T.P. Ry. Co. v. Smith & Johnston, 165 Ky. 235, 176 S. W. 1013.

# (lla)

The Court of Appeals will not reverse because the court below granted a new trial unless there was an abuse of discretion. Nantz v. Sizemore, 156 Ky. 539, 161 S. W. 552; MeClintic-Marshall Const. Co. v. Eckman, 153 Kv. 704, 156 S. W. 382; Norvell v. Paducah Box Basket Co., 157 Ky. 703, 163 S. W. 1106.

# (llb)

After plaintiff has rested and a motion for a peremptory instruction has been made it is in the discretion of the court to reopen the case, and permit the plaintiff to introduce further evidence. Scheben v. Geo. Wiedemann Brew. Co. 161. Ky. 413, 170 S. W. 948.

# (llc)

A judgment will not be reversed for a trivial excess in the amount thereof where the cost of a new trial would exceed the deduction to which the appellant would be entitled. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Veatch, 162 Ky. 136, 172 S. W. 89.

# (lld)

An employee recovered damages for injuries while engaged in interstate commerce; but the case was tried in the circuit court without reference to the Federal Employers' Liability Act. The case was remanded for a new trial under that statute. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Goode. 155 Ky. 153, 159 S. W. 695.

# (lle)

A judgment was reversed because of an erroneous instruction, although the appellant did not ask a correct instruction on the same point, L. & E. Ry. Co. v. Crawford, 155 Ky. 723, 160 S. W. 267: C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Dwyer's Admx., 157 Kv. 590, 163 S. W. 752.

# (llf)

Exceptions to executor's settlement. On an appeal from a judgment settling the accounts of an executor, the appellant is not entitled to a reversal unless he points out specific errors. Costigan v. Kraus, 158 Ky. 818, 166 S. W. 755.

# (llg)

A court may, in its discretion, refuse to allow defendant to amend his answer and set up an entirely new defense after the case has been called for trial a second time and continued. City of Lou. v. Lausberg, 161 Ky. 361, 170 S. W. 962.

# (llh)

The filing of an amended rejoinder after evidence was heard was not an abuse of discretion and was not ground for a continuance in this case. L. & N. R. Co. v. Tuggle's Admr., 151 Ky. 409, 152 S. W. 270.

# (lli)

Exceptions to a deposition were erroneously sustained. Time was given to retake the deposition. This leave of court was not acted on by the party. It was error not to allow the deposition to be read on the trial. Fireren's Ins. Co. v. McGill, 164 Ky. 621, 176 S. W. 27.

# (llj)

Refusal of continuance. An application for a continuance was accompanied by the affidavit of counsel that the just and proper effect of the evidence of the absent witness could not in a reasonable degree be had without a personal examination in court. The circuit court refused a continuance. Upon the whole of the record it was held that the substantial rights of the party asking the continuance were prejudiced. Mad., H. & E. R. Co. v. Allen, 152 Ky. 706, 154 S. W. 5.

# (llk)

Swearing of jury presumed. On an objection urged as ground for reversal that the order impaneling the jury does not show that the jury were sworn, held. although the record does not expressly show that the jury were sworn, yet as there was nothing in the record to show that they were not, the court will assume that they were. Sword v. Bower, 157 Ky. 615, 163 S. W. 733.

# (lll)

Error in bringing action of lunatic cured by pleading. Where in an action in behalf of an infant lunatic brought by his mother as next friend, the defendant pleaded that the lunatic had a committee residing in this State, the refusal of the trial court to abate the action is not prejudicial error where during the pendency of the action the lunatic dies and the mother is therefore permitted to file an amended petition and prosecute the action in her name as heir of the infant lunatic. Skidmore v. Harris, 157 Ky. 756, 164 S. W. 98.

# (llm)

Presumption where several grounds for new trial. Where there are several grounds for a new trial, one of which is insufficient, and it does not appear from the record that the trial court, in awarding the new trial, acted upon the insufficient ground, but may have awarded the new trial upon other grounds which were sufficient, the order granting the new trial will not be set aside. Nowell v. Paducah Box & Basket Co., 157 Ky. 703, 163 S. W. 1106.

# (lln)

Instructions unsupported by evidence. The giving of a certain instruction which was wholly unsupported by

Title XVIII] Trial and Decision [§ 756

evidence was harmless in this case. Major Taylor v. Harding, 182 Ky. 236.

(1lo) Judicial sale under sec. 491. It is error to adjudge a sale of land under Civil Code, sec. 491, if the title papers are not filed. Conrad v. Conrad, 152 Ky. 422, 153 S. W. 740.

(llp) Admission of improper evidence. In an action a servant against his master for injuries received by being overheated, the circuit court admitted evidence that after the injuries were sustained by plaintiff, the defendant employed three men to perform the work that had before been done by two. This was ground for reversal. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Williams, 165 Ky. 386, 176 S. W. 1180.

(llq) Erroneous instruction. A judgment was reversed because of error in an instruction as to the damages for failure to deliver on time. Fairbanks, Morse & Co. v. Carson-Muse Lumber Co., 160 Ky. 346, 169 S. W. 731.

(llr) Improper limitation of argument. On a trial of a certain action arising upon contract a limitation of the argument to ten minutes on each side was prejudicial error. L. P. Hyman & Co. v. Snyder Co., 159 Ky. 354, 167 S. W. 146.

(lls) Fine for violating void ordinance. It is error to inflict a fine for the violation of a void ordinance. United Fuel & Gas Co. v. Com., 159 Ky. 34, 166 S. W. 783.

(l2) Incompetent evidence. In an equity case the Court of Appeals will consider only the competent evidence - even if incompetent is admitted by the chancellor - and from that determine whether the judgment is erroneous or not. Wicks v. Dean, 103 Ky. 69, 19 R. 1708, 44 S. W. 397.

(l2a) Expert testimony. The introduction of expert testimony where the same was incompetent is not ground for reversal, if such testimony was introduced solely to refute and contradict incompetent and expert testimony introduced by appellant. Interstate Conl Co. v. Shelton, 160 Ky. 40, 169 S. W. 546.

(12b) Injunctions. The court on an appeal followed the rulings of an appellate judge on a motion to reinstate a temporary injunction which had been issued by a circuit clerk; in considering the motion six members of the court had concurred. Walker v. Goode, 153 Ky. 795, 156 S. W. 893; Myrick v. Morgan, 153 Ky. 795, 156 S. W. 893; Myrick v. Hogue, 153 Ky. 795, 156 S. W. 893.

(13) Joint and several judgment. A judgment against persons jointly liable is an entirety, and if void as to one is void as to all; but where it is several as to the parties it may be reversed as to one and affirmed as to another. Joyes v. Hamilton, 10 Bush 544.

(14) Judgment correct, but reason wrong. A correct judgment must be affirmed, although it may have been erroneously made to rest on a different ground by the lower court. Poor v. Robinson, 13 Bush 290; or given for a wrong or insufficient reason. Ireland v. Berryman, 3 Bush 356; American Nail Co. v. Bayless, 91 Ky. 94, 12 R. 694, 15 S. W. 10; Com. v. McCauley's Exr., 106 Ky. 450, 179 S. W. 411; Ball v. Wasioto, &c., Ry. Co., 157 Ky. 166, 162 S. W. 818.

(15) Remitter of judgment will in some cases prevent a reversal for excessive damages. City v. Gardner, 19 R. 302, 40 S. W. 779, and notes to sec. 340.

(16) Three verdicts. Where there have been three verdicts for the same party the court will not disturb the last one, although the weight of evidence is against it. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Graves, 78 Ky. 74; Sanford v. Smith, 5 Bush 471. See sec. 341. Wall v. Dimnitt, 141 Ky. 715, 133 S. W. 768.

(l6a) On the third trial of a case a verdict was rendered for defendant, and plaintiff was refused a new trial. Plaintiff's motion to substitute the judgment entered on the verdict, on the first and second trials, was overruled. The Court of Appeals reviewed the judgment and rulings of the circuit court on the first and second as well as the third trial. It appeared that the granting of each of the new trials was authorized because of errors committed by the circuit court in instructing the jury and that the proceedings on the third trial were free from error. The judgment on the last trial was affirmed. Keiner v. Collins, 161 Ky. 696, 171 S. W. 399.

(17) Verdict against evidence. The court will not reverse on the ground that the verdict is not sustained by the evidence, unless it is clearly and palpably against the weight of evidence. Sanford v. Smith, 5 Bush 471; Becker v. Crow, Bush 19s; Varble v. Bigley, 14 Bush 698; Com. v. Williams, 14 Bush 297; I. & N. R. Co. v. Parks Admr. 154 Ky. 269, 157 S. W. 27; Adams Ex. Co. v. Nat. Bk. of Middlesboro, 162 Ky. 400, 172 S. W. 660; L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Netherv, 160 Ky. 369, 169 S. W. 883; Williamson v. Bently, 158 Ky. 346, 164 S. W. 951; White Sewing Mach. Co. v. Mahoney, 150 Ky. 805, 162 S. W. 80; Music v. B. S. &

# Trial and Decision

Ky. R. Ry. Co., 163 Ky. 628, 174 S. W. 44; Middleton v. Potts, 163 Ky. 550, 174 S. W. 9; Denker Trans. Co. v. Pugh, 162 Ky. 81S. 173 S. W. 139. Stafford v. B. S. & Ky. R. Rv. Co.. 164 Kv. 582, 176 S. W. 14; Sadieville Milling Co. v. C.. N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co., 153 Ky. 55, 154 S. W. 396; Chaney v. Com., 149 Ky. 464, 149 S. W. 923; C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co. v. Brandenburg, 149 Ky. 582. 149 S. W. 988; Sizemore v. Nance, 149 Ky. 819, 149 S. W. 1126; Anderson Co. Bd. of Control v. Sou. Ry. in Ky., 152 Ky. 278, 153 S. W. 421: Sou. Rv. Co. v. Alford, J50 Kv. 808. 150'S. W. 9S5; Aetna L. Ins. Co. v. Rustin, 151 Ky. 103, 151 S. W. 366; Cas. Co. of Am. v. Rustin, 151 Kv. 103, 15l S. W. S66; Fid. & Cas. Co. of N. Y. v. Rustin 151 Kv. 103. 151 S. W. 366; Travelers Ins. Co. of Hartford, Conn., v. Rustin, 151 Ky. 103, 151 S. W. 366.

(17a) Upon an appeal it was held that a certain verdict for a plaintiff was flagrantly against the evidence. On the next trial the evidence was not improved. It was proper for the circuit court to let the case go to the jury and afterward to set aside a verdict for the plaintiff as flagrantly against the evidence. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Johnson, 15l Ky. 809, 152 S. W. 962.

(17b) In an action against a railroad company for damages resulting from a fire alleged to have been caused by the emission of sparks and cinders from a locomotive by reason of a failure to provide a proper spark arrester, a verdict based wholly upon circumstantial evidence in favor of the plaintiff, may be sustained. C. & O. Rv. Co. v. Snyder, 164 Ky. 432, 175 S. W. 640.

8) The judgment of the court, when the law and facts are submitted to the court, will not be disturbed unless flagrantly against the evidence. Helm v. Coffey, 80 Ky. 176. 3 R. 677; Russell v. M. & C. T. Co.. 13 Bush 307; Coleman v. Meade, 13 Bush 358, 12 Bush 451; 12 Bush 469.

(19) Waiver of error. No error can be taken advantage of on the appeal unless it was relied upon in the grounds for a new trial. Com. v. Williams. 14 Bush 297: McLain v. Dibble, 13 Bush 297; Alexander v. Humber, 80 Kv. 565. 9 R. 734, 6 S. W. 453; Acme Mills v. Rives, 141 Ky. 783. 133 S. W. 786. See further, notes to secs. 333, 335, 340.

(29) Unless there is a motion and grounds for a new trial, there is nothing before the court except the sufficiency of the pleadings. Helm v. Colfey. 80 Ky. 176. 3 R. 677; Henderson v. Duprec, S2 626 Ky. 678, 6 R. 702; Harper v. Harper, 10 Bush 447; see further, notes to sec. 340.

(20a) Erroneous instructions not complained of in the grounds for a new trial are no grounds for reversal. Mann Bros. v. City of Henderson, 154 Ky. 154, 156 S. W. 1063.

(20b) No error committed during a trial is available on appeal unless it has been relied on as a ground for a new trial. L. & N. R. Co. v. Com.. 154 Ky. 293, 157 S. W. 369.

(20c) Errors in rulings of the circuit court were not reviewed on appeal because no objection was made nor exception taken in the circuit court. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Scalf, 155 Ky. 273, 159 S. W. 804.

(20d) The action of the circuit court in giving a peremptory instruction will not be reviewed by the Court of Appeals in the absence of a motion for a new trial. Witt v. L. & E. Ry. Co., 158 Ky. 401, 165 S. W. 399.

(20e) An error in admitting evidence was no ground for a reversal, because not made a ground for a new trial. L. I. Ry. Co. v. Roemmele, 157 Ky. 84, 102 S. W. 547.

(20f) In a settlement suit the Court of Appeals declined to consider certain objections to an order not presented in the circuit court. Harding v. Harding. 151 Ky. 398, 152 S. W. 259.

(20g) If parol proof of title be received without objection on a trial in the circuit court, the question of the incompetency of such testimony cannot be raised for the first time on appeal. Oncy v. Lovely, 151 Ky. 651, 152 S. W. 785.

(21) In an action attacking a conveyance as fraudulent, it is too late to object for the first time in Court of Appeals that plaintiff did not have a "return of no property." Behan v. Wartield, 90Kv. 151, 11 R. 960, I3 S. W. 439: and see further as to waiver, Hare v. Anderson. 12 R. 303. 14 S. W. 210: Brown v. Vaneleave, 14 R. 821, 21 S. W. 736; and see further as to waiver by failure to take proper exception, notes to secs. 333, 335.

(21a) An objection that a city ordinance was not properly adopted cannot be made for the first time in the Court of Appeals. Creekmore v. Cent. Const. Co 157 Kv. 336, 163 S. W 194.

(21b) The Court of Appeals will not consider a federal question which was suggested for the first time in the argument of the motion for a new trial in a circuit court. L. & N. R. Co. v. Woodford. 152 Kv. 398, 153 S. W. 722.

# Title XVIII

# Trial and Decision, § 756

(2lc) An allegation of negligence in general terms will not authorize a recovery by a servant for failure of the master to use care to furnish a safe place to work or safe appliances. The failure to object to evidence as to unsafe appliances was not a waiver of this objection, where the evidence so admitted was competent on an issue in the case. *Ohio Valley C. & M. Co. v. Heine*, 159 Ky. 586, 167 S. W. 873.

(2ld) Error not pointed out in appellant's brief. The brief for appellants did not point out any error. The judgment was affirmed. *Brown v. Daniels*, 154 Ky. 267, 157 S. W. 3.

(22) Second appeal. Where facts on second appeal are same as on first and first judgment was reversed because contrary to evidence, the second will be reversed for same reason. *I. C. R. Co. v. Long*, 146 Ky. 170, 142 S. W. 212.

(22a) The opinion of the Court of Appeals on a former appeal was held to be the law of the case on a second trial. *Doherty v. First Nat. Bank, 161 Ky. 202, 170 S. W. 615; Nat. Cash Register Co. v. Williams, 161 Ky. 550, 171 S. W. 162; Mut. Ben. L. Ins. Co. v. O'Brien, 149 Ky. 514, 149 S. W. 870; Harcourt v. Redmon, 149 Ky. 612, 149 S. W. 938; Bauer Cooperage Co. v. Ewell & Smith, 149 Ky. 838, 149 S. W. 1137; Brooks v. City of Maysville, 151 Ky. 707, 152 S. W. 788; Gossett v. Ky. Mfg. Co., 153 Ky. 101, 154 S. W. 897; Mahoney v. Mentz, 153 Ky. 484, 155 S. W. 1137; Bates v. Northern C. & C. Co., 162 Ky. 459, 172 S. W. 918; Helm v. C.N.O. & T.P. Ry. Co., 156 Ky. 240, 160 S. W. 945; C., N.O. & T.P. Ry. Co. v. Padgett*, 163 Ky. 284, 173 S. W. 780.

(22b) An opinion of the Court of Appeals is the law of that case not only as to matters stated therein, but as to every matter brought before the court on the appeal and properly presented for adjudication. *New Bell Jellico Coal Co. v. Sowders*, 162 Ky. 443, 172 S. W. 914.

(22c) The mere circumstance that an instruction, although a proper one, was not given on the first trial, and therefore not considered and approved on appeal does not of itself preclude the giving of it on a second trial. *Andonique v. Carmen*, 162 Ky. 154, 172 S. W. 112.

(22d) The expression of an opinion on the part of the Court of Appeals upon an appeal as to the existence of a certain state of facts is not binding on a new trial of the case if another state of facts be shown by evidence. *L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Stewart's Admx.*, 163 Ky. 823, 174 S. W. 744.

(22e) On a former appeal the Court of Appeals ruled that a peremptory instruction should have been given; on the second trial the evidence differed materially from the evidence on the first trial. It was proper to submit the case to the jury. *L. & N. R. Co. v. Com.*, 150 Ky. 176, 150 S. W. 79.

(23) Instructions. When the court on appeal directs the instructions to be given and the evidence on second trial is substantially same as on first, the appellant cannot complain of instructions on second appeal if court followed directions. *C. & O. R. Co. v. Long, 141 Ky. 592, 133 S. W. 570; Goff v. Lowe*, 141 Ky. 799, 133 S. W. 995.

(24) Unless an instruction is disapproved on appeal it must be treated as correct on subsequent trial. *Wall v. Dimmitt*, 141 Ky. 715, 133 S. W. 768.

(25) Errors passed on or ignored on first appeal cannot be raised on second appeal. The opinion on first appeal is law of case. *Wall v. Dimmitt, 141 Ky. 715, 133 S. W. 768; Goff v. Lowe, 141 Ky. 799, 133 S. W. 995; Samuels v. Gilmore*, 142 Ky. 166, 134 S. W. 169.

(25") If on an appeal to the Court of Appeals in the opinion certain instructions given below are not criticized, they are thereby tacitly approved, and become the law of the case; but the court is not precluded from giving on a return of the case a proper instruction on another subject which was not covered by an instruction given on the other trial. *Standard Oil Co. v. Marlow*, 162 Ky. 1, 171 S. W. 436.

(26) Waiver of error. Party who does not appeal or prosecute a cross-appeal cannot complain of errors. *Vanhoose v. Wheeler*, 141 Ky. 746, 133 S. W. 779.

(27) Purchaser at judicial sale cannot appeal from order confirming sale after he has accepted deed and been put in possession. *Haggin v. Montague*, 101 S. W. 893, 125 Ky. 507, 31 R. 123.

(28) Divorce-death of husband appellee. Ordinarily, the death of an appellee after submission of the appeal is not material to the decision. Pending an appeal by a wife from a judgment refusing her divorce or alimony, the husband died after submission and before decision. The court on motion dismissed the appeal at the husband's costs. *Barger v. Barger*, 151 Ky. 234, 151 S. W. 406.

(20) Consent judgment. No appeal lies from a consent judgment. *Com. v. Helm*, 163 Ky. 69, 173 S. W. 389.

# Trial and Decision

(30) Peremptory instructions. If in a common law action there is any evidence to support the plaintiff's claim, the issue should be submitted to the jury. Royal Neighbors of A. v. Laufman, 158 Ky. 358, 164 S. W. 966.

(31) It was not error to refuse to give a peremptory instruction in a case where there was some evidence authorizing the submission of the case to the jury. L. & I. Ry. Co. v. Roemmele, 157 Ky. 84, 162 S. W. 547.

(32) Supplied papers. Papers supplied in the circuit court in place of lost papers may take the place of such papers for the purposes of appeal; objection to the correctness of the supplied record must be made by appeal from an order of the circuit court supplying the record. Such objections cannot be considered on the appeal taken on the merits of the case. Turner v. Hamlin, 152 Ky. 469, 153 S. W. 778.

(33) Absence of bill of exceptions. In the absence of a bill of exceptions the Court of Appeals will not review an alleged error of the court in suspending the trial of a case and using some of the jurors sworn in that case to try other cases. Sams v. Gray, 156 Ky. 615, 161 S. W. 553.

(34) In the absence of a bill of exceptions the Court of Appeals will consider only the sufficiency of the pleadings. Tyler v. Woerner, 158 Ky. 110, 166 S. W. 178; Bobbitt v. Blakemore, 153 Ky. 170, 154 S. W. 941.

(35) Absence of transcript of evidence. Where there is no transcript of the evidence in the record filed in the Court of Appeals, the only question to be considered is whether the pleadings support the judgment. Myers v. Saltry, 163 Ky. 481, 173 S. W. 1138.

(36) On an appeal from a judgment affirming the removal of a guardian the transcript failed to contain the exhibits or evidence upon which the circuit court acted; the judgment was affirmed. Ex Parte Spurling's Gdn., 165 Ky. 349, 176 S. W. 1139.

(37) Absence of some of the pleadings. In the absence from the record of certain pleadings and evidence it was presumed that the omitted portions of the record would support the judgment. Smith v. Berry, 164 Ky. 621, 176 S. W. 17.

(38) Instructions not identified. Instructions which were not identified by a bill of exceptions were disregarded by the Court of Appeals. Gardner v. Alexander, 159 Ky. 713, 169 S. W. 466; Gooch v. Collins, 156 Ky. 282, 160 S. W. 1038.

(39) Reversal of judgment on verdict with instructions to enter judgment for part of the finding. The jury made separate findings on two different items. The Court of Appeals reversed the judgment as to one item and affirmed as to the other. Swann-Day Lumb. Co. v. Cornett, 161 Ky. 98, 170 S. W. 516.

(40) A judgment rendered upon a verdict may be reversed with instructions to exclude certain items included in the verdict. A new trial need not be awarded. West v. King, 165 Ky. 561, 174 S. W. 11.

(41) Where in an action on contract part of the amount awarded is due to an erroneous instruction, the Court of Appeals may set aside so much as is assessed under the erroneous instruction and sustain the amount properly awarded. Sturgeon's Admr. v. MeCorkle, 163 Ky. 8, 173 S. W. 149.

(42) Error to prejudice of appellee where no cross-appeal. Where there was no cross-appeal the court refused to consider errors committed to the prejudice of the appellee. Stark v. Scott, 158 Ky. 596, 165 S. W. 976.

(43) When judgment for costs on appeal a clerical misprision. On an appeal on which costs ought not to be adjudged, the opinion did not direct a judgment for costs. A judgment for costs was a clerical misprision and was set aside after the term. C. & O. Ry. Co. v. Harmon, 159 Ky. 59, 166 S. W. 786.

(44) Right to prosecute appeal and also an action for new trial. A party who has been defeated in an action may prosecute an appeal and an action for a new trial at the same time when the ground upon which he seeks a new trial is one of which he could not avail himself on the appeal. Rice v. Blair, 161 Ky. 280, 170 S. W. 657.

(45) Correction of record by consent after reversal. After a judgment of reversal it was agreed by counsel that there was a mistake in the record. This matter was corrected; a rehearing was granted, and the judgment of the lower court was affirmed. Com. v. Gold & Stock Tel. Co., 164 Ky. 570, 175 S. W. 997.

(46) Grant of a new trial by circuit court pending an appeal. While an appeal was pending in the Court of Appeals the circuit court granted a new trial on petition. The pending appeal was thereupon dismissed by the court without a consideration of its merits. Anshutz v. Lou. Ry. Co., 152 Ky. 741, 154

Title XVIII Trial and Decision.

# § 757 [897] [Dismissal of appeal-enforcement of judgment for part of demand, effect on appeal.]

If it appear from the record that an appeal was improperly granted, or that the appellant's rights to prosecute it further has ceased, the appellee may, upon stating the grounds in writing, move the court to dismiss the appeal. The motion shall not be heard, without the appellant's consent, before the day on which the case is set for trial on the docket [except upon five days' notice served upon the appellant or upon his attorney of record]. [But when a party recovers judgment for only part of the demand or property he sues for, the enforcement of such judgment shall not prevent him from prosecuting an appeal therefrom as to so much of the demand or property sued for that he did not recover.]

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 79, 85, 111, 112.

S. W. 13; Lou. Ry. Co. v. Anshutz, 152 Ky. 741, 154 S. W. 13.

# (47) Appeal from allowance of attorney's fees-attorneys necessary parties.

Complaint was made that certain attorneys' fees were improperly allowed in an action, but the attorneys were not made parties to the appeal. The court refused to review the action. Angel v. Wood, 153 Ky. 195, 154 S. W. 1103.

# (18) Repleader awarded.

Where the record is so imperfect that the court can not determine with fairness to the litigants what judgment should be entered, the case will be rewarded to reform the pleadings and take such evidence as may be necessary to present clearly the issues between the parties, so that justice may be done. Nickels v. Collins, 158 Ky. 117, 164 S. W. 984.

# (49) Dismissal by Court of Appeals, where no real issue between parties.

Where it appeared on a trial in the circuit court upon a writ of forcible detainer that the defendant had already been dispossessed, and that the plaintiff or his vendee was then in actual possession of the property, there was no real issue between the parties. Upon appeal to the Court of Appeals, the court upon its own motion dismissed the appeal. Pittinger v. Gratz, 157 Ky. 401, 163 S. W. 203.

# § 757. (1) Dismissal of appeal.

When an appeal is sought to be dismissed on the ground that it is barred by limitation, a written plea must be filed. The objection cannot be made by motion. Riley v. Reed, 13 Bush 411; Herman v. Martin, 107 Ky. 612, 21 R. 1396, 55 S. W. 429; see sec. 758.

# (2) A motion to dismiss an appeal on the ground that appellant had compromised with appellee will not be entertained unless the plea is presented by a verified answer, as required in sec. 758.

Empire Co. v. McIntosh, 82 Ky. 554, 6 R. 631; and see Spalding v. Wathen, 7 Bush 659, 6 R. 631.

# (2a) Where pending the prosecution of an appeal the defendant effected a settlement with plaintiff, the appeal must be dismissed.

Ohio River Contract Co. v. Pennybacher, 168 Ky. 78, 181 S. W. 946.

# (3) The fact that appellant had agreed to pay, and appellee to receive, a less sum than the judgment as a compromise in full satisfaction of the judgment appealed from cannot be pleaded in the Court of Appeals to show that appellant had no right to prosecute the appeal.

Parks v. Doty, 13 Bush 727.

# (4) Accepting satisfaction of judgments bars right of appeal.

See note 1, sec. 734; but where party only recovers judgment for part of debt that he sues for he may enforce collection of that part and appeal as to part he failed to recover. O'Connor v. Henderson Bridge Co., 95 Ky. 633, 16 R. 244, 27 S. W. 251.

# 5 Whenever any person occupying a fiduciary relation resigns or is removed from office his right to prosecute an appeal terminates, and this fact may be brought to the notice of the court by an answer; so, when the right of a bankrupt passes to his assignee.

Parks v. Doty, 13 Bush 727.

# 6 When appellant dies after judgment and before appeal, or pending the appeal, his death should be taken advantage of in the Court of Appeals by a plea under the provisions of sec. 758.

When an appeal is prosecuted in the name of a dead person the judgment reversing the case is not void. Spalding v. Wathen, 7 Bush 659; and see further cases cited in note to sec. 767.

Trial and Decision

§758 [808] [Pleading to show cause for dismissing appeal.] If the facts mentioned in section 757 be not shown by the record, the appellee may plead them by a verified answer, to which the appellant may file a verified reply; and the questions of law or fact thereon shall be heard and determined by the court on or after the day on which the case is set for trial on the docket.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 85, 86, 112.

(7) The Court of Appeals will take judicial notice of the law of a case as settled by the Supreme Court of the United States. Alexander v. Gish, 13 R. 515, 17 S. W. 287.

(8) Notice of motion to dismiss appeal is necessary if motion is made without consent and before day case is set for hearing. Empire Coal Co. v. McIntosh, 5 R. 599.

(9) If an appeal is irregularly granted the proper remedy is by a motion to dismiss the appeal or discharge the supersedeas. Schmidt v. Mitchell, 95 Ky. 342, 15 R. 768, 25 S. W. 278.

(10) Motion to dismiss appeal should be made when case is submitted or it will be waived. Bailey v. L & N. R. R., 19 R. 1617, 44 S. W. 105; and see Welch v. Nat. Cash Register Co., 103 Ky. 192, 19 R. 1857, 44 S. W. 124; Dearing v. Wilcoxen, 21 R. 195; Standard Oil Co. v. Com., 26 R. 142, 80 S. W. 1150.

(11) Where a party fails to recover amount he sues for he may enforce judgment he does obtain, and appeal from judgment against him as to balance of his claim, and if the appellee supersedes the collection of judgment obtained and prays a cross-appeal, upon the affirmance of cross-appeal damages will be awarded on amount of judgment. O'Connor v. Henderson Bridge Co., 95 Ky. 633, 16 R. 244, 27 S. W. 251; and see further as to damages in appeal, notes sec. 748.

(12) Notice of motion is waived by appearance to it. Haggin v. Montague, 125 Ky. 507, 101 S. W. 893, 31 R. 123.

(13) Taxpayer who brings suit to enjoin expenditure of public money forfeits his right to prosecute appeal by removal.

(14) Accepting satisfaction of judgment where party only recovers part of what he sued for does not prevent him from appealing. Combs v. Bates, 147 Ky. 849, 145 S. W. 759; Combs v. Bates, 150 Ky. 188, 150 S. W. 20; Nicholas v. Alvey, 150 Ky. 343, 150 S. W. 364.

(14a) Neither the voluntary payment of a judgment, nor the acceptance of money in satisfaction of a judgment bars the right to appeal. Cravens v. Merritt, 178 Ky. 727, 190 S. W. 785.

(14b) A judgment was recovered for less than the amount claimed. This judgment was paid, and satisfaction was entered on the margin of the order book. Plaintiff had a right to an appeal from the judgment. Hendrickson v. New Hughes Jellico Coal Co., 172 Ky. 568, 189 S. W. 704.

(15) Defendant may pay or replevy judgment and appeal—and if reversal is had may have restitution. Nashville R. Co. v. Bean, 128 Ky. 758, 109 S. W. 323.

(16) Moot case. Where pending appeal conditions have arisen that would make the judgment on appeal of no effect it should be dismissed. Waller v. Henderson Tel. Co., 31 R. 39, 101 S. W. 372; King v. Tilford, 24 R. 1270, 70 S. W. 1064; Conn v. Desha, 24 R. 1400, 71 S. W. 513.

(17) Dismissal by court where no appeal has been prayed. The defendant in a judgment executed a supersedeas bond and filed a transcript, but it seems that no appeal was granted. The appeal was dismissed. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Smith's Admr., 178 Ky. 681, 199 S. W. 805.

Title XVIII Trial and Decision. [§ 759-760]

# § 759 [800] [Appeal taken for delay—motion to affirm.]

If an appeal with a supersedeas be taken for delay merely, the appellee may at any time move the court to affirm the judgment as a delay case; before making which motion, he or his counsel shall indorse on the record, in substance, that he has carefully examined the record and believes the appeal is prosecuted for delay merely; whereupon, the court shall examine the record, and if they find no error in the proceedings, and believe the appeal was prosecuted for delay merely, they shall affirm the judgment.

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 113.

# § 760 [900] [Rules of court—mandate when to issue—petition for rehearing.]

1. The Court of Appeals may make rules for the convenient dispatch of business, the preservation of order, the argument of cases or motions, and the manner and time of presenting motions or petitions for rehearing, therein; and the time for issuing its mandates and decisions, and the mode of enforcing its mandates and orders, and may change the same: Provided, That no mandate shall issue, nor decision become final, until after thirty days, excluding Sundays, from the day on which the decision is rendered, unless the court, in delay cases, otherwise direct; and, if said thirty days expire during a vacation or recess of the court, a written.

# averments of an answer filed under sec. 758 of Code in the Court of Appeals were not controverted by a reply. They were taken for confessed. Whittle v. W. T. Rawleigh Medical Co., 177 Ky. 1, 197 S. W. 470. See notes to sec. 757.

# § 759.

1. Motion to affirm as delay case will not be entertained until case is regularly on docket. Hamilton v. Ky. Title Co., 25 R. 1575, 79 S. W. 1182; Wyatt v. Ryan, 23 R. 1457, 65 S. W. 129.
2. L. & N. R. Co. v. Selumidt, 104 Ky. 179, 20 R. 456, 46 S. W. 685; Ingram v. Cincinnati R. Co., 127 Ky. 638, 105 S. W. 678, 31 R. 1248.
3. Motion to affirm cross-appeal as delay case will not be heard until original appeal is heard. Cov. Transfer Co. v. Piel, 9 R. 665.
4. Indorsement required by section should be made or motion will not be entertained. Latham v. Lindsay, 150 Ky. 669, 112 S. W. 584.

# § 760.

1. Costs. If an execution for costs is erroneous motion should be made in Court of Appeals to correct it. Shackelford v. Patterson, 110 Ky. 863, 23 R. 316, 62 S. W. 1040.
2. Defective record. A rehearing will not be granted to enable the parties to perfect the transcript. Yeager v. Groves, 78 Ky. 278; Christopher v. Searey, 12 Bush 171; nor will court look outside of record. Martin v. Royse, 21 R. 1353, 54 S. W. 177; Long v. L. & N. R. R., 21 R. 1151, 54 S. W. 178; Georgetown Water Co. v. Central Co., 18 R. 711, 38 S. W. 137; and see further, as to correction of record, after opinion has been delivered, notes see. 742.
3. Where it is suggested that the record is defective by reason of a clerical misprision, time will be given to have it corrected in the lower court. Williams v. Thompson, 80 Ky. 325, 4 R. 9.
4. Final termination of case. There is no final termination of a case in Court of Appeals until the time for filing petition for rehearing has expired, or until petition has been filed and overruled. Cline v. Wrightson, 7 R. 215.
5. Motion filed in clerk's office. A motion filed in the clerk's office during vacation on due notice for an extension of time to file a transcript is treated as if made in court on that day. Creech v. Brock, 159 Ky. 739, 169 S. W. 483.
6. Petition for rehearing. "All extensions of time for presenting petitions for rehearing, unless otherwise expressed in the order, should be counted from the expiration of the thirty days allowed by law, and Sundays should be excluded from the extension of time granted by the court. Such motion, however, must be made while the party has the right.

2. [Rule of court.] The rules of said court in force when this Code takes effect, and consistent with its provisions, shall continue in force until changed by the court. (Rules of court, sec Appendix.)

# § 761 [901] [Proceedings in lower court upon return of case.]

1. [Upon affirmance or dismissal of appeal.] Upon the affirmance of, or the dismissal of, an appeal from a judgment for the payment of money or delivery of personal property, the appellee may file in the clerk's office of the inferior court a copy of the mandate of affirmance or order of dismissal—upon which the clerk shall indorse the time of its being filed; and, thereupon, such writs of execution may be issued as if the mandate or order had been entered in the court.

Provisions in this section authorizing petition does not relate to criminal cases. Powers v. Com., 24 R. 1350, 71 S. W. 494.

(7) Power to modify opinions. The court may, on its own motion, at any time during the term modify an opinion. Georgetown Water Co. v. Cent. Thompson Co., 18 R. 711, 38 S. W. 137.

provision relating to filing of mandate and issuance of execution in this section only applies to the collection of a judgment by ordinary execution. Mayes v. Spalding, 17 R. 1124, 33 S. W. 838.

(2) Appeal enters appearance of appellant in lower court although he may not have been before the court prior to the appeal. L. & N. R. R. v. Chesnut, 115 Ky. 43, 24 R. 1846, 72 S. W. 351; Stoval v. Stoval, 19 R. 917, 39 S. W. 416.

(3) Construction of section—So much of this section as authorizes the appellee to file in office the mandate upon which execution may issue applies only to the collection of judgments by ordinary execution. Mays v. Spalding, 17 R. 1124, 33 S. W. 838.

(4) Costs upon reversal—See Ky. Stat., sec. 891, and Hoskins v. Morton, 27 R. 529, 85 S. W. 742.

(5) Judgment ordered corrected without awarding a new trial when the error made by lower court can be corrected without trial. Dils v. Hatcher, 25 R. 891, 76 S. W. 514; Cook v. Clark, 21 R. 316, 51 S. W. 316; City v. Gardner, 19 R. 302, 40 S. W. 779.

(6) Mandate. "Wherever the record, as it stood at the time the decision was rendered, when considered in connection with the opinion when delivered, shows that the clerk has made a mistake in entering the mandate, this court has power, at any rate during the succeeding term, to correct the error." Wade v. First Nat. Bank, 11 Bush 697; and see Whitchead v. Roorom, 7 Bush 399; Finnel v. Jones, 7 Bush 359.

Title XVIII] Trial and Decision. [5 761

2. [Upon reversal of judgment.] If a judgment be reversed, and the case remanded for a trial, or other proceedings, it shall stand for trial, or for such other proceedings, in the court whence the appeal was taken, at the next succeeding term thereof: Provided, That the mandate of the Court of Appeals be filed in the clerk's office of the lower court, and notice thereof given to the adverse party, if he be in the county; or, if he be absent from the county, to his attorney, ten days before the commencement.

1. The mandate must be considered with reference to the opinion preceding it, and it must be entered before the chancellor has power to suspend the execution of the order directed by it, even for good cause. Watson v. Avery, 3 Bush 635; see sec. 762 and notes.
2. The judgment ordered by the mandate should be entered. The court has no discretion concerning it. Scott v. Scott, 9 Bush 174; MeLean v. Nixon, 18 B. M. 758; Western Bank v. Coldway, 29 R. 653, 94 S. W. 1.
3. The Court of Appeals has power by rule against an inferior court to enforce obedience to its mandate. Watson v. Avery, 3 Bush 635; Smith v. Cochran, 7 Bush 548; and see note to sec. 762.
4. When a cause has been heard by the Court of Appeals and remanded to the circuit court with directions to render a particular decree or judgment, the cause is finally disposed of to that extent, and no pleadings can be filed in the circuit court which will have the effect of avoiding the mandate of the Court of Appeals, unless the pleadings are of such a character as would authorize a final decree or judgment to be reviewed or vacated after the expiration of the term at which it was rendered. Denny v. Wickliffe, 1 Met. 216; McLean v. Nixon, 18 B. M. 768; see Woodeock v. Bowman, 2 Duv. 508.
5. Proceedings in lower court. When opinion and mandate are filed in open court no notice is required to be served on the adverse party in order to give case position it had on docket before appeal was taken, and the case stands ready for submission subject to an order of continuance which the court may grant. Baker v. Baker, 87 Ky. 461, 10 R. 430, 9 S. W. 382; but in Lloyd v. Matthews, 92 Kv. 300, 13 R. 537, 17 S. W. 795; it is held that the above quotation from Baker case was obiter, and that where a case is reversed it does not stand for trial or other proceedings at the next succeeding term of lower court unless the mandate is filed in the clerk's office and notice given ten days before commencement of term; and this rule applies as well where the mandate directs a particular judgment to be entered as where case is remanded for trial or further preparation; and see Chesnut v. Russell, 24 R. 704, 69 S. W. 965; Crigler v. Ford, 26 R. 784, 82 S. W. 599.
6. If action is prematurely tried on return of case, a motion to set aside order as a clerical misprision must be made before appeal will lie. McHargue v. Reams, 24 R. 1385, 71 S. W. 526.
7. Restitution of property. Money received under an erroneous judgment before its reversal may be restored to party entitled to receive it by rule of court or by action on the return of case. Defenses that arose after rendition of erroneous judgment may be presented against restitution, but defenses that might have been or were relied on prior to such judgment cannot be. Gray v. Patton, 3 R. 393; and see Bridges v. McAlister, 106 Ky. 791, 21 R. 428, 51 S. W. 603; Hays v. Griffith, 85 Ky. 375, 9 R. 65, 3 S. W. 431; 11 S. W. 306; Asher v. L.& N. R. R., 26 R. 364, 81 S. W. 678; Drovers Bank v. Northern Coal Co., 133 Ky. 773, 119 S. W. 151; and see note 38, sec. 697.
8. Restitution can only be compelled from parties to the record, their assignees or personal representatives. Schnabel v. Waggener, 26 R. 258, 80 S. W. 1125.
9. Restitution of property sold under a judgment declared by Court of Appeals to be void; chancellor has power to order. Brown v. Vancleave, 14 R. 821, 21 S. W. 756; and see further, Gregory v. Litsey, 9 B. M. 44; Dickerson v. Morgan, 8 Dana 130; Drovers Bank v. Northern Coal Co., 133 Ky. 773, 119 S. W. 151; and see note 38, sec. 697.
10. Ordinarily, where there is no supersedeas, the plaintiff, who recovers in his fiducial capacity and pays out the money before a reversal of the judgment, will not be personally liable. Schultz v. Beatty, 6 R. 662.

# (17) Reversal of judgment-as to effect of acts done under a judgment that is reversed, before reversal, and when judgment is not superseded.

see Bridges v. MeAlister, 106 Ky. 791, 21 R. 428, 51 S. W. 603.

# (18) Reversal with direction to enter judgment in a common law case is not an invasion of the right of trial by jury where the evidence wholly fails to sustain the verdict rendered.

Rosenfield v. Goldsmith, 11 R. 662, 12 S. W. 928; 13 S. W. 3; and court may, when a new trial has been granted and there is an appeal from second judgment, direct entry of first judgment. Couudeau v. Am. Aee. Co., 95 Kv. 280, 15 R. 667. 25 S. W. 6; Riehards v. L. & N. R. R., 20 R. 1478, 49 S. W. 419; L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Ricketts, 21 R. 662, 52 S. W. 939; in Mendenhall v. Tungate, 95 Ky. 208; 15 R. 639, 24 S. W. 431; the court, in reversing a will case, directed county court to reject will.

In Frazie v. Com., 17 R. 347, 30 S. W. 1014; it was held that the parties had a fair trial, but that judgment was rendered for a larger amount than the evidence warranted. therefore in place of ordering a new trial the court directed that judgment for correct amount be entered. To the same effect is Garnett v. The Farmers' Bank, 15 R. 643, 23 S. W. 866; Cooke v. Clark, 21 R. 316, 51 S. W. 316; Reynolds v. Powers, 17 R. 1059, 29 S. W. 299.

# (20) Court of Appeals has no power to reduce excessive judgment except by reversal, nor has it the power to require appellee to consent that he will let judgment stand as security for any judgment that may be rendered in a second trial.

Irvine v. Gilson, 25 R. 1418, 77 S. W. 1106.

# (21) Amended pleading should not be tendered or filed until mandate has been filed.

Fugate v. Gill, 99 S. W. 602, 30 R. 731.

# (22) Amendment after reversal is allowable when opinion leaves case open for new trial.

Fugate v. Gill, 99 S. W. 602, 30 R. 731.

# (23) When petition is ordered dismissed - this ends the litigation.

Penn Lubricating Co. v. Bay State Co., 96 S. W. 1118, 29 R. 1215.

# (24) Reversal for proceedings consistent with opinion in a jury case does not authorize lower court to enter judgment without a retrial.

Quisenberry v. Chenault, 97 S. W. 803, 30 R. 229.

# (25) Reversal with directions to enter judgment that was set aside when new trial was granted.

Brown v. I. & N. R. Co., 144 Ky. 546, 139 S. W. 782; and see notes 15 and 58 to sec. 340.

# (26) Reversal for erroneously sustaining demurrer to petition.

On the reversal of a judgment of the circuit court sustaining a demurrer to a petition, the case stands in the circuit court just as it would have stood if the circuit court had overruled the demurrer to the petition; and the party demurring may plead when his demurrer is overruled. Hunt v. Hunt, 157 Kv. 835, 164 S. W. 102.

# (27) Reversal of judgment rejecting a will.

The Court of Appeals may reverse a judgment rejecting a will in whole or in part, and, instead of ordering a new trial, may direct the circuit court to require the county court to admit the will to probate. Hildreth, Ex., v. Hildreth, 153 Ky. 597, 156 S. W. 144.

# (28) Reversal of judgment against defendant for a tort where defendant dies pending appeal and cause of action does not survive.

A judgment in an action for assault and battery was reversed; pending the appeal the defendant died. On the return of the case the petition was dismissed, as the right of action did not survive. Shields' Admr. v. Rowland 151 Kv. 822, 152 S. W. 943.

# (29) On an appeal by the personal representatives of a decedent against whom judgment had been rendered for damages for an assault and battery, the judgment was reversed; as the action did not survive, the mandate directed a dismissal of the petition.

Shield's Admrs. v. Rowland, 151 Ky. 136, 151 S. W. 408.

A judgment of

# § 762 [0o2] [Mandates—power of court to enforce.]

The Court of Appeals may enforce its mandates upon inferior courts and officers by fine and imprisonment, which imprisonment may be continued until the mandates are obeyed. (Mandate, when to issue, sec. 760.)

Cross reference: See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 123.

# § 763 [oos] [Judgment that court will not reverse.]

Neither a void judgment, nor a judgment against a defendant who shall have been only constructively summoned, and shall not have appeared in the action; nor any judgment which can be set aside or modified by the court which rendered it, upon motion made after the term during which it was rendered, shall be reversed or modified by the Court of Appeals, until a motion to set aside or modify the judgment shall have been made in the inferior court and overruled. (Judgments that inferior court may vacate or modify, secs. 414, 517, 518.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), secs. 453e, 453g.

See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, secs. 37, 64, 112.

A circuit court was affirmed by the Court of Appeals. On appeal to the Supreme Court of the United States the judgment was reversed. The Court of Appeals remanded the case to the circuit court for further proceedings not inconsistent with the opinion of the Supreme Court. St. L. I. M. & S. Ry. Co. v. MelVhirter, Admx., 155 Ky. 301, 159 S. W. 796.

(3l) Effect of affirmance where action has been struck from docket of circuit court. An action in which a decree appointing commissioners to partition land had been rendered was struck from the docket pending the appeal; upon the affirmance of the judgment the commissioners had power to act. Heard v. Cherry, 150 Ky. 318, 150 S. W. 361.

# § 762. (1) Enforcement of mandates.

The Court of Appeals cannot undertake to dictate or control the manner in which the judgment of the lower court, when affirmed by it, is to be enforced, unless the court should absolutely refuse to enforce it; in such case mandamus might be issued by Court of Appeals, but where the lower court attempts to enforce the judgment rendered on appeal by a judgment, the remedy is by appeal. Rohmeiser v. Bannon, 15 R. 114, 26 S. W. 721; see further, notes 6-10, sec. 761.

2 Mandate must be considered with reference to the opinion. Watson v. Avery, 3 Bush 635; but this rule is not applicable to a response to a petition for a rehearing. Denny v. Wickliffe, 1 Met. 216, and see sec. 761 and notes.

(3) Powers of court. "This court has no power to determine the question of the liability of a sheriff for failing to make money on an execution issued from this court," but it has power to compel obedience to its process. Marchand v. Russell, 78 Ky. 517, 1 R. 126; and see Shackelford v. Patterson, 110 Ky. 863, 23 R. 316, 62 S. W. 1040.

As to its power to punish for contempt, see In re Woolley, 11 Bush 95; and as to power to enforce its mandates, see Watson v. Avery, 3 Bush 635; Smith v. Cochran, 7 Bush 548; as to power to change its decision of a former term, see Beazley v. Mershon, 6 Bush 424; Finnell v. Jones, 7 Bush 359; 11 Bush 697.

See further, Constitution, sec. 110, and cases cited in note thereto.

# § 764 [904] [Damages upon affirmance.]

Upon the affirmance of, or the dismissal of an appeal from, a judgment for the payment of money, the collection of which, in whole or in part, has been superseded, as provided in chapter 2 of this title, 10 per cent damages on the amount superseded shall be awarded against the appellant. {And upon the affirmance of, or the dismissal of an appeal from a judgment for money rendered against any insurance, railroad, corporation or company, or against any corporation not created by or organized under the laws of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 10 per centum damages on the amount of the judgment appealed from shall be awarded against the appellant, although such judgment be not superseded.} (Words in brackets added by Act of April 19, 1888, c. 1157, p. 92.)

Cross reference: See Newman's Forms No. 49. See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 124.

# § 756; motion to set aside void judgment

may be made at any time within two years. McClelland v. Troendle, 99 S. W. 329, 30 R. 612.

See Bitzer v. Mereke, 111 Ky. 299, 23 R. 670, 63 S. W. 711; where the question of what is a void judgment is discussed.

1. A judgment sustaining the defendant's demurrer to the plaintiff's petition and dismissing it, is not void where the record shows that the defendant had filed no demurrer to the petition, as it must be assumed that the defendant made the objection orally, or that the court itself raised the objection. Ford Lumber & Mfg. Co. v. Burt & Brabb Lumber Co., 157 Ky. 706, 163 S. W. 1105.
2. Clerical misprision. A demurrer to an answer was overruled; afterward an order was entered submitting the same demurrer; afterward the action was submitted and the court dismissed the petition. Without a motion in the lower court to vacate the judgment, the Court of Appeals cannot review this action. Com. v. Prudential L. Ins. Co., 149 Ky. 671, 149 S. W. 921.
3. The rendition of judgment on a note for more than is claimed in the pleadings to be due is a clerical misprision. Bratcher v. Ohio Co. Bank's Assignee, 152 Ky. 458, 153 S. W. 950.
4. Where an infant brought suit by his next friend, and the judgment was in the name of the infant alone, this clerical mistake is not available error on appeal. The infant was the real party in interest, and if the defendant desired to have the judgment show on its face that it was recovered for his use and benefit in the name of his next friend, he should have made some motion to correct it in the lower court. Sword v. Bowen, 157 Ky. 615, 163 S. W. 733.
5. Failure in a judgment to give credit for the amount of an undisputed counter-claim was a clerical misprision. Sowders v. Gingell, 174 Ky. 127, 191 S. W. 896.
6. Adding interest to the face of a note in a judgment, if improper, was a clerical misprision. Brady v. Equitable Trust Co., 178 Ky. 693, 199 S. W. 1082.
7. Void judgment in favor of Commonwealth against bail. A void judgment in favor of the Commonwealth against bail is within this section. The motion to set aside the judgment may be made effectually after the term at which it is rendered. Ky. Bonding Co. v. Com., 178 Ky. 605, 199 S. W. 807.
8. Judgment rendered out of term time. There was entered upon the judgment book of a circuit court a judgment which was void because the term at which it was rendered had ceased to exist before it was rendered. An appeal taken from that judgment when no motion to set it aside had been made in the circuit court was dismissed. Stevens v. Young, 180 Ky. 154, 202 S. W. 481.
9. Action treated as motion to set aside. An action to set aside a sale and the judgment under which it was made was treated as a sufficient motion under this section. Wyatt's Trustee v. Grider, 158 Ky. 440, 165 S. W. 420.

See further, notes to sec. 748.

judgment of the lower court is affirmed adjudged in lower court. Edelston v. Edelston, 173 Ky. 252, 190 S. W. 1083.

(7) Dismissal for want of jurisdiction. Damages were awarded on a superseded judgment upon a dismissal of the appeal for want of jurisdiction. Am. Acc. Co. v. Slaughter. 101 Ky. 269, 19 R. 418, 40 S. W. 675; Cumb. T. & T. Co. v. Curtiss, 147 Ky. 685, 145 S. W. 377. The court refused to award such damages. Montgomery v. Montgomery, 80 S. W. 1108, 25 R. 2018; Nelson Co. v. City of Bardstown, 123 Ky. 836, 30 R. 408, 97 S. W. 765. The last opinion does not expressly overrule the earlier cases.

(8) Unconstitutional is amendment to this section. Mutual Ins. Co. v. Hammond, 21 R. 204, 51 S. W. 151.

Void appeal. No damages. American Accident Co. v. Reigart, 92 Ky. 142, 13 R. 442, 17 S. W. 280; and see notes 13. 14, sec. 748.

(9a) A supersedeas bond executed before the clerk of the court rendering the judgment when no appeal had been granted by that court imposed no obligation. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Smith's Admr., 178 Ky. 681, 199 S. W. 805.

(9b) A circuit clerk took a supersedeas bond and issued a supersedeas although no appeal was granted by the circuit court during the term. The Court of Appeals discharged the supersedeas; but held the supersedeas void and refused to award damages. Dougherty v. Central Trust Co., Ex., 155 Ky. 380, 159 S. W. 777.

(9e) If an appeal be dismissed for lack of jurisdiction on the part of the circuit court to grant the same no damages will be awarded upon the supersedeas bond. Childers v. Ratliff, 164 Ky. 123, 175 S. W. 25.

(10) Where a superseded judgment has been affirmed the damages must go. although damages were also awarded on prior dismissal of appeal. United States F. & G. Co. v. Citizens Nat. Bank, 147 Ky. 810, 145 S. W. 750.

(11) Issual of supersedeas after time to pray appeal from lower court had expired did not entitle appellee to damages. Torbitt & Castleman v. Middleboro Grocery Co., 147 Ky. 343, 144 S. W. 16.

(12) Interest on damages not allowable when case is taken to United States Supreme Court and judgment affirmed. L. & N. R. Co. v. Melton, 146 Ky. 242, 142 S. W. 382.

(13) Judgment ordering money paid into court and allowing adverse party to withdraw it is a judgment for recovery of money and carries damages. J. M.

# § 765 [005] [Written opinions to be delivered.]

The court must deliver written opinions in all cases.

# § 766 [906] [Reporter of court and duties.]

The reporter of the decisions of the Court of Appeals shall make a brief statement of the facts involved in the decisions, unless they be stated in the opinion of the court, and a brief statement of the legal propositions of counsel on both sides, and a statement of the authorities cited by counsel in support of each proposition. (See further as to reporter, Ky. Stat., sec. 956.)

The provisions of Title XI shall, so far as applicable, regulate cases in the Court of Appeals.

Cross reference: See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), sec. 614e. See Miller's Ky. Appellate Practice and Forms, sec. 116.

(1) Judgment for specific property or its value carries damages that must be enforced in lower court. Cunningham v. Clay, 132 Ky. 129, 116 S. W. 299.

(2) Failure of clerk to enter judgment for damages is a clerical misprision that may be corrected after term. Stewart v. Blue Grass Canning Co., 133 Ky. 118, 121 S. W. 609; Nelson Co. v. City of Bardstown, 123 Ky. 836, 97 S. W. 765, 30 R. 408; I. C. R. Co. v. Com., 143 Ky. 217, 136 S. W. 222.

# § 767

(1) Death of plaintiff in judgment. Appeal-revivor. Hopkins v. Hopkins, 91 Ky. 310, 12 R. 945, 15 S. W. 854; Spalding v. Wathen, 7 Bush 659; Sawyer v. Fuqua, 21 R. 254, 51 S. W. 174; Deppen v. Imoher, 27 R. 43, 84 S. W. 333; Buchanan v. Boyd, 131 Ky. 433, 120 S. W. 295.

(2) Death of appellant. If an appellant dies after the appeal is taken and before submission, a subsequent submission without revivor will on motion of appellee be set aside. Chenault v. Yates, 149 Ky. 816, 149 S. W. 1024.

(3) Death of appellee. A petition seeking to set aside a deed of land was dismissed. An appeal was taken. Pending the appeal, the appellee died. A revivor against the real representatives of the appellee was proper. Williams v. Reese, 176 Ky. 21, 195 S. W. 116.

(4) Death of appellee after submission. When the appellee dies after submission, no revivor in the Court of Appeals is necessary. Wallace v. Wallace's Ex., 150 Ky. 33, 150 S. W. 13.

(5) Divorce and alimony-death of husband appellee after submission. Ordinarily, the death of an appellee after submission of the appeal is not material to the decision. Pending an appeal by a wife from a judgment refusing her divorce or alimony, the husband died after submission and before decision. The court on motion dismissed the appeal at the husband's costs. Barger v. Barger, 151 Ky. 234, 151 S. W. 406.

(6) Time for revivor. An order of revivor is in time if made within one year from the end of the term at which it might have been first made. Ison v. Wolfe, 150 Ky. 34, 149 S. W. 1124.

THE CODE OF PRACTICE IN CRIMINAL CASES

# AN ACT Regulating Practice in Criminal Cases.

# PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS.

§ 1. Name of the act—regulates all prosecutions and penal actions.

§ 2. Proceedings commenced before January 1, 1877.

§ 3. Laws within purview of Code repealed-exception.

# § 1. [Name of act—regulates prosecutions and penal actions.]

That the provisions of this act shall regulate the proceedings in all prosecutions and penal actions in all the courts of this Commonwealth, from and after the first day of January, 1877, and shall be known as "The Code of Practice in Criminal Cases."

# § 2. [Proceedings commenced prior to January 1, 1877.]

That all prosecutions of proceedings in criminal or penal cases, which shall be commenced before the first day of January, 1877, and which, by the existing laws, would be valid, shall not be rendered invalid by this act, but may be prosecuted to their conclusion, and enforced according to the existing laws, as if this act had not been passed.

# § 3. [Laws within purview of Code repealed-exception.]

That all laws coming within the purview of this act shall become repealed when this act goes into effect, except as provided in the preceding section.

# § 3. (1) Application of Criminal Code.

# (2) Conflict between Statutes and Code.

The Criminal Code was intended to provide a new and complete mode of procedure in all criminal and penal cases. It prescribes the form and manner in which indictments must be prepared and presented, and these regulations apply to all prosecutions, whether for felonies or misdemeanors. Com. v. Patterson, 2 Met. 374; and see further, as to application of Code, Walston v. Com., 16 B. M. 15; Com. v. Craig, 15 B. M. 534.

# Public Offenses-Preventing and Prosecuting

# § 1141. Misdemeanors prosecutable by warrant—information-proceedings.

In misdemeanor cases, where the highest penalty that may be imposed is a fine of one hundred dollars and imprisonment for fifty days, the offender may be prosecuted by warrant, as provided in the Criminal Code, or by information filed by the Commonwealth's attorney or county attorney in the circuit court, or before the county judge, or a justice of the peace, or police or city judge. The information shall be signed by the officer filing it, and shall state the nature of the offense charged; and when filed, the court, or judge, or justice, shall at once issue a summons or warrant against the offender, commanding him to appear, within three days, at the time and before the court or justice or judge mentioned therein; and the amount of bail that he may give for his appearance shall be specified in the warrant. The warrant or summons may be directed to any peace officer, and shall be returned by him before the court, or judge, or justice mentioned in the warrant or summons. The proceedings upon the warrant or summons shall be the same as in other like cases prosecuted by warrant or summons. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1141.)

# § 118 [Offenses that may be prosecuted by penal action.]

A public offense, of which the only punishment is a fine, may be prosecuted by a penal action in the name of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, or in the name of an individual or corporation, if the whole fine be given to such individual or corporation. The proceedings in penal actions are regulated by the Code of Practice in civil actions. (Venue of action, Civil Code, sec. 63; see same provision in Ky. Stat., sec. 1139.)

# § 13 [10] [Jurisdiction of various courts.]

The jurisdiction of the courts of this Commonwealth, for the trial of offenses, shall be as follows:

1. [Senate of Kentucky.] The Senate of Kentucky shall have exclusive jurisdiction of impeachments. (Constitution, sec. 67.)
2. [Court of Appeals.] The Court of Appeals of Kentucky shall have exclusive jurisdiction of proceedings for the removal of clerks of courts from office. (Constitution, sec. 124.)
3. [Circuit courts.] The circuit courts shall have general jurisdiction for the trial of all prosecutions and penal actions, unless exclusive jurisdiction be given to other courts. (Exclusive jurisdiction of justices' courts, Appendix, chap. vii.; of police courts, Appendix, chap. vii.)
4. [City and police courts.] City and police courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction of all prosecutions and actions for an infraction of the by-laws or ordinances of the city or town in which they are located, and

Title II Criminal Jurisdictions of the Courts. [§ 13]

# 143. Police courts--establishment and jurisdiction

A police court may be established in each city and town in this State, with jurisdiction in cases of violation of municipal ordinances and by-laws occurring within the corporate limits of the city or town in which it is established, and such criminal jurisdiction within the said limits as justices of the peace have. The said courts may be authorized to act as examining courts, but shall have no civil jurisdiction: Provided, The General Assembly may confer civil jurisdiction on police courts in cities and towns of the fourth and fifth classes, and in towns of the sixth class having a population of two hundred and fifty or more, which jurisdiction shall be uniform throughout the State, and not exceed that of justices of the peace. (Constitution.)

# § 14 [11] [Indictment in circuit court—jurisdiction.]

If an indictment be found in the circuit court for an offense within its jurisdiction, such court shall have jurisdiction in that prosecution of all the degrees of such offense, and of all offenses included in the one charged, although some of those degrees or included offenses are within the exclusive jurisdiction of an inferior or local court. (What offenses included in one charged; degrees of offenses defined, secs. 262 to 266.)

# Title II] Criminal Jurisdictions of the Courts.

# § 15 [12] [Removal of prosecutions from circuit to inferior courts.]

When prosecutions have been commenced in the circuit court, of which a city, magistrates' or police court has concurrent jurisdiction, they may, by an order of the circuit court, be removed into such city, magistrates' or police court for trial. Upon such order being made, the clerk of the circuit court shall deliver to the clerk of the city, magistrates' or police court, all the papers relating to the prosecutions, and a copy of the order of removal, and thereupon the defendant shall be bound to appear in said police, magistrates' or city court, and the prosecution be carried on to final judgment and execution, as if commenced in such court. (See as to jurisdiction of inferior courts, sec. 13; transfer of misdemeanors to county court, Ky. Stat., sec. 1076.)

# § 16 [13] [Grand juries to state if misdemeanor committed in a city or town.]

The judges of circuit courts may require the grand juries, in prosecutions for misdemeanors within the local jurisdiction of city or police courts, to state in the indictments that such misdemeanors were committed within the town or city, or other local limits of the jurisdiction of the city or police courts.

# § 17 [14] [Jurisdiction of Senate and Court of Appeals.]

The jurisdiction of the Senate and Court of Appeals embraces the whole Commonwealth.

# § 18 [15] [Local jurisdiction of circuit and justices' courts.]

The local jurisdiction of circuit courts and justices' courts shall be of offenses committed within the respective counties in which they are held. (See further, as to jurisdiction, sec. 13, and Ky. Stat., sec. 1143.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 885.

# § 18. (l) Evidence-venue.

After the Commonwealth's attorney announced through with his evidence, the question was made that the venue of the offense was not proven. The court should have permitted him to introduce evidence to show the venue. Com. v. Patterson, 10 R. 167, 8 S. W. 604.

Where evidence was introduced showing that an offense was committed in the county seat town where trial was in progress, the court and jury had the right to presume that the town referred to was in the county where the case was being tried, although no evidence of county was introduced. Com. v. Patterson, 10 R. 167. 8 S. W. 694; and in Hays v. Com., 12 R. 611, 14 S. W. 833; it is held that where evidence is introduced showing commission of crime in a certain named precinct in county where trial is in progress, that a verdict of conviction will not be disturbed, although there is no proof of county in which offense was committed; and see to same effect Pickerel v. Com., 17 R. 120, 30 S. W. 617; Combs v. Com., 651.

Criminal Jurisdictions of the Courts.

# § 19 [16] [Local jurisdiction of police or city courts.]

The local jurisdiction of police or city courts shall be of offense, committed within the limits of the jurisdiction of such courts, as prescribed by the special statutes creating or regulating them. (What are police courts, sec. 321. See further, as to such courts, sec. 306, and as to jurisdiction, Appendix.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 885.

# § 1145. Offenses tried in county where committed.

All offenses shall be tried in the courts, or by the tribunals, of that county or city having jurisdiction of them in which they were committed, except in cases otherwise provided for. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1145.)

# § 20 [17] [Jurisdiction on river between counties.]

If a river be the boundary between two counties, the criminal jurisdiction of each county shall embrace offenses committed on the river, or on any island thereof.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 886.

# § 1143. Highway or watercourse between counties.

In all cases where any part of a river, water, watercourse, highway, road or street, shall be the boundary line between two counties, the courts and judges and justices, and all circuit and county officers of both such counties, shall have concurrent jurisdiction in all cases over the whole extent of such parts of said river, water, watercourse, highway, road or street. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1143.)

# § 1144. Counties on Ohio and other rivers.

The circuit court of each county bordering on the Mississippi, Ohio, Big Sandy or Tennessee rivers shall have concurrent jurisdiction of any treason or felony committed on any of said rivers. (Id., sec. 1144.)

Title II] Criminal Jurisdictions of the Courts.

# § 21 [18] [Prosecution in either of several counties -libel.]

If an offense be committed partly in one and partly in another county, or if acts and their effects constituting an offense occur in different counties, the jurisdiction is in either county.

[But all prosecutions against persons publishing a newspaper for any libelous matter contained therein shall be had in the county where the same is printed and issued, or in the county where the party complaining resides.]

(Words in brackets added by Act of May 18, 1886, c. 1268, p. 212.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 887.

# § 1146. Concurrent jurisdiction in case of doubt.

When it is a matter of doubt, in the opinion of the court, in which of two or more counties the offenses was committed, the court of either in which the indictment is found shall have jurisdiction of the offense. (Ky. Stat., sec. I146.)

# § 1147. Injury inflicted in one county, death in another.

If a mortal wound or other violence or injury be inflicted, or poison be administered, in one county or corporation, and death ensues in another, the offense may be prosecuted in either. (Id., sec. 1147; see, also, Criminal Code, sec. 24.)

Boundary and jurisdiction of this State extend to low-water mark on the western or northwestern side of the Ohio river, and one who commits an offense on that river may be punished by the courts of this State. MiFall v. Com., 2 Met. 394. See Ky. Stat., sec. 1144.

(2) Person who sells liquor in a county adjoining the local option county in which he resides cannot be convicted of violating the local option law. Duff v. Com., 24 R. 201, 68 S. W. 390.

# § 21. (1) Jurisdiction.

Receiving stolen property, knowing it to be stolen, is a complete offense, and the circuit court of the county in which the property is received, and not of the county in which the larceny is committed has jurisdiction of the offense. Allison v. Com., 83 Ky. 254, 7 R. 252.

2 A thief can be indicted for a complete larceny either in the county where he first took the property, or in any county into which, the intent to steal continuing, he carries it. Thomas v. Com., 12 R. 903, 15 S. W. 861; Allison v. Com., 83 Ky. 254, 7 R. 252; and see Massie v. Com., 90 Ky. 485, 12 R. 433, 14 S. W. 419; Macklin v. Com., 93 Ky. 294, 14 R. 180, 19 S. W. 931.

Com., 13 Bush 142, and see Com. v. Parker, 108 Ky. 673, 22 R. 368, 57 S. W. 653, holding that an accessory may be prosecuted in either county.

(4) Where false pretenses were made in Ohio and the money or property obtained in this State, the indictment should be found in the county of latter, where money or property was obtained. Com. v. Van Tuyl, 1 Met. 1.

5 Court of county where person who is libeled resides has jurisdiction of offense. Shields v. Com., 21 R. 1588, 55 S. W. 881.

(6) In cases of homicide where the wounding occurs in one county and the death in another—both counties have jurisdiction of the offense. Hargis v. Parker, 27 R. 441, 85 S. W. 704; Com. v. Jones, 26 R. 867, 82 S. W. 643; 118 Ky. 889; and see Jackson v. Com., 100 Ky. 239, 18 R. 795, 38 S. W. 422.

(6a) If the deceased is shot in a county of Kentucky, the court of that county has jurisdiction in the offense of the homicide, no matter where he died. Fields v. Com., 152 Ky. 80, 153 S. W. 29.

6b A penal action was rightly brought in Trimble county against a railroad company for emptying in Henry county into a stream deadly substances which were carried by the stream into Trimble county. Com. v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 175 Ky. 267, 194 S. W. 345.

(6e) A warrant for a violation of a city ordinance made no reference to the ordinance. The warrant was sufficient. City of Owensboro v. Evans, 172 Ky. 831, 189 S. W. 1153.

# Criminal Jurisdictions of the Courts.

# § 22 [19] [Importing property into State-jurisdiction.]

If the offense consist of importing any property into the Commonwealth, the jurisdiction shall be in any county into which the property may be imported.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 888.

# § 23 [20] [Kidnaping-jurisdiction of offense.]

If the offense consist of kidnaping, or seizing or confining a person without lawful authority, the jurisdiction shall be in the county in which the kidnaping, seizing, or confining was committed, or in any county in which it was continued.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 889.

# § 24 [21] [Trial in county of arrest—exception.]

If the jurisdiction of an offense be in two or more counties, the defendant shall be tried in the county in which he is first arrested, unless an indictment for the offense be pending in another county. (See further, Ky. Stat., secs. 1145-1147.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 887.

# § 25 [22] [Writs of prohibition from circuit courts.]

The circuit court of any county may, by writ of prohibition, restrain all other courts of inferior jurisdiction in the limits of the county from exceeding their criminal jurisdiction. (How writ obtained and prosecuted, see Civil Code, sec. 474.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 890.

See Newman's Pl. and Pr. (3d Ed.), Form No. 480.
