# § 122 [121, 128, 120] [Requisites of an indictment.]

The indictment must contain—

1. [Title of prosecution, name of court and parties.] The title of the prosecution, specifying the name of the court in which the indictment is presented and the names of the parties.
2. [Statement of acts constituting offense.] A statement of the acts constituting the offense, in ordinary and concise language, and in such a manner as to enable a person of common understanding to know what is intended; and with such degree of certainty as to enable the court to pronounce judgment, on conviction, according to the right of the case. (Facts that must be stated with certainty, sec. 124.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 966, 969, 982.

# § 122. (1) Abortion.

It is necessary that an indictment for abortion should allege that the mother was quick with child, and that the potion was administered with the intention to destroy the life of the child, and that such was its effect. Mitchell v. Com., 78 Ky. 204; but where the indictment is for the death of the mother as the result of an abortion, it is not necessary to allege that deceased was "quick" with child. Peoples, v. Com., 87 Ky. 487, 10 R. 517, 8 S.W. 509, 810.

# (2) Accessory.

An accessory before the fact is in legal contemplation guilty.

# (3) See further, as to accessory before.

695

# Indictment-Form and Requisites

the fact, Ky. Stat., sec. 1125; after the fact, Ky. Stat., sec. 1129.

1. A certain indictment for conspiracy sufficiently charged all the defendants with being principals. *Daniel v. Com.,* 154 Ky. 001, 157 S. W. 1127.
2. Aiders and abettors. When two persons are jointly indicted for murder, one as principal and the other as aider and abettor, the one charged as principal may be found guilty of aiding and abetting, and the one charged with aiding and abetting may be found guilty as principal. *Benge v. Com., 92 Ky. 1, 13 R. 308, 17 S. W. 146; Gaskins v. Com., 97 Ky. 494, 17 R. 352, 30 S. W. 1017; Howard v. Com., 110 Ky. 356, 22 R. 1845, 61 S. W. 756; Taylor v. Com., 140 S. W. 581, 28 R. 819; Com. v. Hargis, 99 S. W. 348, 30 R. 510, 124 Ky. 356; Terhune v. Com., 144 Ky. 370, 138 S. W. 274; Reed v. Com.,* 125 Ky. 126, 100 S. W. 856, 30 R. 1212.
3. Where an indictment is found against one person only as principal it is error to convict him of the crime of aiding and abetting. *Hollin v. Com.,* 158 Ky. 427, 165 S. W. 407.
4. Aider and abettor in crime created by statute punishable. *Co. v. Carter, 94 Ky. 527, 15 R. 253, 23 S. W. 344; overruling Stamper v. Com., 7 Bush 612; and see Frey v. Com.,* 83 Ky. 191, 7 R. 174.
5. An indictment against A and B, which in one count charges both of them as principals, and in another count charges A as principal and B as an aider and abettor, and in the third count charges B as principal and A as aider and abettor, is good, and one of them may be found guilty of a felony and the other of a misdemeanor. *Cupp v. Com.,* 87 Ky. 35, 9 R. 877, 7 S. W. 405; and see Ky. Stat., secs. 1128, 1129 and cases cited in note 4.
6. Aiders and abettors in crime of housebreaking, *Ward v. Com.,* 14 Bush 233; and see Ky. Stat., 1162.
7. Aiders and abettors in crime of malicious shooting or wounding. See Ky. Stat., sec. 1166. and *Com. v. Patrick, 80 Ky. 605, 4 R. 060; where it is held that under this statute the person who committed the offense must be charged with it, and then others may also be charged with aiding and abetting in it, but all of them cannot be charged as principals; and see Cupp v. Com.,* 87 Ky. 35, 9 R. 877, 7 S. W. 405.
8. Aiders and abettors in crime of rape. *Kessler v. Com., 12 Bush 18; Ky. Stat., 1152; in crime of housebreaking, Mullins v. Com.,* 14 R. 569, 20 S. W. 1035.
9. "In order to convict one as aider and abettor the indictment must include the principal actor jointly, or disclose who he is, together with a description of his acts." *Mulligan v. Com., 84 Ky. 229, 8 R. 211, 1 S. W. 417; but see Benge v. Com., 92 Ky. 1, 13 R. 308, 17 S. W. 146; Howard v. Com., 110 Ky. 356, 22 R. 1845, 61 S. W. 756; Powers v. Com.,* 110 Ky. 386, 22 R. 1807, 61 S. W. 735; and see cases cited in note 4.
10. Appropriation of property in custody of carrier. An indictment for appropriating property in the custody of a common carrier based on Ky. Stat., sec. 1201b, was sufficient, although it did not state the name of the consignee or owner of the property. *Williams v. Com.,* 152 Ky. 610, 153 S. W. 961.
11. Arson. The ownership of the property was sufficiently alleged in an indictment that charged that the house was occupied by Hattie Taylor as a residence. It is not necessary that the name of the owner in fee be given. Under an indictment for arson the defendant may be convicted of the crime of an attempt to commit arson. *Young v. Com.,* 12 Bush 243.
12. An indictment charging that offense was committed "feloniously and wilfully," omitting the word "maliciously," is sufficient for the common law offense. *Aikman v. Com., 13 R. 894, 18 S. W. 937; Young v. Com.,* 12 Bush 243. The Ky. Stat., sec. 1170, in defining certain kinds of arson, uses the word "maliciously," but in cases supra it is held that the word "feloniously" embraces the word "maliciously."
13. The failure of indictment to expressly aver that dwelling house was occupied as a residence does not vitiate it, if it is charged that the dwelling house was occupied. *Com. v. Elliston,* 14 R. 216, 20 S. W. 214; Ky. Stat., sec. 1170.
14. See further, as to indictment for burning a barn or warehouse, *Deshazer v. Com., 12 R. 453, 14 S. W. 542; Evans v. Com., 11 R. 573, 12 S. W. 768, 769; Ky. Stat., sec. 1169; or church edifice, MeDonald v. Com., 86 Ky. 10, 9 R. 230, 4 S. W. 637; or storehouse, Overstreet v. Com.,* 147 Ky. 471, 144 S. W. 751.
15. Assault and battery. An assault and battery is punishable by either fine or imprisonment or both fine and imprisonment, in the discretion of the jury. *Cornelison v. Com.,* 84 Ky. 583, 2 S. W. 235, 8 R. 793.

# Title VIJ

# Indictment-Form and Requisites.

[§ 122]

1. An indictment charging that the defendant "did in sudden heat and passion, without previous malice and not in self-defense, strike and wound G with a pair of blacksmith tongs, which said tongs was then and there a deadly weapon" is good only as an indictment for assault and battery. *Com. v. Hawkins, 11 Bush 603.*
2. Under an indictment for a simple assault it is error to admit proof of a battery; every battery includes an assault, but an assault does not include a battery; an assault and battery is an offense of a higher grade than a simple assault. *Furnish v. Com., 14 Bush 180; see further, as to degrees of offense secs. 262-264, and notes thereto, and Com. v. Heath, 99 Ky. 182, 18 R. 57, 35 S. W. 277;* holding that an assault and battery is not necessarily a lower degree of an assault with intent to kill.
3. Bigamy. An indictment for bigamy charging that "Eliza Davis on the day of May, 18i7, in the county and State aforesaid, and before the finding of this indictment, having a husband then living, unlawfully married John Mackey," is not sufficient; it should aver the place of the first marriage and set out the name of the first husband or wife. *Davis v. Com., 13 Bush 318, overruling Com. v. Whaley, 6 Bush 266;* and see Ky. Stat., sec. 1216.
4. "It is immaterial in an indictment for bigamy at what particular date the first marriage occurred, if the accused had at time of second marriage a living wife to whom he had been previously married." *Faustre v. Com., 92 Ky. 34, 13 R. 347, 17 S. W. 189;* and see as to immaterial variance in name of first wife, note 3, sec. 124.
5. The offense of bigamy committed by marrying in another State while the former marriage is in force is not an offense against the laws of this State. *Johnson v. Com., 86 Ky. 122, 9 R. 419, 5 S. W. 365.*
6. Bribery. As to sufficiency of indictment for, see Ky. Stat., secs. 1586, 1587; and *Johnson v. Com., 90 Ky. 53, 12 R. 20, 13 S. W. 520; Cheek v. Com., 87 Ky. 42, 9 R. 880, 7 S. W. 403; Com. v. Selby, 87 Ky. 594, 10 R. 621, 9 S. W. 819; Com. v. Root, 16 R. 491, 29 S. W. 351; Com. v. Headly, 111 Ky. 815, 23 R. 1104, 64 S. W. 744.*
7. Burglary. An indictment for burglary described the house "as the house of A. Dominick"; held sufficient description. To make out the offense of burglary it is not necessary to prove that goods were actually stolen and carried away; it is sufficient if the storehouse was feloniously broken with the intention of so doing; nor is it necessary to charge that the house was "burglariously" broken and entered. *Charles Olive v. Com., 5 Bush 376; see Com. v. Bruce, 79 Ky. 560, 3 R. 360; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 1159. The indictment should charge the specific felony intended to be committed; it is not sufficient to charge "with intent to commit a felony." Kyle v. Com., 111 Ky. 404, 23 R. 708, 63 S. W. 782; Radley v. Com., 28 R. 477, 89 S. W. 519, 121 Ky. 506.*
8. Caption of indictment which omits name of county in which it was found is not defective when the body of indictment states the county. *Johnson v. Com., 12 R. 835, 15 S. W. 662.*
9. Fraudulent use of checks-"cold check law." In an indictment under Ky. Stat., sec. 1213a (the Cold Check law), it is not necessary to allege that the bank on which the check was drawn or the bank to which it was delivered was authorized by a law of the United States or of any State of the United States or any foreign government. *Siegel v. Com., 177 Ky. 232, 197 S. W. 809.*
10. See note 82a.
11. Confederating and banding together to intimidate. Ky. Stat., sec. 1223; *Buchanan v. Com., 95 Ky. 334, 15 R. 738, 25 S. W. 265; Com. v. Hightower, 149 Ky. 563, 149 S. W. 971.*
12. Conspiracy to commit crime. Definition of and necessary allegations in indictment for. *Com. v. Ward, 92 Ky. 158, 13 R. 422, 17 S. W. 283.*
13. One person may be indicted for the offense of conspiracy without joining his fellow conspirator. *Rutland v. Com., 160 Ky. 77, 169 S. W. 584.*
14. Corporation liable to indictment for an offense which is either a misfeasance or a nonfeasance of duty to the public, the punishment for which is a fine, as for permitting gaming on premises under its control. *Com. v. Pulaski Co. A. & M. Association, 92 Ky. 197, 13 R. 468, 17 S. W. 442.*
15. Counterfeiting. In an indictment for counterfeiting it is necessary to allege that the bank on which the bill purported to be was a bank incorporated by law. *Kennedy v. Com., 2 Met. 36; Clark v. Com., 16 B. M. 206; and to identify with reasonable certainty the coin. Com. v. Fields, 5 R. 610; Waller v. Com., 97 Ky. 509, 17 R. 348, 30 S. W. 1023;* and see Ky. Stat., sec. 1181.
16. Defacing brands on logs-requi-

# § 122 Indictment-Form and Requisites

sites of indictment for. Com. v. Puckett, 92 Ky. 206, 13 R. 547, 17 S. W. 353; Johnson v. Com., 14 R. 257, 20 S. W. 200; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 1409.

(29) Directness and certainty in stating offense, see sec. 124 and notes.

(30) Dueling. See Moody v. Com., 4 Met. 1; Ward v. Com., 132 Ky. 636, 116 S. W. 786; and Ky. Stat., sec. 1269.

(31) Embezzlement. Ky. Stat., sec. 1202, and Shelburn v. Com., 85 Ky. 173, 8 R. 832, 3 S. W. 7; holding that a clerk, business manager or other agent is not guilty of embezzlement where money converted by him was collected by him for his principal and with his authority; and see Com. v. Clifford, 96 Ky. 4, 16 R. 184, 27 S. W. 811; Bailey v. Com., 130 Ky. 301, 113 S. W. 140; Morse v. Com., 129 Ky. 294, 111 S. W. 714. Nor is agent of individual who fails to pay over money to his principal guilty of embezzlement. Com. v. Bull, 5 R. 605. Indictment need not specify the coin or number or denomination of money taken. Jones v. Com., 13 Bush 356; nor is amount of money material. Com. v. Smith, 26 R. 517, 82 S. W. 236.

(32) Generally where the agent has received goods or money to carry, deliver or control for his principal, unless the agent parts with the manual possession and delivers the property to the principal or another for him or places it in some depository to which the principal has access, or over which he has control, he cannot be convicted of larceny for a felonious appropriation of it, the offense being embezzlement. Warmoth v. Com., 81 Ky. 133, 4 R. 937; Johnson v. Com., 5 Bush 430; Barclay v. Breckinridge, 4 Met. 374; Snapp v. Com., 82 Ky. 173, 6 R. 34.

(34a) The use of the word "getting" instead of "obtaining" in an indictment for obtaining money under false pretenses does not render the indictment defective. Hale v. Com., 151 Ky. 639, 152 S. W. 773.

(34b) An indictment for obtaining money by false pretenses described the money as "the sum of $2.50." This was a sufficient description. Hayes v. Com., 173 Ky. 188, 190 S. W. 700.

(34c) An indictment under Ky. Stat., sec. 1213b, for making a false statement concerning one's financial condition must set out the false statement. Gardner v. Com., 164 Ky. 196, 175 S. W. 362.

(35) False swearing. One cannot be punished for false swearing unless the false oath was taken on a subject on which he could be legally sworn and before a person legally authorized to administer an oath. Kerfoot v. Com., 89 Ky. 174, 11 R. 391, 12 S. W. 189; Com. v. Kane, 92 Ky. 457, 13 R. 655, 18 S. W. 7; Ford v. Com., 16 R. 528, 29 S. W. 446; but see Com. v. Turner, 98 Ky. 526, 17 R. 925, 33 S. W. 88; indictment should follow.

# Title VI

# Indictment-Form and Requisites

# [§ 122]

low precise words of statute. Com. v. Taylor, 96 Ky. 394, 16 R. 482, 20 S. W. 138; see Kv. Stat., sec. 1l74; and Goslin v. Com., 121 Ky. 698, 28 R. 683, 90 S. W. 223.

(35a) The omission of the word "knowingly" in an indictment for false swearing did not vitiate the indictment Fisher v. Com., 152 Ky. 411, 153 S. W. 417.

(36) An indictment for false swearing under the statute will be sufficient if it allege that the false oath was taken knowingly and wilfully on a subject concerning which the party could be legally sworn, and before a person legally authorized to administer the oath. It is not necessary that the false oath should be taken in a matter judicially pending at the time, or concerning a matter material to any point in issue. Com. v. Powell, 2 Met. 10; and see Com. v. Still, 83 Ky. 275, 8 R. 208; Richoy v. Com., 81 Ky. 524, 5 R. 543; Biggerstaff v. Com. 11 Bush 169; Com. v. Maynard, 91 Ky. 131, 12 R. 710, 15 S. W. 52; Spencer v. Com., 15 R. 182, 22 S. W. 559; Goslin v. Com., 121 Ky. 698. 28 R. 683. 90 S. W. 223; Com. v. Schwieters, 122 Ky. 847, 93 S. W. 592, 29 R. 417.

(37) The matter alleged to have been falsely sworn to must be negatived by special averment in the indictment. Com. v. Kane, 92 Ky. 457. 13 R. 655, 18 S. W. 7; Com. v. Still, 83 Ky. 275, 8 R. 208; Com. v. Porter, 17 R. 554, 32 S. W. 138; see further, notes to sec. 134.

(37a) False swearing is a felony if the testimony be given on a subject on which the accused is required to be sworn. Sullivan v. Com., 158 Ky. 536, 165 S. W. 696.

(38) Forgery. To charge that the accused "did forge the name of A to an instrument of writing purporting to be the promissory note of M for $380, payable to C fourteen months after date, and dated 4th of March, 187?, with intent to defraud," is not sufficient; if the indictment had charged that the accused signed A's name to the instrument without authority, or that he procured somebody else to sign it, with the intention to defraud, it may have been good. The acts constituting the offense must be set out. Stowers v. Com., 12 Push 342; in Barnes v. Com., 101 Ky. 556. 19 R. 803, 41 S. W. 772; it is held to be necessary to aver the name of the person or corporation intended to be defrauded or to state that name is unknown; and it is also necessary to charge in an indictment for forging names to a paper that the names were signed without the authority of the persons. Com. v. Bowman, 86 Ky. 40, 16 R. 222, 27 S. W. 816; and see Mount v. Com., 1 Duv. 90; Com. v. Harrison, 17 R. 343, 30 S. W. 1009; Com. v. Lemon, 18 R. 480, 37 S. W. 61; and see sec. 133, and Ky. Stat., sec. 1185.

(38a) An indictment for alleged forgery in making a fraudulent tabulation of the votes cast at a Democratic primary in 191l failed to allege that the accused acted in pursuance of a duty imposed on him by the Democratic state central committee. A demurrer to the indictment was rightly sustained. Com. v. Anderson, 151 Ky. 537, 152 S. W. 552.

(38b) Under an indictment for forgery, the defendant cannot be convicted of uttering a forged note. Snyder v. Com., 9 Ky. Opinions 29.

(39) Indictment alleging that defendant did "utter and publish" as true a certain false, "forged and counterfeited" order charged but a single offense. Johnson v. Com., 90 Ky. 488, 12 R. 442, 14 S. W. 492; but an indictment that charges both forgery and uttering is bad, Messer v. Com., 26 R. 40, 80 S. W. 489. One may be guilty of forgery by signing a writing in his own name if it be false in any material part and calculated to induce another to give credit to it as genuine. Com. v. Wilson, 89 Ky. 157, 1I R. 375, 12 S. W. 264; Com. v. Brewer, 113 Ky. 217, 24 R. 72, 67 S. W. 994.

(40) Indictment for uttering a forged writing must state the acts constituting the uttering and not the forging of the writing. An allegation in the indictment that defendant knew the writing to be false embraces the charge that it was so in fact. Lockard v. Com., 87 Ky. 201, 10 R. 102. 8 S. W. 266: Purvis v. Com., 13 R. 744. 18 S. W. 357; see further as to forgery of bank check, Rawlins v. Com., 7 R. 595; Kennedy v. Com., 2 Met. 36; Com. v. Lee, 18 R. 484, 37 S. W. 72; as to alteration or erasure of writing, Com. v. Walls, 27 R. 739, 86 S. W. 684; Com. v. Hide, 94 Ky. 517, 15 R. 264, 23 S. W. 195; as to forgery of witness certificate, Moore v. Com., 92 Ky. 630, 13 R. 738, 18 S. W. 833.

(41) Former convictions. Necessary allegations in indictment charging former convictions. Boggs v. Com., 9 R. 342; Mount v. Com., 2 Duv. 93; Taylor v. Com., 3 R. 783; Stewart v. Com., 2 R. 386; Chenowith v. Com., 11 R. 563, 12 S. W. 585; Sweeney v. Com., 18 R. 1020, 39 S. W. 22; Oliver v. Com., 113 Ky. 228. 24 R. 84, 67 S. W. 983; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 1130.

# § 122] Indictment-Form and Requisites.

# (42) Gaming.

An indictment charging the defendant with permitting "a game of chance called pigeon-hole" to be played for "greenbacks" in his house is good on demurrer. *Coin. v. Branham, 3 Bush 1.*

# (43) An indictment for setting up and keeping a keno table

must aver that it is a contrivance ordinarily used for gaming. *Com. v. Monarch, 6 Bush 298; and see Ritte v. Com., 18 B. M. 35; Ky. Stat., sec. 1960; see as to indictment for permitting slot machine on premises. Com. v. Schatzman, 26 R. 508, 82 S. W. 238; and permitting minor to play on pool table. Com. v. Willis, 26 R. 515, 82 S. W. 236.*

# (44) In an indictment for setting up, exhibiting and keeping a faro bank,

it is sufficient to allege that it was set up, exhibited and kept, without adding any words descriptive or explanatory of the purpose for which it was or may be used, the faro bank being mentioned in the statute by name. *Waddell v. Com., 84 Ky. 276, 8 R. 249, 1 S. W. 480; Com. v. Monarch, 6 Bush 298; Miller v. Com., 25 R. 1236, 77 S. W. 682; Com. v. Estes, 121 S. W. 423; Ky. Stat., sec. 1960.*

# (45) An indictment charging that defendant suffered certain named persons

"to play in a house or on premises in the county aforesaid, then in the occupation and under the control of the said Perrigo, a game of cards, at which game of cards, played as aforesaid, money or property was won and lost," is not sufficient, being too indefinite. *Com. v. Perrigo, 3 Met. 5; Com. v. Schatzman, 26 R. 508, 82 S. W. 238.*

# (46) An indictment charging that the defendant

"was the owner, occupier and controller of a house and ten-pin alley at which games were played and money and other things bet, won and lost by his permission, and that he and others played at such games, and money and other valuable things were bet, lost and won thereon" is sufficient. *Com. v. Fraize, 5 Bush 325.*

# (47) A corporation may be indicted

for permitting gaming on its premises. *Com. v. Pulaski A. and M. Association, 92 Ky. 197, 13 R. 468, 17 S. W. 442.*

# (48) Indictment charging that defendant did

"unlawfully set up, exhibit and keep for himself and another" a faro bank, charges but a single offense. *Vowells v. Com., 84 Ky. 52, 8 R. 74; see Ky. Stat., sec. 1960.*

# (48a) An indictment for suffering gaming on premises

followed the language of Ky. Stat., sec. 1978; it was sufficient. *Lancaster Hotel Co. v. Com., 149 Ky. 443, 149 S. W. 942.*

# (48b) Hog stealing.

An indictment which charged the defendant with stealing hogs belonging to John R. Perry and others, and which did not charge that the hogs were joint property, was bad on demurrer. *Stephens v. Com., 164 Ky. 265, 175 S. W. 353.*

# (48c) Homicide.

A certain indictment for homicide was sufficient. *Rose v. Com., 156 Ky. 817, 162 S. W. 107.*

# (49) Housebreaking.

See Ky. Stat., sec. 1162; and *Com. v. Ward, 14 Bush 233, where it is held that indictment must charge that the taking was with the intention to deprive the owner of his property or to convert it to the use of the accused; but in Com. v. Wicker, 9 R. 474, 5 S. W. 428, the court say it is not necessary to allege the name of the owner of the property taken, nor to aver that it was taken without the owner's consent and with the intention to convert it to his own use; to the same effect is Mitchell v. Com., 88 Ky. 349, 10 R. 910, 11 S. W. 209; where Com. v. Ward, 14 Bush 233. is distinguished; and Johnson v. Com., 87 Ky. 189, 10 R. 100, 7 S. W. 927;* holding that it is not necessary to describe the owner of the house.

# (50) Infanticide.

An indictment charging that the defendant "did feloniously conceal the birth of a bastard child, the issue of her body, by secreting the said child so that it might not be known whether or not it had been born alive" is not good; the acts showing what the defendant did should have been set out. *Foster v. Com., 12 Bush 373; and see Com. v. Hopkins, 9 R. 432, 5 S. W. 392; Wade v. Com., 1 R. 408; Frey v. Com., 83 Ky. 190, 7 R. 174; Clark v. Com., 111 Ky. 443, 23 R. 1029, 63 S. W. 740; and Ky. Stat., sec. 1220.*

Title VI] Indictment-Form and Requisites. [§ 122

(51) Injury to property. Sufficiency of indictment under secs. 1256, 1257, Ky. Stat.; Ellis v. Com., 78 Ky. 130; Com. v. Powell. 8 Bush 7; Com. v. Searls, 3 R. 394.

(52) Joint indictment against several. If it appear in proof that one is guilty, he may be punished the same as if indicted alone. Bush 196. Weatherford v. Com., 10.

(53) Larceny. An indictment charging the defendant with feloniously stealing, taking and carrying away "one twenty-dollar note and one ten-dollar note, both of said notes being of the United States currency commonly called greenbacks. Said notes are not and were not before the grand jury, and could not by said jury be had before it, because the whereabouts of said notes was not and could not be known to said jury; therefore, said notes cannot be more particularly described, either as to their date, number, or as to whether they belonged to the class technically called green or black backs," is sufficient under sec. 135. Jones v. Com., 13 Bush 356; Com. v. Mann, 12 R. 477, 14 S. W. 685; Bailey v. Com., 22 R. 512, 58 S. W. 425; Ky. Stat., sec. 1194.

(54) An allegation that the property stolen was "the property of the Tennessee River Packet Company, D. W. Swan, Little Brothers and others," is sufficient as to the ownership of the property. Reed v. Com., 7 Bush 641; and see Porter v. Com., 22 R. 1657, 61 S. W. 16; an: sec. 128 and notes.

(55) As to what constitutes larceny. see Eiliott v. Com., 12 Bush 176; Nichols v. Com., 78 Ky. 180; Williams v. Com. 78 Ky. 93; Miller v. Com., 78 Ky. 15; Warmouth v. Com., 81 Ky. 133, 4 R. 937; Snapp v. Com., 82 Ky. 173, 6 R. 34; Ky. Stat., sec. 1194.

(56) Chandeliers attached to the freehold are held to be the subject of larceny, and it is immaterial whether the carrying away was immediate and continuous, or the removal was at different periods after severance, Smith v. Com., 14 Bush 31.

(57) Local option. "A person may in the same indictment be charged with more than one violation of the local option law, as it is called; but each offense should be separately charged, and the statement of the particular circumstances of each should be direct and certain." South v. Com., 79 Kv. 493, 3 R. 276; and see Cockerell v. Com., 115 Kv. 296, 24 R. 2149, 73 S. W. 760; Jones v. Com., 104 Ky. 468, 20 R. 651, 47 S. W. 328; indicating how indictment should be drawn; and Ky. Stats., sec. 2257, 2557a, b.

(57a) An indictment for the offense of delivering in local option territory goods brought by a carrier from another state did not show that the liquor was intended to be received or used in violation of any law of Kentucky. The indictment was insufficient. Adams Exp. Co. v. Com., 154 Ky. 462, 157 S. W. 908.

(58) Physician -indictment against under local option law. Com. v. Green, 80 Ky. 178, 3 R. 659; Com. v. Minor, 88 Ky. 422, 10 R. 1008, 11 S. W. 472; Sarrlls v. Com., 83 Ky. 327, 7 R. 473.

(59) Lottery. As to indictments for selling lottery tickets, see Com. v. Bierman, 13 Bush 345; Com. v. Bull. 13 Bush 656. For promoting and aiding the promotion of a lottery, see Miller v. Com., 13 Bush 731; and Ky. Stat., sec. 2573.

(59a) Morphine. An indictment for illegal sale of morphine, under the act of 1912, Ky. Stat., sec. 3766c, is not defective for failure to state that morphine is a derivative of opium. Com. v. Gabhart, 160 Ky. 32, 169 S. W. 514.

(60) Murder. An indictment charging murder in the following language, to-wit: "The said William White heretofore, to-wit: on the - day of April, 1871, in the Commonwealth and county aforesaid, did unlawfully, wilfully, maliciously, feloniously and of his malice aforethought, kill and murder his wife, one Nancy White," is insufficient, in that it fails to state the acts showing how offense was committed. White v. Com., 9 Bush 178. Where the indictment charges that the defendant did "kill and murder" it is not necessary to add that the deceased died within a year and a day. Jane v. Com., 3 Met. 18; and see Alford v. Com., 84 Ky. 623, 8 R. 550, 2 S. W. 234. See Ky. Stat., sec. 1149.

(60a) A certain indictment for willful murder was sufficient. Greer v. Comn., 164 Ky. 396, 175 S. W. 665.

(60b) It is no objection to an indictment charging two persons with murder by shooting with a pistol and cutting with a knife that the particular acts constituting the offense were not severally charged against each defendant, it being claimed that one of them did the shooting, and the other the cutting. MacGehee v. Com., 181 Ky. 422.

(61) An indictment for a common law offense (murder) should charge that the act was done "feloniously," or "with a felonious intent." Kaelin v. Com., 84 Ky. 354, 8 R. 293, 1 S. W. 594; Stroud.

# §122 Indictment--Form and Requisites

v. Com., 14 R. 179, 19 S. W. 976. Where the indictment charges that the defend- ant shot the deceased, inflicting wounds from which death resulted, it is not necessary to aver that the pistol with which the shooting was done was a deadly weapon, or that it was at the time loaded with a leaden ball or other hard substance. Jeffries v. Com., 84 Ky. 237, 8 R. 237, 1 S. W. 442; as to variance between indictment and evidence, see Helmerking v. Com., 100 Ky. 74, 18 R. 576, 37 S. W. 264.

(62) An indictment in which the offense charged is murder is good, although the particular circumstances of the crime as set out in the indictment constitute an accessory before the fact and not a principal. Stricklin v. Com., 83 Ky. 566, 7 R. 627. See further, Hocker v. Com., 111 S. W. 676.

(63) An indictment charging that The said Matt F. Lightfoot, in the county aforesaid, did wilfully, feloniously and with malice aforethought, kill and murder Joseph McDermott, with a pistol, loaded with powder and leaden ball and other such substance, which he, the said Lightfoot, then and there held in his hands, is not aptly drawn, as it does not appear whether the accused shot the deceased or beat him over the head with the pistol, but there being no demurrer it was held sufficient after verdict. Lightfoot v. Com., 80 Ky. 516, 4 R. 463.

(64) Nuisance. As to indictment for nuisance, see C. R. Co. v. Com., 80 Ky. 137, 3 R. 639; P. & E. R. R. Co. v. Com., 80 Ky. 147, 3 R. 650; L., C. & L. R. R. Co. v. Com., 80 Ky. 143, 3 R. 644; Com. v. T. J. Megibben Co., 101 Ky. 195, 19 R. 291, 40 S. W. 694; Com. v. C. B. Cook Co., 102 Ky. 288, 19 R. 1336, 43 S. W. 400; as to indictment for disorderly house, see Com. v. Bessler, 97 Ky. 498, 17 R. 357, 30 S. W. 1012; Cheek v. Com., 100 Ky. 1, 18 R. 515, 37 S. W. 152; Ehrlick v. Com., 125 Ky. 742, 102 S. W. 289, 31 R. 320; I. C. R. Co. v. Com., 96 S. W. 467, 29 R. 750; Com. v. C. & O. R. Co., 128 Ky. 749, 10 S. W. 253; Com. v. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry. Co., 112 S. W. 613; Indian Refining Co. v. Com., 117 S. W. 274.

(64a) An indictment against a railroad company for a nuisance failed to show that the things complained of were unnecessarily done. The indictment was bad on demurrer. L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Com., 158 Ky. 773, 166 S. W. 237.

(64b) An indictment charging a nuisance against a railroad company maintained where a public road crosses the railroad tracks was sufficient as it clearly showed that the nuisance was within the defendant's right-of-way. L. & N. R. Co. v. Com., 149 Ky. 459, 149 S. W. 898.

(65) Peddling. See Com. v. Smith, 6 Bush 303; Monk v. Com., 6 Bush 397; Com. v. Dudley, 3 Met. 221; and Ky. Stat., sec. 4215; peddlers of patent rights cannot be required to pay a license tax. Com. v. Petty, 96 Ky. 452, 16 R. 488, 29 S. W. 291.

(60) Perjury. Ky. Stat., rec. 1173, and Com. v. Maynard, 91 Ky. 131, 12 R. 710, 15 S. W. 52; holding that perjury is the wilful giving under oath in a judicial proceeding or in the course of justice of false testimony material to the issue or point of inquiry; and indictment must negative by special averment the matter alleged to have been sworn and expressly aver that it was material or state facts so showing, and also charge that defendant was sworn by one authorized to administer an oath; and see Com. v. Powell, 2 Met. 10; Dowdy v. Com., 13 R. 350, 17 S. W. 187; Com. v. Compton, 18 Ky. 479, 36 S. W. 1116; Com. v. Still, 83 Ky. 275, 8 R. 208, and see sec. 131 and notes. Com. v. Miles, 140 Ky. 577, 131 S. W. 385; Stamper v. Com., 100 S. W. 286, 30 R. 994; and see notes 35, 36.

(66a) Pool room. A certain indictment for operating a pool room was held to be sufficient. Com. v. Starr, 160 Ky. 260, 169 S. W. 743.

(66b) Detaining a woman. An indictment that fails to charge that the accused feloniously detained the female against her will for the purpose, or with the intent, of having carnal knowledge of her, is not good. Wilder v. Com., 81 Ky. 591, 5 R. 635; Evans v. Com., 79 Ky. 414, 3 R. 30.

(67) Rape. See Blair v. Com., 7 Bush 227; Kessler v. Com., 12 Bush 18; Fenston v. Com., 82 Ky. 549, 7 R. 55; Bethel v. Com., 80 Ky. 526, 4 R. 487; Com. v. Lowe, 116 Ky. 335, 25 R. 534, 76 S. W. 119; Reed v. Com., 25 R. 1029, 76 S. W. 638; Com. v. Landis, 112 S. W. 581, 129 Ky. 445; Jones v. Com., 124 Ky. 26, 30 R. 288, 97 S. W. 1118; and Ky. Stat., sec. 1152.

(68) Receiving illegal vote. See Com. v. Gray, 2 Duv. 373, and Ky. Stat., sec. 1583.

(68a) Receiving stolen goods. If an indictment for receiving stolen goods fails to describe the goods sufficiently, the indictment will be bad on demurrer; but this is no ground for an arrest of judgment. Duncan v. Com., 165 Ky. 247, 176 S. W. 984.

Title VI Indictment-Form and Requisites

# § 122

(68b) In framing an indictment under Ky. Stat., sec. 1199, for knowingly receiving stolen goods, a general charge that the goods had been stolen before the defendant received them is sufficient. A general description of the property originally stolen is sufficient. An allegation in the indictment that the goods were originally stolen from the O. W. Company is a sufficient allegation of ownership by that company. *Newton v. Com., 158 Ky. 4, 164 S. W. 108.*

(69) Robbery. An indictment charging the defendant with having feloniously taken a mare, the property of William Rayburn, "in his presence, and against his will, by putting him in fear of some immediate injury to his person" is sufficient. *Com. v. Brooks, 1 Div. 150; Breckinridge v. Com., 97 Ky. 267, 17 R. 163, 30 S. W. 634; and see Ward v. Com., 14 Bush 233; Dickerson v. Com., 2 Bush 1; Taylor v. Com., 3 Bush 508; Lisle v. Com., 82 Ky. 250, 6 R. 229: Ky. Stat., sec. 1159; larceny is a degree of the offense. Com. v. Prewitt, 82 Ky. 240, 6 R. 195; as is an assault to rob. See Barnard v. Com., 94 Ky. 285, 15 R. 51, 22 S. W. 219; Brown v. Com., 135 Ky. 635, 117 S. W. 281.*

(70) Seduction-as to requisites of indictment for. see *Davis v. Com., 98 Ky. 708, 17 R. 1265, 34 S. W. 699; Com. v. McNutt, 133 Ky. 702, 118 S. W. 979; Ky. Stat., sec. 1214.*

(70a) No new indictment is necessary where a defendant in a prosecution under Ky. Stat., sec. 1214, has married the prosecuting witness and abandoned her within three years. The Commonwealth, however, may dismiss the original indictment and reindict. *Miller v. Com., 154 Ky. 201, 157 S. W. 373.*

(71) Selling liquor. An indictment against a merchant for selling liquor without license must state to whom the sale was made. *Wilson v. Com., 14 Bush 159. See Com. v. White, 18 B. M. 492. An indictment for selling liquor to an inebriate must allege that the defendant knew the party was an inebriate. Com. v. Bell, 14 Bush 433; Ky. Stat., sec. 1307; and see Com. v. Ewing, 7 Bush 105; Com. v. Davis, 12 Bush 240; Megowan v. Com., 2 Met. 3; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 1307. For selling liquor to a minor. See Ky. Stat., sec. 1306, and Com. v. Haderaft, 6 Bush 91.*

(71a) In an indictment for the unlawful sale of malt liquor to an infant without the written consent of the father, it is not necessary to aver that the infant is not the child of the accused. *Ran v. Com., 9 Ky. Opinions 10.*

(72) Indictment for selling "spirituous, vinous or malt liquor" is bad, "or" should be "and." *Raubold v. Com., 111 Ky. 433, 23 R. 735, 63 S. W. 781.*

(73) Several counts-indictment may contain several counts and in one charge accused as principals and in others as aiders and abettors. *Cupp v. Com., 87 Ky. 35, 9 R. 877. 7 S. W. 405; Gambrell v. Com., 23 R. 502, 63 S. W. 272; and note 92.*

(73a) Several counts-failure to name county in one of them. An indictment was in two counts, of which the first charged the accused as principal and the second count as accessory. The first count stated the county in which the offense was committed. The fact that there was no statement of the county in the second count did not in any way vitiate the indictment. *Hollin v. Com., 163 Ky. 392, 173 S. W. 1106.*

(74) Statutory offenses. Sufficiency of indictment for, see sec. 136 and notes.

(75) Sunday - for working on. See requisites of petition in penal action to recover penalty for. *Com. v. L. & N. R. R., 80 Ky. 291, 3 R. 788; L. & N. R. R. v. Com., 92 Ky. 114, 13 R. 430, 17 S. W. 274.*

(70) Tavern. An indictment against a tavern-keeper for failing to set up in a public room a list of rates must allege when the rates were fixed by the county court, or that they had been fixed one month before the offense was committed. *Jackson v. Com., 7 Bush 99.*

(76a) The words in an indictment "being a tavern-keeper" do not amount to a charge that the defendant was a licensed tavern-keeper. *Com. v. Cooney, 9 Ky. Opinions 1; Com. v. Brooks, 9 Ky. Opinions 1.*

(77) Time-allegation as to an indictment; see sec. 129 and notes.

(78) Trespass. An indictment properly describing the offense denounced in sec. 7, art. 28, chap. 39, Gen. Stat. (now Ky. Stat., sec. 1256), but terming it an indictment for "malicious mischief" instead of "wilful trespass," is good. *Com. v. Smith, 6 Bush 263.*

(79) An indictment charging that the defendant "unlawfully and without felonious intent destroyed, injured and carried away timber from four trees on the premises of John W. Wash, which said property belonged to the said Wash and was taken without his consent," is sufficient. *Com. v. Powell, 8 Bush 7; and see Ellis v. Com., 78 Ky. 130.*

# Indictment-Form and Requisites

# (79a) Unlawful assembly.

In an indictment for an unlawful assembly it is necessary to allege that the parties assembled with intent to aid each other and to charge the thing intended to be done. *Downes v. Com., 9 Ky. Opinions 112.*

# (80) Unlawfully detaining a woman.

See Ky. Stat., sec. 1158; and *Wilder v. Com., 81 Ky. 591, 5 R. 635; Gargill v. Com., 12 R. 149, 13 S. W. 916; Owens v. Com., 5 R. 174; Higgins v. Com., 94 Ky. 54, 14 R. 729, 21 S. W. 231.*

# (81) Unnecessary particularity.

If a person or thing necessary to be mentioned in an indictment is described with circumstances of greater particularity than is requisite, then those circumstances must be proved. *Clark v. Com., 16 B. M. 207; but an unnecessary and immaterial averment will be regarded as surplusage and it is not necessary to prove it. Com. v. Jarboe, 89 Ky. 143, 11 R. 344, 12 S. W. 138.*

# (81a) An indictment under Ky. Stat., sec. 124la,

contained averments not necessary to describe or constitute the offense; the Commonwealth was not bound to prove them. *Com. v. Hightower, 149 Ky. 563, 149 S. W. 971.*

# (82) Usurpation.

For requisites of an indictment for usurping the office of judge of an election, see *Wayman v. Com., 14 Bush 466. Usurpation of an office, see Ky. Stat., sec. 1364; Wayman v. Com., 14 Bush 446; Com. v. Adams, 3 Met. 6; Com. v. Jones, 10 Bush 725.*

# (82a) Uttering forged check.

An indictment for uttering a forged check is insufficient if it fails to state under what authority the bank is doing business. *Mason (Alias Scott) v. Com., 156 Ky. 493, 161 S. W. 229.*

# (83) Warehouse.

For requisites of indictment under the act in relation to warehousemen, approved March 6, 1869, Ky. Stat., sec. 4768; see *Com. v. Mason, 82 Ky. 256, 6 R. 244.*

# (84) Weapon.

Under an indictment charging that defendant carried "concealed deadly weapons, to-wit, a bowie knife, and also a dirk or dagger," proof that he carried either will authorize a conviction. *Com. v. Howard, 3 Met. 407; Ky. Stat., sec. 1309.*

# (85) Wilful killing.

Under sec. 1151, Ky. Stat., see *Conner v. Com., 13 Bush 714.*

# (86) Wounding.

Indictment under Ky. Stat., sec. 1166, need not charge that act was done feloniously. *Cundiff v. Com., 26 Ky. 196, 9 R. 537, 5 S. W. 486; and Burns v. Com., 3 Met. 13; Flint v. Com., 81 Ky. 186, 5 R. 51; Gratz v. Com., 96 Ky. 162, 16 R. 465, 28 S. W. 159; as to wounding in sudden heat and passion, as described in sec. 1242, Ky. Stat., see Com. v. Hawkins, 11 Bush 603; Sosh v. Com., 4 R. 254; Com. v. Duncan, 91 Ky. 592, 13 R. 162, 16 S. W. 530.*

# (86a) An indictment which charged two persons

with having maliciously wounded a person with the intent to kill him is not bad as charging more than one offense; it charges but one offense. *Com. v. Lawson, 165 Ky. 4, 176 S. W. 359.*

# (87) Filing away-effect of

is to postpone indefinitely and court has no authority to so order without consent of Commonwealth's attorney. *Com. v. Cundiff, 149 Ky. 37.*

# (88) Manslaughter-requisites of good indictment.

*Com. v. Mosser, 133 Ky. 609, 118 S. W. 915.*

# (88a) It was error to overrule a motion

of the Commonwealth to dismiss a certain indictment for manslaughter with leave to resubmit. *Com. v. McCandless, 156 Ky. 793, 162 S. W. 79.*

# (89) Bill of particulars-when may be required.

*Com. v. C. & O. R. Co., 128 Ky. 740, 110 S. W. 253.*

# (90) Election offenses.

See *Com. v. Drewry, 126 Ky. 183, 103 S. W. 266, 31 R. 632; Com. v. Kaufman, 126 Ky. 536, 104 S. W. 740, 31 R. 1064; Com. v. Goulett, 137 Ky. 464, 125 S. W. 1083.*

# (90a) Betting on election.

An indictment charging that the defendant bet the sum of $5.00 on the election for county court clerk of Henderson county in 1874 did not aver that the bet related to the election or defeat of any person voted for at the election. The indictment was insufficient. *Com. v. Cross, 9 Ry. Opinions 109.*

# (91) Agent of corporation transacting business without license—requisites of indictment.

*Com. v. Loving, 92 S. W. 575, 29 R. 175.*

# (92) Counts in indictment.

See for full discussion of right to charge offense in different counts. *Com. v. Hargis, 124 Ky. 356, 99 S. W. 348, 30 R. 510; Anderson v. Com., 144 Ky. 215, 137 S. W. 1063.*

# (93) Writing that is foundation of indictment

should be set out in full or its absence accounted for. *Kinnard v. Com., 134 Ky. 575, 121 S. W. 489.*

# (94) Sufficiency of indictment generally

in its description of the offense charged-only necessary that it should advise accused of nature of crime. *Overstreet v. Com., 147 Ky. 471, 144 S. W. 751; Com. v. Drewry, 126 Ky. 183, 103 S. W. 266, 31 R. 632.*

Title VI] Indictment-Form and Requisites. [9 123-124

# § 123 [122] [Form of indictment.]

It may be substantially of the following form:

The Commonwealth of Kentucky against Franklin Circuit Court.

George Smith.

The grand jury of Franklin county, in the name and by the authority of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, accuse George Smith of the crime of [here give the name of the offense, if it have one, such as treason, murder, manslaughter, arson, larceny, rape, etc., or the like, or, if it have no general name, then a brief general description as given by law], committed as follows, viz.: The said George Smith, on the - day of —, 19-.-, in the county aforesaid [here insert the acts constituting the offense], against the peace and dignity of the Commonwealth of Kentucky. (See other forms, Forms 243-274.)

# § 124 [12s] [Facts concerning which it must be direct and certain.]

The indictment must be direct and certain as regards—

(95) An indictment under Ky. Stat. sec. íl55, which used the words "wilfully and feloniously" and did not use the word "unlawfully" was good on demurrer. Clark v. Com., 165 Ky. 472, 177 S. W. 251.

123. (1 Form. It is not necessary now that an indictment founded on a statute should conclude "against the form of the statute." Com. v. Kennedy, 15 B. M. 53l; nor is it necessary when the indictment charges the offense was committed to add "in the year of our Lord," or the letters A. D. Com. v. Tray. lo1, 20 R. 97, 45 S. W. 356, 450.

It is not necessary that the style of the court, or the case, or the formal part of the indictment shall be repeated in each count when there is more than one count. Greenwood v. Coni., 11 R. 220, 11 S. W. 8l1; and see Mitchell v. Com., 106 Ky. 602, 21 R. 222, 51 S. W. 17.

Caption of indictment, omitting name of county, does not render indictment bad. Johnson v. Com., 12 R. 835, 15 S. W. AB2.

Failure of Commonwealth's attorney to sign indictment does not render it defective. Brown v. Com., 135 Ky. 635, 117 S. W. 281.

(5) See notes to sec. 122.

124. (1) Certainty in stating offense. A conviction for stealing Philip Daniel's hog cannot be sustained under an indictment for stealing Stephen Daniel's hog. Hensley v. Com., 1 Bush 11.

An indictment for murder described the person murdered as "Barbara Kriel." The proof disclosed her name to be "Margaret Kriel." The variance was held not material. Kriel v. Com., 5 Bush 362; see secs. 125-128.

(2) On the trial of an indictment for stealing the horse of A, evidence that the horse stolen belonged to B does not warrant a conviction, unless the offense be sufficiently described in other respects to identify the property and the act. McBride v. Com., 13 Bush 337; and see Carter v. Com., 25 R. 688, 76 S. W. 337; Riffe v. Com., 21 R. 1331, 56 S. W. 265; Williams v. Com., 78 Ky. 93; and sec. 128.

(3) In a trial for bigamy it appeared that first wife was sometimes called Mag Sharp (her real name) and sometimes Mag White, while the indictment charged her name as Mag White; held not a fatal variance. Robinson v. Com., 88 Ky. 386, 10 R. 972, 11 S. W. 210.

(4) It is essential that the indictment shall state correctly the county in which the offense is committed. When a change of venue is obtained and a new indictment found in the county to which the venue is taken, it should state correctly the county in which the offense was committed. Parker v. Com., 12 Bush 191.

An indictment charging that offense was committed "in the county and circuit aforesaid" was held sufficient, although county was not named. Armstrong v. Com., 16 R. 494, 29 S. W. 342; but the indictment must charge what offense.

# §124 Indictment-Form and Requisites.

1. [The party.] The party charged.
2. [The offense.] The offense charged.
3. [The county.] The county in which the offense was committed.
4. [The circumstances of the offense.] The particular circumstances of the offense charged, if they be necessary to constitute a complete offense.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 966, 970.

was committed in addition to describing it. Brooks v. Com., 98 Ky. 143, 17 R. 608, 32 S. W. 403.

(4a) The court took judicial notice that Burlington is in Boone county. It was the county seat. Com. v. Von Bogeon, 9 Ky. Opinions 142.

(6b) If an indictment, for receiving stolen goods, fails to describe the goods sufficiently, the indictment will be bad on demurrer; but this is no ground for arrest of judgment. Duncan v. Com., 165 Ky. 247, 176 S. W. 984.

(6c) An indictment for false entry in bank books was held sufficient. Head v. Com., 165 Ky. 339, 176 S. W. 1162.

(5a) A certain indictment was held insufficient, because although it stated the particular circumstances of the offense charged, it did not name the offense. Bennett v. Com., 150 Ky. 604, 150 S. W. 806.

(6) "It is essential that an indictment contain a statement of the acts constituting the offense in ordinary and concise language, and in such a manner as to enable a person of common understanding to know what is intended." An indictment charging murder in the following words is insufficient: "The said William White heretofore, to-wit, on the - day of April, 187l, in the Commonwealth and county aforesaid did unlawfully, wilfully and feloniously cut, thrust and stab John Reffit with a knife, from which cutting and stabbing said Reffit did not die." is sufficient. Shouse v. Com., 05 Ky. 621. 16 R. 142, 26 S. W. 814; and see Gratz v. Com., 96 Ky. 162, 16 R. 465, 28 S. W. 159; Com. v. Tupman, 17 R. 217, 30 S. W. 661.

(9) Under a statute providing that if a person "sell to any person in any quantity whisky, brandy, or other spirituous liquor" an indictment charging "that Charles White on the 1st day of July, 1857, in the county aforesaid, did unlawfully sell whisky, brandy and other spirituous liquors" was held insufficient in not being specific enough. Com. v. White, 18 B.M. 492; but see notes 57-58, and 71, 72, sec. 122.

(6a) In an indictment for the embezzlement of personal property, the property must be described with the same particularity as in an indictment for larceny. The description must be such as to give the accused notice of the circumstances of the exact accusation which he is called upon to answer. Clary v. Com., 163 Ky. 48, 173 S. W. 171.

(10) Statutory offenses - indictment for. "It is well settled that where provisions and exceptions are contained in...

Title VI Indictment-Form and Requisites

# § 125

[Error in name of defendant may be corrected.] An error as to the name of the defendant shall not vitiate the indictment, nor proceedings thereon, and if his true name be discovered at any time before execution, an entry shall be made on the record of the court of his true name, referring to the fact of his being indicted by the name mentioned in distinct clauses (of a statute) it is not necessary to aver in the indictment that the defendant does not come within the exceptions, or to negative the provisos it contains.

Com. v. MeClanahan, 2 Met. 8; and see further, sec. 136 and notes.

# (10a)

The accusatory part of an indictment charged the accused with the offense of feloniously breaking and entering into a house of another and so forth, but the descriptive portion showed it was a retail liquor house. The indictment was not demurrable, but was a good indictment under Ky. Stat., sec. 1164. Drury v. Com., 162 Ky. 123, 172 S. W. 94.

# (11)

Surplusage-unnecessary particularity. Where an indictment contains a particular description of the acts constituting the offense, describing them with more minuteness than is necessary, the offense in its character and terms must be proved as alleged in the indictment. Com. v. Magowan, 1 Met. 368; Clark v. Com., 16 B. M. 206.

# (12)

The fact that an indictment for manslaughter charges that offense was committed maliciously does not render it bad, as the word "maliciously" will be regarded as surplusage. Coe v. Com., 94 Ky. 606, 15 R. 284, 23 S. W. 371; and see further, Com. v. Jarboe, 89 Ky. 143, 11 R. 344, 12 S. W. 138.

# (13)

Sufficiency of indictment generally in its description of offense charged - only necessary that it should advise accused of nature of crime. Oyerstreet v. Com., 147 Ky. 47, 144 S. W. 751; Com. v. Drewry, 126 Ky. 183, 103 S. W. 266, 31 R. 632.

# (13a)

The omission of the word "knowingly" in an indictment for false swearing did not vitiate the indictment. Fisher v. Com., 152 Ky. 411, 153 S. W. 417.

# (13b)

An indictment for willful murder did not allege that the accused "willfully" killed the deceased. The indictment was sufficient. Burnett v. Com., 172 Ky. 397, 189 S. W. 460.

# (14)

See notes to sec. 122.

# (15)

Bill of particulars-when necessary. Com. v. C. & O. R. Co., 128 Ky. 749, 110 S. W. 253.

# (15a)

Although an indictment may be sufficient on demurrer, the court may, in the exercise of a sound discretion, direct a bill of particulars to be filed where it seems necessary for the accused to have more detailed information. Head v. Com., 165 Ky. 339, 176 S. W. 1162.

# (16)

Charging prior convictions. Com. v. Finn, 120 Ky. 364, 86 S. W. 693, 27 R. 771.

# (16a)

Averments in an indictment for carrying a concealed deadly weapon, relative to a prior conviction, charging that accused committed the first offense before the offense therein stated, and for which he had been tried and convicted upon the charge at the present term of the court, and that the present was the second offense upon the charge of carrying a concealed deadly weapon, were held insufficient as not showing that the second offense was committed after the prior conviction. Morgan v. Com., 170 Ky. 400, 186 S. W. 132.

# (17)

Bad spelling or incorrect grammar will not affect validity of indictment. Gaither v. Com., 28 R. 1345, 91 S. W. 1124; Overstreet v. Com., 147 Ky. 471, 144 S. W. 751.

# (18)

County — what will be sufficient statement as to. Martin v. Com., 145 Ky. 752, 141 S. W. 54.

# (19)

Surplusage-unnecessary matter will be so treated. Com. v. Tobin, 140 Ky. 261, 130 S. W. 1116.

# (20)

Exceptions and provisos in statute - treatment of in indictment. Com. v. L. & N. R. Co., 140 Ky. 21, 130 S. W. 798.

# § 125. Name.

The omission to set out the first or Christian name of the defendant will not vitiate the indictment. Com. v. Kelcher, 3 Met. 485; nor is it fatal variance where indictment for bigamy stated first wife's name as Mag White, while her real name was Mag Sharp, as she went by both names. Robinson v. Com., 88 Ky. 386, 10 R. 972, 11 S. W. 210; and error in name may be corrected at any stage of the proceedings. Com. v. Jenkins, 115 Ky. 62, 24 R. 1881, 72 S. W. 363; Drake v. Com., 29 R. 981, 96 S. W. 580; Combast v. Com., 137 Ky. 495, 125 S. W. 1092; Clary-

Indictment-Form and Requisites

# §126

The indictment, and the subsequent proceedings shall be in the true name, substantially as follows:

The Commonwealth of Kentucky against A B, indicted by the name of C D.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 987.

# § 126 [125] [Indictment to charge but one offense exception.]

An indictment, except in the cases mentioned in the next section, must charge but one offense, but, if it may have been committed in different modes and by different means, the indictment may allege the modes and means in the alternative. (Jurisdiction of all degrees of offense included, sec. 14; degrees of offense defined, sec. 263.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 966, 972.

ville T. P. Co. v. Com., 107 S. W. 327, 32 R. 861; International Harvester Co. v. Com., 124 Ky. 543, 99 S. W. 637, 30 R. 716.

# § 126. (1) Misjoinder of offenses- what is not.

An indictment for murder contained three paragraphs, "the first of which charges that the murder was committed by all five of the accused jointly; the second charges that Hunter committed the murder and that the other defendants did with malice aforethought counsel, advise and persuade the said Hunter to kill and murder the deceased; and in the third paragraph or count it is charged that the appellant (Thompson) committed the murder, and that the other defendants in like manner counseled and advised the commission of the offense." Held that there was no misjoinder of offenses, that but one offense was charged, although alleged to have been committed in different modes. Thompson v. Com., 1 Met. 13; and see to same effect, Angel v. Com., 14 R. 10, 18 S. W. 849; Benge v. Com., 92 Ky. 1, 13 K 308, 17 S. W. 146; Cupp v. Com., 87 Ky. 35, 9 R. 877, 7 S. W. 405; Howard v. Com., 110 Ky. 356, 22 R. 1845, 61 S. W. 756.

(la) An indictment which charges the defendant with shooting and wounding two persons named with intent to kill, means that he wounded both of them with a single shot. Rutherford v. Com., 9 Ky. Opinions 163.

(2) An indictment charging that defendant did "unlawfully set-up, exhibit and keep for himself and another a faro bank," charges but one offense. Vowels v. Com., 84 Ky. 52, 8 R. 74.

3 An indictment charging the taking defendant destroyed, injured and carried.

Title VI Indictment-Form and Requisites.

# § 127 [126] [Joinder of offenses in indictment.]

The offenses named in each of the subdivisions of this section may be charged in one indictment:

1. Larceny and knowingly receiving stolen property.
2. Larceny and obtaining money or property on false pretenses.
3. Larceny and embezzlement.
4. Robbery and burglary.
5. Robbery and assault with intent to rob.
6. Passing or attempting to pass counterfeit money or United States currency or bank notes, knowing them to be such, and having in possession counterfeit money or United States currency or bank notes, knowing them to be such, with the intention of circulating the same. (One offense may be dismissed, if misjoinder, sec. 168.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 373, 966, 972.

# § 128 [127] [Statement as to person injured, or owner of property.]

Away timber from four trees, the property of another, charges but one offense. Com. v. Powell, 8 Bush 7; Com. v. Searls, 3 R. 394.

(11) When election proper or necessary. If, upon the trial of an indictment charging but one offense, evidence is introduced for the Commonwealth of different acts, any one of which might constitute the offense, it is proper for the prosecutor to elect upon which of them he will rely. If he makes no such election the court may elect. MeCreary v. Com., 163 Ky. 206, 173 S. W. 351.

# § 127. (1) Misjoinder-practice.

Sec indictment charging two offenses under secs. 1256, 1257, Ky. Stat.; Ellis v. Com., 78 Ky. 130; and see further, Com. v. Powell, 8 Bush 7; Com. v. Searls, 3 R. 394; and see as to practice where there is a misjoinder, secs. 165, 168.

(2) Offenses that may be joined. Grand larceny and receiving stolen goods may be joined. Upton v. Com., 14 R. 165; but the offense of forging and uttering a forged paper cannot be joined. Huff v. Com., 19 R. 1064, 42 S. W. 907.

(3) Embezzlement and fraudulent entries in public books cannot be joined. Com. v. Bradley, 132 Ky. 512, 116 S. W. 761.

(4) Demurrer is proper mode of objecting to indictment for misjoinder. Com. v. Bradley, 132 Ky. 512, 116 S. W. 761; Johnson v. Com., 90 Ky. 488, 12 R. 442, 14 S. W. 492.

(9) An indictment that charges the accused with the offense of forging a paper and with uttering and publishing it contains two offenses and is bad. Messer v. Com., 26 R. 40, 80 S. W. 489.

(10) Counts-aider and abettor-ac-

# § 129] Indictment-Form and Requisites.

If an offense involve the commission of, or an attempt to commit an injury to person or property, or the taking of property, and be described in other respects with sufficient certainty to identify the act, an erroneous allegation as to the person injured or attempted to be injured, or as to the owner of the property taken or injured or attempted to be injured, is not material.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 966, 982.

# § 129 [180] [Time offense committed-statement as to.]

The statement in the indictment, as to the time at which the offense was committed, is not material further than as a statement that it was committed before the time of finding the indictment, unless the time be a material ingredient in the offense.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 966, 982.

F. Watson, the defendant can not be convicted if the proof is that he stole the horse of Casson Watson. "In a case like this, where there is a variance in the proof and the averment as to ownership, it must prove fatal to the prosecution, unless the offense be sufficiently described in other respects with such certainty as to identify the act; and if in the present case the horse has been minutely described, so as to have identified it whether it belonged to A or B, as the horse alleged to have been stolen, the fact that the name of the owner had been improperly stated would have been regarded as immaterial." MeBride v. Com. 13 Bush 337, and see notes to sec. 124.

(2) In an indictment for housebreaking it is not necessary to describe the owner of the house broken or the property taken with any more precision or certainty than is necessary to inform the accused of the nature of the accusation against him and to bar a second conviction. Johnson v. Com., 87 Ky. 189, 10 R. 100, 7 S. W. 927.

(3) In an indictment for burglary a description of the house entered will be sufficient that will bar another prosecution for the same offense. Olive v. Com., 5 Bush 376.

(4) Under an indictment for obtaining money under false pretenses it is not material whether the money obtained belonged to the persons alleged in the indictment. Hennessy v. Com., 88 Ky. 301, 10 R.. 823, 11 S. W. 13.

(5) An indictment charging the accused with stealing property from the "Columbus Stave Co." need not have alleged that it was incorporated, but having so alleged it was not necessary to prove it. Com. v. Vineyard, 26 R. 543, 82 S. W. 289; and see Jackson v. Com., 17 R 1197, 34 S. W. 14.

(5a) A misdescription of the ownership of property in an indictment for receiving stolen goods, knowing same to have been stolen, was immaterial. Com. v. McGarvey, 158 Ky. 570, 165 S. W. 973.

(6) Misdescription of ownership or use of property in arson is not fatal to indictment. Com. v. Napier, 84 S. W. 536, 27 R. 131; Overstreet v. Com., 147 Ky. 471, 144 S. W. 751.

(7) Robbery-misdescription of name of person not material if property is well described. Bibb v. Com., 112 S. W. 401.

(8) False personation--error in name of false personator when not material. Com. v. Vaughn, 140 Ky. 559, 131 S. W. 396.

(9) Assault — when error in name of person assaulted not material. Robinson v. Com., 144 Ky. 111, 137 S. W. 533.

(10) Subornation of perjury-When error in name of person suborned not material. Henderson v. Com., 122 Ky. 296, 28 R. 1212, 91 S. W. 1141.

# § 129. (1) Time-Statement as to.

An indictment found at the May term, 1865, which charges that the offense was committed on the - day of July, 1865, and afterward in describing the offense it is in express terms charged to have been committed before the finding of the indictment, is good. Jones v. Com., 1 Bush 34.

(2) In a prosecution for nuisance the Commonwealth was not confined to the

# Title VI

# Indictment-Form and Requisites

# [§ 129]

day alleged in the indictment, but had the right to prove the commission of the offense on any day within twelve months before the finding of the indictment. C. R. Ca. v. Com., 80 Ky. 137, 3 R. 639; Com. v. Ky. Highland R. Co., 147 Ky. 409, 145 S. W. 386.

(2a) In a trial of an indictment under Ky. Stat., sec. 1155, the Commonwealth may prove the commission of the offense at any time prior to the finding of the indictment. MeCreary v. Com., 158 Ky. 612, 165 S. W. 981.

(3) The court is authorized on demurrer to assume that the offense was committed at the time charged in the indictment, although the Commonwealth on the trial would not be confined to the time alleged. Com. v. Cain. 14 Bush 525.

(4) An indictment which charges that the offense was committed on the same day the indictment is filed in court will be good if the indictment charged that the offense was committed before the time of finding the indictment. Com. v. Miller, 79 Ky. 451, 3 R. 231.

(4a) An indictment returned May 11, 1916, charged that the offense was committed May 11, 1916, and did not expressly state that it was committed before the finding of the indictment. The indictment was sufficient. Morgan v. Com., 172 Ky. 684, 189 S. W. 943.

(5) Averment that offense was committed on the - day of July, 1883, imports the commission of it anterior to the 24th of the same month, when indictment was filed. Vonels v. Com., 84 Ky. 52, 8 R. 74.

(6) In a prosecution for false swearing the date of the commission of the offense is not material, and a variance between date charged and record of proceeding in which false oath was taken does not render record incompetent. Com. v. Davis, 94 Ky. 612, 15 R. 262, 23 S. W. 218; Richey v. Com., 81 Ky. 524, 5 R. 524.

(7) Where the defendant is indicted for different offenses of the same character committed within a year, the Commonwealth may in one trial confine itself to one act, and it will not be a bar to other trials. C. & O. R. Co. v. Com., 88 Ky. 368, 10 R. 919, 11 S. W. 87; see note 29 to sec. 176.

(8) An indictment for bigamy charged that on the — day of September, 1891, the accused married, having then a wife, to whom he was married on September 19, 1891; the court held that the date was not material, and that figures 1891 were used by mistake in place of 1881. Faustre v. Com., 92 Ky. 34, 13 R. 347, 17 S. W. 189; and see Williams v. Com., 18 R. 667, 37 S. W. 839.

(9) An indictment for housebreaking, found on December 28, 1891, alleged that offense was committed on December 29, 1891, and contained no specific averment that offense was committed prior to finding of indictment; held good. Williams v. Com., 13 R. 893, 18 S. W. 1024.

(10) An indictment for perjury stated that offense was committed October 1, 1892; the evidence showed that trial when accused swore falsely occurred September 19, 1892; the variance was held immaterial. Com. v. Davis, 94 Ky. 612, 15 R. 262, 23 S. W. 218; and see Com. v. Miles, 140 Ky. 577, 131 S. W. 385.

(11) Two indictments for same offense committed at different times. See as to when proof in one case is and is not a bar to prosecution of other indictment. C. & O. R. R. v. Com., 88 Ky. 368, 10 R. 919, 11 S. W. 87; Cawein v. Con., 110 Ky. 273, 22 R. 1734, 61 S. W. 275.

(12) An indictment for murder failed to state when crime was committed, but, as there was no demurrer filed to it, the court would not consider the objection made. Lightfoot v. Com., 80 Ky. 516, 4 R. 463.

(12a) Time is not a material ingredient in the offense of murder. May v. Com., 153 Ky. 141, 154 S. W. 1074.

(13) In misdemeanor cases time is material, and if indictment shows that offense is barred by limitation a demurrer should be sustained. Williams v. Com., 18 R. 667, 37 S. W. 834; Combs v. Com., 27 R. 273, 84 S. W. 753; and see Kehoe v. Com., 28 R. 35, 88 S. W. 1107.

(13a) A certain indictment under Ky. Stat., sec. 1138, was found on September 16, 1913, and alleged the sale of liquor to have occurred July 4, 1912, within twelve months just prior to the finding of the indictment. The indictment was sufficient. Paul v. Com., 159 Ky. 848, 169 S. W. 544.

(14) Indictment in misdemeanor cases may charge offense on any day within a year and conviction may be had on proof of guilt within the year. Com. v. Miles, 140 Ky. 577, 131 S. W. 385.

(15) Time may be specified in indictment and evidence and thus avoid one conviction barring another. Shirley v. Com., 143 Ky. 183, 132 S. W. 227.

(16) A conviction on a plea of guilty is not a bar to a prosecution for an offense committed on a day other than the day specified in the indictment. 175 Ky. 155, 192 S. W. 1029.

# Indictment-Form and Requisites

# § 130 [182] [Presumptions of law-judicial notice.]

Neither presumptions of law, nor matters of which judicial notice is taken, need be stated in an indictment.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 983.

# § 131 [188] [Judgment, how pleaded—jurisdictional facts.]

In pleading a judgment or other determination of, or proceeding before, a court or officer of special jurisdiction, it is not necessary to state the facts constituting jurisdiction; but the judgment or determination may be stated as given or made, or the proceedings had. The facts constituting jurisdiction must, however, be established on the trial.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 984.

# § 132 [134] [Libel-allegations of indictment for.]

An indictment for libel need not set forth any extrinsic facts, for the purpose of showing the application, to the party libeled, of the defamatory matter; but it is sufficient to state generally that the same was published concerning him.

(As to jurisdiction, see sec. 21.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 979.

# § 133 [135] [Written instrument-description of.]

If a written instrument, which is the subject of an indictment for forgery, larceny or other offense, have been withheld or destroyed by the act or procurement of the defendant, and such destruction or withholding be alleged in the indictment, and proved on the trial, a misdescription of the instrument is immaterial.

# § 134 [130] [Perjury or subornation of-necessary allegations.]

In an indictment for perjury, or subornation of perjury, it is sufficient to set forth the substance of the controversy or matter in respect to which the offense was committed, and in what court and before whom the oath alleged to be false was taken, and that the court, or the person before.

# § 130. See section 119 Civil Code and notes thereto.

of Code. Com. v. Combs, 125 Ky. 273, 101 S. W. 312, 30 R. 1301; Com. v. Tracender, 140 Ky. 660, 131 S. W. 495.

# § 131. See section 122 Civil Code and notes thereto.

(2) A conviction for perjury or false swearing may be had upon documentary or written evidence alone without a living witness. Com. v. Davis, 92 Ky. 460, 13 R. 676, 15 S. W. 10.

# § 132. Libel-sufficiency of indictment for.

Tracy v. Com., 87 Ky. 578, 10 R. 611, 9 S. W. 822; Browning v. Com., 116 Ky. 282, 25 R. 482, 76 S. W. 19; Smith v. Com., 98 Ky. 437, 17 R. 1010, 33 S. W. 419; Com. v. Duncan, 127 Ky. 471, 104 S. W. 997, 31 R. 1277.

(3) Facts being averred in an indictment for perjury showing that defendant was sworn in the usual form, the additional averment that he took his "corporal oath" was mere surplusage. Com. v. Jarboe, 89 Ky. 143, 11 R. 344, 12 S. W. 138.

# § 133. Written instrument that is foundation of indictment must be set out or its absence accounted for.

Kinard v. Com., 134 Ky. 575, 121 S. W. 489.

# § 134. (1) Perjury. See Ky. Stat., sec. 1173 and notes; and note 66 to sec. 122.

# Title VI Indictment-Form and Requisites

# § 135. Larceny or embezzlement of money-sufficiency of indictment.

In an indictment for the larceny or embezzlement of money, or United States currency, or bank notes, it is sufficient to allege the larceny or embezzlement of the same without specifying the coin, number, denomination or kind thereof.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 981.

# § 136 [137] Words of statute defining offense need not be strictly pursued.

The words used in a statute to define an offense need not be strictly pursued in an indictment, but other words conveying the same meaning may be used.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 974.

# § 135. Larceny-sufficiency of description.

An indictment charging that James M. Mann did "feloniously take, steal and carry away from the possession of Penn and Floyd money, currency of the realm, to the amount of over ten dollars," sufficiently describes the money.

Com. v. Mann, 12 R. 477, 14 S. W. 685; and see Jones v. Com., 13 Bush 356; Travis v. Com., 96 Ky. 77, 16 R. 253, 27 S. W. 863; Bailey v. Com., 22 R. 512, 58 S. W. 425; Stone v. Com., 24 R. 10, 67 S. W. 841; Todd v. Com., 93 S. W. 631, 29 R. 473; Bailey v. Com., 130 Ky. 301, 113 S. W. 140; Ky. Statutes, sec. 1223.

(3) Where the words of the statute are descriptive of the offense, the indictment should follow the language and expressly charge the described offense against the defendant, or it will be defective. Com. v. Tanner, 5 Bush 316; Con. v. Turner, 8 Bush 1; Conner v. Com., 13 Bush 714.

(4) When a statute contains provisos or exceptions in distinct clauses, it is not necessary to state in the indictment that the defendant does not come within the exceptions or to negative the provisos it contains. Com. v. Bull, 13 Bush 656; Com. v. Bierman, 13 Bush 345; but "if the statute creating an offense contains in its enacting clause exceptions, it is necessary to negative such exceptions in the indictment so as to show that the defendant does not come within any of them." Conner v. Com., 13 Bush 714; Knoxville Nursery Co. v. Com., 108 Ky. 6, 21 R. 1483, 55 S. W. 691; Com. v. L. & N. R. Co., 140 Ky. 21, 130 S. W. 798.

# § 136. (1) Statute-indictment under.

Where the words of the statute are descriptive of the offense, the indictment will be sufficient if it shall follow the language and expressly charge the described offense of the defendant. But this rule applies only to offenses which are complete in themselves, when the acts set out in the statute have been done or performed. Davis v. Com., 13 Bush 318; Ward v. Com., 14 Bush 233; Mitchell v. Com., 58 Ky. 349, 10 R. 910, 11 S. W. 209; Com. v. Grinstead, 108 Ky. 59, 21 R. 1444, 55 S. W. 720; Com. v. Landis, 129 Ky. 445, 112 S. W. 581.

(2) An indictment under the "Kuklux law" will be sufficient if it charges the offense in the words of the statute. Sellers v. Com., 13 Bush 331; Buchanan v. Com., 2 Met. 8.

Indictment-Form and Requisites.

# § 137

# [Construction of words used in indictment.]

The words used in an indictment must be construed according to their usual acceptation in common language, except words and phrases defined by law, which are to be construed according to their legal meaning.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 971.

For instance, 'robbery,' and proceeds to impose a penalty for its commission, it is not sufficient to charge the accused with its commission in the statutory terms alone; in such cases the pleader must define the offense by stating the common law elements necessary to its commission." Mitchell v. Com., 88 Ky. 349, 10 R. 910, 11 S. W. 209.

1. An indictment for an offense created by statute need not charge that the acts complained of were done "feloniously" unless the statute requires that the act should be done feloniously in order to constitute the offense. Higgins v. Com., 94 Ky. 54, 14 R. 729, 21 S. W. 231; Cundiff v. Com., 86 Ky. 196, 9 R. 537. 5 S. W. 486; Asso. Press v. Com., 110 Ky. 516, 22 R. 1929, 60 S. W. 295, 523.
2. An indictment for the violation of a penal statute need not indicate the statute upon which it is founded; it is sufficient to bring the case within the provisions of the statute. Powers v. Com., 90 Ky. 167, 11 R. 964, 13 S. W. 450.
3. If the statute declares, that it shall be an offense if the act is "unlawfully" done, it need not be charged that accused acted maliciously, wilfully or feloniously; if he be charged in the language of the statute creating the crime and in the manner required by it, then the charge is complete." Higgins v. Com., 94 Kv. 54, 14 R. 729, 21 S. W. 231.
4. An indictment under sec. 2, art. 6, chap. 29, Gen. Stat. [now sec. 1166, Ky. Stat.] is good, although it fails to charge that the person wounded "did not die thereby," and fails to allege that the pistol was "loaded with a leaden bullet or other hard substance." Burns v. Com., 3 Met. 14.
5. An indictment charging that "the defendant did unlawfully, feloniously and maliciously, with intent to kill him, cut and wound one Philip Yeiser," is sufficient under sec. 2, art. 6, chap. 29, Gen. Stat. [now sec. 1166, Ky. Stat.]. Flint v. Com., 81 Ky. 186, 5 R. 51; Gratz v. Com., 96 Ky. 162, 16 R. 465, 28 S. W. 159.
6. Aider and abettor in statutory crime may be punished unless it clearly appear that its provisions were only intended to apply to party actually committing crime. Com. v. Carter, 94 Ky. 527, 15 R. 253, 23 S. W. 344; overruling Stamper v. Com., 7 Bush 612.
7. Although an offense is not charged in the precise words used in the statute in defining it, if it is alleged in words conveying the same meaning it will be sufficient. Taylor v. Com., 3 Bush 508; Moore v. Com., 92 Ky. 630, 13 R. 738, 18 S. W. 853; Gratz v. Com., 96 Ky. 162, 16 R. 465, 28 S. W. 159.

# § 138 [139] [Process on indictment, writ of arrest or summons.]

The process on an indictment consists of the writs for arresting or summoning the defendant to appear and answer the indictment.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 910.

# § 139 [142] [Process of arrest, bench warrant.]

The process of arrest on an indictment shall be a bench warrant. (Form, sec. 142, and Form 213.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 910.

# § 140 [140] [Clerk to issue bench warrant when ordered.]

A bench warrant shall be issued by the clerk, upon the order of the court, and may be reissued from time to time by order of the attorney for the Commonwealth. (See sec. 149.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 910.

# § 141 [141] [Court to order process on indictment—provision if defendant on bail.]

Upon an indictment being found, if the defendant be not in custody, nor on bail, the court shall forthwith make an order for process to be issued thereon, designating whether it shall be for arresting or summoning the defendant; and if for arresting the defendant, and the offense charged be bailable, the sum in which he may be admitted to bail shall be fixed. If he be on bail the court may order a bench warrant to.

# § 140. Process-court must order.

The clerk has no authority to issue process for the arrest of a person who has been indicted without an order directing it to issue, and process issued by the clerk without order confers no authority upon the sheriff to arrest the defendant, or take bail. Shaw v. Com., 1 Duv. 1.

# § 141. If the circuit court be satisfied with the bond given in the examining.

# Process Upon An Indictment

§142-146]    Process Upon An Indictment.                            [Title VI

issue, or commit him to custody, and fix the amount of bail to be given by him. (Forms of orders, Forms 280, 281.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 910.

# § 142 [148] [Bench warrant-form of.]

A bench warrant may be substantially of the following form:

Franklin Circuit Court.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, to any sheriff, coroner, jailer, constable, marshal or policeman in this State: You are hereby commanded forthwith to arrest A B, and bring him before the Franklin Circuit Court to answer to an indictment found in that court against him for felony [or misdemeanor, as the case may be], or, if the court be adjourned for the term, that you deliver him to the custody of the jailer of Franklin county. Given under my hand, this —- day of , 19- P. SwiGERT, Clerk F. C. C. (Forms 213, 214.)

# § 143 [144] [Indorsement as to bail.]

If the offense be bailable, an indorsement shall be made on the bench warrant substantially as follows:

"The defendant is to be admitted to bail in the sum of — dollars, and if he desire to give bail, it may be taken by a sheriff of the county in which he is arrested, or the sheriff of Franklin county." (Bail may be sworn, qualifications of, sec. 76,; 77; rehat peace officers may take bail, sec. 84.)

# § 144 [145] [Officer other than sheriff arresting--how to proceed.]

If the bench warrant be executed by any other officer than a sheriff, such officer, at the defendant's request, shall take him before a sheriff of the county in which he is arrested, or of the county in which the indictment was found, for the purpose of giving bail.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 910.

# § 145 [148] [Indictment for unbailable offense how to proceed.]

If the defendant be on bail, and the indictment be for an offense not bailable, and he appear in pursuance of the bail bond, he shall be forthwith committed to jail; and if he do not appear, on being required by the court, the bail bond shall be forfeited, and a bench warrant issued for his arrest.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 910.

# § 146 [147] [Summons on indictment—form of.]

The summons on an indictment may be substantially of the following form:

(2) Service on agent of non-resident as provided in sec. 51, Civil Code, is good. Com. v. Bullock, 22 R. 528, 58 S. W. 429.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, to any sheriff, coroner, jailer, constable, marshal or policeman in this State: You are hereby commanded to summon A B to appear in the Franklin Circuit Court, on the first day of its next June term, to answer an indictment for misdemeanor found against him in that court. Given under my hand, this —- day of -— , 19

# § 147 [148] [Issual and service of summons.]

The summons shall be issued and served in the same manner as a summons in civil actions. (Provisions of Civil Code concerning, secs. 40 to 56.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 910.

# § 148 [140] [Summons in misdemeanor cases only.]

The summons shall only be issued on indictments for misdemeanor, if the court have not ordered a bench warrant to issue, and may be issued without any order by the court.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 909, 910.

# § 149 [150] [Bench warrant-when may be issued.]

The court may, at its discretion, order a bench warrant to be issued on any indictment; but if the punishment be limited to a fine of one hundred dollars or less, a bench warrant shall not be issued, unless the court be satisfied that there is reason to believe the defendant will escape punishment if a bench warrant be not issued. (Sec sec. 140.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 909, 910.
