# CHAPTER IV. PRODUCTION OF EVIDENCE.

# § 150. Subpoenas to be issued on request.

The clerk of the court, upon the request of the Commonwealth's attorney, or of the defendant, or his attorney, shall issue subpoenas for witnesses. (Form of, Form 217.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1047.

# § 151. Witnesses' attendance—how secured.

The provisions of the Code of Practice in civil actions shall apply to and govern the summoning and coercing the attendance of witnesses, and compelling them to testify in all prosecutions, criminal or penal actions or proceedings, except that the attendance of witnesses residing in any part of the State may be coerced, and it shall never be necessary to tender to the witnesses any compensation for expenses, or otherwise, before process of contempt shall issue. (Provisions of Civil Code referred to, secs. 532 to 542; form of warrant of arrest for witness, Form 219; non-resident witness for Commonwealth, fees of, Ky. Stat., sec. 368; fees generally, Ky. Stat., sec. 367.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 252, 1047, 1099.

§ 1142. Special bailiff to summon witnesses—compensation. That in the trial of any felony case in this Commonwealth when the case has been called, and either party is not ready for trial because of the absence of any witness or witnesses in any county other than that in which said court is sitting, and having been duly subpoenaed and failing to appear, the circuit judge thereof be, and he is hereby empowered to appoint a special bailiff to summon said witness or witnesses, and who shall have the power to arrest and bring said witness or witnesses immediately before said court. Said bailiff shall be allowed by the judge a reasonable compensation for said service, not to exceed that allowed sheriffs for conveying prisoners to the penitentiary. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1142.)

# § 152. Subpoena duces tecum may be issued.

The court on motion of either party may, by its order and process, compel the production of any written document, or of any other thing which may be necessary or proper to be produced or exhibited as evidence on the trial, and

# § 151. Witness in penitentiary.

In a felony case, if a material witness for the defense is confined in the penitentiary, the court may order the warden to produce him at the trial. Expense-how paid. Hancock v. Parker, 100 Ky. 143, 18 R. 622, 37 S. W. 594; and see Com. v. Minor, 89 Ky. 555, 11 R. 775, 13 S. W. 5; Combs v. Com., 15 R. 660, 25 S. W. 590.

Title VI
# Production of Evidence.

may punish a disobedience of its orders or process, as in cases of witnesses refusing to testify. (Form of subpoena duces tecum, Form 218.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1047.

# § 153 [154] Depositions for defendant, when and how taken.

If it appear by affidavit that a material witness is about to leave this State without the procurement or consent of the defendant, or is physically unable to attend for examination in court; or that his death is apprehended; or that he is a non-resident of this State, and beyond the reach of the process of the court, the court or the judge, or any circuit judge in vacation, may authorize the defendant to take his deposition. The court or judge shall, by written order, prescribe the manner of taking the deposition, whether by written interrogatories, or upon notice to the attorney for the Commonwealth; and such deposition shall only be used if the witness be dead or is absent from the State, or physically unable to attend for examination in court at the time of trial.

The deposition may be taken by any officer who is authorized to take depositions by the Civil Code: Provided, That the deposition of a non-resident shall only be taken upon interrogatories to be filed with the clerk of the court in which the trial is pending, and under the same rules that are prescribed in the Civil Code for taking depositions in civil cases; and notice of the filing of the interrogatories shall at once be given to the attorney for the Commonwealth, who may file cross-interrogatories within ten days after the notice of the filing of the interrogatories shall have been given him; and thereupon it shall be the duty of the clerk to issue commissions, with the copies of the interrogatories and cross-interrogatories, if any, annexed in the same manner and to the same persons as is prescribed by section 527 of the Civil Code, and said deposition shall be taken in all respects as is prescribed by the Civil Code in taking depositions in civil cases. (Section as amended by act of January 13, 1893, c. 128, p. 430.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1021, 1047.

# § 153.

1. Depositions can not be used by the defendant in any state of case not provided for by statute. *Nichols v. Com., 11 Bush 575; and see further, as to continuance, sec. 189 and notes.*
2. nor can the State take depositions and read them against the accused, because he is entitled to be confronted with the witnesses against him. *Kaelin v. Com., 84 Ky. 355, 8 R. 293, 1 S. W. 594;*
3. the accused may waive the presence of his own witnesses and allow a statement, as embodied in an affidavit, to be read as evidence, and he will be bound by his consent to this course. *Hobbs v. Taylor v. Com., 9 R. 316, 5 S. W. 46;*
4. Where a witness whose deposition had been taken under this section gave testimony inconsistent with his deposition and the proper foundation was laid on cross-examination for contradicting him by the deposition, it was error to refuse to permit to be read to the jury the portions of the deposition about which he was cross-examined. *Com., 156 Ky. 847, 162 S. W. 104.*

# § 154 [155] [Arraignment defined.]

An arraignment is the reading of the indictment by the clerk to the defendant, and asking him if he pleads guilty, or not guilty, to the indictment. (Reading indictment and stating plea, sec. 219.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 959.

# § 154. (1) Arraignment-consent of defendant-waiver of error by.

In Com. v. Adams, 92 Ky. 134, 13 R. 440, 17 S. W. 276; the court held that the consent of the accused that the judge might change the charge in the indictment did not bind him; in Kennedy v. Com., 78 Ky. 447. an agreement of the accused as to a special judge was held binding upon him, the court holding that there was "a broad distinction between an admission of a fact necessary to put the machinery of the law in motion to the end that a trial might be had and the consent of one charged with felony to be tried by less than twelve jurors or that one not legally qualified should preside as judge."

In Murphy v. Com., I Met. 365, it was held that a defendant on trial for a misdemeanor may consent to be tried by a less number than the regular jury; and in Taylor v. Com., 9 R. 316, 5"S. W. 46,

Title VIj Arraignment and Pleadings by Defendant.

# § 155 (156) [Arraignment may be dispensed with.]

The arraignment shall only be made on indictments for felony, and may be dispensed with by the court, with the defendant's consent.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 959, 994.

# § 156 [157] [Trial of sanity of defendant—proceedings if insane.]

If the court shall be of opinion that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the defendant is insane, all proceedings in the trial shall be postponed.

The accused who consented that an affidavit of his witness should be read as evidence in his behalf was held bound by it; and the consent of accused that jury may be discharged estops him from pleading former jeopardy. Robinson v. Com., 88 Ky. 386, 10 R. 972, 11 S. W. 210; consent that a change of venue may be had is binding. Lightfoot v. Com., 80 Ky. 516, 4 R. 463; and so is agreement that evidence heard before arraignment may be considered without again being introduced. Utterback v. Com., 105 Ky. 723, 20 R. 1515, 49 S. W. 479; and defendant may waive his right to have jury kept together. Wade v. Com., 106 Ky. 321, 20 R. 1885, 50 S. W. 271; and consent that special judge may receive verdict. Rainey v. Com., 19 R. 390, 40 S. W. 682; and see Arnold v. Com., 23 R. 182, 62 S. W. 15.

# (2) Does the defendant's silence or failure to plead amount to a confession of guilt?

The court suggests that where the defendant refuses to file any plea, the safer rule is to have the plea of not guilty entered and counsel assigned. Triplett v. Com., 84 Ky. 193, 8 R. 67, 1 S. W. 84.

# 3Where the bill of exceptions shows

that an arraignment was waived, and that the defendant pleaded not guilty, the objection that there was no arraignment, supported by the defendant's affidavit, cannot avail, as the record is conclusive of that fact and prevents any inquiry into its correctness. Farris v. Com., 14 Bush 362.

# (4) The court in a felony case may

defer calling on the defendant to plead to the indictment until the jury is selected, and then after his plea is entered the jury may be sworn. "Disney v. Com., 9 R. 413, 5 S. W. 360; and see sec. 217.

# (5) Where the trial has commenced by

the examination of a witness and it is then discovered that defendant has not been arraigned or entered any plea the court may, upon the refusal of the accused to then enter his plea or consent that the jury may be discharged, discharge on its own motion the jury, arraign the defendant, reswear the jury and proceed with the trial. Disney v. Com., 9 R. 413, 5 S. W. 360; Minor v. Com., 5 R. 176; and see Galloway v. Com., 5 R. 213; and Utterback v. Com. 105 Ky. 723, 20 R. 1515, 49 S. W. 479; as to waiver by accused.

# (6) The requirement that the indictment

shall be read and the plea of defendant stated to jury is mandatory, and no party can be convicted unless it is substantially complied with. Galloway v. Com., 5 R. 213; Farris v. Com., 111 Ky. 236, 23 R. 580, 63 S. W. 615; but in Meece v. Com., 78 Ky. 586, 1 R. 337; the record failed to show that any plea was entered, but judgment was not reversed, and see Howard v. Com., 24 R. 91, 67 S. W. 1003; Ison v. Com., 23 R. 1805, 66 S. W. 184; and see cases cited in note 9.

# (7) See further, sec. 219 and notes thereto.

# (8) Arraignment not necessary in misdemeanor cases.

Griffin v. Com., 23 R. 2148, 66 S. W. 740.

# (9) Failure to observe requirements of this section is not reversible error,

Reed v. Com., 138 Ky. 568, 128 S. W. 874; and see Bischoff v. Com., 96 S. W. 538, 29 R. 770; Herr v. Com., 91 S. W. 666, 28 R. 1133.

# § 155. Arraignment in felonies necessary.

In felony cases the arraignment cannot be dispensed with unless by consent of the defendant, and a failure to comply with this provision is reversible error. Hendrickson v. Com., 23 R. 1191, 64 S. W. 954. But see cases cited in note 9, sec. 154.

# § 156. (1) Insanity.

The Court of Appeals has no authority to direct an inquiry as to whether the defendant has not become insane since the rendition of the verdict. Brown v. Com., 14 Bush 398; and see secs. 268, 287, and notes thereto, and Com. v. Woelfel, 121 Ky. 48, 88 S. W. 1061, 28 R. 16.

until a jury be impaneled to inquire whether the defendant is of unsound mind, and if the jury find that he is of unsound mind, the court shall direct that he be kept in prison or conveyed by the sheriff to the nearest lunatic asylum, and there kept in custody by the officers thereof until he be restored, when he shall be returned to the sheriff on demand, to be reconveyed by him to the jail of the county. (Verdict and proceedings on plea of insanity, secs. 268, 287.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 960, 1137, 1138.

# § 157 [158] [Plea or motion by defendant when arraigned.]

Upon the arraignment, or upon the call of the indictment for trial, if there be no arraignment, the defendant must either move to set aside the indictment or plead thereto.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 961.

# § 158 [150] [Grounds for setting aside indictment.]

The motion to set aside the indictment can only be made on the following grounds:

1. [Error in summoning or formation of jury.] A substantial error in the summoning or formation of the grand jury.
2. [Outsider in grand jury room.] That some person, other than the grand jurors, was present before the grand jury when they acted upon the indictment. (See sec. 110.)
3. [Not found according to Code.] That the indictment was not found and presented as required by this Code. (Decisions of court upon motion to set aside indictment are not subject to exceptions, sec. 281.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 952, 962.

A finding of insanity is not a bar to further prosecution; it is conclusive only that accused was insane at the time of the finding. Davidson v. Com., 171 Ky. 488, 188 S. W. 631.

# § 157. Filing indictment away-see note 4, sec. 118.

(2) Misdemeanor judgment by default. Upon an indictment for misdemeanor a judgment may be rendered against the defendant by default. Com. v. Cheek, 1 Duv. 26.

(3) Upon an indictment for misdemeanor the defendant may be tried in his absence, and may, by counsel, put in any plea save that of guilty. Walston v. Com., 106 S. W. 224, 32 R. 535. His plea may be put in and defense conducted in his absence, and this right cannot be taken from him by requiring bail. Johnson v. Com., 1 Duv. 244; and see sec. 184 and notes.

(4) Waiver. The right to set aside an indictment is waived if not made at the proper time, and when an indictment for misdemeanor is called for trial, if the defendant fails to move to set it aside or plead to it, he loses his opportunity to plead as a matter of right and should thereafter be refused leave to plead any other than a meritorious plea in bar. Com. v. Smith, 10 Bush 476; and see Com. v. Pritchett, 11 Bush 277; Haggard v. Com., 79 Ky. 366, 2 R. 356; Sutton v. Com., 97 Ky. 308, 17 R. 184, 30 S. W. 661; Moore v. Com., 18 R. 129, 35 S. W. 283; Taylor v. Com., 90 S. W. 581, 28 R. 819.

# § 158.

(1) Error in formation of grand jury. Objection to an indictment because one of the grand jurors was not a housekeeper comes too late at the second term after the defendant is before the court. Com. v. Smith, 10 Bush 476; Haggard v. Com., 79 Ky. 366, 2 R. 356; Com. v. Pritchett, 11 Bush 277; and see Sutton v. Com., 97 Ky. 308, 17 R. 184, 30 S. W. 661; Moore v. Com., 18 R. 129, 35 S. W. 283.

# [§ 159]

[Proceedings when indictment set aside.] If the motion be sustained, the court shall make an order that the case be submitted to another grand jury, to be assembled at that or the next term of the court, and the defendant, if in custody, shall be remanded to jail, or if on bail, the bail shall be liable for the defendant's appearance to answer a new indictment, if one be found. (See further, us to reference to grand jury, scs. 116, 117; dismissal rehen not a bar, sec. 178.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 963, 1139.

# (2)

A white person indicted by a grand jury composed wholly of persons of the white race can not complain because negroes were excluded by statute from the jury. Com. v. Wright. 79 Ky. 22. 1 R. 258. The statute declaring that only white persons were competent as jurors was declared unconstitutional in Com. v. Johnson, 78 Ky. 509, 1 R. 108.

# (3)

Jury commissioners or sheriff, when he acts in their stead, are not bound to select negro jurors; the only right the accused can claim is that no citizen otherwise competent shall be excluded by law on account of race or color. Haggard v. Com., 79 Ky. 366, 2 R. 356.

# (4)

The failure of any member of the grand jury to possess each of the qualifications prescribed by the statute, viz.: being a housekeeper, citizen, and over twenty-one years of age (Ky. Stat., sec. 2248), is not cause for setting aside indictments found by such jury. It was formerly held to be cause. 14 Bush 457; 10 Bush 476; but this rule has been changed by Ky. Stat., sec. 2248.

# (5)

"It is a well-settled principle, and one which is applicable to the action of all juries, that the testimony of the jurors is not competent to explain the grounds of their decision or impeach the validity of their finding." Com. v. Skeggs, 3 Bush 19.

# (6)

That a member of a grand jury was a school trustee when the indictment was found is no ground for setting it aside. The provision of the statute (Ky. Stat., sec. 2248) that no "civil officer" shall be competent does not make the taking of a civil officer on the grand jury a substantial error in its formation. Com. v. Pritchett, 11 Bush 277; Com. v. Rudd, 3 R. 328; nor is it ground for quashing an indictment that one of the grand jurors that found it was the owner of a grist mill. Slagel v. Com., 81 Ky. 485, 5 R. 545.

# (7)

It did not appear in the order of court that the grand jury was sworn, or that one of their number was appointed foreman; but, in the absence of proof to the contrary, the court will presume that these requirements were complied with. Com. v. Pullan, 3 Bush 47.

# (8)

Decisions of court upon motion to set aside indictment are not subject to exception by either party. Com. v. Simmons, 100 Ky. 164, 18 R. 648, 37 S. W. 949; and see sec. 281 and notes thereto.

# (8a)

The failure of the circuit court to set aside an indictment on the ground that it did not contain the names of the witnesses who testified before the grand jury can not be reviewed on appeal. Slaughter v. Com., 152 Ky. 128, 153 S. W. 46.

# (10)

Evidence before grand jury. Court has no power to set aside indictment because incompetent evidence was heard by. Com. v. Minor, 89 Ky. 555, 11 R. 775. 13 S. W. 5; McIntyre v. Com., 26 R. 169, 4 S. W. 1.

# (11)

Presentment of indictment. An indictment for grand larceny, committed by stealing sheep, was indorsed on the back, and described in the order of court filing it as an indictment for "sheep stealing." Held sufficient identification. Com. v. English, 6 Bush 431; see secs. 118, 121, and notes thereto.

# (12)

Qualifications of grand jurors. Incompetent person serving no cause for setting indictment aside. Ky. Stat., sec. 2248.

# (13)

See further secs. 118, 121 and notes thereto.

# 159.

(1) Failure to dispose of former indictment. A person was indicted for murder; after the expiration of several years a new indictment was found for same offense under which he was tried; the court held that his substantial rights were not prejudiced, although no order was made disposing of first indictment. Blyew v. Com., 91 Ky. 200, 12 R 742, 15 S. W. 356.

# § 160-164

# Arraignment and Pleadings by Defendant.

# [Title VI]

# § 160 [161] [Defendant discharged unless new one be found.]

Unless, however, a new indictment be found before the final discharge of the next grand jury, the defendant shall be discharged from custody, or bail, unless for good cause shown the court shall otherwise order. (See further, secs. 116, 117.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 963, 968.

# § 161 [162] [Motion overruled, defendant must plead.]

If the motion be overruled, the defendant shall be required to plead to the indictment.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 964.

# § 162 [1e8] [Pleadings allowed.]

The only pleading on the part of the defendant is a demurrer or a plea.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 964.

# § 163 [164] [Pleadings may be oral—entry of record.]

The demurrer and plea must be entered in open court, and may be oral, but an entry thereof must be made on the record.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 964.

# § 164 [178] [Form of entry of demurrer and plea.]

The entry on the record of the demurrer and pleas may be substantially in the following form:

Filing away an indictment is not a discontinuance of the prosecution and case may be redocketed. Com. v. Bottoms, 105 Ky. 222, 20 R. 1159, 48 S. W. 974; Gross v. Com., 26 R. 870, 82 S. W. 618; and note 4, sec. 118.

§ 164. (1) Pleas in form prescribed in this section are sufficient, and no written reply or denial is necessary on the part of the Commonwealth, sec. 179. Com. v. Rose, 107 Ky. 566, 21 R. 1281, 54 S. W. 802; Shirley v. Com., 143 Ky. 183, 136 S. W. 227; Bernero v. Com., 116 S. W. 312.

Title VI] Arraignment and Pleadings by Defendant. [§ 165

# 1. [Demurrer.]

A demurrer: "The defendant demurs to the indictment."

# 2. [Plea of guilty.]

A plea of guilty: "The defendant pleads that he is guilty of the offense charged in the indictment."

# 3. [Not guilty.]

A plea of not guilty: "The defendant pleads that he is not guilty of the offense charged in the indictment."

# 4. [Former acquittal or conviction.]

A plea of former acquittal, or conviction: "The defendant pleads that he has been acquitted [or convicted, as the case may be] of the offense charged in the indictment, by the judgment of — court [naming it], rendered on the - day of - [naming the time]." (See further, as to bar, sec. 176.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 965, 996, 1006.

# § 165 [165] [Demurrer when proper.]

A demurrer is proper—

1. If it appear from the indictment that the offense was not committed within the local jurisdiction of the court.
2. If the indictment do not substantially conform to the requirements of article 2 of chapter 2, of title 6.

82 S. W. 440; Ball v. Com., 81 Ky. 662, 5 R. 787.

(3) See notes to sec. 176 as to sufficiency of plea of former conviction or acquittal.

(3a) The defense of a former acquittal should be raised by a plea and not by a motion to discharge. Com. v. Daniel, 9 Ky. Opinions 96.

(3b) A conviction of a railroad for failure to block a frog is not a former conviction of the offense of failing to block another frog. L. & N. R. Co. v. Com., 154 Ky. 293, 157 S. W. 369.

(3c) In a prosecution for having possession of intoxicating liquors with intent to sell in local option territory, the accused, to avail himself of the defense that he was entitled to a peremptory instruction on the second trial on the ground that conviction was on the same evidence as on the prior trial, is required to enter the plea of former conviction on the record. Fugate v. Com., 171 Ky. 227, 188 S. W. 324.

(Former indictment pending for same offense is not a defense. Madisonville R. Co. v. Com., 140 Ky. 255, 130 S. W. 1084; Hobbs v. Com., 156 Ky. 847, 162 S. W. 104.)

# 165.

(1) Demurrer. A demurrer admits the facts charged, but only to test the law on such facts; and if it be overruled, the admission is no longer binding. Com. v. Jones, 10 Bush 725.

(1a) If an indictment under Ky. Stat., sec. 1213b, for having made a false statement concerning one's own financial condition and ability shows that the statement made did not concern the defendant's own financial condition and ability, a demurrer should be sustained. Gardner v. Com., 164 Ky. 196, 175 S. W. 362.

(2) If the indictment charges two offenses, except as allowed in sec. 127, a demurrer should be sustained, unless one of them is dismissed, as provided in sec. 168. Nichols v. Com., 78 Ky. 180; Ellis v. Com., 78 Ky. 130, and see Froman v. Com., 19 R. 948, 42 S. W. 728; Smith v. Com., 109 Ky. 685, 22 R. 1349, 60 S. W. 531.

(2a) If the indictment, improperly, charges more than one offense, and the Commonwealth's attorney dismisses one of the charges, the demurrer should be overruled; otherwise it will be sustained. Hence, if the indictment charges only one offense, there is no basis for a demurrer upon that ground. Ratliff v. Com., 182 Ky. 246.

A demurrer is the proper mode of objecting to an indictment upon the ground that it charges two offenses. Johnson v. Com., 90 Ky. 488, 12 R. 442, 14 S. W. 492; Hazelwood v. Com., 141 Ky. 232, 132 S. W. 567; and when indictment for misdemeanor shows that it is barred by limitation. Williams v. Com., 18 R. 667, 37 S. W. 839; Com. v. Megibben, 101 Ky. 195, 19 R. 291, 40 S. W. 694.

4. If the facts stated do not constitute a public offense.

# § 166 [166] [Demurrer for want of jurisdiction—proceedings.]

If the demurrer be sustained, on account of its appearing that the offense was not committed within the jurisdiction of the court, and it appear that the offense is a felony, and was committed in the jurisdiction of another court of this State, the court shall make an order that the clerk transmit to the clerk of the court having jurisdiction of the offense, a copy of the indictment, and all the original papers, including the bail bond, if any there be; and if the defendant be in custody, that he be carried and delivered, with a copy of the order, to the jailer of the county to which the papers are directed to be transmitted; and said order, and the proceedings in obedience thereto, shall operate in all respects as an order of a magistrate holding a defendant to answer the charge, according to Title 4; and the bail, if any, shall be liable for the appearance of the defendant, to answer to an indictment, in the court to which the papers are transmitted, and shall have the right of surrendering the defendant to the jailer of that county, or to that court. (See further as to practice when rant of jurisdiction appears, sec. 230.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 968, 989, 1061.

(5) If a conspiracy be charged against two or more persons for the same offense, that does not render the indictment obnoxious to subsec. 3, of this section. This section must be construed with sec. 234, which authorizes a conspiracy to be charged against two or more persons jointly indicted for the same offense. Salisbury v. Com., 79 Ky. 425, 3 R. 211.

The court will assume on demurrer that the offense was committed at the time charged in the indictment. Com. v. Cain, 14 Bush 525.

Title VI] Arraignment and Pleadings by Defendant.

# § 167 [167] [Want of jurisdiction appearing on trial-proceedings.]

The same orders may be made, and proceedings had, whenever it appears on the trial, by proof, that the offense, being a felony, was committed out of the jurisdiction of the court in which the indictment was found, but within the jurisdiction of another court of this State. (See further as to practice, secs. 166, 230.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 968, 989.

# § 168 [168] [Misjoinder of offenses dismissal of one.]

If the indictment improperly charge more than one offense, the attorney for the Commonwealth may dismiss one of them, and thereupon the demurrer shall not be sustained on that ground. (What offenses may be joined, see sec. 127.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 966, 968.

# § 169 [160] [Judgment on demurrer—when final.]

If the demurrer be sustained because the indictment contains matter which is a legal defense or bar to the indictment, the judgment shall be final, and the defendant shall be discharged from any further prosecution for the offense. (Dismissal when not a bar, sec. 178; when judgment of acquittal not to be reversed, sec. 339.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 963, 967, 968, 1008.

# § 170 [170] [Practice if demurrer sustained on certain grounds.]

If committed within its bounds. Lay on duced of different acts, any one of which might constitute the offense, the election of the Commonwealth to rely upon one of these acts to support a conviction does not amount to nolle prosequi as to the other acts. McCreary v. Com., 163 Ky. 206, 173 S. W. 251.

# § 168. (1) Misjoinder - practice.

If there is a misjoinder and attorney does not dismiss one of the offenses, demurrer should be sustained. Ellis v. Com., 78 Ky. 130; and see Nichols v. Com., 78 Ky. 180; Johnson v. Com., 90 Ky. 488, 12 R. 442, 14 S. W. 492; Froman v. Com., 19 R. 948, 42 S. W. 728; Smith v. Com., 109 Ky. 685, 22 R. 1349, 60 S. W. 531; in Clyner v. Com., 23 R. 1041, 64 S. W. 409; the court holds that an error in overruling demurrer to indictment was cured by the instructions limiting the jury to the consideration of only one offense. See further, Com. v. Bradley, 132 Ky. 512, 116 S. W. 761.

# (2) Demurrer to indictment if sustained is not a bar to a subsequent indictment for same offense unless the first indictment contained matter that was a bar or legal defense.

Com. v. Cook, 102 Ky. 288, 19 R. 1336, 43 S. W. 400; Code, sec. 165.

(Dismissal of indictment not a bar, sec. 178.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 968, 990.

# § 171 [171] [Demurrer overruled, defendant may plead.]

If the demurrer be overruled, the defendant has a right to plead to the indictment; if he fail to do so, final judgment shall be entered against him, and, if necessary, a jury impaneled to fix the punishment.

# § 172 [172] [Pleas that may be entered.]

There are but three kinds of pleas to an indictment—

1. A plea of guilty.
2. Not guilty.
3. A former conviction or acquittal of the offense charged, either of which may be pleaded with or without the plea of not guilty. (Form of pleas, sec. 164, and Forms 275-279.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 965, 992.

Bray, 123 Ky. 336, 96 S. W. 522, 29 R. 757.

(New indictment relates back to finding of original indictment. Bowman v Com., 146 Kv. 4S6, 143 S. W. 47; Berkley v. Com., 164 Ky. 191, 175 S. W. 364.)

# § 172. (1) Failure to plead.

Does the silence or failure of the accused to plead amount to a confession of guilt? 'The court says: "The safer rule would be, where the accused refused to file any plea, to have the plea of not guilty entered and counsel assigned; but when he does plead, no confession, except that of confession of guilt in open court by the accused in person, will authorize a judgment of conviction." Triplett v. Com., 84 Kv. 193, 8 R. 67, 1 S. W. 84.

(Pleas that may be entered - evidence. There are but three kinds of pleas that can be entered: First, guilty; second, not guilty; and, third, former conviction or acquittal; and the court may refuse to allow any other plea to be entered. Although there may be a plea.)

Title VIj
# Arraignment and Pleadings by Defendant. [§ 173-176]

# § 173 [173] [Plea of guilty must be by defendant in person.]

The plea of guilty can only be entered by the defendant himself in open court.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 993.

# § 174 [174] [Plea of guilty may be withdrawn.]

At any time before judgment the court may permit the plea of guilty to be withdrawn and a plea of not guilty substituted.

# § 175 [175] [Effect of plea of not guilty.]

The plea of not guilty is a denial of every material allegation in the indictment; and all matters of fact tending to establish a defense, other than a former conviction or acquittal, may be given in evidence under it.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 996.

# § 176 [176] [Acquittal on verdict, or conviction, a bar.]

An acquittal by a judgment upon a verdict, or a conviction, shall bar another prosecution for the same offense, notwithstanding a defect in form or substance in the indictment on which the acquittal or conviction took place.

(Plea of former acquittal, sec. 164.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1001.

# § 173. See notes to sec. 174.

(1) Plea of guilty may be withdrawn even after verdict, a new trial being granted for that purpose, if it appears that defendant was induced to enter the plea by the threats or promises of the court or attorney for the Commonwealth, and thereby overreached or deceived. Mounts v. Com., 89 Ky. 274, 11 R. 474, 12 S. W. 311; and it may be withdrawn and plea of not guilty entered after evidence has been introduced. Williams v. Com., 25 R. 2041, 80 S. W. 173.

(Plea of guilty-evidence. A plea of guilty does not deprive the Commonwealth of the right to introduce evidence bearing upon the question of punishment where the offense charged is of different degrees, or is punishable in the discretion of the jury, unless such plea has been entered upon the faith of promises by the court or Commonwealth attorney that the minimum punishment will be inflicted. Mounts v. Com., 89 Ky. 275, 11 R. 474, 12 S. W. 311; Cornelison v. Com., 84 Ky. 583, 8 R. 793, 2 S. W. 235; Williams v. Com., 25 R. 2041, 80 S. W. 173.

(3) Plea of guilty entered through fear of violence-new trial should be granted. Little v. Com., 142 Ky. 92, 133 S. W. 1149.

(Plea of guilty by advice of counsel will not be set aside. White v. Com. 140 Ky. 9, 130 S. W. 796.

(5) Plea of guilty in justice's court does not prevent appeal to circuit court and on appeal case will be tried de novo. Holtman v. Com., 129 Ky. 710, 112 S. W. 851.

# § 175. (1) Plea of not guilty.

"The plea of not guilty puts in issue every fact necessary for the Commonwealth to establish in order to a conviction, and as to all these facts or issues, the burden of establishing them by proof that excludes a reasonable doubt remains with the Commonwealth; but when the accused relies upon some separate matter of defense not embraced in those issues, the burden of proof to establish such matter is on the accused." Ball v. Com., 81 Ky. 662. 5 R. 787.

(2) A plea of "not guilty" does not put in issue surplusage in an indictment. Siegel v. Com., 177 Ky. 232, 197 S. W. 809.

# § 176. (1) Application of rule.

The law as to former jeopardy extends to all felonies and to all cases where the punishment inflicted is infamous. Williams v. Com., 78 Ky. 93; Robinson v. Com., 85 Ky. 386, 10 R. 972, 11 S. W. 210; and see Cooper v. Com., 106 Ky. 909, 21 R. 546, 51 S. W. 789, holding that where a person is acquitted he cannot be indicted for false swearing on trial at which he was acquitted.

(2) Arrest of judgment. Where judgment is arrested or reversed because of

# § 176 Arraignment and Pleadings by Defendant

error in not sustaining motion in arrest son v. Com., 83 Ky. 386, 10 R. 972, 11 S. W. 210; Williams v. Com., 78 Ky. 93.

(8) When jury may be discharged see 88 Ky. 386, secs. 250, 251. Secs. 243, 252, in so far they authorize the dismissal of the indictment and discharge of the jury in all cases and say it will not be a bar, are unconstitutional. Williams v. Com., 78 Ky. 93.

(3) Breach of the peace-bars what. A conviction for a breach of the peace is a bar to a subsequent indictment for malicious wounding for the same act. Com. v. Bright, 78 Ky. 238; but in Reddy v. Com., 97 Ky. 784, 17 R. 536, 31 S. W. 730; this case is partially overruled, and it is held that where the same acts constitute one or more misdemeanors that a prosecution for one of the misdemeanors will bar a prosecution for any other on the same state of facts; but a prosecution in an inferior court for a misdemeanor will not bar an indictment for a felony upon the same facts. And a fine for a breach of the peace is a bar to an indictment for assault and battery for the same offense. Com. v. Foster, 3 Met. 1; Linch v. Com., 18 R. 145, 35 S. W. 264.

(3a) Contempt. Punishment for contempt in assaulting a witness does not bar a prosecution for the same assault. Long v. Com., 177 Ky. 391, 197 S. W. 843.

(4) Consent of accused to discharge of jury waives any objection to being tried again, and his consent may appear by implication as well as by express words, but his silence will not be construed into consent. Robinson v. Com., 88 Ky. 386, 10 R. 972, 11 S. W. 210.

(11) After the jury had been sworn and while the evidence was being heard, one of the jurors announced that he was on the grand jury that found the indictment. The court, against the objection of the defendant, ordered the juror to be discharged and another selected, which was done. The defendant was convicted; on appeal the case was reversed, and on the second trial he pleaded in bar the discharge of the juror. Held that his plea should have been sustained, O'Brian v. Com., 9 Bush 333, overruling O'Brian v. Com., 6 Bush 563.

(lla) Distinct crime-prosecution not barred. An acquittal or dismissal of an indictment does not bar a prosecution under an indictment for another distinct crime. Head v. Com., 174 Ky. 841, 192 S. W. 861.

(12) Election to prosecute for misdemeanor. Re-reference to grand jury, before defendant has been put upon trial, is not a bar to prosecution under second indictment for felony, although order electing to prosecute under first indictment—which was also for a felony—for a misdemeanor was not set aside. King v. Com., 15 R. 247, 22 S. W. 750.

(13) Larceny of several articles at the same time. By the same act, and with the same intent, F took a horse, wagon.

# Arraignment and Pleadings by Defendant.

and harness, the property of H. Two indictments were found against F, one for stealing the horse, the other for stealing the wagon and harness. On the trial for stealing the horse F pleaded not guilty and was acquitted. This acquittal was a good plea in bar to the indictment for stealing the wagon and harness. Disney v. Coin., 9 R. 413, 5 S. W. 360; Minor v. Com., 5 R. 176; and see further, as to arraignment, notes to section 154.

(19) Pardon - effect of. A pardon pending the prosecution, but before conviction, is a good plea in bar to the indictment. Com. v. Bush, 2 Duv. 264.

(20) One who has been convicted of a felony and pardoned by the Governor on a subsequent commission of the same offense may be convicted and sentenced for double the time as provided in sec. 12, art. 1, chap. 29, Gen. "Stat. [now sec. 1130, Ky. Stat.]. Mount v. "Com., 2 Duv. 83.

(21) Reversal upon detective indictment. Accused can not plead former conviction to a trial under a new indictment. Mount v. Com., 2 Duv. 93; White v. Com., 9 Bush 178; and where one has been convicted of manslaughter under a murder indictment, he may, upon reversal of case, be tried again for murder. Com. v. Arnold, 83 Ky. 1, 6 R. 181; and see note 17.

(22) Shooting at random on public highway is a bar to a prosecution on same state of facts for flourishing a pistol in a threatening manner. Carman v. Com., 25 R. 1048, 76 S. W. 1078.

(23) Surety of tavern-keeper. The surety of a tavern-keeper is not concluded by a judgment against the tavern-keeper for a breach of his bond, but may contest his liability, and is entitled to ten days' notice and to a jury trial. Margolet v. Com., 3 Met. 406.

(24) Trial begins when jury is sworn. Willis v. Com., 85 Ky. 68, 8 R. 63, 2 S. W. 654; Tye v. Com., 3 R. 59; Collier v. Com., 110 Ky. 516, 22 R. 1929, 62 S. W. 4.

(25) Trial in inferior court. When a person has been indicted for a misdemeanor he may be tried by the quarterly court under the act of March 18, 1876 [Ky. Stat., sec. 1073]; but if he procures a trial under said act, not in good faith, but merely for the purpose of avoiding a trial in the circuit court, a judgment of acquittal in the quarterly court will not be a bar to a trial under the indictment in the circuit court. Carrington v. Com., 78 Ky. 83; Reddy v. Com., 97 Ky. 784, 17 R. 536, 31 S. W. 730.

(26) Trial under ordinance of a town was held, in Kemper v. Com., 85 Ky. 219, 8 R. 763, 3 S. W. 159; not to bar a prosecution for same offense by the Commonwealth.

# § 177-178

# Arraignment and Pleadings by Defendant.

# § 177

[Acquittal or conviction bars all degrees of offense.] If an offense consist of different degrees, a conviction, or acquittal, by judgment upon a verdict, shall be a bar to another prosecution for the offense in any of its degrees. (Degrees of offense defined, sec. 263.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1007.

# § 178

[Dismissal that does not operate as bar.] The dismissal of the indictment by the court, on demurrer, except as provided in section 169 [or for an objection to its form or substance taken on the trial, or for variance between the indictment and the proof*], shall not bar another prosecution for the same offense.

*See notes to this section and to sections 243 and 252.-Ed.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 963, 1008.

wealth. but see now Constitution, sec. 168, changing this rule.

(27) Trial in United States court. A defendant can not be convicted in a State court for an offense after having been tried and convicted for the same offense in the United States court. Com. v. Overby, 80 Ky. 208, 3 R. 704; but if United States court had no jurisdiction of offense trial by it is not bar. Blyew v. Com., 91 Ky. 200, 12 R. 742, 15 S. W. 356.

(27a) Two offenses committed by fraudulent deposit of two checks at one time "cold check law." Two checks drawn by different drawers in favor of the same payee were deposited at the same time by a holder who knew that the drawer had not sufficient funds on deposit to pay either check. Checks amounting to more than the face of either check were drawn against the deposit. The holder who made the deposit was guilty of two offenses; conviction of one offense was not a bar to a prosecution for the other. Siegel v. Com., 177 Ky. 232, 197 S. W. 809.

(28) Two indictments -when one barred. Two indictments were found at same time against a railroad company for nuisance in obstructing a road; on the trial of the second the company pleaded its acquittal under the first as a bar. The court held that the first trial was only a bar as to such offenses as were then attempted to be proved, and that whether acts and time were covered by first trial was a question of fact upon which evidence might be introduced. C. & O. R. R. v. Com., 58 Ky. 368, 10 R. 919, 11 S. W. 87; Cawein v. Com., 110 Ky. 273, 22 R. 1734, 61 S. W. 275.

(29) The State has the right in the trial of certain offenses to single out and rely upon certain acts, and when it confines itself to particular acts the trial is not a bar to other prosecutions for the same offense committed at other times within the year, but if the State on the first trial attempts to embrace or cover the entire period, then the first trial is a bar to a second prosecution for the same offense committed within the period. C. & O. R. R. v. Com., 88 Ky. 368, 10 R. 919, 11 S. W. 87; Cawein v. Com., 110 Ky. 273, 22 R. 1734, 61 S. W. 275.

(30) Commonwealth is not confined to time named in indictment, but may select and rely upon an offense committed at any time within year. C. & O. R. R. v. Com., 88 Ky. 368, 10 R. 919, 11 S. W. 87; see sec. 129 and notes.

(31) Variance. When a prosecution fails in the trial because of a variance, as where an indictment charges false swearing before a magistrate when the false swearing was before the county judge, dismissal of first will not bar a second indictment stating facts correctly. Turner v. Com., 19 R. 1161, 42 S. W. 1129; and see Gaskins v. Com., 97 Ky. 494, 17 R. 352, 30 S. W. 1017; and Code, sec. 178.

(1) Conviction or acquittal for any degree a bar to prosecution for any other degree. Com. v. Bright, 78 Ky. 238; Com. v. Hawkins, 11 Bush 603; but see Keddy v. Com., 97 Ky. 784, 17 R. 536, 31 S. W. 730; Linch v. Com., 18 R. 145, 35 S. W. 264, and notes to secs. 262-4.

(2) Dismissal of indictment is a final order and can not be set aside after term but a new indictment may be found. Com. v. Smith, 140 Ky. 580, 131 S. W. 391.

(1) Dismissal of indictment- when bar. See note to secs. 176, 243.

Title VI] Trial-Mode Of. [§ 179-180

# CHAPTER VI.

# TRIAL.

# § 180 [1s1] [Issues—how tried—jury trial.]

Issues of law shall be tried by the court. Issues of fact, in prosecution for offenses of which the punishment is limited to a fine of sixteen dollars, shall be tried by the court. All other issues of fact shall be tried by a jury.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1023.

(2 An order of court abating an indictment, although superinduced by the mistaken belief that defendant was dead, was a termination of prosecution under that indictment, and it could not be reinstated on docket as a pending prosecution against the defendant. Henry v. Com., 4 Bush 427.

(4) Variance-dismissal of indictment because of-when bar. See Gaskins v. Com., 97 Ky. 494, 17 R. 352, 30 S. W. 1017; Turner v. Com., 19 R. 1161, 42 S. W. 1129.

(5) Indictment dismissed under this section is no bar to another prosecution for same offense. Com. v. Elkins, 116 Ky. 603, 25 R. 485, 76 S. W. 25; and see section 243 and notes.

§ 179. See Com. v. Rose, 107 Ky. 566, 21 R. 1281, 54 S. W. 863, 1128.

733

# Title VI

# § 181 [182] [Issue of law.]

An issue of law arises on a demurrer to the indictment.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 102s.

# § 182 [188] [Issue of fact.]

An issue of fact arises upon a plea of not guilty, or of former acquittal, or conviction.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1025.

# § 183 [184] [Defendant on trial for felony must be present—bail.]

If the indictment be for a felony, the defendant must be present, and shall remain in actual custody during the trial; unless his bail appear personally in court, and consent that he may remain on bail, in which case he shall be placed in actual custody when the case is finally submitted to the jury.

If he escape from custody after the trial has commenced, the trial may either be stopped or progress to a verdict, at the discretion of the Commonwealth's attorney; but judgment shall not be rendered until the presence of the defendant is obtained. (Felony defined, sec. 6. See further, secs. 229. 285.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1027, 1055.

# § 182. Plea of not guilty raises an issue of fact and court must submit issue to jury if demanded.

Lucas v. Com., 26 R. 740, 82 S. W. 440.

# § 183.

1. Accused to be present during trial for felony. "One charged with the commission of a felony cannot be tried during his absence from the courtroom, and when any step is taken during the trial in the absence of the prisoner the record must show affirmatively that he could in no wise have been prejudiced by it." Meece v. Com., 78 Ky. 586, 1 R. 337; Rutherford v. Com., 78 Ky. 639, 1 R. 410; Yarbrough v. Com., 89 Ky. 151, 11 R. 351, 12 S. W. 143; but this rule does not apply to motion for new trial, Howard v. Com., 24 R. 612, 69 S. W. 721.
2. If accused escapes from custody after trial for felony is commenced it may proceed in his absence. Collier v. Com., 110 Ky. 516, 22 R. 1929, 62 S. W. 4.
3. A judgment of conviction for felony was not reversed because of the absence of the defendant during the progress of the trial. May v. Com., 153 Ky. 141, 154 S. W. 1074.
4. Two persons were indicted jointly for murder. During a greater part of the trial of one of them, the other was absent from the courtroom. After the verdict had been returned, he requested that the same jury be empaneled and sworn to try him on the evidence it had already heard on the trial of the other defendant, which was done. The verdict did not prejudice the substantial rights of the accused. Hite v. Com., 14 R. 308, 20 S. W. 217; and see, to same effect, Doyle v. Com., 18 R. 518, 37 S. W. 153.

Title VI] Trial-Time Of. [§ 184-185

# § 184 [185] [Trial of misdemeanor in absence of accused.]

If the indictment be for a misdemeanor, the trial may be had in the absence of the defendant; or, if present, he may remain on bail during the trial. (Misdemeanor defined, sec. 7.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 994, 1028.

# ARTICLE 2.

# TIME OF TRIAL.

# § 185. Trial at term indictment found.

§ 185' (186] [Trial at term indictment found.] If the defendant be in custody, or on bail, when the indictment is found, or be summoned or arrested three days before the time fixed for the trial, the trial may take place at the same term of the court, at a time to be fixed by the court. (Sec. as amended by Act of March 22, 1910, c. 56, p. 187, sec. 1.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1029.

(8 Submission of case to jury in absence of accused is error. Allen v. Com., 86 Ky. 642, 9 R. 784, 6 S. W. 645; Brewer v. Com., 10 R. 122, 8 S. W. 339; but it is not necessary that accused should be present every time after first submission when jury retire to their room. Richey v. Com., 10 R. 181, 8 S. W. 913.

(O) Trial for felony begins when jury is sworn, and after trial has begun the defendant is in the custody of the court and the bail not responsible unless they consent in open court that he may remain on bail. Willis v. Com., 85 Ky. 68 S R. 653; Tye v. Com., 3 R. 59; Collier v. Com., 110 Ky. 516, 22 R. 1929, 62 S. W. 4.

# § 184.

(1) Accused may be absent during trial for misdemeanor. Com. v. Cheek, 1 Duv. 62; Payne v. Com., 16 R. 839, 30 S. W. 416; Johnson v. Com., 1 Duv. 244; Walston v. Com., 106 S. W. 224, 32 R. 535; and see notes to sec. 157.

(la) A defendant in a misdemeanor case may be tried in his absence if he has opportunity to be present; if he is unable by reason of serious illness to be present a refusal of a continuance on that ground may be an abuse of discretion.

(2) One of two defendants jointly indicted may be tried although the other is not before the court. Sizemore v. Com., 108 S. W. 254, 32 R. 1154.

# § 186-188] Trial-Postponement Of. [Title VI

# § 186 [187] [Docketing prosecutions, setting for trial.]

All criminal prosecutions and penal actions not tried at any term of a court shall be docketed by the clerk for the next term, beginning with the first day thereof and setting them for as many days as may be necessary, in the same manner as ordinary civil actions are docketed. But the court may direct the number of days for which such prosecutions and actions shall be docketed; and may order any cause to be set for trial at a time specified in the order.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1024.

# § 187 [188] [Time prosecutions stand for trial.]

All prosecutions shall stand for trial on the day to which they are docketed, if the defendant be in custody, or on bail, or have been summoned or arrested three days before the day fixed for the trial. (Sec. as amended by Act of March 22 1910, c. 56, p. 187, sec. 2.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1024.

# ARTICLE 3.

# § 188. Trial may be postponed for cause.

# § 189. Civil Code regulates postponement, with exceptions—affidavit.

# § 188 [180] [Trial may be postponed for cause.]

When an indictment is called for trial, or at any time previous thereto, the court, upon sufficient cause shown by either party, may direct the trial to be postponed to any time in the same term, or to another term.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1020.

# § 886. Criminal prosecutions-defendant liable for costs, when.

The defendant in a criminal prosecution shall be adjudged to pay the costs of all continuances in his behalf, for which execution or other process may issue, as in civil cases, and, if convicted of a misdemeanor, all costs unpaid may be collected in the same manner as the fine; if convicted of a felony, by execution, or other process, as in civil cases. (Ky. Stat., sec. 886.)

# § 188. (1) Continuances.

A continuance was asked on account of illness of counsel and absence of witnesses; the case was laid over from time to time during the term and the attendance of the counsel and the witnesses was obtained. The refusal of the continuance was not error. May v. Com., 153 Ky. 141, 154 S. W. 1074.

# (2)

In these cases the absence of defendant when a misdemeanor case was called for trial, was not ground for continuance. Truitt v. Com., 177 Ky. 397, 197 S. W. 797; Long v. Com., 177 Ky. 391, 197 S. W. 843.

# (3)

It was error to deny a continuance of the prosecution on the ground that no written request had been filed with the clerk of the court, directing him to issue a subpoena for the absent witness. Com. v. Adkins, 171 Ky. 209, 188 S. W. 401.

# Title VI

# Trial-Postponement Of

# § 189

§ 189 [190] [Civil Code regulates postponement with exceptions—affidavit.] The provisions of the Code of Practice in civil actions, in regard to postponement of the trial of actions, shall apply to the postponement of prosecutions, on application of the defendant, except that, when the ground of application for a continuance is the absence of a material witness, and the defendant makes affidavit as to the facts which such witness would prove, the continuance shall be granted, unless the attorney for the Commonwealth admit upon the trial that the facts are true.

(4) It was not error in this case to refuse a continuance because of the absence of the attorney for the defendant. Tolliver v. Com., 165 Ky. 312, 176 S. W. 1190.

189. (1) Affidavit may be made by a person other than defendant, and if made at same term at which indictment is found the truth of it must be admitted if defendant is forced into trial. Hardesty v. Com., 88 Ky. 537, 11 R. 43, 11 S. W. 589; Wiggins v. Com., 104 Ky. 765, 20 R. 908, 47 S. W. 1073; Holly v. Com., 18 R. 41, 36 S. W. 532; Brock v. Com., 110 S. W. 878.

Amended affidavit. After a motion for continuance has been overruled, the court may permit an amended affidavit to be filed and the motion renewed. Com. v. Hourigan, 89 Ky. 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550; but after overruling first motion court may impose terms upon defendant as to subsequent motions. Smith v. Com., 13 R. 612, 17 S. W. 868.

(3) Attacking affidavit it is error to permit attorney for Commonwealth to ask defendant about certain statements made in an affidavit for continuance that is not read. Lindle v. Com., 111 Ky. 866, 23 R. 1307, 64 S. W. 986.

(3a) Commonwealth must not be put upon terms. A circuit court has no right to put the Commonwealth upon terms to try a felony case at the next term of the court. Every motion to try and to continue a case should be considered upon its merits. Com. v. Flynn, 161 Ky. 289, 170 S. W. 617.

(4) Constitutionality of act of May 15, 1886, providing that affidavit for continuance may be read as a deposition without admitting its truth, is suggested in Pace v. Com., 89 Ky. 204, 11 R. 407, 12 S. W. 271; Unsel v. Com., 87 Ky. 368, 10 R. 90, 8 S. W. 144; Kendall v. Com. 14 R. 15, 19 S. W. 173; Hardesty v. Com., 88 Ky. 537, 11 R. 43, 11 S. W. 589; but in Adkins v. Com., 98 Ky. 539, 17 R. 1091, 33 S. W. 948; Howard v. Com., 24 R. 950, 70 S. W. 295; this act is held valid, provided the accused has had reasonable time and compulsory process to obtain his witnesses.

(5) Counsel-absence of one of two or more counsel for defendant is not sufficient grounds for continuance, unless it clearly appears that a fair trial cannot be had in his absence. Stephens v. Com., 9 R. 742, 6 S. W. 456; Brown v. Com., 7 R. 451; Cook v. Com., 114 Ky. 586, 24 R. 1409, 7 S. W. 522.

Counter affidavits. It is not proper to permit the Commonwealth to read affidavits contradictory of the statements made in the affidavit of the accused for a continuance; but the court may make inquiry to ascertain whether facts stated in affidavit are true, and a continuance may be granted to a day in the term. Mount v. Com., 27 R. 788, 86 S. W. 707; Hughes v. Com., 25 R. 2153, 80 S. W. 197, Salisbury v. Com., 79 Ky. 425, 3 R. 211; Wells v. Com., 12 R. 111, 13 S. W. 915; and see McClurg v. Igleheart, 17 R. 913, 36 S. W. 14.

(6a) Defendant in misdemeanor-inability to be present. A defendant in a misdemeanor case may be tried in his absence if he has opportunity to be present; if he is unable by reason of serious illness to be present a refusal of continuance on that ground may be an abuse of discretion. Veal v. Com., 162 Ky. 250, 172 S. W. 501.

(6b) Deposition-reading affidavit as. In a trial of an indictment for felony which did not take place at the indictment term the affidavit of the accused for a continuance was read as the deposition of witnesses. There was no ground for reversal in the refusal of the continuance. Belcher v. Com., 165 Ky. 049, 177 S. W. 455; Adkins v. Com., 161 Ky. 254, 170 S. W. 607; Caudill v. Com., 155 Ky. 578, 159 S. W. 1149.

(6e) An affidavit for a continuance on account of the absence of a witness was admitted as a deposition; it was proper to refuse to allow to be read to the jury statements in the affidavit which were...

# Trial-Postponement Of

# §189

1. That whenever, in any criminal or penal action pending in any of the courts of this Commonwealth, an application shall be made by the defendant for a continuance, based upon affidavits stating the absence of one or more material witnesses, and the facts which such absent witness or witnesses would, if present, prove, the attorney for the Commonwealth shall not be compelled, in order to prevent a continuance, to admit the truth of the matter which it is alleged in the affidavit such absent witness or witnesses would prove, but only that such absent witness or witnesses, would, if present, testify as alleged in the affidavit. In which event the defendant may, on the trial, read such affidavit as the deposition of such absent witness or witnesses, subject, however, to exception for irrelevancy or incompetency; and the attorney for the Commonwealth shall be permitted to controvert the statement of such affidavit so read by other evidence, and to impeach such absent witness or witnesses to the same extent incompetent as evidence.

Smith v. Com., 151 Ky. 517, 152 S. W. 574.

(6d) In this case it was error not to allow an affidavit for a continuance to be read as evidence, although it was offered to be read out of time. Biggs v. Com., 162 Ky. 103, 172 S. W. 117.

(6e) The court properly denied a continuance on application of the accused because of the absence of a witness, where the court instructed the jury to consider the case as though the witness had so testified to the facts set forth in the application. Tyree v. Com., 160 Ky. 706, 170 S. W. 33.

(9) Discretion of court in refusing to permit an affidavit to be read as true will not be interfered with unless abused. Ross v. Com., 24 R. 1621, 59 S. W. 28.

(9a) In a certain case the refusal of a continuance was not an abuse of discretion. Kelly v. Com., 165 Ky. 483, 177 S. W. 249; Chaney v. Com., 149 Ky. 464, 149 S. W. 923.

(10) Evidence on former trial-when competent to be read. Mount v. Com., 27 R. 788, 86 S. W. 707; and notes 83-87, sec. 221.

(11) Facts showing accused entitled to continuance, although Commonwealth consented to admit affidavit as true. Murphy v. Com., 92 Ky. 485, 13 R. 695, 18 S. W. 163.

(12) Facts affidavit must show. Affidavit must show what steps have been taken to procure attendance of witness, that his evidence is material, that he is within the jurisdiction of the court, and that the affiant believes facts he can prove by him are true. Benge v. Com., 92 Ky. 1, 13 R. 308, 17 S. W. 146; Unsel v. Com., 87 Ky. 368, 10 R. 90, 8 S. W. 144; Stephens v. Com., 9 R. 742, 6 S. W. 456; Smith v. Com., 13 R. 612, 17 S. W. 868; Kendall v. Com., 14 R. 15, 19 S. W. 173.

(7a) A defendant was held to an examining trial on August 10; he was indicted on September 13; the case was set.

# Trial--Postponement Of

as if he were personally present: Provided, however, The court may, when, from the nature of the case, it shall be of opinion that the ends of justice require it, grant a continuance, unless the attorney for the Commonwealth will admit the truth of the matter which it is alleged in the affidavit such absent witness or witnesses would testify to.

2. The provisions of the foregoing [subsection I of this] section shall not apply to a motion for a continuance made at the same term at which the indictment in the action is found. (Words in brackets are an Act of May 15, 1886, c. 1145, p. 112; provisions of Civil Code referred to, sec. 315.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1021.

an ce was insufficient. Graham v. Com., 164 Ky. 317, 175 S. W. 981.

(12b) An affidavit for continuance which did not satisfy the requirements of Civil Code, sec. 315, was insufficient. Armstrong v. Com., 177 Ky. 690, 198 S. W. 24.

(12c) The Court of Appeals will not review an alleged error in refusing a continuance unless an affidavit was tendered or offered to be filed. Hollin v. Com., 163 Ky. 392, 173 S. W. 1106.

(12d) A case was called for trial. Defendants' counsel stated that they would try if the attendance of three out of four specified witnesses could be procured. The case was assigned to another day. On that day defendant applied for a continuance on account of the absence of one of the four, and showed the statutory grounds. It was error to refuse a continuance. Robinson v. Com., 178 Ky. 557, 199 S. W. 28.

(13) Joint defendants. One may rely upon the diligence of others to procure attendance of witnesses, and when the one who has attended to it has used diligence, the other is entitled to a continuance if witnesses are not present, the witnesses for each being the same. Walker v. Com., 5 R. 861.

(14) Filing away indictment does not abate prosecution and the indictment may be redocketed. Com. v. Bottoms, 105 Ky. 222, 20 R. 1159, 48 S. W. 974; Gross v. Com., 26 R. 870, 82 S. W. 618.

(15) Non-resident witness. It is not error to refuse a continuance asked for because of the absence of a witness who resides in another State, when it is improbable that he could be procured at the next term, and there are other witnesses who will testify to the facts that can be proved by him. Kennedy v. Com., 78 Ky. 447; nor to refuse a continuance where the affidavit failed to state that the defendant shows that he is entitled to a continuance.

(16) A continuance should be granted when the evidence of the absent witness is material and proper diligence has been used to secure his attendance, although the witness may be a resident of another State, if there are reasonable grounds to believe his presence can be secured at the next term. White v. Com., 80 Ky. 480, 4 R. 373.

(17) Other evidence introduced, proving what defendant expected to show by absent witnesses, refusal to grant continuance not error. Williams v. Com., 13 R. 753, 18 S. W. 1024; Simmons v. Com., 13 R. 830, 18 S. W. 534; Trabune v. Com., 13 R. 343, 17 S. W. 186; Roberts v. Com., 94 Ky. 499, 15 R. 341, 22 S. W. 845.

(18) Personal attendance of witness. Facts showing defendant was not prejudiced by failure to secure the personal attendance of his witness, although he filed an affidavit stating that personal attendance was necessary. Davis v. Com., 6 R. 654; see sec. 556, Civil Code. Does this section apply in criminal cases? Com. v. Minor, 89 Ky. 555, 11 R. 775, 13 S. W. 5; Hilbert v. Com., 21 R. 537, 81 S. W. 817.

(19) Prejudice against accused is not sufficient grounds for a continuance. Smith v. Com., 19 R. 1073, 42 S. W. 1138.

(20) Recognizance of witness by Commonwealth. When the Commonwealth has a witness recognized to testify in her behalf, the accused need not have the same witness recognized or subpoenaed for him, and can rely on the absence of the witness for a continuance. Saylor v. Com., 97 Ky. 184, 17 R. 100, 30 S. W. 390.

(21) Refusal to grant-reversible error. When the affidavit of the defendant shows that he is entitled to a continuance.

Title VI] Trial-—The Jury. [§ 191-192
# JURIES.

# Summoning, Impaneling, Challenging and Serving Of.

[See Chapter 74, Kentucky Statutes.]

# Selection of Jury.

§ 2264. Panel to be called—how deficiency supplied. On the day the jury shall be summoned to attend court, the panel shall be called, and the names of such as attend, and are not excused by the court, shall be entered of record; and any deficiency in the panel shall be supplied as directed in this chapter. (Ky. Stat., sec. 2264.)

§ 2265. Names to be placed in box and jury drawn from. The clerk shall write the names of the jurors entered of record on separate slips of paper, as near the same size and appearance as may be, and when a jury is wanted for the trial of a case the same shall be drawn from a box after the papers shall have been deposited therein and well mixed. The clerk shall provide and keep for that purpose a suitable box with a sliding lid. (Id., sec. 2265.)

§ 2266. Criminal cases—how jury selected. In criminal cases, where the defendant is entitled to a peremptory challenge of more than four jurors, the clerk, under the direction of the court, shall draw from the box twelve names, who shall compose the jury to try the cause, unless some one or all of them shall be challenged, in which case, the clerk shall draw from the box as many more as may be required, and as often as required until a jury be obtained or the whole panel be exhausted; then the court shall order the sheriff to make up the deficiency as directed in this chapter. (Id., sec. 2266.)

§ 191 [192] [Clerk to draw jury as directed by statute.] When an issue of fact in a criminal prosecution is about to be tried, the clerk shall draw, in the manner directed by the General Statutes [now Kentucky Statutes], the names of twelve jurors, who, if not challenged by the parties, nor excused by the court, shall compose the trial jury. (See Ky. Stat., sec. 2266.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1034.

§ 192 [195] [Panel—how filled.] When a juror is excused or a challenge to a juror is sustained the clerk shall draw the name of another juror to fill the panel until the list of standing jurors is exhausted, when the court shall order such a number of qualified jurors as it shall deem sufficient to complete the jury to be summoned by the sheriff, and the panel shall be filled from time to time from the jurors so summoned, and if they be exhausted, similar orders may be made for summoning other jurors, when all the witnesses to the homicide are present unless it is desired to show other material facts. Thurman v. Com., 142 Ky. 347, 134 S. W. 174.

(26) Continuance should not be granted unless asked before trial is commenced. Napier v. Com., 110 S. W. 842.

(27) Term at which new indictment is found in lieu of old is not indictment. Murphy v. Com. 1 Met. 365; Tyra v. Com., 2 Met. 1.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1034.

# § 193 [194] [Court may appoint person to summon jury.]

The court may, for sufficient cause, designate some other officer or person than the sheriff to summon petit jurors, the officer or person designated being first duly sworn in open court to discharge the duty faithfully and impartially.

# § 194 [105] [Jurors may be summoned from adjoining county.]

If the judge of the court be satisfied, after having made a fair effort, in good faith, for that purpose, that, from any cause, it will be impracticable to obtain a jury free of bias in the county wherein the prosecution is pending, he shall be authorized to order the sheriff to summon a sufficient number of qualified jurors from some adjoining county in which the judge shall believe there is the greatest probability of obtaining impartial jurors, and from those so summoned the jury may be formed.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1035.

# § 193.

(1) Special bailiffs - one or more may be appointed, as provided in this section, to summon jurors. Roberts v. Com., 94 Ky. 499, 15 R. 341, 22 S. W. 845; and may, with the consent of the court, appoint an assistant. Forman v. Com., 86 Ky. 605, 9 R. 759, 6 S. W. 579; Brown v. Com., 20 R. 1552, 49 S. W. 545; Mosely v. Com., 27 R. 214, 84 S. W. 748; Bowman v. Com., 146 Ky. 486, 143 S. W. 47; Sergent v. Com., 117 S. W. 362, 133 Ky. 284; Mosely v. Com., 84 S. W. 748, 27 R. 214; Com. v. Carnes, 98 S. W. 1045, 30 R. 506, 124 Ky. 340.

and the unsupported affidavit of defendant that the person appointed is not suitable, because biased against accused is not sufficient evidence of error in this respect. Forman v. Com., 86 Ky. 605, 9 R. 759, 6 S. W. 579; the court may on its own motion appoint a special bailiff when it appears that sheriff is related to accused. Allen v. Com., 11 R. 555, 12 S. W. 582; and objection that jurors were summoned by unauthorized person must be made before verdict. Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340.

(2) Sheriff should not be relieved of his duty in connection with the trial unless it appear upon satisfactory evidence that he is acting improperly. Jackson v. Com., 100 Ky. 230, 18 R. 795, 38 S. W. 422, 1091.

(3) The refusal of the court to designate some other officer or person to summon petit jurors than the sheriff, where the only showing was accused's affidavit that the sheriff was prejudiced and partisan, is not ground for reversal. MeCandless v. Com., 170 Ky. 301, 185 S. W. 1100.

# § 194.

(1) Jurors from adjoining county. The manner in which the judge is to satisfy himself that a jury cannot be obtained in the county where prosecution is pending is by making a fair effort to obtain a jury in that county, and his action cannot be controlled by the affidavit of the defendant. Roberts v. Com., 94 Ky. 499, 15 R. 341, 22 S. W. 845.

There having been three trials of a case in the same county, the court was authorized to send to an adjoining county for the jury in the fourth trial without making any effort to obtain jury in the county where trial was pending except to exhaust the regular panel. Bratford v. Com., 13 R. 154, 16 S. W. 710; and may send to a county from which a former jury had been obtained. Massie v. Com., 18 R. 367, 36 S. W. 550.

Mode of summoning. "There is no statute directing the summoning officer how he shall discharge his duty in selecting persons jurors from another county." "Where the sheriff is directed to summon extra talesmen from an adjoining county, the common law method.

Title VIj    Trial-The Jury.

# § 195 [1o8] [Mileage and pay of jurors from adjoining county.]

The jurors so summoned shall be allowed the same mileage as witnesses, and the same pay as other jurors while in attendance at the courts.

# § 196 [197] [Sheriff may summon—attendance coerced.]

The sheriff shall have the same power of summoning jurors under such order that he would have in the county of which he is the sheriff, and the court the same power of coercing the attendance of such jurors that it would have if they were summoned in the same county.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, Sec. 1035.

1. To the panel.

# Trial-The Jury

# § 198 [100] [Challenge of one defendant is challenge of all.]

When several defendants are tried together the challenge of any one of the defendants shall be considered the challenge of all.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1040.

# § 199 [200] [Grounds of challenge to panel.]

A challenge to the panel shall only be for a substantial irregularity, in selecting or summoning the jury, or in drawing the panel by the clerk. (Decision of court not subject to exception, sec. 281; see as to selecting and summoning jury, Ky. Stat., sec. 2241.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1037.

# § 200 [201] [Proceedings when challenge to panel sustained.]

If the challenge be sustained on the ground of irregularity in selecting or summoning the jury, all the standing jurors shall be excluded from the trial jury, and it shall be composed of persons summoned by the sheriff, or other officer appointed by the court for that purpose; if sustained because of irregularity in drawing the panel, all the names of the standing jury shall be replaced and another panel drawn.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, Sec. 1038.

# § 201 [202] [Challenge to juror peremptory or for cause.]

The challenge to the individual juror is either-

- Peremptory; or,
- For cause.

# § 202 [205] [Must be made before juror sworn in chief, unless by leave.]

It must be taken before he is sworn in chief, unless the court, for good cause, permit it to be made at any time before the jury is completed.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1040.

# § 203 [204] [Peremptory challenges allowed defendant.]

The defendant is entitled to [fifteen] peremptory challenges in prosecutions for the names provided in sec. 2241, Ky. Stat—when reversible error. Risner v. Com., 5 Ky. 539, 16 R. 84, 26 S. W. 388; and see Smith v. Com., 108 Ky. 531, 21 R. 1470, 51 S. W. 718, holding failure of judge to keep the key to be error, and Curtis v. Com., 110 Ky. 845, 23 R. 267, 62 S. W. 886; Wendling v. Com., 143 Ky. 587, 137 S. W. 205.

# § 199 [1] Challenge-waiver.

An objection for error in the formation of the petit jury is waived unless made at the proper time. Hazzard v. Com., 7 Ky. 366, 2 R. 356. Decisions of the court upon challenges to the panel or for cause are not subject to exception. Sec. 281; Morgan v. Com., 14 Bush 106; Rutherford v. Com., 13 Bush 608; Terrell v. Com., 13 Bush 246; Powers v. Com., 114 Ky. 237, 24 R. 1007, 70 S. W. 644.

Failure to put in wheel number of v. Com., 16 B. M. 15.

Title VIj Trial-The Jury. [§ 204-207]

felony, and to three in prosecutions for misdemeanor. (Fifteen in place of twenty, Act of April 10, 1893, c. 178, p. 747.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1040.

# § 204 [205] [Peremptory challenges allowed Commonwealth.]

The Commonwealth shall be entitled to five peremptory challenges in prosecutions for felony, and to three in prosecutions for misdemeanor.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1040.

# § 205 [206] [Either party may challenge for cause.]

The challenge for cause may be taken either by the Commonwealth or by the defendant (Ruling of court not subject to exceptions, sec. 281.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1040.

# § 206 [207] [Challenge may be general or particular.]

It may be general, that the juror is disqualified from serving in any case, or particular, that he is disqualified from serving in the case on trial.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1041.

# § 207 [208] [Causes of general challenge-newspaper accounts and rumors.]

Causes of general challenge are-

1. A want of the qualifications prescribed in the General Statutes [now Kentucky Statutes].
2. A conviction for a felony.
3. Unsoundness of mind, or such defect in the faculties of the mind or organs of the body as render him incapable of properly performing the duties of a juror.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1041

# 205. Challenge cause.

Decisions of the court upon challenge for cause are not subject to exceptions. Sec. 281 and notes; 14 Bush 106; 13 Bush 246; see, as to practice, notes to sec. 215.

# 207. (1) Challenge.

The whole examination of a juror is to be taken together to determine whether or not he is qualified. Opinion formed from rumor does not disqualify. Smith v. Com., 100 Ky. 133, 18 R. 652, 37 S. W. 586.

Ky. Stat., § 2253. Qualifications when verdict not invalid-exception to juror. No person shall be a competent juryman for the trial of criminal, penal or civil cases in any court unless he be a citizen, at least twenty-one years of age, a housekeeper, sober, temperate, discreet and of good demeanor. No civil officer except notaries public and trustees of schools; no transient person, physician, surgeon, practicing attorney or minister of any religious society, cashier or teller of a bank or those who may be supplying their places for the time, or attending at an asylum, or retail druggist, pharmaceutist, undertaker, depot agent of a railroad, or telegraph operator in employment, or person over 60, shall be compelled to serve on a petit jury; but the fact that a person not competent served on a jury shall not be cause for setting the verdict aside, nor shall exceptions be taken to any juror for such cause after the jury has been sworn.

(State militiamen exempt, Ky. Stat., sec. 2708; road overseers, sec. 4310; asylum employees, sec. 235.)

(3 Kinship of juror will not invalidate verdict when the juror did not know of the relationship. Miracle v. Com., 148 Ky. 453, 146 S. W. 1136.

Trial-The Jury

# § 208 [209] Particular causes of challenge.

Particular causes of challenge are actual and implied bias.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1042.

# § 209 [210] Actual bias defined.

Actual bias is the existence of such a state of mind on the part of the juror, in regard to the case, or to either party, as satisfies the court, in the exercise of a sound discretion, that he can not try the case impartially and without prejudice to the substantial rights of the parties challenging. [It shall not be a cause of challenge that a juror has read in the newspapers an account of the commission of the crime with which the prisoner is charged, if such juror shall state on oath that he believes he can render an impartial verdict according to the law and the evidence; and provided further, that in the trial of any criminal cause the fact that a person called as a juror has formed an opinion or impression, based upon rumor or upon newspaper statements (about the truth of which he has expressed no opinion), shall not disqualify him to serve as a juror in such case, if he shall, upon oath, state that he believes he can fairly and impartially render a verdict therein in accordance with the law and the evidence, and the court shall be satisfied of the truth of such statement.] (Words in brackets, Act of April 10, 1888, c. 945, p.81.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1042

# § 210 [211] Implied bias—what is.

A challenge for implied bias may be made—

1. If the juror be related by consanguinity, or affinity, or stand in the relation of guardian and ward, attorney and client, master and servant, landlord and tenant, employer and employed on wages, or be a member of the family of the defendant, or of the person alleged to be injured by the offense charged, or on whose complaint the prosecution was instituted.
2. If he be adverse to the defendant in a civil suit, or have complained against or been accused by him in a criminal prosecution.
3. If he have served on the grand jury which found the indictment, or on the coroner's jury which inquired into the death of the party, whose death is the subject of the indictment.
4. If he have served on a trial jury, which has tried another person for the offense charged in the indictment.
5. If he have been one of a former jury sworn to try the same indictment, and whose verdict was set aside, or who were discharged without a verdict.

# § 210. (1) Implied bias waiver.

The defendant may waive objection to a juror on account of implied bias. O'Brian v. Com., 9 Bush 333.

Smith v. Com., 100 Ky. 133, 18 R. 652, 37 S. W. 586.
