Title VI] Trial-The Jury. [9 211-215

# § 211 [217] [Exemption from service no ground for challenge.]

An exemption from serving on a jury is not a cause of challenge.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1041.

# § 212 [212] [Challenge tried by court--how determined.]

Challenges shall be tried and determined by the court in a summary manner, without the issues of law or fact arising thereon being reduced to writing, except when the court disallows a challenge for implied bias.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1043

# § 213 [218] [Juror may be examined on challenge.]

The juror may be examined on oath by either party, upon the challenge.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1043.

# § 214 [214] [Other witnesses may be examined.]

Other witnesses may also be examined and their attendance coerced.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1043.

# § 215 [215] [Commonwealth to challenge first—practice.]

The challenges to the juror shall first be made by the Commonwealth, and then by the defendant, and each party must exhaust his challenges to each juror before the other begins.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1044.

# § 213

Each individual juror is subject to challenge. Apkins v. Com., 148 Ky. 662, 147 S. W. 376.

# § 215

1. Challenge-practice. "Under the Code, each party is entitled to have a full panel of twelve jurors, found upon examination qualified to try the case, before being required to exercise the right of challenge to the individual juror; and whenever the number is lessened by challenge of either party, the panel must be filled before being passed on, and so on until the jury is completed. When a full panel of qualified jurors is presented, the Commonwealth must then either accept or challenge. Having accepted or declined to challenge, the challenges as to those then passed on are, in the language of the Code, exhausted; or, having challenged one or more, the others must be considered as accepted. The right of challenge is to be exercised by the Commonwealth and by the defendant in the same manner and upon the same conditions, the only difference being as to the number of peremptory challenges allowed to each. The defendant, as well as the Commonwealth, is therefore required to exhaust his challenges to each juror of a panel when presented to be passed upon." Munday v. Com., 81 Ky. 233, 5 R. 67; Edrington v. Com., 7 R. 377; Wiggins v. Com., 104 Ky. 765, 20 R. 908, 47 S. W. 1073; Colvin v. Com., 22 R. 1407, 60 S. W. 701.
2. "A full panel of twelve jurors found upon examination qualified to try the case should have been first presented to the Commonwealth for its acceptance or challenge, and all not then challenged should have been regarded as accepted; then the full panel should have been presented to the accused for his acceptance or challenge, and all not then challenged should have been regarded as accepted by him and not subject to be again."

Trial-The Jury

# § 216 [Order of challenges.]

The challenges of either party need not be all taken at once, but separately, in the following order:

1. To the panel.
2. To the juror for general disqualifications.
3. To the juror for implied bias.
4. To the juror for actual bias.
5. Peremptory.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1044.

After a jury had been selected, but before they were sworn, the Commonwealth's attorney filed an affidavit showing that one of them had expressed an opinion, thereupon the entire jury was discharged and an order made directing the sheriff to summon twenty-four jurors, including the eleven who had been discharged. Held that it would have been proper to have summoned an entire new jury, but the failure to do so was not a reversible error. Shelby v. Com., 91 Ky. 563, 13 R. 178, 16 S. W. 461; and See Howard v. Com., 25 R. 2213, 80 S. W. 211, 118 Ky. 1; Ellis v. Com., 146 Ky. 715, 143 S. W. 425.

After twelve men had been proven competent jurors upon a voir dire examination and before they were sworn the defendant had a right to challenge without cause; this right may be waived, but it is not waived either by the Commonwealth or the accused by an examination of the individual juror as to his fitness. Shelby v. Com., 91 Ky. 563, 13 R. 178, 16 S. W. 461; Smith v. Com., 20 R. 1848, 50 S. W. 241.

Where a portion of the regular panel are engaged in considering a case bystanders may be summoned to make up with the unoccupied jurors the full panel. McClernand v. Com., 11 R. 301, 12 S. W. 148; Ky. Stat., sec. 2266.

Defendant has right to challenge each individual juror. Apkins v. Com., 148 Ky. 662, 147 S. W. 376.

See notes to secs. 198-210, 281.

§ 217 [218] [Oath of jury to try issue.] When a jury, consisting of twelve qualified jurors, shall have been duly impaneled, they shall be sworn substantially as follows:

§ 217. Oath—to "truly try the case and a true verdict render" is sufficient. Young v. Com., 19 R. 929, 42 S. W. 1141.

749

# § 218-220] Trial--Conduct of the Jury.

"You, and each of you, do solemnly swear, that you will well and truly try the issue, and a true verdict render, in the case of the Commonwealth of Kentucky against A B, defendant."

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1045.

# § 218 [210] [Oath if no issue made.]

If no issue be made by the pleadings, the words "well and truly try the issue, and" shall be omitted from the oath.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1045.

# § 219 [220] [Indictment to be read and plea stated.]

The clerk, or Commonwealth's attorney, shall then read to the jury the indictment, and state the defendant's plea. (What pleas may be entered, sec. 172. Arraignment, secs. 154, 155.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1045.

# § 220. [Statement for Commonwealth.]

The attorney for the Commonwealth may then state to the jury the nature of the charge against the defendant, and the law and evidence upon which he relies in support of it.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1046.

# § 219.

(1) Reading of indictment- although it does not state in terms that plea. Where an indictment charges two offenses and one of them is dismissed it is not error for Commonwealth's attorney to read entire indictment to jury when he states offense being tried. Patterson v. Com., 86 Ky. 313, 9 R. 481, 5 S. W. 387, 765; Patterson v. Com., 99 Ky. 610, 9 R. 481, 5 S. W. 387, 765.

(2) Requirement that indictment shall be read and plea stated is mandatory. but fact that indictment is read by an attorney employed to prosecute and not for Commonwealth is not error. Galloway v. Com., 5 R. 213; Farris v. Com. 111 Ky. 236, 23 R. 580, 63 S. W. 615; and it is not necessary to again state defendant's plea after indictment has been read when it has been theretofore entered. Howard v. Com., 24 R. 91. 67 S. W. 1003; and when record is silent it will be presumed issue was made. Meece v. Com., 78 Ky. 586, 1 R. 337; see holding contrary, Hendrickson v. Com., 23 R. 1191. 64 S. W. 954; error in this respect must appear in motion for new trial. Ison v. Com., 23 R. 1805, 66 S. W. 184; Griffin v. Com., 23 R. 2148, 66 S. W. 740.

(3) Attorney for Commonwealth should not be permitted to read writings he intends to introduce as evidence, but when the writings are introduced the error will not be reversible if writings were competent. O'Brien v. Com., 89 Ky. 354, 11 R. 534, 12 S. W. 471; nor is it proper for attorney to state that defendant has the right to testify. Strutton v. Com., 23 R. 307, 62 S. W. 875.

(4) An order reciting that "same parties, and defendant having heretofore pleaded not guilty of the offense charged in the indictment for his trial puts himself upon his country and the attorney for the Commonwealth does likewise,"

Cross reference: See further, section 154 and notes thereto.

# Title VI

# Trial Conduct of the Jury.

# § 221 [221]

Introduction of evidence for Commonwealth. The counsel for the Commonwealth must then offer the evidence in support of the indictment. (Exclusion of young spectators from court room, see Ky. Stat., sec. 979. Separation of witnesses, see Civil Code, sec. 601.)

(3) Commonwealth attorney is not required to make a statement, but indictment must be read and plea stated. Hendrickson v. Com., 23 R. 1191, 64 S. W. 954; see note 5, sec. 219.

# § 221.

1. Accomplice. See section 241 and notes.
2. Affidavit for continuance admitted to be read as true can not be controverted. Pace v. Com., 89 Ky. 204, 11 R. 407, 12 S. W. 271; Young v. Com., 8 Bush 366; O'Brien v. Com., 89 Ky. 354, 11 R. 534, 12 S. W. 471: and see notes to section 189.
3. Belief of accused that he was in danger when he killed deceased may be shown by his testimony. Williams v. Com., 90 Ky. 596, 12 R. 525, 14 S. W. 595.
4. Burden of proof on defendant. In all statutory crimes it is competent for the Legislature to say that certain acts proven by the Commonwealth shall be sufficient to make out a presumptive case against the accused and cast the burden on him; but the burden can not be cast on defendant until the Commonwealth has first proven some material fact conducing to his guilt. Com. v. Minor, 88 Ky. 422, 10 R. 1008, 11 S. W. 472; as illustrations of such laws, see Ky. Stat., secs. 1967, 2571; and Com. v. Standard Oil Co., 112 S. W. 633, 129 Ky. 546.
5. Character of deceased-evidence to sustain can not be introduced until it has been attacked. Parker v. Com., 96 Ky. 212, 16 R. 449, 28 S. W. 500; Webb v. Com., 11 R. 642, 12 S. W. 769.
6. Upon the trial of the accused for murder it was held proper under the facts of this case to permit him to prove that the deceased was a man of violent, cruel and bloodthirsty disposition, and in the habit of carrying concealed deadly weapons. Payne v. Com., 1 Met. 370; Riley v. Com., 94 Ky. 266, 15 R. 246, 22 S. W. 222.
7. Character of accused, if he testify, may be put in issue as that of any other witness, and the Commonwealth is not confined to his character for truth, but may assail his general moral character. Lockard v. Com., 87 Ky. 201, 10 R. 102, 8 S. W. 266; Burdette v. Com., 93 Ky. 76, 13 R. 960, 18 S. W. 1011; Pace v. Com., 89 Ky. 204, 11 R. 407, 12 S. W. 271; but the inquiry as to his character for morality and truth must relate to the time he testifies and not when he committed the crime. Com. v. Hourigan, 89 Ky. 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550; 78 Ky. 219; McDonald v. Com., 86 Ky. 10, 9 R. 230, 4 S. W. 687; and it is not proper to inquire about particular acts or crimes making up character, whether good or bad. Com. v. Welch, 111 Ky. 530, 23 R. 151, 63 S. W. 984; Smith v. Com., 13 R. 31, 16 S. W. 137; and see Leslie v. Com., 19 R. 1201, 42 S. W. 1005; Saylor v. Com., 97 Ky. 184, 17 R. 100, 30 S. W. 390; Baker v. Com., 20 R. 1778, 50 S. W. 54; Parker v. Com., 21 R. 406, 51 S. W. 573; Pennington v. Com., 21 R. 542, 51 S. W. 818; Britton v. Com., 96 S. W. 556, 123 Ky. 411, 29 R. 860; Haydon v. Com., 140 Ky. 634, 131 S. W. 521.
8. The Commonwealth is not allowed to call witnesses to prove the bad character of the accused unless to rebut the evidence of good character already introduced by him, and evidence as to character must be restricted to the trait of character which is in issue. Young v. Com., 6 Bush 312.
9. Proof of character in aid of the presumption of innocence is admissible. 6 Bush 312; but should be limited to the time of the discovery of the commission of the offense, and should not extend to the date of the arrest, and is not allowed to extend to particular acts or conduct in special cases. White v. Com., 80 Ky. 480, 4 R. 373; nor should proof of the bad character of any of the family of the accused be admitted. McClure v. Com., 81 Ky. 448, 5 R. 468.
10. Character of witness. The Commonwealth can not introduce evidence of particular facts for the purpose of affecting the credibility of the witnesses for the defendant, as that "one of them has been prosecuted for false swearing, and that all of the witnesses introduced by the defendant belonged to a clique that were banded together to swear negroes out of any offense charged against them." Taylor v. Com., 3 Bush 508. Nor is proof that the family or associates of the witnesses are in bad repute competent. Kean v. Com., 10 Bush 190. In impeaching the credit of a witness by general evidence, the examination must be confined to his general reputation, and

Trial-Conduct of the Jury. [Title VI of the Criminal Code. Com. v. Minor, 89 Ky. 555, 11 R. 775, 13 S. W. 5; Hilbert v. Com., 21 R. 537, 51 S. W. 817.]

is not permitted as to particular facts.

1. "In order to impeach a witness the evidence of bad character should be directed to the time of the trial, and to this end, under proper circumstances, evidence of previous bad character is competent; while the evidence of bad character at a time previous may tend to show the character of the witness at the present, it should be admitted with some caution." Mitchell v. Com. 78 Ky. 219.
2. (11) "An impeaching witness should be able to say that he knows the general estimation in which the witness sought to be impeached is held among those who know him, or among whom he lives. If he does, then he may say whether the witness is esteemed by his neighbors and those who know him as a person of good moral character or not." Furnish. v. Com., 14 Bush 180.
3. (12) "Impeachment of character of witness, whose affidavit is read as a deposition, is allowable, and witness may be impeached without regard to materiality of his evidence." Davis v. Com., 95 Ky. 19, 15 R. 396, 23 S. W. 585.
4. (13) Circumstantial evidence. Circumstantial evidence, "when of a satisfactory character, sufficient to warrant a conviction, should be left, like direct or positive evidence, to be considered by the jury, and to have such weight as they deem it entitled to." Brady v. Com., 11 Bush 282.
5. (14) There is no law requiring the court, at the instance of the accused, to have dead bodies taken up and examined at the expense of the State or county for the purpose of furnishing him with evidence. Salisbury v. Com., 79 Ky. 425, 3 R. 211.
6. (15) Circumstantial evidence is competent to establish the fact that the person charged to have been murdered is dead, and it is for the jury to determine its conclusiveness. Johnson v. Com., 81 Ky. 325, 5 R. 197; and see O'Brien v. Com., 89 Ky. 354, 11 R. 534, 12 S. W. 471.
7. (16) Where the evidence of an accomplice is corroborated, he occupies the same position as any other witness so far as the method of contradicting or impeaching him is concerned. Com. v. Craft, 81 Ky. 250, 5 R. 53.
8. (17) "Where a witness states a fact prejudicial to the party calling him, the latter may be allowed to show that such fact does not exist by proving that the fact does not exist."

752

Title VI] Trial-Conduct of the Jury. [§ 221

witness had made statements to others inconsistent with his present testimony." Blackburn v. Com., 12 Bush 1S1; Champ v. Com., 2 Met. 17; but the court should say to the jury that the evidence is only admissible to affect credibility of witness contradicted. Asheraft v. Com., 24 R. 488, 68 S. W. 847.

(26) " witness can not be cross-examined upon facts collateral and irrelevant to the issue for the purpose of contradicting him, his answers to such facts being conclusive against the party calling for them; nor can a witness who fails to testify to substantive facts be asked if he has not made statements to others out of court that such facts exist. for the purpose of proving that he has made such statements. Loving v. Com., 80 Ky. 507, 4 R. 457; Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340; Crittenden v. Com., 82 Ky. 104, 6 R. 20; Champ v. Com., 2 Met. 17; Cornelius v. Com., 15 B. M. 539.

(27) Where a witness on cross-examination stated that he had not said he heard deceased make certain statements, it was error to allow proof that witness had made such a statement. Com. v. Hourigan, 89 Ky. 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550.

(28) Declarations. Statements of the accused explanatory of certain acts which he did shortly before killing were not competent. Oder v. Com.. 80 Ky. 32, 4 R. 1S; Terrell v. Com., 13 Bush 246. "Declarations of one conspirator are only admissible against the others when made before the object of the conspiracy is accomplished." "Miller v. Com., 78 Ky. 15; Thompson v. Com., 1 Met. 13; Hudson v. Com., 2 Duv. 531; Jones v. Coin., 2 Duv. 554; and cases cited in note 22.

(29) A plea of guilty made in an examining court may be proved by oral testimony. Rector v. Com., 80 Ky. 468, 4 P. 323.

(30) Statements of persons who had agreed to act in concert with the accused in the event of a hostile meeting between him and deceased are competent for the Commonwealth; and, when such statements are asked for, the accused has the right to have the whole of the conversation at that time. Cornelius v. Con., 15 B. M. 539: and see Rapp v. Com., 14 B M. 614; Merriweather v. Com., 26 R. 793, 82 S. W. 592.

(31) Defendant as a witness-may be cross-examined as any other witness. Welsh v. Com., 111 Ky. 530. 23 R. 151, 60 S. W. 185, 948, 1118; 63 S. W. 984; 64 S. W. 262; Warren v. Com., 99 Ky. 370, 18 R. 141, 35 S. W. 1028; Burdette v. Com., 93 Ky. 76, 13 R. 960, 18 S. W. 1011; Leslie v. Com., 19 R. 1201, 42 S. W. 1095; Saylor v. Com., 97 Ky. 184, 17 R. 100, 30 S. W. 390; Baker v. Com., 106 Ky. 212, 20 R. 1778, 50 S. W. 54; Parker v. Com., 21 R. 406, 51 S. W. 573; Pennington v. Com., 21 R. 542, 51 S. W. 818; and may be recalled as any other witness, Abbott v. Com., 23 R. 226, 62 S. W. 715.

(31a) In a prosecution for felony, the defendant testified in his own behalf; it was proper to compel him to answer the question whether he had been convicted of a felony. It was the duty of the Circuit Judge to admonish the jury that this was only competent for the purpose of affecting his credibility as a witness. GeBurk v. Com., 153 Ky. 264, 155 S. W. 381.

(32) It is error to allow comment by Commonwealth's attorney on failure of accused to testify on his examining trial. Parrott v. Com., 20 R. 761, 47 S. W. 452; in Taylor v. Com., 17 R. 12l4, 34 S. W. 227, the court held that such comment was not error.

(33) Diagram of premises where killing occurred may be examined and referred to by witness who made it. Com. v. Hourigan, 89 Ky. 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550.

(33a) It is error to require the defendant to stand up by the side of the prosecuting witness during the argument of the Commonwealth's attorney in order that the jury may see the size of the two. Balee v. Com., 153 Ky. 558, 156 S. W. 147.

(34) Dogs as witnesses. In some cases the trialing of bloodhounds of good character and pedigree is admissible to locate the accused and establish his connection with the crime. Pedigo v. Com., 103 Ky. 41, 19 R. 1723, 44 S. W. 143; Denham v. Com., 27 R. 171, 84 S. W. 538, 119 Ky. 508.

(35) Drunkenness is no excuse for crime, but evidence of drunkenness is competent to show want of malice. Buckhanan v. Com., 86 Ky. 110, 9 R. 411, 5 S. W. 358; Wilkerson v. Com., S8 Ky. 20, 10 R. 658, 9 S. W. 836; Shannahan v. Com.. 8 Bush 463; Bishop v. Com., 109 Ky. 558, 22 R. 1161, 60 S. W. 190; and where a felonious intent is necessary to constitute an offense—such as robbery—the accused may show that he was too drunk to have any intent when he committed the act. Keeton v. Com., 92 Ky. 522, 13 R. 748, 18 S. W. 359; and so in prosecution for false swearing. Williams v. Com., 113 Ky. 652, 24 R. 465, 68. S. W. 871.

# Trial--Conduct of the Jury.

(36) Dying declaration. The admission of proof of dying declarations is not an infringement of the constitutional right of the accused to confront his witnesses face to face; but, to be admissible, the declaration must be made in extremis, and under a solemn sense of impending dissolution. *Walston v. Com. 16 B. M. 15; and wife is competent to prove declarations of her husband, Arnett v. Com., 114 Ky. 593, 24 R. 1440, 71 S. W. 635; Hilbert v. Com.*, 21 R. 537, 51 S. W. 817.

(37) A statement by the deceased as to how and by whom he was injured, made about twenty minutes before he became insensible, in which condition he remained until he died, coupled with the statement "that he was ruined and a dead man, unless he got speedy relief," was held not to have been made under a sense of impending dissolution. *Adwell v. Com.*, 17 B. M. 310.

(38) A declaration of a wounded person, made and reduced to writing two hours before his death, when he believed he would recover, if afterward referred to by him shortly before his death, and when he knew he must die, and its truth affirmed, is competent evidence. "Dying declarations are not necessarily either written or spoken. Any method of communication between mind and mind may be adopted that will develop the thought, as the pressure of the hand, a nod of the head or a glance of the eye." *Mockabee v. Com., 78 Ky. 380; and see Young v. Com.*, 6 Bush 312.

(39) Dying declarations should be restricted to the act of killing and the circumstances immediately attending it and forming a part of the res gestae. *Leiber v. Com., 9 Bush 11; and see Collins v. Com., 12 Bush 271; Terrell v. Com., 13 Bush 246; Luby v. Com., 12 Bush 1; Com. v. Hargis, 30 R. 510, 99 S. W. 348, 124 Ky. 356; Kennedy v. Com., 100 S. W. 242, 30 R. 1065; and may be impeached by showing different declarations or by showing them to be untrue, Com. v. Lawson*, 25 R. 2187, 80 S. W. 206.

(40) Dying declaration must relate to the circumstances attending the crime; only so much of it is competent as details the manner of it. Statement that "he was shot for nothing" was incompetent, but, as this was proved by other witnesses, its introduction was not prejudicial. *Pace v. Com., 89 Ky. 204, 11 R. 407, 12 S. W. 271; and see Chittenden v. Com., 10 R. 330, 9 S. W. 386; Pennington v. Com., 24 R. 321, 68 S. W. 451; and the length of time between declarations and death does not affect its competency, Burton v. Com.*, 24 R. 1162, 70 S. W. 831.

(41) To be admissible they must be made when the party has given up all hope of life: but whether this be the case or not may be determined by the surrounding circumstances and by the evident danger of the deceased, and he need not in express words or in equivalent language declare that he knows he is about to die. *Com. v. Matthews, 89 Ky. 287, 11 R. 505, 12 S. W. 333; Peoples v. Com., 87 Ky. 487, 10 R. 517, 10 S. W. 642; Cleveland v. Com., 101 S. W. 931, 31 R. 116, Biggs v. Com.*, 150 Ky. 675.

(42) A mere belief of the party that he would die is not sufficient to warrant the introduction of his statements as a dying declaration. *Vaughan v. Com.*, 86 Ky. 431, 9 R. 644, 6 S. W. 153.

(43) If dying declaration has been reduced to writing, the writing should be introduced as the best evidence; but if writing is excluded by objection of accused then oral evidence is competent. Statements made at different times may be proved if otherwise competent. *Hines v. Com., 90 Ky. 64, 11 R. 865, 13 S. W. 445; Blvew v. Com., 91 Ky. 200, 12 R. 742, 15 S. W. 356; Saylor v. Com.*, 97 Ky. 184, 17 R. 100, 30 S. W. 390.

(44) "To the general rule excluding matters of opinion or belief an exception should be made, allowing the declarations of the deceased in behalf of the accused where they will explain the acts and conduct of deceased, or show his feelings or motives, intent or belief, when they are essential to qualify or aggravate his conduct." *Haney v. Com.*, 5 R. 203.

(45) Defendant may introduce dying declaration in his behalf, and the statement of deceased that he and accused were playing and that the shooting was an accident. *Com. v. Matthews, 89 Ky. 287, 11 R. 505, 12 S. W. 333; or that he was wholly to blame for the difficulty and brought on the trouble himself, and did not want accused prosecuted," is competent. *Brock v. Com.*, 92 Ky. 183, 13 R. 450, 17 S. W. 337; Haney v. Com.*, 5 R. 203.

(46) Any fact which the deceased could testify to, if alive, is admissible as his dying declaration. Brock v. Com., 92 Ky. 183, 13 R. 450, 17 S. W. 337.

(47) Upon trial of one accomplice it is not competent to prove a dying declaration of the other exculpating the one on trial. *Mitchell v. Com.*, 12 R. 458, 14 S. W. 489; nor can defendant prove that another person made a dying declaration.

# Title VIJ

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury. [§ 221

that he and not accused committed crime. Davis v. Com., 95 Ky. 19, 15 R. 396, 23 S. W. 585.

(48) Nor is it any objection that deceased was at the time under influence of narcotics; such objection would go to credibility alone. Hays v. Com., 12 R. 611, 14 S. W. 833.

(49) Error in admitting incompetent evidence under an indictment for murder prejudicial, although accused found guilty of manslaughter. Scott v. Com., 94 Ky. 511, 15 R. 251, 23 S. W. 219.

(50) False swearing. Evidence of one witness not sufficient to convict--corroborating circumstances-written evidence. Com. v. Davis, 92 Ky. 460, 13 R. 676, 18 S. W. 10.

(51) Flight-feigned insanity of accused competent against him. Basham v. Com., 87 Ky. 440, 10 R. 434, 9 S. W. 384.

(52) Foreign records-proper authentication of. Faustre v. Com., 92 Ky. 34, 13 R. 347, 17 S. W. 189; Mount v. Com. 1 Duv. 90:

(53) Husband and wife. Wife is a competent witness against her husband where he is charged with committing or attempting to commit a crime against her. Com. v. Sapp, 90 Kv. 580, 12 R. 484, 14 S. W. 834; overruling Turnbull v. Com., 79 Ky. 495, 3 R. 275; and is competent to prove dying declarations of husband, Arnett v. Com., 114 Ky. 593 24 R. 1440, 71 S. W. 635.

(54) A divorced wife is a competent witness in behalf of her husband, to prove facts which came to her knowledge while the marriage relation existed between them, but which did not come to her confidentially, nor by means of her situation as his wife. Com., 13 Bush 155. Elswick v.

(55) The wife of one of three defendants in an indictment was a competent witness against the other two, after the indictment had been dismissed as to her husband. Ray v. Com., 12 Bush 397.

(56) Under a joint indictment against several for murder, one of them being awarded a separate trial, the wives of the others were competent witnesses for him. Thompson v. Com., 1 Met. 13; Cornelius v. Com., 3 Met. 481.

(57) "Communication between husband and wife-meaning of. Com. v. Sapp, 90 Ky. 580. 12 R. 484, 14 S. W. 834; and see as to competency of letters written by husband to wife. Scott v. Com., 94 Ky. 511, 15 R. 251, 23 S. W. 219; see further, notes to section 606, Civil Code.

(58) Insanity. Opinions of non-professional witnesses as to the sanity of the defendant may be admitted as evidence, but the court should be satisfied before admitting such evidence that the witness has had an opportunity, by association, and observation, to form an opinion as to the sanity of the accused. Brown v. Com., 14 Bush 308; Cotrell v. Com., 13 R. 305, 17 S. W. 149; Massie v. Com., 15 R. 562, 24 S. W. 611; Phelps v. Com., 17 R. 706, 32 S. W. 470; Abbott v. Com., 107 Ky. 624, 21 R. 1372, 55 S. W. 196; Banks v. Com., 141 S. W. 380, 145 Ky. 800.

(59) An expert should not be allowed to express his professional opinion as to the sanity of the accused unless there is a hypothetical case or agreed facts submitted to him. 14 Bush 398; McCarthy v. Com., 14 R. 285. 20 S. W. 229: Davis v. Com., 6 R. 658.

(60) Evidence that the accused has been from infancy afflicted with somnolentia or somnambulism is competent. Fain v. Com., 78 Ky. 183.

(61) As to moral insanity, see Scott v. Com., 4 Met. 227; Graham v. Com., 16 B. M. 587: Smith, v. Com., 1 Duv. 224. Uncontrollable impulse, see Abbott v. Com., 107 Ky. 624, 21 R. 1372, 55 S. W. 196.

(62) Competency of physician who does not claim to be an expert. Montgomery v. Com., 88 Ky. 509, 11 R. 40. 11 S. W. 475; Abbott v. Com., 107 Ky. 624, 21 R. 1372, 55 S. W. 196.

(63) Contradiction of physician by showing former statements in conflict with his testimony. Montgomery v. Com., 88 Ky. 509, 11 R. 40, 11 S. W. 475.

(64) Evidence of insanity both before and after commission of crime is competent. Montgomery v. Com., 88 Ky. 509, 11 R. 40. 11 S. W. 475; Moore v. Com., 92 Ky. 630, 13. R. 738, 18.S. W. 833.

(65) To authorize admission of testimony of insanity of parents or relatives of accused it should first appear by other evidence that defendant was insane when he committed act; but see this case for exception to rule. Murphy v. Com., 92 Ky. 485. 13 R. 695, 18 S. W. 163.

(66) Defense for crime--not sufficient to show alone that accused was insane when he committed crime; it must appear that by reason of his insanity he did not know right from wrong, or did not have sufficient will power to control his actions. Montgomery v. Com., 88 Ky. 509, 11 R. 40, 11 S. W. 475: Abbott v. Com., 107 Ky. 624, 21 R. 1372, 55 S. W. 196.

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury

§ 221

1. Incompetent evidence. Exception 895, 15 S. W. 856; and see Brown v. Com., 102 Kv. 227, 19 R. 1174. 43 S. W. 214.
2. Limiting effect of testimony by instruction is proper where the evidence is only introduced for or competent for the purpose of contradicting a witness. Fueston v. Com., 91 Ky. 230, 12 R. 854, 15 S. W. 177; Collins v. Com., 15 R. 691, 25 S. W. 743.
3. Liquor selling illegally. Evidence upon which conviction may be had. Ky. Stat., sec. 2571. Smith v. Com., 17 R. 416, 31 S. W. 471; Stamper v. Com., 102 Ky. 33, 19 R. 1014, 42 S. W. 915; and Ky. Stat., secs. 2557a, b.
4. Malice-manslaughter. Incompetent to prove declarations showing malice under indictment for manslaughter. Com. v. Matthews, 89 Ky. 287, 11 R. 505, 12 S. W. 333.
5. Motive for crime. Competent to show. O'Brien v. Com., 89 Kv. 354. I1 R. 334. 12 S. W. 471; Martin v. Com., 93 Ky. 189, 14 R. 95, 19 S. W. 580; Carpenter v. Com., 92 Ky. 452, 13 R. 658, 18 S. W. 9.
6. Motive of accused-evidence in his behalf. See Massie v. Com., 15 R. 562, 24 S. W. 611; Riggs v. Com., 103 Ky. 610, 20 R. 276, 45 S. W. 866; Ellis v. Com., 146 Ky. 715, 143 S. W. 425.
7. Part of a statement being proved by defendant, it is proper to allow Commonwealth to prove remainder. O'Brien v. Com., 89 Ky. 354, 11 R. 534, 12 S. W. 471.
8. Prisoner in penitentiary may testify in criminal case unless he is disqualified under sec. 118o, Ky. Stat. Com. v. Minor, 89 Kv. 555. 1I R. 775, 13 S. W. 5; Combs v. Com., 15 R. 660, 25 S. W. 590; Hancock v. Parker, 100 Ky. 143, 18 R. 622, 37 S. W. 594.
9. Provocation. Words tending to excite passions-competent to reduce crime to manslaughter. Massive v. Com., 15 R. 562, 24 S. W. 611; Stott v. Com., 17 R. 308, 29 S. W. 141.
10. Questions that degrade or disgrace may be asked for the purpose of impairing credibility of witness, but he cannot be required to expose himself to punishment or prosecution for crime. Burdette v. Com., 93 Kv. 76. 13 R. 960, 18 S. W. 10ll; nor asked about particular acts or crimes except that he has been convicted of a felony. Civil Code, sec. 597; Howard v. Com., 110 Ky. 356, 22 R. 1845 61 S. W. 756; Com. v. Welch, 111 Ky. 530, 23 R. 151, 63 S. W. 984; Leach v. Com., 112 S. W. 595, 120 Ky. 497.
11. Rape. Evidence sufficient to convict. Malone v. Com., 91 Ky. 307, 12 R. 756.

(84) It is error to permit the prosecuting witness to remain in the court-room and testify when the other witnesses are excluded, on a trial for murder. Salisbury v. Com., 79 Ky. 425, 3 R. 211.

(85) It is proper to allow a witness for prosecution to remain in court-room after he has testified, if his presence will aid the Commonwealth's attorney. Galloway v. Com., 7 R. 165; Marcum v. Com., 8 R. 418, 1 S. W. 727; and where a witness remains in the court-room contrary to instructions and hears witnesses testify, court may reject or permit his testimony; and unless it appear that court abused its discretion its action will not be revised. Haskins v. Com., 8 R. 419, 1 S. W. 730.

(86) Slanderous words. Proper to admit evidence of to reduce crime to manslaughter. Massie v. Com., 15 R. 562, 24 S. W. 611; Riggs v. Com., 103 Ky. 610. 20 R. 276, 45 S. W. 866; Stott v. Com., 17 R. 308, 29 S. W. 141.

(87) Testimony on former trial-reproduction of. When the witness has died since the former trial of the case, and his testimony on the former trial is reproduced, evidence that he had made statements since the former trial that his testimony then given was false is not competent. The rule that a witness cannot be impeached until he has been inquired of concerning the statement applies. Craft v. Com., 81 Ky. 250, 5 R. 53; as to evidence taken down by official stenographer, see Ky. Stat., sec. 4643, and Austin v. Com., 98 S. W. 295, 30 R. 296, 124 Ky. 55.

(88) "The law is that when the witness states that he remembers the substance of all the deceased witness testified to, both on the direct and cross-examination, he is a competent witness; and when the evidence is heard, if it does not clearly appear that the witness does not remember the substance of all that the deceased person testified to, the evidence should be permitted to go to the jury; but if it be manifest to the court that he does not so remember, the evidence should be rejected. The testimony of each witness must be tested by the same rule, and, if admissible, must stand by itself." Bush v. Com., 80 Ky. 244, 3 R. 740.

(89) Where it is not shown that it is impossible to secure the attendance on the final trial of a witness who testified at the examining trial, it is not proper to...

(90) The testimony of an absent witness on the former trial cannot be proved unless the witness is dead. Collins v. Com., 12 Bush 271; O'Brian v. Com., 6 Bush 563.

(91) The written statement in the bill of exceptions of the testimony of a deceased witness on a former trial is not competent. Kean v. Com., 10 Bush 190; Boner v. Com., 19 R. 409, 40 S. W. 700.

(92) Testimony out of time. Evidence in chief after close of testimony may be admitted for good cause, but in these cases it was held error. Williams v. Com., 90 Ky. 596, 12 R. 525, 14 S. W. 595; Abbott v. Com., 23 R. 226, 62 S. W. 715; court has large discretion, and, unless manifestly abused, its action will not be reversed. Cargill v. Com., 93 Ky. 578, 14 R. 517, 20 S. W. 782; Collins v. Com., 15 R. 691, 25 S. W. 743; Williams v. Com., 90 Ky. 590, 12 R. 525, 14 S. W. 595.

(93) Threats. It is competent for defendant to prove a threat made by deceased, although not in terms directed at him if all the circumstances show that it referred to him. Sparks v. Con., 89 Ky. 644, 7 R. 163; but opinion of witness as to whom threats are directed at is not competent. Johnson v. Com., 9 Bush 2224.

(94) Evidence of general threats, not directed at any particular person, was held competent in Whittaker v. Con., 13 R. 504, 17 S. W. 358; Brooks v. Com., 100 Ky. 194, 18 R. 702, 37 S. W. 1043; and see Madison v. Com., 13 R. 313, 17 S. W. 164; Ellis v. Com., 146 Ky. 715, 143 S. W. 425.

(95) Threats by deceased to kill mother of accused were held competent in Rutherford v. Com., 13 Bush 608.

(96) Threats by accused are competent to show malice. Nichols v. Com., 11 Bush 575; but not under a manslaughter indictment. Com. v. Matthews, 89 Ky. 287, 11 R. 505, 12 S. W. 333.

(97) Uncommunicated threats are competent where it is material to show who began the difficulty. Miller v. Com., 89 Ky. 653, 10 R. 672, 10 S. W. 137; Hart v. Com., 85 Ky. 77, 8 R. 714, 2 S. W. 673; Wheeler v. Com., 27 R. 1090, 87 S. W. 757.

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury.

# § 222. [Statement for defendant.]

The defendant or his attorney may then state the nature of his defense, and the law and evidence upon which he relies in support of it: Provided, He may, at his option, make the statement herein referred to immediately before the Commonwealth introduces any of its testimony, and after the attorney for the Commonwealth, if he has so desired, has made the statement for the Commonwealth. (Section as amended by Act of March 24, 1902, c. 89, p. 210.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1046, 1050.

# § 223 [222] [Introduction of evidence for defendant.]

The defendant, or his counsel, must then offer his evidence in support of his defense. (Exclusion of young spectators from court room, see Ky. Stat., sec. 979. Separation of witnesses, see Civil Code, sec. 601.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 214, 990, 1050, 1091.

3106; and where there is evidence of communicated threats it is competent to prove uncommunicated threats. Moore v. Com., 143 Ky. 405, 136 S. W. 608.

(1) Impeaching dying declaration. Cornelius v. Com., 15 B. M. 539. Where a dying declaration of a deceased person was introduced on a trial for murder it was competent for the defense to show his statements inconsistent therewith. Tolliver v. Com., 161 Ky. 81, 170 S. W. 515.

(2) Defendant-failure to testify not subject to comment. Begley v. Com., 107 S. W. 243, 32 R. 890; Boggs v. Com., 128 S. W. 73; Hinton v. Com., 121 S. W. 434, 134 Ky. 511.

(2a) Upon a trial of one accused of felony if he fails to testify and the attorney for the Commonwealth in the course of the argument refers to the fact that certain evidence adduced was not contradicted this does not constitute ground for reversal. Farley v. Com., 165 Ky. 600, 177 S. W. 431.

(2b) If accused does not testify as a witness, evidence attacking his veracity is inadmissible, and evidence attacking his moral character is inadmissible when he has not testified, unless he has introduced other witnesses as to his moral character. Bennett v. Com., 171 Ky. 63, 186 S. W. 933.

(2c) In a prosecution for felony, the defendant testified in his own behalf: it was proper to compel him to answer the question whether he had been convicted of a felony. It was the duty of the Circuit Judge to admonish the jury that this was only competent for the purpose of affecting his credibility as a witness.

(3) Disqualified defendant not competent to testify. Hinton v. Com., 121 S. W. 434, 134 Ky. 511.

# Title VI

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury

# § 1645. Defendant in criminal case may testify-effect of failure

In all criminal and penal prosecutions now pending or hereafter instituted in any of the courts of this Commonwealth, the defendant on trial, on his own request, shall be allowed to testify in his own behalf, but his failure to do so shall not be commented upon, or be allowed to create any presumption against him. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1645.)

# § 1646. Must testify before introducing other testimony

The defendant requesting that he be allowed to testify, shall not be allowed to testify in chief, after any other witness has testified for the defense. (Id., sec. 1646.)

# § 1647. Conspirators may testify unless conspiracy proven

If two or more persons are jointly indicted they may testify for each other, unless a conspiracy is charged in the indictment and proven to the satisfaction of the court.* (Id., sec. 1647.)

# § 1648. Conspirator may testify for himself

If a conspiracy is charged in the indictment, and proven to the satisfaction of the court, then each defendant named in the indictment may testify on his own behalf, as above provided.* (Id., sec. 1648.)

*Sections 1645-1648, inclusive, of the Kentucky Statutes, are respectively sections 22, 23, 24 and 25 of an Act of June 17, 1893, c. 227, p. 1163. They are a verbatim copy of sections 1-4, inclusive, of an Act of May 1, 1886, c. 898, p. 83.

This Act of 1886 was inserted by Judge Bullitt following and in the nature of an amendment, or addition, to section 223 of his 1895 edition of the Code. It was also inserted in Carroll's 1888 edition of the Code following section 223. By an Act of February 21, 1890, c. 217, p. 20, this 1888 edition of Carroll's Codes of Practice, including "sections 1 to 450, inclusive, of the Criminal Code, including amendments to said sections, or any of them," were "adopted as the laws of the land."

As the Act of 1886 was an "amendment to" section 223 of Carroll's 1888 edition of the Criminal Code it was, by the Act of 1890, ratified and confirmed as such by the Legislature. The Act of 1893, being another act upon the same subject and a verbatim copy of the Act of 1886, impliedly repealed the Act of 1886 and the amendment to section 223 of the Criminal Code.

By an Act of March 23, 1894, c. 112, p. 342, "subsections 3 and 4 of section 223 of the Criminal Code of Practice be, and they are hereby, repealed." However, the title to this Act of 1894 was, "An act to amend section 223 of the Criminal Code of Practice; an act to repeal section 234 and subsections 3 and 4 of section 223 of the Criminal Code of Practice." It is submitted that subsections 1-4, inclusive, of section 223, being the Act of 1886, were repealed by the Act of 1893, compiled as sections 1645-1648, inclusive, of the Kentucky Statutes.

It is also submitted that the said Act of 1894 cannot be construed as repealing sections 24 and 25 of the Act of 1893 (identical with sections 4 and 5 of the Act of 1886 and, therefore, with subsections 4 and 5 of section 223 of Carroll's 1888 edition), for the reason that the intention to amend the Act of 1893 was not declared in the title to the Act of 1894, as required by section 51 of the Constitution as construed by the Court of Appeals. Furthermore, the expression of an intention to repeal section 234, if, indeed, that section also was not affected by the Act of 1893, could not be said to inform the reader of any intention to repeal any part of the Act of 1893, now the Kentucky Statutes. See Ogden v. Cronan, Sheriff, 171 Ky. 254, 186 S. W. 941; Exall v. Holland, 166 Ky. 315, 179 S. W. 241; South v. Fish, 181 Ky. 349, and other cases cited in the latter case.-Ed.

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury

# § 224 [225] [Rebutting evidence.]

The parties may then respectively offer rebutting evidence only, unless the court, for good reason in furtherance of justice, permit them to offer evidence upon their original case.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1051.

# § 225 [226] [Instructions to be in writing.]

The court shall, on the motion of either party and before any argument to the jury, instruct the jury on the law applicable to the case, which shall always be given in writing.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1110.

# 224. (1) Rebuttal evidence-rulings

of court in respect to will not be interfered with unless prejudicial. Tetterton v. Com., 59 S. W. 8, 28 R. 146.

The admission of testimony in chief given by rebuttal witnesses was not an abuse of discretion in this case. Traux v. Com., 149 Ky. 699, 149 S. W. 1033.

# § 225. (1) Abstract instructions.

It ought to appear, before an instruction containing merely an abstract proposition is made the sole ground of reversal, that it is at least probable that the prisoner may have been prejudiced by it. Nichols v. Com., 11 Bush 575.

The following instruction contains a mere abstract principle, and should not be given: "Where there is a conflict in the testimony of witnesses, the one side being of an affirmative and the other of a negative character, the affirmative character of the testimony is preferred, and is entitled to the greater weight by the jury in making up their verdict." Lou. Chemical Works v. Com., 8 Bush 179.

# (3) Accomplice.

The court, in instructing the jury as to the weight that should be given to the evidence of an accomplice, should follow substantially section 241. Craft v. Com., 80 Ky. 349, 4 R. 182, and notes to sec. 241.

# (4) After argument begun.

It is bad practice, but not reversible error to give additional or explanatory instructions after the argument has commenced. McDaniel v. Com., 6 Bush 326; West v. Com., 14 R. 217, 20 S. W. 219; and see Pearce v. Com., 19 R. 782, 42 S. W. 107; Wilhelm v. Com., 16 R. 428, 28 S. W. 783; Powers v. Com., 110 Ky. 386, 22 R. 1807, 61 S. W. 735, 63 S. W. 976; Middleton v. Com., 124 S. W. 355, 136 Ky. 354.

# (5) Aiders and abettors.

It is error to say that defendant is guilty if he was present and "approved of" or "consented to" the killing, but it is proper to use the words "encourage, aid or abet," or the words "counsel, advise or assist." True v. Com., 90 Ky. 651, 12 R. 594, 14 S. W. 684; Omer v. Com., 95 Ky. 353, 15 R. 694, 25 S. W. 594; and see Plummer v. Com., 1 Bush 76. Chittenden v. Com., 10 R. 330, 9 S. W. 386; Reed v. Com., 100 S. W. 84, 30 R. 1215.

(5a) An indictment against a single defendant charged him only as a principal; an instruction authorizing his conviction as aider and abettor was error. Hollin v. Com., 158 Ky. 427, 165 S. W. 407.

# Alibi.

As to proper instructions, see Young v. Com., 8 Bush 366.

# (7) Assault and battery-proper instructions.

Cornelison v. Com., 84 Ky. 583, 8 R. 793, 2 S. W. 235.

# (8) Assuming facts.

Instructions that assume as a fact certain propositions which are controverted, and that the jury have a right to determine, are erroneous. Leiber v. Com., 9 Bush 11; Berry v. Com., 10 Bush 15; Edgerton v. Com., 7 Bush 142.

# (9) Bigamy.

For instructions concerning, see Com. v. Jackson, 11 Bush 679.

# (10) "Brought on difficulty"-error to instruct jury that defendant cannot avail himself of right of self-defense if he brought on difficulty.

Allen v. Com., 86 Ky. 643, 9 R. 784, 6 S. W. 645, and see note 49.

# (11) Where right of self-defense is attempted to be denied because of the fault of the accused the jury should be instructed that they must believe beyond a reasonable doubt accused was in fault.

Allen v. Com., 86 Ky. 643, 9 R. 784, 6 S. W. 645; Riley v. Com., 94 Ky. 266, 15 R. 46, 22 S. W. 222; Cockrill v. Com., 95 Ky. 22, 15 R. 328, 23 S. W. 659.

# (12) If one by his own wrongful act makes the harm or danger to himself necessary or excusable he cannot rely on plea of self-defense, but words spoken in jest or act not intended or calculated to

# Title VI

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury.

# § 225

Provoke can not deprive accused of right of self-defense, although they may have caused difficulty. Allen v. Com., 86 Ky. 643, 9 R. 784. 6 S. W. 645; WileoxEn v. Com., 15 R. 261. 23 S. W. 195: Combs v. Com., 15 R. 659, 25 S. W. 592.

(13) Character of accused. It is proper where character of accused has been impeached to say to jury that "evidence of his bad character is admitted only to affect his credibility as a witness and for no other purpose." Hasson v. Com., 10 R. 1054, 11 S. W. 286; and see note 41.

(14) Circumstantial evidence "should be left, like direct or positive evidence to be considered by the jury, and to have such weight as they deem it entitled to, without caution or suggestion on the part of the court as to its value or the necessity to scrutinize it closely." Brady v. Com., 11 Bush 282.

(15) Confessions. It is error to instruct the jury on the trial of one charged with murder, that if they believe the accused confessed the killing, they ought to find him guilty. Butler v. Com., 2 Duv. 435; or to assume in an instruction that a confession has been made. The jury should be left to determine from the evidence whether or not any confession of guilt has been made. Cunningham v. Com., 9 Bush 149.

(16) An instruction in the language of section 240 is held to be proper in Wigginton v. Com., 92 Ky. 282, 13 R. 641, 17 S. W. 634; and see notes to sec. 240.

(17) Consequences of his own acts. Instruction in murder case that a sane man intends probable consequences of his own acts deliberately done erroneous. Rogers v. Com., 16 R. 199, 27 S. W. 813.

(18) Correction of instruction, after verdict has been announced, and while jury is being polled, is error. Roberts v. Com., 90 Ky. 654. 12 R. 681, 14 S. W. 832.

(19) Deadly weapon-instruction defining. Evana v. Cont., 11 R. 551. 12 S. W. 767; Cosby v. Com., 115 Ky. 221, 24 R. 2051, 72 S. W. 1098.

(20) Deadly weapon—what is, under an indictment for striking or injuring with, is a question that should be left to jury if there is room for doubt as to whether it was or not a deadly weapon. Com. v. Dunean, 91 Ky. 592. "16 S. W. 530, 13 R. 162; MeWilliams v. Com., 18 R. 92, 35 S. W. 538.

(21) Defense of dwelling. Proper instructions. Wright v. Com., 85 Ky. 123, 8 R. 718, 2 S. W. 904; Sparks v. Com., 89 Ky. 644, 7 R. 163; Estep v. Com., 86 Ky. 30, 9 R. 278, 4 S. W. 820.

(22) Defense of others. Proper instructions in cases of killing in defense of another. Stanley v. Com., 86 Ky. 440, 9 R. 655, 6 S. W. 155; Chittenden v. Com., 10 R. 330, 9 S. W. 386; Gills v. Com., 18 R. 560, 37 S. W. 269.

(23) Drunkenness. The jury should not be told that drunkenness mitigates the offense. Shannahan v. Com., 8 Bush 463, expressly overruling Smith v. Com., 1 Duv. 224; Blimm v. Com., 7 Bush 320; and impliedly overruling Golliher v. Com., 2 Duv. 163; Curry v. Com., 2 Bush 67, and see Marshall v. Com., 141 Ky. 222, 132 S. W. 139.

(24) Not proper to give insanity instructions where accused was merely drunk when he committed crime. Wilkerson v. Com., 88 Ky. 29, 10 R. 656, 9 S. W. 836; Buchanan v. Com., 86 Ky. 110, 9 R. 411, 5 S. W. 358; nor is it proper to single out from the evidence the fact of drunkenness and tell the jury why they are permitted to hear evidence bearing upon it. Buchanan v. Com., 86 Ky. 110, R. 411, 5 S. W. 358; Bishop v. Com., 100 Ky. 558, 22 R. 1161, 60 S. W. 190; as to defense of drunkenness in crimes that a felonious intention is an essential ingredient of, as robbery. see Kecton v. Com., 92 Ky. 522, 13 R. 748, 18 S. W. 359; Williams v. Com., 113 Ky. 652, 24 R. 465, 68 S. W. 871.

(25) Erroneous instruction-failure to properly instruct. Although jury found defendant guilty of manslaughter, when under the instructions they should have found him guilty of murder or acquitted, did not cure the error in failing to give manslaughter instructions. Campbell v. Com., 88 Ky. 402, 10 R. 975, 11 S. W. 290.

(25a) False swearing-instructions as to. On the trial of an indictment for an offense under Kentucky Statutes, 1174, it is the duty of the circuit court to instruct the jury that before they can convict, the guilt of the accused must be established beyond a reasonable doubt by the testimony of two witnesses, or of one witness and strong corroborating circumstances. Walker v. Com., 162 Ky. 111, 172 S. W. 109.

(26) Former convictions-proper instructions where indictment charges former convictions. Rector v. Com., 80 Ky. 468, 4 R. 323; and see Ky. Stat., sec. 1130; Brown v. Com., 100 Ky. 127, 18 R. 630, 37 S. W. 496; Hall v. Com., 106 Ky. 894, 21 R. 520, 51 S. W. 814; Herndon v. Com., 105 Ky. 197, 20 R. 1114, 48 S. W. 989.

(27) Grouping facts. Prominent facts should not be grouped together in an

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury

§225

instruction. Williams v. Com., 9 Bush W. 381, 145 Kv. 800; in last three cases 274; and see Elswick v. Com., 13 Bush 155. Nor should an attempt be made to enumerate the collateral facts which the evidence tended to prove. "Instructions ought, as a general rule, to be based only upon such facts as must be found by the jury in order to establish guilt or to make out a defense." Coffman v. Com., 10 Bush 495.

(28) Incompetent evidence cured by. Evidence that the defendant had sold liquor on a Sunday subsequent to the date of the warrant was incompetent; but the error in admitting it was cured by an instruction that unless the jury believed from the evidence that the defendant sold liquor on Sunday within the time covered by the warrant, they must acquit him. Megowan v. Com., 2 Met. 3.

Where incompetent testimony was admitted, an instruction telling the jury to disregard such evidence "except so far as it harmonized with facts afterward developed" did not cure the error. Dve v. Com., 3 Bush 3.

(29) Instruction to be based on evidence. When there is no testimony showing that the homicide was committed in self-defense it is not necessary to give any instruction on that question. Fitzpatrick v. Com., 81 Ky. 357, 5 R. 363; Slagel v. Com., 81 Kv. 485. 5 R. 545.

(29a) An instruction should not be given upon a theory of the case wholly unsupported by evidence. Barker v. Com., 159 Kv. 304. 166 S. W. 981; Gamble v. Com., 151 Ky. 372, 151 S. W. 924; MeCoy v. Com., 149 Kv. 447. 149 S. W. 903.

(30) "Impending danger." If accused, when killing occurs, believes and has reasonable grounds to believe that danger to him is impending and that there is no other apparent means of escape, then he may strike in self-defense. (off. man v. Com., 10 Bush 495; Radford v. Com., 9 R. 378, 5 S. W. 343; and see Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340.

(31) Insanity. Defense of need only be sustained by a preponderance of the evidence. Moore v. Com., 92 Ky. 630. 13 R. 738. 18 S. W. 833; Ball v. Com., 81 Ky. 662. 5 R. 787; Phelps v. Com. 17 R. 706, 32 S. W. 470; and see (ira- graham v. Com., 16 B. M. 587; Scott v. Com., 4 Met. 227; Portwood v. Com. 104 Ky. 496, 20 R. 680, 47 S. W. 3:39; Abbott v. Com., 107 Ky. 624, 21 R. 1372, 55 S. W. 196; Jollv v. Com., 110 Ky. 190. 22 R. 1622, 61 S. W. 49; McCartv v. Com., 114 Ky. 620, 24 R. 1427, 71 S. W. 656; Wilcoxin v. Com., 138 Ky. 846, 129 S. W. 310; Banks v. Com., 161 S. 762.

# Title VI

# Trial--Conduct of the Jury.

# § 225

was evidence tending to show that the money had been lost and not embezzled. It was error to fail to instruct the jury as to this defense. *Cline v. Com.,* 161 Ky. 678. 171 S. W. 412.

(39d) In the trial of an indictment for murder it is not necessary to give an instruction as to involuntary manslaughter if it appears upon the whole evidence that the killing could not have been involuntary manslaughter. *Cox v. Com.,* 158 Ky. 435, 165 S. W. 411.

(39e) In a prosecution for homicide the evidence was held sufficient to justify an instruction on the reckless handling of a pistol; but in this case failure to give an instruction on involuntary manslaughter was not a prejudicial error. *Begley v. Com.,* 154 Ky. 30, 156 S. W. 886.

(39f) On the trial of an indictment for murder there was no evidence tending to or consistent with the theory of voluntary manslaughter. It was not error for the Circuit Court to fail to instruct on voluntary manslaughter. *MeElwain v. Com.,* 154 Ky. 242, 157 S. W. 6.

(39g) On the trial of an indictment for larceny where the undisputed evidence showed the value of the stolen property to be in excess of twenty dollars, it was not necessary for the court to instruct on petty larceny. *Klette v. Com.,* 165 Ky. 430, 177 S. W. 258.

(40) Less punishment than statute fixed-instruction prescribing. not prejudicial. *Logsden v. Com.,* 9 R. 431, 5 S. W. 393.

(41) Limiting effect of testimony. Where testimony is only competent for the purpose of contradicting the evidence of other witnesses the jury should be so instructed. *Fueston v. Com., 91 Ky. 230, 12 R. 854, 15 S. W. 177; Collins v. Com., 15 R. 691, 25 S. W. 743; Ashcraft v. Com.,* 24 R. 488, 68 S. W. 847.

(42) Malice. It is error to instruct the jury that "malice is implied by the law from any cruel and unnecessary act done by one person to another, and from the deliberate and unnecessary use of a deadly weapon." The jury may infer malice from the circumstances of the killing, but an instruction telling them that they may infer malice from certain acts is erroneous. *Farris v. Com., 14 Bush 362; and see Buckner v. Com., 14 Bush 601; Trimble v. Com., 78 Ky. 176; Bush v. Com., 78 Ky. 268; Salisbury v. Com.,* 79 Ky. 425, 3 R. 211.

(43) Instruction defining malice as a "predetermination to do the act without lawful excuse, it being immaterial how suddenly or recently this predetermination is formed," is proper. *Armstrong v. Com.,* 15 R. 344; 22 S. W. 750; 23 S. W. 654.

(43a) Malicious cutting. On a trial under an indictment for malicious cutting based on Kentucky Statutes, 1166, instruction should be given under that section and under Kentucky Statutes, 1242. *Lewis v. Com.,* 156 Ky. 336, 160 S. W. 1061.

(44) Manslaughter. "It is not the province of the lower court to weigh evidence for the purpose of determining whether a person on trial for his life is entitled to an instruction as to manslaughter. If there is any evidence tending to show the homicide is of the degree of manslaughter the accused is entitled to an instruction upon that hypothesis." *Bowlin v. Com.,* 94 Ky. 391, 15 R. 149, 22 S. W. 543.

(45) Means of escape. The question is not whether the jury believe that defendant had no safe means of escape; they should be instructed that if defendant believed that he had no safe means of escape. *Oakley v. Com.,* 10 R. 885, 11 S. W. 72.

(45a) Murder. An instruction authorizing the jury to find the defendant guilty of murder, if they believed beyond reasonable doubt that he was present, aiding or assisting another person to kill the deceased, was not prejudicial error, inasmuch as there was no evidence that any one other than the accused killed the deceased. *Caudill v. Com.,* 155 Ky. 578. 159 S. W. 1149.

(45b) Certain instructions given on a trial for murder were approved by the Court of Appeals. *Graham v. Com.,* 164 Ky. 317, 175 S. W. 981.

(46) Murder—manslaughter—involuntary manslaughter. Where the indictment is for murder the accused may be convicted of any degree of homicide as fixed by the common law, viz., Murder, voluntary manslaughter or involuntary manslaughter, and the court should, under an indictment for murder or voluntary manslaughter, when the facts justify it, instruct the jury as to involuntary manslaughter. *Buckner v. Com., 14 Bush 601; Bush v. Com., 78 Ky. 268. But when there is no evidence showing the existence of facts constituting involuntary manslaughter an instruction as to it should not be given. York v. Com., 82 Ky. 360, 6 R. 334; Mitchell v. Com.,* 78 Ky. 219.

(47) When no witness saw the homicide committed, or the parties on the occasion when the killing occurred, the

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury.

Law applicable to murder, manslaughter and involuntary manslaughter should be given in order to meet any state of facts the jury may find from the circumstances in evidence to have existed. Rutherford v. Com., 13 Bush 608; and see cases cited in note 36.

(48) Under an indictment for murder or manslaughter, an instruction as to the statutory offense defined in sec. 2, art. 4, chap. 29, Gen. Stat. [now Ky. Stat., sec. 115l], is not proper. Trimble v. Com., 78 Ky. 176; Buckner v. Com., 14 Bush 601; Conner v. Com., 13 Bush 714.

(49) Murder to manslaughter—provocation that will reduce "must be such as was ordinarily calculated to excite the passions beyond control," and it is proper to give an instruction in these terms. Lewis v. Com., 93 Ky. 238, 14 R. 212. 19 S. W. 664; Crockett v. Com., 100 Ky. 382, 18 R. 835, 38 S. W. 674; Campbell v. Com., 88 Ky. 402, 10 R. 975, 11 S. W. 290; Cottrell v. Com., 13 R. 305, 17 S. W. 149.

(50) It is improper to say that the provocation that will reduce murder to manslaughter must be "considerable provocation." Lewis v. Com., 93 Ky. 238, 14 R. 212, 19 S. W. 664; Slaughter v. Com., 15 R. 230. 22 S. W. 645; or "legal provocation." Payne v. Com., 1 Met. 370; Donnellan v. Com., 7 Bush 676; or to say that "mere words, however opprobrious or insulting, are not sufficient provocation to reduce a killing from murder to manslaughter." Com. v. Hourigan, 89 Ky. 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550.

(51) "Mere words or gestures, though they may excite passion, do not constitute such provocation as will of itself extenuate a homicide, committed with a deadly weapon, and make it manslaughter." Rapp v. Com., 14 B. M. 614; and see Donnellan v. Com., 7 Bush 676; Payne v. Com., 1 Met. 370; Williams v. Com. 80 Ky. 313, 4 R. 3; Oder v. Com. 80 Ky. 32, 4 R. 18; Coffman v. Com., 10 Bush 495.

(52) Mutual rencontre. As to instructions in cases of mutual rencontre, both parties being in fault at the commencement of the difficulty, or where it is uncertain which commenced it, see Smaltz v. Com., 3 Bush 32; Berry v. Com., 10 Bush 15; Luby v. Con., 12 Bush 1; Bohannon v. Com., 8 Bush 481; Minton v. Com., 79 Ky. 461, 3 R. 321; Terrell v. Com., 13 Bush 246.

(53) Negligence, resulting in injury or death, proper instructions. Com. v. Matthews, 89 Ky. 287, 11 R. 505, 12 S. W. 333.

(54) Officer-killing of. As to instructions, where the accused is charged with killing an officer, see Mockabee v. Com., 78 Ky. 380; Fleetwood v. Com., 80 Ky. 1, 3 R. 497. The instructions given in these cases are set out in full in the opinions.

(55) Where an officer, in attempting an arrest of one who knows him to be an officer, is killed, it is murder and the jury need not be told that killing must have been malicious. Dilger v. Com., 88 Ky. 550, 11 R. 67, 11 S. W. 651; and see notes to sec. 36.

(56) Oral instructions. The Code requires that all instructions shall be written, and no departure from this rule can be tolerated: verbal instructions are not cured by subsequently reducing them to writing and handing writing to jury. Payne v. Com., 1 Met. 370; nor should the court orally state to the jury the effect of testimony. Luby v. Com., 12 Bush 1; or make any statement in presence of jury of the reasons for decisions upon questions that might prejudice the rights of the accused. Coppage v. Com., 3 Bush 532; Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340. But defendant may consent to oral instructions. Allen v. Com., 148 Ky. 327, 146 S. W. 762; Ferguson v. Com., 141 Ky. 457, 132 S. W. 1030; Harris v. Com., 141 Ky. 70, 132 S. W. 148.

(56a) In a misdemeanor case the requirement that instructions are to be given in writing may be waived; unless there has been such a waiver, the giving of unwritten instructions is ground for reversal. Adams Ex. Co. v. Com., 163 Ky. 275, 173 S. W. 764.

(56b) On a trial for felony, the instructions by consent were given orally, and were taken down by a stenographer, and were reduced to writing, and were in writing delivered to the jury before a verdict was returned. In this there was no error. Spence v. Com., 181 Ky. 206, 204 S. W. 80.

(56c) The trial court should in every felony case submit its instructions to the jury in writing; yet defendant may, by agreement, waive the Code provision by consenting that instructions be oral, and such an agreement will be binding on him. Spence v. Com., 181 Ky. 206.

(57) Presumption that instruction prejudicial. "The correct rule, we think, is that every improper instruction should be taken as prima facie prejudicial to the accused, subject of course to be shown otherwise by the evidence in the case." Barnett v. Com., 84 Ky. 449, 8 R.

# [§ 225]

448, 1 S. W. 722; Brooks v. Com., 16 R. 356, 28 S. W. 148; but in Galloway v. Com., 7 R. 165, the court held that erroneous instruction as to murder was not prejudicial when defendant was found guilty of manslaughter.

(58) Prolix instructions. In Smith v. Com., 1 Duv. 224, thirty-six instructions were given, and the court intimates that this great number of instructions would be cause of reversal; and see, to same effect, Moore v. Com., 7 Bush 191.

(59) The court should confine the instructions as closely as possible to the essential facts necessary to make out the charge or defense. Brady v. Com., 11 Bush 282; and they should be based only on such facts as must be found by the jury in order to establish the guilt of the accused or make out his defense. Coffman v. Com., 10 Bush 495.

(60) Protection and defense of family - instructions applicable. Campbell v. Com., 88 Ky. 402, 10 R. 975, 11 S. W. 290; Estep v. Com., 86 Ky. 39, 9 R. 278, 4 S. W. 820.

(61) Rape. As to instructions in prosecutions for, see Bethel v. Com., 80 Ky. 520, 4 R. 487; Heilman v. Com., 84 Ky. 457, 8 R. 451, 1 S. W. 731; Kessler v. Com., 12 Bush 18; as to degrees of offense and proper instructions as to, see Bethel v. Com., 80 Ky. 526, 4 R. 487; Fenston v. Com., 82 Ky. 549, 7 R. 55; Young v. Com., 16 R. 496, 29 S. W. 439.

(62) Woman, insane or an imbecile, proper instruction. Higgins v. Com., 94 Ky. 54, 14 R. 729, 21 S. W. 231; Beaven v. Com., 17 R. 246, 30 S. W. 968.

(63) Reasonable doubt. See notes to secs. 238, 239.

(64) Reckless use of fire-arms. See York v. Com., 82 Ky. 360, 6 R. 334; Brown v. Com., 92 S. W. 542, 28 R. 1337; Ewing v. Com., 129 Ky. 237, 111 S. W. 352; Chrystal v. Com., 9 Bush 669; Golligher v. Com., 2 Duv. 163; Sparks v. Com., 3 Bush 111; Smith v. Com., 93 Ky. 318, 14 R. 260, 20 S. W. 229; Montgomery v. Com., 26 R. 356, 81 S. W. 264.

(65) Retreat not necessary by one summoned to aid an officer. Cockrill v. Com., 95 Ky. 22, 15 R. 328, 23 S. W. 659.

(66) Rights of accused on his own premises. The proper instructions. Baker v. Com., 93 Ky. 302, 14 R. 185, 19 S. W. 075; Tingle v. Com., 11 R. 224, 11 S. W. 812; Bledsoe v. Com., 9 R. 1002, 7 S. W. 884; Smith v. Com., 16 R. 112, 26 S. W. 583; Ebersole v. Com., 95 Ky. 623, 16 R. 143, 26 S. W. 816.

(67) Right to prevent intruder from entering house. Proper instructions.

(70) Slanderous words sufficient provocation to reduce crime to manslaughter. Massie v. Com., 15 R. 562, 24 S. W. 611; Stott v. Com., 17 R. 308, 29 S. W. 141.

(71) Special finding. By the special findings the jury were instructed to find.

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury

whether the facts submitted to them were established by a preponderance of the evidence. The court says: "The instructions for special findings as presented are not authorized by the Code, and are contrary to the theory of criminal jurisprudence." Maiden v. Com., 82 Ky. 133, 6 R. 19.

# (72) Statutory offenses

An instruction in a penal case, although it pursues very closely the language of the statute, may be erroneous. It should be so explicit and have such connection with the facts of the case as to enable the jury clearly to apply the facts to the law as expressed in the instruction and meet the evil intended to be remedied. Ritte v. Com., 18 B. M. 35.

# (73) Surgical operation-death ensuing

See Coffman v. Com., 10 Bush 495; Bush v. Com., 78 Ky. 268.

# (74) Threats and assault prior to killing

As to instructions when there have been threats followed by an assault prior to the day of the homicide, see Bohannon v. Com., 8 Bush 481; Holloway v. Com., 11 Bush 344; Phillips v. Com., 2 Duv. 328; Carrico v. Com., 7 Bush 124; Parsons v. Com., 78 Ky. 102; Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 310; Reynolds v. Com., 114 Ky. 912, 24 R. 1742, 72 S. W. 277.

# (75) Threats unexecuted

As to instructions in cases in which threats were made previous to the day the homicide occurred, unaccompanied by any attempt to execute them, see Carrico v. Com., 7 Bush 124; Bohannon v. Com., 8 Bush 481; Young v. Com., 6 Bush 312; Oder v. Com., 5 Ky. 32; Parsons v. Com., 78 Ky. 102; Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340.

# (76) Threats of deceased to take life of accused

See for statement of facts authorizing an instruction presenting this view of a case as a defense. Haverly v. Com., 95 Ky. 33, 15 R. 285, 23 S. W. 664; Com. v. Barnes, 13 R. 163, 16 S. W. 457; Reynolds v. Com., 114 Ky. 912, 24 R. 1742, 72 S. W. 277.

# (77) Voluntary manslaughter-definition of

Couner v. Com., 13 Bush 714; and the court should in an instruction define offense. Com. v. Blackwell, 93 Ky. 309, 14 R. 246, 20 S. W. 199.

# (78) Wilful striking

Under an indictment for murder the accused cannot be convicted of the offense described in sec. 2, art 4, chap. 29, Gen. Stat. [now Ky. Stat., sec. 1151], and where the jury are instructed under that statute, and the verdict of conviction is found under that instruction, the accused should be discharged. Conner v. Com., 13 Bush 714, overruling Terrell v. Com., 13 Bush 246, so far as it conflicts with this opinion.

# (79) Witnesses

An instruction that "the jury are the sole judges for themselves of the weight of the testimony and credibility of the witnesses, and may attach such weight to any and all parts thereof as they may think proper, and if they believe that any witness or witnesses have wilfully sworn falsely as to any material fact, they may, if they deem proper, disregard the entire testimony of such witness or witnesses," is erroneous. Barnett v. Com., 84 Ky. 449, 8 R. 448, 1 S. W. 722; Barnard v. Com., 10 R. 143, 8 S. W. 444; and should not be given, but the court will not reverse for this error if it is not prejudicial. Forman v. Com., 86 Ky. 605, 9 R. 759, 6 S. W. 579; and it is error to direct attention to the interest of witnesses or the character of their statements. Wright v. Com., 85 Ky. 123, 8 R. 718, 2 S. W. 904.

# (80) Burden of proof-when an affirmative defense will put it on defendant

Com. v. Standard Oil Co., 129 Ky. 546, 112 S. W. 632.

# (81) Libel cases - duty of court to give the law

Walston v. Com., 106 S. W. 224, 32 R. 535.

# (82) Admonition as to effect of evidence need not be in writing

Metcalfe v. Com., 86 S. W. 534, 27 R. 704; Ochsner v. Com., 128 Ky. 761, 109 S. W. 326; Wendling v. Com., 143 Ky. 587, 137 S. W. 205; and see note 41.

# (83) Alibi-weight to be given to evidence of should not be singled out in instruction

Smith v. Com., 122 Ky. 444, 91 S. W. 1130, 29 R. 17.

# (84) Peremptory instruction should not be given when defendant's evidence cures weakness in evidence for Commonwealth

Gordon v. Com., 146 Ky. 61, 141 S. W. 1186.

# (84a) Peremptory instruction-when may be given

Where the evidence would not sustain a conviction the court may properly give a peremptory instruction to find the defendant not guilty. Saylor v. Com., 158 Ky. 768, 166 S. W. 254.

# (85) Conspiracy-instructions in

Gambrell v. Com., 130 Ky. 513, 113 S. W. 476.

# (86) Whole law of case-duty of court to give whether requested or not

Buckles v. Com., 24 R. 571, 68 S. W. 1084; Thompson v. Com., 122 Ky. 501, 91 S. W. 701; Gordon v. Com., 136 Ky. 508, 124 S. W. 806.

# (87) Motion for new trial-need of

Errors in instructions given in a criminal case cannot be considered on appeal unless included in the motion and

Title VI] Trial-Conduct of the Jury. [9 226-228

# § 226. [Jury to withdraw during argument on instructions.]

On motion of either party, the court shall cause the jury to withdraw from the courtroom, during any argument upon the law to be embodied in the instructions.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1110.

# § 227 [224] [Order of argument.]

Unless the case be submitted to the jury without argument, the defendant or his counsel shall commence, and the counsel for the Commonwealth conclude, the argument to the jury, unless the defendant's only plea be a former conviction or acquittal, when the order of argument shall be reversed.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1122.

# § 228 [225] [Arguments to alternate.]

If more than one counsel on each side argue the case they shall do so alternately.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1122.

Com., 149 Ky. 5; nor should he be allowed to make any statement in conflict with the instructions or ruling of the court. Bates v. Com., 13 R. 132, 14 S. W. 528, and it is improper for the attorney to read from books or a paper definitions of malice. Duncan v. Com., 13 R. 195, 16 S. W. 584; but when the attention of the court is called to the improper argument and the jury are admonished not to regard it the court will not reverse on this ground. Cotrell v. Com., 13 R. 305, 17 S. W. 149; Hilton v. Com., 13 R. 158, 16 S. W. 826; Handly v. Com., 15 R. 736, 24 S. W. 609: and see Cupp v. Com., 87 Ky. 35, 9 R. 877, 7 S. W. 405; Rhodes v. Com., 107 Ky. 354, 21 R 1070, 54 S. W. 170; Clark v. Com., 111 Ky. 443, 23 R. 1029, 63 S. W. 740.

A verdict will not be set aside on account of misconduct of attorney in argument where the trial is in other respects fairly conducted and it is apparent that no other verdict could have been rendered. Hourigan v. Com., 94 Ky. 520, 15 R. 265, 23 S. W. 355; Ray v. Com., 19 R. 1217, 43 S. W. 221: nor will Court of Appeals consider objection on account of improper argument unless an objection is taken to it at the time it is being made. O'Brien v. Com., 89 Ky. 354, 11 R 534, 12 S. W. 471.

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury.

# § 229 [227] [Defendant to remain in custody during felony trial.]

During the trial of an indictment for felony, the defendant shall be committed to and remain in the custody of the proper officer. (But see sec. 183.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1027.

# § 230 [228] [Proceedings if offense committed out of jurisdiction.]

If, during the trial, it shall appear that the offense was committed out of the jurisdiction of the court, but within the jurisdiction of some other court of this State, the court shall stop the trial, discharge the jury, and take the proceedings in the case directed in sections 16ó and 167.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 989.

# § 231 [220] [Proceedings if offense committed out of State.]

If it appear that the offense was committed out of the State, the trial shall be stopped, and the defendant shall be either discharged or ordered to be retained in custody for a reasonable time, until the counsel for the Commonwealth shall have an opportunity to inform the chief executive officer of the State in which the offense was committed of the facts, and for said officer to require the delivery of the offender.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 989.

# § 232 [230] [Proceedings if higher grade of offense proven.]

If, during the trial, the court shall be of the opinion that the facts proved constitute an offense of a higher nature than that charged in the indictment, it may direct the jury to be discharged, and all proceedings to be suspended, until the case can be resubmitted to a grand jury, and may order the defendant to be committed, or admit him to bail, to answer any new indictment which may be found against him for the highest offense. If an indictment be not found for the higher offense before the next grand jury are discharged, the court must proceed to try the defendant on the original indictment.*

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 990.

*As to constitutionality of right given to discharge jury and retry defendant, see notes to section 176, 243, 252.-Ed.

# (6) Employed counsel may conclude argument for Commonwealth.

Catron v Com., 140 Kv. 61, 130 S. W. 951.

# (7) Argument must alternate.

Kammerer v. Com., 137 Ky. 315, 125 S. W. 723.

# § 229. Custody of accused during felony trial.

See notes to sec. 183.

# § 230. (1) Evidence of venue of crime

should be permitted even after Commonwealth has closed its case for the purpose of showing what court had jurisdiction. Com. v. Patterson, 10 R. 167, 8 S. W. 694; and see Hays v. Com., 12 R. 611, 14 S. W. S33; and notes to sec. 18.

(Misdemeanors - this section applies to. Gearhart v. Com., 129 Ky. 458, 112 S. W. 572.
