# Title VI

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury. [§ 233-235]

# § 233 [231] [Indictment may be quashed-resubmission.]

If, during the trial, the court be of opinion that the facts charged in the indictment do not constitute an offense punishable by law, it shall order the jury to be discharged and the indictment to be quashed, and thereupon may take the proceedings directed in section 170.*

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1121.

*As to right to retry defendant after discharge of jury, see notes to sections 176, 243 and 252.-Ed.

# § 234 [232] [Joint defendants, testimony of, for each other.*]

*This section was repealed by an Act of March 23, 1894, c. 112, p. 342. It formerly read as follows:

§ 234 [232]. Joint defendants, testimony of for each other. If two or more persons be jointly indicted for the same offense, each shall be a competent witness for the others, unless the indictment charge a conspiracy between them.

# § 235 [233] [Questions of law to be decided by court.]

All questions of law arising during the trial shall be decided by the court, and the jury.

# § 233. (1) Discharge of jury, when different rule prevailed.

81 Ky. 465. 5 R. 200; 13 Bush 204; 13 Bush 261; Jeffries v. Com., 84 Ky. 237, 8 R. 276, 1 S. W. 142.

(2) An indictment showed on its face that it was a continuation of a former prosecution and there was no contrary evidence. The question should not have been submitted to the jury by an instruction. Ralee v. Com., 153 Ky. 558, 156 S. W. 147.

# § 234. (1) Charge of conspiracy to commit offense with which two or more persons are charged only aggravates offense and does not render indictment open to objection that two offenses are stated.

Salisbury v. Com., 79 Ky. 425, 3 R. 21.

Conspiracy. What was said or done by one of two conspirators after the commission of the offense not in the presence of or approved by the other is not competent evidence against him. Shelby v. Com., 91 Ky. 563, 13 R. 178, 16 S. W. 461; but declarations or acts before object of conspiracy is accomplished are competent. Miller v. Com. 78 Ky. 15; see full cases in Howard v. Com., 110 Ky. 356, 22 R. 1845. 61 S. W. 756; Powers v. Com., 110 Ky. 386, 22 R. 1807, 63 S. W. 976.

# (5) The act of May 1, 1886, allowing defendants in criminal cases to testify does not change the rule determining the competency of defendants jointly indicted as witnesses nor give the court the right to arbitrarily exclude them.

Jeffries v. Com., 84 Ky. 237, 8 R. 276, 1 S. W. 442.

# § 235. Jury to obey instructions.

The court has the right to instruct the jury as to the law of the case, and it is their duty to regard such instructions as containing the law; but if they disregard them in a criminal case and acquit the accused, the judge cannot for that reason grant a new trial. Com. v. Van Tuyl, 1 Met. 1.

# § 236-238] Trial-Conduct of the Jury. [Title VI

shall be bound to take the decisions of the court on points of law, as the law of the case.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1026.

# § 236 [284] [View of place offense committed.]

When, in the opinion of the court, it is necessary that the jury should view the place in which the offense is charged to have been committed, or in which any other material fact occurred, it may order the jury to be conducted in a body, in the custody of proper officers [and accompanied by the judge, prisoner and counsel for each side] to the place, which must be shown to them by the judge, or by a person appointed by the court for that purpose. The officers must be sworn to suffer no person to speak to, or communicate with, the jury on any subject connected with the trial, nor do so themselves, but shall merely show the place to be viewed, and return them into court without unnecessary delay, or at a specified time. (Words in brackets inserted by c1ct of March 2, 1882, c. 346, p. 33.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1109.

# § 237 [285] [Joint defendants, in felony, entitled to separate trial.]

If two or more defendants be jointly indicted for a felony any defendant is entitled to a separate trial.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1022, 1040.

# § 238 [288] [Reasonable doubt entitles defendant to acquittal.]

If there be a reasonable doubt of the defendant being proven to be guilty, he is entitled to an acquittal.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim: Law, secs. 994, 1111, 1112.

# § 236. (1) View of place by jury.

Pointing out to jury place where offense was committed is the giving of evidence and should not be done in the absence of accused. Rutherford v. Com.. 78 Ky. 639, 1 R. 410; whether jury should be sent to view premises is a matter of discretion in trial court. Roberts v. Com., 94 Ky. 499. 1 R. 410: and it is not material at whose suggestion jury are permitted to view place. Austin v. Com., 4 R. 29. In City of Newport v. Com., 108 Ky. 150, 21 R. 1591, 55 S. W. 914, the failure of judge to accompany jury was held not to be a reversible error, nor is it error to permit accused without objection to point out certain objects at request of juror. Underwood v. Com., 119 Kv. 384, 27 R. 8, 84 S. W. 310.

# (2) State may claim severance.

The State may claim a severance, which the court in its discretion may grant. Jenkins v. Com., 167 Ky. 544, 180 S. W. 961.

# § 238. (1) Reasonable doubt-instructions.

"A rational doubt is one growing out of the evidence, and not a mere chimera existing in the juror's mind." Graham v. Com., 16 B. M. 587.

# (2) In giving an instruction as to reasonable doubt,

the better practice is to follow the language of the Criminal Code, as expressed in this section. Mickey v. Com., 9 Bush 593; Ward v. Com.. 14 Bush 233; Ireland v. Com., 22 R. 478, 57 S. W. 616; Brady v. Com., 11 Bush 282; Payne v. Com., I Met. 370; Tetterton v. Com., 89 S. W. 8. 28 R. 146; Howell v. Com. 104 S. W. 685, 31 R. 983; Gatliff v. Com.. 107 S. W. 739, 32 R. 1063; Mearns v. Com., 164 Ky. 213, 175 S. W. 355; Min- niard v. Com.. 158 Ky. 210, 164 S. W. 770.

Title VI] Trial-Conduct of the Jury. [ 239-240

# § 239 [287] [Conviction to be of lower, if doubt as to degree.]

If there be a reasonable doubt of the degree of the offense which the defendant has committed, he shall only be convicted of the lower degree. (Degrees of offenses defined, sec. 263.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1111, 1119.

# § 240 [238] [Confession out of court must be corroborated.]

A confession of a defendant, unless made in open court, will not warrant a conviction, unless accompanied with other proof that such an offense was committed.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1077, 1081, 1082.

$04; Clary v. Com., 163 Ky. 48, 173 S. W. 171; Laughlin v. Com., 18 R. 640, 37 S. W. 590; but confessions procured by deception are competent. Wiggington v. Com., 92 Ky. 282, 13 R. 641, 17 S. W. 634.

(3) This section applies in all criminal prosecutions. Sowder v. Com., 8 Bush 432.

(4) Burden of proof --when on defendant. Com. v. Standard Oil Co., 129 Ky. 744, 112 S. W. 633.

# §239. (1) Doubt as to degree of offense-instructions.

Whenever there is evidence introduced which might be calculated to raise a reasonable doubt of the guilt of the accused, the jury should be instructed in the language of this section. Williams v. Com., 80 Kv. 313, 4 R. 3: Arnold v. Com., 24 R. 1921, 72 S. W. 753; Biggs v. Com., 164 Ky. 223, 175 S. W. 319; McCandless v. Com., 170 Ky. 301, 185 S. W. 1100.

The giving of an instruction under sec. 23s will not dispense with the giving of an instruction under this section. Breeden v. Com., 151 Ky. 217, 151 S. W. 407.

# §240. (1) Confessions.

Proof of confessions of a party in the presence of the witness only, or of him and the adverse party, although certainly competent testimony, ought to be weighed with caution. It is the most dangerous species of testimony held competent by the law. Becker v. Crow, 7 Bush 198.

(2) It is a general rule that confessions, which are induced by hopes or fears raised by the promise or threats of the prosecutor or of any person having authority over the prisoner at the time, are not considered voluntary, but as having been made under mental duress, and therefore not competent. Rector v. Com., 80 Ky. 468, 4 R. 323. See Green v. Com., 26 R. 1221, 83 S. W. 638; Rutherford v. Com., 2 Met. 387; Young v. Com., 8 Bush 366; Butler v. Com., 2 Duv. 435; Hudson v. Com., 2 Duv. 531; Taylor v. Com., 19 R. 836, 42 S. W. 1125;

# §241

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury.

# § 241 [239] [Testimony of accomplice must be corroborated.]

A conviction can not be had upon the testimony of an accomplice, unless corroborated by other evidence tending to connect the defendant with the commission of the offense; and the corroboration is not sufficient if it merely shows that the offense was committed, and the circumstances thereof.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1077, 1081, 1082, 1083.

to a confession. Cargill v. Com., 93 Ky. 578, 14 R. 517, 20 S. W. 728.

1. Statements made by another in presence of accused but not adopted by him are not competent. Merriweather v. Com., 26 R. 793, 82 S. W. 592.
2. Although an original confession may be incompetent—yet subsequent confessions of like character may be competent. Green v. Com., 26 R. 1221, 83 S. W. 638.
3. Where a confession made out of court is accompanied by other proof independent of the confession that the crime has been committed it is not proper to instruct as provided in this section, but where it is doubtful if the crime has been committed the jury should be instructed in the language of this section. Green v. Com., 26 R. 1221, 83 S. W. 638; Patterson v. Com., 86 Ky. 313, 9 R. 481, 5 S. W. 387, 765; Dugan v. Com., 102 Ky. 241, 19 R. 1273, 43 S. W. 418; Gilbert v. Com., 111 Ky. 793, 23 R. 1094, 64 S. W. 846; Polson v. Com., 108 S. W. 844, 32 R. 1398; Frazier v. Com., 124 S. W. 797; Higgins v. Com., 142 Ky. 647, 134 S. W. 1135.
4. Statements of accused on former trial are competent against him on second trial, although he does not then testify. Bess v. Com., 26 R. 839, 82 S. W. 576; Porter v. Com., 22 R. 1657, 61 S. W. 16.
5. Confession alone unsupported by other evidence will not warrant a conviction. Moseby v. Com., 113 S. W. 850; Com. v. Burgess, 91 S. W. 266, 28 R. 1128.
6. Confession of ignorant boy not admissible against him. Owsley v. Com., 125 Ky. 384, 101 S. W. 366, 31 R. 5.
7. On a trial for felony there was practically but the one issue of identity; it was prejudicial error to submit to the jury in an instruction whether certain statements of appellant amounted to a confession. Conners v. Com., 152 Ky. 57, 153 S. W. 16.
8. It is improper for the court to charge the jury as to confessions, where admissions by accused did not amount to a confession. Bates v. Com., 164 Ky. 1, 174 S. W. 765.
9. Certain evidence was not such proof that the offense was committed as to authorize a conviction upon the voluntary confession of the defendant. Taylor v. Com., 163 Ky. 498, 172 S. W. 9957.
10. In a trial for unlawfully detaining a woman, a witness stated that he heard the accused say he went into the room for the purpose of getting his grandmother's money. This was not a confession; the defendant was not entitled to an instruction under this section. Black v. Com., 154 Ky. 144, 156 S. W. 1043.
11. A certain instruction as to corroboration of a confession was approved. Smith v. Com., 153 Ky. 385, 155 S. W. 1125.
12. If the proof, other than the confession, leaves it doubtful, whether the crime has been committed, an instruction should be given under this section. Ratliff v. Com., 182 Ky. 246; Clary v. Com., 163 Ky. 48, 173 S. W. 171.
13. If there be evidence proving beyond reasonable doubt the corpus delicti independent of the confession of the defendant it is unnecessary to give an instruction that an alleged confession will not warrant a defense unless accompanied by other proof that such a crime was committed. Lee v. Com., 155 Ky. 62, 159 S. W. 648; Clary v. Com., 163 Ky. 48, 173 S. W. 171; Wright v. Com., 155 Ky. 750, 160 S. W. 476; Ratliff v. Com., 182 Ky. 246.
14. A confession made out of court will warrant a conviction if there be other proof that such an offense has been committed by someone. Frierson v. Com., 175 Ky. 684, 194 S. W. 914.
15. A certain statement was not a confession. Allen v. Com., 176 Ky. 475, 196 S. W. 160.

# 241. (1 Accomplice evidence.

An accomplice is one of several equally concerned in the commission of a felony. A conviction can not be had on the testimony of an accomplice unless corroborated by other evidence.

Title VI] Trial--Conduct of the Jury. [§ 242
# § 242 [240] [Instructions to acquit if corroboration not sufficient.]

In all cases where, by law, two witnesses, or one witness with corroborating circumstances, are requisite, to warrant a conviction, if the requisition be not fulfilled, the court shall instruct the jury to render a verdict of acquittal, by which instruction they are bound.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec, 1084

Testimony of an accomplice unless corroborated by other evidence, and the corroboration must extend to every fact necessary to establish the fact that the offense charged was committed, and that the prisoner was the perpetrator. Miller v. Com., 78 Ky. 15; see full discussion of questions relating to accomplices in Howard v. Com., 110 Ky. 356, 22 R. 1845. 61 S. W. 756; Howard v. Com., 114 Ky. 372, 24 R. 1225, 70 S. W. 1055; Powers v. Com., 110 Ky. 386, 22 R. 1807, 61 S. W. 735; 63 S. W. 976; Powers v. Com., 114 Ky. 237, 24 R. 1007, 70 S. W. 644, 1050; 71 S. W. 491.

1. The provisions of this section as to accomplices apply to misdemeanors as well as felonies. Com. v. Barton, 153 Ky. 465, 156 S. W. 113.
2. If two or more accomplices are produced as witnesses they are not deemed to corroborate each other. The wife of an accomplice who has testified against the accused may be introduced as a witness to corroborate the testimony of her husband. Blackburn v. Com., 12 Bush 181; and see Smith v. Com., 13 R. 369, 17 S. W. 182; Porter v. Com., 22 R. 1657, 61 S. W. 16.
3. Proof of a confession by the accused is a sufficient corroboration of the evidence of an accomplice. Patterson v. Com., 99 Ky. 610, 9 R. 481, 5 S. W. 387, 765.
4. "The uncorroborated testimony of an accomplice is wholly insufficient to convict the accused of a crime, or of any of its constituent elements, or to render admissible any confessions or admissions of the parties which depend upon and must be preceded by evidence of a conspiracy unless other evidence besides that of the accomplice is produced tending to prove a conspiracy, and connect the accused therewith, and evidence merely showing that the offense was committed and the circumstances thereof is as insufficient for that purpose as it would be to connect the accused with the commission of the offense itself." Bowling v. Com., 79 Ky. 604, 3 R. 610; Frazier v. Com., 25 R. 461, 76 S. W. 28; Simpson v. Com., 103 S. W. 332, 31 R. 769; Lane v. Com., 134 Ky. 519, 121 S. W. 486; Frazier v. Com., 124 S. W. 797.
5. Under an indictment for murder caused by an abortion a conviction may be had on the statements of the deceased alone, as she is not an accomplice. Peoples v. Com., 87 Ky. 487, 10 R. 517, 10 S. W. 642.
6. Under indictment for incest, a conviction may be had alone upon testimony of woman. Whitaker v. Com., 16 R. 173, 27 S. W. 83; and in a prosecution for gaming a conviction may be had upon the evidence of one engaged in game. Com. v. Bossie, 100 Ky. 151, 18 R. 624, 37 S. W. 844.
7. One jointly indicted with others will not be permitted to prove the good character of persons indicted with him. Omer v. Com., 95 Ky. 353, 15 R. 694, 25 S. W. 594.
8. The mere fact that a person is charged with crime in connection with others does not make him an accomplice; in order to make him an accomplice it is necessary that his criminal participation in the crime charged should be shown by the evidence. Sizemore v. Com., 10 R. 1. 6 S. W. 123; Boyd v. Com., 141 Ky. 247, 134 S. W. 423; Levering v. Com., 132 Ky. 666, 117 S. W. 255.
9. The facts must determine whether the defendant was an accomplice, and in order to make him such, it is necessary that his criminal participation in the crime charged is shown by evidence. Anderson v. Com., 181 Ky. 311, citing Levering v. Com., 132 Ky. 666; Richardson v. Com., 166 Ky. 507; Nicholl v. Com., 169 Ky. 491.
10. If there is not some evidence conducing to show that a witness is an accomplice it is not proper to submit the question as to whether he is an accomplice, to the jury, nor to give the instructions provided for in this section. Anderson v. Com., 181 Ky. 311; Elmendorf v. Com., 171 Ky. 410.
11. Testimony of accomplices jointly indicted are competent, although indictment against them is not dismissed. Powers v. Com., 114 Ky. 237, 24 R. 1007, 70 S. W. 644.

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury.

# § 243 [241] [Attorney may dismiss indictment-effect of.]

The attorney of the Commonwealth, with permission of the court, may, at any time before the case is finally submitted to the jury, dismiss the indictment as to all or a part of the defendants, and such dismissal shall not bar a future prosecution for the same offense.* (See further as to dismissal of indictment, secs. 159, 170, 178.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim, Law, secs. 987, 997, 1008

# (9a) Who are not accomplices.

One who procured whisky for another in violation of the Act of 1912, chapter 146, was not an accomplice of the man who sold it to him. Raum v. Bd. of Council, Danville, 155 Ky. 690, 160 S. W. 255.

(Ob) In this case the theory of the Commonwealth and the theory of the defense as to the facts in the case were in conflict with each other, but under neither theory could the accused and a certain witness both be guilty of the crime. They were not accomplices. Oakley v. Com., 158 Ky. 474, 165 S. W. 691.

(Dc) One who did not participate in a homicide, either as principal, aider, abettor, or accessory, was not an "accomplice" within the meaning of this section. Johnston v. Com., 170 Ky. 766, 180 S. W. 655.

(9d) One is not an accomplice in the absence of evidence that he took part in the altercation between the parties, leading up to the homicide, but that he acted as peacemaker between them. Nicoll v. Com., 169 Ky. 491, 184 S. W. 386.

(9e) Money was offered a person to induce him to commit a crime. He refused the money, and took no part in the commission of the crime. He was not an accomplice. Allen v. Com., 170 Ky. 475, 196 S. W. 160.

# (10) Instructions.

"Where there are no corroborating circumstances it is the duty of the court to instruct the jury to acquit. Before the jury can consider the evidence of an accomplice as a factor in the problem of guilt or innocence, they must first determine that the other evidence heard proves the existence of the corroborating facts. If the evidence claimed to be corroborative does not tend, when its truth is admitted, to this end, it is the duty of the court to exclude it and to direct an acquittal." Craft v. Com., 80 Ky. 349, 4 R. 182.

(11) The jury should be instructed in the language of this section. Craft v. Com., 80 Ky. 340, 4 R. 182; Patterson v: Com., 86 Ky. 313, 9 R. 481, 5 S. W. 387; Taylor v. Com., 10 R. 169, 8 S. W. 461.

(12) The indictment may be dismissed at any time before trial begins, and it...

Title VI] Trial-Conduct of the Jury. [§ 244

123. Dismissal of indictment—written statement. Before the court shall permit any Commonwealth's or county attorney to dismiss any indictment or enter a nolle prosequi in any case, such attorney shall file a statement, in writing, setting forth the reasons for such dismissal or such failure to prosecute, which statement shall be signed by the Commonwealth's or county attorney, as the case may be, and spread upon the order book of the court, and an order entered in accordance therewith. (Ky. Stat., sec. 123.)

*See notes to this section and to sections 176, 232, 233, 252.—Ed.

# § 244 [242, 245] [Cases in which jury must be kept together.]

On the trial of offenses which are or may be punished capitally the jurors, after they are accepted, shall not be permitted to separate, but shall be kept together, in charge of the proper officers. On the trial of other felonies the jurors, before the case is submitted to them, may be permitted to separate, in the discretion of the court, but after the case is submitted they shall be kept together in charge of an officer. On the trial of misdemeanors the jurors may be permitted to separate, at any time until finally discharged, or the court may order them to be kept together. (See sec. 251 as to discharge of jury.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1053.

It is not necessary that Commonwealth's attorney should file a written statement when purpose is to resubmit case to grand jury. Dilger v. Com., 88 Ky. 550, 11 R. 67, 11 S. W. 651.

Where the accused was granted on his motion a new trial the indictment under which he was tried may be dismissed and a new indictment found. Wells v. Com., 9 R. 658, 6 S. W. 150.

See further notes to sec. 176 as to when dismissal of indictment or discharge of jury operates as an acquittal.

See further as to dismissal of indictment Ky. Stat., sec. 123; Husbands v. Com., 143 Ky. 290, 136 S. W. 632; Com. v. Smith, 140 Ky. 580, 131 S. W. 391; Com. v. Cundiff, 149 Ky. 37.

Discretion of court. The circuit judge may in his discretion sustain or overrule a motion by the Commonwealth's attorney to dismiss an indictment for a felony. Com. v. Davis, 169 Ky. 650, 184 S. W. 1121.

# 244.

1. Keeping jury together—an alleged error in not keeping the jury together being objected to for the first time in the motion for a new trial cannot be considered by the Court of Appeals. Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340.
2. "It is the duty of the sheriff to comply with the requirements of the Code, both in keeping the jury together and in not suffering any person to speak to or communicate with them on any subject connected with the trial. A substantial performance of this duty certainly required the jury to be so kept as in fact to be present with each other, whether in the same or adjoining and connected apartments, so situated as to admit of free and unrestricted communication between them; and the officer should himself have remained in such convenient situation as to observe and prevent any attempt to tamper with the jury or any irregularity on their part; but it was not, in our opinion, absolutely required that the entire jury should have been kept within the same room, without regard to comfort or practical convenience, or that the sheriff should have lodged within the same apartment with them." Com. v. Shields, 2 Bush 81.
3. A verdict will not be set aside in the absence of any evidence of undue influence or interference by others merely because two of the jury may have deliberated or conversed apart from the others in another room after submission of case. Blyew v. Com., 91 Ky. 200, 12 R. 742, 15 S. W. 356; and see Holly v. Com., 18 R. 441, 36 S. W. 532.
4. Lodging of jury at a hotel on second floor, in different rooms along the hall, in which sheriff locked them at night, there being no pretense that they were tampered with, is a sufficient com-

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury

# § 245 [242] [Officers in charge of jury to be sworn.]

When the jury is kept together in charge of officers, the officers must be sworn to keep the jurors together, and to suffer no person to speak to, or communicate with, them on any subject connected with the trial, and not to do so themselves. Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 105s.

# § 246 [24s] [Admonition of court to jury.]

The jury, whether permitted to separate or kept in charge of officers, must be admonished by the court that it is their duty not to permit any one to speak to, or communicate with, them on any subject connected with the trial, and that all attempts to do so should be immediately reported by them to the court, and that they should not converse among themselves on any subject connected with the trial, nor form, nor express, any opinion thereon, until the cause is submitted to them.

Compliance with this section. Minor v. Com., 5 R. 176.

# (4a)

Certain facts did not constitute a separation within the meaning of this section. Deacon v. Com., 162 Ky. 188, 172 S. W. 121; Johnson v. Com., 179 Ky. 40, 200 S. W. 35.

# (4b)

The fact that a juror had at one time expressed sentiments of hostility toward the accused before his selection as a juror was not sufficient to justify a new trial. It was no misconduct on the part of the officer in charge of the jury to permit a son of a juror to talk to his father in the presence of the officer and the jury about matters having no connection whatever with the case. It was no misconduct on the part of the officer to permit the jury to go to a moving picture show. Mansficld v. Com., 163 Ky. 488, 174 S. W. 16.

# (5)

Objection to separation of jury, made for first time in motion for a new trial, cannot be considered; objection should have been made at the time. Wilkerson v. Com., 88 Ky. 29, 10 R. 656, 9 S. W. 836; Vinegar v. Com., 104 Ky. 106, 20 R. 412, 46 S. W. 510; and the accused may waive his right to object that jury were not kept together. Wade v. Com., 106 Kv. 321, 20 R. 1885, 50 S. W. 271; Heck v. Com., 163 Kv. 518, 174 S. W. 19.

# (6)

Where one of the jurors was allowed to go in company with a deputy sheriff to his home it was held reversible error in French v. Com., 100 Ky. 63, 18 R. 574. 37 S. W. 269; and see Vinegar v. Com., 104 Kv. 106, 20 R. 412, 46 S. W. 510; and so where eight of the jury separated a short time from the other four. Thacker v. Com., 23 R. 745, 63 S. W. 737.

# (6a)

A conviction of manslaughter under an indictment for murder was reversed, because the sheriff improperly permitted the jury having charge of the case to separate. Campbell v. Com., 162 Ky. 106, 172 S. W. 110.

# (7)

Affidavits of jurors are competent to show that there was no misconduct on their part or separation. Howard v. Com., 24 R. 612, 69 S. W. 721.

# (7a)

The fact that jurors in a capital case separated after they were accepted cannot be shown by the evidence of jurors. Beard v. Com., 149 Ky. 632, 149 S. W. 989.

# (8)

Jurors who have qualified but have not been accepted should be kept together in felony cases although failure to do this is not reversible error. Bowman v. Com., 146 Ky. 486, 143 S. W. 47.

# (8a)

On the trial of an offense not capital it is within the discretion of the trial court to permit the jury to separate during the trial. Chaney v. Com., 149 Ky. 464, 149 S. W. 923.

# § 245. Officer to be sworn

A failure to administer to the officer having charge of the jury the oath required by this section will be good ground for setting aside the verdict and granting a new trial. But, after being once sworn as required, it is not necessary to administer the oath again at each adjournment or recess of the court. Com. v. Shields, 2 Bush S1.

# § 246. Remark to jury by an outsider

that "defendant ought to be hung" was highly improper and grounds for a new trial, unless it appeared that the rights of accused were not prejudiced. Hilton v. Com., 13 R. 158, 16 S. W. 826; and see Campbell v. Bannister, 79 Ky. 205, 2 R. 72.

Title VI] Trial-Conduct of the Jury. [§ 247-250

# § 247 [244, 245] [Room for jury—board and lodging-how expense paid.]

A suitable room must be provided for the use of the jury on their retirement for deliberation, with suitable furniture, fuel, lights and stationery; and suitable food and lodging must be provided, by the sheriff, for the jurors while they are kept together, and the expense thereof paid by the trustee of the jury fund and deducted from the compensation of the jurors.

# § 248 [246] [Jury to take papers and other things received as evidence.]

Upon retiring for deliberation, the jury may take with them all papers and other things which have been received as evidence in the cause.

# § 249 [247] [Information as to law, or evidence after case submitted.]

After the jury retires for deliberation, if there be a disagreement between them as to any part of the evidence, or if they desire to be informed on a point of law, they must require the officer to conduct them into court. Upon their being brought into court, the information required must be given in the presence of, or after notice to, the counsel of the parties. (See practice in civil cases, Civil Code, sec. 321, and notes.)

# § 250. [Sickness of juror before jury completed.]

If, after a juror has been accepted, but before the jury has been completed, one of the accepted jurors becomes so sick as to prevent the continuance of his duty the court may excuse such juror, and complete the formation of the jury as if he had not been originally accepted.

# Cross reference:

See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1039.

# § 248.. (l) Taking papers to room.

It is proper for jury in retiring to deliberate to take the indictment, and where the accused failed at the time to object he can not afterward complain that former verdict was allowed to remain written on indictment. Cargill v. Com., 93 Ky. 578, 14 R. 517. 20 S. W. 782; and see Herrold v. Com., 10 R. 71, 8 S. W. 194; Vinegar v. Com., 104 Ky. 106, 20 R. 412, 46 S. W. 510.

# (2)

The court may refuse to permit jury to take to their room depositions taken for the accused. Baker v. Com., 13 R. 571, 17 S. W. 625.

# (3)

Papers introduced as evidence may be taken to jury room. Taylor v. Com., 92 S. W. 292, 28 R. 1348.

# (4)

Photographs. Under this section the jury have the right to take with them to the jury room photographs of the wounds of the decedent. McCandless v. Com., 170 Ky. 301, 185 S. W. 1100.

# (5)

Map. It is not improper to permit a jury, in a criminal case, to take with them, when they retire for deliberation, a map, which has been properly admitted as evidence upon the trial. Carney alias Kerney v. Com., 181 Ky. 444.

# § 249. Additional instructions after jury retires may be given at their instance.

Pearce v. Com., 19 R. 782, 42 S. W. 107; and see Wilhelm v. Com., 16 R. 128, 28 S. W. 783, and note 4, sec. 225.

# Trial-Conduct of the Jury

# § 251 [248] [Discharge of jury-sickness of juror.]

If, after retirement, one of the jurors becomes so sick as to prevent the continuance of his duty, or other accident or cause occur, preventing them being kept together, or if, after being kept together such a length of time as the court deems proper, they do not agree in a verdict, and it satisfactorily appear that there is no probability they can agree, the court may discharge the jury; or if the sickness of a juror be temporary, the court may permit him to separate from the other jurors, and may place him in charge of an officer, or not, in its discretion.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1004, 1039.

# § 252 [240] [Jury discharged, cause to be tried again.]

In all cases if a jury be discharged, either in the progress of a trial or after the cause is submitted to them, the cause may be again tried at the same or another term of the court.*

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 10o4, 100s.

*See notes to sections 176, 232, 233, and 243.—Ed.

# § 253 [250] [Court always open while jury deliberating.]

While the jury are absent, the court may adjourn from time to time as to other business, but it shall be deemed open for every purpose connected with the cause submitted to the jury, until a verdict is rendered or the jury discharged.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1056.

# § 254 [251] [Final adjournment discharges jury.]

A final adjournment of court discharges a jury.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1056.

# § 251. (1) Discharge of jury.

It was held that sec. 248 of Code of 1854 (same as this section) did not restrict the power of the court to discharge a jury in case of necessity to the causes enumerated in that section; but that section was only intended as an adoption of the legal rule that a case of actual necessity must exist before a jury can be discharged; and that sec. 249 (same as sec. 252) was intended to apply to such cases as are mentioned in sec. 248 (251), and has direct reference to it. O'Brian v. Com., 9 Bush 333.

# (2) Discharge of jury because of failure to agree in the absence of accused does not bar another trial.

Yarbrough v. Com., 89 Ky. 15l, 11 R. 35l, 12 S. W. 143.

# (3) See further, notes 7-1l to sec, 176.

as to when discharge of jury operates as an acquittal: and see note l. sec. 243, and Sacra v. Com., 96 S. W. 858, 29 R. 1012.

# (4) Separation of jury.

Temporary separation of a juror on account of accident or sickness is allowable when made by order of court in the presence of the accused. French v. Com., 100 Ky. 63, 18 R. 574, 37 S. W. 269.

# (5) Matter of discharging jury for failure to agree is in discretion of trial court.

Chaplin v. Com., 142 Ky. 782. 135 S. W. 298.

# § 252. Unconstitutional.

See note I to sec. 243.

# § 255 [252] [Verdict—how rendered.]

When a jury have agreed upon their verdict they must be conducted into court by the officer having them in charge, their names called by the clerk, and if they all appear, their foreman must declare their verdict. (Verdict as to part of defendants, sec. 266; polling jury, sec. 267.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1123.

# § 255.

(1) Verdict-how rendered and received. Verdict returned on Sunday is valid. Meece v. Com., 78 Ky. 586, 1 R. 337; Bales v. Con., 11 R. 297, 11 S. W. 470; and when the term ends on Saturday it may be extended over Sunday and verdict be received on Sunday. Franklin v. Com., 105 Ky. 237, 20 R. 1137, 48 S. W. 986.

(2) The prisoner must be present when verdict is rendered. Temple v. Com., 14 Bush 769; and it may be read by the clerk in the presence of the jury. Hason v. Com., 10 R. 1054, 11 S. W. 286.

(3) After a verdict has been announced and while jury is being polled it is too late to correct an erroneous instruction and resubmit case to jury. Roberts v. Com., 90 Ky. 654, 12 R. 681, 14 S. W. 832.

(4) Trial judge may in presence of jury alter their verdict in form, but not in substance, and when correction is made it should be read to jury and inquiry made if it is their verdict. Blair v. Com., 93 Ky. 493, 14 R. 495, 20 S. W. 779.

# Trial-Verdict.

# § 256 [258] [General or special verdict.]

The jury may render either a general or a special verdict.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1123.

# § 257 [254] [General verdict defined.]

1. [Upon plea of guilty.] Upon a plea of guilty is "guilty," and affixing the punishment in cases in which the jury is required to determine the degree of punishment. (Punishment by labor, Ky. Stat., sec. 1377.)
2. [Upon plea of not guilty.] Upon a plea of "not guilty," is "guilty," or "not guilty"; and if "guilty," fixing the offense, and the degree of the offense and the punishment, in cases in which the jury is required to fix the degree of punishment.
3. [Upon plea of former conviction or acquittal.] Upon a plea of former acquittal or conviction, is "for the Commonwealth," or "for the defendant," and, if for the Commonwealth, fixing the offense and degree of the offense, and the punishment in cases in which the jury is required to determine the degree of punishment. (Pleas defined, sec. 172.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1125.

# § 258 [254] [Penalty—when jury to fix.]

In verdicts of "guilty" or "for the Commonwealth," the jury shall fix the degree of punishment to be inflicted, unless the same be fixed by law.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1126.

Com., 106 Ky. 894, 21 R. 520, 51 S. W. 814. This was a sufficient verdict. The words "without a pardon" are mere surplusage. Winburn v. Com., 181 Ky. 183, 203 S. W. 1073.

(7) The failure of member of jury who signs verdict to style himself foreman is immaterial. Thomas v. Com., 12 R. S03, 15 S. W. 861.

(8) Special judge may be agreed upon to receive the verdict. Rainey v. Com., 19 R. 390, 40 S. W. 682.

# § 256. Special verdict. See note to sec. 259.

# § 257.

(1) Verdict is sufficient which reads "we, the jury, find the defendant guilty and fix his punishment at five years in the penitentiary"; the failure to name the offense is not prejudicial. Hays v. Com., 12 R. 611, 14 S. W. 833; and verdict finding defendant "guilty as charged in the indictment" is sufficient when indictment only charges one offense. Patterson v. Com., 86 Ky. 313, 9 R. 481. 5 S. W. 387, 765; and see Barton v. Com., 17 R. 580, 32 S. W. 171. 396; Gillum v. Com., 121 S. W. 445; Williams v. Com., 140 Ky. 34, 130 S. W. 807.

(Verdict on trial for murder. On the trial of an indictment for murder, the verdict did not fix the offense nor the degree of the offense, but fixed the punishment at imprisonment for life, and added the words "without a pardon." This was a sufficient verdict. The words "without a pardon" are mere surplusage. Winburn v. Com., 181 Ky. 183, 203 S. W. 1073.)

(4) Waiver of formal defects in verdict. When a defendant allows a jury to be discharged without objection or without motion to have them correct or extend their verdict, he will be deemed to have waived formal defects in the verdict. Blair v. Com., 181 Ky. 218, 204 S. W. 67.

258. (1) Construction of section. This section means that if the law fixes the punishment, leaving no room for discretion.

# Title VI] Trial-Verdict

# [§ 259-260]

# § 1136. Jury to fix punishment-confincment in peniteniiary or county jail.

The jury by whom an offender is tried shall fix, by their verdict, the punishment to be inflicted within the periods and amount prescribed by law: persons sentenced to punishment by confinement in the penitentiary shall be kept at hard labor. In cases where the punishment is a fine or imprisonment in the county jail, or both, the imprisonment shall be by close confinement in the jail of the county where the trial was held, unless otherwise provided; and the prisoner shall also be confined in the jail until the costs are paid, unless otherwise provided. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1136.)

# § 1377. Discretion of jury-punishment of male person.

When the punishment for a crime is a fine, or imprisonment in the county jail, or both, the jury may, in their discretion, if the defendant is a male, provide in their verdict that the defendant shall work at hard labor until the fine and costs or imprisonment is satisfied, or until both are satisfied. (Id., sec. 1377.)

# § 1378. Judgment upon the verdict.

When such a verdict is returned, or when the case is tried by the court, a judgment shall be rendered directing that the defendant shall work at hard labor, at the rate of one dollar per day until the fine and costs are paid, or during the time of his imprisonment, or until both are satisfied, as the jury in their verdict may determine. (Id., sec. 1378.)

# § 259 [255] [Special verdict defined.]

A special verdict is a finding of the facts only, leaving the law arising on the facts to the judgment of the court, with an ascertainment of the punishment in the event that the court pronounces a judgment of conviction on the verdict, in cases in which the jury is required to determine the punishment.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1127.

# § 260 [256] [Special verdict—requisites of.]

A special verdict must present the conclusions of fact as established by the evidence, and not the evidence of those facts, and the facts must be so presented that the court has nothing to do but draw the conclusions of law upon them.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1127.

But where an alternative or indefinite punishment is denounced by law for a given offense, then the jury must be instructed, allowed and required to fix the kind and extent of the punishment within the limits prescribed by law. Herron v. Com., 79 Ky. 38, I R. 312.

(4) Indeterminate sentence law is valid. Wilson v. Com., 141 Ky. 341, 132 S. W. 557; Stewart v. Com., 141 Ky. 522, 183 S. W. 202.

(2) Fixing less penalty than law authorized not prejudicial to accused. Logsden v. Com., 9 R. 431, 5 S. W. 393; Johnson v. Com., 90 Ky. 53, 12 R. 20, 13 S. W. 520.

(3) Jury to fix penalty. In all cases where life or liberty is involved or a fine is to be imposed which is not fixed by statute the jury must fix the punishment. Thomas v. Com., 101 S. W. 303.

# Trial-Verdict

# § 261 [257] [Special verdict-how rendered.]

The special verdict must be reduced to writing by the jury, and read to them in the presence of the court. It shall not be received by the court unless it pronounce, affirmatively or negatively, on the facts necessary to enable the court to give judgment.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1127.

# § 262 [258] [Conviction for any degree included in or lower than charged.]

Upon an indictment for an offense consisting of different degrees, the defendant may be found guilty of any degree not higher than that charged in the indictment, and may be found guilty of any offense included in that charged in the indictment. (Degrees of offenses, sec. 263; offenses included, sec. 264; sec further, secs. 14, 265; acquittal of any degree bar, sec. 177.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim Law, secs. 3, 293, 306, 307, 1114, 1120.

# § 263 [250] [Degrees of offenses specified.]

The offenses named in each of the subdivisions of this section shall be deemed degrees of the same offense, in the meaning of the last section:

Evidence are unwarranted. Maiden v Com., 82 Ky. 133, 6 R. 19.

(2) Where an indictment charges for former convictions for felony, the court should instruct the jury to find the fact of former convictions separate from their verdict as to the guilt or innocence of the accused. Rector v. Com., 80 Ky. 468, 4 R. 323. See notes to sec. 263.

(1) Arson. Attempt to commit is degree of. Young v. Com., 12 Bush 243.

(2) Assault and battery is a degree of the offense of malicious wounding, denounced by sec. 1151, Ky. Stat. Com. v. Duncan, 91 Ky. 592, 13 R. 162, 16 S. W. 730; MeWilliams v. Com., 18 R. 92, 35 S. W. 538; and of the offense of an "assault with intent to rob." Barnard v. Com., 84 Ky. 285, 15 R. 51, 22 S. W. 219.

(3) Under an indictment for a simple assault, it is error to allow proof of a battery. Every battery includes an assault, but every assault does not include a battery, and battery is an offense of a higher grade than a simple assault. Furnish v. Com., 14 Bush 180; and see Cornelison v. Com., 84 Ky. 583, 8 R. 793, 2 S. W. 235; and under an indictment for an assault with intent to rob accused.

(4) Although an assault and battery is a lower degree of an assault with intent to kill, it is proper to refuse an instruction for the lower degree unless the facts charged in the indictment and the evidence would sustain a conviction for the lower degree. Com. v. Heath, 99 Ky. 182, 18 R. 57, 35 S. W. 277.

(5) Breach of the peace. A conviction for a breach of the peace is a bar to an indictment for malicious striking and wounding for the same offense. Com. v. Bright, 78 Ky. 238; Cornelison v. Com., 84 Ky. 583, 8 R. 793, 2 S. W. 235; and to a prosecution for an assault and battery for the same offense. Com. v. Hawkins, 11 Bush 603; Lynch v. Com., 18 R. 145, 35 S. W. 264; but see Reddy v. Com., 97 Ky. 784, 17 R. 536, 31 S. W. 730; holding that a prosecution for a misdemeanor in an inferior court will not bar a prosecution for a felony on the same state of facts, and see notes to sec. 176.

(6) Conviction for lower degree. On an indictment for an offense consisting of different degrees, the defendant may be found guilty of any degree not higher than that charged in the indictment, and may be found guilty of any offense included in the offense charged. Com. v. Garland, 3 Met. 478.

# § 263. Degrees defined.

Degrees of offenses mentioned in the Code defined.

# Title VI

# Trial--Verdict. [§ 263]

1. All offenses of homicide.
2. All injuries to the person by maiming, wounding, beating and assaulting, whether malicious or from sudden passion, and whether attended or not with the intention to kill.
3. All offenses of larceny.
4. Arson and house-burning.
5. Burglary and house-breaking.
6. An offense, and the attempt to commit the offense, if the attempt be punishable.
7. Offenses which are or may be punished capitally are of the highest degree; other felonies are of higher degree than misdemeanors; and those punished by imprisonment of higher degree than those punished by fine alone. If the punishment be the same in kind, the amount that may be inflicted fixes the degree. (Conviction of any degree bar, sec. 177.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 3, 56, 97, 293, 306, 1114, 1119, 1120.

Conner v. Com., 13 Bush 714. See Bush v. Com., 78 Ky. 268; Buekner v. Com., 14 Bush 601, and 150 Ky. 374.

# (7a) Forgery-uttering forged note.

Under an indictment for forgery, the defendant can not be convicted of uttering a forged note. Snyder v. Comt., 9 Ky. Opinions 20.

# (8) Homicide-degrees of.

"One who has committed a homicide may be indicted and tried for murder, at the election of the prosecuting officer, or he may be indicted and tried for killing by wilfully striking, under sec. 2, art. 4, chap. 29, Gen. Stat. [now sec. 115l, Ky. Stat.]. Whether he be indicted and tried for the one or the other, the judgment will bar a second prosecution. Where the indictment is for murder, the accused may be convicted of any degree of homicide as fixed by the common law, viz.: murder, voluntary manslaughter or involuntary manslaughter; but under such indictment can not be convicted of the crime mentioned in statute supra." Buckner v. Com., 14 Bush 601; Conner v. Com., 13 Bush 714. See full case of Housenuan v. Com., 128 Ky. 818, 110 S. W. 236; Spriggs v. Com., 24 R. 540, 68 S. W. 1087.

# (9) Housebreaking-burglary.

Former is degree of latter offense. Coin. v. Hurd, 109 Ky. 8, 22 R. 509. 58 S. W. 369.

# (9a) Housebreaking-larceny.

Larceny is not a degree of housebreaking. Thomas v. Com., 150 Ky. 374, 150 S. W. 376.

# (10) Lottery.

Under an indictment for promoting a lottery, the defendant may be convicted of the offense of aiding in such promotion. Miller v. Com., 13 Bush 731.

# (11) Malicious stabbing.

Under an indictment for malicious stabbing with intent to kill, the defendant may be found guilty, either of the felony charged, or of any lower degree of that offense. Tyra v. Com., 2 Met. 1; as an assault and battery, Com. v. Duncan, 91 Ky. 592, 13 R. 162, 16 S. W. 530.

# (12) Merger of offenses.

Under a misdemeanor indictment for conspiracy to defraud acts were set out showing defendants to be guilty of embezzlement, but this did not merge the lesser offense or prevent a conviction for it. Wait v. Com., 113 Ky. 821, 24 R. 604, 69 S. W. 697.

# (13) Offenses that may be joined.

See sec. 127 and notes.

# (14) Rape-Adultery.

Under an indictment for rape upon a female under twelve years of age, the defendant was convicted under sec. 6, art. 4, chap. 29, Gen. Stat. [now Ky. Stat., sec. 1155], of the offense of carnally knowing her, which was held to be a degree of the offense charged in indictment. Fenston v. Com., 82 Ky. 549, 7 R. 55; Young v. Com., 16 R. 496, and under an indictment for rape defendant may be found guilty of an assault with intent to commit that offense. Mider v. Com., 140 Ky. 684, 110 S. W. 236; Regley v. Com., 107 S. W. 243, 32 R. 890; Bethel v. Com., 80 Ky.

# Trial-Verdict

# § 264 [260] [Offenses included in offense charged.]

If an offense be charged in an indictment to have been committed with particular circumstances as to time, place, person, property, value, motive or intention, the offense without the circumstances, or with part only, is included in the offense, although that charge may be a felony, and the offense, without the circumstances, a misdemeanor only.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 3, 56, 171, 193, 306.

# § 265 [2e1] [Conviction can not be of higher degree than charged.]

If the proof show the defendant to be guilty of a higher degree of the offense than is charged in the indictment, the jury shall find him guilty of the degree charged in the indictment. (May be of lower degree, sec. 262.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1121.

# § 266 [262] [Verdict as to part of defendants.]

Upon an indictment against several the jury may return a verdict of "guilty" as to some, and "not guilty" or a special verdict as to others; if the jury can not agree as to all the defendants they may find a verdict as to those concerning whom they do agree.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1128.

526, 4 R. 487; and see Evans v. Com., 79 Kv. 414, 3 R. 30; Howell v. Com., 5 R. 174, but not of unlawfully detaining a woman, Lowry v. Com., 23 R. 1240, 65 S. W. 434. Trial under indictment for adultery is not a bar to an indictment for seduction for the same offense. Smith v. Com., 17 R. 541, 32 S. W. 137.

(l4a) Detaining a woman. The offense denounced by Ky. Stat., sec. 1158, of detaining a woman is not a degree of the offense of carnally knowing a girl under 16 years of age. Head v. Com., 174 Ky. 841, 192 S. W. 861.

(15) Robbery-Larceny. Under an indictment for robbery, a conviction for simple larceny may be had. Com. v. Prewitt, 82 Ky. 240, 6 R. 195; Sullivan v. Com., 9 R. 420, 5 S. W. 365; as to assault with intent to rob, see Barnard v. Com., 94 Ky. 285, 15 R. 51, 22 S. W. 219.

(16) Shooting. The attempt to shoot another is but an inferior degree of the offense of shooting at another without inflicting a wound, and is included in it, and a conviction of the former offense may be sustained under an indictment for the latter. Usher v. Com., 2 Duv. 394.

(17) The indictment charged a shooting with the intent to kill and murder. The jury found the defendant guilty of shooting with the intent to kill or wound. Both of these offenses being included.

(2) Under an indictment for horse stealing, the accused may be convicted of the offense of unlawfully taking the horse without felonious intent. Ford v. Com., 175 Ky. 126, 192 S. W. 1026.

Title VIj Trial--Verdict. [§ 267-268]

# § 267 [26s] [Polling of jury.]

Upon a verdict being rendered, the jury may be polled, at the instance of either party, which consists of the clerk or judge asking each juror if it is his verdict, and if one answer in the negative the verdict can not be received.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, seca. 1123, 1124.

# § 268 [264] [Verdict and proceedings on plea of insanity.]

If the defense be the insanity of the defendant, the jury must be instructed, if they acquit him on that ground, to state the fact in their verdict, and thereupon if the court, after hearing any testimony offered by the Commonwealth or the defendant, be satisfied that he is insane at the time the verdict is rendered, it may order him to be taken to a lunatic asylum. (Trial of sanity of defendant, secs. 156, 287.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1134.

# § 267.

(1) Polling jury. The accused has a right not only to see and know that the whole jury is present assenting to the verdict, but by polling to demand face to face of each juror whether the verdict is his verdict, and to object to it unless each member of the jury shall answer for himself that the verdict is his.

Temple v. Com., 14 Bush 769.

(Waiver of formal defects. A jury returned an unsigned verdict and was discharged. Before the jurors left the courtroom the court recalled the jury; thereupon one juror signed the verdict; the jurors were polled and assented to the verdict. The verdict was sufficient, and in this there was no error. Blair v. Com., 181 Ky. 218, 204 S. W. 67.

# § 268.

(1) Court of Appeals has no jurisdiction to direct an inquiry as to whether or not the appellant (accused) has become insane since rendition of verdict and judgment against him. Brown v. Comn., 14 Bush 393.

(2) Omission to instruct jury as provided in this section, when plea of insanity is relied on, is not prejudicial—this provision is only directory. Phelps v. Com., 17 R. 706, 32 S. W. 470.

§ 269 [2e5] [New trial defined.] A new trial is the re-examination of an issue of fact in the same court, by another jury, after a verdict has been given. Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1130.

§ 270 [2e6] [Effect of new trial-former verdict not referred to.] The granting of a new trial places the parties in the same position as if no trial had been had. All the testimony must be produced anew, and the former verdict cannot be used or referred to in evidence or in argument. Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1130.

§ 271 [2o7] [Grounds for new trial.] The court in which a trial is had upon an issue of fact may grant a new trial, if a verdict be rendered against the defendant by which his substantial rights have been prejudiced, upon his motion, in the following cases (Form of motion and grounds, Form 292):

- § 270. (1) New trial-effect of. Where a new trial is granted to one who has been convicted of manslaughter under an indictment for murder, he is in the same position as if no trial had been had, and may be tried again for murder. *Com. v. Arnold, 83 Ky. 1. 6 R. 181; and if a new trial is granted first indictment may be dismissed and new judgment found. Wells v. Com., 9 R. 658. 6 S. W. 150; Harkins v. Com., 8 R. 419: 2 Duv. 93 9 Bush 178; Fain v. Com., 109 Ky. 545, 22 R. 1111, 59 S. W. 1091; Ward v. Com., 128 S. W. 72.*
- § 271. (1) Defendant who fails to surrender himself or supersede the judgment cannot enter a motion for a new trial, or prosecute an appeal. *Norton v.*

# Title VI

# New Trial and Arrest of Judgment

1. [Trial of felony in absence of accused.] If the trial in a case of felony were commenced and completed in his absence.

2. [Jury receiving evidence out of court.] If the jury have received any evidence out of court other than that resulting from a view as provided in this Code.

3. [Verdict by lot.] If the verdict have been decided by lot, or in any other manner than by a fair expression of opinion by the jurors.

4. [Error in instructions.] If the court have misinstructed or refused properly to instruct the jury.

Com., 78 Ky. 501; and see Wilson v. Com., 10 Bush 526; but in Collier v. Com., 110 Ky. 516, 22 R. 1929, 62 S. W. 4, the court held that the defendant who escaped during trial which proceeded in his absence to verdict and judgment was entitled to have his motion for new trial filed several months afterward passed on by trial court.

(2) Defendant to be present during felony trial. Sec. 183 and notes; but need not be present during trial for misdemeanor. Sec. 184 and notes.

(3) Discharge of jury during trial. Where the jury is guilty of misconduct, or a juror is improperly approached or addressed, or where the defendant is taken by surprise on account of absence of a witness, the proper practice is to ask that jury be discharged. Joseph v. Com., 8 R. 53, 1 S. W. 4; Ellis v. Com., 9 R. 824, 7 S. W. 169; Costigan v. Con., 11 R. 617, 12 S. W. 629.

(4) Errors occurring during trial must be called to attention of court at the time they occur and proper exceptions and objections saved and made, or they will be waived, as where a person said to a juror, "send him up for five years." Ellis v. Com., 9 R. 824, 7 S. W. 169: or made other improper remark. Crockett v. Com., 10 R. 159, 7 S. W. 907; or wrote on window glass, in view of jury, "hang him." Baskett v. Com., 19 R. 1995, 44 S. W. 970. Or where the jury were permitted to separate during trial. Brown v. Com., 14 Bush 398; and this rule applies to all errors occurring during trial that might be available as grounds of reversal if presented to the court, or excepted to at time they occurred; 14 Bush 398; 13 Bush 246; Wilkerson v. Com., 88 Ky. 29, 10 R. 656, 0 S. W. 836; Rankin v. Com., 82 Ky. 424, 6 R. 407; Merritt v. Com., 11 R. 16, 11 S. W. 471; Gombs v. Com., 104 S. W. 261, 31 R. 844; Blanton v. Com., 147 Ky. 812, 146 S. W. 10.

(5) Errors waived if presented for first time in motion. Alleged error in not keeping jury together, or in permitting Commonwealth's attorney to make improper argument, or objection to juror for want of statutory qualification, or because jury have found verdict by lot, can not be considered if made for first time in motion for a new trial. Sec. 281; Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340; Brown v. Com., 14 Bush 398; York v. Com., 82 Ky. 360, 6 R. 334; Terrell v. Com., 13 Bush 246; Redmon v. Com., 82 Ky. 333, 6 R. 227; Vinegar v. Com., 104 Ky. 106, 20 R. 412, 46 S. W. 510; errors not specified in motion be relied on in appeal. 'See note 21. sec. 340.

(6) Improper argument. Sec notes to sec. 227.

(6a) A new trial will not be granted on account of improper language of a Commonwealth's attorney where no request is made of the circuit court for a ruling on same. Thompson v. Com., 155 Ky. 333, 159 S. W. 829.

(7) Instructions. See notes to sec. 225.

(7a) Errors in instructions given in a criminal case can not be considered on appeal unless included in the motion and grounds for a new trial. Cheek v. Com., 162 Ky. 56, 171 S. W. 998.

(8) Jurors can not impeach their verdict. "It is a well-settled principle, and one which is applicable to the action of all juries, that the testimony of the jurors is not competent to explain the grounds of their decision or impeach the validity of their finding." Comn. v. Skeggs, 3 Bush 19; and see notes 29-31 to sec. 340. Civil Code, and 149 Kv. 632.

(9) Misconduct of jury - Affidavit that a juror said he would convict, regardless of evidence, is a matter that trial court alone must determine sufficiency of to set verdict aside. Smith v. Com., 17 R. 439. 31 S. W. 724; see full cases of Gordon v. Com., 136 Ky. 508, 124 S. W. 806; Turpin v. Com., 140 Kv. 294, 130 S. W. 1086, on misconduct and bias of jurors.

# § 271] New Trial and Arrest of Judgment. [Title VI

5. [Verdict against law or evidence.] If the verdict be against law or evidence.

6. [Nealy discovered evidence.] If the defendant have discovered important evidence in his favor since the verdict.

7. [Unfair trial.] If from the misconduct of the jury, or from any other cause, the court be of opinion that the defendant has not received a fair and impartial trial.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1131.

(9a) The fact that the jury obtained and read newspapers containing accounts of the trial was not sufficient ground for setting aside the verdict. O'Hara v. Com., 164 Ky. 403, 175 S. W. 637.

(9b) The fact that a juror had at one time expressed sentiments of hostility toward the accused before his selection as a juror was not sufficient to justify a new trial. It was no misconduct on the part of the officer in charge of the jury to permit a son of a juror to talk to his father in the presence of the officer and the jury about matters having no connection whatever with the case. It was not misconduct on the part of the officer to permit the jury to go to a moving picture show. Mansfield v. Com., 163 Ky. 488, 173 S. W. 16.

(9c) A motion for a new trial was made on the ground of misconduct of one of the jury. The affidavits did not state when affiant first informed appellant or his counsel of the juror's misconduct nor did any affidavit of appellant or his counsel show when they first learned of the misconduct. The motion was properly overruled. Black v. Com., 154 Ky. 144, 156 S. W. 1043.

(9d) In a certain case the evidence in regard to the alleged misconduct of the jurors was insufficient to authorize the granting of a new trial. Harris v. Com., 163 Ky. 751, 174 S. W. 476.

(9e) A new trial will not be granted because the jury adopted an improper method of deciding the verdict, unless the defendant's rights were thereby prejudiced. Bennett v. Com., 175 Ky. 540, 194 S. W. 707; Choate v. Com., 176 Ky. 427, 195 S. W. 1080.

(9f) On an application for a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence or of misconduct of the jury, oral testimony may be heard. Barnes v. Com., 179 Ky. 725, 201 S. W. 318.

(10) Motion and grounds necessary on part of Commonwealth on appeal by it. Com. v. Tarvin, 114 Ky. 877, 24 R. 1663, 72 S. W. 13.

(11) Motion for suspends judgment. A motion for a new trial suspends the judgment until the motion is disposed of. An appeal cannot be prosecuted while the motion is pending.

(12) Surprise. A defendant is not entitled to a new trial on the sole ground of surprise occasioned by a witness, whom he has called, failing to give the evidence, or giving the evidence different from that he was expected to give. See this case for facts authorizing new trial because of improper conduct of the Commonwealth's attorney.

(13) Verdict against evidence - not ground for reversal if there is any evidence to support verdict, but lower court may grant a new trial on this ground, although Court of Appeals would not reverse for this cause. Com. v. Hall, 15 R. 783, 38 S. W. 498, and see notes to sec. 340.

(13a) The Court of Appeals, if the evidence is conflicting, will not under ordinary circumstances review the finding of the jury in a criminal case if there is nothing in the record tending to show that defendant did not have a fair trial. Jones v. Com., 158 Ky. 533, 165 S. W. 673.

(13b) The Court of Appeals will not disturb a verdict in a criminal case unless the same is palpably against the evidence. May v. Com., 164 Ky. 109, 175 S. W. 17.

(14) Ruling of court in granting or refusing a new trial is subject to review. Com. v. Harris, 147 Ky. 702, 145 S. W. 387.

(15) Effect of new trial - see note to sec. 270.

(16) Necessity for motion and grounds. Every error committed in trial except in relation to admission and rejection of evidence must be pointed out in grounds for a new trial. Thompson v. Com., 123.

Title VI] New Trial and Arrest of Judgment. [§ 272-273

# § 272 [268] [Juror can be examined to show verdict was by lot.]

A juror can not be examined to establish a ground for a new trial, except it be to establish that the verdict was made by lot.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1131.

# § 273 [269] [Time motion for new trial must be made.]

The application for a new trial must be made at the same term at which the verdict is rendered, unless the judgment be postponed to another term, in which case it may be made at any time before judgment. (See further, sec. 283.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1132.

Ky. 302, 91 S. W. 701, 28 R. 1137; and see note 4.

(17 Newly discovered evidence--practice in regard to. Ellis v. Com., 146 Ky. 715, 143 S. W. 425; Bowling v. Com., 146 Ky. 715, 143 S. W. 425; Bowling v. Com., 148 Ky. 9, 145 S. W. 1126; Hays v. Com., 140 Ky. 184, 130 S. W. 987; Deaton v. Com., 157 Kv. 308, 163 S. W. 204; Brennon v. Com., 169 Ky. 815, 185 S. W. 489; Knipp v. Com., 159 Ky. 775, 169 S. W. 494.

(18) After conviction in a criminal case defendant seeking a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence must file his own affidavit showing that he could not by the exercise of reasonable diligence have known of the existence of the evidence until after the trial had concluded. Oakley v. Com., 158 Ky. 474, 165 S. W. 691.

(19) A new trial will not be granted for newly discovered evidence merely cumulative; the grounds should be supported by evidence other than defendant's affidavit. Powell v. Com., 149 Ky. 433, 149 S. W. 891.

(20) A new trial will not be granted if the accused fails to show that he has used diligence for the purpose of obtaining the evidence on the original trial or if the evidence be merely cumulative. Traynor v. Com., 149 Kv. 462, 149 S. W. 904; Quinlan v. Com., 149 Ky. 476, 149 S. W. 892.

(21) Newly discovered evidence merely cumulative in character is not a sufficient ground for a new trial. May v. Com., 153 Ky. 141, 154 S. W. 1074; Jones v. Com., 158 Ky. 533, 165 S. W. 673.

(22) A new trial can not be granted for newly discovered evidence after the term at which the verdict was rendered. Greer v. Com., 165 Ky. 715, 178 S. W. 1027.

(23) Failure to testify by advice of counsel appointed by court. A person accused of murder was defended by counsel appointed by the court; acting on the advice of that counsel he failed to testify. This was not ground for a new trial. Ferrell v. Com., 176 Ky. 330, 195 S. W. 495.

# New Trial and Arrest of Judgment

# COURTS HAVING CONTINUOUS SESSION

§ 987. Criminal and penal cases-terms-days for motions and trials. In all criminal or penal cases in which an act is required to be done or motion made, or case set for trial at a given term by the law applicable to courts having terms, said act shall be done, or motion made, or case set for trial, on a day of a corresponding calendar month, as if each month, beginning with the first Monday of a calendar month, were a term. (Ky. Stat., sec. 987.)

# § 274. [Grounds must be in writing and filed.]

The grounds upon which a motion for a new trial is made must be stated in writing, and filed at the time of making the motion. (Form of motion and grounds, Form 292.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1131.

# § 275 [270] [Arrest of judgment-motion for defined.]

A motion in arrest of judgment is an application, on the part of the defendant, that no judgment be rendered upon a verdict against him, or on a plea of guilty. (See as to practice, sec. 279.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim Law, sec. 1133.

# § 276 [271] [Arrest of judgment—grounds for.]

The only ground upon which a judgment shall be arrested is that the facts stated in the indictment do not constitute a public offense within the jurisdiction of the court. (See further, sec. 278.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1133.

Brannon v. Com., 162 Ky. 350, 172 S. W. 703. (3) Motion in arrest of judgment can only be sustained on the ground that the facts stated in the indictment do not constitute a public offense within the jurisdiction of the court. Ward v. Com., 14 Bush 233; Walston v. Com., 16 B. M. 15; Comely v. Com., 17 B. M. 403; Com. v. Haderaft, 6 Bush 91; Weatherford v. Com., 10 Bush 196; Greer v. Com., 164 Ky. 396, 175 S. W. 665; Delk v. Com., 166 Ky. 39, 178 S. W. 1129; Sapp v. Com. 9 Ky. Opinions 2; Combs v. Com., 9 Ky. Opinions 20.

§ 276. (1) Arrest of judgment. The power of the court to arrest a judgment can only be exercised where there is no public offense charged: and, although the alleged offense may be so defectively stated as to make the indictment bad on demurrer; still, if any public offense has been committed by the accused within the jurisdiction of the court, conceding the facts alleged in the indictment to be true, the judgment will not be arrested. Tully v. Com., 11 Bush 154.

(2) It is not necessary to state the ground of a motion in arrest of judgment, as there is only one ground. 11 Bush 154.

# Title VI

# New Trial and Arrest of Judgment

# § 277 [272] [Time motion may be made.]

The motion may be made at any time before judgment, or after judgment during the same term of the court.

# § 278 [278] [Court may arrest judgment without motion.]

The court may arrest the judgment without motion, on observing the defect in the indictment, named in section 276.

# § 279 [274] [Proceedings when judgment arrested.]

If the court be of opinion, from the evidence on the trial, that the defendant is guilty of a public offense, he may be detained in custody, or on bail, in the manner and for the time prescribed in section 159 and section 160; but otherwise he shall be discharged on the arrest of judgment.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1139.

(6) When the indictment is joint, if it appears in the proof that the party on trial was the only one concerned in the commission of the offense, there may be a verdict against him the same as though he was indicted alone. 10 Bush 196.

(7) Transfer of the case from one court to another having jurisdiction, although improperly made, is not ground for arresting a judgment. Tipper v. Com., 1 Met. 6.

(8) An indictment for forgery failing to state that the changes were made without the authority of the makers of the note, the motion in arrest of judgment was properly sustained. Com. v. Bowman, 96 Ky. 40, 16 R. 222, 27 S. W. 816.

(9) Effect if motion sustained—see sec. 279; and White v. Com., 9 Bush 178, holding that, where a judgment of conviction is reversed because of error of court in overruling motion in arrest of judgment, the defendant may be proceeded against upon a sufficient indictment.

# § 279

Defendant may be detained after reversal. Where a judgment in a criminal case is reversed upon the ground that the indictment is defective, and that the motion in arrest of judgment should have been sustained; upon the return of the case to the circuit court, if that court is of opinion from the evidence on the trial that the defendant is guilty of a public offense, he may be proceeded against as provided in this section. Cornelius v. Com., 3 Met. 481; 9 Bush 178.

# CHAPTER VIII.

# BILLS OF EXCEPTION.

# § 280. Decisions subject to exception.

Upon the trial of criminal or penal prosecutions, either party may except to any decision of the court by which the substantial rights of such party are prejudiced, subject to the restrictions in the next section.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1030, 1140.

# § 280 [275] [Decisions subject to exception.]

(1) Continuance -refusal to grant is subject to exception and a reversible error when improperly refused. Bates v. Com., 13 R. 132, 16 S. W. 528; and see note 21, sec. 189.

(2) Exception and objection must be taken and made at the time to any error occurring during the trial that the defendant desires to complain about, unless this is done the error will be waived and it will not avail to call attention to it for first time in motion for a new trial, but mention in the motion for new trial should be made of the errors occurring during trial that were properly excepted and objected to. Blanton v. Com., 147 Ky. 812, 146 S. W. 10; Thompson v. Com., 123 Ky. 302, 91 S. W. 701, 28 R. 1137; Branson v. Com., 92 Ky. 330, 13 R. 614, 17 S. W. 1019; Buckles v. Com., 113 Ky. 795, 24 R. 571, 68 S. W. 1084.

(5) The court has repeatedly held that it would not reverse on the sole ground that the evidence was not sufficient to sustain the verdict if there was any evidence showing guilt of accused. Vowells v. Com., 83 Ky. 193, 7 R. 176; Patterson v. Com., 86 Ky. 313, 9 R. 481, 5 S. W. 387, 765.

(6) Improper argument. See note 2, sec. 227.

(7) Instructions-exceptions to. Instructions should be identified in the record. Weatherford v. Com., 10 Bush 196; and the record should show that it contains all the instructions. Clem v. Com., 3 Met. 10; Jane v. Com., 3 Met. 18; Mitchell v. Com., 78 Ky. 204; and see sec. 341 and notes thereto.

(8) It was formerly held in many cases that an error in giving or refusing instructions must be excepted and objected to as in civil cases, but in Buckles v. Com., 113 Ky. 795, 24 R. 571, 68 S. W. 1084, the court holds that it is the duty of the trial court to give to the jury the whole law of the case whether requested so to do or not and that it is not necessary to except or object to the action of the court in giving or refusing instructions.

(9) Jury, misconduct of. See notes to sec. 244.

# Title VI

# Bills of Exception

# § 281 [276] [Decisions not subject to exceptions.]

The decisions of the court upon challenges to the panel, and for cause, or upon motions to set aside an indictment, shall not be subject to exception. (Sec. as amended by Act of March 23, 1910, c. 92, p. 269.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 961, 962, 1030, 1037, 1140.

# § 282 [277] [Exception noted of record—preparation of bill.]

The exception shall be shown upon the record, by a bill of exceptions, prepared, settled and signed, as provided in the Court of Practice in civil cases. (Provisions of Civil Code referred to, secs. 333 to 340; must contain all instructions, sec. 341. Sec form of bill and rules, Form 291.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1030.

# (10) Jurisdiction - overruling plea to.

A demurrer to a plea to the jurisdiction in a prosecution for nuisance was overruled and the indictment was dismissed. (3) The court will not review the action of the trial court in overruling a motion for a new trial, although prejudicial errors are pointed out in the motion, unless these errors were excepted to at the time they were made and this fact appears in the bill of exceptions. An error pointed out for the first time in motion for new trial will not be considered on appeal, but errors committed during trial that were excepted to and that appear in bill of exceptions should also be mentioned in motion to be available.

# § 281.

(1) Decisions not subject to exception. Decisions of the court upon challenges to the panel or for cause, or as to manner in which jury is selected, or as to qualifications of jurors are not subject to exception and the action of the trial court in respect to these matters, however erroneous or prejudicial to the accused, cannot be reviewed on appeal. Curtis v. Com., 110 Ky. 845, 23 R. 267, 62 S. W. 886; Powers v. Com., 114 Ky. 237, 24 R. 1007, 70 S. W. 644; Vinegar v. Com., 104 Ky. 106, 20 R. 412, 46 S. W. 510; but error of the court in respect to peremptory challenges is reviewable. Smith v. Com., 20 R. 1848, 50 S. W. 241.

The Court of Appeals cannot review rulings of the circuit court in the selecting or empaneling of a jury in a criminal case. Harris v. Com., 163 Ky. 781, 174 S. W. 476; Chaney v. Com., 149 Ky. 464, 149 S. W. 923; Childers v. Com., 161 Ky. 440, 171 S. W. 149; Daniel v. Com., 154 Ky. 601, 157 S. W. 1127; Thurman v. Com., 154 Ky. 555, 157 S. W. 919; Lawson v. Com., 152 Ky. 113, 153 S. W. 56; Deaton v. Com., 157 Ky. 308, 163 S. W. 204; Frasure v. Com., 180 Ky. 274, 202 S. W. 653; Daugherty v. Com., 157 Ky. 348, 163 S. W. 453; Leadingham v. Com., 182 Ky. 291.

# (2) Decisions of the court upon motion to set aside an indictment are not reviewable by Court of Appeals.

Com. v. Simons, 100 Ky. 164, 18 R. 648, 37 S. W. 949; Com. v. Goulet, 140 Ky. 843, 132 S. W. 151; Slaughter v. Com., 152 Ky. 793.

# CIRCUIT COURTS HAVING CONTINUOUS SESSION.

§ 1016. Bills of exceptions—when to be presented. Bills of exceptions must be prepared and presented to the judge within sixty days after the making of the order excepted to; but exceptions taken during the trial need not be noted of record nor reduced to writing, unless by order of the court, until after the trial; within sixty days after the judgment becomes final, the party excepting shall, unless further time be given him, prepare his bill of exceptions, but further time may be given to prepare a bill, but not beyond one hundred and twenty days after the judgment becomes final. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1016.)

# § 283 [278] [Time judgment may be rendered.]

Upon verdicts in cases of misdemeanor, and verdicts of acquittal in cases of felony, and upon trials by the court, and upon a plea of guilty, the court may immediately render judgment; but upon verdicts of conviction in cases of felony,

Title VIj Judgment. [§ 284-285]

# § 284. [Judgment must affix the penalty.]

The court shall, in its judgment, affix the degree of punishment to be inflicted as found by the verdict of the jury or fixed by law.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1142.

# § 1377. Discretion of jury—punishment of male person.

When the punishment for a crime is a fine, or imprisonment in the county jail, or both, the jury may, in their discretion, if the defendant is a male, provide in their verdict that the defendant shall work at hard labor until the fine and costs or imprisonment is satisfied, or until both are satisfied. (Ky. Stat., ser. 1377. See further, Ky. Stat., sec. 1136.)

# § 1378. Judgment upon the verdict.

When such a verdict is returned, or when the case is tried by the court, a judgment shall be rendered directing that the defendant shall work at hard labor, at the rate of one dollar per day until the fine and costs are paid, or during the time of his imprisonment, or until both are satisfied, as the jury in their verdict may determine. (Id., sec. 1378.)

# § 285 [279] [Defendant must be present at judgment in felony.]

Judgment shall not be rendered against a defendant in cases of felony, except in his presence. If he be in custody, he shall be brought into court by the proper officer, and if not in custody, a bench warrant shall be issued for his arrest, similar to the bench warrant on indictments. (See sec. 183.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1027, 1143.
