O'Brien v. Com., 89 Ky. 354, 11 R. 534, 12 S. W. 471; and see Powers v. Com., 26 R. 1111, 83 S. W. 146.

1. When court is about to adjourn for term judgment may be pronounced on same day that verdict is rendered. Lin v. Com., 12 R. 872, 15 S. W. 664; Holly v. Com., 18 R. 441, 36 S. W. 532; and see Franklin v. Com., 105 Ky. 237, 20 R. 1137, 48 S. W. 986; but this reason does not apply to special terms, and where case is tried at special term the Code should be followed. Powers v. Com., 26 R. 1111, 83 S. W. 146.
2. The imposition of a sentence on the day the verdict was rendered is reversible error: but it cannot be reviewed where no objection and exception were taken at the time. Hansford v. Com., 170 Ky. 700, 186 S. W. 498.
3. A judgment of conviction in a criminal case when merely erroneous or voidable, is, until vacated or reversed, conclusive of what it decides. Bd. of Prison Comrs. v. De Moss, 157 Ky. 289, 163 S. W. 183.
4. No court or officer, other than the Governor, has any power to suspend the execution of any part of a judgment in a misdemeanor case; an order attempting to do so is void and should not be obeyed by the jailer. Brabandt v. Com., 157 Ky. 130, 162 S. W. 786.

# § 284. Judgment to fix penalty.

See notes to sec. 258.

# § 285. Judgment must be entered.

The judgment should be entered in full.

# §286-289

# Judgment.

# § 286 [280] [Duty of court before rendering judgment.]

When the defendant appears for judgment, he must be informed by the court of the nature of the indictment, his plea and the verdict thereon, if any; and he must be asked if he has any legal cause to show why judgment should not be pronounced against him. (May be suspended pending appeal, sec. 336.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1144.

# 287 [281] [Causes defendant may show against—insanity.]

He may show for cause against the judgment any sufficient ground for a new trial, or for arrest of judgment; he may also show that he is insane. If the court be of opinion that there is reasonable ground for believing he is insane, the question of his insanity shall be determined by a jury of twelve qualified jurors, to be summoned and impaneled as directed by the court. If the jury do not find him insane judgment shall be pronounced. If they find him insane, he must be kept in confinement, either in the county jail or lunatic asylum, until, in the opinion of the court, he becomes sane, when judgment shall be pronounced. (See further as to insanity, secs. 156, 268, 296.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1135, 1137, 1138, 1144.

# § 288 [282] [Judgment on two or more convictions.]

If the defendant be convicted of two or more offenses, the punishment of each of which is confinement, the judgment shall be so rendered that the punishment in one case shall commence after the termination of it in the others. Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1145.

# § 289 [285] [Judgment if punishment a fine.]

If the punishment of an offense be a fine, the judgment may direct that the defendant be committed. The judgment was committed. Laughlin v. Com., 18 R. 640. The practice of making an entry on the minute book and signing the minutes is condemned; and in this case judgment was reversed because the judgment was not entered on the order book and signed during the term. Johnson v. Com., 80 Ky. 377, 4 R. 210.

§ 288. Judgment on two convictions. Where a defendant at the same term was convicted under two indictments the judgment in second case properly provided that the punishment should not begin until the punishment under first indictment expired, Evans v. Com., 11 R. 573, changing rule adopted by lower court in James v. Ward, 2 Met. 271. Sec as to proper judgment in cases of second and third convictions for felony under sec. 1130, Ky. Stat. Chenowith v. Com., 11 R. 561; Combs v. Com., 14 R. 245. 20 S. W. 268; Herndon v. Com., 105 Ky. 197, 20 R. 1114, 48 S. W. 989; Hall v. Com., 106 Ky. 894, 21 R. 520, 51 S. W. 814.

§ 289. Judgment of imprisonment cannot be satisfied by the payment of money. Com. v. Norton, 96 Ky. 386, 16 R. 478, 29 S. W. 134.

# Title VI

# Judgment

prisoned until the fine be paid, specifying, however, the extent of imprisonment, which shall not exceed one day for each two dollars of the fine. (Judgment may direct punishment at hard labor, Ky. Stat., sec. 1377, quoted after sec. 284, supra.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1159.

# § 290 [284] [Judgment of death must fix day of execution.]

If judgment of death be pronounced, the day of the execution thereof shall be fixed in the judgment, and shall not be in less than thirty days after the judgment. (See further, Ky. Stat., sec. 1137, quoted after sec. 294.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Xy. Crim. Law, sec. 1147.

# § 291 [285] [Costs to be included in judgment.]

In judgments against the defendant for a misdemeanor, a judgment for costs, in addition to the other punishment, shall be rendered, which shall be taxed by the clerk, and shall be for the benefit of the officers rendering the services. (Judgment for costs in all cases, see Ky. Stat., scc. 886, quoted after sec. 186.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1159.

# § 1760. Fees in misdemeanor case to be collected from defendant.

No officer shall be entitled to any fee in a proceeding for a misdemeanor unless the same is recovered and collected from the defendant, in which case the fees allowed and to be taxed shall be the same as for similar services in civil cases. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1760.)

# § 292 [286] [Judgment against officer in cases of malfeasance or other offense.]

When a judge of the county court, justice of the peace, sheriff, coroner, surveyor, jailer, county assessor, attorney for the county, constable, city or police judge or marshal shall be convicted upon an indictment for malfeasance or misfeasance in office, or for wilful neglect in the discharge of their official duties, or for any offense which by the statute law or Constitution creates a forfeiture of his office, the court shall render a judgment of removal from office, in addition to the other penalties and punishment prescribed by law.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 3, 1151.

# § 227. Officers liable to indictment for misfeasance or neglect appeal.

Judges of the county court, justices of the peace, sheriffs, coroners, surveyors, jailers, assessors, county attorneys and constables shall be subject to indictment or prosecution for misfeasance or malfeasance in office, or wilful neglect in discharge of official duties, in such mode as may be prescribed by law; and upon conviction his office shall become vacant, but such officer shall have the right to appeal to the court of appeals. (Constitution.)

# § 290. Governor may fix date of execution when judgment on appeal is affirmed.

Bullitt v. Sturgeon, 127 Ky. 332. 105 S. W. 468, 32 R. 215, and sec. 346.

# § 292. Malfeasance or misfeasance see sec. 227 of Constitution in Ky. Stat. and notes thereto; and sec. 3748, Ky. Stat.

797

# § 292] Judgment.

# [Title VI]

# § 3748. County officers guilty of malfeasance or neglect—penalty.

Judges of the county court, justices of the peace, sheriffs, coroners, surveyors, jailers, assessors, county attorneys, and constables, shall be subject to indictment in the county in which they reside, for misfeasance or malfeasance in office, or wilful neglect in the discharge of official duties, and, upon conviction, shall be fined in any sum not less than one hundred nor more than one thousand dollars; and upon such conviction the office held by such person shall become vacant, and the judgment of conviction shall so recite; but such officer shall have the right to appeal to the court of appeals. (Ky. Stat., sec. 3748; see further, Ky. Stat., secs. 1360, 1366.)

# Restitution of and Damages for Property Taken, Injured or Destroyed.

# § 1132. Restitution of injured or stolen property—damages.

Persons convicted for felony, for taking, injuring or destroying property, shall restore the property or make reparation in damages. The court in which such conviction may be had, if applied to at the same term in which the sentence was pronounced, by petition verified by affidavit, may order restitution or give judgment against the convict for reparation in damages, and enforce the collection of the same by execution or other process. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1132.)

# § 1133. Restitution of stolen property where accused escapes.

In all cases in which the party indicted for stealing property, or for having stolen property in his possession, shall break jail, forfeit his recognizance, or otherwise fail to appear for trial at the proper time, the party or parties claiming the property may make a motion in the court in which the indictment is pending for the return to him or them of such property. (Id., sec. 1133.)

# § 1134. Motion for restitution-notice and proceedings.

The claim shall be supported by his or their affidavit. The motion shall be continued until the next term of the court for final action. A notice of such motion, served upon the attorney for the Commonwealth, shall be taken as a notice served upon the person named in the indictment, and shall give the court jurisdiction of the matter. A jury shall try the facts, and if they find in their verdict that the claimant or claimants are entitled to the possession of the property, the court shall enter judgment accordingly; and the court shall have power to enforce such judgment by rule, attachment and imprisonment as in cases of contempt. The costs of proceedings shall be paid by the claimant, for which he may have judgment and execution against the party stealing the property. It shall be the duty of the attorney of the Commonwealth to defend the motion mentioned in this section. (Id., sec. 1134.)

# § 1135. Motion for restitution—judgment against convict-proceedings.

In motions for restitution or reparation, the court shall cause the prisoners to be brought into court, and demand of him if he has any defense to make to the motion. And if the convict consents to such restitution or reparation in damages, the court shall give judgment accordingly, if the damages are agreed. Otherwise a jury shall be impaneled to try the facts and ascertain the amount and the value of the property, or assess the damages, as the case may be. A failure to pursue the remedy hereby given shall not deprive the party aggrieved of his civil action for the injury sustained. The party injured shall have a lien on the estate of the criminal from the time of his arrest. (Id., sec. 1135.)

# § 293 [287] [Copy of judgment authorizes sheriff to execute.]

If a judgment of death or confinement, either in the penitentiary or the county jail, be pronounced, a certified copy thereof must be furnished forthwith to the sheriff, who shall thereupon execute it, and no other warrant or authority is necessary to its execution.* (Proceeding upon affirmance of death penalty, sec. 346.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1152.

*Mode of infliction of death penalty, see Section 1137, Kentucky Statutes, quoted after Section 294, post.—Ed.

# § 294 [288] [Death penalty—how executed.*]

*This section is impliedly repealed by Section 1137, Kentucky Statutes, as amended by an Act of March 21, 1910, c. 38, p. 111, which is quoted below. So much of this section as provides the time and manner of infliction of the death penalty is repealed by Sections 1137 (1) and 1137 (3), providing for electrocution before sunrise. It is believed, also, that although the sheriff is permitted by Section 1137 (4) to attend the execution, yet he is not required to do so, while by Section 1137 (3) the "warden" shall cause the condemned person to be electrocuted, and such warden "executing the death sentence" shall be paid, etc., for which reason it is concluded that the remainder of Section 294, providing that the sheriff shall execute the judgment of death is also repealed.-Ed.

# MODE OF EXECUTING DEATH PENALTY.

§ 1137 (1). Death penalty—electrocution—details of. That from and after the period that this law shall take effect the mode of the execution of a death sentence must in every case be by causing to pass through the body of the

799

# §294 Execution

Condemned a current of electricity of sufficient intensity to cause death as quickly as possible, and the application of such current must be continued until the condemned is dead. All executions of the death penalty by electrocution shall take place within the walls of the State penitentiary, hereafter indicated by the Board of Prison Commissioners, and in such inclosure as will exclude public view thereof.

# § 1137 (2). Duty of sheriff and jailer

The sheriff and jailer of the county in which the condemned under sentence of death is confined, shall, upon the order of the court in which the condemned was sentenced, cause said condemned person to be conveyed as expeditiously, privately and safely as may be, to said penitentiary, and to deliver said condemned person, together with a duly certified copy of the judgment ordering the execution, to the warden of said penitentiary, and the warden thereof shall receive said condemned person and safely keep the same until the day designated for the execution, unless said condemned person be otherwise disposed of by due course of law. Said sheriff shall be paid therefor the same fees as are now allowed sheriffs for the conveyance of persons convicted of felony to the penitentiary.

# § 1137 (3). Sentence-execution of

The warden of said penitentiary or his deputy shall proceed at the time and place named in the judgment of the court in which the condemned person was tried and convicted to cause the same to be electrocuted according to the provisions of this act, before sunrise on the day designated in the judgment aforesaid, unless the execution be stayed by due process of law. The warden or his deputy executing the death sentence shall be paid therefor the sum of twenty dollars from the State treasury.

# § 1137 (4). Who may attend execution

The electrician and the warden of said penitentiary and his deputy or deputies and guards, the sheriff of the county in which the condemned was convicted, the prison commissioners, the physician and chaplain of the penitentiary, and a clergyman and three other persons selected by the condemned, and one representative of every newspaper published in the county in which the condemned was convicted, and one representative of every daily newspaper published in the State, may attend such execution, but no other persons shall be permitted to be present.

# § 1137 (5). Report of warden

The warden of said penitentiary shall make due return on the copy of the judgment of the court pronouncing the death sentence, of the manner, time and place of its execution by him, which return shall be made by the clerk of said court filed in the papers of the cause in his office.

# § 1137 (6). Disposition of body

The body of the condemned shall be delivered to any friend or relative making request therefor, and the State shall pay the expense for the return of the body to its home, not to exceed thirty dollars, but if no such request is made, it shall be buried and the cost of such burial, not to exceed thirty dollars, shall be a claim against the treasury of the State.

# § 1137 (7). When Governor shall send warrant of execution and name date

If the condemned under sentence of death escapes from custody and be recaptured after the expiration of the date fixed for the execution, the governor, upon being notified of such recapture by the warden of said penitentiary in writing, shall send his warrant of execution to said warden by.

Title VI Execution

# § 295-296

Special messenger and shall name therein the day of execution, and thereupon the warden shall proceed to the execution thereof according to the provisions of this act.

# § 1137 (8). Disposition of condemned--if insane or pregnant.

If the condemned under sentence of death be insane or pregnant with child on the day designated for the execution, said execution shall be suspended until said condemned be restored to his or her right mind or until she be delivered of child, and then said execution shall take place under the warrant of the governor and at the time herein designated by him unless stayed by due process of law.

# § 1137 (9). Appropriation.

There shall be installed and erected in the State penitentiary, under direction and management of the prison commission, the necessary electrical apparatus for the electrocution of persons under sentence of death, and the sum of seven thousand dollars, or so much thereof as is necessary, is hereby appropriated for the purchase and installation of the same.

# § 1137 (10). Act-when applicable.

This act shall not apply to crimes committed before this act takes effect; the punishment of such crimes shall be such as was prescribed by the law in effect when the crime was committed. (Ky. Stat., sec. 1137.)

# § 295 [289] [Suspension of death sentence.]

The only officers who shall have the power of suspending the execution of a judgment of death are the Governor [and, in cases of insanity or pregnancy of the defendant, the sheriff, as provided in the next section;* and, in cases of appeals, the clerk of the Court of Appeals, as prescribed in title ix., chapter 1, article 1.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1150.

*Who now suspends the execution in case of the insanity or pregnancy of the defendant? By Section 1137, Kentucky Statutes, quoted after Section 294, supra, the sheriff and jailer are required to "deliver" the condemned person to the penitentiary warden and the warden is required to electrocute the defendant and "execute the judgment." By Section 1137 (8), if the defendant is insane or pregnant the execution "shall be suspended" until the condition is removed when the judgment will be executed upon warrant of the governor. Section 1137 apparently removes the defendant from the custody of the sheriff, but does not provide who shall conduct the inquest to determine the question of insanity or pregnancy. Formerly, when the sheriff had the custody of the defendant, and the death sentence was executed by the sheriff, Section 295 provided that in such cases he should have power to suspend the judgment and to hold an inquest as provided in Section 296. Section 3711 (8) does not in itself provide who may in such cases suspend the sentence or hold the inquest, if an inquest is required to be held by the officer who may suspend the sentence, although it apparently takes this power from the sheriff.-Ed.

# § 296 [200] [Insanity or pregnancy of defendant-proceedings.]

If the sheriff be satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the defendant is insane or pregnant, he may summon a jury of twelve persons on the jury list, drawn by the clerk, who shall be sworn by the sheriff well and truly to inquire into the insanity or pregnancy of the defendant.

801

and a true inquisition return; they shall examine the defendant and hear any evidence that may be presented; and by a written inquisition, signed by each of them, find as to the insanity or pregnancy. Unless the inquisition find the defendant insane or pregnant, the sheriff shall not suspend the execution; but if the inquisition find the defendant insane or pregnant, he shall suspend the execution and immediately transmit the inquisition to the governor.*

(See further as to insanity, secs. 156, 268, 287.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1136, 1137, 1149.

*See Section 1137 (8), of the Kentucky Statutes, concerning suspension of the judgment during the insanity or pregnancy of defendant. See editorial note to Section 294.-Ed.

# § 297 [201] [Death penalty--when governor to fix day for execution.]

Whenever a judgment of death has not been executed on the day appointed therefor by the court, from any cause whatever, the governor, by a warrant under his hand and the seal of the State, shall fix the day of the execution, which warrant shall be obeyed by the sheriff, and no one but the governor can then suspend its execution.*

(See further, sec. 346.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1147.

*See Section 1137, Kentucky Statutes, to same effect.-Ed.

# § 298 [202] [Imprisonment-execution of judgment.]

The sheriff, in executing a judgment of confinement, shall deliver the defendant, with a certified copy of the judgment, to the keeper of the penitentiary, or to the jailer, according to the judgment.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1152.

# § 299 [203] [Powers of sheriff conveying prisoner to penitentiary.]

In carrying the defendant to the penitentiary, the sheriff shall have all the powers of preventing an escape, of resisting an effort to rescue the defendant, of recapturing him, and of summoning persons to his aid, that he would have in executing a warrant of arrest in his county. (Powers of sheriff, Ky. Stat., sec. 4578; power to employ guards, see Ky. Stat., secs. 2044, 2045, 2046; see sec. 371.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1154.

# § 300 [204] [Sheriff to make written return of execution.]

The sheriff shall make a written return of the execution of the judgment of death or imprisonment, and file the same with the clerk of the court in ten days after such execution.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1155.

# § 301 [205] [Execution to issue on judgment for fines.]

Upon judgment § 301. (1) Capias. A capias pro fine may issue upon a judgment without an order of court. A party arrested under a capias in a county other than that from which it was issued should be taken by the officer to the jail of the county.

# Title VI

# Execution. [§302-305]

ments for fines, whether rendered on indictments, penal actions or otherwise, writs of execution, as provided in the General Statutes [new Kentucky Statutes] may be issued against the person or property of the defendant. (Form of executions, Forms 303, 304, 305; form of capias, same.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1156.

# § 302 [208] [Execution on judgment against bail.]

Upon judgments against bail, only such execution shall be issued as may be issued upon judgments in civil actions for debt. (Form of execution, Form 305.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1156.

# § 303 [297] [Execution of judgment for abatement of nuisance.]

A certified copy of a judgment for the abatement or removal of a nuisance, or for any other thing than the payment of money by the defendant, when delivered to the sheriff, shall authorize and require him to execute it; and he shall make a return of his executing it, and file the same with the clerk of the court, in sixty days after a copy is delivered to him. Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1157.

# § 304 [298] [Period of confinement for fine-effect of—judgments of police courts.]

The defendant shall not be held in confinement under an execution for a fine for a longer period than at the rate of one day for each two dollars of the fine; but such confinement shall not discharge the fine, which thereafter can only be collected by proceedings against the defendant's property. The provisions of this section shall not apply to judgments in city or police courts, which may be discharged by confinement in the city workhouse, according to the provisions of the special statutes regulating them. (Punishment by hard labor, see Ky. Stat., sec. 1377; imprisonment for costs, Ky. Stat., sec. 886, quoted after see. 188, ante: for statutes relating to police courts, see Appendix.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1159.

# § 305. [Replevy of fine after confinement-effect of.]

In all cases in which a person is confined in a jail or workhouse for the non-payment of a fine, it shall be lawful for the clerk of the court in which the judgment from which it issued. Long v. Wood, 78 Ky. 392. § 304. Costs may be collected in same manner as fine. Rowe v. Reneer, 99 S. W. 250, 30 R. 545. Penal action—capias may issue on judgment in. Harp v. Comn., 22 R. 1792. 61 S. W. 467; L. & N. R. Co. v. Com., 112 Ky. 635, 23 R. 1900, 66 S. W. 505: Hilton v. Com., 127 Ky. 486, 105 S. W. 956, 32 R. 377. (3 Action for discovery-upon return of no property may be brought. Howard v. Fuller. 100 Ky. 148, 18 R. 611, 37 S. W. 585. Claryville T. P. Co. v. Com., 32 R. 861, 107 S. W. 327.

The bond shall be for the same time and substantially of the same form, and shall have the same force and effect as a replevin bond. The clerk shall have fifty cents for taking the bond, which shall be included in the amount thereof. (Form of bond, Form 160; hard labor sentence or fine may be replevied, Ky. Stat., secs. 1380, 1667.)

# § 306 [200] [Indictment, when not necessary.]

No indictment shall be necessary in prosecutions for violation of the by-laws or ordinances of a city or town, nor in other prosecutions in police or city courts, unless the punishment of the offense exceed a fine of one hundred dollars or confinement for thirty days. (Jurisdiction of, secs. 13, 19. See further, sec. 10.) Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 946, 950.

# § 307 [soo] [Grand jury—provisions as to.]

If the police or city court have jurisdiction of offenses which can only be prosecuted by indictment, the court may order the peace officer, whose duty it is to attend the court, to summon a competent number of grand jurors, the qualifications of whom shall be as prescribed in the General Statutes [now Kentucky Statutes], and who shall be sworn, and have the powers and duties prescribed in chapter I. of Title VI.

# § 306. (1) Indictment not necessary in prosecutions for violation of ordinance.

Goodman & Co. v. Com., 99 S. W. 252, 30 R. 519.

# (2) A violation of an ordinance of the city of Louisville, the highest penalty of which is a fine of fifty dollars, may be prosecuted by either summons or warrant.

Schwartz v. Boswell, 156 Ky. 103. 160 S. W. 748.

805

# §308-312

# Proceedings in Police or City Courts

# § 308 [so1] [Terms of police court may be fixed by judge.]

The judge of the police or city court may, by orders entered on its minutes, fix terms for its sessions, to which process shall be returnable, but may hold the court at any time for the transaction of business brought before him.

# § 309 [so2] [Proceedings when defendant brought before court.]

When a person has been arrested and brought before the city court or the judge thereof, charged with an offense within the jurisdiction of such court, and not requiring an indictment, he shall be forthwith tried, or at the discretion of the judge, held to bail for his future appearance for trial, or discharged from custody. (See Appendix as to jurisdiction of police courts. Form of bail bond, Form 226.)

# § 310 [303] [Summons to be issued against offender.]

Upon information given by a peace officer, or by a private person, on oath, to the clerk of the court, that an offense within the jurisdiction of such court, and not requiring an indictment for its prosecution, has been committed, the clerk shall issue a summons against the offender.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 909.

# § 311 [804] [Summons—requisites of, to whom directed.]

The summons mentioned in the last section shall command the peace officer to whom it is directed to summon the defendant, naming him, to appear in the court, on a day to be named in the summons, to answer the charge made against him, of having committed an offense, naming or briefly describing it, and to return the summons on a day to be named. It shall be signed by the clerk, and directed to the marshal of the town or city, but may be executed by any peace officer of the city or county. (Form of summons. Form 204.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 909.

# § 312 [305] [Warrant of arrest may be issued by judge.]

Upon information on oath made to the judge of the court, he may order a warrant of arrest to be issued, which shall be similar to the summons, except in the command to arrest the defendant, instead of summons him. (Form of warrant, Form 207.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 940.

# § 310. Power of clerk of Louisville Police Court to issue process.

The provisions of Ky. Stat., sec. 2934, relating to the clerk of the Louisville Police Court repeal the provisions of secs. 310 and 312, Criminal Code, so far as they are inconsistent with it. Schwartz v. Boswell, 156 Ky. 103, 100 S. W. 748.

# 312. (1 Warrant-form of.

It is not necessary that a warrant charging the selling of liquor in violation of an ordinance should specify the quantity sold or the place where it was drunk; nor is an erroneous statement as to the date material; proof may be heard as to any sale within the time allowed for the prosecution of the offense prior to the date of the warrant. Megowan v. Com., 2 Met. 3.

# (2 The same strictness is not required in justice's court as is in ei court.

Wade v. Com., 3 R. 442.

Title VIIj Proceedings in Police or City Courts.

# § 313 [3e6] [Time prosecutions stand for trial.]

All prosecutions in such courts shall stand for trial immediately, if the defendant be in custody, or on bail for the offense charged, or on the day to which the defendant is summoned to appear, provided the summons be executed within the limits of the court's jurisdiction one day, or elsewhere ten days, before the day on which he is warned to appear. If not executed as above provided, the trial shall be postponed to a day to be fixed by the court.

# § 314 [307] [Summons, how executed.]

The summons shall be executed by the officer by reading it to the defendant, or stating to him its contents; and, if required, the officer shall show it to him.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 909, 910.

# § 315 [sos] [Witnesses' attendance, how secured.]

The clerk shall issue subpoenas for witnesses at the request of the officers of the city, or of the defendant, and the court may compel their attendance in the manner and by the process prescribed in the Civil Code. (Form of subpoena and attachment, Forms 217, 219; provisions of Civil Code referred to sec. 537.)

# § 316 [so9] [Postponement of trial.]

The court may, for good cause, grant continuances or postponements of the trial.

# § 317 [310] [Provisions of title six apply.]

The provisions of this Code in Title VI., so far as applicable, shall govern the proceedings of police or city courts, except as provided in this title.

# § 318 [311] [Written pleadings not required if no indictment.]

No written information or pleadings are required in prosecutions in which an indictment is unnecessary.

# § 319 [812] [Trial of issues of law and fact.]

The issues of law and fact shall be tried by the judge, in all cases in which the only punishment is a fine of sixteen dollars or less; in other cases the defendant may demand that issues of fact be tried by a jury.

# § 320 [813] [Clerk of court-laws relating to.]

If the judge acts as the clerk of the court, the provisions of this title as to the clerk shall apply to him.

# § 321 [314] [Police courts defined.]

[Mayors' courts, and courts held]

# § 314. Service of summons on corporation is sufficient if it is read to the officer or agent of the corporation upon whom a summons should be served.

L. & N. R. R. v. Board of Trustees, 8 R. 59; see for form of summons, sec. 146.

# § 319. This section repealed to the extent that it is in conflict with the provisions in the charters of the various classes regulating trials by the judge without a jury.

City v. Holly, 10s Ky. 621, 22 R. 358, 57 S. W. 491.

# § 322 [315] [Louisville City Court.]

The laws regulating the proceedings in the Louisville City Court, and prescribing the powers and duties of the officers of said court, shall not be changed by the provisions of this title, but remain in full force; and the provisions of this title shall only apply to said court so far as they are consistent with the existing laws regulating said court. (See sec. 166 of the Constitution, and sec. 2911, et seq., Kentucky Statutes.)

Title VIIIj    Proceedings in Justices' Courts.    [§ 323-326

# § 323 [816] [Proceedings when accused brought before justice.]

If a person be brought, or, in obedience to a bail bond, come before a justice of the peace charged with the commission of an offense within the jurisdiction of his court, as prescribed in Title II., the justice shall forthwith proceed to the trial of the charge, or for sufficient cause may postpone the trial to a future day. (Jurisdiction of justice, secs. 13. 18; hore justice to proceed in felony case, sec. 71.)

# § 324 [817] [Disposition of defendant if trial postponed.]

If the trial be postponed, the defendant may be committed to the custody of a peace officer, or to jail, or discharged upon bail or without bail.

# § 325 [818] [Bail or bond for good behavior may be required.]

It shall be the duty of the justice to require bail of the defendant upon its being made to appear that, unless bail be required, he will probably escape punishment, or that his discharge without bail would endanger persons or property; in which latter case the justice may also require security for the peace or for good behavior. (Form of bai! bond, Form 226; bond for good behavior, Form 239; other provisions concerning security for good behavior, secs. 382 to 394.)

# § 326 [810] [Summons or warrant—when justice may issue.]

If from his personal knowledge, or from information given to him on oath, a justice be satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for believing that a person has committed a public offense within the jurisdiction of his court, he shall either issue a warrant of arrest or a summons against such person. (See further, secs. 26-31.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 940.

809

Proceedings in Justices' Courts

# § 327 [320] [Warrant of arrest, when may issue.]

The warrant of arrest shall not be issued unless the offense charged be one of actual or apprehended violence to person or property, accompanied with a breach of the peace; or the justice be satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the defendant will escape punishment unless arrested. (See further, secs. 26-31.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 940.

# § 328 [821] [Summons and warrant, form of.]

The warrant of arrest and the summons shall be similar to those prescribed in Title VII. (Form of warrant, Form 209; summons, Form 204.)

# § 329 [822] [Witnesses' attendance, how secured.]

The justice shall issue subpoenas for witnesses and for the production of documentary evidence, and enforce their attendance, or its production, as prescribed in the Civil Code. (Form of subpoena, Forms 217, 218; attachment, Form 219; provisions of Civil Code referred to, sec. 537.)

# § 330 [323] [Pleadings may be oral.]

No written information, nor pleadings, shall be required in prosecutions in justices' courts.

# § 331 [824] [Trial of issues of law and fact.]

The issues of law and fact shall be tried by the justice, in all cases in which the only punishment is a fine of sixteen dollars or less. In other cases the defendant may demand that issues of fact be tried by a jury.

# § 332 [325] [Jury trial—summoning jury.]

Upon a jury trial being lawfully demanded, the justice shall order a peace officer to summon a sufficient number of qualified jurors, from which the jury may be formed.

# § 333 [326] [Provisions of title 6 apply.]

The provisions of Title VI., so far as applicable, shall govern the trial, verdict, judgment and execution in prosecutions in justices' courts, except as provided in this title. (Form of capias pro fine, Forms 303, 304.)

§ 330. Written information or pleadings not required. S. W. 252, 30 R. 519; Bitzer v. Com. Goodman v. Com., 99 132 S. W. 179, 141 Ky. 58.

§ 334 [827] [Appeal in felony cases.] The Court of Appeals shall have appellate jurisdiction in prosecutions for felonies, subject to the restrictions contained in this article.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 899, 1164.

§ 334. (1) Appeal-escaped prisoner cannot prosecute. An escaped prisoner convicted of a felony will not be permitted to prosecute an appeal to reverse the judgment of conviction. Wilson v. Com., 10 Bush 526; and see Norton v. Com., 78 Ky. 501; Collier v. Com., 110 Ky. 516, 22 R. 1929, 62 S. W. 4.

(2) Jurisdiction of Court of Appeals in misdemeanors. See sec. 347.

(3) Petition for rehearing. There is no provision of the Code authorizing a petition for rehearing in a criminal case, but during the term at which case is decided the court may consider such petition. Powers v. Com., 24 R. 1350, 71 S. W. 494.

(4) Right of appeal is not a constitutional right, and the accused desiring to appeal must conform to the rules prescribed.

811

# §335-336 Appeals-To Court of Appeals-Felonies.

# § 335 [828, 840] [Appeal by defendant or Commonwealth.]

An appeal shall only be taken on a final judgment, except on behalf of the Commonwealth. An appeal by the Commonwealth from a decision of the circuit court shall not suspend the proceedings in the case. The decision of the Court of Appeals shall be obligatory on the circuit courts, as being the correct exposition of the law.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1164.

# § 336 [820] [Time and manner of appealing-suspension of judgment.]

An appeal may be taken by the defendant in the following manner only:

1. [Appeal prayed at term judgment is rendered.] The appeal must be prayed during the term at which the judgment is rendered, and the prayer noted on the record in the circuit court. The appeal shall be granted as a matter of right.
2. [Judgment may be suspended.] When an appeal is prayed the court shall, if the defendant desire it, make an order that the execution of the judgment be suspended until the expiration of the period within which the defendant is required to lodge a transcript of the record in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals. After the expiration of such period the judgment shall be executed unless the defendant shall have filed in the clerk's office of the court rendering the judgment, the certificate, as provided in subsection 3 of this section, that the appeal has been taken, or a copy of an order of the Court of Appeals granting further time to lodge the transcript.

(5) Where an appeal was dismissed for want of prosecution it will not on motion of the prisoner be reinstated on docket after lapse of several years. Turner v. Com., 89 Ky. 78, 12 R. 273, 1 S. W. 475.

# 335. (1) Defendant can only appeal from final judgment.

Smith v. Com., 5 R. 851; Riley v. Com., 21 R. 1406, 55 S. W. 7, appeal by Commonwealth, sec. 337.

(la) No appeal lies from a refusal of a Circuit Court to grant one convicted of felony an inquest of lunacy. Davidson v. Com., 174 Ky. 789; 192 S. W. 846.

2 Mistrial - appeal by Commonwealth may be taken for the purpose of reviewing the decisions of the trial court in admitting and rejecting evidence and in giving and refusing instructions. Com. v. Matthews, 89 Ky. 287, 11 R. 505, 12 S. W. 333; Com. v. Bullock, 24 R. 78, 67 S. W. 992; and see sec. 337 and notes.

(3) New trial granted by lower court. Commonwealth may appeal to have law of ease settled before another trial, but can not interfere with action of court in granting a new trial. Com. v. Hourigan, 89 Ky. 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550; Com. v. Robinson, 84 S. W. 319, 27 R. 14; Com. v. Brogan, 163 Ky. 748, 174 S. W. 473.

) The Commonwealth may appeal from a judgment overruling its demurrer to accused's written plea of former jeopardy. Com. v. Anderson, 169 Ky. 372, 183 S. W. 898.

# 336. (1) Appeal by defendant--when record to be filed.

When the transcript in a felony case is not filed within sixty days after judgment, and no order made within that period extending the time for filing, the Court of Appeals is without jurisdiction to try the appeal.

Title IX Appeals-To Court of Appeals-Felonies

# §337

3. *[Record to be filed in sixty days--certificate of clerk.]* The appeal is taken, by lodging in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals, within sixty days after the judgment, a certified transcript of the record. The clerk of the Court of Appeals shall thereupon issue a certificate that an appeal has been taken, which shall suspend the execution of the judgment until the decision upon the appeal.

4. *[Time to prepare bill of exceptions.]* If time be given, beyond the term at which the judgment is rendered, to present a bill of exceptions, the transcript of the record may be filed in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals, within sixty days after the bill of exceptions is made a part of the record.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1150, 1165.

# § 337 [381]

*[Time and manner of appealing by Commonwealth.]* If an appeal on behalf of the Commonwealth be desired, the Commonwealth's attorney shall pray the appeal during the term at which the decision is rendered.

Nor has the Court of Appeals power, after the expiration of the time provided by law for filing the transcript, to make an order extending the time. *Metcalf v. Com., 84 Ky. 485, 8 R. 547, 1 S. W. 878; nor can provisions of Code be dispensed with by agreement. Com. v. Schlitzbaum,* 25 R. 1022, 76 S. W. 835.

(2) To give Court of Appeals jurisdiction in felony case, a certified transcript of the record must be filed in the clerk's office within sixty days after judgment; unless the court extend the time, the fact that record was mislaid by clerk and not found until after sixty days cannot confer jurisdiction. *Stratton v. Com., 84 Ky. 190, 1 S. W. 83; Gray v. Com., 112 Ky. 542, 23 R. 1870, 66 S. W. 387; Com. v. Schlitzbaum, 25 R. 1022, 76 S. W. 835; and see Adkins v. Com.,* 102 Ky. 94, 19 R. 1300, 42 S. W. 834; 44 S. W. 132, for a full discussion of this question.

(2a) Where the transcript in a felony case is not filed within sixty days the appeal must be dismissed; consent will not give the court jurisdiction. *Lane v. Com., 161 Ky. 329, 170 S. W. 627; Flowers v. Com.,* 153 Ky. 436, 155 S. W. 740.

(2b) An application for an extension of time under this section filed in the clerk's office in vacation within sixty days from the judgment was sufficient to maintain the status quo until the court has opportunity to act. *Bennett v. Com.,* 150 Ky. 604, 150 S. W. 806.

(3) When time beyond the term to prepare and present bill of exceptions is given, if the transcript is filed within sixty days after the bill of exceptions is made part of the record it will be in time. *Strassel v. Com., 80 Ky. 525, 8 R. 77; and see Adkins v. Com.,* 102 Ky. 94, 19 R. 1300, 42 S. W. 834, 44 S. W. 132.

(3a) Tax on appeal. Four persons tried jointly and convicted of manslaughter appealed; but one tax of $2 was to be paid. *Gross v. Com.,* 149 Ky. 379, 149 S. W. 833.

(4) Mandate in criminal case, in case of affirmance, may issue immediately. *Nelson v. Com.,* 94 Ky. 594, 15 R. 255, 23 S. W. 348, 350.

(5) Objection that record not filed in time waived if not made until after case is submitted. *Watkins v. Com.,* 123 Ky. 817, 97 S. W. 740, 29 R. 1273.

(6) Judgment is suspended by filing transcript although trial court refuses to make order of suspension. *Slaughter v. Com.,* 148 Ky. 315, 146 S. W. 422.

(7) The granting of an appeal and the suspension of the judgment is a matter of right and is not within the discretion of the circuit court. *Balee v. Com.,* 153 Ky. 558, 156 S. W. 147.

(8) The execution should be stayed until sixty days after the date in the following term fixed by the trial court for incorporating the bill of exceptions into the record; the Court of Appeals cannot grant the stay before the transcript is filed; but it may by mandamus require the circuit court to grant the stay. *Com. v. Crouch,* 170 Ky. 772, 186 S. W. 764.

# §337. (1) Appeal by Commonwealth

Appeals-To Court of Appeals Felonies. [Title IX]

dered, whereupon the clerk shall immediately make a transcript of the record and transmit the same to the attorney-general, or deliver the transcript to the Commonwealth's attorney, to be transmitted by him. If the attorney-general, on inspecting the record, be satisfied that error has been committed to the prejudice of the Commonwealth, upon which it is important to the correct and uniform administration of the criminal law that the Court of Appeals should decide, he may, by lodging the transcript in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals, within sixty days after the decision, take the appeal.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 899, 1166.

§ 338 [332] [Summons or notice not necessary.] No summons nor notice shall be necessary upon an appeal.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1167.

In felony cases any ruling of the lower court may be reviewed whether it be final or not, and without reference to whether the judgment is upon a verdict or whether it be a bar to another prosecution for the same offense. When the lower court assigns a reason for sustaining a demurrer to an indictment, the Court of Appeals is not limited to the consideration of the reason so assigned, but must determine for itself whether the indictment is good or bad. It is the indictment and not the opinion of the lower court that must determine whether the judgment on demurrer shall operate as a bar. Com. v. Cain, 14 Bush 525; and see Com. v. Bruce, 79 Ky. 560, 3 R. 360.

(1a) An appeal was rightly prosecuted by the Commonwealth from an order refusing to quash an indictment. Com. v. Meadors, 149 Ky. 769, 149 S. W. 1005.

(1b) The Court of Appeals can review any appealable order of the trial court within its jurisdiction, and may review an order denying Commonwealth's attorney's motion to dismiss an indictment for a felony. Com. v. Davis, 169 Ky. 650, 184 S. W. 1112.

(1c) The Commonwealth can appeal only from decisions of the court adverse to it. Com. v. Brand, 166 Ky. 753, 179 S. W. 844.

(1d) An appeal in a penal action does not lie except from the final judgment or order therein terminating the action or divesting a right. Com. v. Amer. Exp. Co., 167 Ky. 685, 181 S. W. 353.

Appeal must be prayed during term at which case was tried. Com. v. Fryman, 17 R. 400, 31 S. W. 281; and transcript filed within sixty days, Com. v. Schlitzbaum, 25 R. 1022, 76 S. W. 835; Com. v. Nelson, 24 R. 2155, 73 S. W. 751; Com. v. Barbour, 29 R. 622, 94 S. W. 634.

(3) The Commonwealth cannot appeal from an order allowing witness claims against the Commonwealth. Com. v. Tudor, 110 Ky. 739, 23 R. 122, 62 S. W. 721.

(4) Motion and grounds for new trial necessary in appeal by Commonwealth. Com. v. Tarvin, 114 Ky. 877, 24 R. 1663, 72 S. W. 13.

(4a) After a new trial had been granted to one convicted of voluntary manslaughter the Commonwealth prayed an appeal to the Court of Appeals. There was no bill of exceptions. The appeal was dismissed. Com. v. Fore, 158 Ky. 465, 165 S. W. 676.

(5) Cross-appeal cannot be taken by the Commonwealth, but on the appeal of the accused the court will pass on questions excepted to by the Commonwealth. Terrell v. Com., 13 Bush 246.

(Mistrial-Commonwealth may appeal. Com. v. Matthews, 89 Ky. 267, 11 R. 505, 12 S. W. 333; Com. v. Bullock, 24 R. 78, 67 S. W. 992; and may also appeal if a new trial has been granted by lower court. Com. v. Hourigan, 89 Ky. 305, 11 R. 509, 12 S. W. 550; Com. v. Robinson, 84 S. W. 319, 27 R. 14.)

(7) Commonwealth may appeal from order granting defendant a new trial to have questions of law settled. Com. v. Huber, 126 Ky. 456, 31 R. 845, 104 S. W. 282.

(8) Verdict of acquittal is not affected by appeal. Com. v. Murphy, 109 S. W. 353; see further, Com. v. Cessna, 140 Ky. 25, 130 S. W. 811.

Title IX Appeals—To Court of Appeals-Felonies

# § 339 [8ss] [Judgment of acquittal—when can not be reversed.]

A judgment in favor of the defendant which operates as a bar to a future prosecution for the offense shall not be reversed by the Court of Appeals. (When judgment sustaining denurrer a bar, sec. 169.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1167.

# § 340 [834] [Errors that authorize reversal of judgment of conviction.]

A judgment of conviction shall be reversed for any error of law appearing on the record when, upon consideration of the whole case, the court is satisfied that the substantial rights of the defendant have been prejudiced thereby. (IV ords in brackets added by Act of March 4, 1880. c. 360, p. 42.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1168.

# § 339. Judgment of acquittal for an offense.

The punishment of which is imprisonment, shall not be reversed. Sec. 352.

# § 340.

1. Accused to be present during felony trial. For decisions relative to the right of the accused to be present during the whole of his trial for a felony, see Meece v. Com., 78 Ky. 586, 1 R. 337; Rutherford v. Com., 78 Ky. G39, 1 R. 410; Temple v. Com., 14 Bush 769; and sec. 183, and notes thereto.
2. A brief absence of the trial judge during the progress of the trial and a brief absence of the accused during the progress of the trial were not sufficient grounds to reverse a judgment of conviction. May v. Com., 153 Ky. 141, 154 S. W. 1074.
3. Accused need not be present in misdemeanor trial. Sec. 184 and notes.
4. Agreements- defendant bound by. The defendant and Commonwealth's attorney agreed that a special judge to try his case could not be procured by election, and in consequence thereof the governor appointed one. He was held bound by his agreement. The court says: "There is a broad distinction between an admission of a fact necessary to put the machinery of the law in motion, to the end that a trial may be had, and the consent of one charged with a felony to be tried by less than twelve jurors, or that one not legally qualified shall preside as judge at the trial." Kennedy v. Com., 78 Ky. 447; and see notes to sec. 154.
5. Bill of exceptions-preparation of. Sec. 282 and notes.
6. Challenge to juror. Although the disallowance of a challenge for cause by the defendant may have influenced his course in the use of the peremptory challenges allowed him, it cannot be regarded as a refusal to allow a peremptory challenge within the meaning of sec. 334 (sec. 281) of the Criminal Code, and regarded as a challenge for cause merely, it can constitute no cause for reversal. Moore v. Com., 7 Bush 191.
7. Change of venue. The Court of Appeals will not reverse on account of an error in granting or refusing a change of venue unless the decision was based on some ground not authorized by statute or amounted to an abuse of discretion. Saylor v. Com., 158 Ky. 768, 166 S. W. 254.
8. Code of 1854. Under Code of 1854, sec. 334, a reversal was only authorized for the following errors, apparent in the record, viz.: An error of the circuit court in admitting or rejecting important evidence; or in instructing or refusing to instruct the jury; or in failing to arrest the judgment; or in allowing or disallowing a peremptory challenge. Observe change made in this section.
9. Continuance. Refusal to grant when defendant entitled to continuance is ground for reversal. Sec. 189 and notes; Bates v. Com., 13 R. 132. 16 S. W. 528; Morgan v. Com., 14 Bush 106; Salisbury v. Com., 79 Ky. 425, 3 R. 211.
10. Decisions not subject to exception. A judgment cannot be reversed for a decision of the court upon challenges to the panel, or for cause, or upon motion to set aside an indictment, or upon motion for a new trial. Sec. 281 and notes; Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340; Farris v. Com., 14 Bush 362; 14 Bush 106; 82 Ky. 333. 6 R. 227; 13 Bush 246.

# § 340 Appeals-To Court of Appeals—Felonies

[Note-This rule has been changed as to motions for new trial by Act of 1910, amending Criminal Code, sec. 281.-Ed.]

1. Where the question that the jurors arrived at their verdict by lots is made for the first time in the motion for a new trial, the error can not be considered, as the court has no power (sec. 281) to revise the ruling of the lower court in overruling the motion for a new trial. *Redmon v. Com., 82 Ky. 333, 6 R. 227; overruling P. & E. R. R. Co. v. Com., 80 Ky. 147, 3 R. 650.*
2. On a trial for murder, one juror was of kin to deceased, and another juror had expressed an opinion adverse to the accused. Neither juror disclosed the above named fact on the voir dire. A judgment of conviction was reversed. *Leadingham v. Com., 180 Ky. 38, 201 S. W. 500.*
3. Errors occurring during trial must be excepted to at time they occur, or they will be waived. *Wilkerson v. Com., 88 Ky. 29, 10 R. 656, 9 S. W. 836; Rankin v. Com., 82 Ky. 425, 6 R. 407; Merritt v. Com., 11 R. 16; 14 Bush 398; 13 Bush 246; and see notes to sec. 271.*
4. Objections made privately to the judge to statements made by Commonwealth's attorney in argument do not preserve any question for the Court of Appeals. *Farris v. Com., 14 Bush 362. See Rankin v. Com., 82 Ky. 424, 6 R. 407;* and notes to sec. 227, as to improper conduct of Commonwealth's attorney.
5. Errors that authorize reversal. In reference to the amendment to this section the court says: "If this amendment is ordinarily to have any effect it must be construed to mean that the power of this court to reverse in felony cases depends upon two things: First, that an error appears on the record; second, the court must be satisfied, from consideration of the whole case, that the substantial rights of the accused have been prejudiced by the error complained of." *Tipper v. Com., 1 Met. 6; Chrystal v. Com., 9 Bush 669; Nichols v. Com., 11 Bush 575; Fenston v. Com., 82 Ky. 549, 7 R. 55; Buckles v. Com., 113 Ky. 795, 24 R. 571, 68 S. W. 1084.*
6. Objection must be made at time to introduction of incompetent evidence, and, if admitted, an exception taken, or error will not be available; an exception to the ruling of the court admitting it is not sufficient. *Branson v. Com., 92 Ky. 330, 13 R. 614, 17 S. W. 1019; and see further, Clem v. Com., 3 Met. 10; Burns v. Com., 3 Met. 14; Adwell v. Com., 17 B. M. 310; Buckles v. Com., 113 Ky. 795, 24 R. 571, 68 S. W. 1084.*
7. We must reverse for any error of law appearing in the record to the prejudice of the appellant, but this only applies to such errors as the party had a right to manifest by bill of exceptions. An error in respect to any of the matters which, under sec. 281, can not be excepted to, is not an error appearing 'in the record' within the meaning of the law.

Title IX] Appeals-To Court of Appeals Felonies. [§ 340

view of the whole case as presented. Champ v. Com., 2 Met. 17.

(19) In regard to the admission of incompetent evidence, the court says: What effect it had on the minds of the jury no one can know; we can not speculate on its effect; it is enough for this court to know that it was wrong, and may have operated to the prejudice of the appellant. Kennedy v. Com., 14 Bush 340; and see Coppage v. Com., 3 Bush 532.

(19a) A judgment of conviction of murder was reversed because the circuit court in admitting certain dying declarations did not exclude so much thereof as related to conversations and transactions on a prior occasion, Lucas v. Com., 153 Ky. 424, 155 S. W. 721.

(20) Evidence not sufficient. It was held that the Court of Appeals has no power to reverse a judgment of conviction in a criminal case upon the sole ground there was not sufficient evidence to sustain the verdict, being restricted to the single inquiry whether there was any evidence before the jury conducing to show the guilt of the accused. Vowels v. Com., 83 Ky. 193, 7 R. 176. See Tipper v. Com., 1 Met. 6; Johnson v. Com., 9 Bush 224; Kean v. Com., 10 Bush 190; Murphy v. Com., 1 Met. 365; McDaniel v. Com., 6 Bush 326; Patterson v. Com., 86 Ky. 313. 9 R. 481, 5 S. W. 387, 765; Green v. Com., 26 R. 1221, 83 S. W. 638. This rule has been changed by Act of 1910. amending Criminal Code. see. 281, and in the following cases, judgment of conviction was reversed on the evidence; Lucas v. Com., 147 Ky. 744, 145 S. W. 751; Edmons v. Com., 149 Ky. 242; Hall v. Com., 149 Ky. 42; Crews v. Com., 155 Ky. 122, 159 S. W. 638.

(20a) A judgment will not be reversed because the jury believed one set of witnesses rather than another. Conners v. Com., 152 Ky. 57, 153 S. W. 16; Cornett v. Com., 156 Ky. 795, 162 S. W. 112; Crews v. Com., 161 Ky. 614, 171 S. W. 188; May v. Com., 164 Ky. 109, 175 S. W. 17.

(21) Grounds for new trial. Under the Code of 1854 it was held that it was not necessary in the motion for a new trial to specify as one of the grounds the admission of incompetent testimony to which an exception had been taken. Johnson v. Com., 9 Bush 224; and the court in Turnbull v. Com., 79 Ky. 495, 3 R. 275, held that same rule prevails under this Code, and see Thompson v. Com., 28 R. 1137, 91 S. W. 701. Stating errors that must be pointed out in grounds for new trial. The grounds for a new trial mentioned in this Code, sec. 271, are substantially the same as those mentioned in Code of 1854, sec. 267. This Code, sec. 340, authorizes a reversal for any error of law apparent on the record, if upon the whole case the court is satisfied that the substantial rights of the accused have been prejudiced thereby. The Code of 1854, sec. 334, provided that a judgment of conviction should only be reversed for the following errors: 1. An error of the circuit court in admitting or rejecting important evidence. 2. An error in instructing, or in refusing to instruct, the jury. 3. An error in failing to arrest judgment. 4. An error in allowing or disallowing a peremptory challenge.

(21a) The fact that the jurors in a capital case were allowed to separate will not be considered on appeal from judgment of conviction unless it be made one of the grounds for a new trial. Heck v. Com., 163 Ky. 518, 174 S. W. 19.

(22) Instructions-how error in to be shown. Sec. 341 and notes.

(22a) Instructions not included in the bill of exceptions or made part of the record by order of court can not be considered on appeal. Hollin v. Com., 163 Ky. 392, 173 S. W. 1106.

(23) New trial-observe that exceptions or objections made for first time in motion for new trial will not be considered. Sec. 281 and notes.

(24) Objection and exception both necessary. In criminal as well as in civil cases neither party can rely for reversal upon an erroneous decision made at instance of adverse party, unless objection to motion or offer was made at time, and an exception taken, but where court acts on its own motion an exception alone is sufficient. Branson v. Com., 92 Ky. 330, 13 R. 614, 17 S. W. 1019; Thompson v. Com., 122 Ky. 501, 28 R. 1137, 91 S. W. 701.

(24a) Where no objection is made to the admission of certain evidence, the admission of such evidence is not ground for reversal. Harris v. Com., 163 Ky. 781, 174 S. W. 476.

(24b) The sufficiency of an indictment charging a public offense can not be questioned for the first time on appeal. Cheek v. Com., 162 Ky. 56, 171 S. W. 998.

(25) Reversible errors. Receiving verdict in absence of accused. Temple v. Com., 14 Bush 769; and refusing to permit accused to remain on bail during trial for felony, when his liberty is nec-

Appeals-To Court of Appeals-Felonies.

§340

Necessary to enable him to prepare his case. White v. Com., 80 Ky. 480, 4 R. 373; and permitting Commonwealth's attorney to state orally in the presence of the jury what rejected and incompetent evidence would be. Flint v. Com., 81 Ky. 186, 5 R. 51; and refusing to grant a change of venue when the substantial rights of the accused have been prejudiced by the refusal. Johnson v. Com., 82 Ky. 116, 5 R. 877; or continuance in certain cases, see note 7; and see notes 13a and 13b.

# (25a)

A conviction of manslaughter under an indictment for murder was reversed because the sheriff improperly permitted the jury having charge of the case to separate. Campbell v. Com., 162 Ky. 106, 172 S. W. 110

# (25b)

The propounding of certain questions to witnesses by the trial judge was prejudicial to the substantial rights of the defendant. Smith v. Com., 154 Ky. 613, 157 S. W. 1089.

# (25c)

The error of the trial court in refusing a new trial of a capital case on the ground of separation of the jury, is reviewable by the Court of Appeals, although the error was first brought to the attention of the circuit court on a motion for a new trial. Campbell v. Com., 162 Ky. 106, 172 S. W. 110.

# (26)

On the trial of two boys, fourteen years of age, the Court of Appeals reversed the judgment of conviction on the ground that incompetent evidence was admitted, and because of improper argument of the Commonwealth's attorney; although no objection or exception was saved. McClure v. Com., 81 Ky. 148, 5 R. 468; and see, as to trial of infants, Willet v. Com., 13 Bush 230; Heilman v. Com., 84 Ky. 457, 8 R. 451, 1 S. W. 731.

# (27)

Res Judicata. The opinion of the court is conclusive of all matters actually decided or presented by the record and which should have been decided and on a second appeal such questions will not be considered. Arnold v. Com., 23 R. 182, 62 S. W. 15.

# (27a)

On a second appeal the Court of Appeals is bound by its ruling on the first appeal. Slaughter v. Com., 152 Ky. 128, 153 S. W. 46.

# (28)

Substantial error. It must affirmatively appear that error prejudiced substantial rights of accused or it will not authorize reversal. Collett v. Com., 121 S. W. 426; Hargis v. Com., 135 Ky. 578, 123 S. W. 239; Reed v. Com., 138 Ky. 568, 128 S. W. 874; Parrish v. Con., 136 Ky. 77, 123 S. W. 339; Williams v. Com., 153 Ky. 710, 156 S. W. 372; Gabbard v. Com., 154 Ky. 140, 156 S. W. 1037; Franks v. Com., 163 Ky. 96, 173 S. W. 327; Deaton v. Com., 157 Ky. 308, 163 S. W. 204.

# (29)

An erroneous instruction on voluntary manslaughter was held harmless, in view of an instruction on involuntary manslaughter, that cured the error. Curtis v. Com., 169 Ky. 727, 184 S. W. 1105.

# (30)

The fact that the jury obtained and read newspapers containing accounts of the trial was not sufficient ground for setting aside the verdict. O'Hara v. Com., 164 Ky. 403, 175 S. W. 637.

# (31)

Substantive evidence was introduced in rebuttal. In this case this action was not prejudicial error. Allen v. Com., 176 Ky. 475, 196 S. W. 160.

# (32)

A new trial will not be granted because the jury adopted an improper method of deciding the verdict unless defendant's rights were thereby prejudiced. Bennett v. Com., 175 Ky. 540, 194 S. W. 797; Choate v. Com., 176 Ky. 427, 195 S. W. 1080.

# (33)

Permitting a jury to have a view of the place where a crime is charged to have been committed, without the presence of the defendant, is error; a reversal of a judgment of conviction on account of such error will not be made if it is affirmatively shown that the substantial rights of the defendant were not prejudiced thereby. Carney alias Kerney v. Com., 181 Ky. 443.

# (34)

In a prosecution under the local option law, under Ky. Stat., sec. 2557, an instruction as to punishment which authorizes a minimum jail sentence of ten days, was not prejudicial error where the verdict fixed the jail sentence at the statutory maximum. Shirley v. Com., 170 Ky. 724, 186 S. W. 642.

# (35)

Two persons were indicted jointly for murder. During a greater part of the trial of one of them, the other was absent from the courtroom. After the verdict had been returned, he requested that the same jury be empaneled and sworn to try him on the evidence it had already heard on the trial of the other defendant, which was done. The verdict against him was sustained. Bonar v. Com., 180 Ky. 338, 202 S. W. 676.

# § 341 [885] [Instructions to appear in bill of exceptions-reversible error.]

A judgment shall not be reversed for an error of the court in instructing or refusing to instruct the jury, unless the bill of exceptions contain all the instructions given by the court to the jury, and unless it shall thereupon appear that the law applicable to the case was not correctly and fairly given to the jury. (See as to preparation of bill of exceptions, sec. 282, and form of bill, Form 291.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1030, 1169.

# § 341.

1. Instructions. It is the duty of the trial judge to give to the jury the whole law of the case whether requested so to do or not and no exception or objection is necessary. Buckles v. Com., 113 Ky. 795, 24 R. 571; but it is necessary to specify error in motion for new trial. Thompson v. Com., 122 Ky. 501, 28 R. 1137, 91'S. W. 701.
2. Instructions-statement as to, in bill of exceptions. Instructions given, but not embraced in the bill of exceptions, can not be considered, although counsel consent that they may be considered. Oder v. Com., 80 Kiy. 32, 4 R. 18.
3. Following the bill of evidence and exceptions, signed by the judge, are the following statements made by the clerk, to-wit: "Instructions given are as follows," and after this is what purports to be the instructions referred to. After this is the statement: "Instructions refused by the court are as follows," followed by what purports to be the instructions refused. The instructions were not properly made part of the record and can not be considered. Mitchell v. Com., 78 Ky. 204.
4. "The bill of exceptions shows that an instruction, designated as instruction No. 4, was given on motion of the Commonwealth. It is not, however, embodied in the bill of exceptions; and although a paper purporting to be instruction No. 4 is copied into the record, there is no statement either by the clerk or court tending to identify it; we might, therefore, decline to consider it." Weatherford v. Con., 10 Bush 196.
5. "As the instructions asked by defendant are not copied in the record, being lost as stated by the clerk, it must be presumed that the ruling of the court in relation to them was right." McDaniel v. Com., 6 Bush 326.
6. The following statement is not sufficient to show that bill of exceptions contains all the instructions. "This being all the evidence, the attorney for the Commonwealth asked the court to give the following instructions—going on to enumerate them—to each and all of which the defendant objected, but the court overruled the objection and defendant at the time excepted and still excepts. The defendant then asked the court to give the following instructions, which the court gave—going on in like manner to set them out." Clem v. Com., 3 Met. 10; see Jane v. Com., 3 Met. 18.
7. "It is not necessary for the record to state, in terms, that all the instructions given were contained in the bill of exceptions. Any equivalent expression or any language or statement that would convey, with reasonable certainty, the same idea, would doubtless be held sufficient." Jane v. Coin., 3 Met. 18; 3 Met. 10.
8. 'The statement in the record that the defendant moved "the following instructions," and that the Commonwealth moved "following," and that then "the court also gave to the jury the following instructions in lieu of those refused and in explanation of those given," was held sufficient to show that record contained all the instructions. Smith v. Com., 1 Duv. 224; Colley v. Com., 11 R. 346, 12 S. W. 132.
9. A bill stating that "the court, on motion of the Commonwealth's attorney, instructed the jury as follows, to-wit" (giving the instructions), and that "the court instructed the jury as follows on motion of defendant" (giving them), was held sufficient. Mickey v. Com., 9 Bush 593.
10. Observe that bill of exceptions must be prepared the same as in civil case, sec. 282; and see further, as to bill of exceptions and proper rule as to embodying therein the instructions, notes to sec. 335, Civil Code.
11. At the trial of a certain indictment under Ky. Stat., see. 1155, the girl

# §342-346 Appeals-To Court of Appeals-Felonies.

# § 342 [ss6] [Death of accused terminates appeal.]

No appeal shall be taken after the defendant's death, and upon his death an appeal taken during his life shall abate and shall not be revived.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1170.

# § 343 [337] [Effect of appeal by defendant in penitentiary.]

If a judgment of confinement in the penitentiary have been executed before the certificate of the appeal is delivered to the sheriff whose duty it is to execute the judgment, the defendant shall remain in the penitentiary during the pendency of the appeal, unless discharged by the expiration of his term of confinement, or by pardon; and upon a reversal, if a new trial be ordered, shall be removed back from the penitentiary to the county jail from which he was brought, by the sheriff of said county.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1171.

# § 344 [88] [Removal of defendant from penitentiary after reversal.]

Upon a mandate of reversal, ordering a new trial, being filed in the clerk's office of the circuit court in which the judgment of confinement in the penitentiary, which has been executed, was rendered, the clerk shall deliver to the sheriff a copy of the mandate, and a precept authorizing and commanding him to bring the defendant from the penitentiary to the county jail, which shall be obeyed by the sheriff and keeper of the penitentiary.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1171.

# § 345 [880] [Credit for confinement on second conviction.]

If the defendant, upon the new trial, be again convicted, the period of his former confinement in the penitentiary shall be deducted by the court from the period of confinement fixed in the last verdict of conviction, or be allowed by the court in fixing the period of confinement, although such allowance reduce the period for which the defendant is sentenced below the shortest period prescribed by law as a penalty for the offense of which he has been found guilty; unless the period of confinement fixed in the verdict or by the court be for life.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, sec. 1171.

# § 346 [340] [Affirmance of death penalty--proceedings.]

When a judgment of death has been affirmed, the clerk of the Court of Appeals shall transmit to the governor a certificate of the affirmance and of the judgment of the circuit court, to the end that a warrant for the execution of the judgment may be issued by the governor. The governor shall send his warrant of execution by a special messenger, or by mail, to the proper.

# § 347 [842] [Jurisdiction of Court of Appeals.]

The Court of Appeals shall have appellate jurisdiction in penal actions and prosecutions for misdemeanors, in the following cases only, viz.: If the judgment be for a fine exceeding fifty dollars, or for imprisonment exceeding thirty days; or, if the judgment be for the defendant, in cases in which a fine exceeding fifty dollars, or confinement exceeding thirty days, might have been inflicted.

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 718, 899.

# § 347. (1 Appeal-jurisdiction.

The Court of Appeals has no jurisdiction in misdemeanors unless the judgment be for a fine exceeding fifty dollars, or for imprisonment exceeding thirty days, or if the judgment be for defendant, in cases in which a fine exceeding fifty dollars or confinement exceeding thirty days might have been inflicted. Anderson v. Com., 14 Bush 171; C. & O. R. Co. v. Com., 116 S. W. 323; Com. v. Ainsworth, 147 Ky. 771, 145 S. W. 750; Com. v. Williams, 86 S. W. 553, 27 R. 695; Noe v. Com., 134 Ky. 618, 121 S. W. 421; Com. v. Huber, 126 Ky. 456, 104 S. W. 282, 31 R. 846; Bailey v. Com., 92 S. W. 545, 29 R. 105; Conley v. Com., 141 Ky. 730, 133 S. W. 792; Holcomb v. Com., 149 Ky. 442, 149 S. W. 889.

(la) No appeal lies to the Court of Appeals from a judgment imposing a fine of $25 for violation of a city ordinance of a city of the first class. Keiper v. City of Lou., 151 Ky. 691, 152 S. W. 761.

(lb) The Court of Appeals has jurisdiction of an appeal from a judgment for a fine less than $50, which judgment

Appeals To Court of Appeals-Misdemeanors

# § 348 [848] [Time and manner of appealing by defendant.]

The appeal must be prayed during the term at which the judgment is rendered, and shall be granted upon the condition that the record be lodged in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeal within sixty days after the judgment. (Form of order, Form 296.)

Cross reference: See Gregory's Ky. Crim. Law, secs. 1151, 1172.

also requires a nuisance to be abated. King v. Com., 153 Ky. 404, 155 S. W. 749.

# (lc) An appeal lies from a fine exceeding $50, although recovered in a penal action.

Phoenix Hotel Co. v. Com., 153 Kv. 507, 156 S. W. 117.

# (ld) If a defendant after superseding a judgment for conviction in which the fine exceeds $50 and the imprisonment is less than thirty days pays the fine, the Court of Appeals loses jurisdiction of the appeal.

Eutsler v. Com., 154 Ky. 35, 156 S. W. 855.

# (le) Obstructing passway.

The Court of Appeals has no jurisdiction of an appeal from a judgment for a fine under Ky. Stat., sec. 4354. Rowe v. Com., 153 Ky. 338, 155 S. W. 753.

When judgment is rendered against defendants under an indictment for misdemeanor, they cannot enter a motion for a new trial or prosecute an appeal unless they surrender themselves in execution of the judgment or supersede it. Norton v. Com., 78 Ky. 501; see Wilson v. Com., 10 Bush 526.

# (3) An appeal may be prosecuted by the defendant from an order of the circuit court directing his name to be stricken from the roll of attorneys of such court upon charges of malpractice and official misconduct.

Turner v. Com., 2 Met. 619: and see, as to jurisdiction of appeals under the Code of 1854, Com. v. Crump, 18 B. M. 469; Holden v. Com., 2 Bush 30.

# (4) When the judgment convicts the defendant of an infamous offense and deprives him of the right to hold office he may appeal, although the fine imposed does not exceed fifty dollars.

Cheek v. Com., 87 Ky. 42, 9 R. 880, 7 S. W. 403; Johnson v. Com., 90 Ky. 53, 12 R. 20, 13 S. W. 520.

# (5) If the jury imposes a less fine than fifty dollars, although the fine fixed by law is greater than that amount, the defendant cannot appeal.

Johnson v. Com., 90 Ky. 53, 12 R. 20. 13 S. W. 520; Com. v. Dockery, 23 R. 777, 64 S. W. 460.

# (6) Proceeding to recover amount of a forfeited bail bond is a civil action and no appeal lies unless amount is $200.

Speckert v. Com., 111 Ky. 375, 23 R. 707, 63 S. W. 752.

# (7) An appeal may be taken by the Commonwealth if a fine exceeding fifty dollars might have been inflicted.

Com. v. Williams, 27 R. 695, 86 S. W. 553.

# (8) Nuisance - appeal lies from judgment in prosecution for, although fine is less than $50.

Leitchfield Co. v. Com., 143 Ky. 162, 136 S. W. 639.

# (9) Order requiring peace bond is not appealable.

Lowe v. Com., 129 Ky. 565, 112 S. W. 647.

# (10) Contempt - appeal lies from judgment imposing.

French v. Com., 97 S. W. 427, 30 R. 98.

# §348. (1 Appeal - how taken, and when record must be filed.

Judgment upon verdict for misdemeanor was rendered against defendants. They prayed an appeal and executed supersedeas bond before the clerk of circuit court, but failed to file any transcript of the record or copy of the supersedeas bond, and obtain from the clerk of Court of Appeals a certificate that execution of the judgment was suspended. Held that there was no obstruction to the collection of the judgment in the lower court, and that, having failed to file the transcript in time, their appeal must be treated as abandoned.

Com. v. Howard, 81 Ky. 57, 4 R. 674.

# (2) The court has no jurisdiction of the appeal unless the record is lodged in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals within sixty days after judgment.

Stratton v. Com., 84 Ky. 190, 8 R. 77. 1 S. W. 83; Metcalf v. Com., 84 Kv. 485, 8 R. 547, 1 S. W. 878; Mackey v. Com., 80 Kv. 345, 4 R. 179; Com. v. Adams, 16 B. M. 338; Com. v. McCready, 2 Met. 376; Adkins v. Com., 102 Ky. 94, 19 R. 1300, 43 S. W. 834; Putnam v. Com., 109 S. W. 903; Berge v. Com., 158 Ky. 424, 165 S. W. 410.

# (2a) It is the duty of the circuit court to grant an appeal prayed under this section; but unless it grants the appeal no appeal is pending.

Should an appeal be prayed and the circuit court refuse to grant same, the defendant may by a proper proceeding in the Court of Appeals.

Title IX

# Appeals-To Court of Appeals-Misdemeanors.

# § 349 [844] [Execution not suspended unless bond executed—conditions of bond.]

The appeal shall not suspend the execution of the judgment, unless the defendant cause to be executed before the clerk of the circuit court a covenant, by good surety, to be approved by said clerk, for the payment, in case the judgment be affirmed, of the fine and costs and costs of the appeal and all damages thereon, and for the surrender of the defendant in execution of the judgment, if the judgment be for imprisonment, or, on his failure so to surrender himself, for the payment of a sum equal to two dollars for every day of imprisonment adjudged; and cause said covenant to be copied into the transcript; upon which being lodged with the clerk of the Court of Appeals, he shall issue a certificate that exe-

peals require the circuit judge to enter an order granting the appeal and to sign a proper bill of exceptions. His right of appeal then will continue for sixty days from the time the order directing the granting of the appeal is entered. *King v. Com., 153 Ky. 404, 155 S. W. 749.*

# (2b)

The transcript must be filed in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals within sixty days from the time the judgment is signed by the judge, although it may have been spread on the order book more than sixty days before the filing of the transcript in the Court of Appeals. Within the sixty days the Court of Appeals has power to extend the time for filing the transcript by order; and if the transcript be filed within the time as extended, the Court of Appeals has jurisdiction of the appeal. *Carroll v. Con., 164 Ky. 599, 175 S. W. 1043.*

# (3)

An appeal must be taken during the term at which judgment is rendered. *Com. v. Adams, 16 B. M. 338; and this rule applies whether the appeal be prayed by the Commonwealth or by the defendant. Cqm. v. McCready, 2 Met. 376.*

The provision requiring the record to be filed within sixty days after judgment cannot be suspended by consent of parties. If time can be given until the next term to prepare and file a bill of exceptions it can only be done by withholding or suspending judgment. *Com. v. McCready, 2 Met. 376. Adkins v. Com., 102 Ky. 94, 19 R. 1300, 42 S. W. 834, 44 S. W. 132.*

# (5)

Motion for a new trial suspends the judgment, and if it be continued to the next term and then be overruled, an appeal may then be taken, and the record lodged in the clerk's office of the Court of Appeals within sixty days after the judgment is rendered overruling the motion for a new trial. An appeal cannot be prosecuted until after the motion for a new trial has been disposed of. *Louisville Chemical Works v. Com., 8 Bush 179; Com. v. Bavarian Brewing Co., 114 Ky. 877, 24 R. 1663, 72 S. W. 13.*

# (6)
